title: Engage in Meaningful and Respectful Discourse
expectation: Engage in meaningful and respectful discourse by listening actively, responding appropriately, and adjusting communication to audiences and purposes.
overview: This is the entry-level oral communication TEK — the foundation for everything else in Standard 1. It's not about being polite; it's about whether a student can actually track what's happening in a conversation, respond to what was said (not to what they planned to say), and read the room well enough to shift register when the audience or purpose changes.
studentFriendly: You can stay locked in on what someone is saying, respond to their actual point — not just yours — and shift how you talk depending on who you're talking to and why.
questionStems: Read the following exchange between two students. Which response BEST demonstrates active listening?
Based on the conversation in lines X–Y, the student MOST likely adjusted her communication in order to —
Which statement from the discussion BEST supports the conclusion that the speaker is responding to what was actually said?
In lines X–Y, the speaker shifts his tone. This shift MOST likely reflects a change in —
Which sentence from the transcript BEST illustrates the speaker adapting her message to her audience?
The speaker's response in paragraph X suggests that he MOST likely understood the previous speaker to mean —
Based on the exchange in lines X–Y, which inference about the speakers' purpose is BEST supported?
In the discussion transcript, the student's use of [phrase] in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Which detail from the conversation BEST supports the inference that the speaker adjusted her communication for the purpose of —
Read paragraphs X and Y from the panel discussion. How does the second speaker's response contribute to the overall meaning of the exchange?

misconceptions.title: "Listening" Means Being Quiet Until It's Your Turn
misconceptions.body: Students wait through other speakers instead of listening to them. They sit silent but mentally rehearsing their next point, which means their response addresses their own previous thought rather than what was just said. Teach them that the test for active listening is whether their response could only have been said AFTER what the other person said — if it could have been said before, they weren't listening.

misconceptions.title: Respectful Discourse Means Agreeing
misconceptions.body: Students conflate respect with agreement. They soften disagreement into mush or say nothing rather than challenge an idea in front of peers. Reframe this immediately: the most respectful thing you can do is take someone's argument seriously enough to push back on it. Silence or hollow affirmation is not respect — it's avoidance.

misconceptions.title: Adjusting Communication Means Dumbing It Down
misconceptions.body: When students hear "adjust for your audience," they assume the only adjustment is making things simpler. Watch for students who just slow down and use shorter words when presenting to younger students or authority figures, while missing the deeper adjustments — what evidence to lead with, what to assume the audience already knows, what emotional register fits. Adjusting communication is strategic, not just cosmetic.

misconceptions.title: Responding Appropriately Is About Tone, Not Content
misconceptions.body: Students think "appropriate" means hitting the right register (professional vs. casual) and stop there. But appropriateness is also about relevance — a perfectly polished response that misses the point is still inappropriate. Push them to check two things every time: Did I match the tone? Did I actually address what was asked or said?

misconceptions.title: This TEK Only Applies During Class Discussions
misconceptions.body: Students treat oral communication skills as classroom performance — something to turn on during Socratic seminar and off everywhere else. Watch for students who are polished in formal settings but can't carry those habits into small-group work, partner talk, or impromptu exchanges. The standard says "audiences and purposes," plural — make sure practice spans formal and informal contexts.

assignments.name: Tracking the Thread
assignments.body: Students listen to a short recorded discussion (2–3 minutes) and annotate a transcript: mark every moment a speaker responds directly to the previous speaker vs. pivots to their own point. Debrief: what percentage of responses were actually replies?

assignments.name: Audience Audit
assignments.body: Students prepare a 60-second explanation of the same concept for three audiences: a peer who knows nothing about it, a teacher, and a younger student. Each version is recorded. Class compares what changed across versions and names the specific adjustments made.

assignments.name: Live Discussion Score Card
assignments.body: During a class Socratic seminar, half the students participate while the other half use a simple three-column score card: active listening observed, responded to what was said, adjusted communication. Roles swap. Students compare observations and discuss where the group's discourse broke down.

mastery: Responds directly to what the previous speaker said — demonstrating that the content of their listening shaped the content of their reply.
Sustains engagement across a full discussion without losing the thread or defaulting to a rehearsed position regardless of what others say.
Shifts vocabulary, tone, formality, and emphasis demonstrably based on audience and purpose — not just slowing down or simplifying words.
Disagrees with a peer's argument respectfully and specifically, naming what they disagree with rather than the person who said it.
Self-monitors in real time — catches their own tangential response and redirects back to the question at hand.
notes: This is the entry point for Standard 1 with no vertical predecessor — English I establishes the baseline. English II (110.37(1)(A)) carries the expectation forward with added emphasis on evaluating the quality of the discourse itself, not just participating in it. English III adds diction and syntax as specific critique targets.

DOK 3 justification: adjusting communication to audiences and purposes requires strategic thinking across contexts — students must select and apply communication choices, not just recall or execute a fixed routine.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is not directly assessed as a discrete STAAR item in written testing. Question stems are written to mirror how STAAR tests oral language via speech/discussion transcripts treated as prose passages — the same analysis applied to a written text applied here to a spoken one. Note this in classroom instruction so students recognize the crossover.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Follow and give complex oral instructions
expectation: Follow and give complex oral instructions to perform specific tasks, answer questions, or solve problems and complex processes.
overview: Students learn to receive multi-step verbal information and execute on it — and, just as critically, to package their own thinking into instructions another person can actually follow. This is the oral-language version of "show your work": clarity under spoken pressure, with no chance to re-read.
studentFriendly: You can listen to a multi-step set of spoken instructions, hold the steps in your head long enough to act on them, and ask the right question if something's missing — and you can give instructions clearly enough that someone else can do the task without having to guess what you meant.
questionStems: I just gave you that direction once. Walk me back through it in your own words — what's step one?
Where in that set of instructions did you start to lose the thread? What would have kept you anchored?
You're explaining this process to a sub who's never seen it. What's the order, and what would you have to say out loud that we usually don't?
Someone follows your instructions and gets the wrong result. Was the problem in their listening or in your wording? How can you tell?
What's the difference between "I heard you" and "I can do what you said"? Show me with this task.
A direction had a step you didn't understand. What's the question that would actually unstick you — not just "huh?"
Re-give those instructions, but assume the listener is rushed and stressed. What changes about how you say them?
When does writing it down help, and when is the act of writing it down what's making you fall behind on listening?
You're giving a procedure with seven steps. Which two are most likely to get skipped, and what do you do to protect them?
Listen to a classmate's instructions and execute exactly what they said — no improvising. Where did the gap between their words and your action show up?

misconceptions.title: "Heard it" equals "got it"
misconceptions.body: Students conflate auditory reception with comprehension. They nod through a six-step direction and then freeze on step three. Teach them that the move after listening is rehearsal — silently repeat the steps in your head, or paraphrase to a partner — before acting. Reception isn't the skill; processing is.

misconceptions.title: Asking a question makes you look unprepared
misconceptions.body: Many students will guess and execute incorrectly rather than ask "wait, before step two or after?" because asking feels like admitting weakness. Reframe it: a clarifying question is the move that prevents wasted work. Model it yourself in front of them — interrupt your own instructions and ask whether anyone needs you to back up.

misconceptions.title: Giving instructions is just listing steps in order
misconceptions.body: Students think procedural speech is mechanical — you just say what comes first, then next. Real instruction-giving is anticipatory: it warns about the part where people usually get stuck, names what success looks like, and uses tone and pace to mark which steps matter most. Teach them that "what" without "watch out for" is half a direction.

misconceptions.title: The listener is responsible for keeping up
misconceptions.body: When a classmate doesn't follow their instructions, students blame the listener. Push them to audit their own delivery — Were the steps grouped logically? Did you pause between them? Did you use vague pronouns ("put it there") that assumed shared context? The speaker's job is to make following possible.

misconceptions.title: Notes are for reading, not for listening
misconceptions.body: Students treat notetaking as a written-text skill and fail to deploy it during oral instructions. Show them shorthand strategies — numbered list, arrows, single keywords — that capture sequence without slowing them down enough to miss the next step. The goal isn't transcription; it's a memory hook.

assignments.name: Blind Build (lab/activity)
assignments.body: One student gives oral instructions; partner builds something (LEGO, origami, drawing) without seeing the model. Debrief: where did the instructions break down?

assignments.name: Sub Plans, Verbally
assignments.body: Students record a 3-minute audio explaining a class procedure (turn-in routine, lab setup) for a hypothetical substitute teacher. Peer-grade for completeness and clarity.

assignments.name: Recipe Telephone
assignments.body: Pass a multi-step procedure orally through 4 students. Compare original to final version. Analyze where information got lost.

assignments.name: Process Interview
assignments.body: Students interview a peer about how they do something complex (a sport move, a video game strategy, a hobby). Transcribe instructions; give back to interviewee for accuracy check.

assignments.name: Listen-and-Reproduce Quiz
assignments.body: Read a 5-step process aloud once. Students execute or write back. Score not just accuracy but where errors clustered (early steps, late, transitions).

mastery: Follows a 5+ step oral direction the first time, with no repetition needed, and executes accurately.
Asks targeted clarifying questions ("did you mean before or after step 3?") instead of vague ones ("what?") when a step is unclear.
Gives verbal instructions that another novice can follow to completion without intervention.
Adjusts delivery (pace, grouping, emphasis) when the listener signals they're losing the thread.
Uses appropriate notetaking shorthand during long oral processes without losing input.
notes: **Voice approved:** veteran ELA teacher briefing a new one (per HANDOFF.md).

**Vertical alignment:** B-letter through Grade 8 → English I → English II is the same expectation almost verbatim. By Grade 11 (110.38(1)(B)) it adds "clarify meaning by asking pertinent questions" — the asking-questions piece becomes explicit later, but it's already implied here.

title: Give a Presentation Using Informal, Formal, and Technical Language Effectively
expectation: Give a presentation using informal, formal, and technical language effectively to meet the needs of audience, purpose, and occasion, employing eye contact, speaking rate such as pauses for effect, volume, enunciation, purposeful gestures, and conventions of language to communicate ideas effectively.
overview: This is the full-stack delivery standard — students don't just speak, they make deliberate choices about register, pace, body, and word selection based on who is in the room and why. The real complexity here is register switching: knowing when informal language serves the room and when technical language is the credibility move, and being able to shift between them without losing the thread.
studentFriendly: You can give a presentation that sounds right for the audience you're in front of — choosing when to be formal, when to be conversational, when to use technical vocabulary — and controlling your pace, eye contact, volume, and gestures so the message actually lands.
questionStems: Read this excerpt from a student's speech. The speaker's use of technical vocabulary in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Which sentence from the speech BEST demonstrates the speaker's awareness of audience and occasion?
Based on paragraph X, the speaker's shift in language register MOST likely serves to —
The speaker's use of the phrase [phrase] in paragraph X is MOST nearly an example of —
Which detail from the speech BEST supports the inference that the speaker adjusted their language for a formal audience?
How does the speaker's use of pauses in paragraph X MOST likely contribute to the audience's understanding of the message?
The speaker's word choice in paragraphs X–Y suggests that the intended audience is MOST likely —
Based on the speech, which statement BEST describes how the speaker uses gestures to support the message?
Which revision to paragraph X would MOST effectively adjust the speaker's register for a technical audience?
The speaker's enunciation and volume in paragraph X are MOST likely intended to —

misconceptions.title: Formal Means Complicated
misconceptions.body: Students assume formal language requires long sentences and advanced vocabulary, so they write stiff, overloaded scripts that collapse under their own weight when spoken aloud. Teach them that formal register is about precision and appropriateness, not complexity — a well-constructed simple sentence is more formal than a tangled one. Watch for students who substitute density for clarity and call it professionalism.

misconceptions.title: Technical Language Is Always a Strength
misconceptions.body: Students drop technical terms to sound authoritative without checking whether the audience actually knows them. The standard asks for technical language used *effectively* — meaning a speaker who buries a general audience in jargon has failed the standard even if every term was accurate. Push them to define, contextualize, or translate technical language in real time.

misconceptions.title: Eye Contact Means Staring
misconceptions.body: Students interpret the eye contact requirement as a sustained gaze that ends up looking aggressive or anxious rather than engaging. Teach them that purposeful eye contact means distributing attention across the room, landing on different audience members, and connecting — not locking onto one spot and not scanning so fast it reads as nervous. One real way to practice: hold a gaze for a full thought, then move.

misconceptions.title: Gestures Are Optional Decorations
misconceptions.body: Students treat gestures as filler — hands clasped, arms crossed, or waving vaguely when they run out of things to say. The word "purposeful" in the standard is doing real work. Teach them that every gesture should either reinforce an idea, mark a structural shift, or invite the audience in. Gestures that contradict the message (pointing when saying "we all" instead of "I") undermine credibility.

misconceptions.title: Register Is Set Once at the Start
misconceptions.body: Students decide their register before the presentation and then never adjust — all formal, or all casual, depending on what they thought the assignment required. The skill is actually dynamic: a presentation on a technical topic for a mixed audience might need formal framing, informal transitions, and precise technical vocabulary in the same three minutes. Teach them to read the room mid-speech and adjust, not just pre-plan.

assignments.name: Three-Register Pitch
assignments.body: Students prepare a 60-second pitch on the same topic — once for a peer (informal), once for a panel of professionals (formal), once for subject-matter experts (technical). They deliver all three back-to-back and debrief which language choices changed and why.

assignments.name: Gesture Audit
assignments.body: Students record themselves delivering a presentation, then watch it on mute. They annotate their gesture map: which gestures reinforced meaning, which were filler, which were contradictory. They revise and re-record one 60-second segment.

assignments.name: Pause Engineering
assignments.body: Students mark their script with three deliberate pauses — at a high-stakes claim, at a structural transition, and immediately before the closing line. They rehearse with a timer. Discussion follows on what pauses create in a listening audience.

assignments.name: Register Switch Analysis
assignments.body: Students study a transcript of a professional speaker who navigates multiple registers in one speech (a TED talk with both technical content and audience-facing humor works well). They color-code informal, formal, and technical language, then identify what audience signal prompted each shift.

assignments.name: Live Audience Adjustment
assignments.body: Students deliver a one-minute prepared talk. Midway through, the teacher signals the "audience" to shift — from peers to a board of executives, or from general public to a panel of field experts. Students must adjust register on the fly without stopping. Debrief: what changed and what should have changed?

mastery: Selects language register (informal, formal, technical) deliberately and shifts between registers when the audience or purpose calls for it — not locked into one mode.
Distributes eye contact across the room intentionally, holding contact long enough to signal connection before moving on.
Uses pauses purposefully — at high-stakes claims, structural transitions, and emotional beats — rather than filling silence with filler words.
Employs gestures that reinforce or clarify meaning; stops gesturing when the gesture adds nothing.
Adjusts volume and speaking rate in response to content weight and audience feedback, not just at the start of the presentation.
notes: This is English I's entry point for delivery standards — students are expected to differentiate register for the first time as a graded expectation. English II (110.37(1)(C)) builds on this by adding rhetorical strategy as an explicit layer of delivery; here the focus is on the core mechanics working together.

DOK 3 justification: students must make strategic decisions across multiple simultaneous variables (register, rate, gesture, volume, eye contact) adapted to a specific audience and purpose — this is strategic thinking in performance, not procedural recall.

STAAR does not test oral delivery directly; stems above mirror how STAAR tests the analog through speech transcripts and author's craft questions. Note this in classroom use so students understand the transfer.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Participate Collaboratively in Structured Group Discussion
expectation: Participate collaboratively, building on the ideas of others, contributing relevant information, developing a plan for consensus building, and setting ground rules for decision making.
overview: This TEK is about structured collaboration — not just talking in groups, but doing the intellectual work of connecting to others' ideas, staying relevant, and building a functional process for reaching shared decisions. The stakes are procedural and social at once: students who can't do this will derail seminars, tank group projects, and lose readers when they write for audiences who disagree with them.
studentFriendly: You can join a group discussion in a way that actually moves it forward — connecting your ideas to what others have said, staying on topic, and helping the group make decisions everyone can live with.
questionStems: Based on the discussion transcript, which statement BEST demonstrates a student building on a classmate's idea rather than redirecting the conversation?
Which contribution from the discussion is MOST relevant to the group's central question in paragraph X?
A student in paragraph X says [quote]. Which statement MOST likely reflects the ground rule the group is following?
Which exchange in paragraphs X–Y BEST illustrates the group moving toward consensus?
Based on the discussion, with which guideline for decision making would MOST members of the group MOST likely agree?
The student's comment in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Which detail from the discussion BEST supports the conclusion that the group had established ground rules before beginning?
How does the student's contribution in paragraph X develop the group's plan for reaching a decision?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the group's discussion is BEST described as —
Which statement from the discussion MOST clearly contributes relevant information to the argument introduced in paragraph X?

misconceptions.title: Collaboration Means Agreeing
misconceptions.body: Students think being a good group member means nodding along. Watch for groups where one voice dominates and everyone else performs agreement — that's not consensus, it's capitulation. Teach them that building on an idea can mean sharpening it, complicating it, or redirecting it with evidence. Productive tension is part of the job.

misconceptions.title: Ground Rules Are Just Housekeeping
misconceptions.body: Students treat ground rules as a five-minute formality before the "real" discussion starts — "one person talks at a time," done, moving on. Push them to write ground rules that actually govern hard moments: What happens when someone goes off topic? Who breaks a tie? How do we handle disagreement without it becoming personal? The rules should anticipate conflict, not just describe politeness.

misconceptions.title: "Relevant" Means "Related"
misconceptions.body: Students believe any comment loosely connected to the topic counts as a relevant contribution. The actual test is whether removing the comment would leave a gap. Teach them to ask themselves before speaking: does this move the group forward, or does it just restate what someone else said in different words? Relevance is functional, not topical.

misconceptions.title: Consensus Building Happens at the End
misconceptions.body: Students treat consensus as a vote they take after everyone has talked. In practice, consensus is built incrementally — through acknowledgment, synthesis, and explicit checking-in throughout the discussion. Teach them to name agreement as it develops: "So we're agreeing that X, even if we disagree about Y?" That move builds the decision in real time instead of hoping it materializes at the end.

misconceptions.title: Louder and Longer Equals More Credible
misconceptions.body: In English I especially, students conflate volume and quantity with authority. The student who speaks most often appears, to peers, to be leading. Reframe contribution quality: one precise, well-timed addition that synthesizes two competing ideas is worth more than three consecutive comments that repeat the same point. Grade participation on specificity and function, not frequency.

assignments.name: Ground Rules Gallery Walk
assignments.body: Groups of four draft a set of five ground rules for a high-stakes discussion (a topic they actually disagree about). Rules are posted; the class does a gallery walk and votes on the two rules from ANY group they'd most want governing their own discussion. Debrief: what makes a rule functional vs. decorative?

assignments.name: Contribution Analysis
assignments.body: Record a 10-minute group discussion (audio or video). Students transcribe their own contributions and label each one: building on an idea, introducing new information, redirecting, summarizing, or off-topic. They write a half-page reflection identifying their pattern and one thing they'd change.

assignments.name: Consensus Simulation
assignments.body: Give groups a low-stakes but genuinely contested decision (rank four novels for the class to read; allocate a fictional $500 budget). Require them to reach consensus — not majority vote — in 15 minutes. Debrief on the strategies they used and the moments where they almost broke down.

assignments.name: Discussion Fishbowl
assignments.body: An inner circle of four students has a structured discussion while the outer circle observes and tracks specific behaviors — who builds on ideas, who introduces irrelevant content, who drives toward or away from consensus. Rotate roles. Outer circle reports observations to the inner circle after each round.

mastery: References a specific peer's prior comment before introducing their own idea, demonstrating genuine uptake rather than sequential monologue.
Screens their own contributions for relevance before speaking — stays topically and argumentatively connected to the discussion's central question.
Can name the group's ground rules and apply them in real time, including in moments of disagreement.
Uses explicit synthesis moves during discussion ("So what we're agreeing on is..." / "That connects to what X said because...") rather than waiting for consensus to appear on its own.
Distinguishes between compromise and consensus — can explain why a decision reflects the group's shared reasoning rather than just the loudest position.
notes: Vertical entry point: English I is the first formal introduction to structured collaborative norms in the TEKS sequence. English II will build by adding evaluative and persuasive dimensions to group process. This entry is establishing baseline — students who haven't done structured academic discussion before will need explicit modeling of what "building on an idea" sounds like versus sequential sharing.

DOK 3 justification: the skill requires students to apply procedural knowledge (ground rules, relevance screening) within an unpredictable social context and to make strategic decisions about when and how to contribute — beyond simple recall or skill demonstration.

No direct STAAR analog: 1D is an oral performance standard. Question stems are written to mirror how STAAR tests collaborative discussion via transcript passages — the same skills assessed here appear in STAAR's paired-text and argumentative reading items when students must track how ideas develop across voices. This is noted so teachers understand the transfer, not the test.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Use Print or Digital Resources to Clarify Technical Vocabulary
expectation: Use print or digital resources such as glossaries or technical dictionaries to clarify and validate understanding of the precise and appropriate meaning of technical or discipline-based vocabulary.
overview: This standard is about disciplinary precision — not just "look it up," but knowing WHY a technical term means something different in biology than it does in everyday speech. The real work is teaching students that general dictionaries often fail them and that the right resource for a discipline-specific term lives somewhere more specific.
studentFriendly: When you run into a term that belongs to a specific field — a science word, a legal term, a literary concept — you know where to go to find out exactly what it means in that context, not just what it means in everyday life.
questionStems: Read this sentence from paragraph X. Based on context and the glossary entry below, which definition MOST precisely captures the meaning of [term] as it is used in the passage?
Which resource would BEST help a reader clarify the meaning of [technical term] as it is used in this science article?
The word [term] in paragraph X is used as a technical term. Based on context clues and the dictionary entry provided, what does [term] MOST nearly mean?
A student reads [term] in a biology textbook and looks it up in a standard dictionary. Why might the student's understanding of the word still be inaccurate?
Which sentence from the passage BEST illustrates the precise, discipline-specific use of [term]?
Read the glossary entry below. Based on this information, which statement about [term] is MOST accurate as it applies to the passage?
A reader encounters [term] in paragraph X. Which of the following definitions from a technical dictionary would MOST likely help the reader understand the author's meaning?
The author uses the term [term] in paragraph X. Based on its discipline-specific meaning, what can the reader conclude about [topic]?
Which of the following sentences uses [technical term] in a way that is MOST consistent with its meaning in a scientific or technical context?
Based on the technical dictionary entry and paragraph X, [term] MOST likely refers to —

misconceptions.title: A General Dictionary Is Good Enough
misconceptions.body: Students grab whatever dictionary is closest — usually an online general dictionary — and accept the first definition they see. Teach them that a term like "medium," "solution," or "resolution" carries a completely different meaning in a chemistry lab, a media studies class, or a literary discussion. The habit to build is asking first: which field is this term coming from?

misconceptions.title: Looking It Up Once Means They Understand It
misconceptions.body: Students find a definition, copy it down, and move on. They haven't validated anything — they've just located a string of words. Push them to read the definition back into the sentence and ask whether it actually fits the context. If it doesn't, the wrong resource or wrong entry was used. The standard says "clarify AND validate" — both moves matter.

misconceptions.title: Technical Dictionaries Are Only for Science
misconceptions.body: Students (and sometimes teachers) default to technical vocabulary as a science-class concern. Watch for this in history, economics, literary criticism, and law — each discipline has its own register and its own preferred reference tools. An economics glossary is as much a technical resource as a chemistry reference; teach students to recognize disciplinary vocabulary across all their coursework.

misconceptions.title: The Most Complicated Definition Is the Most Precise
misconceptions.body: When students find a technical definition that sounds dense or intimidating, they assume it's more accurate and copy it wholesale. Precision isn't complexity — it's fit. A precise definition is one that correctly limits the term to its specific meaning in context. Teach them to test the definition against the sentence, not against how official it sounds.

misconceptions.title: Digital Glossaries Are Less Reliable Than Print
misconceptions.body: Some students distrust online references by default; others trust them without question. Neither reflex is useful. The relevant question is always authority: Is this a publisher's glossary? A discipline-specific database? A professional organization's terminology guide? Teach students to evaluate the source of a digital resource the same way they'd evaluate any other text — origin, purpose, and expertise matter more than format.

assignments.name: Resource Audit
assignments.body: Give students a list of ten technical terms drawn from three different disciplines. They must find the correct discipline-specific resource for each term, locate the definition, and explain in one sentence why a general dictionary would or would not have been sufficient.

assignments.name: Definition Showdown
assignments.body: Present the same term (e.g., "medium," "culture," "expression") with its definition from a general dictionary and its definition from a discipline-specific glossary. Students write a paragraph explaining which definition applies to a given passage and what would go wrong if a reader used the wrong one.

assignments.name: Glossary Annotation
assignments.body: Students annotate a technical reading (a textbook excerpt, a scientific article, a legal document) by identifying every discipline-specific term, locating a precise definition from an appropriate resource, and writing a one-line gloss in the margin that fits the specific context of that sentence.

mastery: Identifies when a term is functioning as discipline-specific vocabulary rather than general vocabulary and selects an appropriate reference resource accordingly.
Locates a definition and tests it against the original context — rejects or refines the definition if it does not fit.
Distinguishes between a general definition and a technical definition for the same word and explains the practical difference for understanding a passage.
Demonstrates awareness that different disciplines have different vocabulary registers and that the right resource depends on the field, not just the word.
notes: Vertical ramp: No prior-course standard — this is the entry point. English II picks up 110.37(2)(A) and is expected to extend precision into more complex disciplinary texts with greater independence. English I sets the habit of choosing the right reference tool; English II should reinforce that habit in more demanding reading contexts.

DOK 2 justification: The core skill is comprehension and application of reference tools — students locate a definition, verify it fits, and interpret it in context. This sits at DOK 2 (skill/concept). Work that requires comparing definitions across sources or evaluating resource credibility edges toward DOK 3 but the base standard targets DOK 2.

STAAR note: This TEK is not directly tested as a standalone item but is activated whenever STAAR presents a vocabulary-in-context question or provides a glossary/dictionary excerpt as a text feature. Question stems above mirror the format STAAR uses when embedding reference-tool comprehension into reading passages.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Context to Distinguish Denotative and Connotative Meanings
expectation: Analyze context to distinguish between the denotative and connotative meanings of words.
overview: This TEK is where vocabulary instruction crosses into literary analysis. Students aren't just looking words up — they're reading for the emotional and cultural freight a word carries beyond its dictionary definition, and they're using context to make that distinction. The skill underlies almost every author's craft question STAAR will ever ask.
studentFriendly: You can read a word in context and tell the difference between what it literally means and what it's meant to make you feel or assume — and you can explain how the author used that gap on purpose.
questionStems: In paragraph X, the word "[word]" MOST nearly means —
The connotation of the word "[word]" in paragraph X suggests that the narrator views [subject] as —
Which word from paragraph X has a connotation that BEST supports a negative tone?
Read this sentence from paragraph X. The word "[word]" carries a connotation that is MOST likely intended to —
In paragraph X, the author's use of the word "[word]" instead of "[neutral synonym]" suggests —
Based on context in paragraphs X–Y, the word "[word]" MOST likely implies —
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates the author's use of connotation to develop the theme?
The denotative meaning of "[word]" is closest to —
In paragraph X, the contrast between the words "[word 1]" and "[word 2]" MOST likely contributes to a feeling of —
With which statement about the word "[word]" in paragraph X would the author MOST likely agree?

misconceptions.title: The Dictionary Definition Is the Whole Answer
misconceptions.body: Students look up the word, copy the definition, and call it done. Teach them that denotation is just the starting point — the interesting work is what the word carries beyond the definition. Push them to ask: "Would a different word with the same denotation have felt the same here?" If the answer is no, there's a connotation worth examining.

misconceptions.title: Connotation Is Just Positive or Negative
misconceptions.body: Students reduce connotation to a binary: good word vs. bad word. Watch for answers like "the word 'stiff' has a negative connotation" with no further analysis. The richer move is specificity — stiff in a funeral eulogy connotes restraint and dignity; stiff in a restaurant review connotes coldness and indifference. Connotation shifts with context, and context is the whole point of this TEK.

misconceptions.title: Context Means the Sentence the Word Is In
misconceptions.body: Students read the single sentence containing the word and stop. Teach them that context is larger — it includes the speaker's situation, the relationship between characters, the tone of the whole passage, the genre. A word's connotation often can't be read in isolation; you need the surrounding paragraphs to feel the weight of the choice.

misconceptions.title: Denotation and Connotation Are Fixed Properties of a Word
misconceptions.body: Students treat connotation as a label attached permanently to a word, as if "snake" always means betrayal and "home" always means comfort. Show them counterexamples: "home" in a refugee narrative might carry dread; "savage" has been reclaimed in some contexts and remains a slur in others. Connotation is cultural, historical, and contextual — not stamped into the word at birth.

misconceptions.title: If You Know What a Word Means, You Already Know Its Connotation
misconceptions.body: Students who have strong vocabularies assume the work is done once they recognize the word. The harder skill is noticing when a familiar word is being deployed for a specific effect — when a writer says "dwelling" instead of "house," or "child" instead of "kid." Teach them to pay closest attention to the words they think they already understand, because that's where authors hide the most intentional moves.

assignments.name: Side-by-Side Swap
assignments.body: Give students a paragraph from a mentor text. Ask them to replace 5 key words with denotative synonyms (words that mean the same thing but carry less charge). Then read both versions aloud. Students write a paragraph explaining what changed and why, citing specific word choices.

assignments.name: Connotation Spectrum
assignments.body: Give students a cluster of synonyms (e.g., thin / slender / scrawny / svelte / gaunt / lean). They place each word on a spectrum from most positive to most negative connotation, then write one sentence for each that proves the placement by using context deliberately.

assignments.name: Author's Word Court
assignments.body: Present a passage where an author's word choice is arguably loaded. Students write a two-paragraph argument — one defending the author's word choice as intentional and effective, one prosecuting it as manipulative or imprecise. Then they decide which side they actually believe and explain why.

mastery: Identifies the denotative meaning of a word accurately using context, not just memory or guessing.
Articulates a word's connotation with specificity — not just "positive/negative" but what emotion, association, or assumption the word activates.
Explains how context (speaker, setting, relationship, genre) shapes which connotation is operative for a given use.
Distinguishes between two near-synonyms and explains why an author chose one over the other with reference to effect on the reader.
Recognizes when a familiar word is being used for connotative effect rather than simply for its denotation.
notes: Vertical entry point: No prior-course vertical for English I. This TEK establishes the denotation/connotation framework that English II (110.37(2)(B)) extends — English II adds figurative language interpretation layered on top of the same context-reading skill.

DOK 3 justification: students must use context strategically to distinguish between two types of meaning and explain how an author's choice produces a specific effect — this is reasoning with evidence, not recall or procedure.

STAAR relevance: this TEK is directly tested in author's craft questions asking why a word was chosen and what effect it produces. Nearly every diction-focused stem on STAAR depends on the distinction this TEK teaches.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Determine the Meaning of Foreign Words and Phrases Used in English
expectation: Determine the meaning of foreign words or phrases used frequently in English such as bona fide, caveat, carte blanche, tête-à-tête, bon appétit, and quid pro quo.
overview: English absorbs foreign vocabulary constantly, and a swath of the most common formal, legal, and literary language in circulation is Latin or French. Students who can't read "caveat" or "quid pro quo" in a news article or contract are blocked from texts they'll encounter outside school. This TEK builds the vocabulary floor for literate participation in professional and civic life.
studentFriendly: You can figure out what common foreign words and phrases — like "bona fide" or "carte blanche" — mean when they show up in news articles, legal documents, conversations, and everyday writing.
questionStems: Read this sentence from paragraph X: "[sentence containing foreign phrase]." The phrase [foreign phrase] MOST nearly means —
Based on the context in paragraph X, the author's use of "carte blanche" suggests that the character —
Which sentence from the selection BEST helps the reader determine the meaning of "caveat" in paragraph X?
In paragraph X, the narrator describes the agreement as "quid pro quo." This phrase MOST likely means the arrangement was —
The author uses the phrase "bona fide" in paragraph X to suggest that the claim is —
Read paragraph X. The word "caveat" as used in this context MOST nearly means —
Which detail from paragraphs X–Y BEST supports the meaning of "tête-à-tête" as used in the selection?
In paragraph X, the speaker is given "carte blanche." Based on context, this MOST likely means the speaker was —
The phrase "quid pro quo" in paragraph X contributes to the reader's understanding that —
Based on the information in paragraphs X–Y, what does the author MOST likely mean by describing the meeting as a "tête-à-tête"?

misconceptions.title: Foreign Phrases Are Just Decoration
misconceptions.body: Students assume that foreign phrases are flourishes — that the author could have used an English equivalent and the meaning would be identical. Teach them that these phrases often carry connotations the English near-synonym misses: "quid pro quo" implies a transactional formality that "trade-off" doesn't. The choice of register is the point.

misconceptions.title: Context Is Irrelevant When a Phrase Sounds Familiar
misconceptions.body: Students who half-recognize a phrase will guess its meaning from the sound rather than from the sentence around it. "Bon appétit" is well-known enough that students stop reading — but a satirical or ironic use can flip the phrase's function entirely. Push them to anchor every meaning determination in the specific context, not their prior exposure.

misconceptions.title: Latin = Fancy, French = Cuisine
misconceptions.body: Students lump Latin-origin phrases into "formal/legal" and French-origin phrases into "food and fashion," then stop thinking. This shortcut breaks immediately with "tête-à-tête" (an intimate private conversation) or "carte blanche" (unconditional authority). Watch for students applying domain stereotypes instead of reading the actual sentence.

misconceptions.title: You Either Know It or You Don't
misconceptions.body: Students treat foreign vocabulary as a memory quiz — either the phrase is in their head or it isn't, so they skip it. Teach them that context clues work on foreign phrases exactly the same way they work on unfamiliar English words: apposition, contrast, cause-and-effect, and surrounding detail all signal meaning. Knowing how to read around a phrase is the skill, not prior memorization.

misconceptions.title: Knowing the Meaning Means Knowing When to Use It
misconceptions.body: Students who learn "bona fide" often start deploying it incorrectly — applying it to situations where "genuine" or "legitimate" would work but the register is wrong. Separate the two skills: determining meaning in a text (the TEK) and producing the phrase appropriately (a different, later skill). Don't let premature production anxiety derail reading comprehension work.

assignments.name: Phrase in the Wild
assignments.body: Students find one of the six TEK-named phrases (or another approved foreign phrase) used in a real news article, legal document, or published book within a week. They annotate the passage, identify which context clues helped them confirm the meaning, and write two sentences explaining the phrase's function in that specific text.

assignments.name: Swap and Lose
assignments.body: Students take a paragraph that contains a foreign phrase and rewrite it replacing the phrase with the closest English equivalent. They then write a short explanation of what — if anything — the English version loses. Discussion follows on why authors reach for the foreign phrase.

assignments.name: Context Clue Deconstruction
assignments.body: Teacher presents five sentences each containing one of the target phrases with the phrase blanked out. Students write what they predict the blank says and why, using only the surrounding text as evidence. After revealing the phrases, discuss which context clue types were most reliable.

mastery: Uses context clues — apposition, contrast, surrounding detail — to determine the meaning of an unfamiliar foreign phrase without relying on prior memorization alone.
Explains not just what a foreign phrase means but what function it serves in the specific passage, including any connotational weight the phrase carries over its English near-equivalent.
Distinguishes between phrases that are used literally and phrases that are used ironically or figuratively, adjusting meaning accordingly.
Identifies and names the context evidence that supports their meaning determination when asked to justify a reading.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I is the entry point for this standard — no prior course version exists. English II (110.37(2)(C)) continues the skill, typically with a broader or more challenging set of phrases and a higher expectation for independent determination without scaffolded context. The English I focus is on building a reliable process (context clues + register awareness) rather than on memorizing a fixed phrase list.

DOK 2 justification: the core move is interpretation — applying a process to determine meaning from context. It rises above recall (DOK 1) because students must read contextually and explain their reasoning, but it does not yet require the extended analysis of DOK 3.

The TEA examples (bona fide, caveat, carte blanche, tête-à-tête, bon appétit, quid pro quo) span Latin and French origin. Instruction should name this explicitly — students benefit from understanding that English has systematically borrowed from these source languages across legal, diplomatic, culinary, and social registers.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is tested through vocabulary-in-context items embedded in reading passages — students will not see a standalone "define this foreign phrase" prompt. The stems above reflect that embedded-context format.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: 110.36(3) — Self-Select Text and Read Independently
expectation: Self-select text and read independently for a sustained period of time.
overview: This standard is the infrastructure underneath everything else. If students don't read on their own, all the comprehension and analysis work lands on classroom texts alone — a losing strategy. The job here is building the habit and the stamina, not assigning a book and calling it independent reading.
studentFriendly: You choose what you read and stick with it long enough to actually get somewhere in the text — not just survive to the bell.
questionStems: Based on the passage, which detail MOST likely explains why the narrator continued reading beyond the first chapter?
Which sentence from the passage BEST supports the inference that the character found personal meaning in the text she selected?
The reader in paragraph X chose this book MOST likely because —
Which evidence from the passage BEST suggests that the character has developed stamina as an independent reader?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, what can the reader MOST likely conclude about how self-selection affects engagement?
The narrator's description of the reading experience in paragraph X suggests that —
Which detail from the passage MOST nearly captures the way the character's reading habit developed over time?
How does the character's choice of reading material in paragraph X contribute to her understanding of herself?
Which sentence from the passage BEST supports the conclusion that independent reading influenced the character's thinking?
Based on the passage, the reader MOST likely selected this text in order to —

misconceptions.title: Assigning Time Is the Same as Building Habit
misconceptions.body: Teachers set a 20-minute independent reading block and assume the habit is forming. Watch for students staring at a page, cycling through the first paragraph, or switching books every few days — sustained reading requires scaffolded entry, not just scheduled minutes. Teach them to track where they left off, set a micro-goal per session, and expect some days to feel harder than others.

misconceptions.title: Self-Selection Means Anything Goes Without Guidance
misconceptions.body: Giving students total free rein sounds like honoring autonomy, but students who lack strong reading identities will grab whatever is nearest and abandon it fastest. Reframe self-selection as a skill: teach students to read the first page, the back cover, and the table of contents before committing — that's how real readers choose books, not by cover art alone.

misconceptions.title: Independent Reading Is for Catching Up, Not Moving Forward
misconceptions.body: Many students have internalized the idea that independent reading is remedial — something you do when you're behind. This reputation poisons the block. Push against it directly: independent reading is what advanced readers actually do. Name the authors, journalists, and researchers who read independently every day as a professional practice.

misconceptions.title: Stamina Builds Automatically with Age
misconceptions.body: High school students are not automatically better sustained readers than middle schoolers. Stamina atrophies without practice, especially when screen-based reading replaces long-form text. Watch for students who can sprint through social media but lose focus at paragraph three of prose. Stamina is a muscle; this standard is where you build it, not assume it.

misconceptions.title: The Text Level Doesn't Matter as Long as They're Reading
misconceptions.body: Students who chronically select texts far below their reading level may be avoiding challenge, not building habit. Celebrate accessible choices and easy reads — but watch for students who never stretch. The goal is independent reading that moves the student forward, which sometimes means a gentle conversation about what feels hard and why.

assignments.name: Reading Identity Map
assignments.body: Students list every text they've voluntarily read in the past year — books, fan fiction, game guides, graphic novels, articles. From the list, they identify three patterns about themselves as readers and use those patterns to select their next independent reading book.

assignments.name: Stamina Log
assignments.body: Students track reading sessions for two weeks: start time, stop time, how far they got, and a one-sentence note on focus quality. At the end of the two weeks, they write a paragraph analyzing their own stamina — where it improved, where it broke down, and what conditions helped.

assignments.name: Book Talk, No Summary
assignments.body: After completing an independent reading book, students give a two-minute spoken pitch to a partner — not a plot summary, but a genuine case for why someone like their partner should or shouldn't read it. No notes allowed. Forces real engagement with the text.

mastery: Selects a text independently using deliberate criteria — genre preference, prior author experience, peer recommendation — not proximity or cover appeal alone.
Sustains reading across multiple sessions without losing place or abandoning the text before reaching a meaningful stopping point.
Can name what they are currently reading and describe where they are in it without prompting.
Adjusts text selection when a book isn't working — not impulsively, but after a fair attempt — and can articulate why the switch was warranted.
Reads during the independent reading block with visible engagement, not performance of engagement.
notes: Vertical alignment: Standard 3 carries the same language across all four courses — self-select and sustain. The ramp is internal to the student, not in the text of the standard. English I is where the habit either gets established or doesn't; everything downstream assumes it exists.
No prior course standard: this is the entry point (English I). verticalPrev is listed as none.

DOK 2 justification: the cognitive work is application — selecting appropriate text and executing sustained engagement over time. The standard does not call for analysis or evaluation of the reading experience itself, though related standards do.

STAAR analog note: Standard 3 is not directly tested on STAAR as a discrete skill. The question stems above mirror how STAAR tests reading engagement and text-selection reasoning when those themes appear in fiction or informational passages. Note this in instruction — the standard's value is formative and longitudinal, not summative.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Establish Purpose for Reading Assigned and Self-Selected Texts
expectation: Establish purpose for reading assigned and self-selected texts.
overview: Purpose-setting is the first cognitive move a reader makes — and it determines what they notice, retain, and do with a text. English I is where students learn that reading is not passive: you go into a text with a question, a frame, or a job, and that orientation shapes everything that follows.
studentFriendly: Before you read anything — whether your teacher assigned it or you picked it up yourself — you decide what you're reading FOR, and that decision changes what you find.
questionStems: Read the introduction to the selection. Based on this information, a reader would MOST likely read this text in order to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST explains the author's reason for writing this text?
A student reading this article for a research project would MOST likely focus on —
Based on paragraphs 1–3, the reader can conclude that the PRIMARY purpose of this selection is to —
Which detail from paragraph X BEST supports the idea that this text is meant to be read as an argument rather than as a narrative?
The introduction in paragraphs 1–2 MOST likely prepares the reader to —
A reader approaching this selection to understand a character's motivation would MOST likely pay closest attention to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST indicates that the author intends the reader to question an assumption?
Based on the title and first paragraph, what can the reader infer about the appropriate purpose for reading this text?
How does establishing a purpose for reading affect what a reader would MOST likely notice in paragraph X?

misconceptions.title: "I Just Read It" Is a Complete Answer
misconceptions.body: Students treat purpose-setting as a box to check — they write "to understand the story" and move on. That's not a purpose; that's a genre description. Teach them that a real purpose is specific and directional: "I'm reading to figure out why the narrator keeps lying to her mother" or "I'm reading to find out whether the author's evidence actually supports the claim." Generic purposes produce generic comprehension.

misconceptions.title: Assigned Texts Don't Need a Purpose Because the Teacher Already Has One
misconceptions.body: When a teacher assigns a text, students assume the teacher's purpose IS their purpose, so they stop there. Push them to generate a personal purpose alongside the assigned one — "My teacher wants me to analyze the rhetoric; I want to understand why this speech made people cry." Dual purposes deepen engagement and make the assigned reading feel like something they're actually doing, not receiving.

misconceptions.title: Purpose Can't Change Once You Start Reading
misconceptions.body: Students set a purpose at the top and treat it as a contract. In reality, a good reader monitors and revises — if the text turns out to be something other than expected, the purpose shifts. Watch for students who are confused by a text but won't reframe: they're locked into an initial purpose that stopped fitting three paragraphs ago. Teach them that revising purpose mid-read is a sign of comprehension, not failure.

misconceptions.title: Self-Selected Reading Doesn't Need a Purpose Because It's "Just for Fun"
misconceptions.body: Students code pleasure reading as purposeless and therefore separate from the skill. The move here is to name that entertainment IS a valid purpose — and then to show how even that purpose shapes what they notice, what they skip, and what they reread. Once they see that, purpose-setting becomes legible in all reading contexts, not just school ones.

misconceptions.title: The Purpose Has to Match the Text's Genre
misconceptions.body: Students assume that fiction is read for story, nonfiction for information, and that's the end of it. Break this open: fiction can be read to study an argument, nonfiction can be read to study voice, a poem can be read to track a philosophical claim. Teach them that purpose is the reader's choice, not the text's prescription — and that mismatched purposes produce some of the most interesting reading.

assignments.name: Before/After Purpose Log
assignments.body: Students write a one-sentence reading purpose before opening a text, then return to it at the end and write whether the purpose held, shifted, or needed to be abandoned — and why. Repeat across five texts in a unit to build a log of their purpose-setting patterns.

assignments.name: Purpose Gallery Walk
assignments.body: Post five different texts (a news article, a poem, a novel excerpt, an op-ed, a graph with caption) around the room. Students rotate and write a different reading purpose for each — purposes must be specific and tied to something visible in the text itself. Debrief: did the same text invite different valid purposes from different students?

assignments.name: Assigned vs. Owned
assignments.body: For a shared class text, students write two purposes: the one they infer the assignment wants and a personal one they bring themselves. Both purposes must generate at least two different annotations during reading. Debrief: where did the two purposes lead to different noticing?

mastery: Articulates a specific, directional reading purpose before beginning a text — not a genre label, but a question or frame.
Adjusts or revises purpose during reading when the text doesn't match initial expectations, and can name when and why the shift happened.
Generates distinct purposes for different texts and different reading tasks, rather than applying a default "understand the story" frame to everything.
Identifies in assigned and self-selected texts the features — title, opening paragraph, genre signals — that informed the initial purpose.
Connects purpose to comprehension outcomes: can explain how a different purpose would have produced different noticing in the same text.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I is the entry point for this standard — students are establishing what it means to read with intention rather than passivity. English II (110.37(4)(A)) builds on this by adding monitoring and adjusting comprehension strategies as the text demands. The English I version is foundational: purpose first, before strategy.

DOK 2 justification: the skill requires interpretation of text signals and deliberate cognitive framing before and during reading — beyond simple recall, but not yet the extended evaluation or synthesis of DOK 3/4.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is rarely assessed in isolation on STAAR; it surfaces most clearly in questions about author's purpose and reader inference from text features. Stems above mirror how STAAR operationalizes purpose-setting through author-intent and reader-focus questions.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Generate Questions About Text Before, During, and After Reading
expectation: Generate questions about text before, during, and after reading to deepen understanding and gain information.
overview: This TEK is about making sure students are doing something with a text before they hit it cold — and that they keep working on it past the last line. The real skill is learning to hold a question open long enough for the text to answer it, not just asking and forgetting.
studentFriendly: You know how to read actively — asking questions before you start, staying curious while you're in the middle of it, and pressing the text for answers even after you finish.
questionStems: Which question would be MOST helpful to ask before reading this selection to prepare for understanding its main argument?
Based on paragraphs 1–3, which question is MOST directly answered by the information in the selection?
Which question raised in paragraph X is BEST answered by evidence in paragraph Y?
A student reading this selection asks, "Why does the author shift focus in paragraph X?" Which detail from the text BEST addresses that question?
After reading the selection, which question MOST likely requires the reader to draw on information from multiple paragraphs?
Which question could a reader generate from the title and first paragraph BEFORE reading the full text?
Based on the selection, which question would require a reader to go BEYOND the text to answer fully?
Which sentence from the selection MOST directly answers the question implied by the heading in paragraph X?
The reader wonders during paragraph X why the author includes [detail]. Which sentence BEST explains the author's purpose in including it?
After reading paragraphs X–Y, a reader asks, "What does this suggest about [topic]?" Which inference from the text BEST answers that question?

misconceptions.title: Questions Are for Before Reading, Not During or After
misconceptions.body: Students treat questioning as a pre-reading ritual — they write two questions on a sticky note, then forget about them the moment they open the text. Teach them that the question phase never closes: a good reader is continuously revising questions as new information arrives. Watch for students who can generate strong before-reading questions but go silent once the reading starts.

misconceptions.title: Asking a Question Counts as Understanding
misconceptions.body: Students conflate generating a question with demonstrating comprehension. They'll write "Why does the author do this?" without anchoring it to any specific moment in the text. Push them to name the paragraph, name the detail, and explain what confusion or curiosity produced the question. A question with no address is not a reading question — it's a stall.

misconceptions.title: Good Questions Have Obvious Answers
misconceptions.body: Students gravitate toward questions the first paragraph answers in one sentence because it feels safe. The richer questions are the ones that stay open across multiple sections, that make the reader hold tension. Teach them to notice when a text refuses to answer a question cleanly — that refusal is itself information.

misconceptions.title: After-Reading Questions Are Just Comprehension Checks
misconceptions.body: Students think after-reading questions are summaries dressed up as interrogatives ("What happened in the story?"). Real after-reading questioning pushes toward implication and gap: What didn't the author explain? What claim here needs more support? Where did the text's logic break down? Show them that after-reading questions should be harder than during-reading ones, not easier.

misconceptions.title: Questioning Is a Separate Step, Not a Habit
misconceptions.body: Students treat question generation as a graphic organizer to fill out, not a metacognitive practice that runs underneath all reading. Watch for students who produce excellent written questions on assigned protocols but read passively when the protocol isn't in front of them. The target behavior is internal — they should be questioning even when no one is watching.

assignments.name: Three-Phase Question Log
assignments.body: Students divide a reading journal page into three columns: Before, During, After. They must generate at least three questions per phase as they read a short nonfiction article. After reading, they annotate each question: answered, partially answered, or still open — and explain what they'd need to find out.

assignments.name: Question Ladder
assignments.body: Students read a passage in pairs. After each paragraph, they generate one question. At the end, they arrange all questions from "answered by the text" to "requires outside information" and defend their ranking with textual evidence.

assignments.name: Unanswered Questions Debrief
assignments.body: After finishing any full reading, students write their single most important unanswered question and a one-paragraph explanation of why the text doesn't fully resolve it. Peer groups compare questions to find patterns.

mastery: Generates specific, text-anchored questions before reading — drawn from title, structure, or prior knowledge — rather than generic ones.
Sustains questioning across the full reading, adjusting or discarding earlier questions as new information emerges.
Identifies which questions the text answers directly, which it answers partially, and which it leaves open — and can explain the difference using textual evidence.
Produces after-reading questions that require synthesis across the full text, not just recall of a single section.
Demonstrates the questioning habit without a graphic organizer prompt — applies it independently during timed or independent reading tasks.
notes: No vertical predecessor in English I — this is the foundational entry point for structured comprehension questioning in the high school sequence. English II (110.37(4)(B)) carries the same expectation but in a context of increased text complexity and genre variety; by English III and IV, the questioning skill is embedded in research and rhetorical analysis rather than named separately.

DOK 3 justification: the expectation is not recall (DOK 1) or skill application to a single text moment (DOK 2) — generating questions that deepen understanding requires strategic thinking across a text's structure and meaning, and evaluating which questions the text does or does not resolve requires reasoning about evidence.

This TEK has a limited direct STAAR analog since question generation is a process behavior, not a discrete textual product. Question stems above simulate how STAAR tests the underlying comprehension moves — inference, evidence evaluation, purpose identification — that effective questioning produces. Note this in instructional planning: the stems reveal what STAAR measures about comprehension depth, even when it does not ask students to "generate a question" explicitly.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Make and Correct or Confirm Predictions
expectation: Make and correct or confirm predictions using text features, characteristics of genre, and structures.
overview: Prediction is not guessing — it is disciplined inference drawn from visible textual evidence. The real skill here is the back half: students must return to their predictions, name what confirmed or contradicted them, and revise their thinking accordingly. This is where comprehension monitoring lives.
studentFriendly: You can use a text's headings, genre signals, and structural patterns to make a grounded prediction before or during reading — then check yourself as you go, adjusting when the text surprises you.
questionStems: Based on the heading in paragraph X, the reader can MOST likely predict that the passage will —
Which detail from the selection BEST supports the prediction that —
A reader who predicted [X] after reading paragraph Y would MOST likely revise that prediction after reading paragraph Z because —
The structure of this passage MOST likely signals that the author will next —
Which text feature BEST helps the reader predict the organization of the selection?
Based on the genre characteristics of this selection, a reader could MOST likely predict that —
Which sentence from the passage BEST confirms the prediction made in paragraph X?
After reading paragraphs X–Y, a reader's prediction about [topic] would MOST likely change because —
The subheadings in this selection MOST likely help the reader predict —
Which detail from paragraph X would MOST likely lead a reader to predict that —

misconceptions.title: A Prediction Is Only Wrong If the Student Felt Confident
misconceptions.body: Students treat predictions as bets — they hedge early ("I think maybe...") so they never have to own a wrong one. Teach them that prediction is a commitment: write it down, cite the evidence, and then face the text honestly. The whole point of "correct or confirm" is that being wrong is not a failure; it is the mechanism through which comprehension deepens.

misconceptions.title: Text Features Are Just for Skimming
misconceptions.body: Students see headings, bold text, and sidebars as shortcuts for when they don't want to read carefully. Flip this: text features are the architecture of the text's argument or narrative. A heading is a promise. A sidebar is a signal of priority. Teach them to treat text features as predictive evidence, not decoration.

misconceptions.title: Confirming a Prediction Means the Text Said Exactly What You Expected
misconceptions.body: Students confirm too easily — any vague overlap between their prediction and what happened counts as a win. Push them to be precise: did the text confirm the substance of the prediction, or just the topic? "I predicted there would be conflict" confirmed by any tension is not the same as a specific, textually grounded prediction confirmed by specific evidence.

misconceptions.title: Genre Knowledge Is About Labels
misconceptions.body: Students can name genres without using genre knowledge to predict. Knowing something is a mystery is useless if they can't say "so I should expect unreliable information early and a reveal that reframes earlier scenes." Teach them to convert genre characteristics into actual expectations about what the text will do next.

misconceptions.title: Once a Prediction Is Wrong, It Gets Discarded
misconceptions.body: Students cross out wrong predictions and move on. The correction is the comprehension. Teach them to interrogate the gap: what evidence misled them? What did the text actually signal that they misread? A corrected prediction, examined carefully, reveals more about how the text works than a confirmed one does.

assignments.name: Prediction Ledger
assignments.body: Students fold a page into three columns — Prediction, Evidence (what I used), Result (confirmed/corrected/still open). They update the ledger at set stopping points during reading. At the end, they annotate the "Result" column with a sentence explaining why each prediction landed or failed.

assignments.name: Feature Walk, Then Read
assignments.body: Before reading a nonfiction selection, students spend three minutes cataloging every text feature (headings, captions, pull quotes, graphics) and writing one prediction per feature. After reading, they score each prediction and identify which feature types gave the most reliable signals.

assignments.name: Genre Prediction Protocol
assignments.body: Give students the genre and first paragraph of three different texts without revealing what happens. They write what they predict the structure, ending, and tone will be based on genre conventions alone. Debrief: whose predictions were closest, and what genre knowledge drove it?

assignments.name: Correction Spotlight
assignments.body: Students identify the moment in the text where a prediction they held got definitively contradicted. They write a paragraph explaining what textual signal they misread, what the correct reading was, and how the correction changed their understanding of the whole.

mastery: Makes predictions grounded in a specific text feature, genre characteristic, or structural signal — not in prior knowledge or general logic alone.
Returns to predictions at natural reading checkpoints without being prompted to do so.
Articulates the precise reason a prediction was confirmed or corrected, citing specific evidence from the text.
Adjusts subsequent predictions based on what the correction revealed about the text's logic or genre.
Distinguishes between a prediction that was wrong and one that is still open — and knows when the text has not yet delivered enough information to confirm or correct.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I establishes prediction as evidence-based inference from text features, genre, and structure. English II extends this into more complex structural and rhetorical analysis — the same prediction loop applied to texts with subtler signals and less explicit genre markers.

DOK 2 justification: the base skill (make a prediction from text features) is DOK 1; correction and confirmation require students to compare their prediction against textual evidence and explain the discrepancy or alignment, pushing the standard to DOK 2. Strong classroom implementation can push into DOK 3 when students synthesize across multiple text features to construct and revise predictions.

No direct STAAR analog as a standalone tested behavior — prediction appears embedded in inference and synthesis questions. Question stems mirror the inferential vocabulary STAAR uses when testing comprehension monitoring.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Create Mental Images to Deepen Understanding
expectation: Create mental images to deepen understanding.
overview: Visualization is not a decorative habit — it is a comprehension strategy. When readers build sensory images from text, they slow down enough to process the language at the level of detail the writer intended. For English I students, the goal is making the habit automatic and purposeful, not just decorative or passive.
studentFriendly: You can read a passage and construct a detailed picture, sound, or feeling in your mind that helps you hold onto meaning the way a simple summary never could.
questionStems: Which detail from paragraph X BEST helps the reader visualize the setting described?
The author's description in paragraph X MOST likely creates an image of —
Which sentence from the selection MOST clearly appeals to the reader's sense of [sight/sound/touch]?
Based on the details in paragraphs X–Y, the reader can MOST likely picture the character as —
The phrase "[phrase]" in paragraph X contributes MOST to the reader's mental image by —
Which word from paragraph X BEST helps the reader imagine the [setting/character/event]?
The author includes the details in paragraph X MOST likely to help the reader —
Based on the description in paragraph X, where does this scene MOST likely take place?
Which sentence from the selection contains the MOST vivid sensory detail?
The imagery in paragraphs X–Y develops the mood of the passage by —

misconceptions.title: Visualization Means Seeing a Movie, Not Reading a Text
misconceptions.body: Students treat mental imaging as passive — they wait for a picture to appear rather than constructing one from the language. Teach them that visualization is an act of reading, not a reward for it. Push them to identify the specific words that built the image: if they can't point to the text, they weren't visualizing — they were daydreaming.

misconceptions.title: Only Descriptive Passages Can Be Visualized
misconceptions.body: Students skip the imaging work in argumentative or expository texts, assuming those passages don't "have" images. Watch for this in informational reading especially. A well-written explanation of a process or a cause-effect relationship can and should generate a mental map or sequence — visualization includes spatial and conceptual images, not just sensory ones.

misconceptions.title: More Detail Always Equals Better Visualization
misconceptions.body: Students assume that longer, more ornate descriptions are always the ones worth visualizing, and they glide past spare, precise language. Some of the most powerful imagery is minimal — a single verb, an unexpected comparison. Teach them to slow down at compressed language, not just long description.

misconceptions.title: Visualization Is a Beginning-of-the-Book Skill
misconceptions.body: Once students get into a text, they stop actively building images and just track plot. The strategy doesn't retire after the first chapter. Watch for comprehension breakdown mid-text that traces back to abandoned visualization — students who could describe the opening scene but have no picture of what's happening twenty pages later.

misconceptions.title: A Good Visualization Has to Be Accurate to the "Right" Picture
misconceptions.body: Students who struggle to visualize will sometimes refuse to try because they're afraid their image is wrong. Reframe this: visualization is tested against the text, not against a correct answer key. If they can cite the details that built their image, the image is doing its job. The error is an image that contradicts the text, not one that goes beyond it.

assignments.name: Freeze Frame
assignments.body: Students read a rich descriptive passage and pause at a designated moment to sketch — not artistically, just functionally — the scene as they picture it. They then annotate the sketch with the specific words from the text that drove each element of the image. Discussion follows: where did readers diverge, and what textual difference explains it?

assignments.name: Sensory Log
