assignments.body: Students read a full short story or chapter and keep a running log of which sentences triggered a sensory image, what sense it activated, and how the image affected their understanding of the scene. At the end, they identify the one image that mattered most to their comprehension — and defend the choice with evidence.

assignments.name: Sparse vs. Dense
assignments.body: Students compare two passages: one with dense sensory description, one with minimal but precise language. They visualize both and write a paragraph explaining which passage generated a stronger mental image and why the language drove that result.

mastery: Identifies the specific words and phrases that generated a mental image and explains the connection between language and image.
Applies visualization across text types — narrative, expository, and argumentative — not only in obviously descriptive passages.
Uses a constructed mental image to explain meaning, answer a comprehension question, or support an inference, rather than treating the image as an end in itself.
Recognizes when a mental image is contradicted by later text and adjusts it, demonstrating active rather than passive visualization.
Distinguishes between images grounded in textual evidence and images imported from personal experience without textual support.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I introduces visualization as a named comprehension strategy; English II (110.37(4)(D)) expects students to apply it with greater independence across a wider range of text types and complexity levels, including more sustained analytical writing about how imagery functions.

DOK 2 justification: the core skill is application of a strategy within a text — identifying what the language produces in a reader's mind. Assessment items typically ask students to identify or explain what a detail contributes to the reader's experience, which is a skill-application task rather than extended analysis.

STAAR analog note: this TEK does not have a dedicated STAAR reporting category, but it underpins multiple tested skills — particularly author's craft (how imagery builds mood/tone), inference, and literary analysis. Stems above mirror how STAAR tests the sensory-detail dimension of literary comprehension.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Make Connections to Personal Experience, Other Texts, and Society
expectation: Make connections to personal experiences, ideas in other texts, and society.
overview: Connection-making is the bridge between reading and thinking — the move that converts a text into something a student can actually use. In English I, the task is not surface-level identification ("this reminds me of...") but substantive linking: students must explain how the connection extends, complicates, or deepens meaning in the text they're reading.
studentFriendly: You can read a text and explain how it connects to something in your own life, to an idea you've read somewhere else, or to something happening in the world — and use that connection to understand the text more deeply.
questionStems: Which statement BEST describes how the narrator's experience in paragraph X connects to a broader social issue addressed in the selection?
Based on the selection, the author MOST likely draws on the idea of [concept] to —
A student reading this selection makes the following connection: [connection]. Which evidence from the text BEST supports this connection?
The ideas in paragraph X are MOST similar to which of the following situations in real life?
Which sentence from the selection MOST clearly reflects a theme that connects to current social debates about —
How does the conflict in paragraphs X–Y relate to the central idea developed later in the selection?
Which statement BEST explains how a reader's personal experience with [topic] would contribute to understanding the narrator's decision in paragraph X?
The author's portrayal of [character/situation] in paragraph X MOST nearly reflects which pattern found in other texts about —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which inference about the connection between the text and real-world society is BEST supported?
Which detail from the selection MOST clearly connects the narrator's personal struggle to a universal human experience?

misconceptions.title: "This Reminds Me Of" Is a Connection
misconceptions.body: Students write sentences like "This reminds me of when I went to the beach" and consider the task complete. That is not a connection — that is an association. Teach them the difference: a connection has two directions. It must explain what the personal experience, other text, or social reality reveals about the text being read. No analysis of the text? No credit.

misconceptions.title: Text-to-Self Always Means Sharing Personal Trauma
misconceptions.body: When students hear "connect to personal experience," they either shut down (my life isn't relevant here) or over-share (here's everything). Neither is the target. Reframe it: a personal connection can be as small as recognizing a dynamic, a feeling, or a decision-pattern. The size of the experience is irrelevant; the precision of the connection is everything.

misconceptions.title: Text-to-World Connections Are Just Current Events Name-Drops
misconceptions.body: Students will mention a news event or a social issue without explaining how the connection operates. "This is like racism" is not an analysis. Push them: in what specific way does this text's portrayal of [X] operate the same as — or differently from — the real-world issue? What does the text add to your understanding of the issue, or the issue add to your reading of the text?

misconceptions.title: Text-to-Text Connections Require Reading the Same Genre
misconceptions.body: Students believe they can only connect novels to novels and essays to essays. Break this open. A poem can complicate a nonfiction article. A speech can reframe a short story. The more unexpected the pairing, the richer the analysis — because the student must explain WHY the connection holds despite surface differences.

misconceptions.title: The Connection Is About the Student, Not the Text
misconceptions.body: Students write connection paragraphs where the text disappears entirely after the first sentence. The connection becomes an essay about themselves, another book, or a news story. Watch for this: if a student's connection paragraph could exist without ever having read the assigned text, the paragraph needs to be rebuilt. The text must stay in the center.

assignments.name: Three-Lens Journal
assignments.body: After reading a selection, students write three short paragraphs — one text-to-self, one text-to-text, one text-to-world — each ending with a sentence that explains what the connection reveals about the text's meaning. The payoff sentence is graded most heavily.

assignments.name: Connection Wall, Then Cull
assignments.body: Students generate as many connections as they can in five minutes (sticky notes, rapid-fire). Then they cull: which three connections actually change how they read the text? They discard the rest and write up only the ones that do real analytical work.

assignments.name: Unlikely Pairing
assignments.body: Assign a text-to-text connection between two texts from different genres or time periods. Students write a structured paragraph: what they have in common, how they differ, and what the pairing reveals about the original text that reading it alone wouldn't.

assignments.name: Connection Debate
assignments.body: Two students defend different connections to the same text. They argue which connection is more illuminating — not which is more true — using evidence from the text. Class votes and explains their reasoning.

mastery: Identifies a specific connection — personal, textual, or social — and names it precisely rather than gesturing at a general topic.
Explains the connection in both directions: how the text informs the connection AND how the connection deepens understanding of the text.
Uses textual evidence to anchor the connection, keeping the original text central to the analysis.
Moves beyond surface similarity ("they're both about loss") to examine structural or thematic parallels that complicate or extend meaning.
Connects across genres, time periods, and contexts without requiring obvious surface similarity to justify the link.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I establishes the three-lens connection framework (self, text, world). English II (110.37(4)(E)) extends this by requiring students to analyze how those connections deepen understanding across multiple texts — the move from making connections to evaluating their analytical payoff.
DOK 3 justification: students must go beyond locating a connection to explaining its interpretive function — strategic thinking that requires applying the connection back to the text's meaning.

STAAR testing note: this TEK is tested indirectly — stems about theme, inference, and author's purpose often reward students who can draw on background knowledge and social context. Direct "connection" prompts appear on short-answer or essay items rather than multiple choice. Stems above mirror how STAAR would frame the underlying thinking.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Make Inferences and Use Evidence to Support Understanding
expectation: Make inferences and use evidence to support understanding.
overview: This is the backbone of comprehension — students move beyond what the text says to what it means, and they anchor that move in specific textual evidence. In English I, the emphasis is on building the habit: students who can't yet name what an inference IS need to see what distinguishes one from a summary, a guess, or a personal opinion.
studentFriendly: You can read between the lines of a text — figuring out what the author implies but doesn't say directly — and point to specific words or sentences that back up your thinking.
questionStems: Based on paragraph X, the reader can infer that —
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the inference that the narrator feels —
The details in paragraphs X and Y MOST likely suggest that —
What conclusion about [character/subject] is BEST supported by the evidence in the selection?
Which sentence from paragraph X BEST supports the idea that —
The reader can conclude from the information in paragraph X that —
Which piece of evidence from the selection MOST strongly supports the inference that —
Based on the details in paragraphs X–Y, what is the MOST likely reason that —
Which statement is BEST supported by evidence from the selection?
The evidence in paragraph X MOST nearly suggests that the author believes —

misconceptions.title: An Inference Is a Fancy Guess
misconceptions.body: Students treat inference as permission to say whatever they feel about the text, without evidence. They're not wrong that an inference goes beyond what's stated — but they skip the second move entirely: anchoring the inference in specific textual evidence. Teach them that an inference without evidence is just a guess, and a guess isn't analysis. Require them to name the sentence or detail before they name the inference.

misconceptions.title: Evidence Is Whatever You Liked About the Text
misconceptions.body: When asked to cite evidence, students often reach for dramatic moments or lines they personally found interesting rather than lines that actually support the inference at hand. Watch for citations that are tangentially related — emotionally satisfying but logically loose. Reframe evidence as a reason: "This line proves my inference because..." If the "because" doesn't hold, the evidence doesn't hold.

misconceptions.title: Summarizing IS Inferring
misconceptions.body: Students conflate inference with retelling. They'll paraphrase a plot event and call it an inference. Show them the distinction with a direct example: "The character slams the door" is summary; "The character is trying to control her anger by leaving before she says something she'll regret" is inference. The inference names something the text implies but does not state. Drill the gap between those two moves until students can tell them apart on instinct.

misconceptions.title: One Good Quote Is Enough
misconceptions.body: Students find one line of evidence and stop. They treat the first supporting detail as the end of the job. Push them toward corroboration — two pieces of evidence that converge on the same inference strengthen the claim; one piece leaves room for a competing reading. Teach them that stronger readers look for the pattern, not just the moment.

misconceptions.title: The Author's Meaning and the Reader's Opinion Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students will state a personal reaction ("I think this character is selfish") and treat it as textual inference. The question isn't what you think about the character — it's what the text implies about the character, using the evidence available. Teach them to test every "inference" against the question: "Could a reader who felt differently still reach this conclusion from the same evidence?" If yes, it's an inference. If no, it's an opinion.

assignments.name: Two-Column Evidence Log
assignments.body: As students read, they maintain two columns: "What the Text Says" and "What I Can Infer." At the end of each section or chapter, they write one sentence explaining how their inference follows from the textual evidence. This makes the invisible reasoning process visible and repeatable.

assignments.name: Inference Ladder
assignments.body: Students start with a base detail from the text and write a chain of three connected inferences, each one moving a step further from the literal text. They then evaluate which rung on the ladder is still well-supported by evidence and which one has slipped into speculation. Debrief as a class — where does inference become overreach?

assignments.name: Competing Readings
assignments.body: Pairs of students each argue for a different inference about the same character or event, both using evidence from the text. They must acknowledge their partner's best piece of evidence and explain why their inference still holds. Grades on the strength of the evidence, not on who "wins."

assignments.name: Evidence Audit
assignments.body: After a written response, students highlight every piece of evidence they cited and draw a line connecting it to the inference it supports. Any evidence without a clear connection gets cut. Any inference without connected evidence gets flagged. Students revise accordingly.

mastery: Distinguishes between what the text states directly and what it implies, and names the inference without collapsing that gap.
Selects textual evidence that logically connects to a stated inference — not just a memorable or adjacent line.
Uses multiple pieces of evidence to corroborate an inference rather than relying on a single detail.
Tests their own inferences against the question: "Could another reader with the same evidence reach the same conclusion?"
Revises or abandons an inference when new evidence from the text contradicts it.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I 4F establishes the foundation — making and supporting inferences is the core move. English II and beyond build on this by adding complexity of text type and the expectation that students evaluate competing inferences. English I students need volume of practice more than increased text complexity at this stage.

DOK 3 justification: moving from literal comprehension to inference requires reasoning across the text, not just retrieval; supporting an inference with evidence requires strategic thinking about why a detail connects to a claim.

This TEK underpins nearly every reading standard in the course. If students cannot infer, they cannot analyze author's craft, evaluate argument, or synthesize across sources — treat it as a gateway skill, not a discreet unit.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Evaluate Details to Determine Key Ideas
expectation: Evaluate details read to determine key ideas.
overview: This is the pivot from "what does it say?" to "what does it mean?" Students must do more than locate details — they must weigh them, decide which ones carry the most weight, and construct what the text is actually about. It's the move that separates surface readers from thinkers.
studentFriendly: You can read a text, sort through all its details, and figure out which ones actually matter — and from those, land on what the text is really saying.
questionStems: Which detail from the selection BEST supports the key idea that —
Based on the details in paragraphs X–Y, what is the MOST likely central idea of the selection?
Which sentence from paragraph X BEST develops the author's key idea about —
The detail in paragraph X MOST likely contributes to the key idea by —
Which of the following details is LEAST relevant to the key idea expressed in the selection?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, what conclusion about [topic] is BEST supported by the details?
The author includes the detail about [specific detail] in paragraph X MOST likely to —
Which statement BEST summarizes the key idea developed across paragraphs X–Y?
The details in paragraph X suggest that —
Which two details from the selection MOST strongly support the inference that —

misconceptions.title: Every Detail Is a Key Idea
misconceptions.body: Students list everything the text mentions and call all of it equally important. The skill is discriminating — not cataloguing. Teach them that a key idea is something the text keeps returning to, builds evidence for, or structures itself around. A detail is a brick; the key idea is the building. Make them identify the architecture, not inventory the materials.

misconceptions.title: The First or Last Sentence Is Always the Key Idea
misconceptions.body: Students gravitate toward topic sentences and concluding lines as automatic homes for key ideas. That's a reasonable heuristic that becomes a crutch. Watch for students who grab the opening sentence, mark it, and stop reading. Push them to ask: does the rest of the text actually support this, or does the real argument live somewhere in the middle?

misconceptions.title: Supporting Details and Key Ideas Are Interchangeable
misconceptions.body: Students conflate the claim with its evidence. They'll name a specific example — a statistic, a quote, an anecdote — and call it the key idea. Teach them the hierarchy explicitly: details are in service of something larger. Ask them to name what the detail is proving, and that answer is closer to the key idea.

misconceptions.title: A Key Idea Must Be Explicitly Stated
misconceptions.body: Students who haven't been pushed toward inference assume the key idea is always a sentence they can underline. Many texts — literary and informational alike — build toward an implied central idea that no single sentence captures. Reframe this: if you can copy-paste the key idea directly, you're finding the topic, not the idea. The idea requires the reader to synthesize.

misconceptions.title: More Details Always Mean a Stronger Key Idea
misconceptions.body: When students write or evaluate arguments, they assume piling on details strengthens the central idea automatically. They don't evaluate whether the details are actually relevant or whether they're pointing in different directions. Teach them to read the detail bank as a whole: do these details converge, or do some of them pull against the key idea? That tension is where the real reading happens.

assignments.name: Detail Sorter
assignments.body: Give students a short text (400–600 words) and a three-column chart: "Key Idea Details," "Supporting Details," and "Irrelevant Details." Students sort every detail from the text into one column, then write one sentence naming the key idea the first column proves. Discussion follows on disagreements in sorting.

assignments.name: The Cut
assignments.body: Students read a 6–8 paragraph text, then must cut it to three paragraphs without losing the key idea. They must defend in writing which details they removed and why those were expendable. The defense is the assessment.

assignments.name: Two Texts, One Key Idea
assignments.body: Pair two short texts on the same topic — one that states its key idea explicitly, one that implies it. Students write a paragraph naming both key ideas and explaining which text made them work harder as a reader and why.

assignments.name: Key Idea Detectives
assignments.body: Students read a text cold, then write down what they believe the key idea is. They then re-read the text and highlight every detail that either supports or complicates their first answer. Final product: a revised key idea statement with an explanation of what changed and why.

mastery: Identifies a key idea that accounts for the text as a whole, not just one section.
Distinguishes between details that directly develop the key idea and details that are tangential or merely decorative.
Names specific textual details as evidence when articulating the key idea — doesn't state the key idea in a vacuum.
Recognizes when a key idea is implied rather than stated and constructs it through synthesis across the text.
Evaluates whether a given detail strengthens, complicates, or is irrelevant to the central idea rather than treating all details as equally weighted.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I introduces evaluation of details as a means to construct key ideas — this is a foundational comprehension move. English II (4G) builds on this toward more complex synthesis and source evaluation across texts.

DOK 3 justification: students are not retrieving or identifying details but evaluating their weight and function relative to an inferred or constructed central idea — strategic thinking requiring reasoning across the whole text.
STAAR analog note: this TEK maps directly to key-idea and central-idea question types on STAAR Reading; stems in this entry reflect standard STAAR item phrasing for those question types.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Synthesize Information from Two Texts to Create New Understanding
expectation: Synthesize information from two texts to create new understanding.
overview: Synthesis is the hardest move in reading comprehension — not just comparing what two texts say, but building something neither text says alone. English I students are often skilled at summarizing each text separately; the challenge is teaching them to let the texts talk to each other and arrive at an insight the reader constructs.
studentFriendly: You can read two texts on the same topic and combine what they're each saying to reach a conclusion or understanding that neither text spells out on its own.
questionStems: Based on both selections, which statement BEST represents a conclusion a reader could draw that neither text states directly?
The author of Selection 1 and the author of Selection 2 would MOST likely agree that —
Which idea from Selection 2 BEST develops or extends the argument made in paragraph X of Selection 1?
A reader who synthesizes both selections would MOST likely conclude that —
How does the information in Selection 2 change or deepen the reader's understanding of Selection 1?
Which statement BEST combines the central ideas of both selections into a single new understanding?
Based on both texts, what is the MOST likely reason [phenomenon/event/concept] occurs?
Which detail from Selection 1, combined with paragraph X of Selection 2, BEST supports the inference that —
With which statement would the authors of both selections MOST likely agree?
A reader uses both selections to write a report. Which claim could BEST be supported using evidence from both texts?

misconceptions.title: Synthesis Means Saying What Both Texts Said
misconceptions.body: Students write a paragraph about Text 1, write a paragraph about Text 2, and call that synthesis. Watch for the "first... then..." structure that signals side-by-side summary rather than true integration. Teach them: if you could remove one text from your synthesis and it still makes sense, you haven't synthesized — you've just summarized twice.

misconceptions.title: The New Understanding Has to Be Profound
misconceptions.body: Students freeze up trying to produce an insight worthy of a thesis defense. They already know how to build small inferences; synthesis is just stacking two of them. Push them to ask the practical question: "What do I now believe that I couldn't have believed from reading only one of these?" That's the bar — not brilliance, just additive reasoning.

misconceptions.title: Both Texts Have to Agree for Synthesis to Work
misconceptions.body: Students assume synthesis only happens when texts align. The richer synthesis often comes from tension — two texts that partially contradict each other, or that each explain only part of the picture. Teach them that disagreement between sources is an invitation, not a dead end: "Text 1 says X, Text 2 says Y — so what's actually going on?"

misconceptions.title: Quotation Is Synthesis
misconceptions.body: Students pull a quote from each text, place them next to each other, and consider the job done. The quotation is the raw material, not the product. Teach them that synthesis lives in the sentence they write AFTER the quotes — the one that explains what the two pieces mean together.

misconceptions.title: The Synthesized Idea Has to Come from the Texts Equally
misconceptions.body: Students think synthesis means weighting both texts 50/50. One text may supply most of the evidence while the other shifts the interpretation. Watch for students who force artificial balance by padding one source with weak support. The question is not "Did I use both?" but "Did I genuinely need both to reach this conclusion?"

assignments.name: The Third Text
assignments.body: Students read two short paired texts on the same topic. They write a "third text" — a paragraph that could not have been written without reading both. Assess whether their paragraph contains claims that require both sources to be true.

assignments.name: Synthesis Equation
assignments.body: Students complete a graphic organizer structured as: [Key idea from Text 1] + [Key idea from Text 2] = [New understanding]. They then turn the equation into a paragraph. The organizer makes the additive logic visible before students try to write it in prose.

assignments.name: Two-Source Claim Defense
assignments.body: Students are given a claim and must defend it using evidence from both texts simultaneously — not alternating between them, but weaving. Peer reviewers mark any sentence that relies on only one source and label it "summary, not synthesis."

assignments.name: The Disagreeing Texts
assignments.body: Pair two texts that partially contradict each other on the same topic. Students must write a synthesis that accounts for the tension — what can a reader conclude even though the texts don't fully align?

mastery: Produces a unified claim or conclusion that genuinely requires both texts — one that could not be reached from either text alone.
Integrates evidence from both texts within the same sentences or closely connected reasoning, rather than alternating between sources in separate sections.
Explains what the combination of both sources reveals — names the "so what" that emerges from putting the two together.
Handles both agreement and disagreement between sources, using tension as evidence rather than treating it as a problem.
Distinguishes between summary, comparison, and synthesis when asked to label their own or a peer's writing.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I introduces synthesis at the two-text level, which is the foundation. English II (110.37(4)(H)) extends this expectation — students synthesize across multiple sources rather than a fixed pair, increasing complexity and requiring more sophisticated source management.

DOK 3 justification: synthesizing information to construct new understanding requires strategic reasoning — students must build a claim that did not exist in either source, which goes beyond retrieval or skill application and into genuine knowledge construction. Borderline DOK 4 when the synthesis demands extended writing with original argument.

STAAR context: Synthesis appears on STAAR in the paired-passage section. Question stems typically ask students what "both" texts support, what conclusion "a reader" would draw from both, or which claim "could best be supported" using evidence from both. Coaching students on this language directly improves performance.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Monitor Comprehension and Make Adjustments
expectation: Monitor comprehension and make adjustments such as re-reading, using background knowledge, asking questions, and annotating when understanding breaks down.
overview: This TEK is about metacognition at the sentence level — students knowing when they've lost the thread and having a toolkit for recovering it. The work is teaching students to notice confusion instead of skating past it, then making deliberate moves rather than rereading the same line blank-eyed until the period comes.
studentFriendly: You know when you read a sentence, get to the end, and realize nothing stuck — this TEK is about what you do next, on purpose, instead of just hoping the next paragraph makes it clearer.
questionStems: Which strategy would BEST help a reader who does not understand the meaning of paragraph X?
A student is confused by the events described in paragraph X. Which action would MOST likely help the student clarify the sequence?
Based on paragraph X, what question would a reader MOST likely ask to monitor their own comprehension?
Which sentence from the selection BEST indicates that a reader should pause and re-read the previous paragraph?
A reader annotates paragraph X with the note "who is speaking here?" Which conclusion MOST likely led to that annotation?
Which strategy would MOST likely help a reader connect the ideas in paragraph X to information introduced earlier in the selection?
The reader's confusion in paragraph X could BEST be resolved by —
Which sentence from the selection contains a shift that would MOST likely require a careful reader to adjust their understanding?
Based on the selection, which inference would a reader using background knowledge MOST likely make about the events in paragraph X?
A reader does not understand the author's claim in paragraph X. Which adjustment would BEST support the reader's comprehension of that paragraph?

misconceptions.title: Re-reading Means Reading It Again the Same Way
misconceptions.body: Students believe that re-reading is just repetition — run your eyes over the same words a second time and hope something shakes loose. Teach them that re-reading is a strategy only if something changes: slower pace, smaller chunks, a specific question in mind. Watch for students who re-read a paragraph three times at the same speed and declare it hopeless. Push them to name what they're looking for on the second pass.

misconceptions.title: Confusion Is a Sign You're in the Wrong Class
misconceptions.body: Students who hit a comprehension wall often interpret it as evidence the text is too hard for them personally, rather than as a normal step in reading difficult material. Reframe confusion as data, not verdict — it tells them exactly where to direct their attention. Teach them that expert readers get confused; the difference is they don't stop there.

misconceptions.title: Annotation Is Decoration
misconceptions.body: When students annotate, they often underline the sentences they like or circle vocabulary words without doing anything with them. Real annotation is argument — a running conversation with the text. Teach them that an annotation should either name a question, flag a contradiction, make a connection, or record a prediction. Underlining a line because it sounds important is not annotation; it's highlighting in pencil.

misconceptions.title: Background Knowledge Means Personal Experience
misconceptions.body: Students think "using background knowledge" means dropping a personal anecdote onto the text — "this reminds me of when my cousin did something like that." The useful move is schema activation: what do I already know about this topic, this genre, this structure, this time period, that helps me predict what's happening here? Teach them to distinguish between connection-as-proof and connection-as-entry-point.

misconceptions.title: If You Don't Understand It Now, You'll Never Understand It
misconceptions.body: Students treat comprehension as binary — either they get it on the first read or they don't get it at all. Show them the difference between immediate confusion (which adjustments can fix) and accumulated confusion (which requires starting over at a larger unit). Some confusion resolves in the next sentence; some requires going back three pages. Teach them to diagnose before they decide they're stuck.

assignments.name: Confusion Log
assignments.body: Students read a complex passage in two columns: left column is the text, right column is a running log of every moment they notice confusion and the specific adjustment they made. Debrief focuses on which adjustments worked and why.

assignments.name: Annotate for a Reason
assignments.body: Give students a passage and assign each of four annotation types to a different color or symbol: question, contradiction, connection, prediction. After reading, students reflect on which annotation type appeared most and what that reveals about how they read.

assignments.name: Re-read Audit
assignments.body: Students re-read a paragraph they found difficult, but before the second read they must write down one specific thing they're trying to figure out. Compare first-read notes to second-read notes. Did naming the question change what they found?

assignments.name: What Did You Do When You Got Lost?
assignments.body: After a timed independent reading segment, students write a one-paragraph metacognitive reflection: where did understanding break down, what adjustment did they try, and did it work? This is collected, not graded for correctness — graded for honest engagement with the process.

mastery: Identifies the specific moment in a text where comprehension broke down, rather than saying "I didn't understand it" about the whole passage.
Selects an appropriate adjustment strategy based on the type of confusion — re-reading for syntax, background knowledge for context, questioning for purpose, annotation for accumulating meaning.
Produces annotations that function as a record of thinking — questions, contradictions, connections — not just underlining.
Demonstrates improved comprehension after applying an adjustment strategy, as evidenced by more accurate summary or inference.
Treats confusion as a reading event to address, not as a reason to disengage.
notes: This TEK has no direct STAAR analog as a tested metacognitive process — STAAR does not ask students to name what adjustment they would make as a freestanding skill. However, the comprehension monitoring skills here are the prerequisite for nearly every inference and synthesis item on the assessment. The stems above mirror how STAAR tests the products of this skill (inference, sequence, author's craft) in scenarios where a reader would need to apply an adjustment. This is noted per HANDOFF.md doctrine for TEKs without direct STAAR analogs.

Vertical ramp: English I establishes the monitoring repertoire (re-reading, background knowledge, questioning, annotating). English II and beyond are expected to apply these habits automatically to increasingly complex texts — the skill itself doesn't reappear as a named standard but underlies all comprehension work at higher levels.

DOK 3 justification: selecting and applying the appropriate adjustment strategy requires strategic thinking, not just recall or skill application — students must diagnose the type of confusion before choosing a response.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Describe Personal Connections to a Variety of Sources
expectation: Describe personal connections to a variety of sources, including self-selected texts.
overview: This standard launches the response strand by grounding students in their own experience as readers — not interpretation, not analysis, but connection. The move is to get students speaking and writing honestly about what a text activated in them, before the more formal demands of literary and rhetorical analysis kick in.
studentFriendly: You can read something — a novel, a news article, a song lyric, a video transcript — and explain, in real words, exactly what in your own life or thinking it lit up and why.
questionStems: Which sentence from the passage MOST likely describes an experience the narrator shares with the reader?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, what personal truth does the speaker MOST likely expect the audience to recognize in their own lives?
The narrator's description of [event] in paragraph X MOST nearly reflects —
Which detail from the selection BEST supports the idea that the author is drawing on a universal personal experience?
A student reading paragraph X would MOST likely connect the narrator's situation to —
Which sentence from the passage BEST suggests that the author intends the reader to see themselves in the text?
Based on the selection, what does the author MOST likely assume readers have experienced?
The author's use of [detail/phrase] in paragraph X contributes MOST to the reader's ability to —
Which statement BEST describes how the events in paragraph X relate to an experience most readers would recognize?
A reader who has experienced [situation] would MOST likely respond to paragraph X by —

misconceptions.title: "My Connection" Means Retelling Your Own Story
misconceptions.body: Students turn the response into an autobiography — they summarize a personal anecdote that roughly rhymes with the plot and call it a connection. The connection must anchor in the text. Teach them: the move is to name the specific moment in the source that activated the memory or belief, not to use the text as a springboard to talk about themselves. If they can't point to a line or image, they haven't made a textual connection — they've just remembered something.

misconceptions.title: Only "Big" Life Events Count
misconceptions.body: Students who haven't experienced trauma, family crisis, or the dramatic arcs they see in literary fiction often say they "can't connect" to a text. Watch for this early — it shuts down the standard before it has a chance to work. Teach them that the most durable connections are mundane: the embarrassment in a crowded hallway, the silence after a bad conversation, the feeling of wanting something they couldn't name. Connections don't need to be dramatic to be real.

misconceptions.title: Self-Selected Texts Are Lower-Status
misconceptions.body: Students (and some teachers) treat self-selected texts — manga, sports journalism, fan fiction, YouTube scripts — as less legitimate than assigned literature. This standard explicitly includes them, and for good reason: the richest personal connections often live in texts students chose themselves. Push back hard when self-selected sources get dismissed. The analytical move is the same regardless of the source's prestige.

misconceptions.title: "Sources" Means Books
misconceptions.body: Students collapse "a variety of sources" to novels and short stories. They miss that the standard names sources — plural, varied — meaning film, podcasts, articles, song lyrics, speeches, and visual texts all qualify. Teach them that the skill of describing personal connection transfers across media, and that noticing how the connection feels different across formats is itself part of the response.

misconceptions.title: Description Equals Summary
misconceptions.body: When asked to describe a personal connection, students summarize the text instead — they explain what happened to the character rather than what happened to them as a reader. Teach the difference explicitly: description of connection points inward ("this made me think / feel / remember / question") while summary points at the text ("the character did X and then Y"). These are different moves, and conflating them is the most common failure mode for this standard.

assignments.name: Connection Annotation
assignments.body: Students read any text of their choice and annotate it using one mark for "this connected to something I've experienced" and another for "this connected to something I believe." After reading, they select their three strongest annotations and write a paragraph for each explaining the connection with a quoted anchor from the text.

assignments.name: Source Variety Response Log
assignments.body: Over two weeks, students respond to five sources from at least three different formats (one print literary text, one informational text, one non-print source). Each entry: one paragraph, one specific quoted or described moment from the source, one honest account of what it activated and why.

assignments.name: Connection vs. Summary Sort
assignments.body: Provide students with ten sample responses to the same passage — five that describe genuine connections and five that summarize the text in disguise. Students sort them and explain their reasoning. Debrief as a class.

mastery: Names a specific moment in the source — line, image, scene, or idea — that triggered the connection, rather than describing the source in general.
Describes the connection inward: what the source activated in their own experience, belief, memory, or thinking.
Makes connections across at least two different source formats, not just print literary texts.
Articulates what is similar AND what is different between the source and their personal experience — not just "this reminded me of" but "this was like X, except..."
Sustains the same quality of description for self-selected texts as for assigned texts, without treating either as higher-status.
notes: Vertical entry point: This is the first standard in the Response Skills strand for English I students. There is no verticalPrev for this course. English II's 110.37(5)(A) builds on this by requiring students to make more evaluative and analytical responses — English I establishes the baseline habit of honest personal connection that English II then asks students to interrogate and extend.

DOK 2 justification: describing a personal connection requires more than recall — students must interpret their own experience in relation to a text — but the task does not yet require extended analysis or defense of an interpretive claim.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog in the assessment's scored portions, as personal connection is pre-analytical. Question stems above are written to mirror how STAAR tests reader inference and author-audience relationship, which is the closest tested analog. Note this limitation when using stems for assessment prep.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Write Responses That Demonstrate Understanding of Texts
expectation: Write responses that demonstrate understanding of texts, including comparing texts within and across genres.
overview: This TEK is where reading comprehension becomes visible on the page. Students must do more than summarize — they have to show that they understood the text well enough to put it in conversation with another one, even when those two texts don't look alike.
studentFriendly: You can write about what you've read in a way that proves you understood it — and when comparing two texts, even ones from totally different genres, you can explain what they share and where they split.
questionStems: Which detail from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that the narrator feels —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which statement MOST accurately compares how the two authors develop their central ideas?
Which sentence from the selection BEST demonstrates the student's understanding of the speaker's purpose?
The author of Selection 1 and the author of Selection 2 MOST likely agree that —
Which piece of evidence from the text BEST supports the inference that —
How does the poet's treatment of [theme] in Selection 2 differ MOST from the essayist's treatment of the same theme in Selection 1?
A student writing a response to both selections would MOST likely conclude that —
Which detail from paragraph X BEST supports a comparison between [character/subject A] and [character/subject B]?
The student's response would be MOST strengthened by adding evidence from which paragraph?
Based on both selections, which statement MOST accurately describes a similarity in how the two texts approach [topic]?

misconceptions.title: Demonstrating Understanding Means Retelling the Plot
misconceptions.body: Students write long summaries and call it a response. They've described what happened; they haven't shown they understood what it means. Teach them the difference: understanding is the "so what" behind the events. If a student can't get past "and then," they're summarizing, not responding.

misconceptions.title: Cross-Genre Comparison Means Finding Opposites
misconceptions.body: When students compare a poem to an article or a story to an essay, they immediately go hunting for differences and treat the genres as inherently incompatible. Push them to look for shared moves — both texts might develop the same theme, use the same rhetorical strategy, or construct the same kind of emotional arc, even if the form looks different.

misconceptions.title: One Good Quote Is Enough
misconceptions.body: Students anchor their entire response to a single piece of evidence and consider the job done. A response that demonstrates understanding leans on multiple moments across the text. Watch for responses where one paragraph has no textual support at all — that's usually where the student ran out of confidence, not ideas.

misconceptions.title: Comparing Means Treating Both Texts Equally at All Times
misconceptions.body: Students write responses that rigidly alternate — one sentence on Text 1, one on Text 2, one on Text 1 — like a tennis match. Real comparative writing focuses where the analysis demands: sometimes one text carries more weight on a given point, and the response should reflect that. Teach them to follow the argument, not the formula.

misconceptions.title: If I Liked the Text, I've Understood It
misconceptions.body: Students confuse personal response with analytical response. A student who loved the poem can still fail to demonstrate understanding of how the poem builds its argument. Teach them that emotional connection and textual understanding are different tools — both valid, but only one is the target here.

assignments.name: Two-Text Analysis Paragraph
assignments.body: Students read one short story excerpt and one related nonfiction article, then write a single body paragraph (150–200 words) that compares how each text develops the same central idea. Graded for evidence use, not length.

assignments.name: Genre Translation Comparison
assignments.body: Students read a poem and a personal essay on the same topic. They write a response explaining what each text understands about that topic and what each one leaves out — then argue which text offers the more complete picture.

assignments.name: Margin-to-Draft Protocol
assignments.body: Before writing the response, students annotate both texts for moments that could serve as evidence. Then they draft. After drafting, they identify each claim and check that it has at least one piece of textual support. Any unsupported claim gets flagged for revision.

assignments.name: Cross-Genre Gallery Walk
assignments.body: Post six short excerpts (two poems, two stories, two nonfiction pieces) around the room — all connected by a common theme. Students circulate and jot potential comparison points. Then they write a response comparing any two, defending why those two texts are worth putting in conversation.

mastery: Moves beyond summary to interpret what the text means or how it works, not just what it says.
Uses evidence from both texts when comparing, not just one, and integrates quotations or paraphrase without dropping them in without context.
Identifies a meaningful basis for comparison — theme, structure, argument, tone — rather than listing surface-level similarities and differences.
Maintains a clear controlling idea across the response rather than listing observations that don't connect.
Adjusts claims when comparing texts in different genres, acknowledging that form shapes meaning without abandoning the comparison.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I 5B introduces written response with cross-genre comparison. English II 5B builds on this with additional emphasis on synthesizing multiple sources and refining the analytical stance. The English I version is the foundational move — understanding must be demonstrated in writing before synthesis across sources becomes viable.
DOK 3 justification: students must construct a written response that applies understanding to a comparison task — not recall, not locate, but interpret and connect across two distinct texts.

STAAR analog note: 5B maps directly to STAAR's paired-passage constructed response tasks and to the analytical essay prompt when two anchor texts are used. Stems here mirror the question types students encounter in that context.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Use Text Evidence and Original Commentary to Support a Comprehensive Response
expectation: Use text evidence and original commentary to support a comprehensive response.
overview: This is the foundational move of literary writing: students don't just quote — they think. The skill lives at the intersection of reading comprehension and analytical writing, where selecting the right evidence and explaining why it matters are equally weighted. English I students need to learn that quotation without commentary is just summary wearing a costume.
studentFriendly: You can find a passage from a text that actually proves your point, then explain in your own words why that passage means what you say it means — not just drop it in and walk away.
questionStems: Which quotation from the selection BEST supports the inference that the narrator feels isolated from those around him?
A student writes the following claim about the text: [claim]. Which sentence from the selection provides the MOST relevant evidence to support this claim?
Read the following response to the text. Which sentence provides the original commentary needed to explain the significance of the quoted evidence?
The author's statement in paragraph X MOST likely supports which conclusion about the central theme?
Which of the following BEST completes the response by connecting the evidence in paragraph X to the writer's overall claim?
Based on paragraph X, which detail from the text would MOST effectively support the idea that —
A student wants to write a comprehensive response arguing that [claim]. Which piece of evidence from the selection BEST develops this argument?
Read the following student response. The response would MOST benefit from —
Which sentence from the selection provides evidence that MOST directly supports the idea in paragraph Y of the student response?
The student's commentary in paragraph X is MOST effective because it —

misconceptions.title: Dropping a Quote Is an Argument
misconceptions.body: Students learn to include quotations and assume the job is done. They write a claim, paste in a line from the text, and move to the next paragraph — as if the quote speaks for itself. Teach them that every quotation creates a debt: the writer owes the reader an explanation of what the evidence actually proves. No commentary, no argument.

misconceptions.title: Commentary Means Restating What the Quote Already Says
misconceptions.body: When students do attempt commentary, they often just paraphrase the quotation they just introduced — restating the words rather than extending the thinking. Push them deeper: the commentary should do something the quote can't do alone — connect it to the claim, name a pattern, explain a nuance, or draw out an implication the quote leaves unstated.

misconceptions.title: "Comprehensive" Means "Long"
misconceptions.body: Students interpret a comprehensive response as a long one and pile in as many quotes as they can find. A comprehensive response covers the claim fully — it doesn't pad. Watch for responses that quote the same idea three times from different places in the text without advancing the argument between citations. More evidence is not better evidence.

misconceptions.title: Any Evidence Will Do
misconceptions.body: Students grab the first quotation that mentions a keyword from their claim and call it support. Teach them to distinguish between relevant evidence (the quote directly addresses the claim) and incidental evidence (the quote mentions a related word or character but doesn't actually prove the point). Precision in selection is half the analytical skill.

misconceptions.title: Commentary Must Be Formal to Be Original
misconceptions.body: Students conflate "original" with "fancy" and produce commentary that sounds like a thesaurus collision. Original commentary means the student's own thinking about why the evidence matters — not borrowed phrasing, not plot summary dressed up in academic language. The commentary should be traceable to this student's reading of this text, not any student's reading of any text.

assignments.name: Evidence Sandwich Audit
assignments.body: Students draft a paragraph using a three-part structure: claim sentence, quoted evidence, commentary sentence. They then trade with a partner who covers the quote and asks: "Does the commentary stand alone as original thinking, or does it just restate the quote?" Students revise based on the answer.

assignments.name: Same Quote, Different Arguments
assignments.body: Give the class a single strong quotation from a shared text. Each student must use it to support a DIFFERENT claim — and write the commentary that makes the connection work. Students see that evidence is neutral; the argument is in the commentary.

assignments.name: Comprehensive Response Build
assignments.body: Over three days, students draft a full multi-paragraph response to a literary text, adding one layer per session: Day 1, claims only; Day 2, evidence added; Day 3, original commentary added. Final draft is assessed on how effectively commentary extends — not restates — the evidence.

assignments.name: Weak Response Revision
assignments.body: Provide a student sample response that uses real evidence but weak or missing commentary. Students diagnose what's wrong and rewrite only the commentary sentences. Compare revisions as a class to see which moved the argument furthest.

mastery: Selects text evidence that is specifically relevant to the stated claim — not just topically related.
Writes original commentary that extends the evidence rather than restating it, connecting the quotation to the broader argument.
Produces responses that cover the claim fully without inflating length through redundant evidence or paraphrase.
Balances quoted evidence and original commentary within paragraphs so neither element crowds out the other.
Sustains analytical thinking across multiple paragraphs in a comprehensive response — the argument builds; it doesn't repeat.
notes: Vertical entry point: No prior course feeds into this (English I is first). English II's 5C will build toward more sophisticated analysis of multiple texts, requiring students to synthesize evidence across sources rather than within a single text.
DOK 3 justification: Students must select appropriate evidence AND construct original reasoning that connects evidence to a claim — this is strategic thinking, not recall or simple application.

STAAR analog note: This TEK maps directly to the written composition and short-answer response components of STAAR English I. The question stems above simulate STAAR's embedded-response and revision-task framing, which assesses evidence selection and commentary quality within student-written paragraphs.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Paraphrase and Summarize Texts
expectation: Paraphrase and summarize texts in ways that maintain meaning and logical order.
overview: This TEK targets a skill that sounds easy but exposes comprehension in a hurry: students can't paraphrase or summarize what they don't actually understand. The twin goals — maintaining meaning AND logical order — are where students most frequently collapse. Getting both right requires reading for structure, not just content.
studentFriendly: You can take a complex text, put it in your own words, and condense it in a way that keeps the original idea intact and in the right sequence — without copying, distorting, or leaving out what matters.
questionStems: Which of the following BEST summarizes the central idea of paragraphs X–Y?
Read this student summary of paragraph X. Which detail from the original text did the student MOST likely misrepresent?
Which sentence BEST paraphrases the author's claim in paragraph X?
Based on the passage, which summary MOST accurately maintains the logical order of events?
Which of the following is the MOST complete summary of the entire selection?
The student's paraphrase of paragraph X changes the meaning because it —
Which sentence from the passage BEST supports a summary that focuses on the author's conclusion?
A student summarizes the passage by writing [summary]. Which important detail has the student omitted?
Which of the following paraphrases of lines X–Y maintains the author's original meaning?
Based on the selection, which summary MOST accurately reflects both the sequence and the significance of the events described?

misconceptions.title: Paraphrase Means Copy and Change a Few Words
misconceptions.body: Students swap out a handful of nouns and call it paraphrasing. They haven't actually processed the meaning — they've shuffled vocabulary. Teach them the close-then-write approach: read the passage, close it, write what it said in your own language, then check for accuracy. If their paraphrase sounds like a thesaurus attacked the original, it's a swap job, not a paraphrase.

misconceptions.title: A Summary Is a Shorter Version of Everything
misconceptions.body: Students try to include all the details at a reduced length, producing compressed lists rather than summaries. Push them to identify the author's main move first — the central claim or the core arc of events — and then ask which details are essential to support that move. Everything else gets cut. Summary is selection and judgment, not compression.

misconceptions.title: Logical Order Means Chronological Order
misconceptions.body: Students default to retelling events in the order they appear on the page. For narrative texts that's often fine; for argumentative or expository texts it goes wrong fast. Teach them to distinguish the order the author CHOSE (which may be deliberate and rhetorical) from the sequence the reader needs to track the argument. Summary must honor the logic of the text's structure, not just its paragraphs.

misconceptions.title: Maintaining Meaning Means Using the Author's Exact Words
misconceptions.body: When students are afraid of distorting the meaning, they fall back on copying. They think the safest move is to stay close to the original wording. Reframe it: using the author's exact words is quotation, not paraphrase or summary. The goal is to prove they understood the text well enough to say it differently — that requires trusting their own comprehension.

misconceptions.title: Summarizing and Responding Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students fold their opinions into summaries without realizing they've crossed a line. They write what the text is "trying to say" or what they think "the author should have said," rather than what the text actually says. Watch for evaluative language sneaking in — words like "unfortunately," "correctly," or "the author fails to." A summary reports; it does not editorialize. Separating those two moves is its own teachable skill.

assignments.name: Cover and Write
assignments.body: Students read a paragraph, cover the text, and write a paraphrase from memory. They then uncover the original and mark any meaning they dropped, added, or distorted. Discussion follows: what forced the error?

assignments.name: Three-Sentence Summary Constraint
assignments.body: Students summarize a full article in exactly three sentences — one for the central idea, one for the key supporting move, one for the conclusion or resolution. The constraint forces triage.

assignments.name: Broken Summary Repair
assignments.body: Provide a flawed student summary that either breaks logical order, adds opinion, or misrepresents a key detail. Students identify the specific failure and rewrite the summary correctly. Works well as a paired activity.

assignments.name: Side-by-Side Compare
assignments.body: Students read two paraphrases of the same passage — one accurate, one subtly distorted. They identify which is faithful and explain why, citing the original text. Builds precision vocabulary for talking about textual fidelity.

mastery: Produces paraphrases that use original language while fully preserving the source's meaning — no copying, no distortion.
Summarizes a text by identifying central ideas and essential supporting details, omitting peripheral content without losing the author's argument.
Maintains the logical order of the source — whether that order is narrative, causal, or argumentative — rather than defaulting to a flat list.
Keeps personal opinion out of paraphrase and summary, accurately distinguishing reporting from response.
Can identify and explain the specific way a flawed paraphrase or summary fails — meaning loss, order collapse, distortion, or insertion of outside ideas.
notes: Vertical entry point: English I is where this skill is formally introduced as a named expectation. English II carries the same language in 110.37(5)(D) — the ramp up is in the complexity of the texts students are summarizing, not in the skill definition itself.

DOK 2 justification: paraphrase and summary require processing and restating meaning accurately, which goes beyond recall but does not require analysis or judgment about the text's choices — that's Std 4 and Std 8 territory.

STAAR testing note: this TEK is tested indirectly on STAAR — summary and paraphrase logic underpin many "central idea" and "best supports" question types. The stems above mirror those question frames. Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Interact with Sources in Meaningful Ways
expectation: Interact with sources in meaningful ways such as notetaking, annotating, freewriting, or illustrating.
overview: This TEK is about the physical and intellectual habits students bring to a text before they write or discuss it. The "meaningful" qualifier is the whole game — circling words you don't understand is not the same as noting why the author chose those words. The standard builds the infrastructure that makes all other response and analysis work possible.
studentFriendly: You leave marks on a text — notes, annotations, sketches, freewriting — that show your thinking while you read, not just after you're done.
questionStems: Read the annotation a student wrote in the margin next to paragraph 3. Which statement BEST explains how the annotation reflects the central idea of the passage?
A student underlined the phrase [phrase] in paragraph X. Which of the following MOST likely explains why the student marked this detail?
Based on the notes a student took while reading the passage, which inference can BEST be drawn about the author's purpose?
Which detail from the selection would MOST likely be included in a reader's notes about the development of the central conflict?
A student freewriting after reading the passage wrote: "[freewrite excerpt]." Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the student's response?
Which detail from paragraph X would MOST likely be annotated by a reader tracking the author's use of figurative language?
A student illustrated the sequence of events in the passage. Which event would MOST likely appear first in the illustration?
Based on the marginal notes shown, which conclusion about the speaker's attitude can BEST be supported by evidence from the text?
A student's notes identify [detail] as significant. Which sentence from the selection MOST likely explains why this detail matters to the author's argument?
Which of the following annotations MOST accurately reflects the meaning of paragraph Y?

misconceptions.title: More Marks = More Thinking
misconceptions.body: Students believe that covering a page in highlights or underlining proves engagement. Watch for the student who has highlighted three-quarters of a passage and can't explain what any of it means. Teach them that annotation is a selection act, not a decoration act — the choice of WHAT to mark is the thinking, not the marking itself.

misconceptions.title: Freewriting Is Just Summarizing
misconceptions.body: When students freewrite after reading, they tend to retell what happened instead of responding to it. Push them past summary by giving a sentence-starter that forces a position: "What surprised you, and why?" or "What did the author assume about you as a reader?" The goal is contact with the text's ideas, not proof they read it.

misconceptions.title: Notetaking Means Copying Sentences
misconceptions.body: Students treat their notebook like a transcript — they copy lines verbatim and call it notetaking. Teach them to translate: if you can write it in your own words, you understand it; if you can only copy it, you don't. Paraphrase-first note discipline catches confusion early.

misconceptions.title: Annotations Are for the Teacher
misconceptions.body: Students who annotate for a grade write neat, surface-level notes that perform engagement rather than enact it. They annotate what they think the teacher wants to see, not what actually struck them. Reframe annotation as a conversation with the author — messy, honest, and useful for them first.

misconceptions.title: Illustrating Is the Easy Option
misconceptions.body: When given a choice of annotation modes, lower-confidence readers often pick illustrating because it feels less academic — but then they draw literal images (a picture of a house for a scene in a house) with no analytical value. Teach them that a useful illustration shows structure, relationship, or shift — a timeline, a cause-and-effect web, a character change arc — not a scene from the plot.

assignments.name: Annotation Audit
assignments.body: Students annotate a short passage (1–2 pages) with no guidance on what to mark. Then they write a one-paragraph explanation of their annotation choices: what did they mark, why those moments, and what patterns emerged? Grade the explanation, not the quantity of marks.

assignments.name: Three-Column Notes
assignments.body: Students fold their paper into three columns: Quote / Paraphrase / So What? For every note they record, they must complete all three columns. The "So What?" column is where the thinking lives — collect and discuss only those.

assignments.name: Freewrite-to-Argument Pipeline
assignments.body: Students freewrite for five minutes immediately after reading. Then they underline the one sentence in their freewrite that could become a thesis. They draft that thesis formally. The exercise makes the intellectual move from raw response to arguable claim visible.

assignments.name: Annotation Gallery Walk
assignments.body: Students annotate the same passage. Pages are posted anonymously. Class walks the gallery and marks which annotations pushed their thinking furthest. Discussion focuses on what made the strongest annotations strong — specificity, connection, question, or inference.

mastery: Selects specific moments in a text to annotate and can explain why each selection matters — not just that it was "interesting."
Produces freewriting that moves beyond summary into response, question, or inference.
Uses notes that translate text into their own language rather than transcribing it verbatim.
Chooses an annotation strategy (notes, freewriting, illustration) that fits the type of text and task at hand.
Returns to their own annotations during discussion or drafting and uses them as evidence or a starting point — not as a completed task they're done with.
notes: No direct vertical predecessor: English I is the entry point for this standard. English II (110.37(5)(E)) builds on this by expecting students to interact with sources in ways that support synthesis across multiple texts — the move from engaging ONE source meaningfully to tracking ideas ACROSS sources.

DOK 2 justification: the standard asks students to apply annotation and notetaking strategies to a text — skill application, not just recall. Consistent meaning-making across different texts lifts it above DOK 1.

STAAR does not test annotation behavior directly; question stems simulate how STAAR would assess a student's ability to identify significant textual details — the cognitive work annotation is supposed to develop. Note this when using stems in class: the test doesn't ask "did you annotate?" but it absolutely tests whether the student engaged with the text at the level good annotation produces.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Respond Using Acquired Content and Academic Vocabulary
expectation: Respond using acquired content and academic vocabulary as appropriate.
overview: This TEK is about transfer — students have to actually deploy the vocabulary they've been taught, not just recognize it on a word wall. The measure is whether they reach for the right term when constructing a response, without being prompted to do so.
studentFriendly: When you write or speak a response about a text or topic, you use the specific content and academic vocabulary you've learned — not because you have to, but because those words say things more precisely than anything else would.
questionStems: Which sentence from the student's response MOST effectively uses academic vocabulary to support the central claim?
The word "juxtaposition" in the student's response to paragraph X MOST nearly means —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which term BEST describes the author's structural technique?
Which revision of the underlined sentence BEST incorporates precise content vocabulary from the selection?
The student's use of the term "perspective" in the response to paragraph X suggests that the student —
Which sentence BEST demonstrates the use of acquired vocabulary to explain the theme developed in paragraphs X–Y?
A student writes, "[draft response]." Which content term from the selection would MOST strengthen the student's claim in paragraph X?
Based on the selection, which academic term MOST accurately describes the relationship between the two characters in paragraphs X–Y?
The student's response in paragraph X is MOST weakened by the failure to —
Which of the following revisions BEST replaces the underlined phrase with content-specific vocabulary that accurately reflects the author's technique in paragraph X?

misconceptions.title: "Using big words" is the same as using academic vocabulary
misconceptions.body: Students treat academic vocabulary as a prestige move — they drop in words like "plethora" or "utilize" to sound sophisticated, without any relationship to the text's actual content. Teach them that academic vocabulary earns its place when it carries meaning that everyday language can't do as efficiently: "internal conflict" is academic because it names something precisely; "the character was sad inside" doesn't. The test is always whether the word is doing work.

misconceptions.title: Academic vocabulary is only for the essay, not the discussion
misconceptions.body: Students compartmentalize. They'll write "the narrator's tone shifts" on paper but say "the writing feels different" out loud. Push them to use content and academic vocabulary in every spoken response, not just formal writing. The "as appropriate" in the TEK matters — it's not a license to skip the words in discussion; it's a reminder that register matches context.

misconceptions.title: If they know the word, they've met the standard
misconceptions.body: Students who can define "foreshadowing" on a quiz have not met this TEK. The standard is deployment — using the term in a response about a specific text in a way that shows comprehension, not just memorization. Watch for students who list vocabulary terms without connecting them to a claim: "The author uses foreshadowing, imagery, and tone" is not a response; it's a word wall.

misconceptions.title: Content vocabulary and academic vocabulary are the same thing
misconceptions.body: Students conflate domain-specific content words ("iambic pentameter," "soliloquy") with general academic vocabulary ("analyze," "perspective," "evidence"). Both matter, and the distinction matters — teach them that academic vocabulary crosses all disciplines ("compare," "develop," "structure"), while content vocabulary is specific to the genre, period, or subject at hand. Both categories belong in a strong response.

misconceptions.title: Vocabulary density signals quality
misconceptions.body: Students pile on terms because they assume more vocabulary equals a better score. A response stuffed with jargon but missing a coherent argument is worse than a clear claim using three well-chosen terms. Teach them that vocabulary serves the argument — it's not decoration. One precisely used term in the right place outweighs five terms lobbed at the reader with no connective tissue.

assignments.name: Vocabulary-in-the-Wild Response
assignments.body: After reading a selection, students write a short constructed response (one paragraph) without access to a word bank. Teacher highlights every piece of content or academic vocabulary that appears and maps it back to the word's instructional origin. Goal: visible transfer, not prompted retrieval.

assignments.name: Revision with Precision
assignments.body: Give students a generic response to a text ("The author makes the reader feel sad by describing the setting"). Students revise it, replacing vague language with accurate content and academic vocabulary. They must justify each substitution in a margin note — what does the new word carry that the original word couldn't?

assignments.name: Paired Response Audit
assignments.body: Two students exchange their written responses and annotate each other's work: circle every piece of academic or content vocabulary, underline every place where a specific term was needed but missing. Writers revise based on feedback. Discussion follows: which absences cost the most?

mastery: Uses content and academic vocabulary naturally in constructed responses without requiring explicit prompts or word banks.
Selects vocabulary that is accurate and precise — the term matches the textual evidence and the claim being made.
Integrates vocabulary into arguable sentences rather than listing terms in isolation.
Demonstrates the same vocabulary transfer in spoken responses and discussion as in formal writing.
Adjusts vocabulary level to the register of the task — more formal and technical in analytical writing, appropriately conversational but still precise in discussion.
notes: Vertical alignment: This is a first introduction to explicit vocabulary deployment as a response standard. English II builds on it by extending expectations into more complex argumentation and cross-textual response; English III and IV raise the bar for rhetorical precision and disciplinary register.

DOK 2 justification: The skill requires application of acquired vocabulary to a new context (a specific text or prompt) — more than recall, but the primary cognitive demand is accurate use rather than extended reasoning.

STAAR analog note: This TEK is most directly assessed through constructed-response scoring rubrics (vocabulary use as a dimension) rather than discrete multiple-choice items. The question stems above mirror how STAAR assesses vocabulary precision in student-written responses and how STAAR tests vocabulary selection in revision tasks.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Discuss and Write About Explicit or Implicit Meanings of Text
expectation: Discuss and write about the explicit or implicit meanings of text.
overview: This is the core response move of English I: can a student say what the text actually says AND what the text is doing beneath the surface — and then write that down with enough precision that someone else can follow it? Explicit/implicit is the axis around which most STAAR literary and informational response questions turn.
studentFriendly: You can read a text, identify what it says directly on the surface, pull out what it only hints at or implies, and explain both in discussion and in writing.
questionStems: Which sentence from the selection BEST states an explicit claim the author makes about —
Based on paragraph X, what can the reader MOST likely infer about —
Which detail from paragraphs X–Y BEST supports the inference that —
The author implies in paragraph X that — How does the information in paragraph Y support this interpretation?
What does the phrase "[phrase]" in paragraph X suggest about —
Which of the following BEST states an implicit message of the selection?
Based on the information in the selection, what conclusion can MOST reasonably be drawn about —
The details in paragraphs X and Y MOST likely suggest that the author believes —
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the idea that the narrator feels —
A student reading this selection would MOST likely conclude that the central implied message is —

misconceptions.title: "It Says It Right There" Is Always Enough
misconceptions.body: Students locate explicit details and stop, treating the surface meaning as the complete response. Teach them that explicit evidence is a starting point, not a finish line — a thorough response always asks what the detail is doing, not just what it says. Watch for responses that are just quote-stacking with nothing attached.

misconceptions.title: Implicit Means "Made Up"
misconceptions.body: Students confuse implicit meaning with personal opinion, writing interpretations that have zero grounding in the text. The implicit is what the text earns through accumulated detail, syntax, and pattern — not what the student wishes the text meant. Teach them to trace every inference back to at least two textual anchors before they commit to it in writing.

misconceptions.title: Discussion and Writing Are the Same Skill
misconceptions.body: Students who talk fluently in discussion often freeze when asked to write the same idea down, and vice versa. The two modes are related but not identical — discussion lets them test a claim; writing forces them to commit to it with structure. Treat discussion as a drafting space for written response, not a substitute for it.

misconceptions.title: Implicit Meaning Lives in One Place
misconceptions.body: Students hunt for one buried sentence that "contains" the implicit meaning, as if implication works like a hidden message. Push them to see that implicit meaning accumulates across the whole text — in repetition, in what's omitted, in who speaks and who doesn't, in how scenes are ordered. One sentence rarely carries the whole weight.

misconceptions.title: A Strong Inference Needs No Proof
misconceptions.body: Students write confident implicit-meaning claims and then offer no textual support, assuming the claim is self-evident.
