Remind them that the more subtle the inference, the MORE evidence it requires — the implicit is harder to prove than the explicit, not easier. Grade for the quality of the support, not just the quality of the claim.

assignments.name: Split-Screen Annotation
assignments.body: Students annotate a passage in two colors: one for explicit statements (things the text says outright) and one for places where meaning must be inferred. After annotation, they write a paragraph explaining the relationship between the two layers.

assignments.name: The Gap Between Said and Meant
assignments.body: Students read a short literary text and write two paragraphs: the first explains what the text explicitly states; the second explains what the text implicitly communicates. A final sentence identifies which layer they found more complex and why.

assignments.name: Inference Chain
assignments.body: Students select one implicit meaning they've identified and build a written chain showing exactly how they got there — listing at least three specific textual details that together earn the inference. Classmates evaluate whether the chain holds.

assignments.name: STAAR-Style Extended Response
assignments.body: Students respond to a prompt requiring them to discuss both an explicit theme statement and the implicit evidence that develops it across the text. Scored with a rubric that awards separate points for explicit identification, implicit analysis, and textual support.

mastery: Distinguishes in writing between what a text directly states and what it implies, without collapsing one into the other.
Supports every implicit meaning claim with at least two specific textual details, named and explained.
Uses discussion as a space to test and refine interpretations before committing them to writing.
Produces written responses that move from explicit surface meaning to implicit deeper meaning with logical, text-grounded reasoning.
Recognizes that a single passage may carry multiple implicit meanings and selects the most defensible one for a focused written response.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I establishes the explicit/implicit distinction as a foundational reading and response skill. English II (110.37(5)(G)) extends this to more complex texts and more sustained written argument. The skill ramps from identifying and naming both layers to deploying them as the engine of analytical writing.

DOK 3 justification: Students must move beyond retrieval (explicit) and into strategic reasoning (implicit), then translate that reasoning into organized written and oral response — requiring inference, synthesis, and evidence selection.

STAAR analog: Explicit/implicit meaning is among the most heavily tested skills on STAAR Reading — nearly every literary and informational response question tests one layer or the other, or the relationship between them. Stems here model the exact phrasing students will encounter.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Respond with Appropriate Register, Vocabulary, Tone, and Voice
expectation: Respond orally or in writing with appropriate register, vocabulary, tone, and voice.
overview: This TEK is the practical delivery end of response: students don't just understand a text, they answer it — and how they answer reveals whether they've actually calibrated to audience, purpose, and context. Register slippage (texting a formal prompt, or stuffing casual conversation with academic jargon) is the most visible failure mode in English I.
studentFriendly: You can read or listen to something and respond — in writing or out loud — using the right level of formality, the right words, and a voice that fits who you're talking to and why.
questionStems: Which response BEST matches the formal register expected in a written analysis of the text?
The student's use of [phrase] in the response is MOST likely inappropriate because it —
Which revision to the underlined sentence would BEST adjust the tone to match the purpose of the response?
Based on the passage, which vocabulary choice would MOST effectively support an academic written response about the author's argument?
The shift in the writer's voice between paragraph X and paragraph Y MOST likely suggests that —
Which sentence from the response BEST demonstrates an appropriate academic register for the task?
A student writes the following response to the passage: [excerpt]. Which revision would MOST improve the appropriateness of the vocabulary for a formal audience?
How does the tone of the response in paragraph X contribute to the writer's overall purpose?
Which word or phrase, if substituted for [word] in line X, would MOST likely maintain the appropriate register of the response?
Based on the context of the writing task, the writer's voice in paragraph X is MOST nearly —

misconceptions.title: Formal Means Long Words
misconceptions.body: Students think elevating register means swapping every simple word for a polysyllabic one — "utilize" instead of "use," "commence" instead of "start." Teach them that formal register is about precision and audience alignment, not syllable count. A simple, exact word in the right place outranks a bloated one every time. Watch for responses that read like a thesaurus walked through a perfectly clear idea and mugged it.

misconceptions.title: Voice and Personality Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students collapse "voice" into "personality" — they think a strong voice means adding jokes, first-person tangents, or casual asides. Push them to see that voice in academic response is the distinct, consistent way a writer pursues their argument: the confidence of the claim, the selectivity of evidence, the rhythm of reasoning. Voice isn't decoration; it's how the writer sounds THINKING.

misconceptions.title: Register Is a Switch, Not a Dial
misconceptions.body: Students treat register as binary — formal or informal — and miss all the gradations in between. A letter to a city council is not the same register as a literary analysis, even if both are "formal." A text to a friend explaining a hard day is not the same as a journal entry. Teach them that register responds to the specific audience and purpose of each task, not a one-size category.

misconceptions.title: Tone Is How You Feel About the Topic
misconceptions.body: Students write "the tone is angry" or "the tone is happy" without connecting tone to specific language choices. They confuse their emotional reaction to content with the writer's tonal craft. Reframe: tone is what the WORDS are doing — the cumulative effect of diction, syntax, and stance. Push them to name the language choices producing the tone, not just the feeling it creates.

misconceptions.title: Their Vocabulary Bank Is Fixed
misconceptions.body: Students default to the same ten academic words across every response because they believe switching vocabulary mid-unit is risky or "fake." Watch for flat, repetitive word choices even when the text offers more precise alternatives. Teach them that vocabulary appropriateness is context-dependent — the word that worked perfectly in a narrative response may be too vague or too charged for an argumentative one.

assignments.name: Register Remix
assignments.body: Give students a single prompt (e.g., "Why does the character make this choice?") and ask them to respond to it three ways: in a casual text to a friend, in a class discussion post, and in a formal analytical paragraph. Students compare the three and name every shift they made — vocabulary, sentence structure, tone, and voice.

assignments.name: Tone Audit
assignments.body: Students draft a response to a short passage, then trade papers. The reader marks every word or sentence that signals tone, writing a one-word label in the margin (e.g., "formal," "dismissive," "hedging"). Writers revise based on whether the tone labels match their intent.

assignments.name: Voice Transplant
assignments.body: Provide two sample responses to the same text — one with a confident, precise voice and one that waffles. Students identify what SPECIFICALLY in each creates its voice, then revise the weaker sample by making targeted changes to diction and sentence structure, not by rewriting wholesale.

mastery: Selects vocabulary that is precise, contextually appropriate, and consistent with the register demanded by the task — not interchangeable from one response to the next.
Maintains a consistent tone throughout an oral or written response without unintentional slippage into informality or inappropriate affect.
Distinguishes register requirements across different response types (discussion, literary analysis, argument) and adjusts accordingly without prompting.
Produces a recognizable, stable voice in written response — the reader can tell this writer is in control of the reasoning, not just reciting it.
Identifies when their own draft has shifted register or tone mid-response and revises the specific language causing the break.
notes: Vertical entry: English I is the first formal encounter with this TEK. English II (110.37(5)(H)) holds the same expectation but in a broader range of text types and response modes — by English II, students are expected to sustain register and voice across longer, more complex analytical work.

This TEK pairs tightly with the composition strand (Std 9) and the author's craft strand (Std 8): students can only respond with appropriate register and voice if they understand how writers construct those elements in the texts they're responding to.

STAAR analog note: This TEK rarely generates its own standalone item, but it underlies nearly every written response task on STAAR — extended constructed responses are scored in part on whether the student's language register and tone match the task demands. Question stems above simulate how STAAR evaluates register and tone in student writing passages used as stimulus texts.
DOK 3 justification: selecting and sustaining appropriate register requires strategic reasoning about audience, purpose, and context — not just retrieval or skill application.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Reflect on and Adjust Responses When Valid Evidence Warrants
expectation: Reflect on and adjust responses when valid evidence warrants.
overview: This is the intellectual honesty TEK. Students have to do the hard thing: hold a position, encounter evidence that complicates it, and change their mind without falling apart — or without refusing to budge out of stubbornness. The skill is metacognitive and evaluative simultaneously.
studentFriendly: You can read something new, realize it changes what you said earlier, and update your thinking — without just caving to pressure, but also without ignoring evidence that actually matters.
questionStems: Based on the information in paragraphs X–Y, which adjustment to the student's original interpretation is MOST warranted?
Which evidence from the selection BEST supports revising the inference that —
After reading paragraph X, a reader would MOST likely reconsider which earlier conclusion?
The details in paragraph X MOST nearly require the reader to —
Which sentence from the selection provides the MOST valid reason to adjust the claim that —
A student initially concluded [claim]. Based on paragraph X, which evidence MOST directly challenges that conclusion?
Which detail from the selection BEST indicates that an earlier interpretation of [character/idea/event] should be revised?
The new information in paragraph X MOST likely affects the reader's understanding of —
Which statement BEST describes how the evidence in paragraph X changes the meaning established in paragraphs Y–Z?
Based on the selection as a whole, which conclusion requires the MOST significant revision from the reader's initial reading?

misconceptions.title: Changing Your Mind Means You Were Wrong
misconceptions.body: Students treat revision as failure — if they update a claim, they lose face. Teach them that revision in response to valid evidence is the definition of good reasoning, not a reversal of it. Writers, scientists, and lawyers all do this. The failure isn't changing your mind; the failure is refusing to when the evidence demands it.

misconceptions.title: Any Pushback Is "Valid Evidence"
misconceptions.body: Students collapse "someone disagreed with me" into "I should adjust." Peer pressure, volume, and confidence are not evidence. Teach them to ask: what is the actual textual or factual basis for the challenge? If someone says "I don't think that's right" without citing anything, that's not a warrant to revise — it's a prompt to defend.

misconceptions.title: Adjustment Means Complete Reversal
misconceptions.body: Watch for students who, once they decide to update a response, throw out everything they said before. Revision is surgical, not demolition. Teach them to name exactly what changed and what still holds. A response that says "I was totally wrong about everything" is not more honest than a targeted update — it's less precise.

misconceptions.title: Reflection Happens After the Discussion, Not During
misconceptions.body: Students treat reflection as a post-activity ritual — they'll write a paragraph at the end saying "this changed my thinking" because that's what the prompt asked. The real skill is in-the-moment: encountering evidence, pausing, and deciding whether your position needs to move. Build in structures (annotation, margin notes, think-aloud) that make mid-process adjustment visible.

misconceptions.title: All Evidence Is Equally Valid
misconceptions.body: Students struggle to evaluate the quality of evidence before deciding whether to adjust. They'll update a carefully supported claim because someone cites a vague example, or they'll refuse to update because the challenging evidence feels inconvenient. Push them to evaluate the source, specificity, and relevance of any evidence before deciding whether it actually warrants a change.

assignments.name: The Position Tracker
assignments.body: Students write an initial response to a text, read three additional sources, and produce a revised response that explicitly names which claims held, which shifted, and what specific evidence caused each shift. Revision without annotation doesn't count.

assignments.name: Devil's Advocate Exchange
assignments.body: Pairs swap written responses and write the strongest possible textual challenge to each other's claim. Writers then decide in writing: does this evidence warrant adjustment, and why or why not? Both decisions — to revise OR to hold — must be defended.

assignments.name: Before/After Protocol
assignments.body: After a Socratic seminar or fishbowl discussion, students produce a two-column document: original claim with original evidence on the left, adjusted (or defended and unchanged) claim with new evidence on the right. A third column explains the reasoning for what changed and what didn't.

mastery: Distinguishes between evidence that genuinely warrants revision and social pressure or unsupported challenge that doesn't.
Names the specific evidence that caused a position to shift — not just "I read more and changed my mind."
Revises precisely — updates the part of the claim that evidence challenges, rather than abandoning the full response or restating it unchanged.
Defends a position under challenge when the evidence on the table does not actually warrant revision.
Produces written or oral responses that show visible metacognitive movement — the reader can track what the student originally thought, what they encountered, and why the response changed or held.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I introduces the metacognitive loop — reflect, evaluate the evidence, decide whether to move. English II is expected to perform the same skill with greater independence and across more complex sources. The ramp is not in the standard itself but in the complexity of texts and the sophistication of positions students are asked to revise.

DOK 3 justification: Students must evaluate whether evidence meets the threshold for warranting revision — a strategic, multi-step cognitive move that requires reasoning about their own reasoning, not just retrieval or application.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog in the oral metacognitive sense, but it is tested indirectly: STAAR asks students to select the BEST revision to an interpretation based on new textual details, which is precisely what this standard targets in reading/writing form. See Note in stems: paragraph-reference stems were written to mirror that testing pattern.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Defend or Challenge Authors' Claims Using Text Evidence
expectation: Defend or challenge the authors' claims using relevant text evidence.
overview: This TEK is where response becomes argument. Students don't just agree or disagree with an author — they build a case using the text itself. The harder skill is challenging a claim the student actually agrees with, or defending one they don't, because the text demands it.
studentFriendly: You can read what an author claims, decide whether the evidence in the text actually holds that claim up, and make a clear written or spoken case for why the author is right or wrong — using the text itself, not just your opinion.
questionStems: Which piece of evidence from the selection BEST supports a challenge to the author's claim in paragraph X?
The author claims that [claim]. Which sentence from the selection MOST effectively defends this claim?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which statement BEST describes a weakness in the author's argument?
A student states that the author's claim in paragraph X is unsupported. Which evidence from the selection BEST supports the student's position?
Which sentence from the selection would MOST likely be used to challenge the author's conclusion in paragraph X?
The author uses the evidence in paragraph X to support the claim that — based on this evidence, a reader could MOST reasonably conclude —
Which detail from paragraphs X–Y provides the STRONGEST support for the author's central claim?
A reader wants to challenge the author's position in paragraph X. Which sentence from the selection BEST supports that challenge?
The author's claim in paragraph X is MOST weakened by which piece of evidence found later in the selection?
Based on the selection, which statement would the author MOST likely use to defend against a challenge to the argument in paragraph X?

misconceptions.title: "I disagree" is a position, not an argument
misconceptions.body: Students write "I challenge this claim because I don't think it's true" and stop there. They've stated a preference, not built a case. Teach them that defend/challenge is a two-part move: name the specific claim and then tether your response to a passage from the text. Opinion without evidence is just noise with confidence.

misconceptions.title: Defending means agreeing with everything the author says
misconceptions.body: Students who agree with an author's conclusion defend it wholesale — they don't notice the claim that overreaches, the statistic misapplied, or the logical gap in paragraph four. Defense is not loyalty. Teach them to defend each individual claim on the evidence available for that claim, not because they like where the author lands.

misconceptions.title: The strongest evidence is the most dramatic quote
misconceptions.body: Students reach for the most vivid sentence in the passage — the shocking statistic, the emotional anecdote — and assume it's the strongest evidence. Push them to ask: does this piece of evidence actually address the specific claim being defended or challenged, or does it just sound good? Relevance beats drama every time.

misconceptions.title: Challenging the author means the author is bad at writing
misconceptions.body: Students treat challenge as a verdict on the author's competence. Watch for responses that drift into dismissal ("the author clearly doesn't know what they're talking about"). A challenge is a claim about a specific gap between assertion and evidence — it's surgical, not a general takedown. Model the difference between "this sentence contradicts that claim" and "this author is wrong about everything."

misconceptions.title: You can challenge a claim without citing the text
misconceptions.body: Students pull in outside knowledge, personal experience, or common sense to challenge an author — and those things feel convincing. They're not TEKS-compliant, and they're not the skill. Relevant text evidence means the challenge lives inside the four corners of the selection. Train students to find the contradiction or gap using only what the author provided.

assignments.name: Claim Court
assignments.body: Give students a short argumentative or informational text. Assign half the class to defend the author's central claim and half to challenge it — using only text evidence. Each side presents its three strongest pieces of evidence. The class then votes on which side made the better textual case (not whose position they agree with).

assignments.name: Relevance Ranking
assignments.body: Provide a list of six quotes from a text and a specific author's claim. Students rank the quotes from most to least relevant evidence for defending or challenging that claim and write one sentence explaining each ranking decision. This isolates the "relevant" requirement students most often skip.

assignments.name: Flip the Defense
assignments.body: Students read an argumentative text and write a one-paragraph defense of the author's claim. Then they flip it: using the same text, write a one-paragraph challenge using different evidence. Debrief on how the same text can support both moves when read carefully.

mastery: Identifies the specific claim being addressed before selecting any evidence — doesn't begin with the quote and work backward.
Selects evidence that is relevant to the targeted claim, not just generally interesting or emotionally resonant.
Explains the connection between the cited evidence and the position being defended or challenged — doesn't drop quotes and assume they speak for themselves.
Can make both moves (defend AND challenge) on the same text, driven by the evidence rather than personal agreement with the author.
Distinguishes between weakening a claim and disproving it entirely — calibrates the strength of the challenge to what the text actually supports.
notes: Vertical alignment: English II 5J extends this skill — students continue to defend or challenge claims but are expected to do so across multiple texts, increasing the complexity of synthesis required. English I establishes the foundational move: one author, one claim, relevant text evidence.

DOK 3 justification: students must evaluate which evidence is relevant to a specific claim, construct a reasoned position, and explain the logical connection — this requires strategic thinking beyond simple retrieval or application.

STAAR note: this TEK is tested primarily through short-answer and essay prompts on STAAR. The question stems above mirror the multiple-choice framing STAAR uses to assess claim-evidence reasoning in informational and argumentative selections.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze How Themes Are Developed Through Characterization and Plot
expectation: Analyze how themes are developed through characterization and plot in a variety of literary texts.
overview: This is the engine of literary analysis in English I — not just naming a theme, but tracing the mechanism by which the text builds it. Students have to show the wiring: how what a character wants, does, believes, and becomes connects to what the story is finally saying.
studentFriendly: You can read a story, track what the characters do and how the plot moves, and explain how those two things work together to build the story's central message — not just state the theme, but show where in the text it comes from.
questionStems: Which statement BEST expresses a theme developed in the selection?
The author MOST likely includes the events in paragraphs X–Y to develop which theme?
How does the character's decision in paragraph X contribute to the development of the theme?
Based on the events in paragraphs X–Y, what theme is MOST clearly supported?
Which detail from the selection BEST supports the inference that the author's theme involves —
How does the conflict in paragraphs X–Y develop the theme of the selection?
The character's change from paragraph X to paragraph Y MOST likely supports the idea that —
Which sentence from the selection BEST demonstrates how characterization develops the theme?
The events in paragraph X contribute to the theme by —
Based on the selection as a whole, which statement MOST accurately describes how the plot develops the central theme?

misconceptions.title: Theme Is Just a Topic Word
misconceptions.body: Students write "the theme is friendship" or "the theme is courage" and stop there. That's a topic, not a theme — a theme makes a claim about the topic. Teach them the difference by requiring every theme statement to be a complete sentence that could be agreed or disagreed with. "Friendship can survive betrayal only if both people choose to change" is a theme; "friendship" is not.

misconceptions.title: Characterization Means Physical Description
misconceptions.body: When students hear "characterization," they reach for eye color and hair length. Push them toward the actual analytical targets: what the character says, what the character does, what others say about them, and — hardest — what the character believes even when the text doesn't say it directly. Physical description is the least interesting kind of characterization for theme analysis.

misconceptions.title: The Theme Is Stated Somewhere in the Text
misconceptions.body: Students hunt for the "moral of the story" sentence, often latching onto a character's direct speech as the theme. Real theme analysis deals with what the text demonstrates, not what a character announces. Teach them: a character can be wrong; the text as a whole is smarter than any single character in it.

misconceptions.title: Plot and Theme Are Two Separate Assignments
misconceptions.body: Students analyze the plot in one paragraph and the theme in another and never connect them. The expectation is the relationship — how plot events are selected and ordered to build meaning. Push them to ask: why does this thing happen at this point in the story? What does the timing of the conflict or resolution reveal about what the author believes?

misconceptions.title: Every Text Has One Correct Theme
misconceptions.body: Students defer to the "right answer" and argue by consensus rather than by evidence. Teach them that a sophisticated text supports multiple defensible themes — the quality of the claim is in the quality of the evidence, not in matching what the teacher is thinking. At the same time, watch for students who claim any theme is valid; themes have to be earned by the text.

assignments.name: Theme Trace
assignments.body: Students select a theme statement they can defend and build an evidence chain: three moments from the plot and two moments of characterization that each contribute to it. Written analysis, one paragraph per piece of evidence, all tied back to the same theme claim.

assignments.name: Character Arc Map
assignments.body: Students chart a central character across four or five plot points, noting what the character wants, believes, and does at each stage. Then they write a paragraph explaining what the arc — taken as a whole — suggests the text is saying thematically.

assignments.name: Same Plot, Different Theme
assignments.body: Two students read the same text and defend different theme statements using overlapping evidence. They present their arguments side by side and discuss which claim is better supported — not which theme is "right."

assignments.name: Revision as Analysis
assignments.body: Students rewrite one scene — changing a character's decision or a plot event — and explain in a paragraph what theme that change would support instead. The exercise forces them to see how choices drive meaning.

assignments.name: Comparative Theme Analysis
assignments.body: Students read two short texts in the same genre and write an essay arguing whether the two texts develop the same theme through different methods, or genuinely different themes entirely. Evidence from both texts required.

mastery: States a theme as a complete, defensible claim — not a topic word — and ties it to specific plot events and character behaviors.
Distinguishes between what a character says and what the text demonstrates, analyzing both as separate but related layers of meaning.
Identifies how the timing and structure of plot events shape thematic development, not just their content.
Analyzes characterization beyond physical description — using dialogue, action, motivation, and internal conflict as evidence.
Sustains a theme argument across multiple pieces of evidence from different parts of the text, including the ending's contribution.
notes: Vertical entry point: English I is students' first formal exposure to the expectation that theme must be traced through textual mechanism, not just named. English II (110.37(6)(A)) extends the same skill to include an explicit expectation around complex and universal themes across diverse texts — the bar for abstraction and cross-text comparison rises.

DOK 3 justification: Students must apply an interpretive frame (theme as claim) to specific textual evidence, explain the relationship between characterization/plot and meaning, and sustain that reasoning across a complete text. Strategic thinking is required; this is not recall or summary.
This TEK spans genres ("a variety of literary texts") — short stories, novels, drama, and narrative poetry all qualify. Misconceptions and assignments should rotate across genres throughout the year.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze How Authors Develop Complex Characters in Fiction
expectation: Analyze how authors develop complex yet believable characters in works of fiction through a range of literary devices, including character foils.
overview: This TEK asks students to move beyond "the character changed" and into the craft behind the change — the specific literary devices an author deploys to make a character feel real and contradictory at once. The foil requirement raises the stakes: students must track how characters are built relationally, not just individually.
studentFriendly: You can read a piece of fiction and explain exactly how the author made a character feel like a real, complicated person — including how other characters in the story reveal things about them that they'd never reveal alone.
questionStems: Read paragraphs X–Y. The author MOST likely includes the character of [Name] in these paragraphs to —
Which detail from the selection BEST develops [character]'s internal conflict?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the reader can infer that [character]'s attitude toward [situation] is —
In paragraph X, the author develops [character] as a foil to [character] MOST clearly by —
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that [character] is more complex than [character] initially believes?
The contrast between [character A] and [character B] in paragraphs X–Y MOST likely serves to —
How does the author's use of [device — e.g., indirect characterization / internal monologue] in paragraph X contribute to the reader's understanding of [character]?
In paragraphs X–Y, [character]'s response to [event] suggests that —
Which detail from paragraph X MOST clearly reveals a contradiction in [character]'s behavior?
The author MOST likely includes [character]'s reaction in paragraph X to develop the idea that —

misconceptions.title: Complexity Means the Character Is Confusing
misconceptions.body: Students treat contradictory behavior as a sign the author made a mistake — "I don't get why she would do that." Teach them that contradiction is the point: real people hold competing loyalties, fears, and desires simultaneously, and the author's job is to make those contradictions feel earned rather than random. Push them to ask what pressures are pulling the character in opposite directions before concluding a character's behavior doesn't make sense.

misconceptions.title: A Foil Is Just "The Opposite"
misconceptions.body: Students reduce foils to a binary checklist: one is brave, one is cowardly; one is kind, one is cruel. That's a starting point, not an analysis. The richer move is explaining what the contrast REVEALS — what we know about the protagonist precisely because we've seen the foil handle the same situation differently. Teach them: a foil is a lens, not a list.

misconceptions.title: Character Development Equals Plot Events
misconceptions.body: Students describe what happened to a character rather than how the author constructed that character. They'll write "she grows up over the course of the novel" when the prompt asks about literary devices. Watch for responses that summarize plot without naming a single technique — those students haven't analyzed character development; they've retold it.

misconceptions.title: Believability Means Likability
misconceptions.body: Students assume a believable character is one they can sympathize with. Show them compelling antagonists and morally compromised protagonists — characters who are fully realized without being easy to root for. Believability is about internal consistency and human recognizability, not warmth. A believable villain is one whose choices make terrible sense.

misconceptions.title: Literary Devices Are Decoration
misconceptions.body: Students treat devices like foils, indirect characterization, and interior monologue as bonus observations rather than as the mechanisms through which character is built. They'll note "the author uses a foil here" without explaining what that device accomplishes. Teach them the device is the argument — naming it without explaining its function earns partial credit at best.

assignments.name: Foil Comparison Chart to Essay
assignments.body: Students identify a foil pair in a shared text and complete a structured comparison chart (parallel traits, contrasting responses to the same situation, what each contrast reveals about the protagonist). They then convert the chart into a focused analytical paragraph.

assignments.name: Complexity Autopsy
assignments.body: Students select one character from independent reading and write a one-page analysis identifying at least three literary devices the author uses to make that character contradictory yet believable. Each device must be named, quoted, and explained — not just noticed.

assignments.name: Add the Foil
assignments.body: Students write a short scene inserting a new foil character into a passage the class has already read. After writing, they swap with a partner who identifies what the foil reveals about the original character — and whether it works.

assignments.name: Before and After Characterization
assignments.body: Students pull two passages featuring the same character from different points in a text and write a brief analysis of how the author's techniques shift across the two moments and what that shift signals about the character's development.

mastery: Names a specific literary device and explains what it reveals about a character — not just that it is present, but what work it is doing.
Explains a foil relationship by articulating what the contrast between two characters reveals, not just cataloguing their differences.
Distinguishes between character description (what a character is like) and character development (how the author constructs that impression over time).
Uses textual evidence to support claims about internal contradiction or complexity without reducing the character to a simple label.
Maintains the distinction between believability and likability when evaluating how successfully an author has rendered a character.
notes: Vertical alignment: This is the entry point for complex character analysis in the course sequence. English II (110.37(6)(B)) extends the expectation by adding analysis of how character development reflects larger cultural or historical contexts. In English I, the focus stays on the craft of the individual text — what devices the author uses and what they accomplish.

The explicit mention of character foils is a useful anchor for instruction but can produce tunnel vision. Students should encounter the full range of characterization devices (interior monologue, indirect characterization through dialogue and action, juxtaposition, narrative distance) alongside foils.

DOK 3 justification: students must apply understanding of literary devices to explain how specific textual choices produce character complexity — this is strategic thinking across multiple elements, not retrieval or basic skill application.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Non-Linear Plot Development and Compare to Linear Structure
expectation: Analyze non-linear plot development such as flashbacks, foreshadowing, subplots, and parallel plot structures and compare it to linear plot development.
overview: This TEK asks students to do more than label a flashback — they have to understand WHY an author scrambles the sequence and what the reader loses or gains compared to a straight chronological telling. The comparison move is the hard part: students have to hold two versions of the same story in their head simultaneously.
studentFriendly: You can read a story that jumps in time or follows multiple characters at once, explain why the author built it that way, and compare what that structure does to you as a reader versus a story told straight from beginning to end.
questionStems: The author's use of a flashback in paragraphs X–Y MOST likely serves to —
Which detail from the selection BEST foreshadows the outcome in paragraph X?
How does the subplot introduced in paragraph X contribute to the development of the main conflict?
Read paragraphs X–Y. The shift in time MOST likely affects the reader by —
Which statement BEST describes how the parallel plot structure in the selection develops the theme?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, what does the flashback reveal about the narrator that could NOT have been established through linear narration?
The author's decision to begin the story at the end MOST likely creates —
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that the subplot and the main plot share a common theme?
How does the non-linear structure of the selection differ from a linear retelling of the same events?
The foreshadowing in paragraph X contributes to the overall mood of the selection by —

misconceptions.title: A Flashback Is Just "Going Back in Time"
misconceptions.body: Students can spot a flashback but treat it as a pause in the story rather than an active structural choice. Teach them to ask: what does the reader know NOW that they couldn't have understood if the author had revealed this earlier? If the answer is "nothing," the flashback is probably not doing its job — and neither is the student's analysis.

misconceptions.title: Foreshadowing Means Predicting the Plot Twist
misconceptions.body: Students reduce foreshadowing to a guessing game — "I knew the dog was going to die!" — and stop there. Push them deeper: foreshadowing creates dramatic irony, controls tension, and shapes the emotional contract between narrator and reader. The analysis isn't "I was right"; it's "what did the author make me feel while I waited to find out I was right?"

misconceptions.title: Subplots Are Just Distractions
misconceptions.body: Students dismiss subplots as filler — stuff that happens while you wait for the real story. Watch for students who summarize subplots but can't connect them to theme, character development, or the main conflict. A well-analyzed subplot should show how it mirrors, complicates, or reframes the main plot. If they can't make that connection, they haven't analyzed it.

misconceptions.title: Non-Linear Always Means More Sophisticated Than Linear
misconceptions.body: Students assume anything non-chronological is automatically more artistically serious than a straightforward narrative. This flattens the comparison. Teach them: linear structure creates momentum, cause-and-effect clarity, and its own kind of suspense. The comparison question is never "which is better?" but "what does this structure make possible that the other one can't?"

misconceptions.title: Parallel Plots Are Just Two Stories at Once
misconceptions.body: Students see parallel plot lines as simply alternating between characters, like switching TV channels. They miss the structural point: parallel plots run alongside each other to create meaning through contrast or echo. Ask them what would be lost if you collapsed the two plots into one timeline — if their answer is "nothing," they haven't read the parallelism yet.

assignments.name: Structure Swap
assignments.body: Students take a passage with a flashback or in medias res opening and rewrite it in strict chronological order. They then write a paragraph explaining what changed — not just in sequence, but in effect, tension, and character understanding.

assignments.name: Parallel Plot Diagram
assignments.body: Students map two parallel plot lines on a split timeline, marking every point where the two lines intersect, echo, or contrast. Below the diagram, they write a paragraph explaining what the parallel structure argues about the text's theme.

assignments.name: Foreshadowing Evidence Log
assignments.body: As students read a novel with non-linear elements, they maintain a running log of foreshadowing moments with three columns: the detail, where the payoff appears, and what emotional effect the waiting created. Review entries at the midpoint and end of the text.

assignments.name: The Subplot Defense
assignments.body: Students choose a subplot from a shared text and write a short argument explaining why the subplot is necessary to the story — not just "it adds character development" but specifically what the reader would misunderstand about the main conflict without it.

assignments.name: Comparison Paragraph Pair
assignments.body: Students write two analytical paragraphs back to back: one analyzing a non-linear structural choice in a text, and one explaining how a linear version of the same narrative would change the reader's relationship to a central character or idea.

mastery: Identifies non-linear devices (flashback, foreshadowing, subplot, parallel structure) by name AND explains the specific effect of each on meaning, not just on sequence.

Makes a substantive comparison between a non-linear structure and its linear alternative — not "non-linear is more interesting" but a precise claim about what the structure makes possible or impossible.
Connects subplot and parallel plot lines to theme or central conflict rather than summarizing them in isolation.
Explains foreshadowing in terms of tension and dramatic irony, not just accurate prediction.
Produces written analysis that uses textual evidence to support structural claims, not just to retell events.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I introduces the conceptual comparison between linear and non-linear structure. English II (110.37(6)(C)) extends this by asking students to analyze the effects of non-linear structure more independently and across more complex texts. The ramp is from recognition + labeled comparison to autonomous structural analysis.

DOK 3 justification: students must hold the structural logic of two narrative arrangements simultaneously and evaluate what each makes possible — this is strategic thinking, not recall or skill application alone.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is tested through prose fiction passages in which structural choices are embedded. Stems address how specific passages function within the whole text's organization. The stems above mirror STAAR's phrasing for author's craft and structural analysis questions.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze How Setting Influences Theme
expectation: Analyze how the setting influences the theme.
overview: Setting is not backdrop — it's argument. This TEK asks students to trace the causal relationship between where and when a story happens and what the story is ultimately claiming about human experience. The move is analytical, not descriptive: not "the setting is a crumbling plantation" but "the crumbling plantation makes the theme of inevitable decline legible."
studentFriendly: You can read a story and explain why it could not make its point if it happened somewhere else — showing exactly how the time and place shape what the author is saying about life.
questionStems: Read paragraph X. The setting described in this paragraph MOST likely contributes to the theme by —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which statement BEST describes how the setting influences the theme of the story?
The author's description of the setting in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to develop which theme?
Which detail from the selection BEST supports the inference that the setting shapes the story's central message?
How does the shift in setting between paragraphs X and Y contribute to the development of the theme?
The setting in paragraph X MOST nearly suggests that the author's theme concerns —
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates the connection between the physical environment and the story's theme?
Based on the details in paragraphs X–Y, what theme does the setting MOST likely reinforce?
Read paragraph X. The contrast between the two settings described here MOST likely develops which theme?
Which statement MOST accurately describes how the time period of the story contributes to its central theme?

misconceptions.title: Setting Is Just Description to Skip Over
misconceptions.body: Students treat setting paragraphs as filler — they skim to get back to plot. Teach them that every intentional detail of place, weather, season, and era is doing thematic work. When a student says "I skipped the part about the landscape," that's the part they needed most. Train them to slow down precisely where the author slows down.

misconceptions.title: Theme Is a Topic, Not a Claim
misconceptions.body: Students say "the theme is loneliness" and then try to connect setting to a noun. Push them to articulate theme as a full claim — "isolation destroys identity" or "survival requires betrayal" — before they can meaningfully analyze how setting supports it. A noun cannot be influenced by a setting; a claim can.

misconceptions.title: Any Setting Could Work for Any Theme
misconceptions.body: Students assume setting is interchangeable — that the story's message would survive a transplant to a different time or place. Counter this directly: ask them to move the setting somewhere else and watch the theme collapse or shift. If it doesn't change, the student hasn't found the real theme yet. The transplant test is diagnostic.

misconceptions.title: Influence Only Goes One Direction
misconceptions.body: Students understand that setting affects characters, but they miss how setting encodes theme independently of character action. A decaying house means something even before anyone walks through the door. Teach them that authors use setting as a direct vehicle for theme — the environment itself can make an argument without a character voicing it.

misconceptions.title: Symbolic Setting Means Every Detail Is a Symbol
misconceptions.body: Once students learn that setting can be symbolic, they over-apply it — every window is freedom, every storm is conflict, every river is change. Watch for students listing symbols without connecting them to a specific, defensible claim about the theme. Rigor means choosing the details that actually matter and explaining the mechanism, not cataloguing everything that could theoretically mean something.

assignments.name: Setting Transplant
assignments.body: Students take a key scene and rewrite the setting — different region, era, or climate — then write a paragraph explaining how the theme shifts or breaks. The comparison does the analytical work.

assignments.name: Setting-to-Theme Evidence Chain
assignments.body: Students complete a three-column chart: specific setting detail | what it creates (mood, constraint, possibility) | how that pushes toward the central theme. Five rows minimum. The middle column is where the analysis lives.

assignments.name: Annotated Close Read
assignments.body: Students annotate a two-page passage for setting details only, then write a half-page explaining what those details, taken together, are arguing thematically. They must quote at least three details and use the word "because" to force a causal claim.

assignments.name: Comparative Setting
assignments.body: Students read two short texts with contrasting settings but related themes (e.g., confinement in an urban apartment vs. a rural farmhouse). They argue in writing whether the difference in setting produces a different version of the same theme or a genuinely different claim.

mastery: States the theme as a complete claim, not a one-word topic, before attempting to connect it to setting.
Identifies specific setting details — not general atmosphere — and explains the mechanism by which each detail presses toward the theme.
Distinguishes between setting that creates mood and setting that drives thematic argument, handling both without collapsing one into the other.
Can articulate why the story's theme would be weakened or altered if the setting changed — the transplant test passes.
Produces written analysis with evidence, claim, and causal reasoning rather than a list of observations.
notes: Vertical entry point: this is English I's first formal encounter with setting-theme causality. In middle school (Grade 8, 110.32), students identify theme and analyze how it is conveyed through setting, plot, and characters — but the analytical demand is identification, not causal argument. English I raises the floor: students must explain the mechanism, not just name the connection.

Vertical next: English II (110.37(6)(D)) extends this to analyzing how setting influences both theme AND plot structure — adding a second analytical target and requiring students to track how those two interact.

DOK 3 justification: students must reason beyond the text — applying understanding of theme and setting to construct a causal argument that is not stated explicitly by the author. Strategic thinking required.

No direct STAAR analog as a standalone item; STAAR typically embeds setting-theme connections inside broader literary analysis questions (author's craft, inference). Stems above reflect the phrasing those items use. Note this when using stems for test prep.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Read and Respond to American, British, and World Literature
expectation: Read and respond to American, British, and world literature.
overview: This is the breadth mandate — the TEK that keeps English I from becoming a single-culture reading list. It establishes that students will encounter texts from multiple literary traditions and produce responses that show genuine engagement with those texts, not just plot recall.
studentFriendly: You read literature from American, British, and world traditions — not just one country, not just one era — and you respond to those texts in ways that show you've actually thought about what they're doing.
questionStems: Which statement BEST describes the central idea developed across the selection?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the reader can MOST likely infer that the narrator —
The author's use of [detail/event] in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that the speaker values —
How does the setting introduced in paragraph X contribute to the development of the theme?
The author MOST likely includes the description in paragraph X to —
Based on the selection, with which statement would the author MOST likely agree?
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates a shift in the narrator's perspective?
The events in paragraphs X–Y MOST likely suggest that —
Which detail from the selection BEST supports the inference that the conflict in this text is primarily —

misconceptions.title: "World Literature" Means an Occasional International Story
misconceptions.body: Teachers sometimes treat this TEK as a checkbox — one García Márquez story satisfies "world," and everything else defaults to canonical British and American texts. Teach them that the strand requires genuine distribution across traditions, not tokenism. Push toward texts from African, South Asian, East Asian, and Latin American literary traditions as first-tier course reading, not bonus material.

misconceptions.title: Responding Is the Same as Summarizing
misconceptions.body: When students "respond" to a text, they frequently retell it. Summary is not response — it's the floor, not the ceiling. Watch for written responses that describe what happened without making any claim about why it matters, what it does, or how it works. Reframe the task: responding means taking a position, making an inference, tracing a pattern, or drawing a connection — not reporting back the plot.

misconceptions.title: Students Can't Connect Across Cultural Distance
misconceptions.body: Students often claim a text "doesn't apply to them" when it's set far from their experience. That's a reading problem masquerading as a preference. Teach them that literary response requires entering a text on its own terms before evaluating it on theirs. The distance between reader and text is a feature — productive friction that reveals what the student assumed was universal.

misconceptions.title: American and British Are Interchangeable
misconceptions.body: Students and teachers sometimes treat "American and British" as one tradition — shared language, roughly shared forms. The literatures are distinct in history, class consciousness, irony register, and political context. A student reading Steinbeck and Dickens in the same unit should be able to name the difference, not blend them into a generic "Western canon."

misconceptions.title: Response Quality Is About Length
misconceptions.body: Students believe a longer response is a better response. Watch for responses that pad argument with plot summary to hit a word count. Mastery in response is about precision: a claim, textual evidence, and reasoning that connects them. One tight paragraph beats three pages of retelling. Teach students to measure responses by whether the reasoning holds, not whether the page is full.

assignments.name: Three Traditions, Three Responses
assignments.body: Over a unit, students read one American, one British, and one world text — each short enough for close reading (short story, essay excerpt, poem). After each, students write a focused 1-paragraph response making a claim about how the text works. At the end of the unit, they write a 1-page reflection comparing what was similar and what was different across the three traditions.

assignments.name: Same Theme, Different World
assignments.body: Select a universal theme (exile, grief, loyalty, justice). Assign three texts from different national traditions that address it. Students write a comparative response: where do the texts agree about what the theme means, and where do they diverge? Requires students to treat cultural context as an interpretive lens, not background noise.

assignments.name: Response Sharpening Workshop
assignments.body: Students write a first-draft response to a world literature text, then trade with a partner who must identify: (a) the claim, (b) the evidence, (c) the reasoning. If any of the three is missing, the response goes back for revision. This makes the structure of response explicit without reducing it to a formula.

mastery: Engages with texts from all three named traditions across the course without defaulting exclusively to American or British canonical texts.
Produces responses that open with a claim, use specific textual evidence, and include reasoning that connects the two — not summaries in disguise.
Names specific features of a text — narrator, structure, imagery, conflict — when explaining how the text works, rather than only reporting what happened.
Approaches culturally distant texts with genuine inquiry rather than dismissal, entering the text's context before judging from their own.
Distinguishes between American and British literary traditions at the level of voice, historical context, and cultural assumptions — not just geography.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I is the entry point for this three-tradition mandate. English II (110.37(7)(A)) carries the same expectation forward with increased text complexity and more sophisticated response demands. Students arriving in English I may have had geographically narrow reading lists in middle school — this TEK is the institutional correction.

DOK 3 justification: reading and responding at the level expected requires students to synthesize meaning across text and context, make inferences, and support claims with evidence — not recall or apply a procedure.

STAAR testing note: this TEK is tested through literary and informational text passages that reflect diverse authorial origins; stems will not identify the tradition by name but will probe the comprehension and analytical skills the TEK builds. The question stems above mirror how STAAR would assess those skills regardless of source tradition.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Structure, Prosody, and Graphic Elements in Poetry
expectation: Analyze the structure, prosody, and graphic elements such as line length and word position in poems across a variety of poetic forms.
overview: Poetry is not just content — it is a built object, and English I students have to learn to read the building. This TEK asks students to move past "what is the poem about" and into the mechanics: how stanza breaks, line length, metrical patterns, and the physical placement of words on the page are themselves meaning-making choices.
studentFriendly: You can look at a poem and explain not just what it says, but how the way it is built — the line breaks, the rhythm, the white space, the shape — is doing work to shape what you feel and understand.
questionStems: Read lines X–Y. The poet's choice to end the line after [word] MOST likely creates which effect?
How does the structure of the poem in stanzas X–Y contribute to the development of the speaker's central idea?
The poet's use of [metrical pattern / rhythm] in lines X–Y MOST nearly suggests —
Which line from the poem BEST illustrates how word position is used to emphasize the speaker's tone?
Based on the poem's structure, the shift between stanza X and stanza Y MOST likely indicates —
The poet's use of [short/long] line lengths in lines X–Y is MOST likely intended to —
How does the graphic arrangement of lines X–Y contribute to the poem's overall meaning?
Which of the following BEST describes the effect of the white space in lines X–Y?
The prosody of lines X–Y — including [meter/rhythm/sound pattern] — MOST likely reinforces which idea?
Compared to the structure of stanza X, the structure of stanza Y suggests that the speaker —

misconceptions.title: Line Breaks Are Just Where the Poet Ran Out of Room
misconceptions.body: Students treat line breaks as arbitrary — as though the poem would say the same thing if printed as a paragraph. Teach them that every break is a decision: it controls pace, creates emphasis, withholds or delivers information. Have them reformat a stanza into prose and read both aloud. The poem's intentions become audible only when the prose version flattens them.

misconceptions.title: Prosody Means Identifying Iambic Pentameter and Nothing Else
misconceptions.body: Students learn one metrical pattern and declare the work done. Push them deeper: what does it mean when the meter breaks? Where does the poem deviate from its own pattern, and why does it matter at that exact moment? The deviation is often the argument. Teach them to hunt the irregularity first, then map the regularity around it.

misconceptions.title: Form Is Decoration, Not Argument
misconceptions.body: Students frequently separate a poem's "meaning" from its "form" as though the content were the real poem and the sonnet structure were a fancy frame. Watch for essays that discuss theme in one paragraph and then note "the poem is a sonnet" in a disconnected aside. Reframe: the form IS the argument. A villanelle repeats because its speaker cannot stop repeating. A fragmented free verse poem is fragmented because the speaker is.

misconceptions.title: Graphic Elements Only Matter in Visual Poetry
misconceptions.body: When students encounter a conventionally typeset poem, they assume graphic elements are irrelevant — "that's only for concrete poetry." Teach them that every poem has graphic choices: indentation levels, stanza spacing, capitalization at the start of lines, long versus short lines creating visual rhythm on the page. The page is a canvas even when the poem looks traditional.

misconceptions.title: "Analyzing Poetic Form" Means Labeling It
misconceptions.body: Students will confidently write "this is a sonnet with three quatrains and a couplet" and stop there as though naming the form completes the analysis. The label is only the entry point. Push them: what does the couplet do at the end? Does it resolve or undercut? Why did the poet choose fourteen lines for this particular argument and not sixteen? Form analysis without interpretive consequence is not analysis — it is taxonomy.

assignments.name: Side-by-Side Reformatting
assignments.body: Students take a short poem and reformat it three ways: as prose, with different line breaks, and with the original line breaks. They write a paragraph explaining what changes in tone, pace, or emphasis between each version and why the original line breaks serve the poem best — or arguing that one of their alternatives is actually stronger.

assignments.name: Deviation Hunt
assignments.body: Students are given a poem with a dominant metrical pattern. Their job is to mark every line that breaks the pattern and write one sentence for each deviation explaining what the disruption does at that moment in the poem. Class discusses whether the deviations are accidents or arguments.

assignments.name: Across-Form Comparison
assignments.body: Students read three poems across three different forms (e.g., a Shakespearean sonnet, a free verse lyric, a haiku sequence). They complete a graphic organizer tracking structure, line length, prosodic patterns, and graphic elements for each, then write a paragraph explaining how form shapes meaning differently in each one.

assignments.name: Build-Your-Own Stanza
assignments.body: Students write two stanzas on the same subject: one in strict form (fixed line length, metrical pattern) and one in free verse. They annotate both, explaining the structural choices they made and how each version changes what the poem does. This exercise makes form decisions legible by forcing students to make them.

mastery: Identifies specific structural features — stanza length, line break placement, indentation, white space — and connects each to an interpretive claim about the poem's meaning or effect.
Reads prosodic patterns (meter, rhythm, sound repetition) and explains what shifts or irregularities signal at the moment they occur.
Compares how the same subject or emotion is handled differently across two or more poetic forms, naming structural reasons for the difference.
Writes analysis that treats form as argument, not decoration — every formal observation is attached to an interpretive consequence.
Recognizes graphic elements (word position, line length variation, white space) in conventionally typeset poems, not only in visual or concrete poetry.
notes: Vertical alignment: This is the first formal exposure to prosody and graphic elements as analytical targets. English II (110.37(7)(B)) will ask students to evaluate the effect of these choices across a wider range of poetic traditions and cultural contexts — English I builds the observational vocabulary that evaluation requires.

DOK 3 justification: Students must apply knowledge of poetic form to interpret unfamiliar poems and reason about how structural choices produce meaning — not merely recall definitions or label features.
The substrand designation "Informational & Argumentative" is the TEA filing for Standard 7 broadly; poetry sits here in the TEKS strand structure despite being a literary genre.

STAAR analog note: Poetry analysis appears on STAAR as a literary text passage. Stems for this entry are written to match STAAR's passage-based format with paragraph/line references, as students will encounter this TEK tested through paired or standalone poem passages.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze the Function of Dramatic Conventions
expectation: Analyze the function of dramatic conventions such as asides, soliloquies, dramatic irony, and satire.
overview: This TEK asks students to go past naming dramatic conventions and explain what each one is doing — what gap in information it creates or closes, what effect it produces on the audience, and why the playwright chose it at that moment. The real skill is functional analysis, not identification.
studentFriendly: You can read a scene and explain exactly why the playwright has a character whisper to the audience, talk to themselves, or say the opposite of what the audience knows to be true — and what each of those choices is supposed to make the audience feel or understand.
questionStems: The aside in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Read the soliloquy in paragraphs X–Y. What does the soliloquy reveal about the character that the other characters do not know?
The dramatic irony in paragraphs X–Y is BEST created by —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the playwright MOST likely uses satire to —
Which statement BEST describes the function of the aside in paragraph X?
How does the soliloquy in paragraph X contribute to the development of the central conflict?
The playwright's use of dramatic irony in paragraphs X–Y MOST likely creates a sense of —
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the inference that the character's aside is intended to build suspense?
The satirical tone in paragraphs X–Y MOST likely suggests that the playwright —
Based on the soliloquy in paragraph X, what can the reader MOST likely conclude about the character's true motivation?

misconceptions.title: Naming the Convention IS the Analysis
misconceptions.body: Students write "this is an aside" and stop. They treat identification as the finish line when identification is just the starting line. Teach them that every convention answer requires a second sentence: what does it DO to the audience's relationship with the character or situation? Push them to ask "so what?" every time they name a device.

misconceptions.title: Dramatic Irony Is Just "They Don't Know What We Know"
misconceptions.body: Students recite the definition without analyzing the effect. The point of dramatic irony is the emotional or thematic work it does — tension, dread, comedy, sympathy. Watch for students who describe the information gap but never name what that gap makes the audience feel or understand. Push them: knowing something a character doesn't is not the payoff — the payoff is what that knowledge makes you feel while watching them.

misconceptions.title: Soliloquies Are Just Plot Summaries
misconceptions.body: Students treat soliloquies as the playwright's way of catching the audience up on events. In fact, soliloquies are the playwright's most direct access to interiority — they reveal what a character actually believes, fears, or plans. Teach them: if the soliloquy could be cut without losing story information, it's doing something else — probably revealing character. That's the thing to analyze.

misconceptions.title: Satire Requires Explicit Jokes
misconceptions.body: Students think satire only counts when there's obvious comedy. They miss the sharper, quieter satirical moments where a playwright frames a situation to expose an institution's absurdity without a punchline in sight. Teach them that satire is a stance toward a target, not a comedy bit — the tone of mockery can be cold and dry, and that's often when it lands hardest.

misconceptions.title: Asides Are Just Stage Directions
misconceptions.body: Students confuse asides with stage directions or narration, treating them as neutral informational interruptions. Push them to see that an aside is a trust transaction — the character steps out of the fictional world to speak directly to the audience, which creates a conspiratorial relationship. Ask: who is included? Who is excluded? What does that do to the audience's loyalty in the scene?

assignments.name: Convention Autopsy
assignments.body: Students select one scene from a class text and write a paragraph on each of three conventions present (or potentially present — they must argue for the function even if the convention is subtle). Each paragraph names the convention, locates it in the text, and explains its function in one specific claim.

assignments.name: Rewrite Without the Convention
assignments.body: Students take a scene that relies heavily on one dramatic convention (e.g., a scene built on dramatic irony) and rewrite it without that convention. They then write a short reflection: what did the scene lose? What did the audience's experience change?

assignments.name: Cross-Play Convention Map
assignments.body: Students read brief excerpts from two plays and map which dramatic conventions appear in each. They then write a comparative paragraph: do the plays use the same conventions for the same purposes, or does one playwright use an aside where another uses dramatic irony — and why might that be?

assignments.name: Satire Identification Panel
assignments.body: Present three short excerpts — one clearly satirical, one ambiguous, one not satirical. Students must argue in writing for which is which and explain how they know, citing the playwright's specific choices as evidence.

mastery: Identifies a dramatic convention in a text and names its specific location without being prompted.
Explains the function of the convention — what it does to the audience's knowledge, emotion, or relationship with the character — in a specific, arguable claim.
Distinguishes between what a convention reveals and what it conceals, and names the effect of that gap.
Analyzes satirical tone as a stance toward a target rather than simply noting the presence of humor.
Uses textual evidence to support functional claims about dramatic conventions rather than resting on definition alone.
notes: This TEK sits under "Informational & Argumentative" in the TEKS strand mapping, but the conventions it names (asides, soliloquies, dramatic irony, satire) are all drawn from drama — likely a TEA classification artifact. Teach and assess it through dramatic texts; the strand label should not redirect instruction toward nonfiction.

Vertical alignment: English I introduces functional analysis of these four conventions. English II (110.37(7)(C)) likely extends this to analyzing how conventions interact across a full work or comparing their use across dramatic traditions. Ground the English I work in single-scene analysis before scaling up.

DOK 3 justification: students must use textual evidence to construct a strategic analysis of how a convention functions — not just recall definitions or identify examples, but explain purpose and effect within a specific dramatic context.
STAAR analog note: this TEK is tested through prose-drama excerpts on STAAR; stems are written to mirror STAAR's author's-craft question framing applied to dramatic texts.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Characteristics and Structural Elements of Informational Texts
expectation: Analyze characteristics and structural elements of informational texts such as clear thesis, relevant supporting evidence, pertinent examples, and conclusion.
overview: This is the foundational move for reading nonfiction: students learn to name the parts of an informational text and explain how those parts work together. The real skill is not labeling — it is recognizing whether the thesis is actually clear, whether the evidence actually supports it, and whether the conclusion actually closes the argument or just restates the introduction.
studentFriendly: You can read any article, essay, or report and identify how it is built — where the author makes their claim, what evidence they bring in, which examples they chose and why, and whether the ending actually lands.
questionStems: Which sentence from paragraph X BEST states the author's thesis?
Which detail from the selection MOST effectively supports the author's central claim?
Based on the selection, which conclusion about the author's argument is MOST supported?
The example in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST functions as the conclusion of the author's argument?
How does the evidence in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the development of the author's thesis?
Which of the following BEST describes the organizational structure of the selection?
The author includes the example of [specific detail] in paragraph X MOST likely to —
Which sentence from the selection provides the MOST relevant supporting evidence for the author's claim in paragraph X?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the reader can infer that the author believes —

misconceptions.title: The First Sentence Is Always the Thesis
misconceptions.body: Students scan the opening line, declare it the thesis, and move on. Many professional writers bury the claim, open with anecdote, or withhold the central argument until the second paragraph or later. Teach them to ask: what is the ONE claim every paragraph in this text is working to prove? That is the thesis — wherever it sits.

misconceptions.title: "Supporting Evidence" Means Any Detail from the Text
misconceptions.body: Students treat any sentence they did not highlight as evidence for the one they did. Evidence is specifically information the author uses to demonstrate the claim is true — data, expert testimony, documented examples. Watch for students who cite emotional appeals or vivid descriptions as "evidence" when those moves are doing rhetorical work, not logical proof.

misconceptions.title: Examples and Evidence Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students collapse these two categories. Evidence establishes that something is true; examples illustrate what that truth looks like in practice. Push them to distinguish: a statistic about dropout rates is evidence; the story of one student who dropped out is an example. Both matter, but they work differently, and a text built entirely on examples with no evidence is structurally weak.

misconceptions.title: The Conclusion Just Restates the Introduction
misconceptions.body: Students call any paragraph that echoes earlier language the "conclusion" — and they accept that as sufficient. Teach them that a strong conclusion does more: it shows what the argument means, what follows from it, or what the reader should now think or do. A conclusion that only restates is a missed opportunity. When grading, ask: does this ending add anything — or is it the introduction wearing a hat?

misconceptions.title: If the Text Has These Parts, It Is Well-Written
misconceptions.body: Students mistake presence for quality. They find a thesis, find evidence, find a conclusion — and declare the text effective. The harder move is evaluating: Is the thesis actually clear? Is the evidence actually relevant? Are the examples actually pertinent, or are they distracting? DOK 3 lives here: not just identifying the parts, but judging whether they are doing their jobs.

assignments.name: Structural Dissection
assignments.body: Students receive a two-page informational article stripped of paragraph numbers and annotate it: bracket the thesis, underline each piece of supporting evidence, circle each example, and box the conclusion. Then they write one sentence evaluating whether each structural element is doing its job.

assignments.name: Thesis Quality Scale
assignments.body: Collect five thesis sentences from different informational texts (ranging from vague to precise). Students rank them 1–5 for clarity and specificity, write a brief justification for each ranking, and then rewrite the two weakest ones.

assignments.name: Evidence vs. Example Sort
assignments.body: Give students a list of 12 passages pulled from informational texts. They sort them into three columns: evidence, example, or neither. Debrief as a class on the borderline cases — those carry the real discussion.

assignments.name: Rebuild the Conclusion
assignments.body: Find an informational text with a weak, restatement-only conclusion. Students read the full article, then write an improved conclusion that adds genuine closure. Compare drafts and discuss what each one does that the original did not.

assignments.name: Structural Comparison
assignments.body: Students read two short informational texts on the same topic. They diagram the structure of each (thesis, evidence, examples, conclusion) and write a paragraph explaining which text is better constructed and why — with specific textual references.

mastery: Locates the thesis regardless of its position in the text and distinguishes it from the topic sentence of a single paragraph.
Differentiates supporting evidence (data, documented facts, expert sources) from illustrative examples and explains how each functions in context.
Evaluates whether the thesis, evidence, examples, and conclusion are performing their structural roles — not just identifies that they exist.
Cites specific passages when explaining how structural elements contribute to the author's argument.
Reads unfamiliar informational texts and maps their structure without a scaffold, identifying where the argument is strong and where it is weak.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I establishes the baseline — students learn to name and evaluate the structural parts of informational texts. English II (110.37(7)(D)(i)) extends this by asking students to analyze how those parts interact and whether they are logically sufficient. The ramp is from identification and basic evaluation to sustained logical analysis.

DOK 3 justification: Mere identification of thesis, evidence, examples, and conclusion is DOK 1–2. The expectation in English I requires students to analyze whether those elements are clear and relevant — that judgment, supported by textual evidence, pushes the task to DOK 3.

This TEK is regularly tested on STAAR through paired selections and standalone informational passages. Stems targeting "which sentence BEST states the thesis" and "how does the evidence in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the author's claim" directly mirror released STAAR phrasing.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Multiple Organizational Patterns Within Informational Texts
expectation: Analyze characteristics and structural elements of informational texts such as multiple organizational patterns within a text to develop the thesis.
overview: A sophisticated informational text rarely commits to one structure — it layers cause-effect inside a compare-contrast frame, or embeds a chronological narrative inside a problem-solution argument. This TEK asks students to see how those patterns stack and why an author deploys each one in service of a thesis, not just to identify which category the text belongs to.
