studentFriendly: You can read a complex article or essay and explain how the author switches between organizational patterns — like moving from cause-and-effect to comparison — to build and support their central argument.
questionStems: Which organizational pattern BEST describes the structure of paragraphs X–Y?
The author MOST likely shifts from a cause-and-effect structure in paragraph X to a compare-and-contrast structure in paragraph Y in order to —
How does the organizational pattern in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the development of the author's thesis?
Which of the following BEST describes how the author organizes the central argument in the selection?
Read this sentence from paragraph X: [sentence]. The author MOST likely uses a problem-solution structure here to —
Based on the selection, the author uses chronological order in paragraphs X–Y MOST likely to —
How do the two organizational patterns in paragraphs X–Y and Z–W work together to support the author's claim?
Which sentence from the selection BEST indicates a shift from one organizational pattern to another?
The author's use of [pattern] in paragraph X suggests that the thesis is MOST nearly concerned with —
Based on the selection, which statement BEST describes how the author's organizational choices develop the central argument?

misconceptions.title: A Text Has One Structure, Full Stop
misconceptions.body: Students learn the six organizational patterns as a classification game — read the text, pick a category, done. Watch for students who label the whole piece "cause and effect" because the first two paragraphs feel that way and then stop looking. Teach them that real informational writing shifts patterns at the paragraph level, and their job is to track those shifts and explain WHY the author made them.

misconceptions.title: Organizational Patterns Exist Independently of the Thesis
misconceptions.body: Students can name a pattern without connecting it to the argument it's serving. They'll write "the author uses compare and contrast" as if that's analysis. Push them to finish the sentence: compare and contrast in order to do WHAT to the thesis? The structure is never decorative — it's always doing argumentative work.

misconceptions.title: Transition Words Are the Pattern
misconceptions.body: Students are trained to look for signal words — "however," "as a result," "first, second, third" — and use them as a substitute for structural thinking. A text can use "however" without being organized comparatively; a text can be organized chronologically without a single "first" or "then." Teach them to look at what the paragraphs are actually DOING, not just what conjunctions appear.

misconceptions.title: Switching Patterns Means the Writing Is Weak
misconceptions.body: When students encounter a text that changes structural strategies mid-argument, they sometimes read it as inconsistency or poor organization. Reframe this hard: skilled writers use multiple patterns because no single structure can carry a complex thesis alone. The shift is the move — help students see it as craft, not confusion.

misconceptions.title: Identifying the Pattern Is the Same as Analyzing It
misconceptions.body: Students believe that once they've named the pattern, they've met the standard. Mastery here means explaining how the pattern functions: how does the shift from problem-solution in paragraph 3 to cause-and-effect in paragraph 5 deepen or complicate the thesis? Push students past labeling into explanation of rhetorical purpose.

assignments.name: Pattern Map
assignments.body: Students read a 4–6 paragraph informational text and produce a paragraph-by-paragraph structural map — labeling the organizational pattern of each section and writing one sentence explaining how that pattern serves the thesis. Submitted as an annotated diagram or table.

assignments.name: Two Texts, One Strategy
assignments.body: Students compare two articles on the same topic and identify where each author switches organizational patterns. They write a short analysis explaining whose structural choices more effectively develop their respective thesis — and why.

assignments.name: Build the Argument Backwards
assignments.body: Give students a thesis and three distinct organizational patterns. Students construct a plan (not a full essay) showing how each pattern would contribute a different kind of support. Then discuss which pattern order is most rhetorically effective and why.

assignments.name: Passage Reconstruction
assignments.body: Cut a multi-pattern informational text into labeled paragraphs and scramble the order. Students reconstruct the text, defend their sequencing, and identify which paragraph signals the pattern shift most clearly.

mastery: Identifies two or more distinct organizational patterns within a single informational text, locating the boundaries of each.
Explains how each organizational pattern serves the author's thesis — not just names it, but articulates its argumentative function.
Traces the logic of the author's pattern shifts: why does the structure change when it does, and what does that accomplish?
Avoids reducing structural analysis to transition-word hunting; grounds analysis in paragraph-level behavior.
Produces written analysis that moves from naming a pattern to explaining its contribution to the text's central argument.
notes: This is a Roman-numeral sub-expectation under 7(D), which covers "characteristics and structural elements of informational texts." The full standard lists multiple examples; 7(D)(ii) isolates the multi-pattern structure element — students must move beyond single-pattern classification into analysis of how layered structures build a thesis.

Vertical: English I students identify and analyze multiple patterns; English II (110.37(7)(D)(ii)) extends this to evaluating the effectiveness of those choices. The ramp is from "how does this work?" to "how well does this work?"

DOK 3 justification: recognizing a pattern is DOK 1–2; explaining how two or more patterns interact to develop a thesis requires strategic thinking and text-based reasoning across multiple paragraphs — DOK 3.

No direct STAAR analog as a standalone item, but organizational pattern questions appear routinely on STAAR's informational reading passages. Stems above mirror the embed-in-passage format STAAR uses rather than treating structure as a standalone vocabulary test.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Characteristics and Structural Elements of Argumentative Texts
expectation: Analyze characteristics and structural elements of argumentative texts such as clear arguable claim, appeals, and convincing conclusion.
overview: This is the student's first formal encounter with argumentative texts as objects of analysis rather than just persuasion to agree with or resist. The work is structural — identifying the claim, locating the appeals, and evaluating whether the conclusion earns its landing — not about whether the argument "worked" on the reader emotionally.
studentFriendly: You can read a persuasive article, editorial, or speech and identify exactly how the writer built their argument — where the claim is, what kinds of evidence and appeals they used, and whether the conclusion actually closes the case.
questionStems: Which sentence from the selection MOST clearly states the author's central claim?
The author's use of [statistic/expert opinion/anecdote] in paragraph X is BEST described as an appeal to —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which statement BEST describes how the author develops the argument?
The author MOST likely includes the information in paragraph X to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that the author's argument is convincing?
The author's conclusion in paragraph X is effective MOST likely because it —
Read this sentence from paragraph X. The author's claim is BEST supported by which of the following pieces of evidence from the selection?
Based on the structure of the selection, the author MOST likely organizes the argument in order to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates the author's use of an emotional appeal?
The author includes paragraph X MOST likely to —

misconceptions.title: The Claim Is Wherever the Writer States Their Opinion
misconceptions.body: Students hunt for opinions and label the first one they find as "the claim," missing the difference between a claim and a passing value judgment. Teach them that a claim is an arguable assertion the rest of the text is designed to prove — it controls what evidence appears and why. A sentence expressing a preference doesn't become a claim just because someone disagreed with it. Watch for students circling the introduction's first opinionated sentence and moving on without checking whether the text is actually organized to support it.

misconceptions.title: Appeals Are Just "Emotional Tricks"
misconceptions.body: Students learn ethos, pathos, logos as labels and collapse them into "the writer was trying to manipulate the reader." Push them deeper: appeals are legitimate architectural tools — logos structures the reasoning, ethos establishes why the source deserves to be heard, pathos connects the stakes to a human reality. Teach them that a persuasive text without emotional appeal is often just dry and unconvincing, not more rational. The question is whether the appeal is proportionate and honest, not whether it exists.

misconceptions.title: A Strong Conclusion Just Restates the Thesis
misconceptions.body: Students think "convincing conclusion" means summarizing what the writer already said. The more sophisticated move is closure that either escalates the stakes, widens the frame, or calls the reader to action — it moves the argument forward rather than reversing over it. Watch for students evaluating conclusions as strong because they "mentioned the claim again." Teach them to ask: does this ending make me feel like the case is closed, or just finished?

misconceptions.title: If the Text Has Paragraphs and a Topic, It's Argumentative
misconceptions.body: Students over-apply the genre label. An explanatory article with a thesis isn't automatically an argument. Teach them the diagnostic question: is the writer trying to change what I believe, or just inform me? The claim must be arguable — someone must reasonably be able to disagree with it — and the text must be organized to overcome that disagreement. An article explaining how climate works is not the same genre as one arguing the government must act on climate.

misconceptions.title: Structural Analysis Means Listing Parts, Not Reading Relationships
misconceptions.body: Students produce "the author has a claim, then evidence, then a conclusion" as their analysis and stop there. That's inventory, not analysis. Teach them the follow-up move: how does the placement of each appeal affect the reader's progress through the argument? Why does a credibility appeal usually come early? What happens when emotional evidence arrives before the logical case is made? Structure is sequence, and sequence is a choice.

assignments.name: Claim Surgery
assignments.body: Give students three editorials on the same topic — one with a clear arguable claim, one with a vague claim, and one with no real claim at all. Students identify (or note the absence of) each claim, then rank the texts by argumentative strength and defend their ranking in a paragraph.

assignments.name: Appeals Audit
assignments.body: Students annotate a full argumentative text, labeling every appeal (logos, pathos, ethos) in the margins with a one-phrase justification. After annotation, they write a two-sentence summary: which appeal does the most structural work in this argument, and why?

assignments.name: Broken Conclusion Rewrite
assignments.body: Provide a strong argumentative text with a weak or abrupt conclusion removed and replaced by a placeholder. Students write a replacement conclusion, then compare their version to the original. Class discusses: what choices did they make? What did the author do differently?

assignments.name: Genre Sort
assignments.body: Give students six short texts — three argumentative, three informational. Students sort them and write one sentence per text justifying the placement. Debrief focuses on the arguable-claim test and structural evidence of intent.

assignments.name: Structure Map
assignments.body: Students diagram a full argumentative text — claim, each piece of supporting evidence, type of appeal, and conclusion — in a visual map. They then write a paragraph evaluating whether the structure is logically sound or whether any section is out of place.

mastery: Identifies the arguable central claim and distinguishes it from background information or passing opinions.
Labels each appeal by type (logos, pathos, ethos) and explains the function the appeal performs in the argument's structure, not just its presence.
Evaluates the conclusion by articulating what it accomplishes beyond restating the claim.
Distinguishes argumentative texts from informational texts using structural and intentional criteria, not surface features.
Explains how the sequence of elements — where the claim appears, when each appeal is introduced — affects the argument's persuasive force.
notes: This is the opening entry for argumentative analysis in English I — students arrive with prior knowledge of persuasion as a social act (being convinced or not) but little formal vocabulary for structural critique. The skill ramps in English II to include more nuanced evaluation of reasoning and source credibility, and in English III to full rhetorical critique including diction and syntax.
DOK 3 justification: students must apply the structural frame (claim / appeals / conclusion) to unfamiliar texts and explain relationships among elements — not just retrieve definitions.

STAAR analog note: this TEK maps directly to argumentative text analysis passages on STAAR, particularly stems targeting how the author develops an argument, the function of specific paragraphs, and whether evidence supports the central claim.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Evidence and Counterargument Structures in Argumentative Texts
expectation: Analyze characteristics and structural elements of argumentative texts, including various types of evidence and treatment of counterarguments, including concessions and rebuttals.
overview: Argumentative reading is where students learn that a text isn't just making a claim — it's staging a fight. The structural moves that matter here are how writers choose their evidence and how they handle the opposition: do they acknowledge it, concede part of it, or knock it down? English I is the entry point for that analysis.
studentFriendly: You can read an argumentative text and explain not just what the writer claims, but what kinds of evidence they use to back it up and how they deal with the people who disagree with them.
questionStems: Which sentence from the passage BEST represents the author's use of a concession?
The author includes paragraph X MOST likely to —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which type of evidence does the author rely on MOST to support the central argument?
Which sentence from the selection BEST functions as a rebuttal to an opposing viewpoint?
The author's use of statistical evidence in paragraph X serves MOST likely to —
How does the author's treatment of the counterargument in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the overall effectiveness of the argument?
Which statement BEST describes how the author responds to the opposing position introduced in paragraph X?
The author includes the anecdote in paragraph X MOST likely to —
Based on the selection, with which statement would the author MOST likely agree about the role of concession in argumentation?
Which sentence from the passage BEST illustrates the author shifting from acknowledging an opposing view to refuting it?

misconceptions.title: A Concession Is Admitting You Lost
misconceptions.body: Students read a concession — "While critics argue that X is true..." — and assume the writer has just undermined their own case. Teach them that conceding a point strategically is a sign of a sophisticated arguer, not a weak one. Writers grant minor ground to establish credibility and pivot: the concession sets up the rebuttal. If students can't see the pivot, they haven't finished reading the move.

misconceptions.title: All Evidence Works the Same Way
misconceptions.body: Students treat every piece of support as interchangeable. They'll note "the author uses evidence" without distinguishing whether it's statistical, anecdotal, expert testimony, or logical reasoning — and without asking whether the evidence type fits the claim. Push them to name the kind of evidence and then interrogate the match: does a personal story actually prove a policy claim, or does it just make it feel true?

misconceptions.title: The Counterargument Section Is a Tangent
misconceptions.body: Students skip counterargument paragraphs or dismiss them as "the author talking about what other people think." Reframe this as the most structurally important part of the text — a writer who never acknowledges opposition is asking the reader to ignore reality. The counterargument passage is where the argument either holds up or collapses. Teach students to read it first, then read the rebuttal, then assess the gap.

misconceptions.title: A Rebuttal Means the Other Side Is Just Wrong
misconceptions.body: Students reduce rebuttal to "the author says the critics are wrong." Watch for answers that summarize a rebuttal without explaining HOW it works — does the writer attack the evidence, question the logic, or introduce a stronger competing claim? The technique matters as much as the outcome. Push them to name the rhetorical move, not just the conclusion.

misconceptions.title: More Evidence = Stronger Argument
misconceptions.body: Students assume piling up examples makes an argument more convincing. They'll evaluate an argument as strong simply because it cites many sources. Teach them that one well-matched, precisely analyzed piece of evidence beats five vague ones, and that volume can actually obscure weak reasoning. If a writer spends three paragraphs throwing statistics at a claim and never analyzes any of them, that's a problem, not an asset.

assignments.name: Counterargument Dissection
assignments.body: Students read a short argument (two to three pages) and annotate each paragraph as: claim, evidence (with type labeled), counterargument, concession, or rebuttal. They then write a paragraph explaining whether the writer's treatment of the opposition strengthens or weakens the overall argument.

assignments.name: Evidence Audit
assignments.body: Students list every piece of evidence in an argument and classify each by type (anecdote, statistic, expert testimony, logical reasoning, analogy). They then write one sentence for each explaining why the author likely chose that type for that particular claim.

assignments.name: Rebuttal Upgrade
assignments.body: Students find a counterargument in a mentor text that they think the author handled weakly. They draft a more effective rebuttal — same position, stronger move — and explain in a short reflection what they changed and why.

assignments.name: Concede and Pivot Practice
assignments.body: Students write a four-sentence passage using this structure: concession, acknowledgment of why the opposing view has some merit, pivot, rebuttal. Then they exchange with a partner and identify whether each move lands.

mastery: Labels each type of evidence the author uses and explains why that type supports or fails to support the specific claim it accompanies.
Distinguishes between a concession and a rebuttal — and explains the argumentative function of each within the text's structure.
Evaluates how effectively the author handles an opposing viewpoint: does the rebuttal actually address what was conceded, or does it sidestep?
Avoids reducing "strong argument" to "many examples" — looks instead at the fit between evidence type, claim, and reasoning.
Identifies the structural sequence of an argumentative passage (claim → evidence → counterargument → concession → rebuttal) and explains how each move builds on the last.
notes: This is the entry point for formal counterargument analysis — English I students are encountering these structural labels (concession, rebuttal) in a disciplined way for the first time. Vertical progression in English II (110.37(7)(E)(ii)) raises the stakes by adding evaluation of how effectively the counterargument treatment strengthens or weakens the overall argument, not just identification of its presence.

DOK 3 justification: students must go beyond locating evidence types or labeling counterargument moves — they must explain how those structural choices function within the argument's logic, which requires strategic thinking across the full text.

STAAR regularly tests this TEK through paired or extended argumentative passages where one question asks about evidence type and another asks about how the author handles opposition. The stems in this entry mirror those patterns directly.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Argumentative Texts: Identifiable Audience or Reader
expectation: Analyze characteristics and structural elements of argumentative texts such as an identifiable audience or reader.
overview: Every argument is written FOR someone — and the intended audience shapes everything from word choice to evidence selection to tone. This TEK asks students to read argumentative texts with that awareness: who is this written for, and how does targeting that audience drive the choices the writer made?
studentFriendly: You can read an argumentative text and identify who the writer was trying to convince — and explain how aiming at that specific audience shaped the way the argument was built.
questionStems: Based on paragraph X, the author MOST likely intended this text for an audience that —
Which detail from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that the author's intended audience is —
The author's use of [word/phrase] in paragraph X suggests that the intended reader is MOST likely —
Which sentence from the selection BEST reveals the audience the author is trying to persuade?
The author includes [evidence/example] in paragraph X MOST likely because the intended audience —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, what inference can be made about the audience this argument was written for?
How does the author's awareness of the audience MOST likely contribute to the structure of the argument in paragraph X?
The tone the author adopts in paragraphs X–Y suggests that the intended reader is MOST likely someone who —
Which statement BEST describes how the author tailors the argument in paragraph X to a specific audience?
The author's decision to include [counterargument/concession] in paragraph X MOST likely reflects an assumption that the audience —

misconceptions.title: "Audience" Means "Everyone"
misconceptions.body: Students default to "the audience is anyone who reads it" because they haven't yet internalized that argument is always situational. Push them to ask: what does this writer assume the reader already believes? What does the writer feel the reader needs to be convinced of? Teach them that if an argument works equally well on everyone, it probably works well on no one.

misconceptions.title: The Audience Is Named Somewhere in the Text
misconceptions.body: Students hunt for an explicit statement of who the argument is for — a sentence like "To all citizens of Texas..." — and give up when they don't find one. Teach them that audience is almost always inferred, not declared. Evidence is in the vocabulary level, the examples chosen, the background knowledge assumed, and the values appealed to.

misconceptions.title: Identifying the Audience Is the End of the Analysis
misconceptions.body: Students name the audience ("This is written for parents") and consider the job done. The analytical move is the NEXT step: how does targeting that audience explain specific structural and rhetorical choices? Watch for students who stop at identification and never connect audience to evidence selection, diction, or argument structure.

misconceptions.title: A Different Audience Would Get the Same Argument
misconceptions.body: Students sometimes assume the content of an argument is fixed and the audience just receives it. Reframe this: the audience is a structural constraint. A writer arguing for stricter gun laws to a rural hunting community versus a suburban school board will make different moves — different evidence, different concessions, different emotional appeals. Same position; fundamentally different argument.

misconceptions.title: Audience Only Matters in "Old-Fashioned" Persuasion
misconceptions.body: Students see audience analysis as a tool for formal speeches or political essays and miss it in op-eds, social media posts, podcasts, or open letters. Teach them to apply the same lens to every argumentative text regardless of format — the rhetorical situation doesn't disappear because the medium is casual.

assignments.name: Audience Swap
assignments.body: Give students a short argumentative text. After they identify the intended audience, have them rewrite one body paragraph targeting a completely different audience. They write a reflection explaining what changed and why.

assignments.name: Two Texts, Same Position
assignments.body: Provide two argumentative texts making the same claim to different audiences (e.g., an op-ed for a general newspaper vs. a letter to a city council). Students analyze how audience shapes structure, evidence, and tone in each — and write a comparative paragraph.

assignments.name: Audience Evidence Chart
assignments.body: Students annotate an argumentative text by marking every passage that reveals something about the intended audience, then categorize the clues: vocabulary level, assumed prior knowledge, values appealed to, evidence type. Chart turns into the basis for a written analysis.

assignments.name: Cold Read Audience ID
assignments.body: Students receive an argumentative text with the publication source stripped out. They identify the intended audience and write a paragraph defending their claim with three specific pieces of textual evidence, then check against the actual source.

mastery: Identifies the intended audience of an argumentative text using textual evidence rather than assumption or guessing.
Connects specific structural and rhetorical choices — evidence selection, diction, concessions — to the demands of the intended audience.
Distinguishes between the topic of an argument and the situational context that shapes how that argument is built.
Infers audience from indirect signals (vocabulary, assumed knowledge, appeals) when no explicit statement of audience exists.
Explains how a different intended audience would require different structural and rhetorical choices in the same argument.
notes: This sub-expectation is the third element of 110.36(7)(E), which asks students to analyze characteristics and structural elements of argumentative texts. The three sub-elements (specific claims, logical sequence, identifiable audience) build a complete picture of how argument works structurally — this one targets the rhetorical situation rather than internal logic.

Vertical ramp: English I introduces identification and analysis of intended audience as a structural feature. English II is expected to deepen toward evaluating how effectively the argument reaches that audience and synthesizing across multiple texts.

DOK 3 justification: students must go beyond identification to explain how audience shapes structural and rhetorical choices — requiring strategic thinking and reasoning across multiple textual elements.

STAAR analog note: audience analysis appears most often in STAAR's author's purpose and craft questions — expect items framing it as "why does the author include X" or "what does the author assume about the reader." The stems above mirror that framing.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Characteristics of Multimodal and Digital Texts
expectation: Analyze characteristics of multimodal and digital texts.
overview: Most of what students actually read outside school is multimodal and digital — but they read it passively. This TEK asks them to get analytical: what does it mean that the argument lives in a video, a webpage, an infographic, or a podcast rather than on a printed page? The medium is a design decision, and design decisions have rhetorical consequences.
studentFriendly: You can look at a website, video, infographic, or social media post and explain how its layout, images, links, and format shape the message — not just what it says, but how the form itself does persuasive work.
questionStems: How does the graphic in paragraph X contribute to the author's central argument?
Based on the layout of the digital text, which element MOST likely signals what the author considers most important?
Which feature of the multimodal text BEST supports the claim made in paragraph X?
The hyperlink embedded in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
How does the use of images alongside the written text in paragraphs X–Y affect the reader's understanding of the author's purpose?
Which sentence from the selection BEST explains why the author chose to present this information in a multimodal format rather than a traditional essay?
The author's use of [visual element/audio element/interactive feature] in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, what can the reader infer about the intended audience from the design choices of this digital text?
How does the organizational structure of the digital text in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the development of the author's argument?
The author's decision to include [video clip/infographic/chart] alongside the written text MOST likely serves to —

misconceptions.title: "Multimodal" Just Means "Has Pictures"
misconceptions.body: Students treat any text with an image as automatically multimodal and stop analyzing there. Push them deeper: multimodal means multiple sign systems — visual, linguistic, spatial, gestural, audio — working together as a designed whole. Teach them to ask what each mode is doing independently and what it does only in combination with the others. A caption and an image together make an argument that neither makes alone.

misconceptions.title: The Words Are the Real Text; Everything Else Is Decoration
misconceptions.body: Students default to transcribing only the verbal content when they analyze digital texts, treating sidebars, color, layout, and embedded media as aesthetics rather than rhetoric. Watch for summaries that ignore the visual and spatial entirely. Reframe this as a design problem: every layout choice is an editorial decision. If the photograph didn't matter, the author would have used plain text.

misconceptions.title: Digital Texts Are Less Credible by Default
misconceptions.body: Students arrive with a folk epistemology that print is more trustworthy than digital. They apply this heuristic instead of doing actual analysis — dismissing a well-sourced interactive data visualization and accepting a poorly reasoned printed editorial without scrutiny. Teach them that credibility is a function of sourcing, evidence, and reasoning, not of medium. The medium analysis question is different from the credibility question.

misconceptions.title: Hyperlinks Are Just Navigation — They Don't Mean Anything
misconceptions.body: Students click past hyperlinks without noticing that linking is a rhetorical act. What an author links to — and what they don't — reveals assumptions about the audience, defines the bounds of the argument, and can either support or quietly undermine the text's claims. Have them audit a piece's links the way they'd audit a bibliography: what does this author want you to read next, and why?

misconceptions.title: Analyzing Format Means Describing It
misconceptions.body: Students describe the text's features ("this webpage has a header, three columns, and embedded video") and call it analysis. Description is the floor, not the ceiling. Push them to explain the effect: Why three columns? Why video rather than a written transcript? What would be different if this were a linear essay? Analysis requires a claim about what the design choices accomplish.

assignments.name: Mode Audit
assignments.body: Students select a digital text (news article with multimedia, nonprofit homepage, explainer video with on-screen text) and map every mode present — written, visual, spatial, audio, interactive. For each mode, they write one sentence explaining what it contributes that the other modes don't. Final product: a one-page mode map with explanatory annotations.

assignments.name: Same Story, Different Medium
assignments.body: Students find the same news event covered in three formats: a traditional print article, an infographic, and a video report. They write a comparative analysis identifying what each format foregrounds, what each obscures, and what a reader would miss by consuming only one.

assignments.name: Redesign Argument
assignments.body: Students take a printed editorial or short essay and redesign it as a digital multimodal text — deciding which claims need visual support, where interaction would help, what audio would add. They write a one-paragraph justification for each design decision, explaining the rhetorical purpose.

assignments.name: Link Audit
assignments.body: Students read a web-based argumentative piece and examine every embedded hyperlink: where does it go, who produced it, and does it actually support the claim it's attached to? They produce a brief report evaluating whether the author's linking choices strengthen or weaken the text's credibility.

mastery: Names the specific modes present in a text (visual, linguistic, spatial, audio, interactive) and distinguishes what each mode contributes to the overall message.
Explains design choices — layout, image selection, hyperlinking, color, embedded media — as rhetorical decisions rather than aesthetic decoration.
Compares how the same information functions differently across formats and articulates what is gained or lost in each.
Evaluates how modes interact with each other, not just what each does independently — identifying moments where modes reinforce, complicate, or contradict one another.
Avoids substituting credibility judgment for formal analysis — treats medium as a design variable, not a trust signal.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I 7F establishes the analytical vocabulary for multimodal and digital texts. English II builds on this by asking students to evaluate the effectiveness of those characteristics — moving from "what is here and what does it do?" to "how well does it work?" Teachers should introduce mode-mapping vocabulary (visual, linguistic, spatial, gestural, audio) in English I so English II can build on a shared framework rather than re-teaching the same descriptive baseline.

DOK 3 justification: analysis requires students to move beyond identification of features into explanation of how those features interact and what rhetorical work they perform — multi-step reasoning against an interpretive framework.

STAAR analog note: STAAR does test digital and multimodal texts on the English I assessment, though items typically present a static rendering of a webpage or infographic rather than live interactive content. Stems that reference layout, embedded graphics, and organizational structure mirror how STAAR frames multimodal analysis.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Author's Purpose, Audience, and Message
expectation: Analyze the author's purpose, audience, and message within a text.
overview: This is the foundational author's-craft move — before students can evaluate rhetoric, challenge argument, or trace craft decisions, they have to be able to answer why this text exists, who it was made for, and what the author actually wants them to think or do. The skill binds purpose, audience, and message together rather than treating them as three separate checkboxes.
studentFriendly: You can read any text — a novel, an editorial, a speech, a product warning — and explain why the author wrote it, who they wrote it for, and what they actually wanted the reader to walk away believing or feeling.
questionStems: Based on the selection, what is the author's MOST likely purpose for writing this text?
Which sentence from the selection BEST reveals the author's intended audience?
The author MOST likely included paragraph X in order to —
Which statement BEST expresses the central message of the selection?
The author's purpose in paragraphs X–Y is MOST likely to —
Based on the details in paragraph X, the author MOST likely intended this text for an audience that —
Which detail from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that the author's purpose is to persuade?
How does the author's choice of audience MOST likely shape the message in paragraph X?
The author includes [specific detail] in paragraph X MOST likely to —
Based on the selection as a whole, with which statement would the author MOST likely agree?

misconceptions.title: Purpose Is Just "To Entertain, Inform, or Persuade"
misconceptions.body: Students reach for the three-bucket taxonomy and stop there. "To inform" is the beginning of an answer, not the answer. Teach them to push through to specificity: to inform whom, about what, in order to produce what effect? A nature essay and a CDC bulletin both "inform" — but they're doing completely different things. Watch for students who write "the author's purpose is to inform" without ever naming the subject or the intended impact.

misconceptions.title: Message Means Moral
misconceptions.body: Students flatten "message" into the kind of life-lesson sentence they wrote in fifth grade: "The message is to never give up." Push them deeper — what is this specific author saying to this specific audience about this specific topic? The message of a persuasive editorial on school start times is not "sleep is important." It's the particular claim the author is making about policy, evidence, and who bears responsibility. Teach them that message is argument, not fortune cookie.

misconceptions.title: The Author's Audience Is "Everyone"
misconceptions.body: When students can't identify the audience, they default to "everyone" or "all readers." That's almost never true. Teach them to read for signals: assumed prior knowledge, vocabulary level, references that require insider context, the publication source, the rhetorical framing. An op-ed in a medical journal and an op-ed in a local newspaper about the same topic are aimed at different people — that difference shapes every sentence.

misconceptions.title: Purpose and Message Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students conflate why the author wrote with what the author is saying. Purpose is about the author's goal — the effect they're trying to produce. Message is the content of the claim or the takeaway. A charity appeal has the purpose of moving the reader to donate; its message might be that the water crisis is solvable with political will. They're related, but collapsing them causes students to misread both. Make them define each separately before connecting them.

misconceptions.title: You Figure Out Purpose Last
misconceptions.body: Students treat purpose as a conclusion you reach after finishing the text. In practice, purpose shapes every micro-decision in the text — and competent readers are tracking it from sentence one. Watch for students who read passively and then guess at purpose in retrospect. Teach them to ask "Why is this here?" while reading, not after. Purpose is a lens, not a summary.

assignments.name: Three-Column Breakdown
assignments.body: Students select a short text (editorial, speech excerpt, short story opening) and produce a three-column written analysis: Purpose | Audience | Message. Each column requires at least two pieces of textual evidence and a sentence explaining the connection. The final row asks: how do the three interact?

assignments.name: Same Topic, Different Author
assignments.body: Give students two texts on the same subject written for different audiences with different purposes (e.g., a scientific article on climate and a children's picture book on weather). Students compare how purpose and audience shape the message in each — and write a paragraph explaining what changes and what stays the same.

assignments.name: Author's Note Reconstruction
assignments.body: Students read a text and write a brief fictional "Author's Note" in which the author explains, in first person, why they wrote the piece, who they had in mind, and what they hoped the reader would leave believing. Students then mark specific passages in the actual text that support each claim in their reconstruction.

mastery: Names a specific, detailed purpose — not just a category ("to inform") but the effect the author is working toward in this text with this audience.
Identifies the intended audience using textual evidence: word choice, assumed knowledge, publication context, rhetorical appeals.
Articulates the message as a claim or takeaway — specific to the text, not a generic moral.
Explains how purpose, audience, and message interact and constrain each other — why one choice affects the others.
Tracks purpose while reading rather than inferring it only in retrospect.
notes: This is English I's entry point into Author's Purpose and Craft. The vertical ramp in English II (110.37(8)(A)) adds evaluation of the effectiveness of the author's choices — English I asks students to identify and analyze; English II asks them to judge whether those choices work.

DOK 3 justification: the standard requires going beyond retrieval or basic identification to explaining how purpose, audience, and message function together and shape each other within a specific text — reasoning that demands inference, synthesis, and evidence-based explanation.

STAAR analog note: this TEK maps directly to STAAR Reading items testing author's purpose and central message. Stems for this entry follow released STAAR phrasing; expect paired-passage items in which purpose and message comparison is the target.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Use of Text Structure to Achieve the Author's Purpose
expectation: Analyze use of text structure to achieve the author's purpose.
overview: Structure is never neutral — the way a text is organized is itself a rhetorical move. In English I, students learn to read organizational choice as intentional craft: why does this argument open with concession? why does this narrative delay the climax? The goal is moving students from "this is organized by compare/contrast" to "this is organized by compare/contrast because the author needs the reader to feel the gap before the solution."
studentFriendly: You can look at how a piece of writing is put together — whether it compares, narrates, builds an argument, or circles back — and explain exactly why the author built it that way to get a specific effect on the reader.
questionStems: The author MOST likely structures paragraph X as a cause-and-effect sequence in order to —
How does the organizational structure of paragraphs X–Y contribute to the development of the author's argument?
The author begins the passage with [description of opening] MOST likely to —
Which sentence from the passage BEST supports the conclusion that the author uses problem-solution structure to —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the author's use of compare-and-contrast structure MOST likely emphasizes —
How does the author's decision to place [detail/claim] at the end of the passage rather than the beginning affect the reader?
The shift in structure between paragraph X and paragraph Y MOST likely signals that the author intends to —
Which statement BEST describes how the chronological structure of the passage contributes to the author's purpose?
The author's use of [structural feature] in paragraphs X–Y is MOST likely intended to —
Based on the passage, which statement MOST nearly describes the relationship between the author's organizational choice and the passage's central idea?

misconceptions.title: Naming the Structure IS the Analysis
misconceptions.body: Students learn to label — "this is cause-and-effect" — and believe the work is done. The label is just the door; the analysis is what's on the other side. Teach them to always follow the name with a "because the author wanted the reader to feel/think/do ___." Watch for essays that list structural features like a checklist with zero payoff about purpose.

misconceptions.title: Text Structure Only Means the Five Patterns
misconceptions.body: Students treat text structure as a vocabulary quiz: cause-effect, compare-contrast, chronological, problem-solution, description. Real texts blend patterns, abandon them mid-piece, and invent hybrids. Push them to ask not just "which pattern is this?" but "what is this pattern doing here, and where does it break down?"

misconceptions.title: The Author "Just Organized It That Way"
misconceptions.body: Students frequently treat structure as a default or accident — as if the text were assembled rather than built. Teach them that every structural choice was made against alternatives: the author could have opened differently, cut differently, sequenced differently. When students say "the author just used this structure," that's the signal to ask: what would happen if they hadn't?

misconceptions.title: Purpose Equals Topic
misconceptions.body: When asked why an author structured the text a certain way, students loop back to what the text is about. "The author used chronological order to talk about the civil rights movement." That's topic, not purpose. The purpose is what the author wants the reader to understand, feel, or do — the structural choice is in service of THAT, not in service of the subject matter.

misconceptions.title: Paragraphs Are the Only Structural Unit Worth Examining
misconceptions.body: Students learn to identify structure at the whole-text level and stop there. But structure operates at the sentence level, the paragraph level, the section level, and across the full text simultaneously. Push them to notice where a single paragraph reverses the pattern of the whole piece, or where a sentence's structure (syntax included) mirrors the macro-structure argument the author is making.

assignments.name: Structure Autopsy
assignments.body: Students select a short informational or argumentative text (400–600 words) and annotate the structural moves: where patterns begin, shift, and resolve. They write a one-paragraph explanation linking each major structural choice to a specific authorial purpose. Grade for specificity — "to inform" is not a purpose.

assignments.name: Rebuild It Differently
assignments.body: Students take a passage and reorder it: flip the opening and conclusion, reorganize by a different pattern. Then they write: what did the restructuring cost? What did the original structure do that the new version cannot? The exercise makes structural choice visible by making it painful.

assignments.name: Side-by-Side Structure Comparison
assignments.body: Pair two texts on the same topic that use different organizational structures (e.g., one chronological, one problem-solution account of the same historical event). Students analyze how the structural difference produces a different effect on the reader even though the content overlaps.

mastery: Identifies the organizational structure(s) at work in a text and can explain where patterns shift or blend.
Articulates a specific connection between a structural choice and the author's purpose — not topic, not theme, but the intended effect on the reader.
Uses textual evidence at the paragraph or sentence level (not just the whole-text level) to support structural claims.
Can explain what an alternative structure would have cost — what the chosen structure makes possible that another would not.
Applies structural analysis to both literary and informational texts without treating the skill as genre-specific.
notes: Vertical alignment: This is the first formal entry point for text-structure-as-craft analysis. English II (110.37(8)(B)) adds comparative analysis across texts — students are expected to bring this single-text structural skill into cross-text comparison. English III and IV layer in rhetorical and ideological critique of structural choice. In English I the task is establishing that structure is purposeful.

DOK 3 justification: Students must reason across structural patterns, connect organizational choice to authorial intent, and evaluate evidence — this requires strategic thinking beyond recall or skill application.

STAAR tests this TEK regularly in informational and argumentative passages; stems frequently ask how a paragraph or section functions within the whole, or why the author placed a specific element where they did.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Evaluate the Author's Use of Print and Graphic Features
expectation: Evaluate the author's use of print and graphic features to achieve specific purposes.
overview: This TEK moves students past noticing design choices into judging whether those choices actually work. The target is purposeful visual rhetoric — headers, sidebars, pull quotes, infographics, photographs, bold text — evaluated not as decoration but as argument.
studentFriendly: You can look at how a text is designed — its headings, images, charts, and layout — and explain whether those design choices actually help the author accomplish what they set out to do.
questionStems: The author MOST likely includes the photograph in paragraph X to —
How does the use of bold text in paragraph X contribute to the author's purpose?
Which graphic feature in the article BEST supports the author's central argument?
The sidebar in the article MOST likely serves to —
Based on the infographic and paragraphs X–Y, what conclusion can the reader MOST reasonably draw about the author's intended message?
The author's decision to use a pull quote from paragraph X MOST likely emphasizes —
How does the chart in paragraph X develop the author's claim about [topic]?
Which statement BEST describes the effect of the heading structure on the reader's understanding of the text?
The author MOST likely includes the diagram alongside paragraph X in order to —
Based on paragraphs X–Y and the accompanying image, the reader can infer that the author's purpose is MOST likely to —

misconceptions.title: Graphic Features Are Optional Decoration
misconceptions.body: Students treat images, charts, and headers as extras the publisher threw in — nice to look at, not worth analyzing. Push them to ask who made the decision to include this feature and what the text loses without it. If the answer is "nothing," that's itself a valid critique; if the answer is "the argument falls apart," they've found the feature doing real work.

misconceptions.title: "It Makes the Text More Interesting" Is an Analysis
misconceptions.body: Watch for students who evaluate every graphic feature as successful because it "grabs attention" or "makes you want to read." That's a starting observation, not a finished evaluation. Teach them to name the specific purpose — informing, persuading, clarifying, framing — and then judge whether the feature actually achieves it, with evidence from the text.

misconceptions.title: Photographs Are Neutral Evidence
misconceptions.body: Students assume photographs document facts while graphs and charts do the persuading. Reframe this: a photograph is always a choice — of subject, framing, angle, and caption. Teach them to read an image the same way they read a loaded word: who selected it, what it emphasizes, and what it leaves out.

misconceptions.title: Evaluation Means Rating, Not Reasoning
misconceptions.body: Students collapse "evaluate" into giving a thumbs-up or thumbs-down without constructing an argument. They say "the infographic was effective" and stop. Push them to complete the circuit: effective at what, for which audience, compared to what alternative, and why does that matter to the author's overall purpose?

misconceptions.title: Print Features and Graphic Features Are the Same Category
misconceptions.body: Students lump bold text, headers, and bullet points together with photographs and charts and evaluate them all the same way. Teach them the distinction: print features shape how language is read (emphasis, hierarchy, pacing); graphic features carry meaning through image and data. The evaluation criteria overlap but aren't identical — a student who can name which type they're analyzing is thinking at a higher level.

assignments.name: Feature Audit
assignments.body: Students select a magazine article or longform web piece and catalog every print and graphic feature it uses. For each feature, they write one sentence naming its apparent purpose and one sentence judging whether it achieves that purpose. Class discussion surfaces where students agree and where they diverge.

assignments.name: The Same Article, Redesigned
assignments.body: Students receive the body text of an informational article stripped of all features (plain prose only) and are asked to redesign it — deciding what graphic and print features to add and why. They then compare their choices to the original and evaluate whose version better serves the author's purpose.

assignments.name: Feature vs. Argument
assignments.body: Students read two versions of the same argument — one with strong graphic support, one without — and evaluate which is more persuasive and why. The point is to isolate whether the persuasion lives in the prose or the design.

mastery: Names specific print and graphic features by type and connects each to a distinct authorial purpose rather than treating all features as interchangeable.
Produces an evaluation that argues for a judgment — effective or not, and why — with textual and visual evidence.
Distinguishes between a feature that decorates and a feature that carries argumentative or informational weight.
Accounts for audience when evaluating effectiveness — a feature that works for one reader might fail for another, and strong students can articulate that tension.
Critiques features that undermine the author's purpose, not just those that support it.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I establishes evaluation of print and graphic features as purposeful choices; English II (110.37(8)(C)) extends this to multi-modal texts and asks students to compare how features function across genres and media. The ramp is from single-text feature evaluation to cross-text, cross-modal comparison.
DOK 3 justification: evaluation of authorial choice requires strategic reasoning — students must construct and defend a judgment using evidence, not simply locate or describe features.

This TEK has a strong STAAR analog in informational reading passages, where released items frequently pair prose with a graph, photograph, or sidebar and ask students to evaluate its relationship to the author's argument. Stems here mirror that exact testing pattern.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze How an Author's Use of Language Achieves Specific Purposes
expectation: Analyze how the author's use of language achieves specific purposes.
overview: This is the engine underneath most of the Author's Purpose and Craft strand — students have to move from noticing what an author does with language to explaining WHY it works. The target isn't vocabulary identification or labeling devices; it's the causal link between a specific language choice and the effect that choice produces on a reader.
studentFriendly: You can read a passage, pinpoint exact word choices, figurative language, or sentence structures, and explain what the author was trying to make the reader feel, think, or believe — and why those specific choices were the best tools for the job.
questionStems: The author's use of the phrase [phrase] in paragraph X MOST likely creates a sense of —
Which sentence from the passage BEST illustrates the author's purpose in paragraph X?
How does the author's use of [figurative language/diction/syntax] in paragraph X contribute to the overall tone of the selection?
Read this sentence from paragraph X: "[sentence]." The author MOST likely includes this detail to —
Which statement BEST describes the effect of the author's language in paragraphs X–Y?
The word [word] in paragraph X MOST nearly means —
The author's use of [device] in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Based on paragraph X, what conclusion can the reader draw about the author's purpose?
How does the author's choice of language in paragraph X develop the [mood/tone/theme] of the selection?
Which detail from the passage BEST supports the inference that the author intended to [purpose]?

misconceptions.title: "Use of Language" Means Figurative Language Only
misconceptions.body: Students narrow this TEK to metaphor, simile, and personification because those are the devices they've been drilled on longest. Watch for students who skip right past charged diction, sentence rhythm, and syntax shifts because those don't have a label to cite. Teach them that language is everything on the page — figurative devices are just the loudest members of a much larger family.

misconceptions.title: Identifying the Device IS the Analysis
misconceptions.body: Students write "The author uses a metaphor" and consider the job finished. The TEK demands the next step: what work does that metaphor do? Reframe the task explicitly — the device is the evidence, the purpose is the claim. If they can't say what the author achieved with the choice, they haven't analyzed anything yet.

misconceptions.title: Purpose Means the Whole Text's Message
misconceptions.body: Students conflate purpose at the sentence level with the thesis of the entire text. A single word choice can serve a local purpose — building tension in this paragraph, undercutting a character's credibility in this moment — without equaling the whole text's central argument. Push them to be specific: what is this choice doing HERE, in this sentence, at this moment?

misconceptions.title: Every Effect the Reader Feels Is the Author's Purpose
misconceptions.body: Students flip the logic: they feel something, so they assume that's what the author intended. The discipline is tying the effect back to a specific language choice that produced it. Teach them the chain: this word / this structure → this effect → this purpose. Without the chain, it's just impressionism.

misconceptions.title: Strong Language Analysis Requires Big Vocabulary Words
misconceptions.body: Students believe they can only analyze language if they find sophisticated or rare words to comment on. In practice, the highest-leverage words in any passage are often the most ordinary — "always," "only," "we," "they," "natural." Teach them to be suspicious of what seems plain. The word the author chose over ten other words it could've been replaced with is worth examining, regardless of how small it looks.

assignments.name: Purpose-Function Chart
assignments.body: Students choose ten lines from a passage — five that feel significant and five that seem unremarkable — and for each, write one sentence identifying the language choice and one sentence stating its purpose. The second group often produces the richest discussion.

assignments.name: Author Swap
assignments.body: Rewrite a key paragraph replacing the author's word choices with flatter, more neutral equivalents. Students then write a paragraph comparing what changed — not just in tone but in the reader's experience and the argument's strength.

assignments.name: Annotated Close Read
assignments.body: Students annotate a short passage (150–300 words) in two passes: first marking WHAT the author did, then in a different color marking WHY. The two-pass method forces them to separate identification from analysis.

assignments.name: Purposeful Language Gallery Walk
assignments.body: Post six short excerpts from different genres around the room. Students rotate with sticky notes, adding one "language choice → purpose" observation per excerpt. Debrief by asking which choices were easiest to explain and which required the most inference about intent.

mastery: Identifies specific language choices — diction, syntax, figurative language, tone — without prompting and moves immediately to explaining their effect.
Articulates a clear causal link between the author's language choice and the purpose it serves, rather than stopping at identification.
Distinguishes between local purpose (what this sentence achieves) and global purpose (what the whole text argues), and knows which the question is asking about.
Applies analysis to unmarked language — plain diction, short sentences, absence of figurative language — not just to obvious devices.

Produces written analysis that names the choice, states the effect, and connects both to the author's apparent intent without relying on vague phrases like "to make it interesting" or "to hook the reader."
notes: Vertical entry point: English I is the first formal encounter with this skill mapped to the Author's Purpose and Craft strand. English II (110.37(8)(D)) extends this to evaluation of how language informs and shapes perception — the ramp is from analysis of purpose to evaluation of effect on audience.

This TEK sits at DOK 3 because students must move through identification and into explaining causal relationships between language choices and authorial intent — a task requiring reasoning beyond retrieval or skill application.

Because this TEK has no direct STAAR standalone prompt, stems above mirror how STAAR embeds language-purpose analysis within reading comprehension items — particularly through "MOST likely intended to," "contribute to," and "develop" frames. Note this in instruction planning.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Literary Devices Such as Irony and Oxymoron
expectation: Analyze the use of literary devices such as irony and oxymoron to achieve specific purposes.
overview: This TEK moves students past identification into analysis — the question isn't whether irony is present but what the author is doing with it and why. Irony and oxymoron are both mechanisms for creating distance between surface meaning and deeper truth, and that gap is where all the real work happens.
studentFriendly: You can read a text, spot where the author is saying one thing but meaning another (or smashing two contradictory ideas together), and explain exactly what effect that move has on the reader and what it reveals about the author's purpose.
questionStems: The author's use of irony in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates situational irony?
Read paragraph X. The phrase [oxymoron phrase] creates what effect on the reader?
The author uses dramatic irony in paragraphs X–Y MOST likely to —
Which statement BEST describes the purpose of the oxymoron in paragraph X?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the verbal irony in [character's] dialogue MOST likely suggests that —
How does the author's use of irony in paragraph X contribute to the tone of the selection?
The phrase [oxymoron phrase] in paragraph X creates a contrast that MOST nearly conveys —
Which of the following BEST explains how the author uses irony to develop the theme?
Read paragraphs X–Y. The irony in these paragraphs is MOST likely intended to make the reader —

misconceptions.title: "Irony" Means "Coincidence" or "Bad Timing"
misconceptions.body: Students (and, let's be honest, many adults) use "ironic" to mean anything unfortunate that happened at an inconvenient moment. Teach them the distinction early and hard: irony requires a gap between expectation and reality, or between what is said and what is meant. A flat tire on the way to a job interview is bad luck — the mechanic getting a flat tire is situational irony. Get specific on all three types: verbal, situational, and dramatic.

misconceptions.title: Identifying the Device Is the Same as Analyzing It
misconceptions.body: Students will underline the oxymoron, write its name in the margin, and stop. Teach them that identification is the price of admission, not the analysis. The real question is always: what does this device DO? What would be lost if the author had used a straightforward phrase instead? Push them to the purpose every single time.

misconceptions.title: Oxymorons Are Just for Poetry
misconceptions.body: Students compartmentalize oxymorons as decorative flourishes found in poems and nowhere else. Show them oxymorons at work in speeches, arguments, and prose fiction — "deafening silence," "living death," "open secret" — and ask why plain language would fail where the contradiction succeeds. The oxymoron signals that ordinary logic isn't enough to hold the idea.

misconceptions.title: Dramatic Irony Only Matters to the Audience
misconceptions.body: Students understand that dramatic irony means the reader knows something the character doesn't, but they leave it there. They miss the purposeful tension the author is building by sustaining that gap. Watch for students who label the device correctly but fail to connect the reader's superior knowledge to the author's larger intent — suspense, sympathy, critique, doom.

misconceptions.title: All Irony Is Sarcastic
misconceptions.body: Students flatten irony into snark. They read verbal irony as the author mocking someone and miss the cases where irony creates empathy, highlights injustice, or produces tragedy. Reframe this: sarcasm is a weapon; irony is a lens. The author might be using it to make the reader see something more clearly, not to score a point.

assignments.name: Device Dissection
assignments.body: Students read a short excerpt (one to two pages) containing at least two instances of irony or oxymoron. They write a paragraph per device: name it, quote it, explain the purpose it serves, and identify what would be lost if the author had used direct language instead.

assignments.name: Three Types, Three Texts
assignments.body: Students find one example each of verbal, situational, and dramatic irony across three different texts studied in class. They present their three examples in writing, explaining how each type serves its specific context differently.

assignments.name: Author's Swap
assignments.body: Students take a passage containing irony or oxymoron and rewrite it in plain, literal language. They then write a reflection comparing the two versions: what did the revision gain in clarity? What did it lose in meaning, tone, or resonance?

assignments.name: Device for Purpose Chart
assignments.body: As the class reads a longer anchor text, students maintain a running chart: device used, location, and purpose. At unit end, patterns emerge — students write a claim about how this author characteristically uses these devices and why.

mastery: Names the specific type of irony at work (verbal, situational, or dramatic) and explains what distinguishes it from the other types.
Quotes the text precisely and connects the quoted device to the author's larger purpose, not just the surface effect.
Explains what the device accomplishes that direct language could not — articulates the gap between surface meaning and deeper truth.
Identifies oxymorons and explains the conceptual tension they hold, rather than treating them as decorative word combinations.
Uses the device to make a claim about theme, tone, or the author's stance — analysis that goes beyond the sentence level.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I introduces irony and oxymoron as targets of purposeful analysis; English II raises the stakes by adding extended analysis of how multiple devices interact across a text. Students arriving in English I may have encountered irony informally in middle school but rarely with the precision this TEK demands.

DOK 3 justification: the TEK requires students to go beyond retrieval (naming) and comprehension (defining) into strategic thinking — explaining how the device functions and why the author deployed it. Full purposive analysis across multiple device types reaches DOK 3.

No direct STAAR analog for oxymoron as a standalone tested device; STAAR more often tests irony through inference and author's purpose frames. Stems above mirror the closest STAAR-style question types for both devices.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze How Diction and Syntax Contribute to Mood, Voice, and Tone
expectation: Analyze how the author's diction and syntax contribute to the mood, voice, and tone of a text.
overview: This is the TEK where students stop describing a text and start explaining HOW the writing works. The target isn't naming mood, voice, or tone in isolation — it's tracing those effects back to specific word choices and sentence structures that produce them. The craft move and the effect belong together.
studentFriendly: You can read a passage and explain not just what feeling it creates or whose voice comes through, but exactly which words and sentence structures are doing that work.
questionStems: Read paragraph X. The author's use of [word/phrase] MOST likely contributes to which mood in the passage?
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates the author's tone toward [subject/character]?
The author's diction in paragraph X suggests a tone that is MOST nearly —
How does the syntax in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the narrator's voice?
The author's use of [sentence structure — e.g., short declarative sentences / long, winding clauses] in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Read paragraphs X and Y. How does the author's word choice shift between the two paragraphs, and what does this shift suggest about the tone?
Which of the following BEST describes how the author's diction in paragraph X establishes mood?
The repetition of [word/phrase] throughout the passage MOST likely serves to —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the author's syntax contributes to the voice by —
The author's use of [word/phrase] in paragraph X is MOST likely meant to convey a tone of —

misconceptions.title: Mood, Voice, and Tone Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students — and sometimes teachers — use these three terms interchangeably, which collapses the analysis before it starts. Teach them the distinction with sharp definitions: tone is the author's attitude toward the subject, mood is the feeling the text creates in the reader, and voice is the personality of the narrator or speaker on the page. They can overlap, but they are not synonyms. When a student writes "the tone is sad," ask: sad toward what — the subject, the character, the situation? Who feels sad — the author, the reader, the narrator?

misconceptions.title: Diction Analysis Stops at the Adjectives
misconceptions.body: Students reach for adjectives and adverbs first because they feel obviously descriptive. Watch for students who annotate only the obvious emotional words ("beautiful," "devastating") and ignore the verbs, articles, and prepositions that carry just as much weight. Teach them to ask: what does the author choose NOT to say? Omission and precision in neutral-seeming words often do more tonal work than dramatic adjectives.

misconceptions.title: Syntax Just Means Sentence Length
misconceptions.body: Students reduce syntactic analysis to "long sentences = slow and sad, short sentences = fast and tense." That's a start, not a finish. Push them deeper: Where does the subject land in the sentence? What is buried in a subordinate clause versus placed at the end for emphasis? Does a fragment interrupt the rhythm deliberately? Syntax is structure; structure creates expectation; expectation manipulates the reader.

misconceptions.title: Naming the Effect Is the Analysis
misconceptions.body: Students identify mood, voice, or tone — "the mood is eerie" — and stop there, treating the label as the answer. The analysis is everything that follows: which word, in which position, producing which effect, for which reader. Teach them that every claim about mood, voice, or tone must be followed by a "because" that points to the text. The label is the claim; the diction or syntax is the evidence; the explanation is the analysis.

misconceptions.title: Voice Belongs to the Author, Not the Text
misconceptions.body: Students often conflate voice with biography: "The author sounds angry because she was mistreated." That's a reach to context, not an analysis of the text. Voice on the page is constructed through diction, syntax, detail selection, and point of view — things they can point to in the words. Teach them to keep the analysis in the text: not "she sounds like someone who..." but "the narrator's repeated use of [construction/word] creates a voice that..."

assignments.name: Diction-to-Effect Annotation
assignments.body: Students annotate a 1–2 page passage, underlining every word or phrase that contributes to mood, voice, or tone and labeling what it contributes. They write a 1-paragraph explanation connecting at least three annotations to a single claim about the text's overall tone.

assignments.name: Rewrite to Shift the Tone
assignments.body: Students take a short passage (8–12 sentences) and rewrite it with the opposite tone — same events, same basic structure, entirely different diction and syntax. They then annotate both versions, identifying exactly which substitutions produced the tonal shift. The goal is to understand craft by having to reverse-engineer it.

assignments.name: Voice Comparison
assignments.body: Students read two passages on the same subject from different authors or narrators. They produce a chart identifying five specific diction and syntax choices from each passage and explain how those choices produce different voices. Final writing: one paragraph comparing the two voices, evidence-based.

mastery: Distinguishes among mood, voice, and tone and can define each with reference to specific textual features rather than vague summary.
Identifies diction choices beyond adjectives — verbs, noun precision, deliberate absence of modifiers — and connects them to a specific tonal or atmospheric effect.
Analyzes syntactic structures (fragment, inversion, periodic sentence, cumulative sentence) and explains what each contributes to mood or voice, not just what it looks like.
Constructs analytical claims of the form: [specific word/structure] + [position or context in text] + [effect on reader or narrator] — not "the mood is dark" followed by nothing.
Transfers the skill across genres — applies the same diction-and-syntax lens to fiction, memoir, and argument without treating each as an entirely different task.
notes: This is an English I entry point for a skill that deepens across all four years. English II is expected to extend the same analysis to more complex texts; by English III and IV, students are producing multi-criteria critique and evaluating the effectiveness of choices, not just identifying them. The vertical trajectory is: identify → explain → evaluate → critique.

DOK 3 justification: students must move beyond retrieval and definition to strategic analysis — connecting specific textual features to abstract effects (mood, voice, tone) and explaining the mechanism of that connection.

STAAR tests this TEK consistently, particularly in paired literary passages where tonal contrast or voice distinction between two texts is a primary comprehension target. Stems 6 and 10 above reflect that paired-passage structure. No STAAR analog gap — this is a directly tested skill.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Explain the Purpose of Rhetorical Devices and Logical Fallacies
expectation: Explain the purpose of rhetorical devices such as understatement and overstatement and the effect of logical fallacies such as straw man and red herring arguments.
overview: This TEK separates students who can name a device from students who can explain what the author is doing with it. The rhetorical side (understatement, overstatement) asks why a writer reaches for distortion; the fallacy side (straw man, red herring) asks what the argument is trying to avoid. Both halves point at the same thing: text is always doing something to the reader, and the reader has to catch it.
studentFriendly: You can read a text, spot when a writer is intentionally overstating or understating something or using a misleading argument, and explain exactly what that move is designed to do to the reader.
questionStems: The author's use of overstatement in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates the author's use of understatement?
Read this sentence from paragraph X: [sentence]. The author MOST likely uses this statement to —
Which of the following BEST describes the effect of the straw man argument in paragraphs X–Y?
The author introduces the claim in paragraph X in order to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST identifies a red herring argument the author uses?
The logical fallacy in paragraph X MOST likely causes the reader to —
The author's overstatement in paragraph X contributes to the tone of the selection by —
Based on paragraph X, the author's use of understatement suggests that —
Which statement BEST explains why the author's argument in paragraphs X–Y is a logical fallacy?

misconceptions.title: Overstatement Is Just Hyperbole — It Doesn't Need Analysis
misconceptions.body: Students recognize overstatement as hyperbole and stop there, as if identifying the label IS the analysis. Push them past the label: what is the author trying to make the reader feel, fear, or accept? The device is a strategy, not a decoration. Teach them to ask "why exaggerate THIS, and why HERE?"

misconceptions.title: Understatement Means the Author Doesn't Care
misconceptions.body: Students misread understatement as indifference or weak writing. In reality, controlled understatement is often the most loaded move a writer makes — the gap between what's said and what's meant does the rhetorical work. Watch for students who dismiss understated passages as "not important" because they're quiet. Those are frequently the moments worth the closest reading.

misconceptions.title: Straw Man Means the Author Is Wrong
misconceptions.body: Students conflate spotting a straw man with disproving the author's entire position. A writer can misrepresent an opposing view AND have a legitimate central argument — the fallacy is local, not total. Teach them to name the fallacy precisely: "In this passage, the author distorts the opposing position by..." rather than "The author is wrong."

misconceptions.title: Red Herring Is Any Irrelevant Detail
misconceptions.body: Students over-apply "red herring" to any tangent — background context, illustrative anecdote, transitions. A red herring is a deliberate argumentative misdirection: it's introduced specifically to shift attention away from a weak point. Teach them the test: does this detail change the subject AT A MOMENT when the argument would be hard to sustain? If yes, red herring. If it just adds context, it isn't.

misconceptions.title: Rhetorical Devices and Logical Fallacies Are Always Intentional Deception
misconceptions.body: Students assume that any fallacy or exaggeration means the author is lying or manipulating in bad faith. Writers use overstatement for comic effect, for emphasis, for emotional intensity — not always to deceive. And authors sometimes commit fallacies through careless reasoning rather than deliberate manipulation.
