Teach students to name the effect on the reader and the purpose of the move, and stay agnostic about intent unless the text supports a claim about intent.

assignments.name: Device Dissection
assignments.body: Students receive a 2-3 paragraph argumentative excerpt with at least two rhetorical devices or fallacies embedded. They annotate each move, name it, and write one sentence explaining its purpose or effect — not just its identity. Annotation is graded on explanation quality, not recognition.

assignments.name: Before and After Revision
assignments.body: Students rewrite a passage that uses understatement so it uses overstatement instead, then reverse the exercise. They write a paragraph explaining how the revision changed the effect on the reader. The point: distortion is a dial, and turning it changes everything.

assignments.name: Fallacy Hunt in Editorial Writing
assignments.body: Students read a newspaper editorial or opinion column and mark every straw man and red herring with sticky notes or digital comments. For each marked passage, they write one sentence: what argument should have been addressed here, and what does the author address instead?

assignments.name: Build the Fallacy, Then Catch It
assignments.body: Students draft a short persuasive paragraph using either a straw man or a red herring intentionally. They exchange with a partner who must identify the fallacy and explain its effect. Seeing the inside of the trick sharpens their ability to catch it from the outside.

mastery: Names a rhetorical device or logical fallacy AND explains the specific rhetorical purpose or argumentative effect it produces in context.
Distinguishes understatement from weak writing and overstatement from sloppy exaggeration — understanding both as intentional strategies.
Correctly identifies a straw man as a misrepresentation of an opposing position, not a general error in reasoning.
Applies "red herring" precisely — to deliberate argumentative misdirection, not to any tangential detail.
Evaluates the effect of a device or fallacy on a reader without conflating that effect with a judgment about the author's overall credibility.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I introduces understatement, overstatement, straw man, and red herring by name and asks students to explain purpose and effect — this is the entry point. English II is expected to analyze a broader and more sophisticated range of rhetorical and logical moves. The ramp from I to II is from naming-with-explanation to sustained analytical argument about how those moves shape a text's persuasive project.

DOK 3 justification: students must reason beyond identification — explaining purpose requires understanding authorial intent and reader effect in context, which demands strategic thinking rather than recall or basic skill application.

STAAR note: this TEK maps to Author's Purpose and Craft questions on STAAR. Stems typically embed a quoted sentence or passage and ask students to explain what the author's move accomplishes — not just name it. Question stems in this entry follow that pattern.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Plan a Piece of Writing Through Generative Strategies
expectation: Plan a piece of writing appropriate for various purposes and audiences by generating ideas through a range of strategies such as brainstorming, journaling, reading, or discussing.
overview: Planning is where most student writing actually breaks down — not at the sentence level, but before a word gets written. This TEK asks students to match their idea-generation strategy to their purpose and audience, not just fill out a graphic organizer because the teacher required one.
studentFriendly: You can look at a writing task, figure out what you're trying to say and who you're saying it to, and use a real strategy — brainstorming, journaling, reading around, or talking it through — to get actual ideas on the page before you start drafting.
questionStems: A student is writing an argument about a local issue for the school newspaper. Which prewriting strategy would MOST likely help the student identify the strongest supporting points?
Read the following journal entry a student wrote before drafting an essay. Which statement BEST describes how the journal entry helps the student develop a central idea?
A student wants to write a personal narrative for an audience of younger students. Which planning strategy would MOST likely help the student identify relevant details for that audience?
Based on the student's brainstorming notes in the box, which idea would BEST support the purpose of an informational essay?
Which revision to the student's prewriting plan would MOST likely improve the clarity of the draft that follows?
The student's notes show two possible angles for the essay. Which angle is MOST appropriate for the stated audience and purpose?
A student reads three articles before drafting a research-based argument. How does this strategy MOST likely contribute to the quality of the student's planning?
Which sentence from the student's brainstorming list would MOST likely develop into a strong thesis for the essay?
A student discusses her topic with a partner before drafting. Which outcome would MOST likely result from this planning strategy?
Based on the student's prewriting notes, which organizational structure would MOST likely fit the purpose of the piece?

misconceptions.title: The Graphic Organizer IS the Plan
misconceptions.body: Students treat the completed graphic organizer as the end product — once the boxes are filled, they think they're done planning. Watch for students who transfer organizer bullets directly into their draft as sentences without developing any of them. Teach them that the organizer is a thinking tool, not a writing substitute; the real plan is in the decisions they've made about purpose, order, and emphasis.

misconceptions.title: Brainstorming Means Writing Down Whatever Comes First
misconceptions.body: Students mistake volume for thinking — they list the first five things that come to mind, circle one, and call it brainstorming. Push them to generate past the obvious. The first few ideas are usually the most generic; the fourth and fifth pass is where original thinking starts. Teach them to ask "what else?" at least twice before they stop.

misconceptions.title: Planning Is Only for Long or Formal Assignments
misconceptions.body: Students assume planning is what you do before a research paper, not before a short constructed response or an in-class essay. They dive directly into drafting short pieces and then wonder why their argument wanders. Teach them that even a 200-word response benefits from sixty seconds of purposeful prewriting — the habit is the same regardless of length.

misconceptions.title: "For Various Purposes and Audiences" Is Decoration, Not a Real Constraint
misconceptions.body: Students ignore the audience and purpose cues on a prompt because they've learned to write a generic essay and swap in the topic. Reframe this: audience and purpose are not about tone softening — they change what counts as a relevant idea. A piece written for a skeptical audience needs different supporting ideas than one written for someone already convinced. Teach them to make a planning decision about audience BEFORE they generate content.

misconceptions.title: Discussing Is Not Real Prewriting
misconceptions.body: Students (and sometimes teachers) treat talk as prep for the real work rather than as a legitimate generative strategy. But the act of explaining an idea aloud, hearing pushback, and being asked "what do you mean by that?" is cognitively demanding planning work. Watch for students who dismiss discussion-based prewriting as "just talking." Teach them to capture what the conversation surfaced — disagreement, new angles, gaps — as planning material.

assignments.name: Strategy Comparison
assignments.body: Students plan the same prompt twice using two different strategies (e.g., brainstorming then journaling). They write a one-paragraph reflection explaining which strategy produced better ideas and why, pointing to specific evidence from each set of notes.

assignments.name: Audience Pivot
assignments.body: Give students a topic and three different audiences (e.g., a city council, a peer, a younger sibling). Students generate a separate brainstorm for each audience and annotate how the audience shifted their ideas — not just their tone, but what counts as relevant evidence or detail.

assignments.name: Prewriting Audit
assignments.body: After students draft an essay, they return to their original prewriting notes and highlight which ideas made it into the draft, which were dropped, and which were missing from the plan entirely. Class discussion: what would have changed if those missing ideas had been planned from the start?

assignments.name: Discussion-to-Draft
assignments.body: Students have a structured five-minute discussion about a topic in pairs, then immediately write for ten minutes without stopping. They annotate the draft to identify which ideas came from the discussion. Debriefing question: did the conversation change what you thought you were going to write?

mastery: Selects a planning strategy based on the task's purpose and audience, not out of habit or convenience.
Generates a range of ideas — including ones they ultimately reject — rather than producing only the first thought.
Makes visible connections between the planning notes and the decisions embedded in the draft.
Adjusts the content of planning (not just tone) when the audience shifts.
Treats planning as a recursive step — willing to return to prewriting when drafting reveals a gap.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I introduces the planning expectation with explicit strategy options listed (brainstorming, journaling, reading, discussing). English II (110.37(9)(A)) carries the same language but students are expected to deploy these strategies with greater independence and purpose-awareness. The ramp is in sophistication of execution, not a change in the named strategies.

DOK 2 justification: planning requires students to apply strategy knowledge to a specific task — it goes beyond recall (DOK 1) because students must make purposeful choices about which strategy fits their purpose and audience, but it does not yet require extended reasoning across multiple criteria (DOK 3/4), which arrives when students revise and reflect on the entire writing process.

STAAR analog note: this TEK surfaces on STAAR primarily through revising/editing passages that include prewriting-stage errors — a student's draft that lacks focus, has an unclear thesis, or mismatches audience. Stems in this entry are written to approximate how STAAR tests planning decisions in that context.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Develop Drafts Using an Organizing Structure Appropriate to Purpose, Audience, Topic, and Context
expectation: Develop drafts into a focused, structured, and coherent piece of writing in timed and open-ended situations by using an organizing structure appropriate to purpose, audience, topic, and context.
overview: This is the foundational drafting move: matching how you organize ideas to WHY you're writing, WHO you're writing for, and WHAT the writing needs to do. Students who never learn this default to one shape for everything — five-paragraph for all, bullets for all, chronology for all — regardless of fit.
studentFriendly: You can look at a piece of writing and make real decisions about how to organize it based on what it's trying to do, who it's for, and what the topic actually needs — instead of forcing every draft into the same shape.
questionStems: Which organizational structure BEST supports the development of the author's central argument in this passage?
The author organizes paragraph X by presenting [claim/evidence/counterargument] MOST likely in order to —
Based on the structure of paragraphs X–Y, the author MOST likely chose to arrange the ideas in this order to —
Which sentence from the passage BEST demonstrates a transition that supports the overall organizational structure?
How does the organizational structure of this passage contribute to the clarity of the author's message?
The author's decision to open the essay with paragraph X rather than paragraph Y MOST likely serves to —
Which of the following BEST describes the organizational pattern used in paragraphs X–Z?
The structure of paragraph X suggests that the author's PRIMARY purpose is to —
Based on the context of the passage, which organizational choice BEST fits the author's purpose and intended audience?
Which revision to the structure of paragraph X would MOST improve the coherence of the passage?

misconceptions.title: Five Paragraphs Fits All
misconceptions.body: Students arrive in English I with one template burned into their heads — intro, three body paragraphs, conclusion — and try to force every assignment into it. Watch for the student who writes a five-paragraph personal narrative, or who gives a three-point breakdown to a two-sided issue. Teach them that structure is a tool, not a rule: the shape of the writing should emerge from what the writing is trying to accomplish.

misconceptions.title: "Organized" Means "Has Headings and Bullets"
misconceptions.body: When students don't know how to structure prose, they default to visual formatting — bullet lists, bold headers, numbered steps — and call it organization. Push them to recognize that structure in prose lives inside the logic of the sentences and paragraphs: what comes first, what depends on what, where the reader needs a signpost versus a push forward. Formatting can mask disorganization as easily as it can support it.

misconceptions.title: The Audience Doesn't Actually Change Anything
misconceptions.body: Students frequently write "for the reader" in the abstract and make no real structural adjustments when the audience shifts. Teach them that structure IS audience accommodation: writing for a skeptical reader requires counterargument early; writing for a general audience requires more context before claims; writing for a specialist can front-load the argument because setup is shared. Structure is where audience lives, not just word choice.

misconceptions.title: Timed Writing Gets a Free Pass on Structure
misconceptions.body: In timed conditions, students assume speed excuses disorganization. Correct this directly: timed writing rewards students who can deploy a structure quickly and confidently — not students who write in whatever order thoughts arrive. Teach them to spend 2–3 minutes deciding structure BEFORE drafting, not after.

misconceptions.title: Context Is Just Another Word for Topic
misconceptions.body: Students collapse "context" into "what the piece is about" and ignore the situational constraints that should shape structure — assignment type, genre conventions, whether the writing is persuasive or exploratory, formal or informal. Push them to ask: What does this genre expect? What has the reader already been told? What does this moment in the conversation require? Context isn't background noise; it's a structural constraint.

assignments.name: Structure Sort
assignments.body: Give students four short excerpts from published texts — a personal narrative, an argument, a how-to explanation, and a compare-contrast analysis. Students identify each structure and then write one paragraph justifying why that structure fits the piece's purpose and audience. No generic labels allowed; they must name specific structural choices.

assignments.name: Audience Swap Draft
assignments.body: Students write a single 250-word draft on an assigned topic, then reorganize it completely for a different audience (e.g., from peers to school board, from general public to a specialist). The goal is NOT a rewrite of content but a rebuild of structure. Students annotate what changed and why.

assignments.name: Timed Structure Sprint
assignments.body: Students receive a prompt and spend exactly 3 minutes creating a structural plan (not an outline with full sentences — just the logic sequence). They then draft for 20 minutes. Afterward, they compare their plan to their actual draft and mark where structure held, where it collapsed, and what they'd do differently.

assignments.name: Structure Autopsy
assignments.body: Students select a passage from their independent reading and diagram its organizing structure — labeling what each paragraph or section is doing relative to the whole. They then write a one-paragraph analysis of whether the structure fits the purpose and audience.

mastery: Selects an organizing structure before drafting that demonstrably fits the piece's purpose, audience, and genre — not just the topic.
Maintains the chosen structure across a full draft without reverting to a default five-paragraph mold when the content gets difficult.
Adjusts structure between timed and open-ended conditions appropriately — showing they can deploy structure under constraint, not just with time to revise.
Explains WHY a structural choice serves the rhetorical situation, not just that a structure was used.
Produces drafts where structure is visible in the logic of paragraphs, not only in surface formatting like headers or bullets.
notes: This is the first entry in the vertical ladder for organizing structure; English II will refine the skill to expect greater sophistication in matching structure to genre conventions and rhetorical context. English III and IV add explicit argumentative and analytical genre expectations on top of this base.

DOK 3 justification: students must make a strategic choice — selecting an appropriate structure — that requires reasoning about the relationship between rhetorical situation and organizational form, not just recall or reproduction of a template.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is tested primarily through the Revision strand of the STAAR Writing test, where students evaluate organizational choices in a draft and select revisions that improve structure, coherence, or fit to purpose. Question stems above mirror that Revision task format.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Develop Drafts with Engaging Ideas, Specific Details, and Commentary
expectation: Develop drafts into a focused, structured, and coherent piece of writing in timed and open-ended situations by developing an engaging idea reflecting depth of thought with specific details, examples, and commentary.
overview: This is where vague writing gets fixed — or doesn't. Students who can assert a claim but can't develop it beyond a surface example are stuck here. The work is teaching them that an engaging idea isn't just an interesting topic; it's a claim with something real behind it, held up by evidence and then explained rather than merely restated.
studentFriendly: You can take a central idea and build it into something that actually holds together — not by repeating yourself or just listing facts, but by choosing details that matter and then explaining what those details actually mean.
questionStems: Which sentence from the draft BEST develops the writer's central idea with specific detail?
The writer includes the example in paragraph X MOST likely to —
Which revision of the underlined sentence would MOST effectively add depth to the writer's argument?
Read paragraph X. The commentary in the final sentence contributes to the draft MOST by —
Which detail from the passage BEST supports the inference that the writer has considered more than one dimension of the topic?
The writer's use of [specific example] in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Which sentence from the draft MOST clearly reflects the writer's depth of thought about the topic?
Based on paragraphs X and Y, the writer develops the central idea PRIMARILY through —
Which of the following revisions would MOST improve the development of the idea in paragraph X?
The writer concludes paragraph X with commentary that MOST nearly suggests —

misconceptions.title: More Details = Better Development
misconceptions.body: Students pile on examples — three, four, five in a row — believing quantity equals quality. What they produce is a list, not an argument. Teach them that one well-chosen example with two sentences of real commentary outweighs five examples with none. Watch for drafts that read like a catalogue and redirect students to ask: what does this detail actually prove?

misconceptions.title: Commentary Means Summarizing the Example Again
misconceptions.body: Students think commentary is restating what they just said. "This shows that..." followed by a near-identical paraphrase of the detail is not analysis — it's echo. Push them to ask what the example proves, what it implies, and what someone who disagrees would say. The commentary is where the writer's thinking lives; if it sounds like the example, the thinking hasn't happened yet.

misconceptions.title: An "Engaging Idea" Is a Surprising Topic
misconceptions.body: Students confuse novelty of subject with depth of thought. They pick edgy or unusual topics believing the topic itself does the work. Teach them that a familiar topic with a precise, arguable angle — "loyalty makes cowards of people who think themselves brave" — is more engaging than a shocking topic with nothing to say about it. The idea has to go somewhere.

misconceptions.title: Specific Details Are Quotes and Statistics Only
misconceptions.body: Students treat specificity as a text-based operation: find a quote, cite a number, done. But a specific detail is any concrete, verifiable particular — a name, a moment, a mechanism, a consequence. Teach them that "students feel overlooked" is vague while "a student who raises their hand and is passed over three times in one class period" is specific, and neither requires a source citation to do real work.

misconceptions.title: Timed Writing Kills Development
misconceptions.body: In timed situations, students abandon elaboration entirely and rush to cover the surface of every part of the prompt. What they produce is wide and shallow. Teach them that a focused draft that develops two points fully is stronger than a draft that touches four points without developing any. Depth is the skill, timed or not — the constraint changes the planning, not the standard.

assignments.name: Commentary Upgrade
assignments.body: Students take a draft paragraph that has a claim and an example but no commentary, and write three different commentary sentences for it — one that explains, one that connects to the broader argument, and one that anticipates an objection. They select the strongest and defend the choice.

assignments.name: Detail Audit
assignments.body: Students highlight every detail in a draft in one color and every piece of commentary in another. Where the draft is all one color, that's the revision target. Class share-out makes the pattern visible without singling students out.

assignments.name: One Idea, Full Development
assignments.body: Students write a single body paragraph — and only one — on a timed topic. The paragraph must include a claim, a specific example, and at least three sentences of commentary that do not repeat the example. Grade only on depth, not breadth.

assignments.name: Idea Sharpening Conference
assignments.body: Before drafting, students pitch their central idea in one sentence and receive three questions from a partner that the idea doesn't yet answer. Students revise the idea to absorb the hardest question, then draft with that sharper angle in hand.

mastery: Selects a central idea with a specific, arguable angle — not a topic, not a thesis-by-formula, but a claim that has something to prove.
Chooses details and examples that are concrete and purposeful, not decorative or generic.
Writes commentary that extends and interprets the example rather than restating it, in both timed and open-ended writing situations.
Distinguishes between showing depth (what the evidence implies, connects to, or challenges) and listing evidence (what the evidence is).
Maintains development across a full draft, not only in the paragraph that received the most revision attention.
notes: This is the first formal introduction to elaboration as a discrete writing skill in the high school sequence. English II (9)(B)(ii) extends the same expectation but with greater emphasis on sustained coherence across longer pieces; the English I focus should be on establishing the habit of commentary as a distinct move from evidence-dropping.

DOK 3 justification: students must apply a strategic approach to selecting details and generating genuine analytical commentary — this is not recall or procedure, it requires making choices about meaning.

The timed-writing clause matters instructionally: students need repeated practice developing ideas under time pressure, not only in open-ended drafting. Both conditions should appear in classroom tasks across the year.

STAAR does not test this TEK directly as a performance task in the multiple-choice section, but the revision and editing strands of the writing portion assess the same elaboration moves. Stems above are written to mirror STAAR's revision-item framing. Note this in planning: the skills here surface most directly in Expository and Argumentative essay scoring.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Revise Drafts for Clarity, Development, and Sentence Effectiveness
expectation: Revise drafts to improve clarity, development, organization, style, diction, and sentence effectiveness, including use of parallel constructions and placement of phrases and dependent clauses.
overview: Revision is where writing actually happens — and this TEK makes that explicit. Students are not editing for correctness; they are rethinking structure, word choice, and sentence architecture. The specific call-outs (parallel constructions, phrase and clause placement) mean students must have command of syntax as a revision tool, not just a grammar rule.
studentFriendly: You can return to a draft you wrote and make it genuinely better — not just fix errors, but restructure sentences, sharpen word choice, cut what's vague, and rearrange ideas so a reader can actually follow your thinking.
questionStems: Which revision of the underlined sentence in paragraph X BEST improves the clarity of the writer's idea?
A student wants to revise the sentence in paragraph X to create parallel structure. Which revision BEST accomplishes this goal?
Read paragraph X. Which sentence should be moved to BEST improve the organization of the paragraph?
The writer wants to revise the underlined phrase in paragraph X to make the diction more precise. Which revision MOST effectively accomplishes this goal?
Which revision of paragraph X BEST improves the development of the writer's central idea?
A student underlined a dependent clause in paragraph X. Which placement of this clause MOST improves the effectiveness of the sentence?
The writer wants to combine the two sentences in paragraph X into one sentence using a subordinating phrase. Which revision BEST accomplishes this goal?
Which sentence from the draft MOST weakens the organization of paragraph X and should be revised or removed?
Read paragraphs X and Y. Which revision BEST improves the transition between these two paragraphs?
The writer wants to revise the underlined sentence in paragraph X to improve sentence variety and style. Which revision MOST effectively accomplishes this goal?

misconceptions.title: Revision Means Fixing Typos
misconceptions.body: Students treat revision as proofreading — they run spell-check, fix a comma or two, and call it done. Teach them that revision operates at a completely different level: restructuring arguments, cutting sentences that don't earn their place, and rebuilding paragraphs that wander. Name the difference explicitly: *editing* is surface-level; *revision* means you are willing to cut a sentence you love because the draft doesn't need it.

misconceptions.title: A Draft Is Good Enough If It Has All the Parts
misconceptions.body: Students believe that having an introduction, body paragraphs, and a conclusion satisfies the assignment. Watch for drafts where every section exists but none of them actually develops an idea — the structure is present but hollow. Push them to ask: does each paragraph move the argument forward, or does it just occupy space?

misconceptions.title: Parallel Structure Is a Grammar Rule, Not a Thinking Tool
misconceptions.body: Students learn parallel structure as a correctness item ("don't mix forms in a list") and never see it as a tool for clarifying logic. The real value of parallelism is that it forces the writer to commit to comparing equivalent things. Teach them to use parallel revision as a diagnostic: if three items in a list won't go parallel, the writer may not actually know what they're comparing.

misconceptions.title: Where You Put a Clause Doesn't Matter Much
misconceptions.body: Students drop dependent clauses and participial phrases wherever they happen to fall in the sentence, usually at the end, usually as an afterthought. Show them that placement controls emphasis — what lands at the end of a sentence carries the most weight. A misplaced modifier doesn't just confuse; it buries the point the sentence was supposed to make.

misconceptions.title: Their First Instinct About Organization Is Correct
misconceptions.body: Students resist reorganizing because they wrote the draft in a particular order and assume that order made sense. They'll defend a paragraph's placement because "that's what I thought of first." Teach them to number their paragraphs and physically rearrange them, then read the result aloud — often the draft reorganizes itself within five minutes when they stop defending the original sequence.

assignments.name: Revision in Three Passes
assignments.body: Students revise a draft three times with a different focus each pass: first for development (does every paragraph prove the point?), second for organization (does the sequence make logical sense?), third for sentence effectiveness (parallel structure, clause placement, diction). They submit all three marked-up drafts with the final version.

assignments.name: Sentence Surgery
assignments.body: Provide students with a paragraph containing five sentences with weak or misplaced clauses. Students revise each sentence at the syntactic level — relocating dependent clauses for emphasis, creating parallel structure in lists, tightening diction — then write a one-sentence explanation of each change.

assignments.name: Before-and-After Exhibit
assignments.body: Students select their two weakest paragraphs from a prior draft and revise them substantially. They present both versions side by side and explain, in writing, what each revision accomplished and why the original version failed.

assignments.name: Peer Revision Protocol
assignments.body: Partners exchange drafts and annotate in three colors: yellow for clarity problems, green for sentences that could be parallel but aren't, and pink for any phrase or clause that seems misplaced. Writers revise from annotations, then meet with their partner to explain what they changed and what they chose not to change — and why.

mastery: Produces a revised draft that is substantively different from the original — not just surface corrections, but restructured sentences, sharpened diction, and reorganized development.
Applies parallel construction deliberately in lists and compound structures, and can explain what the parallelism is clarifying in the logic.
Places dependent clauses and participial phrases at positions of appropriate emphasis within sentences, not just wherever they fit grammatically.
Identifies and cuts or rebuilds sentences that weaken development, even when those sentences are grammatically correct.
Can articulate in writing what each major revision accomplishes — naming the specific problem the revision was meant to solve.
notes: Vertical entry point: English I is the first course where students are explicitly held accountable for syntactic revision — parallel constructions and clause placement are named targets here. English II's 9C ramps by adding awareness of audience and purpose as revision criteria beyond structure and mechanics.

DOK 3 justification: students must apply revision strategies strategically across multiple dimensions (clarity, development, organization, diction, syntax), requiring judgment about which revision moves serve the draft's specific needs — not rule-application, but reasoning about effect.
This TEK is closely linked to reading standards — students who analyze how published authors use syntax (8D) are better positioned to revise their own syntactic choices intentionally.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Edit Drafts for Sentence Completeness and Control
expectation: Edit drafts using standard English conventions, including a variety of complete, controlled sentences and avoidance of unintentional splices, run-ons, and fragments.
overview: This is the sentence-level house-cleaning TEK — students learn that control and variety are both requirements, not trade-offs. A draft full of safe, identical simple sentences is almost as broken as one full of splices. The goal is editors who can spot structure problems AND who know why the fix matters.
studentFriendly: You can read your own draft and catch the places where your sentences fall apart — fragments that start ideas and stop, run-ons that barrel through where a stop sign should be, and splices that stitch two sentences together with a comma and a prayer — and fix them without making every sentence the same.
questionStems: Which edit is needed to correct the sentence in paragraph X?
Which sentence from the draft contains an unintentional fragment?
Which revision BEST corrects the comma splice in paragraph X?
A student wrote the following sentence: [run-on]. Which of the following is the BEST revision?
Read paragraph X. Which sentence should be revised to avoid a run-on?
Which of the following revisions corrects the error in sentence Y while BEST maintaining the writer's intended meaning?
Which sentence from the passage is a complete, controlled sentence that is free of structural errors?
A student wants to combine sentences X and Y into one controlled sentence. Which revision MOST effectively accomplishes this?
Which revision of the underlined sentence BEST corrects the fragment without changing the author's intended meaning?
Based on the paragraph, which sentence should the writer revise to correct an unintentional splice?

misconceptions.title: A Comma Can Join Any Two Sentences
misconceptions.body: Students treat the comma as a universal connector — "I finished the essay, it was really long." They genuinely don't know this is wrong, because spoken language does pause there. Teach them the comma splice test: if you can put a period between the two halves and they both stand alone, a comma alone won't hold. Show them the actual options — coordinating conjunction, semicolon, period — and let them choose, because the choice shapes emphasis.

misconceptions.title: Fragments Are Always Accidents
misconceptions.body: Students think every fragment is a mistake, so they start "fixing" intentional stylistic fragments in mentor texts. Then they think their own fragments are fine by analogy — "but the author did it." Teach them the distinction: intentional fragments are controlled and purposeful; unintentional ones leave the reader waiting for the main point that never arrives. The word in the TEK is *unintentional* — hold them to that precision.

misconceptions.title: Fixing a Run-On Means Adding a Period
misconceptions.body: Students know the run-on is broken, so they sledgehammer it: period, done. The result is a choppy string of simple sentences that loses the logical relationship the writer actually meant. Push them to consider whether the two clauses are cause-effect, contrast, or sequence — then pick the joining strategy that preserves that relationship. Editing is not demolition; it's reconstruction.

misconceptions.title: Variety Means Length
misconceptions.body: Students think "a variety of sentences" means some short and some long, so they alternate deliberately: short, long, short, long. Real variety is structural — simple, compound, complex, compound-complex — and the choice should follow the logic of the content, not a visual pattern. Watch for students who manufacture fake variety without ever changing how their clauses relate.

misconceptions.title: Standard Conventions Are Style Preferences
misconceptions.body: Some students treat sentence-boundary rules as optional — "my teacher in middle school let me write that way" or "it sounds better as one sentence." Reframe it: these aren't preferences, they're signals to the reader about where ideas start and stop. A splice tells the reader the ideas are one thing when they might be two. A fragment promises a full idea and doesn't deliver. Conventions are communication tools, not grammar police.

assignments.name: Sentence Triage
assignments.body: Give students a paragraph with 3–5 embedded errors — one fragment, one run-on, one comma splice, and two correct sentences. Students identify the error type for each and write the corrected version. Debrief focuses on HOW they knew something was wrong, not just whether they fixed it.

assignments.name: Repair and Explain
assignments.body: Students select 5 sentences from their own current draft that they're uncertain about, label each as correct/fragment/run-on/splice, and revise the errors in writing. They turn in the original + revised + a one-sentence explanation of what they changed and why. This makes editing metacognitive, not mechanical.

assignments.name: Mentor Text Deconstruction
assignments.body: Project three sentences from a published essay — one simple, one compound-complex, one deliberately short. Students label structure type and explain what the structure does for the reader. Then they write three sentences of their own, one in each structure, about the same topic. The point: variety is a tool.

mastery: Identifies fragments, run-ons, and comma splices in their own drafts without being prompted to look for errors.
Chooses among multiple valid correction strategies (period, semicolon, subordinating conjunction, coordinating conjunction) rather than defaulting to a single fix.
Produces drafts with observable structural variety — not just sentences of different lengths, but sentences of different grammatical types.
Distinguishes intentional stylistic fragments from unintentional ones and can defend that distinction in a revision note.
Edits without flattening the draft — fixes the error while preserving the intended relationship between ideas.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I establishes the baseline expectation — complete, controlled sentences across a variety of types, with unintentional errors caught and corrected. English II and beyond expect the same control at greater syntactic sophistication and within longer, more complex compositions.

DOK 2 justification: the task is application of grammar knowledge to locate and repair errors — pattern recognition plus procedural execution. DOK 3 enters if students must evaluate competing revisions for rhetorical effect, which is more appropriate to 9(C) or 8(D).

STAAR note: this TEK is tested primarily through multiple-choice revision/editing items that embed error sentences in student-draft contexts. Stems are written to mirror that embedded-draft format precisely.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Edit for Verb Tense and Voice Consistency
expectation: Edit drafts using standard English conventions, including consistent, appropriate use of verb tense and active and passive voice.
overview: Verb tense and voice aren't decoration — they carry the reader's sense of time, agency, and emphasis. English I writers tend to drift tense mid-draft without noticing, and they either overuse passive voice out of habit or misuse active voice in contexts where passive is the correct professional choice. The editing work here is precision work: teaching students to read their own sentences for what they're actually claiming, not just what they meant to say.
studentFriendly: You can read your own draft and catch the moments your verbs are telling the wrong story — wrong time, wrong agent, wrong emphasis — and fix them so every sentence says exactly what you mean.
questionStems: Read this paragraph. The writer shifts verb tense in sentence 4. Which revision BEST corrects this error while maintaining the meaning of the paragraph?
Which sentence from the draft uses passive voice in a way that MOST clearly obscures the responsible party?
The writer uses the verb "was written" in sentence 6. Which revision BEST replaces it with active voice without changing the meaning?
Read paragraphs 2 and 3. Which sentence MOST disrupts the consistent verb tense established in paragraph 2?
Which of the following revisions BEST corrects the inconsistent use of past and present tense in this passage?
In sentence 8, the passive construction is used. In which situation would this passive construction MOST likely be appropriate?
Which sentence from the draft MOST effectively uses active voice to emphasize the subject's action?
The writer wants to maintain past tense throughout the passage. Which revision to sentence 5 BEST achieves this goal?
Read the following sentence: "The report was submitted by the team before the deadline." Which revision MOST clearly strengthens this sentence by using active voice?
Based on the conventions in paragraphs 1–3, which sentence MOST likely contains an error in verb tense consistency?

misconceptions.title: Passive Voice Is Always Wrong
misconceptions.body: Students arrive believing passive voice is a flat error — something to eliminate on sight. But passive voice is correct and preferred in scientific writing, formal reports, and situations where the agent is unknown or irrelevant. Teach them the question: "Does the doer matter here?" If it matters, go active. If it doesn't, or if foregrounding the action matters more than who did it, passive may be the right call. The error is inappropriate passive, not passive itself.

misconceptions.title: Tense Consistency Means Never Changing Tense
misconceptions.body: Students hear "be consistent" and freeze every tense in the document, even when a shift is grammatically required — a historical present inside a past-tense narrative, a general truth stated in present within a past-tense argument. Watch for students who "correct" legitimate tense shifts back into errors because they're pattern-matching instead of thinking. Reframe the rule: tense must be purposeful and signaled, not uniform.

misconceptions.title: The Passive Voice Is Just "The Verb 'To Be'"
misconceptions.body: Students spot "is," "was," and "were" and flag them all as passive. They are not. "She was tired" is a linking verb construction, not passive. "The essay was written by the student" is passive. Teach the real test: passive voice = a form of "to be" + a past participle where the grammatical subject is the receiver of the action. Watch for this conflation repeatedly — it's one of the most durable grammar misconceptions in the building.

misconceptions.title: Editing Verb Tense Is a One-Pass Fix
misconceptions.body: Students find and fix one tense error, feel done, and move on. The real work is reading the whole draft as a timeline: what time frame is established in the first paragraph, and does everything after it stay coherent with that frame? Push them to map the time logic of the piece — not just hunt individual sentences for "wrong" verbs. Assign them to underline every main verb in a paragraph before they edit a single one.

misconceptions.title: Active Voice Always Makes the Writing Stronger
misconceptions.body: Students apply the "use active voice" rule as a performance and produce sentences that are technically active but awkward, thin, or emphatic in the wrong place. Active voice is stronger when the doer is the most important element. When the receiver is what matters — "The suspect was detained" — forcing active voice ("Police detained the suspect") may actually shift the emphasis in ways that distort the meaning. Teach them that the goal is precision, not formula.

assignments.name: Verb Audit
assignments.body: Students underline every main verb in a body paragraph of their own draft — then label each one: past, present, future, or other. They identify any shifts and decide: purposeful or accident? They revise the accidents and annotate the purposeful shifts with a one-sentence justification.

assignments.name: Active/Passive Sort
assignments.body: Give students ten sentences — a mix of active, passive-appropriate, and passive-inappropriate. They sort them into three columns and write a one-sentence justification for each call. Debrief the contested ones as a class.

assignments.name: Revision in Two Passes
assignments.body: Students revise a paragraph twice: once converting all passive constructions to active, once converting all active constructions to passive. Then they choose which version is better for the specific rhetorical situation and explain why in writing. The point is control, not preference.

mastery: Identifies tense shifts within and across paragraphs and distinguishes purposeful shifts from errors.
Correctly identifies active and passive constructions — not just sentences containing "to be."
Revises inappropriate passive voice to active without distorting meaning or emphasis.
Selects passive voice deliberately in contexts where it is the appropriate professional or rhetorical choice and can explain the decision.
Produces an edited draft in which verb tense is consistent across the whole piece, with any intentional shifts marked and justified.
notes: This is the first formal encounter with verb tense consistency and active/passive voice as explicit editing targets in the English course sequence. English II (110.37(9)(D)(ii)) continues the same convention in increasingly complex compositional contexts — longer, multi-source arguments where tense management across embedded quotation and paraphrase becomes the harder challenge.
DOK 2 justification: students apply grammatical concepts (tense, voice) to their own drafts and make editing decisions — skill application with judgment, not mere recall.

STAAR does not test this expectation in isolation through a standalone grammar section; instead, it appears in the revision/editing passages where students select the "BEST revision" among options. The question stems above mirror that passage-embedded format.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Edit Drafts for Pronoun-Antecedent Agreement
expectation: Edit drafts using standard English conventions, including pronoun-antecedent agreement.
overview: Pronoun-antecedent agreement is where grammar becomes a clarity problem. When a pronoun doesn't match its antecedent in number, gender, or person, the reader loses the thread — and in an essay arguing something, losing the thread costs the writer credibility. This TEK is about teaching students to edit with enough distance from their own drafts to catch the errors they read right past.
studentFriendly: You can read your own draft like an editor and fix every pronoun so it matches the exact noun it's replacing — no vague "they," no "it" floating in the void, no switch from "one" to "you" mid-paragraph.
questionStems: Which edit is needed in sentence X to correct the pronoun-antecedent agreement error?
Read this sentence from paragraph X. Which revision BEST corrects the error in pronoun-antecedent agreement?
The underlined pronoun in paragraph X does NOT agree with its antecedent. Which revision BEST corrects this error?
In paragraph X, the writer uses the pronoun [pronoun]. Which word is MOST likely the intended antecedent?
Which sentence from the passage contains an error in pronoun-antecedent agreement?
Read paragraph X. Which revision BEST clarifies the unclear pronoun reference in sentence Y?
The student wants to revise the underlined sentence to correct a pronoun error. Which revision is MOST effective?
Which of the following sentences uses pronouns in a way that MOST clearly matches standard English conventions for agreement?
In paragraphs X–Y, the writer shifts pronoun reference. Which revision BEST corrects this inconsistency?
The student is editing a draft and finds the sentence: [sentence with agreement error]. Which revision BEST corrects the underlined pronoun?

misconceptions.title: "They" Always Means More Than One
misconceptions.body: Students default to "they/their" whenever they want to avoid gendered pronouns — including when the antecedent is clearly singular. Teach them that singular "they" is standard for indefinite or unknown antecedents ("Every student should bring their ID"), but not a catch-all escape hatch. Watch for constructions like "The committee made their decision" alongside "Each member brought their notes" — the logic is different in each, and students need to learn to feel the difference.

misconceptions.title: The Closest Noun Is the Antecedent
misconceptions.body: Students scan backward from a pronoun and land on the nearest noun, regardless of grammar. A sentence like "The principal told the teacher that she needed to sign the form" will almost always get "she = teacher" because "teacher" is closer — even when context points to "principal." Push them deeper: antecedents are grammatical, not just proximate. If the sentence is genuinely ambiguous, the fix is to rewrite it, not to guess.

misconceptions.title: Editing Is Just Reading It Again
misconceptions.body: Students "edit" by reading their draft at the same pace they wrote it — which means they read what they meant to say, not what's on the page. Teach them that agreement errors require a different strategy: underline every pronoun, then draw an arrow back to its antecedent. If the arrow can't land cleanly, there's a problem. The mechanical step is the point.

misconceptions.title: Number Agreement Only Matters for People
misconceptions.body: Students catch singular/plural mismatches with people ("a student... they") but miss the same pattern with things, concepts, and collective nouns. "The data supports their conclusion" — students read right past it. Collective nouns are the worst offenders: "the team," "the jury," "the class" get treated as plural by instinct. Teach them to decide whether the group is acting as one unit or as individuals, and let that decision determine the pronoun.

misconceptions.title: If It Sounds Fine Out Loud, It Is Fine
misconceptions.body: Spoken English allows pronoun drift that written academic English doesn't. Students trust their ear, and their ear has been calibrated to informal speech. "Everybody grab their bag" sounds natural in the hallway; in a graded essay, it needs examination. Teach them that editing for conventions means checking against a written standard, not a spoken one — and those two things are not always the same.

assignments.name: Pronoun Arrow Edit
assignments.body: Students receive a paragraph (student-generated or teacher-crafted) with five pronoun-antecedent errors. They draw a physical arrow from each pronoun to its intended antecedent, identify the mismatch, and write the correction. Then they apply the same technique to one paragraph of their own draft.

assignments.name: Error Autopsy
assignments.body: Collect (anonymized) pronoun-agreement errors from students' own drafts. Display them one at a time. Students name what went wrong, why they think it slipped past the writer, and write two possible corrections — then decide which correction is cleaner.

assignments.name: Collective Noun Gauntlet
assignments.body: Give students ten sentences using collective nouns as antecedents ("the jury," "the data," "the faculty"). They must determine whether each collective is acting as one unit or as individuals, choose the correct pronoun, and write a one-sentence justification for their choice.

assignments.name: Peer Edit Protocol
assignments.body: Students exchange drafts and underline every pronoun. For each underlined pronoun, they write the antecedent in the margin. Any pronoun without a clear, matching antecedent gets flagged for revision. Writers then respond to each flag before returning the draft.

mastery: Identifies the antecedent for every pronoun in a draft and confirms number, gender, and person agreement without prompting.
Recognizes ambiguous pronoun references — cases where the pronoun could plausibly match two different antecedents — and rewrites to eliminate the ambiguity.
Applies correct pronoun agreement to collective nouns, indefinite pronouns, and compound antecedents, not just simple singular/plural pairs.
Uses a deliberate editing strategy (underlining, arrow-tracing) rather than relying solely on a read-aloud pass.
Transfers the skill from isolated exercises to their own drafts — pronoun errors decrease measurably across drafting cycles.
notes: No English I vertical predecessor; this is where formal editing of pronoun agreement is introduced in the high school sequence. English II (110.37(9)(D)(iii)) continues the same convention with the expectation that students apply it independently to more complex prose — compound-complex sentences, longer argumentative essays, and source-integrated writing where antecedents can be separated from their pronouns by entire clauses.

DOK 2 justification: the skill requires applying a rule to a context — students must identify the antecedent, assess agreement, and produce a correction. It is not pure recall (DOK 1) because the antecedent must be located and the relationship interpreted, but it does not yet rise to the extended analysis of DOK 3.

STAAR analog note: pronoun-antecedent agreement is tested on STAAR Revision and Editing passages, typically as an underlined sentence requiring a corrective revision. Stems in this entry mirror that format directly.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: 110.36(9)(D)(iv) — Edit Drafts for Punctuation That Sets Off Phrases and Clauses
expectation: Edit drafts using standard English conventions, including punctuation — commas, semicolons, colons, and dashes — to set off phrases and clauses as appropriate.
overview: This TEK is where convention knowledge becomes editing judgment. Students must know not only what commas, semicolons, colons, and dashes do in isolation, but when to deploy each one to set off embedded material — phrases, appositives, introductory elements, interrupters — without over-punctuating or under-punctuating. The editorial decision is the skill.
studentFriendly: You can read your own draft and know where a comma, semicolon, colon, or dash belongs — and actually put it there — because you understand what each mark is doing to the sentence around it.
questionStems: Which edit is needed in sentence X to correctly set off the appositive phrase?
In sentence X, which punctuation mark BEST replaces the underlined portion?
Which sentence uses a dash correctly to set off an interrupting phrase?
The writer wants to combine sentences X and Y so that the first clause introduces the second without using a conjunction. Which revision BEST accomplishes this?
In sentence X, the comma after [phrase] is unnecessary because —
Which revision of sentence X MOST correctly uses a semicolon?
The writer is considering adding the phrase "[phrase]" to sentence X. Where should the punctuation be placed to set off this phrase correctly?
Which sentence from the passage uses a colon correctly to introduce a phrase or list?
Read sentence X. Which edit correctly punctuates the nonrestrictive clause?
Which of the following is the MOST effective revision of sentence X to correct the punctuation error?

misconceptions.title: Commas Go Wherever You'd "Pause"
misconceptions.body: Students insert commas wherever their internal voice breathes, treating punctuation like stage directions for reading aloud. Teach them that the pause test is a heuristic, not a rule — it explains roughly half the cases and misleads on the other half. Reframe: a comma earns its place by separating a specific grammatical unit (introductory phrase, nonrestrictive clause, items in a series). If they can't name the unit, the comma is a guess.

misconceptions.title: Dashes Are Just Dramatic Commas
misconceptions.body: Students use dashes interchangeably with commas because both can set off a phrase, and dashes feel edgier. Watch for drafts where every interruption gets a dash regardless of whether the emphasis is warranted. Teach them that a dash signals interruption, emphasis, or a deliberate tonal shift — it's a louder mark. When everything is emphasized, nothing is.

misconceptions.title: A Semicolon Goes Between Any Two Long Sentences
misconceptions.body: Students treat the semicolon as a sophisticated period — if the sentence feels long, they drop one in. Push them to articulate the relationship between the two independent clauses before they punctuate: a semicolon implies the clauses are closely linked in meaning, not just nearby on the page. If they can't say why the clauses belong together, the semicolon doesn't belong there either.

misconceptions.title: Colons Introduce Any List Anywhere in a Sentence
misconceptions.body: Students write "I need: time, space, and quiet" — a colon wedged between a verb and its objects — because they've been told colons introduce lists. Teach them that the colon works when a complete independent clause precedes it. If the colon interrupts the main verb's connection to what follows, pull it out. The test: cover everything after the colon and ask whether what's left is a complete sentence.

misconceptions.title: Removing Punctuation Around a Clause Doesn't Change Meaning
misconceptions.body: Students see punctuation around nonrestrictive clauses as optional decoration. Have them compare "My teacher who stayed late helped me" against "My teacher, who stayed late, helped me" — the first implies more than one teacher; the second doesn't. The commas carry meaning. Watch for students who strip them out during editing because the sentence "reads fine" either way — they're not hearing the semantic difference.

assignments.name: Punctuation Swap Lab
assignments.body: Give students a paragraph with all internal punctuation removed from phrases and clauses. Students reinsert commas, semicolons, colons, and dashes and then annotate each choice with a one-phrase justification. Compare results across partners and adjudicate disagreements as a class.

assignments.name: Sentence Revision Stations
assignments.body: Post five sentences around the room, each with a different punctuation error involving set-off phrases or clauses. Students rotate, identify the error type, and write a corrected version on an attached response sheet. Debrief by having students argue their corrections rather than reveal an answer key.

assignments.name: Editing Your Own Draft
assignments.body: Students return to a piece already in their writing portfolio, read for punctuation accuracy around embedded phrases and clauses only, and submit a one-page annotation log: what they changed, what mark they used, and why. The annotation is graded; the essay is not re-graded.

mastery: Correctly places commas around nonrestrictive phrases and clauses and omits them around restrictive ones without being told which is which.
Distinguishes contexts for semicolons, colons, and dashes and can explain the choice between them in a specific sentence.
Edits their own drafts for punctuation without relying solely on a pause-test strategy.
Can identify punctuation errors in sentences other than their own and name the rule being violated.
Produces revised sentences where added or changed punctuation demonstrably alters or clarifies meaning.
notes: This is a sub-expectation of 9(D), which covers editing for standard English conventions broadly. The (iv) sub-item isolates punctuation that sets off embedded material — a more targeted skill than punctuation in general.

Vertical: English I students encounter this as foundational convention work; English II builds on it with more complex sentence structures. The vertical skill ramp is less about adding new marks and more about handling them in increasingly complex syntactic contexts.
DOK 2 justification: the task requires applying punctuation rules to specific grammatical structures — more than recall, less than extended analysis or evaluation.

STAAR analog: 9D.iv is directly tested on STAAR Writing through revision and editing multiple-choice items. Question stems in this entry follow the Revision/Editing section conventions students encounter on the assessment.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: 110.36(9)(D)(v) — Edit Drafts for Punctuation: Commas, Semicolons, Colons, and Dashes
expectation: Edit drafts using standard English conventions, including punctuation — commas, semicolons, colons, and dashes — to set off phrases and clauses as appropriate.
overview: This TEK is where sentence-level craft becomes sentence-level correctness. Students are not just placing punctuation marks — they are making rhetorical decisions about how clauses relate, where pauses land, and what deserves to be set off from the main thought. The edit is the argument about structure.
studentFriendly: You can read your own draft and choose the right punctuation mark — comma, semicolon, colon, or dash — to control how ideas are separated, introduced, or interrupted inside a sentence.
questionStems: Which of the following correctly uses a semicolon to join the two independent clauses in the sentence?
Read this sentence from the draft. Which revision BEST corrects the punctuation error in the underlined portion?
A student wants to use a colon to introduce a list in paragraph 3. Which revision MOST effectively accomplishes this?
Which sentence uses a dash MOST appropriately to set off an interrupting phrase?
Read this sentence from the draft: [sentence]. The comma after [phrase] is MOST likely used to —
Which of the following revisions would MOST clearly separate the two independent clauses without creating a comma splice?
A student revises paragraph 2 by adding a dash after [word]. How does this revision MOST likely affect the sentence?
Which sentence from the draft contains a punctuation error in the use of a colon?
Read the following sentence. Which change, if any, should be made to the punctuation of the underlined phrase?
A student wants to set off a nonrestrictive appositive in sentence 4. Which revision uses punctuation MOST correctly to accomplish this?

misconceptions.title: Semicolons Are Just Fancy Commas
misconceptions.body: Students drop semicolons between a dependent clause and an independent clause, or between items in a list that don't need them, because they associate semicolons with formality rather than grammatical function. Teach them the rule is structural: a semicolon joins two complete thoughts that could each stand alone. If either side can't stand alone, the semicolon is wrong — full stop.

misconceptions.title: The Dash Is a Catch-All for Everything They Don't Know
misconceptions.body: When students are unsure which mark to use, they default to the dash because it "looks like it could work." Watch for drafts littered with dashes where commas, colons, or parentheses belong. The dash signals interruption or dramatic emphasis; teach them that overused, it loses exactly the force that makes it useful.

misconceptions.title: Colons Can Appear Anywhere Before a List
misconceptions.body: Students routinely write "The essay includes: a hook, three body paragraphs, and a conclusion" — breaking a colon rule by placing it after a verb that completes the sentence naturally without it. Teach them the colon follows only a grammatically complete statement. If removing the colon sounds fine, the colon probably shouldn't be there.

misconceptions.title: A Comma Before Any Conjunction Is Correct
misconceptions.body: Students learn the comma-before-FANBOYS rule and apply it without checking whether both sides are independent clauses. They punctuate "She edited her draft, and found three errors" as if it were a compound sentence. Push them to test both sides: can each stand alone? If not, the comma goes away.

misconceptions.title: Punctuation Is a Proofreading Afterthought
misconceptions.body: Students treat punctuation correction as the last-minute cleanup after "the real writing is done." This produces drafts where sentence structure was designed with no awareness of how clauses would actually need to be punctuated. Teach them that punctuation decisions are structure decisions — make them during drafting, not just during editing.

assignments.name: Error Sort
assignments.body: Provide 20 sentences drawn from published student essays and mentor texts, each with one punctuation error involving a comma, semicolon, colon, or dash. Students identify the error, name the rule being violated, and rewrite the sentence correctly. Discuss the five most contested sentences as a class.

assignments.name: Punctuation Autopsy
assignments.body: Students exchange drafts and mark every comma, semicolon, colon, and dash in a partner's essay. For each mark, they write one word: *justified* or *questionable*. Partners confer on every questionable mark and resolve it by citing the relevant rule. Final edit is made before returning the draft.

assignments.name: Sentence Surgery
assignments.body: Give students a paragraph with all internal punctuation removed — only end punctuation remains. Students restore commas, semicolons, colons, and dashes, then compare their versions. Discuss how different punctuation choices change the rhythm and meaning of the same sentences.

mastery: Correctly identifies and repairs comma splices, missing commas around nonrestrictive phrases, and misplaced semicolons when editing their own or a peer's draft.
Chooses between a comma, semicolon, colon, and dash based on the grammatical and rhetorical relationship between clauses — not based on feel or length of pause.
Explains in writing why a specific punctuation mark was chosen or changed during the editing process.
Produces edited sentences where internal punctuation is consistent and rule-governed, with no unresolved comma splices or colon errors.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I establishes the core four marks — comma, semicolon, colon, dash — with emphasis on phrases and clauses. English II (110.37(9)(D)(v)) builds on this by adding more complex syntactic contexts and expecting students to handle these marks with greater automaticity in longer, more sophisticated drafts.

DOK 2 justification: the skill requires applying a rule set to a specific context (the student's own or a provided draft), not just recalling the rule. Extension activities that ask students to explain their choices or adjudicate contested edits can push this to DOK 3.

STAAR context: punctuation editing items appear directly on the Editing strand of STAAR. Stems for this TEK mirror the "which revision corrects the error" and "which change, if any, should be made" frames used on released tests. This TEK has a strong STAAR analog.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: 110.36(9)(D)(vi) — Edit Drafts for Correct Spelling
expectation: Edit drafts using standard English conventions, including correct spelling.
overview: Spelling in English I is an editing skill, not a word-list skill — students are expected to catch and correct errors in their own prose during the revision process, not just pass a Friday quiz. The real work is building the habit of treating every draft as something that needs systematic scrutiny before it goes out.
studentFriendly: You can read your own draft with fresh eyes, catch words you misspelled — including tricky homophones and words your spell-checker ignored — and fix them before anyone else sees them.
questionStems: Which sentence from the draft contains a spelling error that the writer should correct?
In sentence X, the underlined word is spelled incorrectly. Which of the following is the correct spelling?
The writer wants to revise paragraph X to correct all spelling errors. Which version of the underlined sentence uses correct spelling throughout?
Which revision of the following sentence BEST corrects the spelling error?
Read the sentence from the draft: [sentence]. The word [word] is used incorrectly because —
Which of the following sentences from the draft does NOT contain a spelling error?
The writer uses the word [homophone] in sentence X. Which of the following BEST explains why this is an error and what correction should be made?
A student reads the following paragraph and identifies one spelling error. Which word in the paragraph is misspelled?
The writer wants to produce a polished final draft. Which edit MOST improves the correctness of sentence X?
Which of the following correctly replaces the underlined word in sentence X with the properly spelled standard English convention?

misconceptions.title: Spell-Check Did It, So It's Done
misconceptions.body: Students submit drafts assuming spell-check caught everything. It didn't — homophone errors ("their" for "there," "affect" for "effect"), correctly spelled wrong words, and autocorrect substitutions sail through cleanly. Teach them that a spell-checker is a first pass, not a final pass. Require a read-aloud step specifically for spelling so their ear catches what their eyes skip.

misconceptions.title: Spelling Only Matters on "Final" Drafts
misconceptions.body: Students treat spelling as a last-minute cosmetic fix rather than something that degrades clarity at every stage. Watch for students who wave off peer feedback on spelling as "I'll fix it later" — and then don't. Reframe this: a reader who stumbles on misspellings loses trust in the argument before they've assessed the ideas. Spelling is a credibility issue, not just a grammar issue.

misconceptions.title: If I Know What I Meant, the Reader Does Too
misconceptions.body: Students often read through their own errors without seeing them because their brain fills in the intended word. Teach them to read backward — sentence by sentence, from the end of the draft to the beginning — so each word is isolated from the logic of the argument. The goal is to see what's actually on the page, not what they intended.

misconceptions.title: Homophones Are a Vocabulary Problem, Not a Spelling Problem
misconceptions.body: Students think they've "spelled it right" when they've produced a real English word, even if it's the wrong one. Push them to understand that correct spelling means the right word in the right place — "two," "too," and "to" are all spelled correctly in isolation, but only one is right in any given sentence. This requires meaning-awareness, not just letter-pattern awareness.

misconceptions.title: Hard Words Are the Only Words to Watch
misconceptions.body: Students hyperfocus on long or technical words and miss errors in short, common ones — "its/it's," "loose/lose," "of/off," "then/than." Teach them that the most frequent spelling errors in student writing are almost never the exotic words; they're the high-frequency ones used hundreds of times a week. Short words deserve the same scrutiny as long ones.

assignments.name: Homophone Hunt
assignments.body: Students exchange drafts with a partner specifically to identify homophone errors. Each student marks every instance where a real word is used but the wrong one — no marking of other errors. Pairs then discuss and correct together. Grade for thoroughness of the hunt, not just the final product.

assignments.name: Backward Read Proof
assignments.body: Students read their own draft aloud one sentence at a time, starting from the last sentence and working to the first. They mark every word they hesitate on. The hesitation list becomes their personal spelling watch list for the unit.

assignments.name: Error Autopsy
assignments.body: After a graded draft is returned, students compile a list of every spelling error they made, categorize them (homophone, dropped letter, transposition, autocorrect substitution, etc.), and write one sentence of analysis per category explaining why that error type recurred. The autopsy becomes a personal editing checklist for future drafts.

mastery: Identifies and corrects spelling errors in their own drafts without relying solely on automated spell-check tools.
Distinguishes between homophones and selects the spelling that matches the intended meaning in context.
Applies a consistent editing strategy — such as reading aloud or reading backward — to catch errors before submission.
Produces a final draft in which spelling errors do not impede the reader's understanding or trust in the writing.
notes: This sub-expectation is one of approximately eight listed under 110.36(9)(D), each targeting a distinct editing convention. Spelling is the most surface-level of these but is also the one most frequently delegated to technology — which is precisely why it needs explicit instructional attention.

Vertical alignment: English I establishes the expectation of correct spelling in edited drafts; English II (110.37(9)(D)(vi)) carries the same expectation forward with the assumption that students are applying it to more complex genres and arguments. The skill does not escalate in kind but in the sophistication of the writing it must serve.

DOK 1 justification: the editing act itself is recall and procedural application — identify the error, apply the correct convention. The higher-order thinking in Standard 9 lives in revision (9C) and genre awareness (9E); 9D sub-expectations are intentionally procedural.

STAAR note: spelling is assessed indirectly through the Revision and Editing strand of STAAR Writing, where students must select the correctly edited version of a sentence. Stems in this entry mirror that format. No standalone "spelling test" appears on STAAR, but error identification and correction are live in every editing passage.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Publish Written Work for Appropriate Audiences
expectation: Publish written work for appropriate audiences.
overview: Publishing is the moment writing stops being private and becomes communicative — and the word "appropriate" is doing all the real work here. Students must make deliberate decisions about platform, format, and register based on who is actually going to read the piece. The TEK is deceptively simple; the skill it demands is sophisticated.
studentFriendly: You can take a finished piece of writing and put it in front of the right readers in the right format — whether that's a classroom display, a school publication, a blog, a formal submission, or a community presentation — and you can explain why that venue fits that piece.
questionStems: Based on the passage, which publication venue would BEST suit the author's intended purpose and audience?
The author revised the piece before submitting it to [publication/venue]. Which change MOST likely reflects an awareness of that audience?
Which sentence from the draft MOST clearly indicates the writer is addressing a specific audience?
Based on the context of the piece, the writer MOST likely chose to include [detail/section] in order to —
The formatting choices in the final version of the piece MOST likely serve to —
Which revision would BEST prepare this piece for publication in a [formal/informal] venue?
The writer's decision to [add/remove/change] in the final draft MOST likely reflects —
Based on the passage, which statement BEST describes the relationship between the writer's purpose and the selected audience?
The writer's use of [formal/informal/technical] language in paragraph X suggests that the intended audience is MOST likely —
Which change to the draft would MOST improve its appropriateness for the stated audience?

misconceptions.title: "Published" Means Posted Online
misconceptions.body: Students conflate publishing with social media posting — click send, done. Teach them that publishing is a deliberate act of audience selection, not a default behavior. Push them to articulate WHY a given venue fits a given piece before they submit anything. If they can't name the audience, they haven't published — they've just uploaded.

misconceptions.title: Audience Is Whoever Happens to Read It
misconceptions.body: Students treat audience as whoever shows up rather than whoever is being sought. Watch for students who write "my audience is everyone" — that answer is a signal they haven't thought about it at all. Reframe: every publishing decision shrinks the audience intentionally, and that narrowing is a feature, not a failure.

misconceptions.title: Final Draft Equals Published Draft
misconceptions.body: Students assume the piece is done when the teacher marks it complete. But publishing requires a separate layer of decisions — format, length, tone, visual presentation — that a classroom final draft often doesn't address. Teach them to ask: "Would this piece look right in the place I'm sending it? Does it need a different header, a shorter bio, a different font, a citation format this venue requires?"

misconceptions.title: Publishing Is Just the End of the Process
misconceptions.body: Students see publishing as a finish line with no choices left to make. Flip it: publishing is a rhetorical act with its own set of decisions. Where you publish shapes how readers receive the piece, what authority the writer claims, and what the piece is asking the reader to do. The choice of venue is an argument.

misconceptions.title: A Good Piece Works Everywhere
misconceptions.body: Students believe a strong piece of writing can be dropped into any context without adjustment. Teach them that the same essay that earns praise in a classroom might be too long, too informal, or missing required conventions for a specific publication — and that adapting a piece for a venue is a skill, not a compromise of the writer's vision.

assignments.name: Venue Proposal
assignments.body: Before publishing a final piece, students submit a one-paragraph written proposal naming their chosen publication venue, identifying the target audience, and explaining why the piece — in its current form or with stated revisions — fits that audience. Grade the reasoning, not just the choice.

assignments.name: Same Piece, Two Venues
assignments.body: Students take one finished draft and adapt it for two distinct audiences — for example, a letter to the editor and a post for a school literary magazine. They write a brief reflection identifying what changed between versions and why.

assignments.name: Published Portfolio Entry
assignments.body: At the end of a writing unit, students select one piece to formally publish (class blog, school newspaper, community board, literary magazine submission, etc.), make any audience-driven revisions, and include a brief cover note explaining their publication decision.

assignments.name: Audience Audit
assignments.body: Students exchange final drafts and each student writes a one-paragraph "audience audit" identifying the most likely reader, what the piece assumes the reader knows, and whether the format and register match the named venue. Writers use the audit to revise before publishing.

mastery: Selects a publication venue that genuinely matches the piece's purpose, tone, and intended audience — not just the most convenient option.
Makes deliberate revisions to a final draft based on the requirements and conventions of the chosen venue.
Articulates in writing or in conference who the audience is, what they need from this piece, and how the chosen venue reaches them.
