Produces a published piece that meets the formatting, length, and register expectations of the selected context without being prompted to adjust.
Reflects accurately on the gap between a classroom draft and a publication-ready draft — and closes that gap independently.
notes: English I is the entry point for explicit publishing instruction — students arrive with habits of submitting rather than publishing, and this TEK begins the work of separating the two. English II builds on this by increasing the complexity of venue choices and requiring more sophisticated audience adaptation.

DOK 2 justification: the core act is application of audience-awareness criteria to a finished piece; the skill is procedural at its base, though reflection on venue choice introduces DOK 3 reasoning when tied to written rationale.

STAAR does not test publishing directly; stems in this entry mirror how STAAR tests author's purpose and audience decisions in published informational and argumentative texts — the closest analog. This is noted per doctrine.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Compose Literary Texts Using Genre Characteristics and Craft
expectation: Compose literary texts such as fiction and poetry using genre characteristics and craft.
overview: This is the production side of literary reading — students don't just analyze how fiction and poetry work, they have to make something that works. The challenge for teachers is that "genre characteristics and craft" requires students to operate simultaneously as readers, technicians, and artists, which is harder to scaffold than it sounds.
studentFriendly: You can write original fiction or poetry that uses the moves — structure, voice, imagery, pacing — that make those genres do what they're supposed to do.
questionStems: The author MOST likely uses the shift in point of view in paragraph X to —
Which line from the poem BEST demonstrates the use of figurative language to develop the speaker's tone?
How does the structure of the poem in stanzas X–Y contribute to the meaning of the text?
The narrator's word choice in paragraph X MOST likely reveals —
Which sentence from the story BEST illustrates the author's use of pacing to build tension?
In paragraph X, the author's use of [literary device] MOST nearly serves to —
Which detail from paragraph X BEST develops the central conflict?
The shift in the speaker's attitude between stanza X and stanza Y suggests —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the author uses setting MOST likely to —
Which line from the poem BEST supports the inference that the speaker feels —

misconceptions.title: Plot Is the Same Thing as Story
misconceptions.body: Students think a story is just a sequence of events — they hand in a paragraph of "and then, and then, and then" with no arc, no tension, no stakes. Teach them that plot is what happens; story is why the reader cares. Push them to identify the moment their draft would make a reader feel something, and if they can't find it, that's where revision starts.

misconceptions.title: Poetry Is Just Line Breaks on a Page
misconceptions.body: Students default to broken prose — full sentences cut arbitrarily at the right margin — and call it a poem. The real move is understanding that every line break is a decision that creates or withholds meaning. Teach them to read their drafts aloud and notice where the ear wants to pause, where a surprise needs space, where the image should land alone.

misconceptions.title: Using a Genre Feature Once Counts as Mastery
misconceptions.body: A student drops in one simile or writes one line of dialogue and considers the genre box checked. Watch for this especially in fiction: a single sensory detail doesn't make a scene immersive. Reframe genre craft as systemic — the question isn't "did you use it?" but "did it do something every time you used it?"

misconceptions.title: Literary Writing Doesn't Have Rules, Just Creativity
misconceptions.body: Students (and sometimes parents) push back on instruction during creative writing because "there are no wrong answers." Teach them that craft IS constraint — the sonnet's fourteen lines exist to create pressure that free verse doesn't, the short story's word limit forces economy that a novel can dodge. Knowing the rules well enough to break them intentionally is the actual creative skill.

misconceptions.title: Revision Means Fixing Spelling
misconceptions.body: Students consider a draft finished once spell-check clears it. Watch for submissions where the content, structure, voice, and imagery are all first-draft-flat but the words are correctly spelled. Reframe revision as rethinking the architecture: Does the poem end in the right place? Does the story's opening scene earn the rest? Proofreading is the last ten percent — not the whole job.

assignments.name: Genre Imitation Draft
assignments.body: Students select a published short story or poem they've analyzed in class and write an original piece that deliberately imitates three specific craft moves from the mentor text. They submit the draft with a one-paragraph annotation identifying each craft move they borrowed and how they deployed it.

assignments.name: Two-Genre Same Subject
assignments.body: Students take a single subject — a specific memory, image, or moment — and write it once as a short fiction scene and once as a poem. They write a one-paragraph reflection on what each genre forced them to do differently. The assignment reveals that genre shapes content, not just form.

assignments.name: Revision for Craft
assignments.body: Students return to a previous literary draft and revise it targeting one specific craft element — pacing, imagery, point of view, or line breaks. Final submission includes the original, the revision, and a short explanation of every change made and why.

mastery: Produces fiction or poetry that demonstrates deliberate use of at least three genre-specific craft features (e.g., pacing, imagery, speaker's voice, conflict structure, figurative language).
Makes revision decisions based on craft — not just surface correction — and can name the reasoning behind structural or stylistic choices.
Distinguishes between what the genre allows and what a specific piece needs, rather than applying genre features mechanically.
Produces a piece that creates a consistent effect — tension, atmosphere, emotion — sustained across the full text, not just present in a single moment.
notes: Vertical ramp from none (English I entry point): students arrive with varied informal creative writing experience but no standardized prior-course expectation. English II will build on this by requiring more sophisticated control of genre features and extending the range of literary forms.

DOK 3 justification: students must apply genre knowledge strategically to produce an original text — not recall definitions or analyze existing examples, but deploy craft in real writing decisions. Extended creative production with multiple craft constraints qualifies as DOK 3 (strategic thinking/application) rather than DOK 4, which would require critique across multiple frameworks or systemic evaluation.

STAAR does not directly assess original student composition on the reading assessment; question stems here mirror how STAAR tests analogous craft decisions in published literary texts, which is the functional flip of what this TEK asks students to produce. Note this in instructional framing: reading like a writer is the reciprocal skill.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Compose Informational Texts Using Genre Characteristics and Craft
expectation: Compose informational texts such as explanatory essays, reports, and personal essays using genre characteristics and craft.
overview: This is the first time English I students are held accountable for producing finished informational writing that actually behaves like the genre it claims to be. The skill is not "write some facts down" — it's learning that explanatory essays, reports, and personal essays each make different promises to the reader and require different structural and rhetorical moves to keep them.
studentFriendly: You can write informational texts — explanatory essays, reports, personal essays — that use the right moves for the genre, not just fill pages with information.
questionStems: Which organizational structure BEST supports the writer's explanatory purpose in paragraphs X–Y?
The writer MOST likely includes the anecdote in paragraph X in order to —
Which sentence from the essay BEST establishes the writer's controlling idea?
How does the writer's use of transitional language in paragraph X contribute to the coherence of the essay?
The writer's decision to address the reader directly in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Which revision to paragraph X would MOST improve the clarity of the writer's explanation?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, what is the MOST likely purpose of the comparison the writer draws?
Which sentence from the report BEST supports the claim made in the introduction?
The writer MOST likely includes the detail in paragraph X to —
Which sentence from the essay MOST clearly reflects the characteristics of a personal essay rather than a report?

misconceptions.title: Information Is Enough — Genre Doesn't Matter
misconceptions.body: Students treat all informational writing as one task: put facts together and call it done. They hand in a report that reads like a list and a personal essay that reads like a report because they haven't internalized that genre creates distinct reader expectations. Teach them that a personal essay without a distinct authorial voice isn't a personal essay — it's just facts with "I" sprinkled in. Genre is a contract with the reader, not a label on the top of the page.

misconceptions.title: A Strong Thesis Means Stating Your Topic
misconceptions.body: Students confuse naming the subject with making a claim. They write "This essay is about the effects of social media" and believe they have a thesis. Push them to turn every topic statement into an assertion the essay will actually defend or illuminate — something that couldn't be answered with "yes" or a single sentence. Watch for thesis statements that are really just titles in disguise.

misconceptions.title: Personal Essays Should Sound Casual Because They're "Personal"
misconceptions.body: Students conflate personal with informal and submit personal essays that abandon precision and craft in favor of stream-of-consciousness rambling. The personal essay is one of the most technically demanding informational forms — it requires controlling a narrative arc, managing the distance between the experiencing self and the reflecting self, and arriving at an insight. Teach them that "personal" refers to perspective and subject matter, not to the abandonment of craft.

misconceptions.title: Reports Are Just Summaries of Sources
misconceptions.body: Students think a report means gathering information and restating it. They produce Wikipedia-adjacent documents with no organizational logic and no synthesis. Show them that a report has an argument embedded in its structure — the selection of information, the order of sections, and the framing of evidence are all rhetorical choices. Watch for reports where the student has never decided what they want the reader to understand by the end.

misconceptions.title: Transitions Are Filler Words Between Paragraphs
misconceptions.body: Students understand "first, second, finally" and nothing else. They drop these markers between unconnected paragraphs and believe cohesion is handled. Teach them that real transitional craft reaches backward and forward simultaneously — it names what was just established AND signals why the next move matters. A transition is a micro-argument about the relationship between ideas, not a paragraph divider.

assignments.name: Genre Swap Comparison
assignments.body: Students choose a topic and write two short pieces (one explanatory essay, one personal essay) on the same subject. They then write a paragraph identifying the specific choices they made differently for each genre and why.

assignments.name: Report With an Argument
assignments.body: Students produce a report on a local or school issue where the structure itself carries a claim — they must defend their organizational logic in a writer's note attached to the final draft. Graded on both content and structural reasoning.

assignments.name: Thesis Surgery
assignments.body: Collect ten weak thesis statements from anonymous student drafts. Students revise each one from a topic statement into an arguable, specific claim. Then students return to their own drafts and apply the same surgery to their own opening.

assignments.name: Personal Essay Reverse Outline
assignments.body: After drafting, students map their personal essay onto a reverse outline: narrating self → reflecting self → insight. They identify where the transitions between these registers break down and revise two passages where the voice lost control.

assignments.name: Peer Genre Audit
assignments.body: Students exchange drafts and use a genre checklist — structural features, voice register, evidence use, transitions — to identify moments where the draft drifts out of its intended genre. Writers revise one section based on the audit before final submission.

mastery: Produces explanatory essays, reports, and personal essays that are distinguishable from one another by structure, voice, and evidence use — not just topic.
Opens with a controlling idea or thesis that makes a specific claim the text will actually support or explore, not merely a restatement of the prompt.
Uses transitional language that reflects the logical relationship between ideas, not just sequence markers.
In personal essays, maintains a clear distinction between narrating and reflecting, arriving at a stated or clearly implied insight.
Selects and frames evidence or detail deliberately — each paragraph advances the informational purpose, and the student can explain why each piece of content is where it is.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I introduces the three informational subgenres (explanatory essay, report, personal essay) as distinct genre forms. English II raises the bar — students are expected to manipulate genre conventions more deliberately and integrate more complex source material. The move from I to II is from "compose using characteristics" to "compose with control over those characteristics."

DOK 3 justification: Students must apply genre knowledge strategically, making craft decisions across a sustained piece of writing. The task requires more than recall or skill application — it requires reasoning about audience, purpose, and convention throughout the composing process.

STAAR note: Informational writing is assessed on STAAR Writing. Stems mirror the revision and editing tasks students encounter — identifying effective thesis, organizational logic, transitional coherence, and purposeful detail selection.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Compose Argumentative Texts Using Genre Characteristics and Craft
expectation: Compose argumentative texts using genre characteristics and craft.
overview: English I students are writing their first formal arguments — which means they need to internalize what the genre actually demands: a defensible claim, evidence that does work, reasoning that connects the two, and moves that account for the reader's resistance. The craft piece is what separates argument from assertion.
studentFriendly: You can write a real argument — not just an opinion — that uses the specific moves the genre requires: a clear claim, evidence that actually supports it, reasoning that explains the connection, and choices about structure and language that make a reader take you seriously.
questionStems: Which sentence from the student's essay BEST states a defensible claim?
The writer includes the counterclaim in paragraph X MOST likely to —
Which evidence from paragraph X BEST supports the writer's central argument?
How does the organizational structure in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the effectiveness of the argument?
The writer's use of [word/phrase] in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Which revision would MOST strengthen the argument in paragraph X?
Based on paragraph X, the writer concedes that — in order to —
The student's essay would BEST be improved by —
Which sentence from the essay MOST clearly connects the evidence to the writer's claim?
The writer's word choice in paragraph X suggests an intended audience that is —

misconceptions.title: An Opinion Is Not a Claim
misconceptions.body: Students write "I believe capital punishment is wrong" and think they've opened an argument. They haven't — they've announced a preference. Teach them that a claim is a position on a question that reasonable people dispute, stated in a way that demands evidence. Push them to rewrite their opening until someone could argue the opposite with equal footing.

misconceptions.title: Evidence Speaks for Itself
misconceptions.body: Students drop a quote or statistic and move on, assuming the reader sees what they see. Watch for paragraphs where evidence sits in the middle with no explanation on either side. Reframe this: the evidence is the middle child — it needs a setup that introduces it AND a sentence after that does the analytical work of explaining why it proves the claim.

misconceptions.title: Acknowledging the Other Side Is Weakness
misconceptions.body: Students avoid counterclaims because they think raising the opposition undermines them. The opposite is true — a writer who never addresses counterarguments looks like they haven't thought the issue through. Teach them that a well-handled concession-rebuttal sequence is a power move, not a retreat.

misconceptions.title: Longer Essays Are Stronger Arguments
misconceptions.body: Students pad introductions, repeat the claim in every paragraph, and circle back to the same evidence twice, operating under the assumption that length signals effort. The stronger argument is the tighter one. Watch for students who have four body paragraphs but only two actual pieces of reasoning — they've just split one paragraph into two.

misconceptions.title: Structure Is Separate from Argument
misconceptions.body: Students treat the five-paragraph form as a container they pour argument into, rather than a set of rhetorical choices. Teach them that where they put the counterclaim, how they order their evidence, and whether they open with narrative or data are all argumentative decisions that affect how a reader receives the claim. Form is argument.

assignments.name: Claim Surgery
assignments.body: Give students five weak, opinion-only opening sentences. They rewrite each as a defensible, specific claim — then rank them by arguability and discuss what makes one claim stronger to argue than another.

assignments.name: Evidence + Reasoning Sandwich
assignments.body: Students write one body paragraph three times: once with evidence but no reasoning, once with reasoning but no evidence, and once with both. They compare all three and identify which version actually proves the claim.

assignments.name: Concede and Counter
assignments.body: Students pick one strong objection to their own argument and write a full concession-rebuttal paragraph. Grade for whether the rebuttal actually neutralizes the objection — not just whether they mentioned the counterargument existed.

assignments.name: Full Argumentative Draft with Self-Assessment
assignments.body: Students write a complete argumentative essay (4–6 paragraphs) and then complete a structured self-assessment rubric: one sentence naming their claim, one sentence identifying their strongest piece of evidence, and one sentence explaining where their reasoning is weakest.

mastery: States a defensible claim that is specific enough that evidence could confirm or challenge it.
Selects evidence that directly supports the claim and explains the connection rather than expecting the evidence to speak for itself.
Addresses at least one counterargument with a concession-rebuttal move rather than ignoring or dismissing it.
Makes deliberate structural and language choices that serve the argument — can explain why, not just what.
Produces a draft where each paragraph does distinct argumentative work rather than restating the same reasoning.
notes: This is English I's first formal encounter with argumentative writing as a genre with named characteristics — not just "persuasion." The vertical ramp to English II (110.37(10)(C)) expects students to apply the same genre skills with greater sophistication in source integration and rhetorical complexity.

DOK 3 justification: composing an original argumentative text requires students to apply genre characteristics strategically, make craft decisions, and construct extended reasoning — not retrieve or demonstrate isolated skills.

STAAR connection: STAAR Writing tests argumentative composition directly. Question stems here mirror the analytical lens of STAAR's revision-and-editing passages, adapted to the genre expectations of a student-produced essay. The stems target claim identification, evidence quality, structure, and rhetorical purpose — all of which appear in released STAAR items at this level.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Compose Correspondence in a Professional or Friendly Structure
expectation: Compose correspondence in a professional or friendly structure.
overview: This TEK is about genre literacy in written communication — students need to know that the format IS part of the message. A business letter that opens with "Hey!" has already failed before the first sentence of content. English I is where students first formally learn to code-switch between professional and friendly registers in writing.
studentFriendly: You can write a letter, email, or formal request that looks and sounds exactly like it belongs in the situation you're writing for — whether that's a job inquiry or a thank-you note to a friend.
questionStems: Read the letter in the box. The author MOST likely chose a professional structure instead of a friendly one because —
Which sentence from the letter BEST supports the conclusion that the writer intended a formal audience?
The greeting in the letter is MOST likely written as it is to —
Based on the structure of the letter, the reader can infer that the writer's purpose is MOST likely to —
Which revision to the closing of the letter would BEST make it appropriate for a professional audience?
The writer's word choice in paragraph 2 contributes MOST to which of the following effects?
Which of the following changes to the letter would MOST undermine its professional structure?
Based on the format of the letter, the reader can conclude that the relationship between the writer and the recipient is MOST likely —
Which sentence from the letter is LEAST consistent with a friendly letter structure?
The writer includes a subject line MOST likely because —

misconceptions.title: "Professional" Just Means No Contractions
misconceptions.body: Students think they've written a professional letter the moment they replace "can't" with "cannot" and "I'm" with "I am." Watch for letters that use formal vocabulary but entirely skip required structural elements — no date, no inside address, no proper closing. Teach them that professional structure is architectural: the conventions of placement and sequence signal competence before the reader reads a single sentence of content.

misconceptions.title: Friendly Letters Don't Have Rules
misconceptions.body: Students treat the friendly letter as a free-for-all — no greeting, no closing, just a paragraph dump. The friendly structure has its own conventions (greeting, body paragraphs, closing, signature) that are distinct from professional format but equally deliberate. Push them to see both forms as genre contracts: the reader expects certain signals, and a writer who ignores them is breaking an agreement.

misconceptions.title: The Closing Is Cosmetic
misconceptions.body: Students rush the closing — "Sincerely," "Thanks," "Bye" — without thinking about whether the closing matches the register of the rest of the letter. A letter that maintains formal tone throughout and then signs off "Later!" has lost the plot. Teach them that the closing is the last impression: it should confirm the relationship and tone established at the top.

misconceptions.title: One Template Fits All Professional Situations
misconceptions.body: Students memorize one business letter format and apply it everywhere — including emails, which have their own structural conventions. Watch for students who include a full block-letter inside address in an email body, or who omit a subject line because "letters don't have those." Teach them that professional correspondence is a family of formats, not a single form, and they need to read the situation to pick the right one.

misconceptions.title: Audience Doesn't Change Anything That Matters
misconceptions.body: Students who haven't internalized audience will write the same letter regardless of recipient — same word choice, same sentence length, same level of formality. Reframe this: the audience determines the register, the register determines the structure, and the structure is how you demonstrate you've thought about who's reading. The letter isn't about what you want to say — it's about what the reader needs to receive.

assignments.name: Side-by-Side Letters
assignments.body: Students write two letters on the same topic — requesting a day off work (professional) and telling a friend why they missed their plans (friendly). Same content, two structures. Students annotate the structural differences after drafting.

assignments.name: Fix the Format
assignments.body: Students receive four deliberately broken letters — two professional with friendly intrusions, two friendly with misapplied professional elements. They identify every structural error and revise one of the four to correct form.

assignments.name: Real-World Correspondence Portfolio
assignments.body: Over three weeks, students compose three pieces of correspondence: a professional email to a teacher or employer requesting something, a formal letter of recommendation request to an adult who knows them, and a friendly letter to a specified recipient. Each submission includes a one-paragraph self-assessment explaining why they made the structural choices they did.

mastery: Correctly applies all structural elements of both professional and friendly correspondence — greeting, date, inside address (where appropriate), body, closing, and signature — without prompting.
Matches register and word choice to the structural conventions of the chosen format without mixing signals.
Adjusts structural choices when the recipient, purpose, or medium changes — recognizes that a professional email and a professional letter are not identical.
Can explain in writing WHY a structural choice serves the audience and purpose, not just that they followed the format.
Catches structural mismatches in sample correspondence and articulates specifically what makes them wrong.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I introduces both professional and friendly correspondence structures as distinct genre forms. English II builds on this by adding complexity of purpose and audience — students are expected to produce correspondence that not only follows structural conventions but does so with greater rhetorical intentionality. English III pushes further toward audience analysis and adaptation.

DOK 2 justification: The skill is primarily application of learned structural conventions to a new writing task. Some items — particularly those requiring students to evaluate whether a format matches an audience — reach DOK 3, but the core expectation is accurate genre application.

This TEK has a clear STAAR analog in the editing/revision strand: released items frequently present flawed correspondence and ask students to identify the revision that best suits the audience or purpose.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Develop Questions for Formal and Informal Inquiry
expectation: Develop questions for formal and informal inquiry.
overview: This is the entry point to the entire research strand — before students can find sources, evaluate evidence, or synthesize claims, they have to know how to ask a question worth pursuing. The real work is teaching them the difference between a topic (immigration), a question (What causes immigration?), and a researchable question that actually has teeth (How have federal immigration policies since 1990 affected asylum-seeker outcomes in border states?).
studentFriendly: You can turn a subject you're curious about into a focused, specific question that is narrow enough to actually answer and big enough to be worth researching.
questionStems: Which question BEST reflects a narrowed research focus appropriate for a formal inquiry on the topic presented in paragraph X?
Based on the information in paragraphs X–Y, which question would MOST likely guide a researcher toward a specific, answerable claim?
The student's inquiry question in paragraph X is MOST likely too broad because —
Which revision of the question in line X BEST prepares a student for formal research?
A student wants to investigate the topic described in paragraph X. Which question MOST likely represents an appropriate starting point for informal inquiry?
Which question BEST distinguishes between a research question and a topic statement?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the researcher would MOST likely need to narrow the inquiry question in order to —
Which question MOST nearly reflects the kind of researchable, specific claim the author develops in the passage?
The inquiry question in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Which sentence from the passage BEST supports the conclusion that the researcher began with a focused, answerable question?

misconceptions.title: A Topic Is Already a Question
misconceptions.body: Students write "My research question is: immigration" and consider the job done. They've named a subject, not asked anything. Teach them the test: a question has a verb and ends with a question mark. Then push further — "What causes immigration?" is still too wide. The question has to be specific enough that a reasonable person could actually answer it with evidence.

misconceptions.title: Formal and Informal Are Just Fancy Words for Big and Small
misconceptions.body: Students interpret "formal inquiry" as "a big project" and "informal inquiry" as "a quick Google search." The real distinction is in the structure and purpose: formal inquiry follows a defined process with documented sources and a defensible conclusion; informal inquiry is exploratory and open-ended, designed to orient the thinker, not deliver a verdict. Watch for students who treat every research task as informal — they'll stop digging the moment they have a surface answer.

misconceptions.title: The First Question Is the Right Question
misconceptions.body: Students land on a question in the first five minutes and treat revision as failure. They don't know that the best research questions are usually the third or fourth version — refined by what you already know, what sources exist, and what's actually arguable. Teach them to generate at least three versions of their question and evaluate which one is most researchable before committing.

misconceptions.title: Closed Questions Make Better Research Questions
misconceptions.body: Students gravitate toward yes/no questions because they feel answerable. "Did child labor exist in the 1800s?" has an answer in thirty seconds, which means it isn't a research question — it's a fact lookup. Push them toward how, why, to what extent, and in what ways: those constructions require analysis, not retrieval.

misconceptions.title: Informal Inquiry Has No Standards
misconceptions.body: Students treat informal inquiry as a free-for-all — no need to be specific, no need to revise, no need to think carefully about what they're actually asking. Reframe it: informal inquiry is still purposeful. It generates the raw material that formal inquiry refines. Sloppy informal questions produce sloppy formal ones. Watch for students who write one vague brainstorm question and then copy it directly into their formal research plan.

assignments.name: Question Ladder
assignments.body: Students begin with a broad topic and write five successive questions, each narrower than the last. They annotate each step: why is this version better than the one before? What did you cut, and why? Final product is the ladder itself plus one paragraph defending their final question.

assignments.name: Formal vs. Informal Sort
assignments.body: Give students twelve questions on cards. Half are appropriate for formal inquiry; half are informal exploratory prompts. Students sort and defend, then discuss the gray-area cases — the ones reasonable people could classify either way.

assignments.name: Question Autopsy
assignments.body: Provide three actual student-generated research questions from previous years (anonymized). Students diagnose what's wrong with each — too broad, closed, not arguable, not researchable — and rewrite each one into a functional inquiry question with a one-sentence explanation of the fix.

assignments.name: Driving Question Workshop
assignments.body: Students choose a topic from a current unit, generate three candidate research questions, then get feedback from a peer before selecting and refining their final question. The inquiry question becomes the anchor for a subsequent research task.

mastery: Distinguishes between a topic, a general question, and a focused, researchable inquiry question — and can articulate the difference in their own words.
Generates multiple versions of a question and revises based on scope, arguability, and the availability of evidence.
Correctly identifies whether a question is appropriate for formal or informal inquiry and explains why.
Produces inquiry questions using analytical constructions (how, why, to what extent) rather than closed yes/no framing.
Treats question development as a recursive process — returns to the question after early research and refines it when scope or focus shifts.
notes: Vertical alignment: This is English I's entry into the research strand — no prior course standard. English II's 11A will extend this by adding the expectation that students critique and revise their inquiry questions in relation to gathered sources. English I's job is to build the habit of asking before searching.

DOK 3 justification: Developing a question that is specific, arguable, and researchable requires strategic thinking — students must evaluate scope, anticipate the research landscape, and make choices about what is and isn't answerable. It is not recall (DOK 1) or simple skill application (DOK 2); it requires planning and self-monitoring.

STAAR analog note: This TEK is not directly tested in its own right on STAAR; instead it underpins the research scenario tasks embedded in the writing component. The question stems above mirror how STAAR frames the research scenario — students encounter a student researcher and evaluate the quality of that researcher's inquiry process, including the quality of their questions.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Critique the Research Process at Each Step
expectation: Critique the research process at each step to implement changes as needs occur and are identified.
overview: This TEK turns the research unit into a self-correcting process, not a one-pass march to a bibliography. Students aren't just doing research — they're evaluating their own process in real time and adjusting before the train leaves the track. The skill is metacognitive and procedural at once: identifying where a step went wrong AND knowing what to do about it.
studentFriendly: You can stop at any point in a research project, look honestly at what you've done so far, spot where things went sideways, and fix the process before the problem compounds.
questionStems: The student in the passage realized her sources were too narrow after completing her outline. Which revision to her research process would MOST likely address this problem?
Based on paragraph X, which step in the research process did the student fail to evaluate critically before moving forward?
Which of the following questions would BEST help a researcher identify a flaw in the source-selection step of the research process?
A student discovers midway through drafting that two of her primary sources contradict each other. Which action would MOST likely strengthen the research process at this stage?
Which sentence from the passage BEST supports the conclusion that the researcher identified a gap in her process before completing the project?
The researcher in paragraph X chose to revise his research question after gathering initial sources. This decision MOST likely reflects —
Which of the following changes would BEST improve the research process described in paragraphs X–Y?
Based on the passage, which step in the research process would have MOST benefited from earlier self-evaluation?
A student's research log shows she did not reconsider her search terms after her first round of results. What is the MOST likely consequence of this choice?
Which statement MOST accurately describes the difference between completing a research step and critiquing it?

misconceptions.title: Critique Happens at the End
misconceptions.body: Students treat the research process like a form to fill out: gather sources, take notes, draft, done. They save any reflection for the conclusion paragraph — by which point it's too late to fix anything. Teach them that critique is embedded in each transition between steps: before you move from gathering to organizing, you stop and evaluate what you have. The end-of-project reflection is a postmortem, not the critique itself.

misconceptions.title: Finding a Problem Means Starting Over
misconceptions.body: When students identify a flaw mid-process — wrong search terms, a biased source base, a question that's too broad — they panic and assume the whole thing is ruined. Watch for learned helplessness dressed up as self-awareness. Reframe it: noticing a problem at step three is a success, not a failure. The question isn't "do I restart?" but "what's the minimum targeted adjustment that fixes this without torching what's working?"

misconceptions.title: Good Research Doesn't Need Mid-Course Adjustment
misconceptions.body: High-performing students sometimes resist critique because they believe strong researchers get it right the first time. This is the most dangerous misconception in the room — it turns self-evaluation into a punishment for weakness rather than a mark of sophistication. Show them published researchers' revision logs, editorial histories, and retraction notices. Expert researchers change direction constantly; only novices treat the first plan as sacred.

misconceptions.title: Critiquing the Process Means Critiquing the Sources
misconceptions.body: Students collapse "critiquing the process" into "evaluating sources" because source credibility is the only research-evaluation skill they've been explicitly taught. But process critique is upstream: Am I asking the right question? Are my search terms too narrow? Have I diversified my source types? Did I record enough information to go back? Push them to distinguish between the quality of individual sources and the quality of the decisions that led them to those sources.

misconceptions.title: Identifying a Problem Is Enough
misconceptions.body: Students believe that writing "I should have used more sources" in their reflection constitutes process critique. It doesn't — that's just naming a symptom. Teach them that a real critique names the problem, explains when and why it emerged, and specifies what change was or should be made. "I should have used more sources" becomes: "After outlining, I found that three of my four subtopics relied on the same two articles. I adjusted by running a new search limited to peer-reviewed sources from the last five years."

assignments.name: Research Checkpoint Log
assignments.body: Students maintain a running log with a brief entry at the end of each research phase (question formation, source gathering, note-taking, organizing, drafting). Each entry answers three questions: What did I find? What's missing or wrong? What am I changing and why? Graded for honesty and specificity, not just completion.

assignments.name: The Mid-Process Pivot
assignments.body: At a designated midpoint in a research unit, students stop, read what they have, and write a one-page critique identifying one thing that isn't working and one specific adjustment they will make. They then execute the adjustment and submit both the original plan and the revised one.

assignments.name: Process Autopsy
assignments.body: After completing a research project, students annotate their own research timeline: where did they stay on track, where did they drift, and where did they catch a problem before it damaged the product? The annotation is submitted alongside the final paper as a required component.

assignments.name: Broken Research Diagnosis
assignments.body: Provide students with a fictional researcher's notes and process log that contains embedded flaws (circular sources, a question that drifted, missing step evaluation). Students identify each flaw, explain why it's a problem, and recommend a targeted fix for each.

mastery: Stops at each major research transition and produces a brief, specific written evaluation of what is working, what is missing, and what adjustment is needed — without being prompted.
Distinguishes between evaluating individual sources and evaluating the decisions that shaped the overall research process.
Names a research problem with precision: what it is, when it emerged, and what specific change addresses it — not just that a problem exists.
Treats mid-process adjustment as normal and expected rather than as evidence of failure.
Demonstrates an adjusted product that reflects identified critique — the revision is visible and traceable in the final work.
notes: Vertical alignment: Standard 11(B) does not appear in an English I predecessor — this is an entry-level treatment of research metacognition. English II (110.37(11)(B)) deepens this to include evaluating the reliability of the process itself against disciplinary research conventions. The ramp is from "notice and fix" to "evaluate against standards."

DOK 4 justification: Critiquing a multi-step process in real time, proposing and executing adjustments, and reflecting on the quality of one's own inquiry requires extended thinking that goes beyond applying or analyzing any single step — it requires synthesis across the full arc of the process.

STAAR analog note: Standard 11 is rarely tested as a discrete skill on STAAR; the testing analog is informational reading about research processes or paired passages where a researcher's process is evaluated. Question stems reflect how STAAR frames those scenario-based research process questions.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Develop and Revise a Research Plan
expectation: Develop and revise a plan.
overview: Research without a plan is just wandering. This TEK is about the metacognitive discipline of structuring inquiry before it starts and then adjusting that structure when reality pushes back — which it always does. The revision piece is what separates this from a checklist.
studentFriendly: You can map out a research plan before you start digging, then adjust it when your sources take you somewhere unexpected or your question turns out to be too big, too small, or the wrong question entirely.
questionStems: Read the research plan in the box. Which revision to the plan would MOST likely improve the student's ability to answer the research question?
A student is researching the effects of social media on teen mental health. Which addition to the plan would BEST help narrow the research question?
Based on the research plan, which step is MOST likely missing that would help the student locate credible sources?
The student revised her research plan after finding that her original question was too broad. Which change BEST reflects an effective revision?
Which sentence from the research plan MOST clearly identifies the student's central inquiry question?
A student's original plan focused on national statistics, but most available sources address local data. Which revision to the plan would BEST address this problem?
Read the two versions of the research plan. Which revision in Version 2 MOST improves the organization of the student's inquiry?
Based on the research plan, which research question is MOST likely to produce focused, manageable findings?
The student added a step to her plan after her first round of research. Which addition BEST suggests she is responding to a gap in her sources?
Which detail from the plan MOST clearly indicates that the student has considered multiple types of sources?

misconceptions.title: A Plan Is a Contract, Not a Map
misconceptions.body: Students treat the research plan as a document they must follow exactly — so when their question evolves or their sources run dry, they freeze instead of revise. Teach them that a plan's value is in the thinking it records, not the steps it locks in. Revision of the plan is a sign of intellectual growth, not failure. Build in at least one structured revision checkpoint so that changing the plan feels expected, not like cheating.

misconceptions.title: "Develop a Plan" Means Write a List of Topics
misconceptions.body: Students produce a bulleted list of subtopics and call it a research plan. Watch for plans with no research question, no source strategy, and no timeline — just a topic outline. Push them to define what they're actually trying to find out, where they'll look, and how they'll know when they have enough. The question drives everything; the list of topics is just scaffolding.

misconceptions.title: Revision Means Starting Over
misconceptions.body: When students hit a dead end or find a contradiction in their sources, they think the plan is broken and want to scrap it. Teach them that revision is surgical — adjust the question, swap a source type, reorder the steps. The whole plan rarely needs to be rebuilt. Show them a marked-up plan with targeted changes so they can see what productive revision actually looks like on paper.

misconceptions.title: The Research Question Is Fixed Before You Start
misconceptions.body: Students think they must have a perfect, final question before they can plan. They get paralyzed trying to make it perfect. Reframe this: the first question is a working hypothesis, not a thesis. Research plans in professional inquiry are always tentative. Model writing a rough question, building a plan around it, and then revising both after preliminary reading.

misconceptions.title: Planning Is Busywork Before the "Real" Research
misconceptions.body: Students want to get to the sources immediately and see the plan as a box to check before the teacher lets them go. Watch for students who fill in the plan template after the fact — writing down what they already did. Make the plan functional: require that students bring it to research sessions and annotate it as they work. The plan should show wear.

assignments.name: Two-Column Plan and Reality
assignments.body: Students develop an initial research plan, then conduct one round of source gathering. Afterward, they fill in a second column documenting what changed — what sources didn't exist, what the question became, which steps were skipped or added. The comparison IS the assignment.

assignments.name: Plan Autopsy
assignments.body: Give students a completed but flawed research plan (teacher-created). They must identify three specific weaknesses and write revised versions of those steps. Criteria: each revision must target a real problem, not just reword the original.

assignments.name: Checkpoint Conference
assignments.body: Mid-research, students meet individually with the teacher and bring their working plan. They must explain one thing they changed and why. The conference grades for intellectual honesty — students who say "nothing changed" need a closer look.

assignments.name: Question Stress Test
assignments.body: Before students build a full plan, they write their research question and submit it for peer review. Peers ask: Is it too broad? Too narrow? Is it actually a question or just a topic? Students revise the question before the plan is built — so the plan starts on solid ground.

mastery: Produces a research plan that includes a focused inquiry question, a source strategy, and a working timeline — not just a topic list.
Revises the plan at least once in response to a specific problem encountered during research, with a written explanation of what changed and why.
Distinguishes between revising the question (scope or focus) and revising the method (source types, search strategies) — and chooses the right kind of revision for the problem.
Brings the plan to research sessions and uses it as a working document, not a form turned in and forgotten.
Can articulate why their final plan looks different from their first plan — tracing the intellectual decisions that drove revision.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I introduces developing and revising a research plan as a foundational inquiry habit. English II builds on this by adding complexity in source diversity and argument integration. The revision component — adjusting the plan in response to what research actually yields — is the discipline this TEK is trying to install at the freshman level.

DOK 3 justification: students must apply planning strategies to a novel research context AND analyze the plan's adequacy in light of findings, then revise accordingly. This requires strategic thinking beyond recall or skill application.

STAAR does not directly test research planning as a discrete process skill; stems above mirror how STAAR tests research-process reasoning through scenario-based questions and plan-revision prompts on the Writing portion. Note this in instructional context.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Modify the Major Research Question to Refocus the Research Plan
expectation: Modify the major research question as necessary to refocus the research plan.
overview: Research questions are not contracts — they're hypotheses about what's worth investigating. This TEK asks students to treat revision as a sign of intellectual growth, not failure. The real work is recognizing when the question no longer fits what the evidence is actually revealing.
studentFriendly: You know how to step back in the middle of a research project, look at what you've actually found, and rewrite your guiding question so it matches the real investigation — not the one you planned at the start.
questionStems: Read the research plan in the box below. Based on the sources the student has gathered, which revision to the major research question would BEST refocus the research plan?

A student's original research question is [question]. After reviewing three sources, the student discovers that [new finding]. Which revised research question MOST effectively incorporates this new information?
Which detail from the sources MOST likely indicates that the student's original research question needs to be modified?
Based on paragraphs X–Y of the research notes, which revised question would BEST narrow the scope of the student's investigation?

The student's research question was [question], but the sources consistently address [related topic] instead. Which modification to the research question would MOST likely produce a more focused research plan?
Which revised research question MOST likely reflects what the student's gathered evidence actually supports?
A student notices that her original question is too broad to answer in a single paper. Which revision BEST refocuses the research plan?
Based on the sources listed, which modification to the research question would MOST likely lead the student to more relevant evidence?
Which sentence from the student's research notes BEST supports the conclusion that the major research question should be modified?

After reviewing the sources, the student concludes that the original question cannot be answered with available evidence. Which revised question would MOST nearly address the same topic while remaining researchable?

misconceptions.title: Changing the Question Means Starting Over
misconceptions.body: Students treat modifying the research question as an admission that their whole project is broken — so they refuse to do it and instead try to force their evidence into an ill-fitting original question. Teach them that professional researchers revise their questions routinely; the first question is a starting hypothesis, not a commitment. Frame modification as the research working, not failing.

misconceptions.title: A "Good" Research Question Never Changes
misconceptions.body: Students internalize the idea that getting the question right the first time is the goal, so revising it feels like incompetence. Watch for students who quietly abandon half their sources rather than adjust the question, because adjusting feels like admitting error. Reframe this: a question that doesn't evolve means you didn't learn anything from your sources.

misconceptions.title: "Refocus" Means "Make It Narrower" — Always
misconceptions.body: Students default to narrowing as the only valid modification move. But refocusing can also mean broadening a question that's too specific for available sources, shifting the angle of approach, or changing the population or time period. Push them to consider what kind of mismatch they're actually fixing before they decide how to fix it.

misconceptions.title: The New Question Just Describes the Sources They Already Found
misconceptions.body: When students do modify, they often swing too far — rewriting the question to perfectly match whatever sources they happened to find, rather than asking whether those sources actually address something worth investigating. Watch for research questions that are just summaries of three websites. The modified question still has to be intellectually generative, not just convenient.

misconceptions.title: Modification Happens Once, at the End
misconceptions.body: Students treat question revision as a one-time checkpoint rather than an ongoing process. They modify once when the teacher requires it and consider the task complete. Teach them that strong researchers loop back to the question repeatedly — after each batch of sources, after drafting, after peer review. Modification is a habit of mind, not a box to check.

assignments.name: Question Audit
assignments.body: At the midpoint of a research project, students write their original question, list three things they've learned from sources that surprised or challenged it, then write a revised question that reflects what the evidence is actually pointing toward. They submit a one-paragraph rationale explaining what changed and why.

assignments.name: Before/After Research Question Gallery
assignments.body: Students post their original and revised research questions side by side on a shared document. The class reviews each pair and rates the revision: Did it genuinely refocus the plan? Did it just get narrower? Did it dodge the hard parts? Discussion follows.

assignments.name: Source Mismatch Diagnosis
assignments.body: Give students a pre-written research question and a set of four sources that don't quite answer it. Students diagnose the mismatch — is the question too broad, too narrow, off-angle? — then write a revised question that the sources could actually support. They explain their diagnosis in two sentences.

mastery: Identifies the specific reason a research question needs modification — too broad, too narrow, unsupported by available evidence, or misaligned with what sources actually address.
Writes a revised research question that is more precisely matched to the evidence while remaining genuinely investigative.
Produces a brief rationale connecting what the sources revealed to why the question changed.
Treats question modification as a recursive process rather than a single required step.
Distinguishes between a question that needs refinement and a question that has fundamentally broken down and requires a new direction.
notes: This is the first course in the vertical sequence — no verticalPrev applies. English II picks up the same skill (110.37(11)(D)) with greater independence expected in source evaluation and rationale complexity.

DOK 3 justification: students must evaluate the relationship between their question and their evidence and make a strategic decision about revision — this requires strategic thinking beyond recall or skill application.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog; question stems are written to mirror how STAAR tests research-process reasoning through scenario-based items presenting a student's research situation and asking about the BEST revision or next step.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Locate Relevant Sources
expectation: Locate relevant sources.
overview: Finding sources is not the same as finding good sources — and English I students routinely confuse proximity (what Google surfaces first) with relevance (what actually addresses the research question). This TEK is the intake valve for the entire research process: if students can't locate relevant sources, everything downstream fails.
studentFriendly: You can find sources that actually connect to your research question — not just whatever shows up first in a search — by knowing where to look, what to look for, and how to tell the difference between relevant and merely related.
questionStems: A student is researching the long-term effects of deforestation on local wildlife. Which source would MOST likely provide the BEST relevant information for this research?
Based on the research question in paragraph 1, which search term would MOST likely locate the MOST useful sources?
Which of the following sources would be LEAST relevant to a research project on the history of public education in Texas?
The student in paragraph 2 wants to find peer-reviewed information about teen mental health. Which type of source would MOST likely meet this need?
A researcher finds a source published in 1987 on renewable energy technology. This source is MOST likely —
Which sentence from the source description BEST indicates that the source is relevant to the student's research topic?
A student's research question focuses on the economic impact of immigration. Which source would MOST likely be irrelevant to this question?
Based on the information in paragraphs 3–4, which action would MOST likely help the student locate more relevant sources?
The student wants to narrow her search results to academic sources only. Which search strategy would MOST likely accomplish this?
Which statement BEST explains why a source with a broad title might still be relevant to a narrow research question?

misconceptions.title: The First Result Is the Best Result
misconceptions.body: Students treat search engine ranking as a proxy for relevance — if it shows up on page one, it must be what they need. Teach them that algorithms optimize for popularity and click-through, not accuracy or alignment with a specific research question. Run a live search in class and have them evaluate the first five results against a research question; the gap between "what ranked" and "what fits" is almost always immediately visible.

misconceptions.title: "About the Same Topic" Equals Relevant
misconceptions.body: Students conflate topic overlap with genuine relevance. A source about climate change is not automatically relevant to a research question about the economic impact of carbon taxes on rural communities. Push them to test every source against the specific research question, not the broad subject area — relevance is a relationship between source and question, not between source and topic cloud.

misconceptions.title: Relevance Is Fixed
misconceptions.body: Students think a source is either relevant or not, as if it's a binary property of the source itself. In reality, relevance is relational and purpose-driven — the same article can be highly relevant for one question and useless for another. Watch for students who accept or reject sources before they've read enough to know what the source actually argues. Teach them: relevance is determined after reading, not from the title alone.

misconceptions.title: More Sources Always Means Better Research
misconceptions.body: Students under pressure pad their source lists — they locate ten sources when three strong, relevant ones would do more work. The instinct is grade-driven ("the rubric says five sources") rather than argument-driven. Reframe the standard: the question is not how many sources you can find, but whether each source is doing a job no other source on the list is already doing.

misconceptions.title: Databases Are for Advanced Researchers
misconceptions.body: Students avoid library databases because they feel intimidating or "too academic." They default to open-web searches even when better tools are available. Teach them that databases like EBSCO, JSTOR, or ProQuest are just search engines with better filters and more reliable sources — the skill is nearly identical, the results are dramatically different. Show them the same search run in Google and in a database side by side; the contrast is the lesson.

assignments.name: Relevance Triage
assignments.body: Give students a specific research question and a list of ten sources (titles, brief descriptions, and URLs). Students rate each source on a 1–3 scale for relevance and write one sentence justifying each rating. Debrief as a class, focusing on disagreements.

assignments.name: Same Question, Three Databases
assignments.body: Students run the same research question through Google, a school library database, and one subject-specific resource (e.g., a government database). They collect the top three results from each, then compare: which sources overlapped, which were unique, and which were most relevant. One-page written comparison.

assignments.name: Source Audit
assignments.body: Students bring in five sources from a previous or current research project. Working in pairs, they challenge each other's sources: is this actually relevant to your question, or just related to your topic? Each student revises or defends at least two sources based on the conversation.

mastery: Identifies sources that directly address the specific research question rather than the general topic area.
Uses multiple search tools and databases strategically, not just open-web search engines.
Evaluates relevance based on content after reading, not title or ranking alone.
Can articulate why a given source is or is not relevant to a specific question in precise terms.
Adjusts search strategies when initial results are insufficiently relevant rather than accepting whatever first appears.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I establishes the foundational skill of locating relevant sources. English II (110.37(11)(E)) raises the bar by requiring students to evaluate sources for credibility and bias in addition to relevance — the move from "does this fit my question?" to "can I trust what it says and why is this source making this argument?" This entry covers the intake step only.

DOK 2 justification: locating relevant sources requires applying a standard (relevance to a specific question) to concrete cases — it is a skill-level decision, not a recall task, but it does not yet require the sustained analysis of DOK 3.

STAAR does not test source-location directly as a stand-alone skill, but research process questions appear in Expository and Argumentative writing contexts. Question stems above mirror how STAAR frames research scenario items — students reading a passage about a researcher's process and asked to evaluate source choices.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Synthesize Information from a Variety of Sources
expectation: Synthesize information from a variety of sources.
overview: Synthesis is the hardest research move English I students face — and the one they most reliably fake. They find three sources, copy a sentence from each, and call it a paragraph. The real skill is building a claim from the convergence of sources, not assembling a quote collage.
studentFriendly: You can pull information from multiple sources — articles, books, data sets, interviews — weave it together into a single coherent argument, and make a point none of the individual sources made alone.
questionStems: Which statement BEST synthesizes the information presented in both passages?
Based on the information in paragraphs X and Y, which conclusion is MOST supported by both sources?
A student conducting research on [topic] would MOST likely use information from both sources to support which claim?
How does the information in Source 2 contribute to the claim introduced in Source 1?
Which sentence from Source 3 BEST connects to the central argument developed in Source 1?
Based on all three sources, which of the following statements MOST accurately represents the relationship between [concept A] and [concept B]?
With which statement would the authors of both Source 1 and Source 2 MOST likely agree?
A researcher synthesizing Sources 1 and 2 would MOST likely conclude that —
Which detail from Source 2 BEST supports the inference drawn from the data presented in Source 3?
The information in paragraph X of Source 1, combined with the information in Source 2, MOST strongly suggests —

misconceptions.title: Quoting from Multiple Sources Is the Same as Synthesizing
misconceptions.body: Students believe that pasting one sentence from Source 1, one from Source 2, and one from Source 3 into a paragraph constitutes synthesis. Teach them that synthesis is a transaction — the sources must talk to each other, and the student's thinking must explain what they say when they do. Watch for paragraphs with no student-generated sentences between the quotations; that's a collage, not an argument.

misconceptions.title: Synthesis Means Finding Agreement
misconceptions.body: Students assume synthesizing sources means locating the thing they all agree on. Push them deeper: sources can synthesize through tension, qualification, or contradiction — where Source A makes a claim, Source B complicates it, and Source C provides the condition under which each is true. The richest synthesis often lives in the disagreement, not the consensus.

misconceptions.title: The Most Recent Source Is the Most Important
misconceptions.body: When students gather multiple sources, they often anchor to whichever one they read last or found first on a search page. Teach them that recency doesn't determine relevance or authority — a 2003 longitudinal study may outweigh a 2024 opinion piece depending on the claim. Synthesis requires evaluating which source carries more weight for which part of the argument.

misconceptions.title: You Need to Use Everything Equally
misconceptions.body: Students think a fair synthesis gives each source the same amount of attention. In practice, different sources do different work — one source might establish context, another provide data, a third supply an expert counterpoint. Teach them to let the argument decide how much airtime each source gets, not a self-imposed fairness rule that produces flat, mechanical writing.

misconceptions.title: Synthesis Happens in One Pass
misconceptions.body: Students treat synthesis as a single step at the end of research — "now I write it up." Watch for students who stop reading once they have enough quotes. Real synthesis is recursive: reading a fourth source should prompt them to revisit how they understood the first three. Teach them that synthesis is an act of rethinking, not just assembling.

assignments.name: The Convergence Draft
assignments.body: Students research a debatable topic using at least three sources from different genres (article, data visualization, interview or speech excerpt). They write a one-page argument in which every paragraph must draw on at least two sources — no standalone source paragraphs allowed. Teacher grades for integration, not source count.

assignments.name: Source Conversation Map
assignments.body: Before writing, students map their sources on paper: draw a node for each source, then draw lines between nodes labeled with what those two sources say to each other (agree, contradict, qualify, extend). Only after completing the map may they draft. Collect the map with the final paper.

assignments.name: Synthesis vs. Summary Revision
assignments.body: Provide students a model "synthesis" paragraph that is actually just a sequence of summaries. Students annotate where synthesis is missing, then revise the paragraph to genuinely integrate the sources. Discuss as a class what changed and why it's stronger.

mastery: Produces paragraphs in which ideas from multiple sources are visibly in conversation — not sequenced but integrated.
Writes a claim that could not have come from any single source alone, requiring the combined evidence to hold.
Selects and weights sources appropriately for different parts of an argument rather than distributing attention evenly by default.
Identifies tension or contradiction between sources and uses it productively rather than resolving it by ignoring one source.
Revises synthesis in response to new sources, treating research as iterative rather than linear.
notes: This is an English I entry point for synthesis; the skill reappears in every subsequent course with increased source complexity and argumentative demand. English II adds the expectation that students synthesize across genres and disciplines; English III and IV layer in evaluation of synthesis itself as a rhetorical move.

DOK 3 justification: synthesis requires strategic thinking across multiple sources — it is not recall or skill application on a single text, but it also stops short of DOK 4 because students are not yet asked to design original research questions from synthesized material (that lives in 11G and beyond).

STAAR analog note: this TEK is tested indirectly through multi-source paired-passage sets. STAAR does not label an item "synthesis," but questions asking students to identify a claim both passages support, or to select the sentence that best integrates information from two sources, are operationalizing this standard. The stems above mirror those item types directly.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Examine Sources for Credibility and Bias, Including Omission
expectation: Examine sources for credibility and bias, including omission.
overview: This is where research stops being a scavenger hunt and starts being an act of judgment. Students have to evaluate not just whether a source says something, but whether the source has earned the right to say it — and whether what's missing from the source is as revealing as what's there.
studentFriendly: You can read any source — article, website, documentary, study — and determine whether it's trustworthy, whether it has an agenda, and whether something important has been left out on purpose.
questionStems: Which detail from the source MOST calls its credibility into question?
Based on paragraph X, the author's bias is MOST clearly revealed by —
Which sentence from the article BEST supports the inference that the author has omitted information that would weaken the argument?
The author's decision NOT to include [counterargument/evidence] in paragraph X MOST likely suggests —
Which of the following statements about Source 1 and Source 2 is BEST supported by both texts?
A reader examining this source for bias would MOST likely note that the author —
Based on the information in paragraphs X–Y, which source would MOST likely be considered more credible, and why?
The omission of [specific data or perspective] from the article MOST likely affects the reader by —
Which detail from the source BEST indicates that the author has a particular point of view on this topic?
A student using this source for a research paper should MOST be concerned that —

misconceptions.title: Credibility Is About the Website, Not the Argument
misconceptions.body: Students learn ".org and .gov are good, .com is bad" and stop there. They'll cite a biased nonprofit or an outdated government report without a second look because the domain checked out. Teach them that credibility is about the specific claim being made, the evidence marshaled for it, and the expertise of the author — not the URL's suffix.

misconceptions.title: Bias Means Wrong
misconceptions.body: Students treat bias as a synonym for error — if a source is biased, it must be false. Push them deeper: a biased source can contain accurate facts selectively arranged to produce a misleading impression. The bias lives in the selection and framing, not always in the facts themselves. Teach them to ask, "What true things aren't being said here?"

misconceptions.title: Omission Is Accidental
misconceptions.body: Students assume that when a source leaves something out, it was an oversight. Watch for students who give authors the benefit of every doubt. Introduce them to the concept of strategic omission — a source that covers climate data from one decade but not another, or that profiles victims from one group but not another, is making a choice. The gap is the argument.

misconceptions.title: A Source That Agrees With Me Is Credible
misconceptions.body: When students already believe the conclusion, they stop scrutinizing the source. This is the most dangerous failure mode in research, and it's the hardest to teach because it requires students to distrust their own instincts. Require them to name one thing they wish the source had included or addressed — that question alone breaks the confirmation bias loop.

misconceptions.title: All Sources Need to Be "Neutral" to Be Usable
misconceptions.body: Students sometimes dismiss any source with a discernible point of view, concluding that bias disqualifies. Teach them that most expert sources have a position — the question is whether the position is transparently argued, supported with evidence, and honest about what it's working against. Credibility and neutrality are not the same thing.

assignments.name: Side-by-Side Source Audit
assignments.body: Students find two sources on the same topic — one from an advocacy organization, one from a news outlet — and complete a credibility/bias checklist for each. Final step: write one paragraph explaining which source they'd cite and why, accounting for what each one omits.

assignments.name: The Missing Paragraph
assignments.body: Give students a one-sided article and ask them to write the paragraph the author left out — the counterevidence, the alternative perspective, the inconvenient statistic. Debrief: how does adding that paragraph change the article's argument?

assignments.name: Source on Trial
assignments.body: Students choose a source they've found for a research assignment and present a 3-minute "case" to the class — arguing whether the source should be admitted as evidence. Classmates act as jurors and ask one question about credibility, bias, or omission before voting.

mastery: Evaluates a source's credibility by examining author expertise, publication context, evidence quality, and currency — not domain name alone.
Identifies specific language, framing choices, or rhetorical moves that reveal an author's bias.
Recognizes strategic omission by asking what a source does not address and why that absence matters.
Applies consistent standards across sources regardless of whether the source's conclusion aligns with their own position.
Selects and justifies sources for a research task with explicit attention to credibility, bias, and what each source's omissions require them to compensate for elsewhere.
notes: This is the first formal introduction to source evaluation in the English I vertical — there is no prior-course version. English II builds on this by adding complexity of source types and the synthesis demand. The skill ramps from "can this student name a bias?" to "can this student triangulate across sources and account for what each one leaves out?"

The sub-item structure (G)(i) and (G)(ii)) means this expectation is paired with accuracy and objectivity in the next entry — teach them together so students understand that credibility, bias, omission, accuracy, and objectivity are overlapping lenses, not a checklist of independent categories.
DOK 3 justification: students must apply evaluation criteria to novel sources and reason about strategic absence — this exceeds procedural application and requires inferential judgment.
No direct STAAR analog in the oral language sense, but source evaluation appears in STAAR's paired-passage and research simulation tasks; stems above mirror the framing of those item types.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Examine Sources for Faulty Reasoning
expectation: Examine sources for faulty reasoning such as ad hominem, loaded language, and slippery slope.
overview: This is where research skills become critical thinking skills. Students aren't just finding sources — they're interrogating them, looking for the moves a source makes to manipulate rather than inform. The target is recognizing how faulty reasoning hides inside otherwise credible-looking text.
studentFriendly: When you're researching, you can spot the moves a source uses to mislead you — attacking the person instead of the argument, loading the language to trigger emotion, or pretending one small step leads inevitably to catastrophe.
questionStems: Which sentence from the passage MOST clearly contains an example of ad hominem reasoning?
The author's use of the phrase [phrase] in paragraph X is MOST likely an example of —
Which statement from the article BEST illustrates a slippery slope argument?
The loaded language in paragraph X is intended MOST likely to —
Based on paragraph X, the author's reasoning is flawed because the author —
Which sentence from the passage BEST demonstrates that the author is using loaded language rather than objective evidence?
The author's argument in paragraphs X–Y is MOST likely weakened by —
Which of the following BEST describes the logical error the author makes in paragraph X?
The author attacks the opponent's character in paragraph X rather than addressing the argument. This is MOST likely an example of —
Based on the passage, which statement BEST explains how the author's faulty reasoning affects the reliability of the source?

misconceptions.title: A Source Is Either Reliable or Worthless
misconceptions.body: Students treat source evaluation as a binary — trusted or trashed. Teach them that a source can be factually accurate in places AND use faulty reasoning in others. The goal is to identify the specific moves that undermine credibility, not to wholesale reject or accept a source. Watch for students who find one fallacy and declare the entire article invalid without engaging its evidence.

misconceptions.title: Ad Hominem Just Means "Being Mean"
misconceptions.body: Students see any insult as ad hominem. The actual test is whether the author is substituting an attack on the person for engagement with the argument. Teach them: if the argument still stands or falls on its own merits without the personal attack, it's ad hominem.
