If the person's credibility is genuinely relevant to the claim (a financial advisor with fraud convictions giving investment advice), it's not. The distinction matters.

misconceptions.title: Loaded Language Is Only in Opinion Pieces
misconceptions.body: Students assume loaded language is obvious and only shows up in editorials or political speeches. Push them to read research summaries, nonprofit websites, and news headlines with the same suspicion. Words like "radical," "common-sense," "proven," and "crisis" are doing rhetorical work in sources that otherwise present themselves as neutral. The most effective loaded language is invisible until you name it.

misconceptions.title: Slippery Slope Is Always Wrong
misconceptions.body: Students sometimes misread this: they assume any claim about consequences is a slippery slope. The fallacy is specific — it requires asserting an inevitable chain of increasingly extreme outcomes without demonstrating the causal links. A valid prediction of consequences with evidence is not a slippery slope. Teach them to ask: does the author provide logical steps, or does the argument jump? That's the line between analysis and fallacy.

misconceptions.title: Identifying the Fallacy Is the Finish Line
misconceptions.body: Students name the fallacy and stop — "that's a slippery slope" — without explaining how it damages the argument or affects the source's reliability. For research purposes, what matters is the consequence: does this fallacy mean the claim is unsupported? Does it signal bias that affects how I should use the source? Teach them to follow the identification with so what — that's where the real thinking lives.

assignments.name: Fallacy Hunt
assignments.body: Students read two short sources on the same research topic — one credible, one intentionally fallacious (from a curated set or teacher-created). They identify and label every instance of ad hominem, loaded language, and slippery slope, annotating in the margin and writing a one-paragraph evaluation of each source's reliability.

assignments.name: Headline Autopsy
assignments.body: Students collect five real news headlines on a single issue. For each headline, they identify any loaded language and write one sentence explaining how the language might shape a reader's interpretation before they read the article. Discuss as a class which sources they'd trust for a research paper and why.

assignments.name: Fallacy in the Wild
assignments.body: Students bring one example from a source they actually found during a current research unit. They present the fallacy to the class, name it, explain why it qualifies, and explain how they'll adjust their use of the source in their paper.

mastery: Identifies ad hominem, loaded language, and slippery slope by name and locates specific textual evidence for each.
Explains how identified faulty reasoning affects the source's credibility or usefulness for research — not just that the fallacy exists, but what it means for the argument.
Distinguishes between legitimate rhetorical choices and manipulative ones — does not flag all persuasion as fallacious.
Applies source examination consistently across multiple source types: news articles, advocacy websites, academic summaries, and opinion pieces.
Integrates source evaluation into the research process — adjusts how they use or cite a source based on what they find.
notes: This sub-expectation sits under 11(G), "examine sources for," alongside credibility/bias (i) and propaganda (iii). The three sub-expectations should be taught as a cluster — students examining sources are doing all three simultaneously, not in isolation.

Vertical alignment: English I introduces named fallacies (ad hominem, loaded language, slippery slope) in the context of research source evaluation. English II is expected to extend this to a broader set of fallacies and apply the skill to more complex, discipline-specific sources.

DOK 3 justification: naming a fallacy is retrieval (DOK 1–2); explaining how that fallacy damages a source's credibility within the context of a research task requires strategic thinking across multiple criteria.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is assessed obliquely — STAAR tests fallacy recognition and loaded language identification through author's purpose and craft questions (Std 8) applied to argumentative and informational texts. The stems above mirror that testing context. Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Display Academic Citations and Use Source Materials Ethically
expectation: Display academic citations, including for paraphrased and quoted text, and use source materials ethically to avoid plagiarism.
overview: This is where research becomes accountable. Students learn that citation is not bureaucratic paperwork — it is the mechanism by which academic integrity is made visible, and that paraphrase carries the same obligation as direct quotation.
studentFriendly: You know how to give credit where it's due — in both your own words and the author's — so that your reader can trace every idea back to its source and your work stays honest.
questionStems: Read the following passage from a student's research paper. Which citation format BEST matches the source information provided in the works-cited entry?
A student paraphrases the following sentence from a source but does not include a citation. Which statement BEST explains why the citation is still required?
Based on the works-cited entry in paragraph X, which information would MOST likely appear in the in-text citation for the same source?
The student's paper includes the quotation in paragraph X without quotation marks or attribution. This would MOST likely be considered —
Which revision of the student's in-text citation in paragraph X is MOST accurate based on the source information provided?
A student copies a source's sentence structure but replaces the vocabulary with synonyms and does not cite. This passage would MOST likely be described as —
Which sentence from the student's paper BEST demonstrates ethical use of a source by integrating a quotation with proper attribution?
Read paragraphs X and Y from a student's paper and the original source. Which statement MOST accurately describes how the student used the source?
The student wants to add a citation for the paraphrased idea in paragraph X. Which element is MOST important to include in the in-text citation?
Based on the source information listed, which works-cited entry is formatted MOST correctly for an academic paper?

misconceptions.title: Paraphrase Is a Plagiarism-Free Zone
misconceptions.body: Students believe that because they "put it in their own words," they no longer owe the source a citation. Watch for papers where every direct quote is cited but paraphrased ideas float without attribution. Teach them that changing the words does not change whose idea it was — the citation follows the idea, not the prose style.

misconceptions.title: Citation Format Is Just Busywork
misconceptions.body: Students treat MLA or APA formatting as arbitrary rule-following with no intellectual purpose. Reframe it as a precision skill: the citation exists so any reader can locate the exact source. Every missing comma, reversed name, or omitted page number is a failure of reproducibility, not a style crime. The format is a map; a bad map misleads.

misconceptions.title: Only Copy-Pasted Text Counts as Plagiarism
misconceptions.body: Students draw the plagiarism line at verbatim copying. They do not recognize that submitting patchwork paraphrase — swapping synonyms across a source's structure — is plagiarism in substance even if not in letter. Show them side-by-side examples where patchwork "passes" a surface check but is still academic theft. Name the behavior precisely so students can recognize it in their own drafts.

misconceptions.title: One Citation Covers the Whole Paragraph
misconceptions.body: Students will drop a single citation at the end of a paragraph that draws on three different source moments and think they're covered. Teach them that attribution follows every discrete borrowed idea — if the paragraph shifts sources, the citation shifts with it. The reader needs to know where each claim came from, not just that the paragraph touched a source somewhere.

misconceptions.title: A Works-Cited Entry Means the Source Was Used Ethically
misconceptions.body: Students conflate listing a source with using it responsibly. A works-cited page with no in-text citations, or in-text citations with no works-cited entry, breaks the citation contract in both directions. Teach them the two-part system: every in-text citation must point to a works-cited entry, and every works-cited entry must be called up somewhere in the paper. An orphaned entry is its own integrity problem.

assignments.name: Citation Audit
assignments.body: Students receive a sample student paper with intentional errors — missing paraphrase citations, malformed works-cited entries, an uncited direct quote. They annotate every violation, classify it (missing citation, formatting error, or plagiarism), and rewrite the offending passages correctly.

assignments.name: Paraphrase vs. Patchwork Sorting Activity
assignments.body: Students receive four "paraphrases" of the same source paragraph, ranging from genuine synthesis to patchwork synonym-swapping. They rank them from most to least ethical, justify their ranking in writing, and produce a fifth version that meets the standard.

assignments.name: Source-to-Paper Trace
assignments.body: Students submit a research paragraph alongside the original source and their works-cited entry. The teacher traces each borrowed idea back through in-text citation to the entry. Students see exactly where the citation chain breaks or holds.

assignments.name: Citation Reconstruction
assignments.body: Students receive scrambled elements (author, title, publisher, date, URL, page numbers) from three different source types and must assemble correct works-cited entries from scratch without a template — only the style guide.

mastery: Cites every paraphrased idea with an in-text citation that matches a works-cited entry, not just direct quotations.
Produces correctly formatted works-cited entries for at least three source types (website, book, article) without significant mechanical errors.
Identifies the difference between genuine paraphrase and patchwork synonym-swapping when reviewing their own or a peer's draft.
Maintains a complete citation chain — every in-text citation has a works-cited entry, and every works-cited entry is called up in the text.
Explains, in their own words, why ethical source use matters beyond avoiding punishment.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I establishes the citation obligation for both quoted and paraphrased text. English II (110.37(11)(H)) builds toward more complex source integration and documentation across longer research projects. English III and IV raise the expectation toward discipline-specific documentation and evaluating source relationships.

DOK 2 justification: the core skill is procedural application — students apply citation conventions to new source situations. The ethical reasoning layer (why plagiarism is wrong, not just what it is) pushes toward DOK 3 in discussion, but the testable performance is DOK 2.

This TEK has a limited direct STAAR analog; stems are written to mirror how STAAR tests source integration and citation reasoning in reading-adjacent research scenarios and released composition tasks.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Use an Appropriate Mode of Delivery to Present Research Results
expectation: Use an appropriate mode of delivery, whether written, oral, or multimodal, to present results.
overview: This is the culminating move of the research strand — students don't just find and synthesize information, they choose HOW to deliver it. The skill is rhetorical: matching mode (written, oral, multimodal) to audience, purpose, and content. A student who defaults to PowerPoint because that's what they always do hasn't made a choice; they've followed a habit.
studentFriendly: You can look at your research, your audience, and your purpose and make a deliberate decision about whether a written report, an oral presentation, or a combination of both will communicate your findings most effectively.
questionStems: A student is preparing to share research about a local environmental issue with city council members. Which mode of delivery would MOST likely be appropriate for this audience and purpose?
Read the following excerpt from a student's research presentation. Which detail from the excerpt BEST supports the claim that the student selected an appropriate mode of delivery?
Based on the research scenario in paragraph 3, which mode of delivery would MOST likely allow the presenter to reach the broadest audience?
The student in paragraph 2 chooses to present findings using a written report rather than an oral presentation. Which reason BEST explains why this choice may or may not suit the purpose described?
Which of the following BEST describes the advantage of a multimodal presentation over a written-only report when the research includes visual data?

A student's research conclusion in paragraph 4 relies heavily on audio recordings and photographs. Which mode of delivery would MOST likely allow these elements to contribute most effectively to the audience's understanding?
Read paragraphs 1–3 of the student's research report. Which revision would MOST likely make the delivery mode better suited to the stated audience?
Based on the information in paragraph 5, the student MOST likely chose an oral delivery because —
Which sentence from the student's presentation BEST demonstrates an understanding that mode of delivery should match the research's purpose?
A student presents written research findings to an audience unfamiliar with the topic. Which change to the delivery mode would MOST likely improve the audience's comprehension of the material?

misconceptions.title: "Multimodal" Just Means a Slideshow
misconceptions.body: Students treat PowerPoint or Google Slides as the default multimodal format, regardless of whether it suits the research. Push them to think about what "multimodal" actually means — combining text, image, audio, video, or live demonstration in ways that each carry distinct meaning. Teach them: if you could remove all the images and lose nothing, the presentation wasn't actually multimodal — it was a written report with decoration.

misconceptions.title: The Assignment Determines the Mode
misconceptions.body: Students assume the teacher has already decided the delivery mode, so they don't think about it as a choice. Even when given options, they'll pick what feels safest rather than what fits the content. Make the selection process explicit and graded — students should defend why they chose written over oral, or multimodal over either, in a short rationale attached to the final product.

misconceptions.title: Oral Means Informal
misconceptions.body: When students choose oral delivery, they often treat it as permission to be casual — less structured, less evidence-based, shorter. Teach them that oral presentation demands MORE discipline than written, not less, because the audience can't re-read. Watch for students who confuse "I'm talking instead of writing" with "I don't need to be as precise."

misconceptions.title: Written Is Always the Safest Option
misconceptions.body: Students who are anxious about presenting often default to written delivery because they can hide behind it. The written report then becomes bloated — cramming everything in because there's no other outlet. Reframe written delivery as a genre choice, not a fallback: it works when your audience will read carefully and when depth matters more than accessibility.

misconceptions.title: Mode Is Chosen Once and Never Revisited
misconceptions.body: Students lock in a mode early and don't reconsider it as their research develops. If a student planned a written report but their strongest evidence is a collection of interview audio clips, the mode should shift — or at minimum the student should explain why written still serves best. Teach them that mode selection is part of revision, not just planning.

assignments.name: Mode Justification Memo
assignments.body: Before students begin their final research presentation, they submit a one-page memo arguing why their chosen mode of delivery fits their audience, purpose, and evidence type. Grade the reasoning, not just the choice.

assignments.name: Same Research, Three Modes
assignments.body: Small groups research the same topic and each member presents findings in a different mode — one written, one oral, one multimodal. After all three present, the class debriefs: what did each mode do well? What did each lose?

assignments.name: Audience Shift Revision
assignments.body: Students take a completed written research report and redesign it for oral delivery to a specific different audience (younger students, community members, a skeptical panel). They identify what changes, what stays, and what gets cut entirely.

assignments.name: Multimodal Audit
assignments.body: Students evaluate a peer's multimodal presentation by identifying each mode used (text, image, audio, etc.) and rating whether each element added meaning or was redundant. Written feedback must cite specific moments in the presentation.

mastery: Selects a delivery mode based on explicit reasoning about audience, purpose, and the nature of the evidence — not habit or convenience.

Executes the chosen mode with discipline: written delivery is structured and evidence-dense; oral delivery is organized and precise; multimodal delivery integrates elements that each carry distinct meaning.
Articulates why a different mode would have been less effective for their specific research context.
Adjusts mode or presentation strategy when evidence types don't fit the originally planned delivery.
Produces a final product whose form genuinely serves the research — the audience could not have received the same information as effectively through a different mode.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I introduces the concept of deliberate mode selection as a culminating research skill. English II raises the stakes by requiring students to consider how mode shapes the audience's interpretation of findings — not just whether the mode fits, but how it frames the message.

This TEK has limited direct STAAR analog in traditional reading passages; stems above are written to mirror how STAAR tests authorial choice and purpose applied to student-produced research scenarios, consistent with the inquiry strand's extended writing and presentation contexts.

DOK 3 justification: choosing an appropriate mode requires strategic reasoning — students must evaluate multiple options against audience, purpose, and evidence type, then execute the choice with discipline. The justification component pushes into higher-order application.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Engage in Meaningful and Respectful Discourse
expectation: Engage in meaningful and respectful discourse by listening actively, responding appropriately, and adjusting communication to audiences and purposes.
overview: English II's foundational oral language standard is about the full cycle of communication — not just talking, but listening with intent, responding to what was actually said, and recalibrating tone and register for different audiences. The challenge is that students think they already know how to talk to people. They don't know they're being imprecise.
studentFriendly: You can hold a real conversation — one where you're actually listening, responding to what the other person said, and shifting how you talk depending on who you're talking to and why.
questionStems: Read the transcript of the conversation in paragraphs 1–4. Which response from the student MOST clearly demonstrates active listening?
Based on paragraphs 3–5, the speaker adjusts her communication in a way that MOST likely —
Which sentence from the discussion BEST supports the inference that the speaker is responding to the audience's concerns rather than restating her original point?
In paragraph 6, the speaker's shift in tone is MOST likely intended to —
Which detail from paragraphs 2–4 BEST illustrates that the speaker has accurately understood the previous speaker's argument?
The speaker's word choice in paragraph 7 suggests that he is adjusting his communication for an audience that —
Based on the exchange in paragraphs 5–8, which statement BEST describes how the speakers maintain respectful discourse?
Which sentence from the transcript MOST clearly shows the speaker responding appropriately rather than redirecting to a new topic?
The speaker's decision to restate the opposing view before responding in paragraph 4 MOST likely serves to —
Based on the entire transcript, the speakers' communication is MOST effective when they —

misconceptions.title: Talking More Means Participating More
misconceptions.body: Students equate volume of speech with quality of participation. They'll dominate a discussion and count that as engagement. Teach them the difference: meaningful discourse is measured by whether your contribution moves the conversation forward, not by how many words you added. Watch for the student who speaks often but never responds to what anyone else said — that's not discourse, that's a series of monologues.

misconceptions.title: "Respectful" Means Agreeing
misconceptions.body: Students have been conditioned to confuse politeness with agreement. They hold back real pushback to avoid conflict, which collapses the discourse into a room of people nodding. Reframe respect as precision: a respectful disagreement names what the other person got right before explaining where you part ways. Silence in the name of politeness is not discourse — it's avoidance.

misconceptions.title: Adjusting Communication Means Dumbing It Down
misconceptions.body: When students hear "adjust for your audience," they default to simplification — shorter words, fewer ideas. Push them deeper: adjustment is about register, formality, emphasis, and what background knowledge you can assume. Speaking to a panel of adults isn't about using bigger words; it's about restructuring what you lead with and what you can skip. Show them examples where the same argument is pitched differently to a peer, a teacher, and a public audience.

misconceptions.title: Active Listening Is Waiting for Your Turn
misconceptions.body: Students listen for a gap in the conversation, not for content. They're preparing their next point while someone else is still talking. Teach them the behavioral markers of actual active listening: paraphrasing before responding, asking a follow-up that proves they tracked the previous speaker's logic, or naming a specific word choice the other person used. If they can't do any of those, they weren't listening — they were waiting.

misconceptions.title: Discourse Has a Winner
misconceptions.body: Students frame discussion as debate — there's a side that wins and a side that loses. This kills the "meaningful" part of meaningful discourse. Teach them that the goal is collective understanding that no individual could have reached alone. When a discussion ends and everyone thinks the same thing they thought at the start, that's a failure of discourse regardless of who "won."

assignments.name: Transcript Audit
assignments.body: Record a 5-minute class discussion. Transcribe it. Students annotate the transcript marking moments of genuine response (where someone built directly on a previous speaker) vs. topic pivots (where someone ignored what came before). Discuss the ratio.

assignments.name: Same Idea, Three Audiences
assignments.body: Students choose a position on any topic (school policy, a literary argument, a local issue). They write three short pitches — one for a peer, one for a teacher, one for a community board — and present all three. Class identifies what changed and what stayed constant.

assignments.name: Facilitated Socratic Seminar with Self-Assessment
assignments.body: Students hold a seminar, then receive a transcript of their own contributions. They mark each of their statements: Did this respond directly to someone? Did this move the conversation forward? Was my register appropriate? Grade on honest self-assessment, not just performance.

mastery: Responds directly to a previous speaker's point — including paraphrasing or quoting them — before adding new content.
Adjusts register, formality, and emphasis when speaking to different audiences without losing the substance of the message.
Disagrees respectfully by acknowledging the other speaker's position before explaining a point of departure.
Asks follow-up questions that prove comprehension of the previous speaker's argument, not just the topic.
Identifies when a conversation has stalled or broken down and can name the communication failure that caused it.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I's 1A asks students to engage in meaningful and respectful discourse with basic active listening. English II keeps the same expectation but adds explicit attention to *adjusting* communication — the skill shifts from participating appropriately to reading and responding to audience and purpose as dynamic variables.

Vertical ramp to English III: English III's 1A narrows the target — students are now critiquing speakers' *diction and syntax* specifically. The broad discourse competency established here becomes the baseline from which analytic critique grows.

DOK 3 justification: Students must apply listening and communication strategies in real-time, unpredictable discourse contexts — then evaluate the effectiveness of their own and others' adjustments. This goes beyond recall or skill demonstration in isolation.

No direct STAAR analog for live discourse performance; question stems are written to mirror how STAAR tests the oral language strand via speech and conversation transcripts presented as prose passages.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Follow and Give Complex Oral Instructions
expectation: Follow and give complex oral instructions to perform specific tasks, answer questions, or solve problems and complex processes.
overview: This TEK is about whether students can actually do something with spoken language — not just hear it. Following complex oral instructions requires holding multi-step sequences in working memory, tracking conditional logic, and executing without defaulting to re-reading. Giving those instructions requires the reverse: translating a complex process into spoken language precise enough that someone else doesn't fail.
studentFriendly: You can listen to a complex set of spoken directions — with multiple steps, conditions, or decision points — and carry them out correctly, and you can give those same kinds of instructions to someone else clearly enough that they succeed.
questionStems: Read the following spoken instructions. Which step MOST likely comes immediately after step three based on the directions given?
A student follows the oral directions from paragraphs 2–3. Which action BEST completes the task as described?
Based on the instructions in paragraph 1, what should the listener do FIRST?
Which detail from the oral directions is MOST important for completing the task without error?
If the speaker's instructions in paragraph 4 are followed correctly, which outcome is MOST likely?
The speaker in paragraph 2 says, "only if the first condition is met." This detail MOST likely serves to —
Which sentence from the directions BEST clarifies what the listener should do when a problem arises?
A listener misses the instruction in paragraph 3. Which part of the task would MOST likely be completed incorrectly?
Based on paragraphs 1–4, the speaker's instructions are BEST described as —
Which revision to the speaker's directions in paragraph 5 would MOST improve a listener's ability to follow the process?

misconceptions.title: "I Understood It" Is Not the Same as "I Can Do It"
misconceptions.body: Students believe comprehension means execution. They nod along, confident they followed — and then stall at step two because they held the gist and dropped the specifics. Teach them to track instructions actively: jotting sequence markers, flagging conditionals, numbering steps in their head. Passive hearing is not following.

misconceptions.title: Complex Instructions Just Need to Be Repeated Slower
misconceptions.body: When students struggle, they ask for repetition at a lower speed — as if the problem were pace. Usually it isn't. The complexity lives in the conditional logic ("if X, then skip Y") or in the dependencies between steps. Watch for students who repeat the same misexecution even after a slower re-delivery. Push them to identify WHERE the instruction broke down, not just ask for the whole thing again.

misconceptions.title: Giving Instructions Means Listing Steps
misconceptions.body: Students approach giving oral instructions as a numbered list recitation and stop there. The harder skill is anticipating where listeners will get lost — naming landmarks, flagging transitions, calling out the decision points explicitly. Teach them: the instruction isn't done when you've said every step; it's done when the listener can do it.

misconceptions.title: Specificity Is Excessive Detail
misconceptions.body: Students trim their instructions for brevity and then watch their partner fail. They think "concise" and "precise" are the same word. They aren't. A concise instruction drops what doesn't matter; a precise instruction keeps everything that controls success. Watch for students who call their vague instructions "clear" because they didn't want to seem pedantic.

misconceptions.title: Solving Problems Orally Is Just Talking Through It
misconceptions.body: When instructions involve problem-solving, students confuse thinking aloud with instructing. They narrate their own reasoning without shaping it into directions a listener could act on. Reframe this: giving instructions for solving a problem means externalizing your logic in a sequence someone else can replicate, not broadcasting your internal monologue.

assignments.name: Blind Build
assignments.body: Partner A reads written assembly instructions silently, then delivers oral instructions only — no showing, no pointing — while Partner B executes a task (paper folding, a simple diagram, a sorting activity). Debrief on what broke down and why.

assignments.name: Instruction Autopsy
assignments.body: Students listen to a recorded set of complex oral instructions (teacher-recorded or sourced), then write a transcript from memory and compare it to the original. They annotate every point of divergence and categorize the error: sequence gap, dropped conditional, vague terminology, or missed qualifier.

assignments.name: Process Transfer
assignments.body: Students choose a process they know well (a recipe, a workflow, a game rule set) and deliver it as oral instructions to a small group. The group attempts the task; the speaker cannot intervene. Afterwards, speaker and group identify the two or three moments the instructions were insufficient.

mastery: Executes a multi-step oral instruction sequence correctly on the first delivery, including conditionals and embedded decision points.
When giving instructions, anticipates listener confusion at transitions and decision points and addresses those proactively without prompting.
Identifies the specific step or condition in a set of oral instructions where a breakdown occurred, rather than requesting a full repetition.
Distinguishes between instructions that are merely sequential and instructions that account for process complexity, and adjusts delivery accordingly.
Calibrates the level of specificity in oral instructions to the task — neither over-explaining simple steps nor under-explaining high-stakes ones.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I 1B targets following and giving oral instructions for tasks and questions; English II adds "complex processes" and "solve problems" — the instruction sequences grow longer, more conditional, and require the speaker to manage listener confusion, not just sequence correctness.

Vertical ramp to English III: English III 1B shifts to "pertinent questioning" as a clarification strategy — students who can give and follow complex instructions in English II are positioned to recognize when and how to ask the right question when instructions break down.

DOK 3 justification: following complex oral instructions requires strategic thinking — students must hold sequence, conditional logic, and process dependencies simultaneously while monitoring their own execution.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog. Question stems above mirror how STAAR tests listening comprehension and procedural text analysis when speech transcripts are presented as reading passages — this is the closest testable proxy for oral instruction fidelity.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Give a Formal Presentation with Thesis, Evidence, and Delivery
expectation: Give a formal presentation that incorporates a clear thesis and a logical progression of valid evidence from reliable sources and that employs eye contact, speaking rate such as pauses for effect, volume, enunciation, purposeful gestures, and conventions of language to communicate ideas effectively.
overview: This TEK is the convergence point for both research and rhetoric: students have to build an argument that would hold up on paper AND deliver it in a way that commands a room. The two halves — content and delivery — are equally graded, equally difficult, and equally avoided when teachers feel pressed for time.
studentFriendly: You can stand up in front of an audience, make a clear argument backed by solid sources, and deliver it in a way that makes people actually want to listen — controlling your volume, your pace, your eyes, and your hands with intention.
questionStems: Read the following excerpt from a student's speech. Which sentence BEST functions as the thesis of the presentation?
The speaker opens paragraph 2 with a statistic from a government report. This detail MOST likely serves to —
Based on paragraphs 3 and 4, which piece of evidence BEST supports the speaker's central claim?
The speaker pauses before delivering the final sentence of paragraph 5. This technique MOST likely is intended to —
Which sentence from the speech BEST demonstrates the speaker's use of logical progression in presenting evidence?
The speaker's shift in volume in paragraph 6 MOST likely contributes to the overall presentation by —
Based on the speech as a whole, the speaker's use of purposeful gestures would MOST likely —
Which sentence from paragraph 4 suggests the speaker is drawing on a reliable source to support the argument?
The speaker's word choice and sentence structure throughout the speech suggest the speaker MOST likely intends to —
A student is revising a formal presentation. Which revision would MOST effectively strengthen the logical progression of evidence between paragraphs 2 and 3?

misconceptions.title: The Thesis Is Just the Topic
misconceptions.body: Students announce a subject instead of staking a claim. "Today I'll be talking about social media" is a topic; "social media platforms are designed to maximize engagement at the cost of adolescent mental health" is a thesis. Watch for presentations that meander through information without ever landing on an actual argument. Teach them: if you removed your first sentence, would the audience know what side you're on? They should.

misconceptions.title: Valid Evidence Means a Lot of Evidence
misconceptions.body: Students pile on sources and assume quantity signals credibility. Push them to ask whether each piece of evidence is actually doing argumentative work — not decorating the speech, but advancing the claim. A presentation with three precise, well-chosen citations from credible sources is stronger than one that namechecks ten websites. Teach them to interrogate every piece: what does this prove, and does the audience know how it connects to the thesis?

misconceptions.title: Delivery Is Personality, Not Skill
misconceptions.body: Students who are naturally loud and animated think they've got delivery handled. Students who are quieter think delivery just isn't their thing. Both groups are wrong. Delivery is a set of learnable, observable techniques — eye contact held for three seconds before moving, volume dropped deliberately to pull listeners in, a pause deployed right before the punchline. Name the moves explicitly so every student has something concrete to practice.

misconceptions.title: Eye Contact Means Scanning the Room
misconceptions.body: Students learn "make eye contact" and turn it into a nervous pendulum sweep across the audience. Real eye contact lands on one person for one thought, then moves. It signals confidence and connection, not surveillance. Teach them the difference between eye contact and eye sweeping — and have them watch themselves on video to see which one they're doing.

misconceptions.title: Conventions of Language Are Optional in a Speech
misconceptions.body: Students conflate casual talk with formal presentation. They drop subject-verb agreement, lean on filler words, and use sentence fragments under the assumption that speaking is inherently looser than writing. Reframe it: conventions of language in a formal presentation are what tell the audience this speaker has thought carefully about what they're saying. A speaker who says "there was a lot of, like, problems" has signaled something to the audience — just not what they intended.

assignments.name: Blueprint Before the Build
assignments.body: Before any practice delivery, students submit a written outline with thesis, three evidence blocks (each with source citation and warrant), and a note on one delivery technique they plan to deploy in each section. Grade the plan before they speak.

assignments.name: Timed Delivery Lab
assignments.body: Students deliver a 4–6 minute formal presentation on a self-selected researched topic. Peers complete a structured observation form tracking thesis clarity, evidence count and sourcing, and three delivery elements: eye contact, volume variation, and gestures. Each observer writes one strength and one specific suggestion.

assignments.name: Watch and Diagnose
assignments.body: Students watch two clips of formal presentations — one strong, one with clear delivery breakdowns — and write a short diagnostic: what is the speaker doing well, what is failing, and what one specific revision would improve the weaker presentation most.

assignments.name: Revision Round
assignments.body: Students who score below a set threshold on delivery submit a recorded re-delivery within one week. They annotate the re-recording at three timestamps, naming which technique they adjusted and why. Grade for self-awareness, not just improvement.

mastery: Opens with a thesis that stakes a clear, arguable claim — not a topic announcement.
Sequences evidence in a logical order where each piece visibly builds on or supports the one before it, and explicitly names or cites sources during delivery.
Deploys at least three specific delivery techniques — eye contact, volume, pacing, gestures — with observable intentionality rather than habit.
Uses formal conventions of language throughout: complete sentences, subject-verb agreement, academic vocabulary appropriate to the topic.
Maintains composure and control across the full length of the presentation, not just in the opening minute.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I 1C asks students to give a formal presentation incorporating a clear thesis and logical evidence. English II keeps the same skeleton but adds the explicit delivery inventory — eye contact, speaking rate, pauses for effect, volume, enunciation, purposeful gestures — making the performance dimension as codified as the content dimension.

Vertical ramp to English III: English III 1C will expect rhetorical control and stylistic choices layered on top of the delivery mechanics established here. English II is where students build the foundation those English III moves depend on.
DOK 3 justification: students must apply content knowledge (argumentation, sourcing) and delivery technique simultaneously in a novel performance context — that's strategic thinking, not recall.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog; question stems are written to mirror how STAAR tests presentation transcripts as prose passages — author's craft, purpose, and evidence-quality questions applied to a speech transcript format. Noted per HANDOFF.md doctrine for oral language TEKs.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Participate Collaboratively in Structured Group Discussion
expectation: Participate collaboratively, building on the ideas of others, contributing relevant information, developing a plan for consensus building, and setting ground rules for decision making.
overview: This TEK is about the mechanics of democratic group talk — not just being nice, but knowing how to move a group toward a decision without steamrolling or collapsing. It asks students to do four distinct things simultaneously: listen and build, contribute relevantly, engineer consensus, and establish rules before the conversation breaks down.
studentFriendly: You can walk into any group discussion — a Socratic seminar, a project team, a school board comment period — and help the group actually get somewhere: you build on what others say, add information that matters, and help the group agree on how to decide before the arguing starts.
questionStems: Read the group discussion in paragraphs X–Y. Which response from Student A BEST demonstrates building on a classmate's idea rather than redirecting the conversation?
Based on the discussion transcript in paragraphs X–Z, which contribution is MOST relevant to the group's central question?
Which statement from the discussion MOST likely represents an attempt to establish a ground rule for decision making?
The student's comment in paragraph X suggests she is MOST likely trying to —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which action BEST supports the group's movement toward consensus?
A student wants to contribute relevant information to the discussion. Which of the following additions would MOST effectively support the group's current line of reasoning?
Which exchange from the transcript BEST illustrates the difference between contributing new information and simply restating a prior speaker's point?
Read paragraphs X–Y. The group's decision to establish speaking-order guidelines MOST likely serves to —
Based on the discussion in paragraphs X–Z, with which statement would MOST participants in the group likely agree?
Which revision to Student B's comment in paragraph X would MOST improve its relevance to the group's stated objective?

misconceptions.title: Building On Means Agreeing With
misconceptions.body: Students hear "build on others' ideas" and conclude that they should affirm before pivoting — saying "great point" and then going wherever they wanted to go anyway. That's not building; that's decorating. Teach them that genuine building means explicitly connecting their contribution to the logic of the previous speaker: "Because she said X, I'd push that further to suggest Y." Watch for the hollow affirmation that masks a full topic change.

misconceptions.title: Relevant Means Related to Me
misconceptions.body: When students contribute information, they default to personal experience or whatever they already know about the topic — not what the group's question actually needs. Relevance is relational, not topical: a contribution is relevant when it moves the group's specific line of reasoning forward, not just when it's on the same subject. Push them to name the gap their contribution is filling before they fill it.

misconceptions.title: Consensus Means Everyone Caves
misconceptions.body: Students confuse consensus with majority rule or capitulation — they think someone has to lose for the group to reach a conclusion. Teach them the distinction: consensus is a plan everyone can live with and defend, not a plan that makes everyone equally unhappy. When groups stall, it's usually because no one has articulated what a minimally acceptable outcome looks like for each person. That articulation is the skill.

misconceptions.title: Ground Rules Are Bureaucracy, Not Strategy
misconceptions.body: Students see setting ground rules as a waste of time — the real work is the discussion, so why spend five minutes on process? Flip the frame: ground rules are what let the smartest person in the room get heard, not just the loudest. When a group skips ground rules and then falls apart, ask them to name the specific moment the rules would have helped. Make the cost of skipping legible.

misconceptions.title: The Quietest Person Isn't Collaborating
misconceptions.body: Teachers often assume that visible verbal participation is the measure of collaboration. Students who speak rarely but redirect the group, ask a question that unlocks a stuck discussion, or summarize competing positions to move toward a decision are doing the deepest collaborative work. Watch for students who perform collaboration loudly without moving the group, and students who barely speak but are keeping the whole thing on track.

assignments.name: Ground Rules First
assignments.body: Before any Socratic seminar, student groups spend five minutes drafting written ground rules for how they will reach a decision if they disagree. After the seminar, they evaluate: which rules helped, which they ignored, and what they'd add. The debrief, not the seminar, is the assessment.

assignments.name: Transcript Analysis
assignments.body: Provide students with a lightly edited transcript of a real group discussion (a city council debate, a mock trial deliberation, a recorded classroom discussion). They annotate: label each speaker move as "builds on prior idea," "contributes new information," "redirects," "establishes process," or "stalls." Discuss what the pattern reveals about how decisions do and don't get made.

assignments.name: Consensus Mapping
assignments.body: A group of four is given a contested low-stakes decision (rank these five books for the class reading list). They must reach consensus using a visible process: each member names their non-negotiable and their flexible preference, then the group builds upward from the shared ground. Written reflection names the moment consensus became possible and why.

assignments.name: Contribution Audit
assignments.body: Students record a 10-minute group discussion, then individually listen back and score their own contributions: Was each one relevant? Did it build on the prior speaker? Did it move the group forward or sideways? Self-scoring rubric, then teacher conference.

mastery: Explicitly links their contribution to the prior speaker's reasoning, not just the general topic.
Can name, before contributing, what gap or angle their information is meant to address.
Helps the group articulate minimally acceptable outcomes before calling for a decision.
Proposes or enforces a ground rule in real time when the discussion structure is breaking down.
Recognizes and names the difference between a group that has reached consensus and a group where one position simply ran out of opposition.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: 110.36(1)(D) asks students to participate in discussions with structured roles. English II adds the procedural dimension — students are now responsible not just for their own participation but for the group's decision-making architecture: ground rules, consensus plans, and relevance discipline.
Vertical ramp to English III: 110.38(1)(D) extends toward advocacy and formal deliberative contexts; the collaborative infrastructure built here is the prerequisite.

DOK 3 justification: students must apply collaborative protocols in real-time, unpredictable group contexts and analyze whether contributions and process moves are serving the group's stated goal — not a recall or single-answer task.

STAAR analog note: this TEK has no direct STAAR tested form. Stems are written to mirror how STAAR assesses collaborative and procedural reasoning through discussion transcripts treated as prose passages — the same analytical moves apply even if the specific format differs from a standard reading item.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Use Reference Resources to Clarify Technical and Discipline-Based Vocabulary
expectation: Use print or digital resources such as glossaries or technical dictionaries to clarify and validate understanding of the precise and appropriate meaning of technical or discipline-based vocabulary.
overview: This TEK is about disciplinary precision — knowing that "theory" in biology means something sharply different from "theory" in casual conversation, and knowing where to go to settle that difference. The real skill isn't using a dictionary; it's recognizing when a general definition won't cut it and a specialized one is required.
studentFriendly: You can identify when a technical or field-specific word demands more than a standard dictionary and know how to find a resource — a glossary, a discipline-specific reference, a technical handbook — that gives the precise, field-appropriate meaning.
questionStems: Based on the glossary entry in paragraph X, the term [term] MOST nearly means —
The author uses the term [term] in paragraph X. Which definition from the technical dictionary BEST clarifies the author's use of this word?
Which sentence from the selection BEST demonstrates the author's use of discipline-based vocabulary?
According to the glossary provided, which of the following MOST accurately describes [term] as it is used in this text?
The word [term] in paragraph X is MOST likely drawn from which field of study?
Based on context and the reference entry, [term] in paragraph X means —
A student reading paragraph X encounters the word [term]. Which resource would BEST help the student determine its precise, field-specific meaning?
Which sentence from the passage uses [term] in a way that is MOST consistent with its technical definition?
The definition provided in the passage glossary indicates that [term] refers to — which MOST supports the idea that —
How does the author's use of [term] in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the precision of the text's explanation?

misconceptions.title: Any Dictionary Will Do
misconceptions.body: Students reach for Google's top result or a general-purpose dictionary and accept whatever definition appears first. Teach them that technical vocabulary lives in discipline-specific resources — a medical glossary, an engineering handbook, a legal dictionary — and that the general definition will often be imprecise or outright misleading in a specialized context. The word "culture" in microbiology is not the word "culture" in sociology.

misconceptions.title: Knowing a Word Means Knowing Its Technical Meaning
misconceptions.body: Students who have encountered a term before assume they already understand it correctly and skip the validation step. Watch for students who breeze past terms like "osmosis," "irony," or "variable" because they've "heard it before." The whole point of this TEK is that familiarity is not precision — push them to check even when they feel confident.

misconceptions.title: Glossaries Are for Struggling Readers
misconceptions.body: Students treat reference-checking as a remediation tool and resist using it because they don't want to signal weakness. Reframe this as expert behavior: specialists in every field consult references regularly. Lawyers check Black's Law Dictionary. Scientists cross-reference terminology in style guides. The habit of validation is what separates precise thinkers from approximate ones.

misconceptions.title: The Definition in Context Is Good Enough
misconceptions.body: Students use context clues to infer technical meanings and stop there. Context clues are a starting point, not an endpoint — they can narrow a word's meaning without landing on its precise, field-correct definition. Teach them that context tells you the neighborhood; the reference tool gives you the address. Both steps are required.

misconceptions.title: All Technical Resources Are Equally Reliable
misconceptions.body: Students find a digital resource with a definition and trust it without evaluating its source. A random blog post and a peer-reviewed glossary are not equivalent. Watch for students citing crowd-sourced or commercially motivated definitions for technical terms. Teach them to check the producer of the resource — who made it, for what audience, under what authority — before accepting the definition as precise and validated.

assignments.name: Definition Audit
assignments.body: Students read a short informational or technical text and flag every term they believe has a discipline-specific meaning. They then look each one up in both a general dictionary and an appropriate technical reference, compare the two definitions, and write two sentences explaining the difference and which definition fits the text's usage.

assignments.name: Resource Scavenger Hunt
assignments.body: Given a list of ten technical terms drawn from four different disciplines (biology, economics, rhetoric, engineering), students identify the most appropriate reference resource for each term and explain their reasoning before looking up the definitions.

assignments.name: Glossary Build
assignments.body: Over the course of a unit, students maintain a personal technical glossary for the discipline they are reading in. Each entry must include the term, its source resource, the precise field-specific definition, and one sentence from the text demonstrating its use. At the end of the unit, entries are peer-reviewed for accuracy and precision.

mastery: Independently identifies when a term carries technical or discipline-specific meaning that a general dictionary cannot resolve.
Selects and uses an appropriate print or digital reference resource (glossary, technical dictionary, discipline handbook) for the specific field in question.
Articulates the difference between a general definition and a field-precise definition and explains why the distinction matters in context.
Validates word meaning by cross-referencing use in the text against the technical definition — not just recording a definition but confirming it fits the author's actual usage.
Evaluates the credibility and authority of a reference resource before accepting its definition as precise.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I 2A establishes the same resource-use skill. English II holds the same expectation but the complexity of texts being read has increased, meaning the technical vocabulary students encounter is denser and more discipline-specific. The behavior looks identical; the stakes are higher because the texts demand more precision.
Vertical ramp to English III: English III 2A carries this skill forward into the same reference-validation practice against increasingly complex and varied informational and argumentative texts.

DOK 2 justification: the core task is using a skill (locating and applying a definition from an appropriate resource) rather than simply recalling a definition — but it does not yet require extended reasoning about how vocabulary shapes argument or text structure, which would push to DOK 3.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is rarely tested in isolation on STAAR but is embedded in informational reading passages where technical vocabulary appears and students must use context and embedded glossary entries to determine precise meaning. Question stems here are modeled on that embedded-glossary and context-clue format.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Context to Distinguish Among Denotative, Connotative, and Figurative Meanings of Words
expectation: Analyze context to distinguish among denotative, connotative, and figurative meanings of words.
overview: This TEK is about precision — the difference between what a word means in the dictionary and what it's actually doing in a text. By English II, students need to hold all three layers simultaneously: the literal, the loaded, and the figurative. The challenge is that most students default to one layer and ignore the others.
studentFriendly: You can read a word and tell not just what it means in the dictionary, but what emotional weight it carries and whether it's being used in a completely non-literal way — and you can prove your reading using the text around it.
questionStems: In paragraph X, the word [word] MOST nearly means —
The connotation of [word] in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to suggest —
Read this sentence from paragraph X: [sentence]. The figurative meaning of [phrase] is MOST nearly —
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the idea that the author uses [word] to convey a negative connotation?
The author's use of the phrase [phrase] in paragraph X is MOST likely an example of —
In paragraph X, the word [word] has both a denotative and a connotative meaning. Which statement BEST describes the connotative meaning as it is used in the selection?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the author's use of [figurative phrase] suggests that —
Which word from paragraph X carries the STRONGEST negative connotation in context?
How does the meaning of [word] in paragraph X differ from its dictionary definition?
The author's repeated use of [word] throughout the selection MOST likely develops the idea that —

misconceptions.title: Connotation Is Just "Positive or Negative"
misconceptions.body: Students reduce connotation to a binary — they slap a plus or minus on a word and call it done. Real connotative analysis asks: negative to whom? In what cultural or historical context? Teach them that "urban," "ambitious," and "aggressive" don't have universal connotations — the weight of those words shifts depending on who is speaking and who is being described. Push them past the thumbs-up/thumbs-down reflex.

misconceptions.title: The Dictionary Definition Is the Real Meaning
misconceptions.body: Students treat denotation as the truth and everything else as decoration. Reframe this: in literary texts, the connotative and figurative meanings often carry more of the author's argument than the denotation does. A character described as "calculating" technically just means someone who plans carefully — but no careful reader takes it as a compliment. The dictionary is the floor, not the ceiling.

misconceptions.title: Figurative Means Made Up
misconceptions.body: When students encounter a metaphor or personification they don't immediately parse, they write it off as the author being weird or flowery. Watch for explanations like "the author is just being creative here" that go nowhere. Teach them that figurative language makes an argument — the author chose THIS comparison because it does work that literal language cannot. Push them to name what the figure reveals or claims about the subject.

misconceptions.title: Context Is Just the Sentence the Word Is In
misconceptions.body: Students read the sentence with the target word and stop there. Real context analysis extends outward: Who is the narrator? What has just happened in the text? What tone has the author established across the paragraph? Teach them to triangulate — a single sentence is a clue, not a verdict. STAAR loves items where the surrounding paragraph flips the obvious reading of a word.

misconceptions.title: Denotation, Connotation, and Figurative Language Are Three Separate Questions
misconceptions.body: Students answer each layer in isolation and never integrate them. Mastery looks like: "The word 'home' literally means a place where one lives, but the connotation here suggests safety and belonging — and the extended metaphor the author builds around it in paragraphs 3–5 suggests the character has lost access to that feeling entirely." One word, three layers, one argument. Teach them to stack the analysis.

assignments.name: Three-Layer Annotation
assignments.body: Students annotate a short passage (one page) using three colored markers: one for words they can analyze denotatively, one for connotative weight, one for figurative use. Then they write a paragraph explaining how all three layers work together in one sentence of their choice.

assignments.name: Same Word, Different Contexts
assignments.body: Give students the same word — "natural," "clean," "domestic," "foreign" — embedded in three different sentences from three different texts. They write a sentence for each explaining how context shifts the word's meaning across the three examples, then draw a conclusion about what drives connotative meaning.

assignments.name: Author Swap
assignments.body: Students take a sentence from a text and rewrite it using words with opposite connotations but the same denotation (e.g., "the crowd gathered" vs. "the mob assembled"). They explain in writing what changes and what doesn't — and what that reveals about the original author's intent.

assignments.name: STAAR Stem Practice
assignments.body: Give students a released STAAR passage and have them locate every item that targets word meaning. They label each one: denotative question, connotative question, or figurative question. Then they compare their labels with a partner and reconcile any disagreements.

mastery: Identifies the denotative meaning of a word and distinguishes it from the connotative or figurative meaning operating in context.
Explains the connotative weight of a word choice and connects it to the author's purpose or tone, not just labeling it "positive" or "negative."
Interprets figurative language by naming what the comparison reveals, not just restating the figure in literal terms.
Uses context extending beyond the immediate sentence — narrator, tone, plot position — to support a reading of a word's meaning.
Integrates all three layers of meaning into a unified claim about how the author's language is working in the text.
notes: Vertical ramp: English I 2B asks students to distinguish among denotative, connotative, and figurative meanings of words using context. English II adds "analyze" — the task shifts from distinguishing (categorizing) to explaining the function and effect of each layer. English III 2B extends further into how language shapes perception and argument, building toward rhetorical critique.

DOK 3 justification: Students must apply semantic understanding to unfamiliar contexts, use textual evidence to support claims about meaning, and draw inferences about authorial intent — requiring strategic thinking beyond simple retrieval.

STAAR alignment: This is one of the most directly and frequently tested vocabulary TEKs. STAAR uses "MOST nearly means," "connotation," and figurative language identification regularly across both fiction and informational passages. All 10 stems above are grounded in authentic STAAR phrasing.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Determine the Meaning of Foreign Words and Phrases Used in English
expectation: Determine the meaning of foreign words or phrases used frequently in English such as pas de deux, status quo, déjà vu, avant-garde, and coup d'état.
overview: English absorbs foreign vocabulary constantly, and the words that stick tend to carry conceptual weight that English didn't already have a tidy word for. This TEK asks students to treat those borrowed terms as meaningful choices — not decoration — and to decode them using context, morphology, and cultural knowledge.
studentFriendly: You can figure out what foreign words and phrases like *status quo*, *déjà vu*, and *avant-garde* actually mean when you encounter them in essays, articles, speeches, and arguments — and you understand why English kept borrowing them instead of inventing its own words.
questionStems: In paragraph X, the author uses the phrase *status quo* to suggest —
Based on context in paragraph X, the phrase *avant-garde* MOST nearly means —
Which sentence from the selection BEST helps the reader determine the meaning of *coup d'état* as it is used in paragraph X?
The author's use of *déjà vu* in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
In paragraph X, *pas de deux* is used to suggest that the relationship between the two figures is —
Which detail from paragraphs X–Y BEST supports the conclusion that the narrator views the *status quo* as —
The phrase *avant-garde* in paragraph X contributes to the author's argument by —
Based on the context in paragraphs X–Y, what does the author imply by describing the movement as a *coup d'état*?
The author's choice to use a foreign phrase rather than an English equivalent in paragraph X MOST likely suggests —
Which sentence from the selection provides the MOST context clues for determining the meaning of the foreign phrase in paragraph X?

misconceptions.title: Foreign Phrases Are Just Fancy Filler
misconceptions.body: Students assume writers drop in French or Latin terms to sound sophisticated rather than to communicate something precise. Teach them the opposite: *coup d'état* exists in English because there is no compact English phrase that carries its exact combination of surprise, political violence, and illegitimacy. The borrowing happened because the concept demanded it. Push students to ask what English word they'd substitute — and then notice what gets lost.

misconceptions.title: Context Clues Don't Work on Foreign Words
misconceptions.body: Students will skip over unfamiliar foreign phrases and assume they're inaccessible. Watch for students who mark the foreign phrase and move on without attempting the surrounding sentences. Reframe: the context clues are right there. The author is writing in English to an English-speaking audience — they've already embedded enough scaffolding for a careful reader. Train students to treat the phrase as a blank and fill it from what surrounds it.

misconceptions.title: Memorizing the List Is the Skill
misconceptions.body: Students (and some teachers) reduce this TEK to flashcard work on the six examples in the standard. The actual skill is transfer: encountering *zeitgeist* or *carte blanche* or *bête noire* in a text and deploying the same decoding strategies cold. If the only terms students can handle are the ones on the list, they've studied the TEK but not learned it.

misconceptions.title: Pronunciation Is Required for Comprehension
misconceptions.body: Students become so anxious about mispronouncing *pas de deux* or *déjà vu* that they disengage from meaning. Acknowledge the anxiety and set it aside. Pronunciation matters eventually — comprehension matters now. Teach them to bracket the sounds and attack the semantics. Oral work can come after they've got the meaning anchored.

misconceptions.title: Foreign Means Old or Irrelevant
misconceptions.body: Students treat borrowed phrases as artifacts of older, more formal writing. Show them *déjà vu* in a sports column, *status quo* in a school board debate, *avant-garde* in a music review. These terms are alive and active in contemporary texts. The moment students see them in the wild — outside the classroom, in things they actually read — the TEK starts to matter.

assignments.name: Phrase in the Wild
assignments.body: Students find one foreign phrase used in English (from a news article, podcast transcript, or social media post) that does NOT appear on the TEK's list. They write one paragraph explaining the phrase's meaning based on context, one sentence on why English kept the borrowing, and cite the source. Grade for reasoning, not just correct definitions.

assignments.name: Substitution Test
assignments.body: Give students four or five paragraphs, each containing a bolded foreign phrase. For each, students write the best English substitute they can, then write a sentence explaining what the substitute gains and what it loses. The goal is to surface the precision the original carries.

assignments.name: Author's Choice Defense
assignments.body: Students read two short excerpts — one that uses *coup d'état* and one that uses "government overthrow" — and write a paragraph arguing which author made the stronger word choice and why. Forces them to treat the foreign phrase as a deliberate rhetorical decision, not decoration.

mastery: Uses context clues — surrounding sentences, paragraph structure, author's purpose — to determine the meaning of an unfamiliar foreign phrase without relying on a glossary.
Explains why a specific foreign phrase was retained in English rather than replaced with a native equivalent, citing what conceptual precision the borrowing preserves.
Applies decoding strategies to foreign phrases not encountered in class instruction, demonstrating transfer beyond the TEK's named examples.
Identifies foreign phrases in authentic texts (journalism, essays, speeches) and accurately interprets their meaning in context.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I's 2(C) introduces foreign words as a recognition task — students identify and define them. English II sharpens this into a contextual decoding task, adding the expectation that students explain why the borrowing happened. English III moves toward using borrowed terms analytically in argument and rhetorical analysis.

DOK 2 justification: the primary cognitive demand is contextual interpretation — applying knowledge of context clues and word relationships to determine meaning. Individual items can reach DOK 3 when students must synthesize morphological, contextual, and cultural knowledge to decode a phrase not encountered before.
STAAR analog note: this TEK is tested embedded in vocabulary-in-context items on STAAR rather than as a standalone strand. Stems are written to mirror that embedded format.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: 110.37(3) — Self-Select Text and Read Independently
expectation: Self-select text and read independently for a sustained period of time.
overview: This standard is about building the habit before building the skill. Students who don't read on their own don't get better — independent volume is what makes every other ELA standard stick. The teacher's job here is to protect reading time, teach students to choose books that actually hold them, and cultivate the identity: I am someone who reads.
studentFriendly: You choose what you read and actually read it — long enough, often enough, and with enough investment that it changes how you think about reading.
questionStems: Based on the information in paragraphs X–Y, which text feature would MOST likely help a reader select this passage for independent research?
The reader in paragraph X chooses a text because — which detail from the passage BEST supports this conclusion?
Which sentence from the passage BEST indicates that the narrator has developed a sustained interest in the subject?
Based on the passage, what would a reader MOST likely do to determine whether a text is appropriate for independent reading?
Which detail from paragraph X suggests that the narrator is reading independently rather than under direction?
A student reading this passage for the first time would MOST likely conclude that the central purpose of the text is —
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the inference that the reader has chosen this text based on personal interest?
The reader's engagement with the text in paragraph X is MOST nearly described as —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which strategy would MOST likely help a reader sustain attention through a difficult text?
Which statement from the passage BEST explains how a reader's self-selection contributed to their comprehension of the text?

misconceptions.title: Any Reading Counts as Sustained Reading
misconceptions.body: Students — and sometimes teachers — treat any exposure to print as equivalent to sustained independent reading. Scanning a phone for five minutes, reading a meme account, or skimming a handout is not the same as holding a book for thirty consecutive minutes and tracking meaning across pages. Teach them the difference between exposure and engagement, and hold the time long enough that the distinction becomes visible.

misconceptions.title: Letting Students Pick Means Anything Goes
misconceptions.body: When teachers hand over selection entirely without scaffolding, students gravitate to books they've already read, picture books that don't push them, or books they pick to impress rather than to read. Watch for students who carry the same book for four weeks and never turn a page. Self-selection is a taught skill — book talks, browsing time, and abandonment permission all have to be modeled explicitly.

misconceptions.title: If They're Not Reading Above Grade Level, It Doesn't Count
misconceptions.body: Some teachers quietly redirect students away from "easy" books because they feel the texts aren't rigorous. This is a mistake. Volume matters more than difficulty level for building fluency, vocabulary, and reading identity. A tenth grader reading a seventh-grade novel they can't put down is getting more reading value than one sitting in front of a grade-level text they resent. Reframe: the goal is sustained engagement; difficulty can layer in from there.

misconceptions.title: Quiet Means Reading
misconceptions.body: A classroom that looks calm during independent reading time is not the same as a classroom where students are actually reading. Students become expert at performing reading — staring at a page, moving a bookmark, nodding — while absorbing nothing. Watch for students who can never tell you anything about where they left off. The fix is low-stakes accountability: a one-sentence exit note, a quick share, a reading log that asks only what happened and what they think about it.

misconceptions.title: Self-Sustained Reading Is a Filler Activity
misconceptions.body: When standardized test prep, essay workshops, and direct instruction crowd the calendar, independent reading gets cut first because it "doesn't produce a grade." This is the wrong hierarchy. Sustained reading volume is the highest-leverage long-term literacy investment in the schedule. Guard the time. Name it as instruction. Treat loss of reading time as a loss of rigor, not a gain of it.

assignments.name: Book Pass
assignments.body: Spend one class period circulating 15–20 books around the room — each student has 90 seconds per book before passing it on. At the end, every student commits to a first choice and a backup. Repeat quarterly.

assignments.name: Reading Log With a Twist
assignments.body: Students keep a log that records not pages but moments — one sentence about something they noticed, wondered, or felt during each sitting. No summaries. This surfaces whether they were actually reading.

assignments.name: Abandonment Conferences
assignments.body: Any student can abandon a book after 50 pages without penalty — but they must have a two-minute conversation with the teacher about why, and leave with a replacement in hand. Normalize quitting the wrong book and starting the right one.

assignments.name: Reading Identity Reflection
assignments.body: At the end of each grading period, students write one paragraph: What did you read? What worked? What didn't? What does that tell you about what kind of reader you are right now? File these — they're the most useful data in the room.

mastery: Selects texts based on genuine interest or curiosity, not performance for the teacher — and can articulate why the book appealed to them.
Reads for a sustained block of time without visible prompting, redirection, or page-flipping performance.
Can speak specifically about where they are in a text — character, plot moment, argument — at any given check-in.
Self-corrects selection when a book isn't working, using a principled reason ("the pacing lost me after chapter two") rather than a vague dismissal ("it's boring").
Connects reading volume to their own growth as a reader across the year — describes themselves as someone who reads, not someone who completes assignments.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I Std 3 establishes the same expectation — the standard language is functionally identical across the four courses. The ramp is implicit: students in English II should be reading longer, selecting more independently, and sustaining attention across more complex texts than in English I. Teachers should hold a higher bar for the length and difficulty of texts students choose even though the TEKS language doesn't specify this.

DOK 1 justification: the standard itself is recall and execution — choose a book, read it. The cognitive complexity lives in the volume and the habit, not in a specific analytical move. Mastery at higher DOK emerges through the companion response and comprehension standards, not here.

STAAR analog note: Self-Sustained Reading is not tested directly on STAAR. Question stems above are written to mirror how STAAR tests reading behavior and self-selection through prose passages — a student reading about a reader, or a narrator describing an encounter with a book. Treat these stems as proxies, not direct test-item representations.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Establish Purpose for Reading Assigned and Self-Selected Texts
expectation: Establish purpose for reading assigned and self-selected texts.
overview: Purpose-setting is the ignition switch for everything else in Comprehension Skills — without it, students skim, drift, and miss the thread. The harder work here is teaching students to hold a purpose through an entire text, not just name one at the start and forget it.
studentFriendly: Before you read anything — whether a teacher assigned it or you picked it yourself — you decide what you're reading FOR, and that decision shapes everything you notice along the way.
questionStems: The reader MOST likely established which purpose before reading this selection?
Based on the introduction in paragraph 1, the reader can MOST likely expect the author to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST helps the reader establish a purpose for reading?
The title and subheadings of this article MOST likely signal that the reader should read in order to —
Which statement BEST describes the purpose a reader would establish after reading the first two paragraphs?
How does the information in paragraph X contribute to the reader's understanding of the author's main purpose?
A student reading this text to understand [topic] would MOST likely focus on which detail?
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the inference that the author intended this text for a reader seeking —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, a reader who established the purpose of evaluating the author's argument would MOST likely conclude —
The organizational structure of this selection MOST nearly suggests that a reader should approach it in order to —

misconceptions.title: Purpose Is Something the Teacher Sets, Not the Reader
misconceptions.body: Students treat purpose as a prompt box on a graphic organizer — they write what they think the teacher wants and move on. Teach them that a self-set purpose is a genuine question or goal they carry INTO the text, and it changes what they slow down for, what they underline, what confuses them. If the stated purpose doesn't affect their reading behavior, it wasn't a real purpose.

misconceptions.title: "I'm Reading to Understand" Is a Purpose
misconceptions.body: Students default to the most generic possible framing — "to learn about it" or "to understand the story" — and consider the task complete.
