Push them to name something specific: to understand WHY the author opposes X, to track how the narrator's attitude shifts, to find evidence for or against a claim. Vague purpose produces vague reading.

misconceptions.title: Self-Selected Means Purposeless
misconceptions.body: When students choose their own texts, they assume pleasure-reading has no purpose structure. Watch for students who read freely but can't describe what drew them in or what they were looking for. Even recreational reading has a purpose — escape, curiosity, identification with a character — and naming that purpose helps students become more deliberate readers across both assigned and chosen texts.

misconceptions.title: Purpose Is Set Once and Stays Fixed
misconceptions.body: Students set a purpose at the start of a unit, then abandon it when the reading gets difficult or the text doesn't deliver what they expected. Teach them that purpose is live and adjustable — if paragraph 3 reveals the text is doing something different than anticipated, a strong reader revises the purpose and re-orients. Mechanical compliance with the original purpose misses that skill entirely.

misconceptions.title: Assigned Texts Don't Require Personal Purpose
misconceptions.body: Students believe that because a teacher assigned the text, the teacher's purpose IS their purpose. The problem is they read passively, waiting for the teacher to tell them what mattered. Teach them that even mandatory texts require a reader-owned lens — a question they bring to the text, not just a summary they extract from it. That's the difference between a student who gets something out of a text and one who just gets through it.

assignments.name: Pre-Reading Purpose Ticket
assignments.body: Before any assigned reading, students write one sentence stating their purpose — framed as a genuine question or reading goal, not a generic statement. At the end of reading, they revisit the ticket: did the purpose hold? Did they revise it mid-reading? Grade for specificity and honesty, not correctness.

assignments.name: Purpose Audit Across Text Types
assignments.body: Students read three short texts in the same week — one assigned literary, one assigned informational, one self-selected. For each, they record the purpose they set and describe in one paragraph how that purpose shaped what they noticed. Compare the three in class discussion: how did the setting (assigned vs. self-selected) affect the quality of the purpose?

assignments.name: Mid-Reading Purpose Check
assignments.body: Stop students exactly halfway through a longer text. They write their original purpose, then write whether it still holds and what — if anything — they've revised. This catches students who set a purpose ritually and then disengage.

mastery: States a specific, reader-owned purpose before beginning a text — not a restatement of the assignment prompt.
Adjusts purpose mid-reading when the text reveals itself to be doing something the initial purpose didn't anticipate.
Explains, after finishing a text, how the purpose they set shaped what they attended to while reading.
Sets purposeful reading goals even for self-selected texts, naming what drew them in and what they were tracking.
Distinguishes between the author's purpose and the reader's purpose, treating them as related but separate.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I 4A asks students to establish purpose for reading; English II holds the same expectation, with the implicit ramp being the complexity of texts and the range of genres students now navigate — literary, argumentative, and informational. English III 4A extends further into self-monitoring and metacognitive adjustment. The skill looks identical across years on paper; the differentiation is in the sophistication of the texts it must be applied to.
DOK 2 justification: establishing purpose requires students to process text features, context, and genre signals to construct meaning — more than recall, but not yet extended reasoning.

STAAR analog note: this TEK rarely appears as an isolated stem; it is tested indirectly through questions that ask what a reader would focus on, what the author's purpose signals to the reader, or how organizational features orient the reader's expectations. Stems are written to mirror that embedded testing pattern.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Generate Questions About Text Before, During, and After Reading
expectation: Generate questions about text before, during, and after reading to deepen understanding and gain information.
overview: This TEK is about making reading an active, recursive process — not a single pass from first word to last. Students who generate questions before, during, and after reading are building a habit of mind that separates passive decoding from genuine comprehension. The skill is less about the questions themselves and more about what sustained questioning reveals: gaps, assumptions, and the places where a text earns or loses the reader's trust.
studentFriendly: You read a text by constantly asking it questions — before you start, while you're in the middle of it, and after you finish — and those questions are what keep you from just moving your eyes across a page without understanding anything.
questionStems: Which question would BEST help a reader clarify the meaning of paragraphs X–Y?
Based on the information in paragraph X, which question would MOST likely lead a reader to a deeper understanding of the author's purpose?
Which of the following questions could BEST be answered using information from the selection?
A student reading this passage for the first time would MOST likely ask which question before reaching paragraph X?
After reading paragraph X, a reader would MOST likely ask which question to better understand the author's central claim?
Which question is MOST directly answered by information found in paragraphs X–Y?
Which question BEST reflects the kind of thinking a reader must do to understand the relationship between paragraphs X and Y?
Based on the passage, which question remains unanswered after reading?
Which question would MOST likely help a reader evaluate the evidence presented in paragraph X?
A reader who generates questions while reading paragraph X would MOST likely conclude that the author —

misconceptions.title: Generating Questions Means Guessing What the Teacher Will Ask
misconceptions.body: Students treat pre-reading questions as prediction games — they try to reverse-engineer what will be on the quiz rather than genuinely interrogate the text. Watch for students whose "questions" are really just restated headings or surface-level who/what/when items. Teach them that strong questions target ambiguity, not facts the text already states plainly.

misconceptions.title: After-Reading Questions Are Just Comprehension Checks
misconceptions.body: Students think post-reading questioning means checking whether they understood — so they generate questions like "What happened at the end?" that any reader who finished the text could answer immediately. Push them toward questions that open outward: what the text didn't explain, what evidence might complicate the author's claim, how the ending reframes what came before. Post-reading is when the most important questions become visible.

misconceptions.title: A Good Question Has an Answer in the Text
misconceptions.body: Students have been trained by close-reading exercises to believe every legitimate question points to a textual answer. Some of the most generative questions don't — they point to what the text assumes, omits, or refuses to say. Teach them that a question revealing a gap in the text is doing just as much comprehension work as a question with a clean answer.

misconceptions.title: Questioning Is a Pre-Reading Ritual, Not an Ongoing Practice
misconceptions.body: Students perform the before-reading question step as a compliance move — they write three questions in the margin, then stop asking anything for the rest of the passage. The "before, during, and after" structure is meant to describe a continuous stance, not three discrete boxes. Reframe questioning as a reflex that never turns off: every paragraph should be generating new questions, not just satisfying old ones.

misconceptions.title: More Questions Means Better Reading
misconceptions.body: Students who embrace questioning sometimes overcorrect — they generate a question about every sentence, losing track of which ones are actually productive. Teach them to distinguish between questions that move understanding forward and questions that stall it. Not every confusion needs a question; some confusion resolves itself by reading further. The skill is knowing when to hold a question and when to let it go.

assignments.name: Three-Column Question Log
assignments.body: Students divide a page into three columns — Before, During, After — and populate each as they read. After finishing, they review the log and identify which questions were answered by the text, which were answered by inference, and which remain genuinely open. They write a short paragraph about what the unanswered questions reveal.

assignments.name: Question Quality Sort
assignments.body: Give students a list of twenty pre-written questions about a shared text — ranging from literal recall to inferential to evaluative to questions the text simply cannot answer. Students sort them into tiers (surface, deep, unanswerable) and defend their rankings in small groups. The sorting argument is the lesson.

assignments.name: Mid-Reading Pause
assignments.body: Students read the first half of a text, stop, and generate five questions they expect the second half to address. After finishing, they evaluate which questions were answered, which were irrelevant, and which turned out to be the wrong questions entirely. The mismatch between predicted and actual is the discussion entry point.

mastery: Generates before-reading questions that target structure, argument, and implied meaning — not just topic prediction.
Adjusts and adds new questions mid-reading in response to what the text actually does, not just what was expected.
Distinguishes between questions the text answers, questions the text invites but doesn't answer, and questions that expose the text's assumptions.
Uses post-reading questions to evaluate the quality of the text's reasoning or evidence, not just to confirm comprehension.
Explains what a specific question revealed about their understanding rather than simply listing questions as output.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I's 4B asks students to generate questions to deepen understanding — English II holds the same expectation but is applied to increasingly complex informational and literary texts requiring inferential and evaluative questioning rather than primarily literal recall.
Vertical ramp to English III: English III's 4B advances into questioning as a tool for critique and synthesis across multiple texts, not just comprehension of a single passage.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog — STAAR does not ask students to identify what question they would generate. However, the skill underpins nearly all inference and author's purpose items. Question stems above are written to mirror the reasoning processes that questioning develops, framed as STAAR-style comprehension and inference items.

DOK 3 justification: generating productive questions requires strategic thinking — students must assess what they know, what the text implies, and where their understanding breaks down, which exceeds recall or skill application.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Make and Correct or Confirm Predictions
expectation: Make and correct or confirm predictions using text features, characteristics of genre, and structures.
overview: Prediction is a comprehension strategy only when it's disciplined — anchored to actual textual evidence, revised when the text pushes back, and confirmed with specificity. By English II, students should be reading ahead and reading back simultaneously, using what they know about how genres and structures work to sharpen their forecasts before they're proven right or wrong.
studentFriendly: You can look at how a text is built — its headings, its genre conventions, its structural patterns — and make a reasonable forecast about where it's going, then revise that forecast honestly when the text surprises you.
questionStems: Based on the text features in paragraph X, the reader can MOST likely predict that —
Which detail from paragraph X BEST supports the prediction that the author will argue —
Based on the structure of the text, what would the reader MOST likely predict about the conclusion?
After reading paragraphs X–Y, which prediction about the narrator's next action is BEST supported?
The heading before paragraph X MOST likely suggests that the section will —
Which evidence from the selection BEST confirms the prediction that —
Based on the characteristics of this genre, the reader would MOST likely expect —
Which sentence from paragraph X would cause a reader to CORRECT a prediction that —
The structure of paragraphs X–Y MOST likely signals that the author intends to —
Based on the text features and genre of this selection, what can the reader MOST likely infer about the author's purpose?

misconceptions.title: Predicting Is Guessing, Not Reasoning
misconceptions.body: Students treat prediction as an opening-night impression with no obligation to update — they say something at the start and never return to it. Teach them that prediction is a two-way contract with the text: you forecast based on evidence, then you go back and reckon with whether the text honored or broke your forecast. If they can't point to specific textual evidence that made them predict what they predicted, they weren't predicting — they were guessing.

misconceptions.title: Genre Knowledge Means Plot Formulas
misconceptions.body: Students know that mysteries end with the culprit revealed, so they assume genre knowledge is just knowing the ending shape. Push them deeper: genre conventions include narrative distance, pacing patterns, the types of conflict that recur, the kind of evidence a genre values. A student who knows that an argumentative essay typically builds toward counterargument is using genre to predict structure — that's the move. Watch for students who use genre as an excuse to stop reading carefully because they think they already know what happens.

misconceptions.title: "I Was Right" Is the Whole Point
misconceptions.body: Students experience confirmation of a prediction as the goal — being right feels good, and teachers sometimes reward it. Reframe this: a prediction that gets corrected is more intellectually valuable than one that gets confirmed, because correction means the text did something the student hadn't accounted for. Grade the quality of the revision, not the accuracy of the original forecast.

misconceptions.title: Text Features Are Just Decoration
misconceptions.body: Students skim headings, bold terms, and subheadings as navigational aids rather than predictive tools. Teach them to read every text feature as a forecast — a heading isn't just labeling what you just read, it's telling you what the author thinks matters enough to flag. A student who ignores the subheading "Complications Arise" before reading a section and then claims to be surprised by the conflict hasn't learned to read the whole page.

misconceptions.title: Structure and Prediction Apply to Fiction Differently
misconceptions.body: Students connect text structure to informational texts and disconnect it entirely from narrative. But narrative has structure too — rising action, the placement of chapter breaks, flashback positioning, shifts in narration. A chapter that opens with a funeral forces a prediction about what the rest of the chapter will reveal. Watch for students who apply structural prediction only to essays and treat fiction as structureless experience.

assignments.name: Prediction Log with Evidence Columns
assignments.body: Students divide a page into three columns: Prediction, Text Evidence That Prompted It, and Confirmed / Corrected / Still Open. They complete this in real time as they read. At the end of the text, each prediction must be resolved with a specific line citation.

assignments.name: Genre Forecast
assignments.body: Before reading, students examine the genre, the text features (title, subheadings, any pull quotes or captions), and the structural signals visible without reading the body text. They write a half-page prediction about argument, plot, or structure. After reading, they assess how their genre knowledge helped or misled them.

assignments.name: The Correction Worth More Than the Confirmation
assignments.body: Students identify the single prediction they made that the text proved wrong most decisively. They write a paragraph explaining what textual evidence overturned their forecast and what that correction reveals about how the text is built.

assignments.name: Structural Signal Hunt
assignments.body: Students annotate a text by marking every structural feature (heading, transition phrase, paragraph break, numbered list, section division) and writing a one-sentence prediction beside each about what the next section will do. Compare predictions to what actually follows.

mastery: Identifies specific text features, genre characteristics, or structural signals as the basis for a prediction — not general impressions.
Revises predictions explicitly when the text contradicts them, citing the moment of contradiction by paragraph or line.
Distinguishes between a prediction that was confirmed and one that requires correction, and articulates why the correction matters for understanding the text.
Applies genre knowledge beyond surface-level plot formulas — uses knowledge of how genres structure argument, evidence, or conflict to forecast the text's moves.
Treats both informational and literary texts as structurally predictive objects, not just literary texts.
notes: Vertical ramp: English I 4C establishes making and revising predictions using text features and genre. English II carries the same expectation forward — the ramp is less about new skill introduction than about applying the strategy with greater precision across more complex texts and a wider range of genres. English III pushes toward synthesis: predictions become part of larger analytical claims rather than standalone reading moves.

DOK 3 justification: confirming or correcting predictions requires applying knowledge of genre and structure to make reasoned judgments — more than recall or skill application, but below the extended analytical reasoning of DOK 4.

This TEK has limited STAAR analog as a discrete tested skill — STAAR tests prediction implicitly through inference and structural-analysis items. Stems are written to reflect how those embedded test moves work in practice.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Make Connections to Experience, Other Texts, and Society
expectation: Make connections to personal experiences, ideas in other texts, and society.
overview: This TEK is where comprehension becomes meaning-making. Students aren't just decoding what a text says — they're positioning it against what they know, what they've read, and what's happening in the world. The quality of the connection is the skill; surface-level "this reminds me of my life" doesn't cut it at English II.
studentFriendly: You can read a text and explain how its ideas connect to something you've lived, something you've read, or something real in the world — and say WHY that connection matters for understanding the text.
questionStems: Which idea from the selection MOST closely connects to the theme developed in the excerpt from [other text]?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the narrator's experience MOST likely reflects which broader social reality?
Which statement BEST describes a connection between the central idea of this text and a contemporary issue?
The author's argument in paragraph X MOST likely relates to which of the following experiences?
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that the speaker's personal experience shapes the text's meaning?
Both texts develop a similar idea about [topic]. Which statement BEST explains how the two authors use this idea differently?
Based on the selection, which inference MOST likely applies to society beyond the events described in the text?
The connection between the events in paragraph X and [named social issue] MOST likely suggests —
Which detail from the text BEST illustrates a link between the narrator's experience and a universal human condition?
With which statement would BOTH authors MOST likely agree, based on their respective texts?

misconceptions.title: "Connecting to My Life" Is Enough
misconceptions.body: Students treat text-to-self as the gold standard because it feels personal and easy. They'll write paragraphs about their own experience and forget to circle back to the text. Teach them that a connection is analytical currency — it has to buy something, meaning it must illuminate the text, not replace it. If the connection doesn't deepen comprehension, it's not a reading skill; it's a diary entry.

misconceptions.title: Society Just Means "Everyone Does This"
misconceptions.body: When students try to connect to society, they vague out: "this relates to society because everyone has problems." Push them to name a specific social structure, institution, historical pattern, or current event. The move is to locate the text inside a real social context — not to invoke "society" as a catch-all. Watch for papers that use "society" without a noun anywhere nearby.

misconceptions.title: Text-to-Text Means Same Genre, Same Topic
misconceptions.body: Students assume they can only connect two texts that are obviously similar — two novels about war, two poems about grief. The richer connections are lateral: a poem to a news article, a speech to a short story, a memoir to a scientific text. Teach them that intertextual connections work best when the two texts share a tension or idea but approach it from different angles.

misconceptions.title: The Connection Proves the Text Is Right
misconceptions.body: Students use personal experience to validate a text's argument rather than to interrogate it. "I agree because this happened to me too" is not analysis. Model the harder move: using personal or social knowledge to push back, qualify, or complicate what the author claims. Agreement is fine; agreement without friction is not analysis.

misconceptions.title: Stronger Connection = More Personal Detail
misconceptions.body: Students believe the more personal detail they include, the better the connection. This is backwards — more personal detail usually means less textual focus. The best connections are precise: one specific point of contact between the reader's knowledge and the text's meaning, explained with evidence from both sides. Teach them to trim, not expand.

assignments.name: Connection Ladder
assignments.body: Students write three short paragraphs — one text-to-self, one text-to-text, one text-to-world — all on the same passage. Then they rank which connection most deepened their understanding and explain why. Forces them to evaluate, not just generate.

assignments.name: Intertextual Dialogue
assignments.body: Students select a second text (from a provided list across genres and disciplines) that connects to the anchor text, then write a one-page explanation of what idea the two texts share and where they diverge. No personal experience allowed — the constraint sharpens the intertextual reading.

assignments.name: Social Context Frame
assignments.body: Before reading a new text, students research one relevant social or historical context. After reading, they write a paragraph explaining how that context changes or deepens their reading of a specific passage. The before/after structure makes the connection visible and testable.

assignments.name: The Complicating Connection
assignments.body: Students identify a personal experience that seems to contradict something in the text and write a paragraph using that contradiction to complicate — not dismiss — the author's claim. Grades for intellectual honesty and textual evidence.

mastery: Names a specific connection (personal, intertextual, or societal) and explains what that connection reveals about the text — not just what it reminds them of.
Uses textual evidence alongside the connection rather than letting the connection stand alone.
Distinguishes between surface-level similarity ("both are about loss") and substantive connection ("both texts position grief as socially isolating rather than communally shared").
Applies social or historical knowledge to complicate or extend a text's meaning, not merely to agree with it.
Selects the most illuminating type of connection for a given text rather than defaulting to text-to-self every time.
notes: Vertical ramp: English I 4E establishes the basic habit of connecting texts to experience, other texts, and society. English II is expected to deepen the quality and analytical utility of those connections — connections must do interpretive work, not just exist. English III pushes further toward evaluating HOW connections across texts reveal patterns of meaning and how authors position themselves relative to cultural or historical contexts.

DOK 3 justification: making meaningful connections requires reasoning beyond retrieval — students must select, evaluate, and explain the relevance of connections, which involves strategic thinking and evidence integration.

This TEK appears across all four courses with nearly identical language; the vertical differentiation lives in the depth and precision of the connection expected, not in the structure of the task itself. Anchor instruction to quality of analysis, not presence of connection.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Make Inferences and Use Evidence to Support Understanding
expectation: Make inferences and use evidence to support understanding.
overview: This is the workhorse of reading comprehension — the expectation that students don't just receive what the text says but actively complete meaning the text leaves implicit. In English II, "making inferences" has to move beyond guessing; the evidence piece requires students to anchor their reading in what is actually on the page.
studentFriendly: You can read between the lines of a text, figure out what the author didn't say outright, and back up your reading with specific evidence from the selection itself.
questionStems: Based on paragraphs X–Y, what can the reader infer about —
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the inference that —
The details in paragraph X MOST likely suggest that —
What inference can be drawn from the information in paragraphs X and Y?
Which evidence from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that —
The reader can conclude from paragraph X that —
Which two sentences from the selection BEST support the inference that —
Based on the information in paragraph X, what is the MOST likely reason that —
Which statement is BEST supported by evidence from the selection?
The details in paragraphs X–Y contribute MOST to the inference that —

misconceptions.title: An Inference Is Anything You Think
misconceptions.body: Students treat "inference" as a synonym for "opinion" — whatever they feel is happening must count. Watch for responses that have no textual anchor whatsoever: "I think the character is sad because I would be sad too." Teach them the formula isn't just "I think" — it's "The text says ___; therefore I conclude ___." The inference lives in the gap, but both sides of the gap must exist.

misconceptions.title: Evidence Means Quoting the Most Dramatic Sentence
misconceptions.body: Students scan for the boldest, most vivid line in the passage and quote it regardless of whether it actually supports their inference. Push them to evaluate relevance, not just prominence. Ask: does this piece of evidence actually lead to the inference, or did you work backwards from the quote to the claim?

misconceptions.title: More Evidence Is Always Better
misconceptions.body: Students pile on four or five quotes as if volume equals strength. Teach them that one precise, well-explained piece of evidence outweighs three loosely connected ones. The STAAR stems rarely ask for a list — they ask for the BEST. Train students to apply that filter themselves before picking.

misconceptions.title: Inferences Are Optional After You Have the Main Idea
misconceptions.body: Once students feel they've "gotten" the text, they stop pressing into it. They write a solid summary and coast. The inference is exactly what the main idea doesn't cover — the why behind the what, the implication that lives under the surface. Teach them: if your inference could be copied directly from the text, it isn't an inference.

misconceptions.title: You Support an Inference by Repeating It Louder
misconceptions.body: Students restate their inference in different words and call it evidence. "I know the character is guilty because everything about her seems guilty" is circular, not supported. Watch for this in written responses. Require that evidence come from the text, not from the student's restatement of their own claim, and push them to name the specific paragraph or sentence they're drawing from.

assignments.name: Two-Column Evidence Log
assignments.body: Students read a short text and record inferences in the left column and the specific textual evidence for each in the right. They must have at least one inference per paragraph and cannot use the same sentence as evidence twice.

assignments.name: Inference Trial
assignments.body: Students write one inference about a character, setting, or event. They then "prosecute" it — finding two pieces of supporting evidence — and "defend against" it by finding one piece of counter-evidence. They conclude with a verdict: does the evidence hold?

assignments.name: Precision Revision
assignments.body: Students receive a set of weak inference-evidence pairs (pre-written by the teacher). They diagnose what is wrong with each — inference is unsupported, evidence is irrelevant, reasoning is circular — and rewrite each pair correctly using the same passage.

mastery: Distinguishes between an inference and a restatement of stated information, naming what the text leaves implicit and what it makes explicit.
Selects evidence that directly supports the inference rather than evidence that is merely related to the same topic.
Explains the logical connection between the evidence and the inference — doesn't treat the relationship as self-evident.
Evaluates competing pieces of evidence and identifies which one BEST supports a given inference.
Maintains this precision across literary and informational texts without needing separate instruction for each genre.
notes: Vertical ramp: English I introduces making inferences and using evidence to support understanding at the same standard number and letter. English II holds the same expectation language but the texts grow in complexity — inference demands increase because the author's implied meaning is buried deeper, requires more cross-paragraph synthesis, or depends on rhetorical context students must supply. English III builds toward analysis of how authors construct meaning, where inference becomes the foundation for evaluating craft.

DOK 3 justification: students must draw on evidence across multiple text locations, evaluate relevance among competing options, and construct reasoning chains — all strategic thinking that exceeds simple recall or skill application.
This standard maps directly to the STAAR Reading Comprehension strand and is among the highest-frequency inference stems on the assessment; stems in this entry reflect that frequency and variation.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Evaluate Details to Determine Key Ideas
expectation: Evaluate details read to determine key ideas.
overview: This is the skill that separates reading from skimming. Students don't just list what happened — they weigh the details on the page and decide which ones actually build the central idea and which ones are scaffolding. In English II, the emphasis is on evaluative reasoning: not "what does this say?" but "does this matter, and how much?"
studentFriendly: You can read a text and figure out which details actually matter — which ones build the central idea — and which ones are just background noise.
questionStems: Which detail from paragraph X BEST supports the key idea that —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which statement MOST accurately identifies the central idea of the passage?
The author includes the detail in paragraph X MOST likely to —
Which sentence from the selection provides the BEST evidence for the conclusion that —
Read this sentence from paragraph X. What does this detail contribute to the key idea of the passage?
Which detail from the selection is LEAST relevant to the author's central idea?
Based on the details in paragraphs X–Y, the reader can infer that —
Which two details from the selection BEST support the inference that —
The author's inclusion of [specific detail] in paragraph X suggests that the key idea of the passage is —
Which statement MOST accurately summarizes the key ideas developed across paragraphs X–Z?

misconceptions.title: "Key Idea" Means "What Happens First"
misconceptions.body: Students chronologically list events and call the first or most dramatic one the key idea. Teach them that key ideas are not chronological — they're structural. The key idea is what the details collectively prove, not what appears earliest. Push them to ask: "If I removed this detail, would the central claim still stand?" That's the weight test.

misconceptions.title: Every Detail the Author Included Must Be Equally Important
misconceptions.body: Students assume authorial intent means every sentence carries equal load. Watch for students who can't rank details — who say "they're all important" when asked to identify which one matters most. Teach them that authors make deliberate choices about emphasis: sentence placement, repetition, and proportion signal importance. Not every brick is a load-bearing wall.

misconceptions.title: The Key Idea Is Just the Topic
misconceptions.body: Students write "the key idea is dogs" instead of "the key idea is that dogs develop loyalty through early socialization." They're naming the subject, not the claim the details are building. Teach them the difference between a topic (a noun) and a key idea (a statement about that noun that the details defend). Require a complete sentence for every key idea they identify.

misconceptions.title: Details That Are Surprising Must Be Important
misconceptions.body: Students gravitate toward vivid or unexpected details and assume they're central. The unusual anecdote, the shocking statistic — these feel important because they're memorable. But prominent placement doesn't equal centrality. Teach them to check: does this surprising detail tie back to the main claim, or is it an illustration that makes one supporting point? Memorable does not mean foundational.

misconceptions.title: Evaluating Means Judging Whether the Author Is Right
misconceptions.body: Students collapse "evaluate the details" into "decide if I agree with the author." They start arguing with the text instead of analyzing it. Reframe this: evaluation here means measuring the detail's relationship to the key idea — does it support it, complicate it, qualify it, or drift away from it? Their opinion of the author's position is irrelevant to this task.

assignments.name: Detail Weigh-In
assignments.body: Students read a two-page informational text and rank the five most significant details in order of importance, with a one-sentence justification for each ranking. The class then compares rankings and argues for their ordering — requiring students to articulate the standard by which a detail earns its weight.

assignments.name: Key Idea Surgery
assignments.body: Students highlight every sentence in a short passage, then mark each one as "load-bearing" (directly builds the key idea), "supporting" (illustrates a point), or "decorative" (adds texture, not structure). They write a one-paragraph explanation of their sorting and identify the key idea that emerges from the load-bearing sentences alone.

assignments.name: Two Texts, One Idea
assignments.body: Students read two short passages on the same topic. They identify the key idea of each and then explain how different details were selected to build different key ideas — even when the subject is identical. This surfaces the evaluative move: the author chose these details because they support THIS claim, not some other one.

mastery: Identifies the key idea of a passage as a complete, arguable statement — not a topic label.
Selects details that directly support the key idea and can explain why each one matters to the central claim.
Distinguishes between details that are vivid or memorable and details that are structurally important to the argument or narrative.
Recognizes when a detail complicates or qualifies a key idea rather than simply supporting it.
Produces written responses that connect specific textual evidence to an explicitly stated key idea without drifting into personal agreement or disagreement.
notes: Vertical ramp: English I 4G requires students to "evaluate details read to determine key ideas" — the expectation language is identical across courses, but English II raises the bar through text complexity and the sophistication of the inferential moves required to connect details to key ideas across longer, multi-source, or ambiguous texts.
English III 4G will extend this into synthesis across multiple texts; in English II, the evaluative work is primarily within a single text or a paired set.

DOK 3 justification: students are not retrieving stated key ideas — they are weighing details against each other and constructing a key idea claim, which requires strategic thinking and justification, not mere recall or skill application.

STAAR analog: this TEK is directly tested in both literary and informational reading; stems targeting "which detail BEST supports" and "which statement MOST accurately identifies the central idea" are the primary surface forms.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Synthesize Information from Multiple Texts
expectation: Synthesize information from multiple texts to create new understanding.
overview: Synthesis is the hardest move in the comprehension stack — it requires students to hold two or more texts in mind simultaneously, find the friction or overlap between them, and generate an idea that neither text states alone. This is where reading becomes thinking.
studentFriendly: When you read two or more texts together, you can build an idea that none of the texts say on their own — one that only exists because you put them in conversation with each other.
questionStems: Based on both selections, which statement BEST represents an idea supported by both authors?
Which conclusion about [topic] is BEST supported by information from both selections?
A student reads both selections and writes the following claim: [claim]. Which evidence from BOTH texts BEST supports this claim?
The authors of both selections would MOST likely agree that —
How does the information in selection 2 develop or challenge an idea presented in selection 1?
Which statement MOST accurately describes how the two authors differ in their understanding of [topic]?
Based on both selections, what can the reader infer about [concept] that neither author states directly?
A student synthesizes both selections and concludes that [conclusion]. Which detail from selection 1 and which detail from selection 2 BEST support this conclusion?
Which idea from paragraph X of selection 2 contributes MOST to a new understanding of the argument made in paragraphs X–Y of selection 1?
With which statement would BOTH authors MOST likely agree based on the evidence in their respective selections?

misconceptions.title: Synthesis Is Just Summary With Two Sources
misconceptions.body: Students list what each text says, one after the other, and call it synthesis. Watch for the "Text 1 says... Text 2 says..." paragraph structure — it signals comparison, not synthesis. Push them to write the sentence that only exists because both texts were read: the idea neither author stated but both support.

misconceptions.title: The Texts Have to Agree for Synthesis to Work
misconceptions.body: Students freeze when two sources contradict each other, assuming conflict means one source is wrong and must be discarded. Teach them that tension between texts is the most generative raw material — a new understanding can emerge precisely because the authors disagree. The disagreement becomes the insight.

misconceptions.title: New Understanding Means a Personal Opinion
misconceptions.body: When students hear "create new understanding," they reach for their own feelings about the topic. Synthesis is not a reaction — it is a constructed claim that is grounded in and traceable to the source texts. Teach them to ask: "Could someone check my synthesis against the texts and confirm it?" If not, it's opinion, not synthesis.

misconceptions.title: Synthesis Only Happens at the End
misconceptions.body: Students treat synthesis as a final product — something to write at the end of a reading unit. But real synthesis happens in the act of reading: noticing where text two reframes a claim from text one, where an example in text three resolves a contradiction between texts one and two. Teach them to annotate with synthesis moves as they read, not after.

misconceptions.title: More Sources Always Means Better Synthesis
misconceptions.body: Students assume that pulling from all available texts signals thoroughness. Watch for kitchen-sink synthesis that quotes everything but generates nothing. Two texts used precisely produce stronger synthesis than five texts used superficially. Teach them to choose the most productive collision points, not the most sources.

assignments.name: The Third Text
assignments.body: Students read two short informational texts on a shared topic and write a "third text" — a one-page piece that makes a claim only possible because both sources were read. They must cite specific evidence from each and explicitly name what is new in their synthesis.

assignments.name: Synthesis Sentence Showdown
assignments.body: After a paired reading, students each draft a single synthesis sentence on a notecard. Cards are read aloud. The class evaluates: Is this actually new? Is it grounded in both texts? Is it summary disguised as synthesis? The strongest card is used as a shared thesis for a group essay.

assignments.name: Contradiction to Claim
assignments.body: Students receive two texts that take opposing positions on the same issue. Their task is not to pick a side but to write a synthesis claim that accounts for both — a claim that could not exist if only one text had been read. This is scored for whether the tension between texts is productive, not resolved.

mastery: Produces a claim or understanding that is demonstrably absent from any single source text but is traceable to evidence across all texts used.
Moves fluidly between texts, using one to complicate, extend, or qualify claims from another rather than treating each source in isolation.
Distinguishes between synthesis, comparison, and summary in their own writing when pressed to explain the difference.
Generates new understanding from texts in tension as readily as from texts in agreement.
Traces every synthesized claim back to specific textual evidence, maintaining credibility even when the claim itself goes beyond what any single source states.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I (4)(H) asks students to synthesize information from multiple texts — the expectation language is identical, but the complexity of paired texts and the sophistication expected in the "new understanding" increase meaningfully by English II. Teachers should look for whether students are generating ideas that genuinely extend beyond the individual sources, not just paraphrasing both.

Vertical ramp to English III: English III (4)(H) maintains the same frame; the vertical progression is carried by the increased complexity of the texts assigned and the rigor of the written synthesis expected, particularly in argument-based genres.

DOK 4 justification: creating new understanding across sources requires sustained reasoning, cross-textual analysis, and construction of an original claim — all beyond retrieval or single-source analysis.

STAAR note: synthesis appears most frequently on STAAR in paired-passage sets where the final question asks for a claim both authors support or an inference neither author states directly. The question stems above mirror that structure.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Monitor Comprehension and Make Adjustments When Understanding Breaks Down
expectation: Monitor comprehension and make adjustments such as re-reading, using background knowledge, asking questions, and annotating when understanding breaks down.
overview: This is the metacognitive backbone of the comprehension strand — the skill that makes all the others recoverable. Without it, students who lose the thread just keep going, arriving at the end of a passage with nothing. The TEK asks students not only to NOTICE when understanding fails but to DO something about it, using a specific toolkit.
studentFriendly: You know the moment when you finish a paragraph and realize you have no idea what just happened — this TEK is about catching that moment early and knowing exactly what to do next to get back on track.
questionStems: The reader in paragraph 3 most likely re-reads lines 12–15 in order to —
Which detail from paragraph 2 BEST helps a reader clarify the meaning of the events described in paragraph 1?
Based on paragraphs 4–6, which question would MOST help a reader who lost track of the central argument?
A reader annotates the phrase "without precedent" in line 22. Which of the following BEST explains what that annotation would MOST likely help the reader understand?
Which sentence from the selection BEST signals a point where a careful reader should pause and check comprehension?
A reader uses background knowledge about [topic] to clarify the events in paragraph 5. Which inference does that background knowledge MOST likely support?
How does the information in paragraph 7 help the reader resolve confusion introduced in paragraph 3?
Which detail from the selection would MOST likely prompt a reader to re-read the opening paragraph?
Based on the structure of paragraphs 2–4, which question would a strategic reader MOST likely ask before continuing to paragraph 5?
A reader who annotates the shift in paragraph 6 would MOST likely note that —

misconceptions.title: Finishing the Page Counts as Reading
misconceptions.body: Students treat physical completion — eyes passing over every word — as equivalent to comprehension. They hit a wall, keep moving, and call it done. Teach them that the contract of reading is understanding, not coverage. If you can't summarize the last paragraph in one sentence, you didn't read it — you scanned it. Build a classroom norm where stopping is a sign of skill, not failure.

misconceptions.title: Re-Reading Means Starting Over
misconceptions.body: When students hear "re-read," they imagine returning to page one. The repair strategy is surgical, not wholesale. Watch for students who, told to re-read, flip back six pages when the confusion lives in the sentence they just finished. Teach them to identify the exact sentence or phrase where understanding broke down and re-read from that seam, not from the beginning.

misconceptions.title: Annotation Is Highlighting
misconceptions.body: Students conflate annotation with color-coding. They highlight entire paragraphs in yellow and consider it done. Real annotation names the breakdown: "I don't understand why she changes her mind here," "This contradicts what he said in paragraph 2," "What does this word do to the argument?" Push them toward marginal questions and summaries — language that forces them to process, not decorate.

misconceptions.title: Background Knowledge Is Just "Knowing Stuff"
misconceptions.body: Students assume background knowledge means knowing the topic generally. The move is more specific: connecting a precise prior schema to a precise confusion in the text. Teach them to ask "What do I already know that could explain THIS?" — targeting the breakdown, not the subject at large. A student who knows about the Civil Rights Movement needs to apply the relevant piece of that knowledge to the sentence that confused them, not dump everything they know into the margin.

misconceptions.title: You Only Monitor Once Per Passage
misconceptions.body: Students check comprehension at the end — if the ending made sense, they conclude they understood the whole thing. Comprehension monitoring is continuous, not terminal. Show them that a passage can make sense at the end because the ending is clear, even though they missed the argument in paragraph 3. Endings don't retroactively fix a broken middle. Teach them to monitor per paragraph, per section, per shift in argument.

assignments.name: Breakdown Map
assignments.body: Students read a complex passage and mark every point where understanding slipped — not content annotations, just confusion flags. After finishing, they return to each flag and record which repair strategy they used (re-read, question, background knowledge, annotate) and whether it worked. Debrief focuses on pattern: where did most breakdowns happen, and why?

assignments.name: Repair Strategy Log
assignments.body: Over three independent reading sessions, students keep a two-column log: left column records the sentence or passage that broke down; right column records the strategy used and what it produced. At the end of the unit, students write a one-paragraph reflection on which strategy works best for them and in what conditions.

assignments.name: Think-Aloud Transcript
assignments.body: In pairs, one student reads aloud while the other scribes their metacognitive commentary — every "wait, what?" and every repair move. The pair then reviews the transcript together and categorizes each breakdown and repair. This makes the invisible visible.

assignments.name: Annotated Re-Read
assignments.body: Students receive a passage they read cold in a prior class. They re-read it now with the explicit task of annotating for comprehension: marking where they were confused the first time, noting what they now understand, and explaining what repair strategy — even in retrospect — would have helped.

mastery: Identifies the specific sentence or phrase where comprehension broke down, rather than gesturing at a general section or page.

Selects a repair strategy that matches the type of breakdown — re-reading for lost thread, background knowledge for unfamiliar context, annotation for tracking argument, questioning for ambiguous meaning.
Evaluates whether the chosen strategy worked and pivots to a second strategy if the first did not restore understanding.
Produces annotations that name the confusion and track the repair, not just highlight or underline.
Sustains monitoring continuously across a passage rather than checking only at the end.
notes: Vertical ramp: English I 4(I) introduces monitoring and repair as a discrete set of named strategies. English II holds the same four strategies (re-reading, background knowledge, questioning, annotation) but expects students to deploy them against increasingly complex texts — longer arguments, denser syntax, less explicit structure. English III 4(I) adds independence: students are expected to have internalized the selection and sequencing of strategies without teacher scaffolding.

DOK 3 justification: selecting and applying an appropriate repair strategy requires strategic thinking beyond retrieval or skill application — it demands situational judgment about which tool fits which breakdown.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is tested obliquely. STAAR does not ask "what would you do if you were confused?" but embeds comprehension monitoring in items that ask which detail clarifies an earlier ambiguity, which re-reading would resolve a contradiction, or which annotation captures a pivot. The stems above mirror those item types. Note this for teachers who wonder why the stems don't say "monitor comprehension" explicitly.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Describe Personal Connections to a Variety of Sources
expectation: Describe personal connections to a variety of sources, including self-selected texts.
overview: This TEK is the floor of response work: students articulate not just what a text says but what it does to them personally. The word "describe" is load-bearing — it demands specificity, not vague resonance. The "variety of sources" language is a deliberate push against the canonized reading list; students' own choices belong in the conversation.
studentFriendly: You can read something — a book you picked yourself, an article, a poem — and explain in specific, honest terms what it connects to in your own life or thinking, and why that connection matters.
questionStems: Which sentence from the selection MOST likely explains why the narrator's experience would feel familiar to a reader who has —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, a reader who has experienced [situation] would MOST likely identify with the narrator because —
The speaker's description in paragraph X MOST nearly reflects the feeling of —
Which detail from the text BEST supports the inference that the narrator understands what it means to —
A student reading this passage for the first time would MOST likely connect the events in paragraph X to which personal experience?
The author's use of [detail/image] in paragraph X suggests that readers who have faced [situation] would MOST likely feel —
Based on the passage, which statement BEST describes how the narrator's situation compares to a common personal experience?
Which line from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that the text is speaking to readers who have felt [emotion or experience]?
How does the narrator's reaction in paragraph X contribute to a reader's ability to see their own experience in the text?
A reader reflecting on the events in paragraphs X–Y would MOST likely conclude that the author intended to connect with readers who —

misconceptions.title: "I Can Relate" Is a Complete Response
misconceptions.body: Students write "I can relate to this because it happened to me too" and stop there. That's recognition, not description. Teach them the difference: recognition says the connection exists; description explains what specific element of the text triggered it, what specific moment in their life it maps to, and what that overlap reveals. Push for the "so what" — a connection without significance is just a coincidence.

misconceptions.title: Personal Connection Means the Text Has to Be About You
misconceptions.body: Students dismiss whole categories of text — historical fiction, speculative writing, nonfiction about unfamiliar cultures — because they can't find a one-to-one match with their biography. Teach them that connection operates through emotion, theme, and situation, not identity overlap. A student who has never lost a parent can still connect to grief; a student who has never traveled can connect to longing. The skill is translation, not mirroring.

misconceptions.title: Self-Selected Texts Don't Count as Serious Reading
misconceptions.body: Students (and sometimes teachers) treat self-selected texts as less rigorous than assigned ones — fan fiction, manga, sports journalism, YouTube scripts. Push back on this. The TEK names self-selected texts explicitly for a reason: the reading students choose on their own is where the most honest personal connection lives. If you only ever ask for connections to what you assigned, you've cut off half the skill.

misconceptions.title: More Emotion Equals Better Connection
misconceptions.body: Students who are strong feelers sometimes outrun the text — they're describing their own memory rather than connecting it to specific textual evidence. Watch for responses that wander away from the page entirely. A personal connection response still has to be anchored: the connection must be traceable back to language, image, or event in the source. Emotion is the bridge; the text is one of the banks.

misconceptions.title: Describing a Connection and Analyzing a Connection Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: At English II, students often conflate these and either go too shallow (just naming the connection) or too deep too fast (jumping to interpretation before the connection is grounded). The DOK 2 target is clear description with enough specificity to be verifiable. Analysis — asking why the connection matters structurally or thematically — belongs in adjacent standards. Keep the sequence straight: describe first, then interpret.

assignments.name: Source Portfolio Response
assignments.body: Students select three texts from different genres or formats — one assigned, one self-selected, one from another class or outside school. For each, they write a focused paragraph describing one specific personal connection, naming the exact passage or moment that triggered it and what it connects to in their experience.

assignments.name: Parallel Moments
assignments.body: Students find a moment in a text that parallels a moment in their own life and write a double-entry journal: the left column holds the text passage verbatim, the right column holds a precise description of the personal moment. Then one paragraph explains the overlap without collapsing the differences.

assignments.name: Connection Roundtable
assignments.body: Small groups, each member having read a different self-selected text. Each student describes one personal connection to their text; the group asks two follow-up questions requiring more specificity. The goal is to sharpen vague connections into precise ones through peer pressure — productive pressure.

mastery: Names the specific passage, image, or moment in the text that triggered the connection — not just the general topic.
Describes the personal experience or knowledge the text connects to with enough detail that a reader who doesn't know them could understand the link.
Draws on a variety of source types, including self-selected texts, not only teacher-assigned reading.
Maintains the distinction between the text's meaning and their own experience — the connection is a bridge, not a merger.
Produces responses that are specific and verifiable: another reader could confirm the connection is grounded in the actual text.
notes: Vertical ramp: English I asks students to describe personal connections as well — the English II version does not dramatically raise the ceiling in wording, but the expectation in practice is that connections are more precisely anchored to textual evidence and more varied in source type. English III shifts the response work toward analysis of theme and author's craft rather than personal connection alone. This standard is a bridge: students who can describe connections honestly are ready to interpret why the text constructs those connections.

DOK 2 justification: "describe" requires students to process and organize information (not just recall), but the target is accurate description rather than extended argumentation — the skill lives at the comprehension-to-application boundary.

No direct STAAR analog: personal connection as a standalone construct does not appear on STAAR as its own question type, but the inference and reader-response framing of many STAAR literary items tests the same underlying ability to map textual details onto recognizable human experience. Stems above mirror that STAAR framing.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Write Responses That Demonstrate Understanding of Texts, Including Comparing Texts Within and Across Genres
expectation: Write responses that demonstrate understanding of texts, including comparing texts within and across genres.
overview: This standard is the written proof of reading. It's not enough for students to have understood a text — they must construct a response that reveals that understanding, and at the English II level, the harder move is cross-genre comparison: holding a poem and an essay in the same hand and finding what they share or where they break apart.
studentFriendly: You can write a response that shows — not just tells — that you genuinely understood what you read, and when you read two different texts, you can point to what they have in common and where they diverge, even when they're written in completely different forms.
questionStems: Both selections deal with the theme of [theme]. Which statement BEST describes how the authors' approaches to this theme differ?
The student's response in paragraph X MOST likely fails to demonstrate understanding of the passage because —
Based on the two selections, which statement would BOTH authors MOST likely agree with?
Which piece of evidence from Selection 1 BEST supports the comparison made in paragraph X of the student response?
How does the genre of Selection 2 MOST likely affect the way the author develops the central idea compared to Selection 1?
Which sentence from the student response BEST demonstrates understanding of the author's purpose in the passage?
The student compares the two texts in paragraph X. Which revision would MOST strengthen the comparison?
Based on both selections, what conclusion can MOST reasonably be drawn about how [concept] is portrayed across genres?
Which sentence from Selection 1 and which sentence from Selection 2 BEST support the inference that both authors share a similar concern about —
The student's response addresses only one of the two texts. Which addition would MOST effectively complete the cross-genre comparison?

misconceptions.title: Summary Is the Same as Response
misconceptions.body: Students retell the text and call it a response. They'll give you a tidy three-paragraph plot summary and expect full credit because they "showed they understood it." Teach them the distinction: a response uses the text as evidence for a claim — it doesn't just report what happened. Watch for the tell: if the first sentence of their response is "This story is about...," they're summarizing.

misconceptions.title: Cross-Genre Just Means Noticing the Format Difference
misconceptions.body: Students compare a poem to an essay and write "the poem has stanzas but the essay has paragraphs." That's genre observation, not cross-genre analysis. Push them to ask what the formal difference DOES — how does a poem's compression change what the author can say? How does the essay's extended argument change how a reader is persuaded? The genres matter because they enable different moves, not just different appearances.

misconceptions.title: Comparing Means Finding Similarities
misconceptions.body: When students hear "compare," they often look only for common ground and stop there. But comparison includes contrast, and sometimes the contrast is the whole point — two texts on grief can share the subject and be doing completely opposite things with it. Teach them that "compare" in an academic context means both sides of the ledger: what aligns and what diverges.

misconceptions.title: Understanding Is Demonstrated by Quoting
misconceptions.body: Students believe that dropping a quote into their response proves comprehension. It doesn't — it proves they can locate text. Understanding is demonstrated by what they do AFTER the quote: do they explain it, connect it, use it to advance a claim? Watch for the unintroduced, unexplained quote sitting in a paragraph like a borrowed piece of furniture nobody knows what to do with.

misconceptions.title: Genre Comparison Requires Identical Topics
misconceptions.body: Students freeze up when asked to compare texts on different surface subjects, assuming cross-genre comparison only works when both texts are "about the same thing." The deeper skill is thematic and structural: two texts can share an argument about power, justice, or identity even when one is a short story set in 1940 and the other is a contemporary editorial. Reframe comparison as reading for what both texts are DOING, not just what they're about.

assignments.name: The Two-Column Pre-Write
assignments.body: Before students write a comparison response, they complete a two-column organizer: one column per text, same rows (central idea, key evidence, author's purpose, form/genre effect). The organizer is graded — students must demonstrate understanding of each text individually before the comparison is written.

assignments.name: Repair the Response
assignments.body: Give students a weak sample response that summarizes rather than analyzes. Students identify exactly where the response fails to demonstrate understanding, then revise one paragraph to show what a genuine response looks like. Debrief: what did the revision add that the original lacked?

assignments.name: Same Theme, Different Genre
assignments.body: Pair texts that share a theme but differ in genre (e.g., a narrative essay and a lyric poem on loss, or an op-ed and a short story on justice). Students write a focused two-page response identifying how each text develops the theme and what the genre difference makes possible or impossible.

assignments.name: Live Comparison Build
assignments.body: Students start with a response to one text, then receive a second text mid-class and must expand their response to account for both. The expansion must include explicit cross-genre analysis. This simulates the time-pressure of a timed writing while building genuine comparative thinking.

mastery: Writes a response with a defensible claim that goes beyond restating the text's content.
Uses textual evidence from both selections purposefully — quotes are introduced, cited, and explained in service of a comparison claim.
Identifies how genre shapes meaning, not just how genre shapes form.
Maintains a clear organizational structure that makes the comparison readable without relying on "one similarity is... one difference is..." filler scaffolding.
Demonstrates understanding of both texts independently before — and as the basis for — their comparison.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I 5B asks students to write responses demonstrating understanding of texts including comparing texts within and across genres — same expectation on its face. The English II jump is in the complexity of paired texts and the sophistication expected of the comparative argument: students should be moving from basic "both authors say X" toward "the genre of Selection 2 forces the author to compress what Selection 1 can unfold at length."
Vertical ramp to English III: English III 5B adds extended analysis and research-based response expectations; the comparative mode established here becomes the baseline, not the goal.

DOK 3 justification: students must apply understanding of both texts to construct an original comparative claim — this requires strategic thinking beyond recall or skill application, but stops short of the extended meta-level evaluation that would push it to DOK 4.

STAAR note: this TEK appears directly in paired-passage timed writing prompts and in selected-response items that ask students to identify the BEST comparison between two texts. Stems written to mirror that context.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Use Text Evidence and Original Commentary to Support an Interpretive Response
expectation: Use text evidence and original commentary to support an interpretive response.
overview: This is the core writing-about-reading move in English II: students don't just drop a quotation and leave it on the table. They interpret — they say what the evidence means, why it matters, and how it serves the larger claim. The danger zone is the quotation sandwich that has no real meat in the middle.
studentFriendly: You can select a specific moment from a text, quote or paraphrase it accurately, and then explain in your own words what that moment reveals about the work's larger meaning — not just what happened, but why it matters.
questionStems: Which quotation from the selection BEST supports the student's interpretation in paragraph X?
A student writes the following response: [response]. Which sentence from the selection would MOST effectively support the student's claim?
The student's commentary in paragraph X suggests that —
Which detail from the selection BEST develops the student's interpretive claim that [claim]?
Read the following student response: [response]. The evidence cited in paragraph X MOST likely supports the idea that —
Which sentence from the selection would MOST effectively strengthen the student's interpretation in paragraph Y?
A student claims that [claim]. Which quotation from the selection BEST supports this interpretation?
The student's use of evidence in paragraphs X–Y MOST clearly supports the inference that —
Which of the following is the MOST effective original commentary a student could add after the quotation in paragraph X?
Based on the student's response, which statement BEST describes how the evidence supports the interpretation?

misconceptions.title: Quotation Is Commentary
misconceptions.body: Students believe that dropping a quotation counts as explaining it. They quote, then move on — as if the text speaks for itself. Teach them the rule: every quotation is a question that must be answered. What does this mean? Why does it matter here? Where does it connect to the claim? No quotation gets to exit without commentary.

misconceptions.title: "In this quote, the author says..." Is Analysis
misconceptions.body: Students mistake plot summary or paraphrase for original commentary. They restate what the text already said and call it an explanation. Watch for commentary that simply renames the quotation in different words — that is not interpretation, it is transcription. Push them to say what the evidence reveals about character, theme, or authorial intention that wasn't already stated.

misconceptions.title: More Evidence Means a Stronger Argument
misconceptions.body: Students pile up quotations under the impression that quantity builds credibility. Three thin quotations with no commentary are weaker than one quotation with a developed, specific explanation. Reframe the task: the evidence is not the argument — the commentary is. Evidence only does work when someone actually works it.

misconceptions.title: "I Think" Makes It Interpretive
misconceptions.body: Students believe hedging with "I think" or "in my opinion" transforms a claim into an interpretive response. The personal qualifier does nothing structural. An interpretation requires a defensible claim about the text's meaning and textual evidence that actually supports it. Teach them to test their commentary: could another reader dispute this reading using the same text? If no, it's summary; if yes, it's interpretation.

misconceptions.title: Original Commentary Means Creative or Personal
misconceptions.body: Students read "original" and write about their own lives or produce metaphors disconnected from the text. Original commentary means the student's own analytical thinking applied directly to the evidence — not autobiography, not creative flourish. The commentary must tie back to the claim and stay anchored in what the text is actually doing.

assignments.name: Quote, Evidence, So What
assignments.body: Students practice a three-step written structure: (1) introduce and cite a quotation, (2) paraphrase what the text says, (3) write at least two sentences of original commentary explaining what the evidence reveals. Repeat with three different quotations from the same text. Peer review focuses solely on step three.

assignments.name: Commentary Surgery
assignments.body: Provide students with five student-written literary response paragraphs — some with strong commentary, some with placeholder commentary or none. Students identify which paragraphs have real commentary and which are "naked quotations," then rewrite the weak paragraphs' commentary without changing the quoted evidence.

assignments.name: Competing Interpretations
assignments.body: Two students write interpretive responses to the same passage, arguing opposite claims. Each student must use the same three quotations but write entirely different commentary. Class discussion examines which commentary is more persuasive and why — demonstrating that the evidence alone does not make the argument.

mastery: Selects textual evidence that directly and specifically supports the stated interpretive claim rather than restating plot.
Writes original commentary that explains what the evidence reveals — not what it says — and connects it explicitly to the larger interpretation.
Distinguishes between paraphrase, summary, and analytical commentary in their own writing and in peer writing.
Produces interpretive responses in which evidence and commentary are proportioned correctly: commentary outweighs quotation in length and analytical depth.
Sustains a defensible interpretive claim across multiple pieces of evidence without allowing the argument to drift into personal narrative or unsupported opinion.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I 5C asks students to use text evidence to support a response. English II adds "original commentary" as an explicit requirement — the analytical layer between evidence and claim becomes its own assessed component. English III pushes further into critique and synthesis across multiple texts.

DOK 3 justification: students must select evidence strategically AND construct original analytical commentary that makes a defensible interpretive claim — going beyond retrieval or simple application into genuine reasoning about textual meaning.

STAAR tests this TEK primarily through literary and informational response prompts scored on the STAAR Writing rubric; the question stems above are framed as item-set questions that assess evidence selection and commentary quality in student-written response excerpts — the format used on STAAR's analytical writing assessments.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Paraphrase and Summarize Texts to Maintain Meaning and Logical Order
expectation: Paraphrase and summarize texts in ways that maintain meaning and logical order.
overview: This TEK is where reading comprehension becomes written proof of comprehension. Paraphrasing and summarizing are distinct moves — one restates, one condenses — and both demand that students hold meaning accountable through the process. The recurring failure is students who change the words but not the structure, or who write a summary that's actually a list of plot events in original sentence fragments.
studentFriendly: You can read something and then restate it accurately in your own words — whether you're condensing the whole thing into a tight summary or translating a specific passage into language that shows you actually understood it.
questionStems: Read the following summary of paragraphs X–Y. Which detail from the summary BEST captures the central idea of those paragraphs?
Which sentence MOST accurately paraphrases the author's claim in paragraph X?
A student summarized paragraphs X–Y as follows: [student summary]. Which statement BEST explains why this summary does NOT maintain the logical order of the original?
Which of the following BEST summarizes the author's main point in paragraphs X–Y?
The author's argument in paragraphs X–Y is MOST nearly restated by which of the following?
Which sentence from the text would MOST likely be included in an accurate summary of the passage?
A student paraphrased paragraph X as follows: [student paraphrase]. Which revision BEST corrects this paraphrase to maintain the original meaning?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which of the following summaries MOST clearly preserves the author's logical order?
Which detail from paragraph X is MOST important to include in a summary of the entire passage?
The events in paragraphs X–Z are BEST summarized by which of the following?

misconceptions.title: Changing the Words Is Paraphrasing
misconceptions.body: Students run sentences through a mental thesaurus — swap a noun here, an adjective there — and call it a paraphrase. Teach them that paraphrasing is a comprehension act, not a word-substitution act. The test: put the original face-down and write the restatement from memory. If they can't, they don't understand it well enough to paraphrase it.

misconceptions.title: A Summary Is a Shorter Version of Everything
misconceptions.body: Students try to compress every detail and end up with a list of events wearing the original's structure. Push them to identify what the text is *doing* — making an argument, tracing a cause, building a character — and let that shape the summary. A summary isn't the text on a diet; it's an answer to "what does this text accomplish and how?"

misconceptions.title: Logical Order Means Chronological Order
misconceptions.body: Students lock "logical order" to sequence — first, then, next, finally — and treat every text as if it were a narrative. For argumentative and analytical texts, logical order may be claim, evidence, concession, rebuttal. Teach them to map the logic of the original before they write the summary, not after.

misconceptions.title: Paraphrase Means Opinion-Free Restatement
misconceptions.body: Students often slide their own interpretation into a paraphrase — softening an author's assertion, adding hedges that weren't there, or sharpening a claim the author kept nuanced. Watch for paraphrases that are subtly more or less confident than the source. Teach them to check their paraphrase against the original: "Did the author actually say this, or is this what I think the author meant?"

misconceptions.title: A Summary Is Just the Introduction and Conclusion
misconceptions.body: Students gravitate to the first and last paragraphs and ignore the body. This produces summaries that sound correct but miss the intellectual work of the text — the evidence, the counterargument, the complication. Teach them that the body paragraphs are the meaning; the introduction and conclusion are the frame.

assignments.name: Face-Down Paraphrase
assignments.body: Students read a complex paragraph, close the text, and write a paraphrase entirely from memory. They then open the text and compare their version to the original, marking places where meaning slipped, shifted, or was lost. Brief written reflection required.

assignments.name: Three-Sentence Summary Drill
assignments.body: Students summarize a full essay or chapter in exactly three sentences: one for the central idea, one for the method or structure, one for the significance. Constraint forces prioritization over compression.

assignments.name: Broken Summary Fix
assignments.body: Provide students with a summary that has one meaning error, one logical-order violation, and one unsupported addition. Students identify and correct all three, then write a brief explanation of each fix.

assignments.name: Side-by-Side Comparison
assignments.body: Students paraphrase the same paragraph individually, then compare paraphrases in pairs. Where do their versions agree? Where do they diverge — and does the divergence reveal a comprehension gap or a genuine ambiguity in the original?
