mastery: Produces paraphrases that accurately restate meaning without borrowing the source's sentence structure or substituting synonyms mechanically.
Writes summaries that preserve the text's central idea and logical sequence without importing minor details or personal interpretation.
Distinguishes between paraphrase (restatement of a specific passage) and summary (condensation of the whole) and applies each appropriately to the task.
Identifies when a paraphrase or summary has distorted the original meaning and revises accordingly.
Maintains neutral, accurate representation of the source text — neither softening nor amplifying the author's claims.
notes: Vertical ramp: English I 5D establishes the same expectation at the foundational level — students are paraphrasing and summarizing with teacher support and shorter texts. English II expects independence across more complex texts and more varied logical structures (argumentative, analytical, literary). English III 5D pushes further: students are expected to synthesize paraphrase and summary within their own compositions, not just as response exercises.

DOK 2 justification: the core skill is comprehension-level — restating and condensing — but the "maintains meaning and logical order" qualifier lifts it to DOK 2 because students must make interpretive judgments about which information is central and how ideas are sequenced.

No direct STAAR analog for the discrete act of paraphrasing, but summarization is embedded in open-ended response scoring and in prose constructed response prompts. Question stems above mirror how STAAR tests summary accuracy through incorrect-summary identification and best-summary selection.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Interact with Sources in Meaningful Ways
expectation: Interact with sources in meaningful ways such as notetaking, annotating, freewriting, or illustrating.
overview: This TEK is about getting students off the sidelines of a text and into it — physically, verbally, visually. The real concern is that students who don't interact with sources are not really reading them; they're skimming and hoping. Annotation, freewriting, and notetaking are the tools that make thinking visible and accountable.
studentFriendly: You can read a text and leave marks on it — notes, questions, highlights, sketches — that prove you were thinking the whole time, not just moving your eyes across the page.
questionStems: Read the student's annotation in the margin of paragraph 3. Which statement BEST describes what the annotation reveals about the student's understanding of the passage?
A student underlines the phrase [phrase] in paragraph X and writes "why?" in the margin. Which inference about the student's thinking is MOST supported by this annotation?
Which detail from the passage would MOST likely be included in a reader's notes focused on the author's central argument?
Based on the student's freewriting response after reading paragraphs X–Y, which statement BEST captures the student's interpretation of the author's purpose?

A reader annotates paragraph 4 by circling the word [word] and drawing an arrow to a detail in paragraph 7. What does this annotation MOST likely suggest about the relationship between the two paragraphs?
Which sentence from the passage would MOST likely prompt a reader to write a marginal question about the author's reasoning?
A student's notes after reading the passage include the phrases [phrase A] and [phrase B]. Which statement BEST describes what these notes reveal about the student's comprehension of the text?
Which detail from the passage would MOST likely be captured in a reader's notes as evidence supporting the author's claim in paragraph 5?
Based on the freewriting excerpt below, which statement MOST accurately reflects the reader's response to the author's use of [technique] in paragraph X?
A reader illustrates the sequence of events described in paragraphs X–Z. Which statement BEST describes how this illustration demonstrates comprehension of the passage's structure?

misconceptions.title: Highlighting IS Annotating
misconceptions.body: Students take out a highlighter, paint half the page yellow, and think they've engaged with the text. Marking a passage without writing why it matters is decoration, not thinking. Teach them: every mark needs a margin note that explains what the mark means — connection, question, claim, confusion, contradiction. The note is the annotation; the highlight is just a pointer.

misconceptions.title: Freewriting Means Writing Whatever Comes to Mind
misconceptions.body: Students treat freewriting as stream-of-consciousness journaling with no stakes. They fill lines with "I think this is interesting" and "I liked this part" and call it done. Push them deeper: freewriting is fast, generative thinking about the TEXT — what's the author doing, what does this remind you of, what doesn't add up, what's the move here. Speed is the tool; the text is the subject.

misconceptions.title: Good Notes Copy the Text
misconceptions.body: Students transcribe whole sentences from the source and call it notetaking. Then they go back to their notes and have the text again, not their thinking about the text. Teach them to close the source, write from memory in their own words, and only return to quote when precision matters. Notes that rephrase are notes that digest.

misconceptions.title: Illustrating Is for Students Who Can't Write
misconceptions.body: When given the option to illustrate, some students treat it as a lesser alternative — a fallback for kids who don't know the words. Others use it to avoid committing to an interpretation. Reframe illustration as translation: converting a textual structure, sequence, or relationship into visual form requires the same comprehension as writing about it, just through a different medium. Hold illustrated responses to the same interpretive standard.

misconceptions.title: Interaction Is Something You Do Before the Real Work
misconceptions.body: Students treat annotation as a pre-reading activity — a hoop to jump through before the essay, the discussion, the test. In practice, they annotate on a first read and never return to their marks. Teach them that the marks are a record of thinking in progress, not a warmup. The best writers and researchers return to their annotations the way builders return to blueprints.

assignments.name: Two-Column Notes + Reflection
assignments.body: Students read a challenging nonfiction passage and produce two-column notes (left column: what the text says; right column: what they think about what the text says). Then they write one paragraph explaining which note surprised them most and why.

assignments.name: Annotated Paragraph Exchange
assignments.body: Students annotate the same paragraph independently, then swap papers with a partner. Each student writes one sentence explaining where their partner's annotation revealed thinking they had missed. Discussion follows.

assignments.name: Freewrite-to-Argument
assignments.body: After reading a complex text, students freewrite for five uninterrupted minutes. They then highlight the one sentence in their freewriting that contains the seed of an arguable claim. That sentence becomes the thesis of a short analytical paragraph. The assignment makes freewriting purposeful and shows students that ideas emerge in the mess.

mastery: Produces annotations that include a mix of question marks, paraphrases, connections, and challenges — not just underlines or stars.
Freewriting is demonstrably about the text, not about personal reaction in the abstract; the text is the engine of the thinking, not just the occasion for it.
Notes capture ideas in the student's own language rather than transcribed sentences from the source.
Returns to prior annotations when writing or discussing, using them as evidence of their thinking process rather than ignoring them after the first pass.
Selects the interaction method (notes, annotation, freewrite, illustration) that fits the task and can explain why that method served their comprehension.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I 5(E) establishes the same list of interaction strategies (notetaking, annotating, freewriting, illustrating). English II holds and deepens the expectation — the TEA language is identical, which means mastery in English II is about sophistication and consistency of habit, not a new skill set. English III continues the same expectation; the progression is in the complexity of the sources, not the strategies themselves.

DOK 2 justification: The skill targets application of a practiced set of cognitive tools to new texts. A student executing genuine annotation or freewriting is operating at the Skill/Concept level — using a procedure in a context that requires understanding, not just recall.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog; STAAR does not test annotation or freewriting behavior directly. Question stems above mirror how STAAR would test the PRODUCTS of source interaction — inferences drawn from annotated passages or freewriting excerpts presented as reading comprehension items.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Respond Using Acquired Content and Academic Vocabulary
expectation: Respond using acquired content and academic vocabulary as appropriate.
overview: This TEK is about whether students can actually USE the words they've been taught — not just recognize them on a vocabulary quiz, but deploy them in written and oral responses with precision and naturalness. The real test is "appropriate": knowing when a term earns its place in a response and when it would be forced.
studentFriendly: You can use the precise vocabulary from what you've read and studied — terms from the text, the discipline, and the course — to say exactly what you mean when you respond to a prompt, a discussion, or a writing task.
questionStems: Which sentence from the student response MOST effectively uses content vocabulary to support the central claim?
The word [term] as used in paragraph X MOST nearly means —
Based on the passage, which of the following BEST explains how the author uses the term [term] to develop the argument?
Which revision of the underlined sentence BEST replaces the informal language with appropriate academic vocabulary?
In paragraph X, the use of the phrase [phrase] MOST likely signals —
Which sentence from the response BEST demonstrates the student's understanding of the term [concept]?
Read the following student response. Which word or phrase, if added to sentence X, would MOST improve the precision of the response?
The author's use of [discipline-specific term] in paragraph X contributes to the development of the central idea by —
Which of the following sentences from the response MOST clearly demonstrates appropriate use of academic register?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which inference about the author's use of [term] is BEST supported by the text?

misconceptions.title: "Using Big Words" Is the Same as Using Vocabulary Well
misconceptions.body: Students reach for impressive-sounding words regardless of fit — "utilize" for "use," "demonstrate" when they mean "show" — and call it academic vocabulary. Teach them that precision beats prestige every time. The question isn't "does this word sound academic?" but "does this word say exactly what I mean?" Watch for inflated diction that actually obscures the student's thinking rather than sharpening it.

misconceptions.title: Content Vocabulary Only Goes in the Body Paragraph
misconceptions.body: Students learn vocabulary in context — while reading the anchor text or during instruction — and then park it exclusively in the middle of an essay, treating it as a body-paragraph requirement. Push them to use content and academic terms in introductions, conclusions, and transitions too. Vocabulary mastery shows up in structure, not just in isolated evidence paragraphs.

misconceptions.title: Vocabulary Is Decoration, Not Load-Bearing
misconceptions.body: Students treat content terms as garnish — they drop in "juxtaposition" or "claim" or "synthesis" once to prove they know it, then proceed to explain the concept in vague everyday language anyway. Teach them that academic vocabulary is doing real argumentative work when it compresses a complex idea into a single precise term. If they can remove the term without losing any meaning, they haven't used it — they've only performed it.

misconceptions.title: "Appropriate" Means "Always Formal"
misconceptions.body: Students read "appropriate" as a command for maximum formality, then produce stilted, unreadable responses that sound assembled rather than written. The word "appropriate" is calibrated — some response tasks call for formal register, others for a more conversational academic voice. Watch for students who over-correct into robotic prose as a substitute for genuine clarity.

misconceptions.title: They Know the Word If They Can Define It
misconceptions.body: Students who can produce a textbook definition of "allusion" or "rhetoric" will still write responses that use those words loosely or incorrectly in context. Knowing a definition is not the same as acquiring a word. Push for use across multiple contexts and response types — summaries, arguments, analyses — before calling vocabulary acquired. If students only ever use a term in the type of task where they first encountered it, they haven't owned it yet.

assignments.name: Vocabulary in the Wild
assignments.body: Students take five content or academic vocabulary terms from the current unit and write a short analytical paragraph using each one in a way that carries genuine argumentative weight. Then they swap with a partner and mark every term that could be removed without losing meaning — those are the ones that need reworking.

assignments.name: Register Audit
assignments.body: Give students two versions of the same response to a text-based prompt — one written in informal language, one in precise academic vocabulary. Students annotate both: what does the vocabulary version gain? What does the informal version lose? Then they revise one of their own recent responses using the same lens.

assignments.name: Term Deployment Log
assignments.body: Throughout a unit, students maintain a running log tracking each content or academic vocabulary term they use in a written or oral response. For each entry they record the term, the sentence it appeared in, and whether it was doing real work or just filling space. Debrief at the end of the unit.

mastery: Selects content and academic vocabulary terms that sharpen meaning rather than signal effort — the word choice is precise, not decorative.
Uses discipline-specific and academic terms across multiple response types (argument, analysis, summary) without prompting.
Calibrates register to the task — appropriately formal in analytical writing, appropriately clear in explanatory writing — without defaulting to either extreme.
Can explain, if asked, why a specific vocabulary term appeared in a response and what argumentative or analytical work it is doing.
Sustains accurate vocabulary use throughout a full response, not just in the sentence immediately following a prompt.
notes: Vertical ramp: English I 5(F) establishes the expectation of responding with acquired vocabulary; English II holds the same language but applies it within a broader range of text types and genre complexity at the sophomore level, requiring consistent cross-context deployment. English III raises the stakes by anchoring vocabulary use inside increasingly complex rhetorical and argumentative responses.

DOK 3 justification: Students are not recalling definitions (DOK 1) or locating examples (DOK 2) — they are selecting and applying vocabulary strategically within a response, which requires reasoning about fit, precision, and register.

STAAR testing note: This TEK does not generate its own isolated STAAR question type, but it underlies virtually every written response task on STAAR. The question stems above mirror how vocabulary-in-context and response-quality questions surface across the reading and writing sections. Note this in classroom instruction: every STAAR extended response implicitly tests 5(F).
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Discuss and Write About Explicit or Implicit Meanings of Text
expectation: Discuss and write about the explicit or implicit meanings of text.
overview: The real work here is the gap between what the text says and what the text means — and demanding that students articulate the difference in writing, not just in their heads. Discussion without writing lets students skate on vague impressions; writing without discussion skips the interpretive wrestling that gets them to defensible claims.
studentFriendly: You can read a text, find what it says on the surface, dig into what it's really suggesting underneath, and explain both in writing with evidence.
questionStems: Which sentence from the selection BEST states an explicit idea the author presents?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, what can the reader MOST likely infer about [character/subject]?
The author's use of [word/phrase] in paragraph X suggests that —
Which statement BEST expresses an implicit meaning in the selection?
What conclusion can the reader draw from the information in paragraphs X–Y?
Which detail from the text MOST strongly supports the inference that [claim]?
The narrator's reaction in paragraph X implies that —
Based on the selection, what does the author suggest about [theme/idea] without stating it directly?
Which sentence from the selection states the author's meaning MOST explicitly?
The reader can infer from paragraphs X–Y that [character/subject] MOST likely —

misconceptions.title: The Quote IS the Analysis
misconceptions.body: Students believe that pulling a line from the text and writing "this shows that..." constitutes analysis. They've named the explicit detail but haven't moved to what it means — the inference step gets skipped entirely. Teach them that the evidence opens the door; they still have to walk through it and explain what's on the other side.

misconceptions.title: Implicit Means Hidden, So Anything Goes
misconceptions.body: When students realize implicit meaning isn't spelled out, they treat inference as a free pass for any interpretation that crosses their minds. Push them to test every inference against the text: if the passage doesn't contain the seeds of the interpretation, it's not implicit — it's invented. Inference is constrained, not liberated, by the text.

misconceptions.title: Explicit Means Important and Implicit Means Deep
misconceptions.body: Students rank implicit meaning as automatically more sophisticated than explicit. Watch for responses that dismiss stated ideas as "too obvious to analyze" while praising any inference as a sign of critical thinking. Explicit information still requires interpretive work — how it's framed, where it appears, what it does rhetorically. The level isn't in the type of meaning; it's in the quality of the reasoning applied to either.

misconceptions.title: Discussion and Writing Are Two Separate Tasks
misconceptions.body: Students treat discussion as the warm-up and writing as the graded thing they do after. They'll nail an insight in conversation and then write something flatter because they think the written response is supposed to sound different — more formal, less argumentative, more summarized. Teach them that the sharpest thing they said out loud is the thing they should put on the page first.

misconceptions.title: "The Text Is Saying..." Gets You Through
misconceptions.body: Students learn to open every analytical sentence with "the text is saying" or "the author is trying to say" as a hedge that lets them gesture at meaning without committing to a claim. Watch for this filler. Teach them to drop the throat-clearing and make the assertion: "The author implies that..." or "The passage establishes that..." — these constructions force a claim rather than a shrug.

assignments.name: Two-Column Reading Log
assignments.body: Students divide a journal page into two columns: "What the text says" (explicit) and "What the text means" (implicit). After reading a passage or chapter, they fill both columns with at least three entries each, then select their strongest implicit reading and develop it into a paragraph with textual evidence.

assignments.name: Surface to Depth Writing Prompt
assignments.body: Students receive a short passage (literary or informational) and a two-part prompt: first, identify and explain one explicit idea; second, identify and explain one implicit meaning. Both parts require direct textual evidence and a sentence explaining the connection. The point is to make the two moves visible as distinct skills before they're asked to blend them.

assignments.name: Discussion-to-Draft
assignments.body: Class discusses a text for 10–15 minutes, with the teacher tracking the most precise claims on the board. Students then write a short response using at least one idea from the discussion — named and attributed ("As we said in discussion, the passage implies..."). The goal is collapsing the gap between oral analysis and written argument.

assignments.name: Claim Quality Sort
assignments.body: Students receive 8–10 sample inference statements about a shared text, ranging from vague to unsupported to defensible. They sort them by quality and write a brief explanation of what makes the strongest ones work. This exposes the criteria for a valid implicit reading without lecturing about inference.

mastery: Distinguishes clearly in writing between what the text states and what it implies, without conflating the two or ranking one as more important than the other.
Supports every inference with at least one specific textual detail and explains the logical connection between the evidence and the conclusion.
Writes about both explicit and implicit meaning with the same argumentative commitment — no hedging, no throat-clearing, no "the author is trying to say."
Participates in discussion by building on others' readings rather than restating the surface of the text or asserting unsupported interpretations.
Revises initial readings when stronger textual evidence challenges them, rather than defending a first impression.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I Std 5G asks students to discuss and write about explicit or implicit meanings — the expectation is identical in language, but English II raises the ante through the complexity of texts assigned and the sophistication of response expected, not through a change in the verb. Teachers should resist treating this as review and instead push the texts harder.

Vertical ramp to English III: English III Std 5G continues the same expectation, again with text complexity and response depth as the differentiators. The skill is a through-line across all four courses; the work is in holding students to increasingly rigorous evidence and precision of claim.

DOK 3 justification: moving between explicit and implicit meaning within a single analytical response requires students to apply strategic thinking — selecting evidence, constructing an inference, and defending it in writing — rather than simply retrieving or identifying information.

No direct STAAR analog for "discuss" (oral component), but the written analysis of explicit and implicit meaning is among the most frequently tested skills across STAAR Reading; question stems here mirror released STAAR inference and conclusion items directly.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Respond with Appropriate Register, Vocabulary, Tone, and Voice
expectation: Respond orally or in writing with appropriate register, vocabulary, tone, and voice.
overview: This TEK is about code-switching in practice — not as a linguistic concept to define, but as a real-time decision students make every time they open their mouths or pick up a pen. The question isn't whether students can respond; it's whether they can read the room and calibrate accordingly.
studentFriendly: You can adjust how you talk and write — your word choice, your attitude, your level of formality — to fit the situation, whether you're responding to a text, a teacher, a debate judge, or a job application.
questionStems: Which response BEST demonstrates the appropriate register for the context described in the passage?
The student's word choice in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to convey —
Based on the passage, which revision would BEST adjust the tone of the response for a formal audience?
The shift in voice between paragraphs X and Y MOST likely suggests that the writer —
Which sentence from the response BEST supports the idea that the writer has matched vocabulary to purpose?
The tone of the response in paragraph X can BEST be described as —
Which revision to paragraph X would MOST likely make the response appropriate for an academic audience?
The writer's use of [word/phrase] in paragraph X is MOST nearly an example of —
Based on paragraphs X and Y, how does the writer's register contribute to the overall effectiveness of the response?
Which sentence would MOST appropriately replace the underlined sentence to maintain a consistent tone throughout the response?

misconceptions.title: Formal Means Complicated
misconceptions.body: Students equate academic register with long sentences and rare vocabulary — and produce responses that are stiff, bloated, and hard to follow. Teach them that formal register is about precision and distance, not complexity. A clear, direct sentence with accurate word choice is more formally effective than a tangled one stuffed with SAT words.

misconceptions.title: Register Is a Binary Switch
misconceptions.body: Students think there are only two modes: school voice and street voice. They miss the full spectrum — casual conversation, academic discussion, professional correspondence, public argument, intimate reflection. Push them to identify at least four distinct registers before asking them to use any of them. The skill is granular, not binary.

misconceptions.title: Tone and Mood Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students conflate the writer's attitude (tone) with the feeling the text creates (mood). In their responses, this shows up as inconsistency — their tone drifts because they don't know what they're managing. Teach them: tone is a choice the writer makes on purpose, and it has to hold across the whole response, not just in the opening paragraph.

misconceptions.title: Voice Means Personality, So Formal Writing Has None
misconceptions.body: Students believe that academic writing requires them to sand off all personality — and what they produce is flat, evasive, and generic. Real voice in formal writing is disciplined precision: a writer who knows exactly what they mean and says it without hedging. Show them models of writers who are both formal and distinctly present on the page.

misconceptions.title: They're Done Once They Have a Draft
misconceptions.body: Students treat register, tone, and voice as decisions made once at the start — then they forget to maintain them. Watch for responses that open in a strong formal register and collapse into casual phrasing by paragraph three. Teach revision as re-calibration: read back through and ask whether every sentence sounds like it belongs to the same writer, in the same room, for the same audience.

assignments.name: Same Message, Four Registers
assignments.body: Give students a single situation (a tardiness explanation, a request for an extension, an opinion about a school policy). They write four responses — one to a friend, one to a teacher, one to a principal, one to a scholarship committee. Discuss: what changed? What stayed?

assignments.name: Register Autopsy
assignments.body: Provide two student-written responses to the same prompt — one with mismatched register, one calibrated well. Students annotate both: mark every word, sentence, or phrase that signals register, and write one paragraph diagnosing what each writer got right or wrong.

assignments.name: Tone Maintenance Check
assignments.body: Students draft a response to a literary prompt. In revision, they highlight every sentence and rate it on a 1–5 tone scale. Any sentence that breaks pattern gets revised. Final submission includes the annotated draft and the clean revision.

assignments.name: Live Oral Register Switch
assignments.body: A classroom prompt is posed (e.g., why should we read this novel?). Students respond first in casual oral register, then in formal academic oral register. Peers score both on a four-point rubric: register, vocabulary, tone, voice.

mastery: Identifies the register, tone, and audience expectations of a given context before responding — not after.
Sustains a consistent tone and register across the full length of a response, oral or written, without drift.
Selects vocabulary that fits both the formality level and the purpose of the response, not just one or the other.
Distinguishes between their own voice and the demands of the context — and adjusts without erasing themselves entirely.
Revises drafts specifically for register and tone, not just for grammar and content.
notes: Vertical ramp: English I 5H asks students to respond with register and tone; English II maintains that expectation and adds the explicit pairing of oral and written modalities — the skill is now required to travel across contexts, not just satisfy one. English III sharpens the expectation further toward rhetorical control and audience analysis.

DOK 3 justification: selecting and sustaining appropriate register, vocabulary, tone, and voice requires students to apply abstract knowledge about language to a specific context and then monitor that application across the full response — strategic rather than procedural.

This TEK has no direct STAAR passage-response analog in the sense of a standalone item type, but register and tone appear in the editing and revision tasks on STAAR (Revising and Editing passages). Stems above are written to mirror those task types.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Reflect on and Adjust Responses When Valid Evidence Warrants
expectation: Reflect on and adjust responses when valid evidence warrants.
overview: This TEK targets intellectual honesty in motion — students who change their minds only when the evidence actually earns it. It's about calibrated revision: neither refusing to budge nor caving to the loudest voice in the room.
studentFriendly: You can read new evidence, decide whether it actually changes your thinking, and update your response in a principled way — not because someone pushed back, but because the evidence genuinely warranted it.
questionStems: Which piece of evidence from the selection would MOST likely cause a reader to revise an initial interpretation of the narrator's motive?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which detail BEST supports a reader changing their conclusion about the author's central claim?
After reading paragraph X, a student who initially concluded that [claim] should MOST likely —
Which sentence from the selection provides the STRONGEST reason to reconsider the inference that —
A reader who initially agreed with [position] would MOST likely adjust that response after reading —
Which evidence from the text BEST demonstrates that the author's position shifted from paragraph X to paragraph Y?
A student argues that [initial claim]. Which detail from the selection MOST weakens that student's position?
Based on the selection, which conclusion BEST reflects an accurate revision of the claim that [initial claim]?
The author includes the information in paragraph X MOST likely to —
Which sentence from paragraphs X–Y would MOST likely prompt a careful reader to revise their interpretation of [character/event/claim]?

misconceptions.title: Changing Your Mind Means You Were Wrong the First Time
misconceptions.body: Students treat revision as failure — if they update their interpretation, they think it means their original reading was stupid. Teach them that revision is the goal, not an embarrassment. Show them explicitly: if new evidence couldn't change anyone's interpretation, the evidence wouldn't matter. A reading that never updates isn't a strong reading; it's a stubborn one.

misconceptions.title: Any Pushback Warrants a Response Change
misconceptions.body: Some students swing the opposite direction: the moment a peer or teacher questions them, they abandon their position wholesale. This is social capitulation, not intellectual revision. Push them to ask: did someone give me NEW evidence, or just express disagreement louder? Teach them to name what specifically in the evidence warranted the change — if they can't name it, they haven't actually revised; they've just retreated.

misconceptions.title: "Reflecting" Means Saying You Changed Your Mind
misconceptions.body: Students learn to perform reflection without doing it. They write "upon reflection, I now believe..." and then produce a barely modified version of their original claim with no evidence driving the change. Watch for revision statements that lack a specific textual anchor. The reflection must be traceable — something in the text moved them, and they must name it.

misconceptions.title: Valid Evidence and Convincing Evidence Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students confuse evidence that sounds persuasive with evidence that actually holds up. An emotional anecdote might feel convincing but carry little logical weight; a statistical comparison might be dull but decisive. Teach them to distinguish between "this moved me" and "this is accurate, relevant, and sufficient." The TEK says *valid* evidence — that word is doing real work.

misconceptions.title: Adjustment Means Complete Reversal
misconceptions.body: Students assume that if evidence warrants a response change, they must abandon their whole position. Teach them that adjustment can be partial — narrowing a claim, adding a qualification, revising a conclusion about one character while holding their reading of another. Real intellectual adjustment is granular. Watch for students who either dig in completely or flip entirely, with nothing in between.

assignments.name: Evidence Audit
assignments.body: After a Socratic seminar or fishbowl discussion, students review their written pre-discussion response. They must identify at least one specific claim they're revising and one they're holding, with textual evidence justifying each decision. No revision allowed without naming the evidence that earned it.

assignments.name: Red/Green Revision Protocol
assignments.body: Students write an initial claim in response to a passage. They then receive two or three contrasting excerpts. Using two colors, they mark claims they're keeping (green) and claims the new evidence forces them to revise (red). They write a final response that traces the revision logic sentence by sentence.

assignments.name: The Warranted vs. Unwarranted Sort
assignments.body: Give students a list of response changes from fictional student essays. For each, they determine: was this revision warranted by valid evidence, or was it social/emotional capitulation? They argue their sorting in writing, citing what evidence (or absence of evidence) drove their judgment.

mastery: Identifies the specific piece of evidence that warranted a response change and can articulate why that evidence is valid, not merely persuasive.
Distinguishes between adjusting a response because of logical evidence and adjusting because of social pressure or repetition.
Produces revised claims that are traceable — the revision is proportional to the evidence, neither ignored nor overcorrected.
Can defend a position they did NOT revise, naming the evidence that failed to meet the threshold for valid warrant.
Revises at the right level of granularity — qualifying a claim, narrowing a conclusion, or updating one inference while holding others — rather than wholesale flipping or blanket refusal.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: Std 5I in English I asks students to reflect and adjust responses when evidence warrants — the language is identical. English II students are expected to carry greater independence in evaluating whether evidence is *valid*, not just present, as they encounter more complex and competing texts across genres.

Vertical ramp to English III: English III increases the sophistication of the texts and the precision of the reasoning expected; the standard continues using identical language across the vertical, making execution-level rigor the differentiator.

DOK 3 justification: the skill requires students to evaluate evidence against an existing claim, reason about its validity, and decide whether revision is logically warranted — this is strategic thinking, not recall or application.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is rarely tested as a standalone item. STAAR tests its components — evaluating evidence, drawing conclusions, revising inferences — through comprehension and author's craft questions. Stems above mirror those testing contexts. Note this in instruction: the TEK describes a *habit of mind* that STAAR assumes throughout, even when it doesn't name it.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Defend or Challenge Authors' Claims Using Relevant Text Evidence
expectation: Defend or challenge the authors' claims using relevant text evidence.
overview: This TEK is where response skills get teeth. Students aren't just summarizing or identifying what an author argues — they're taking a position on whether that argument holds up, and they have to prove it with the text itself. The skill demands that students move between their own judgment and the author's words without losing either.
studentFriendly: You can read what an author claims, decide whether you agree or disagree with their argument, and use specific evidence from the text to back your position — not just your opinion, but proof pulled from the page.
questionStems: Which piece of evidence from the selection BEST supports the author's central claim?
A student claims that the author's argument in paragraph X is not fully supported. Which sentence from the selection BEST defends this challenge?
Which statement would MOST likely challenge the author's claim in paragraphs X–Y?
Based on the selection, which sentence from paragraph X BEST supports the inference that the author's claim is well-reasoned?
Which of the following pieces of evidence from the selection MOST effectively supports the author's position?
The evidence in paragraph X MOST likely weakens the author's claim because —
Which detail from the selection could BEST be used to challenge the author's conclusion in paragraph X?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which statement BEST describes a limitation in the author's supporting evidence?
Which sentence from the selection MOST clearly demonstrates that the author's claim depends on an unstated assumption?
A reader argues that the evidence in paragraph X does not support the author's claim. Which statement from the selection MOST directly contradicts this challenge?

misconceptions.title: Defending Means Agreeing, Challenging Means Disagreeing
misconceptions.body: Students collapse this TEK into opinion writing — they defend the author when they liked the piece and challenge when they didn't. Teach them that defense and challenge are analytical stances, not emotional ones. A student can personally disagree with a claim and still defend it if the text evidence holds up. That reversal is exactly where real argument lives.

misconceptions.title: Any Quote Is "Relevant" Evidence
misconceptions.body: Students treat quotation as automatic proof. They paste in a line from the text and consider the job done, without explaining how the evidence connects to the claim being evaluated. Push them to articulate the logical link: what does this evidence actually show, and why does it defend or challenge THIS claim rather than some other one?

misconceptions.title: Challenging an Author Means Disproving Everything
misconceptions.body: When students go on the attack, they often aim to tear down the entire argument when one weak support beam would do. Show them that the sharpest challenge is precise and narrow — this specific piece of evidence doesn't hold up, or this claim is stronger than the evidence the author provides. Wholesale dismissal is a weaker move than a targeted, evidence-based challenge.

misconceptions.title: Paraphrase Counts as Text Evidence
misconceptions.body: Students often restate the author's point in their own words and treat it as if they've quoted evidence. Watch for responses that never actually touch the text — they're describing the argument, not proving something with it. Require direct citation, not paraphrase, as the minimum entry for "relevant text evidence."

misconceptions.title: You Can Only Defend or Challenge — Not Both
misconceptions.body: Students assume the task forces a binary, so they commit to one side and ignore complicating evidence on the other. Teach them that a sophisticated response names the strongest evidence FOR the claim and the most significant gap in that evidence, then decides which side wins. Acknowledging the other side before dismissing it is what makes a defense or challenge credible.

assignments.name: Verdict Paragraph
assignments.body: Students read a short argumentative or informational text, then write a single focused paragraph that either defends or challenges the author's central claim. The paragraph must open with a clear position, cite at least two pieces of text evidence with page or paragraph references, and end with a sentence explaining why the evidence is or isn't sufficient. No summaries allowed as opening sentences.

assignments.name: Steel-Man/Straw-Man Swap
assignments.body: Students write two short responses to the same text — one that makes the strongest possible case FOR the author's claim using only text evidence, and one that makes the strongest possible case AGAINST it using only text evidence. After completing both, they annotate which response was harder to write and why.

assignments.name: Evidence Sorting Court
assignments.body: Provide students with a list of 10–12 sentences drawn from a source text. Students sort the sentences into three piles: evidence that defends the claim, evidence that challenges the claim, and evidence that is irrelevant to the claim. Discuss the contested middle pile as a class — that's where the real thinking happens.

assignments.name: Peer Challenge Draft
assignments.body: Students write a one-page defense of an author's claim. They then swap with a partner, whose job is to find the single weakest piece of evidence cited and write two sentences explaining why it doesn't do the work the defender claims. Original writers respond in one paragraph. Grade the exchange, not just the original draft.

mastery: States a clear position — defend or challenge — without hedging into summary or vague agreement.
Selects evidence that is directly relevant to the specific claim being evaluated, not to the text generally.
Explains the logical connection between cited evidence and their position, rather than assuming the evidence speaks for itself.
Acknowledges the strongest counterevidence before dismissing or outweighing it.
Distinguishes between evidence that is present in the text and evidence that is sufficient to prove the author's claim — recognizes that the two are not always the same.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I 5J requires students to defend or challenge authors' claims — the expectation is parallel in language but English II students are expected to work across more complex texts and more nuanced argument structures, with greater precision in identifying what counts as "relevant" evidence and why.

Vertical ramp to English III: English III 5J extends this skill by requiring students to evaluate the credibility of evidence and the logic of the argument's structure, not just its presence in the text.
DOK 3 justification: students must reason across the text to evaluate the sufficiency of evidence relative to a claim — that's strategic thinking, not retrieval or simple application.

STAAR analog: this TEK maps directly to STAAR questions that ask students to identify evidence supporting or undermining an author's claim, evaluate logical sufficiency, and select the BEST supporting or challenging detail from a set of options.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze How Themes Are Developed Through Characterization and Plot
expectation: Analyze how themes are developed through characterization and plot, including comparing similar themes in a variety of literary texts representing different cultures.
overview: This standard is about mechanism — not just naming a theme, but showing how the machinery of character and plot produces it. The cross-cultural comparison requirement pushes students past surface pattern-matching into genuine inquiry about why human beings across different contexts keep telling versions of the same story.
studentFriendly: You can read two stories from completely different parts of the world, trace how each one builds its central idea through what characters want, what they do, and what happens to them — and then explain what the comparison reveals that neither story could show you alone.
questionStems: Which statement BEST expresses a theme developed in both selections?
How does the author MOST likely use the character's actions in paragraphs X–Y to develop the theme?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the conflict between [character A] and [character B] MOST likely contributes to the theme by —
Which detail from the selection BEST supports the inference that the author's theme concerns —
How does the plot event in paragraph X develop the central theme of the selection?
The characterization of [character] in paragraphs X–Y MOST nearly suggests that the author's theme is —
In both selections, how do the authors use similar plot structures to develop a shared theme?
Which sentence from Selection 1 MOST closely parallels the theme developed in Selection 2?
Based on both selections, with which statement about [shared theme] would BOTH authors MOST likely agree?
How does [character]'s response to conflict in paragraph X advance the theme established in paragraph Y?

misconceptions.title: Theme Is the Topic, Not the Insight
misconceptions.body: Students write "the theme is loyalty" and consider the job done. Teach them that a topic is a noun — loyalty, loss, identity — and a theme is a complete claim that the text is making about that topic. Push them to finish the sentence: loyalty does what, to whom, under what conditions? If the theme statement could apply to a Wikipedia article, it isn't a theme yet.

misconceptions.title: Characters Exist Separately from Theme
misconceptions.body: Students analyze character and theme in parallel columns, never connecting the two. They'll describe a character's personality in one paragraph and state the theme in another, with no bridge. Watch for papers where "characterization" and "theme" appear in separate sections that never touch. Teach them that characterization IS the delivery vehicle — a character's desire, flaw, or transformation is how the author manufactures the theme in the reader's mind.

misconceptions.title: Comparing Cultures Means Listing Similarities
misconceptions.body: When students compare texts from different cultures, they produce side-by-side summaries: "In the Japanese story, the character does X. In the Mexican story, the character does Y. Both are about family." That's not comparison — that's inventory. The move they need to make is asking: if the same theme emerges from completely different cultural contexts, what does that tell you about the theme itself? What does the difference in how it's developed reveal?

misconceptions.title: The Protagonist Always Carries the Theme
misconceptions.body: Students go to the main character first, every time, as the sole source of thematic evidence. Secondary characters, antagonists, and minor figures often carry the author's counter-argument, the complication, or the resolution of the theme. Teach them to treat antagonists and foils as thematic instruments, not obstacles — what the antagonist wants is often the version of the theme the author is testing or refuting.

misconceptions.title: Plot Events Are Evidence; Plot Summary Is Not
misconceptions.body: Students cite the plot as proof of theme by summarizing it: "In the end, the character loses everything, so the theme is that life is unfair." That's still summary. The analytical move is explaining WHY that plot event — that specific loss, at that specific moment, with those specific consequences — produces the thematic meaning. Cause and effect inside the narrative is not the same as cause and effect on the reader's understanding. Teach them to name the mechanism, not just the outcome.

assignments.name: Theme Autopsy
assignments.body: Students read a short story and reverse-engineer the theme by mapping three specific character decisions and three specific plot turns onto a single thematic claim. Each connection requires a cited passage and a one-sentence explanation of the mechanism. No bullet-point summaries — every entry must explain how, not just what.

assignments.name: Cross-Cultural Theme Pairing
assignments.body: Students read two short texts from different cultural traditions that share a broad thematic territory (e.g., a West African folktale and a Latin American short story, both concerning obligation to community). They produce a written comparison of at least two pages: one section on how each text develops the theme through characterization, one section on plot, and one section that explains what the comparison reveals that neither text shows alone.

assignments.name: Character as Theme Machine
assignments.body: Students choose one character from a novel or story currently in class and write a character study focused entirely on thematic function: what desire does this character have, how does the plot test it, and what does the result assert? The paper cannot summarize — every sentence must be making an argument about theme.

assignments.name: Thematic Debate
assignments.body: Two students take opposing interpretations of a shared text's theme and debate the claim in front of the class, citing specific characterization and plot as evidence. The teacher scores on specificity of evidence, not on who "wins."

mastery: Articulates a theme as a complete claim — not a topic word — and anchors it in specific textual evidence.
Traces a direct line from at least two character decisions and two plot events to the thematic claim, explaining the mechanism not just the outcome.
Compares similar themes across culturally distinct texts and identifies what the comparison reveals beyond what either text shows independently.
Uses secondary characters, antagonists, and foils as thematic evidence, not only protagonists.
Distinguishes between plot summary and thematic analysis in both reading and writing.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I 6A asks students to analyze theme development through characterization and plot without the cross-cultural comparison requirement. English II adds the comparative dimension — students must hold two culturally distinct texts simultaneously and produce insight from the gap between them. English III will push this further toward author's craft and rhetorical purpose.

DOK 3 justification: students must apply thematic analysis across multiple texts and strategic thinking about cultural context — this exceeds recall and skill application (DOK 1–2) but stops short of the extended self-directed investigation required for DOK 4.

The cross-cultural comparison clause is frequently undertaught. It is not an optional enrichment task — it is in the standard. Select text pairings intentionally; superficial pairings ("both have a main character who grows up") produce surface comparisons. The texts need genuine thematic tension between them.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze How Authors Develop Complex Characters Through Setting and Events
expectation: Analyze how authors develop complex yet believable characters, including archetypes, through historical and cultural settings and events.
overview: This standard asks students to read character not in isolation but as a product of world — the historical moment, the cultural pressures, the specific events that shape who a person becomes on the page. The added wrinkle of archetypes means students must hold two lenses at once: recognizing the universal type AND tracking how the text complicates or subverts it.
studentFriendly: You can explain how a character's specific time period, culture, and experiences make them feel like a real, complicated person rather than just a type you've seen before.
questionStems: In paragraphs X–Y, the historical setting MOST likely influences the character's decision to —
Which detail from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that the character functions as an archetypal figure?
How does the cultural setting described in paragraph X contribute to the development of the character's internal conflict?
The author MOST likely includes the event in paragraph X to show that the character —
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates how the character both fits and departs from an archetypal role?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the reader can infer that the historical events described cause the character to —
How does the author use the setting in paragraphs X–Z to make the character's motivation more believable?
The character's response to the cultural expectations described in paragraph X MOST likely suggests that —
Which statement BEST describes how the author develops the character's complexity across the selection?
Based on the selection, with which statement about the relationship between setting and character would the author MOST likely agree?

misconceptions.title: Archetypes Are a Checklist, Not a Lens
misconceptions.body: Students identify "the hero" or "the outcast" and stop there, treating the archetype label as the endpoint of analysis. Teach them that naming the archetype is the starting gun, not the finish line — the real move is explaining what the text does to stretch, complicate, or destabilize that type. Watch for papers that catalog archetypes without ever touching the word "however."

misconceptions.title: Setting Is Background, Not Pressure
misconceptions.body: Students treat historical and cultural setting as scenery — a date and place that situates the story without doing anything to it. Push them to read setting as force: What does it demand of the character? What does it deny them? What does it make possible that wouldn't exist in a different time or place? A character's complexity is often the gap between what the setting requires and what the person actually wants.

misconceptions.title: Complex Means Contradictory, Not Confused
misconceptions.body: When students encounter a character who behaves inconsistently, they often conclude the author made a mistake or the character "doesn't make sense." Reframe this: contradiction IS the evidence of complexity. A character who holds competing loyalties, fears, or values without resolving them cleanly is exactly what believability looks like. Teach them to name the tension rather than explain it away.

misconceptions.title: Historical Setting Means Historical Fiction Only
misconceptions.body: Students apply this standard narrowly, thinking it only matters for novels set in the past. But every text has a cultural moment — a contemporary story still has characters shaped by gender norms, class expectations, community pressures. Push them to name the cultural forces operating on characters in any text, not just ones with a year in the first paragraph.

misconceptions.title: Believability Is About Likeability
misconceptions.body: Students conflate a believable character with one they root for. Teach them that believability is about internal logic and earned response to circumstance — a villain can be completely believable, a hero can be unbelievable. If the historical or cultural forces in the text would reasonably produce this behavior, the character is working, regardless of whether the reader approves.

assignments.name: Character Excavation
assignments.body: Students select a major character from the current text and write a two-column analysis: on one side, the archetype the character resembles; on the other, the specific historical or cultural details that complicate or deepen that type. Final paragraph synthesizes what the gap between columns reveals about the author's intent.

assignments.name: Setting-as-Pressure Map
assignments.body: Students annotate three to five passages where setting (historical event, cultural norm, specific place) directly produces a character choice or reaction. For each, they write a sentence explaining what the character would have done differently in a different world — and what that difference reveals about who they are.

assignments.name: Same Archetype, Different World
assignments.body: Pairs read two short texts featuring the same archetype (e.g., the outcast, the trickster, the reluctant hero) in different historical or cultural settings. They write a comparative paragraph explaining how setting produces two different versions of the same type, and which feels more believable and why.

mastery: Identifies an archetype operating in a text AND names specific ways the character departs from or complicates that type.
Traces at least two direct lines of causation between historical or cultural details and specific character behaviors or decisions.
Distinguishes between surface character traits and the deeper forces (setting, events) that produce those traits.
Uses textual evidence to support claims about believability — not "I related to them" but "the text earns this response through X."
Sustains analysis across a full text rather than focusing on a single moment or scene.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I 6B asks students to analyze how characters develop and respond to challenges — the frame is primarily internal (motivation, response). English II adds the historical and cultural setting as a shaping force AND introduces archetypes as an analytical lens, requiring students to hold universal patterns and specific textual particulars simultaneously. English III continues this trajectory by adding complexity of perspective across multiple texts.

DOK 3 justification: Students must use knowledge of archetypes and historical context as analytical tools to reason about character, then defend conclusions with textual evidence — strategic thinking rather than retrieval or skill application alone.
This standard pairs naturally with 8A and 8D when examining how an author's language choices construct not just what characters say but who they are within their world.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Isolated Scenes and Their Contribution to Plot
expectation: Analyze isolated scenes and their contribution to the success of the plot as a whole.
overview: This TEK asks students to zoom into a single scene and then zoom back out — connecting what happens locally to what the entire narrative is doing. It's a double move: close reading plus structural thinking. The trap is that students analyze scenes in isolation without ever asking what would break if the scene were cut.
studentFriendly: You can read one scene closely — what happens, how it's built, what it reveals — and then explain exactly why the story wouldn't work without it.
questionStems: Read paragraphs X–Y. How does this scene MOST likely contribute to the development of the plot?
Which detail from the scene in paragraphs X–Y BEST supports the conclusion that it creates a turning point in the narrative?
The events in paragraphs X–Y are MOST likely included to —
Based on the scene in paragraphs X–Y, what can the reader infer about how this moment affects later events in the plot?
Which sentence from paragraphs X–Y BEST illustrates the scene's function in the overall structure of the story?
The author's decision to include the scene in paragraphs X–Y MOST likely serves to —
How does the scene in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the resolution of the central conflict?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which statement BEST describes how this scene shifts the direction of the plot?
The scene in paragraphs X–Y is MOST nearly an example of —
Which evidence from the passage BEST supports the inference that the scene in paragraphs X–Y is essential to the plot's success?

misconceptions.title: A Good Scene Analysis Is Just a Summary
misconceptions.body: Students describe what happens in the scene and call it analysis. Teach them the scene-to-whole question: "What would the story lose if this scene were removed?" That question forces them off plot summary and into structural reasoning. If they can't answer it, they haven't analyzed anything — they've retold it.

misconceptions.title: Every Scene Is Equally Important
misconceptions.body: Students treat all scenes as having equal weight, assigning vague importance ("this scene shows character") without differentiation. Push them to rank: is this scene the hinge the whole plot swings on, or is it one brick in a longer build? That discrimination — not just "it matters" but "it matters THIS WAY" — is the skill STAAR is after.

misconceptions.title: "Isolated" Means Unconnected
misconceptions.body: Students read "isolated scene" as permission to ignore the surrounding text. The word is an instruction about method (zoom in here), not a claim that the scene is disconnected. Watch for students who refuse to cite earlier or later passages as evidence when explaining a scene's contribution. The isolation is analytic, not textual.

misconceptions.title: Contribution Equals Theme
misconceptions.body: Students conflate a scene's contribution to the plot with its thematic meaning — "this scene shows that family matters." Contribution to plot means something structural: it introduces conflict, raises stakes, reveals a secret, produces a consequence. Teach them to separate what the scene MEANS from what it DOES to the narrative machinery.

misconceptions.title: The Climax Is the Only Scene That Matters
misconceptions.body: When asked about plot contribution, students default to the climax as the only scene worth studying. Show them that exposition scenes plant the stakes, mid-story scenes shift allegiances, and falling-action scenes reframe what the climax meant. A scene doesn't have to be the loudest moment to be the most necessary one.

assignments.name: Cut It and Break It
assignments.body: Students select a scene the teacher designates, write one paragraph explaining what the scene does in the plot, and then write a second paragraph arguing what would collapse in the narrative if the scene were removed. The second paragraph must cite specific later events as evidence.

assignments.name: Scene Dissection Chart
assignments.body: Students complete a two-column chart: left column identifies the internal components of the scene (conflict, revelation, shift in relationship, etc.); right column maps each component to a specific later plot consequence. Chart must be evidence-based with line or paragraph citations.

assignments.name: Director's Commentary
assignments.body: Students write a 1-page "director's commentary" in the voice of the author explaining why a specific scene was kept in the final draft — what it accomplishes that no other scene does and where exactly in the plot arc it does its work.

assignments.name: Scene Swap
assignments.body: Students rewrite the scene they analyzed with one key element changed (a character doesn't show up; a revelation is withheld; the setting shifts). They then write a paragraph explaining how that one change would alter the plot trajectory. Makes the original scene's contribution visible by contrast.

mastery: Identifies the specific structural function of a scene (introduces conflict, raises stakes, produces a consequence, shifts a relationship) rather than summarizing its events.
Connects scene-level details to specific plot moments that occur before and after, using textual evidence from both.
Distinguishes between a scene's thematic meaning and its plot contribution, articulating both without collapsing one into the other.
Evaluates the scene's placement in the narrative arc — explains why it lands where it does and what would shift if it were repositioned or removed.
Sustains close reading of the scene itself without losing sight of the larger structural argument the analysis is building.
notes: Vertical ramp: English I 6C asks students to analyze the plot and how conflicts are introduced and resolved. English II lifts that into scene-level precision — not just "what happens" but "what does this discrete scene do to the whole." English III 6C pushes further toward evaluating how structural choices produce meaning across complex texts.

DOK 3 justification: students must use analytical and abstract reasoning to connect a local scene to global plot function — not retrieving a fact, not summarizing, but constructing a structural argument with evidence.

This TEK has a clear STAAR analog: released STAAR items regularly ask students to identify why a scene or passage is included and what it contributes to plot, conflict, or character — often framed as "MOST likely serves to" or "how does this scene contribute to."
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze How Historical and Cultural Settings Influence Characterization, Plot, and Theme
expectation: Analyze how historical and cultural settings influence characterization, plot, and theme across texts.
overview: This is the TEK where setting stops being backdrop and starts being engine. Students have to read the world a character lives in — its laws, its hierarchies, its silences — and trace how that world shapes who the character becomes, what conflicts drive the plot, and what ideas the text is finally reaching toward. The "across texts" phrase matters: comparison is required, not optional.
studentFriendly: You can read two texts from different times and places and explain how each character's world — its history, its culture, its rules — shapes what that character wants, what stands in their way, and what the story is ultimately saying.
questionStems: Based on paragraphs X–Y, how does the historical setting MOST directly affect the choices the main character makes?
Which detail from the text BEST supports the inference that the cultural setting limits the character's options?
The conflict in paragraphs X–Y develops MOST directly from —
Which statement BEST describes how the setting in [Text 1] and [Text 2] shapes the theme in both texts?
In paragraph X, the character's response to [event] MOST likely reflects —
The author uses the details in paragraph X to suggest that the historical period MOST influences the character by —
Which sentence from the text BEST illustrates how cultural expectations shape the character's behavior?
How does the setting in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the development of the central conflict?
Based on both texts, with which statement about the relationship between setting and theme would both authors MOST likely agree?
The theme of [Text] is MOST directly shaped by the historical context because —

misconceptions.title: Setting Is Just the Time and Place Label
misconceptions.body: Students write "the story takes place in the 1930s South" and consider their context work done. Teach them that historical and cultural setting is not the label — it's the pressure. The 1930s South means Jim Crow, the Depression, rigid gender roles, sharecropping. Every one of those forces has to show up in what characters can do, fear, want, and become. If the setting label isn't connected to a specific force bearing down on a character, they haven't analyzed anything.

misconceptions.title: Characters Would Act the Same Anywhere
misconceptions.body: Students sometimes argue that a character's choices reflect "human nature," as if the same person would make the same decisions in any era or culture. Push them: would the character's options even exist in a different historical moment? Would the conflict exist? The goal is for students to see that character and culture are co-constructed — you cannot fully separate who someone is from the world that shaped them.

misconceptions.title: Theme Is Universal, So Context Doesn't Matter
misconceptions.body: Students learn to state themes as universal claims ("love conquers all," "power corrupts") and then stop there, as if the historical setting is just decoration. Reframe this: a theme is the author's response to a specific cultural moment. The same idea lands differently in a text set during wartime than in peacetime. Teach them to ask what the setting makes the theme mean — not just what the theme is.

misconceptions.title: "Across Texts" Means Summary Plus Summary
misconceptions.body: When asked to compare, students often produce two separate paragraphs of plot summary and call it analysis. The comparison is not additive — it's relational. They need to name what each setting does to characterization and theme, then identify what the juxtaposition reveals that neither text would show alone. Teach them to find the conversation between the texts, not just describe each one.

misconceptions.title: The Author's Own Culture Is Irrelevant
misconceptions.body: Students sometimes treat the historical setting as purely internal to the fictional world, forgetting that the author also inhabits a cultural moment that shapes what gets included, emphasized, or silenced. Watch for students who ignore author context entirely — the biography section at the top of a test passage is there for a reason. Push them to ask: what does the author's position in history make them able to see or unable to see?

assignments.name: Setting Dissection
assignments.body: Students read two texts with contrasting historical and cultural settings. For each, they map three forces the setting creates (legal, social, economic, religious, etc.) and identify one moment in the text where each force directly shapes a character decision, a plot turn, or a thematic statement.

assignments.name: Same Story, Different World
assignments.body: Students rewrite one scene from a historically-set text, transplanting it to a different era or culture. They then write a paragraph explaining what changed — not just in plot mechanics, but in what the character could want, fear, or become. The transplant reveals how load-bearing the original setting was.

assignments.name: Cross-Text Theme Comparison
assignments.body: Two texts, one theme. Students write an essay arguing how historical and cultural setting causes each author's treatment of the shared theme to differ. The thesis must name what the settings make the themes mean differently — not just that the texts share a topic.

assignments.name: Cultural Pressure Points
assignments.body: Students annotate a text, flagging every moment where a character's choice is constrained or enabled by a specific historical or cultural condition. After annotation, they write a paragraph arguing which pressure point is most determinative for the story's outcome.

mastery: Names specific historical and cultural forces — not just era labels — and connects each force to a concrete moment of characterization, plot development, or thematic statement.
Explains how character options, conflicts, and resolution are enabled or foreclosed by setting, not just influenced by it in a vague sense.
Compares how two texts' settings produce different treatments of a shared theme, with evidence from both texts.
Moves beyond summary when discussing setting, treating historical and cultural context as active pressure on every element of the narrative.
Sustains analysis across both texts without collapsing into parallel summaries — the cross-text argument is relational, not additive.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I Std 6(D) asks students to analyze how setting shapes character and plot; English II extends that skill to *across texts* (comparative) and adds theme as an explicit target. The move is from single-text analysis to cross-text synthesis, which raises the cognitive demand substantially.

Vertical ramp to English III: English III Std 6(D) adds complexity by requiring analysis of how setting shapes universal themes and the author's philosophical assumptions — the historical and cultural lens goes deeper into ideology and worldview.

DOK 3 justification: comparing how setting influences character, plot, and theme across multiple texts requires strategic thinking and evidence-based generalization, not just retrieval or application of a single procedure.
This TEK is a natural pairing with informational research on historical context (Std 11) — students who can read primary sources about an era come to literary analysis with richer raw material.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Read and Analyze World Literature Across Literary Periods
expectation: Read and analyze world literature across literary periods.
overview: This TEK is the broadest mandate in the English II curriculum — students must read texts that span cultures, centuries, and traditions, and analyze those texts with awareness of when and where they emerged. The skill is historical AND literary simultaneously: understanding what a text means requires knowing what world it came from.
studentFriendly: You can read a poem, story, or speech from another part of the world or another century and explain what it means, where it fits in history, and why it matters — not just whether you liked it.
questionStems: Based on the historical context described in the introduction to the selection, which statement BEST explains why the author's perspective in paragraph X would have been considered radical at the time?
Which detail from the selection MOST clearly reflects the cultural values of the literary period in which it was written?
The author's use of [literary element] in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Read the following lines from the selection. Based on these lines, what can the reader MOST likely infer about the narrator's relationship to the society described?
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that the text reflects a [specific period, e.g., Romantic / Enlightenment / postcolonial] worldview?
How does the setting described in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the central conflict of the selection?
The word "[word]" as used in paragraph X MOST nearly means —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, with which statement would the author MOST likely agree?
Which detail from the selection BEST develops the theme that —
The shift in tone between paragraph X and paragraph Y suggests that the narrator MOST likely —

misconceptions.title: "World Literature" Means Everything Except American or British
misconceptions.body: Students — and sometimes syllabi — treat world literature as a catch-all for texts that aren't canonical American or British works. Teach them that world literature is a category defined by reach and cultural dialogue, not by subtraction. Chaucer and Whitman can be world literature. A Yoruba oral poem and a Brazilian short story absolutely are. The frame is global conversation, not geographic exclusion.

misconceptions.title: Literary Periods Are Universal Timelines
misconceptions.body: Students assume that "Romanticism" or "the Enlightenment" happened everywhere at the same time in the same way. They export European period labels onto texts that don't fit them. Push them to ask: what was happening in THIS region during THIS century? A text from nineteenth-century West Africa was not produced in the Romantic tradition — it was produced in a context of colonialism, oral tradition, and political upheaval. Teach them period labels as local phenomena, not global clocks.

misconceptions.title: Analyzing World Literature Means Explaining What's Different
misconceptions.body: Students default to contrast: "In this culture, they believe X, which is different from what we believe." That's a start, not a finish. The richer analysis asks what the text does — how it builds meaning, what it argues, how its form serves its purpose. Watch for students who spend their entire analysis describing cultural difference without ever engaging with the text as a literary object.

misconceptions.title: Historical Context Is Background, Not Analysis
misconceptions.body: Students treat historical context as the paragraph before the "real" analysis — a box to check before getting to themes and imagery. Teach them that context IS analysis: knowing that a text was written under censorship, during famine, in the shadow of empire, or in a tradition of oral performance changes what every word means. Context isn't the setup; it's the lens.

misconceptions.title: Older Texts Are Harder, So Lower Expectations Are Fine
misconceptions.body: When students hit Gilgamesh or a Tang dynasty poem or a medieval Arabic ghazal, teachers sometimes lower the bar — "just get the gist." Resist this. The difficulty is often cultural and linguistic, not intellectual. Provide scaffolding (good translation notes, historical framing, glosses), but hold the same analytical expectations. Students who can analyze a Shakespeare sonnet can analyze a Hafiz ghazal; they just need the context.

assignments.name: Period Placement
assignments.body: Students receive an unlabeled excerpt from a world literature text and must identify its likely literary period and region using textual evidence. They write a paragraph arguing for their placement and naming the three details that most clearly anchor the text in time and place.

assignments.name: Cross-Cultural Theme Trace
assignments.body: Students read two texts from different regions and centuries that share a theme (exile, justice, grief, power). They write a comparative analysis arguing for how each text develops that theme differently — and what those differences reveal about the cultures that produced them.

assignments.name: Context Swap
assignments.body: Students take one passage from their world literature text and rewrite it as if the author were writing from a different historical moment (e.g., a feudal Japanese tale rewritten from a postcolonial vantage). Then they analyze: what changed? What does that reveal about how context shapes meaning?

assignments.name: Literary Period Anchor Chart
assignments.body: Over a unit, students build a running reference card for each period studied — dates, region, defining characteristics, 2-3 representative texts. The chart becomes a revision tool and a study guide for recognizing period markers in unseen texts.

assignments.name: Close Reading with Context
assignments.body: Students select one paragraph from a world literature text and annotate it twice — once without historical context, once after reading a brief contextual passage. They write a reflection comparing what they noticed each time and how context changed their reading.

mastery: Identifies specific textual evidence that reflects the historical period or cultural context in which a work was produced, without relying solely on the introductory blurb.
Applies literary analysis — theme, imagery, structure, tone — to world literature texts with the same precision they bring to canonical American or British texts.
Resists flattening unfamiliar texts into "exotic" or "different" and instead engages with the text as a crafted artifact making an argument or creating an experience.
