Connects a text's period markers to its meaning — explains WHY a text's historical moment matters, not just THAT it has one.
Reads across multiple world literature texts within a unit and identifies continuities and ruptures across periods and regions without collapsing distinct traditions into each other.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I 7A asks students to read and analyze world literature as well — the English II expectation deepens the periodization demand. By English II, students must demonstrate awareness of literary periods as historical phenomena, not just read globally representative texts. English III continues this ramp with additional emphasis on rhetorical and structural analysis.

The substrand label "Informational & Argumentative" in the TEKS framework applies to Standard 7 broadly; this expectation covers literary (not purely informational) texts. The strand label may cause confusion — this TEK is about literary world texts analyzed in historical context, not argumentative reading per se.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog as a standalone tested skill; STAAR embeds literary period awareness in passage analysis questions rather than isolating it. Question stems are written to mirror the way STAAR tests contextual and analytical reading of literary passages.
DOK 3 justification: students must apply knowledge of historical period and cultural context to interpret a text — requiring strategic thinking, not just retrieval or skill application.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Poetic Conventions Across Forms
expectation: Analyze the effects of metrics; rhyme schemes; types of rhymes such as end, internal, slant, and eye; and other conventions in poems across a variety of poetic forms.
overview: This TEK asks students to move past identification — knowing that a poem has iambic pentameter or a slant rhyme is not the same as explaining what those choices DO to a reader. The real task is connecting formal decisions to effect, and doing it across multiple poetic traditions, not just the sonnet.
studentFriendly: You can read a poem and explain how its beat, its rhyme choices, and its structural conventions are doing real work — shaping sound, building tension, or creating meaning that the words alone wouldn't carry.
questionStems: Read lines X–Y. The poet's use of [meter/rhyme scheme] in these lines MOST likely creates a feeling of —
Which line from the poem BEST illustrates the use of slant rhyme?
How does the shift in meter in stanza X contribute to the poem's meaning?
The rhyme scheme in stanzas X–Y is MOST likely intended to —
Which statement BEST describes the effect of the internal rhyme in lines X–Y?
The poet's use of eye rhyme in lines X and Y suggests that the speaker —
Based on the poem, the regular meter in stanzas X–Y MOST likely emphasizes —
Which detail from the poem BEST supports the conclusion that the poet deliberately breaks the established rhyme scheme to —
The poetic form used in this poem MOST likely affects the reader's experience by —
How does the end rhyme in lines X and Y develop the central theme of the poem?

misconceptions.title: Rhyme = Decoration, Not Decision
misconceptions.body: Students treat rhyme as wallpaper — it's just there because that's what poems do. Push them to ask WHY two words rhyme at that particular moment: does the rhyme create closure, irony, inevitability? When a student writes "the poet uses end rhyme to make the poem sound nice," mark it wrong and send them back. Nice is not an effect.

misconceptions.title: Slant Rhyme Means the Poet Made a Mistake
misconceptions.body: Students who know "real" rhyme often read slant rhyme as a failure — the poet almost got there. Teach them that slant rhyme is typically the harder technical choice, not the easier one. Frost, Dickinson, Wilfred Owen — these are not poets who couldn't find the matching word. The near-miss creates friction, and friction is the point.

misconceptions.title: Meter Is Just Stress-Counting
misconceptions.body: Students learn to scan iambs and anapests and think the analysis is done. Watch for students who annotate every syllable correctly and still say nothing about what the rhythm DOES. A line that breaks its meter after six steady stanzas is a seismic event — push students to treat the break as a signal, not an error.

misconceptions.title: Poetic Form Is a Container, Not a Participant
misconceptions.body: Students think the sonnet or the villanelle is just a shape the poem happens to sit in. Teach them that form makes promises to the reader — about resolution, about return, about argument structure — and the poet's relationship to those promises IS part of the poem's meaning. A villanelle that breaks its refrains is not misbehaving; it's arguing.

misconceptions.title: Eye Rhyme Is a Visual Quirk With No Function
misconceptions.body: Students learn the term, get it right on the quiz, and then never connect it to anything else. Teach them that eye rhyme often marks historical pronunciation shifts, creates a sense of artifice or performance on the page, or unsettles the reader's expectation that the eye and ear will agree. Ask: what does it mean that this poem looks like it rhymes but doesn't sound like it does?

assignments.name: Form Autopsy
assignments.body: Students select two poems from different traditions — one with strict formal conventions (sonnet, villanelle, ballad) and one with looser conventions (free verse with selective rhyme, prose poem). They produce a side-by-side written analysis of three formal choices in each and explain the effect of each choice on the reader. No "this makes it more interesting" allowed — requires specific, precise effect claims.

assignments.name: Break the Convention
assignments.body: Students take a poem with a regular rhyme scheme and rewrite one stanza deliberately breaking that pattern — then write a paragraph explaining what the break does to the poem's meaning and whether the original poet might have done this on purpose anywhere in the poem.

assignments.name: Slant Rhyme Gallery
assignments.body: Class compiles a gallery of slant rhymes drawn from a set of poems. For each pair of near-rhymes, students write a sentence explaining what the imperfect sound creates — unease, understatement, irony, etc. Gallery is annotated and posted; class votes on the most interesting slant rhyme pair and defends the choice.

mastery: Identifies the type of rhyme or metrical pattern present and connects it to a specific, named effect — not just "it sounds good" or "it flows."
Explains what happens at moments of formal disruption — a broken rhyme scheme, a shifted meter — as an intentional craft decision rather than an error.
Compares how two poems in different forms use convention differently to achieve different emotional or argumentative effects.
Reads slant rhyme and eye rhyme as deliberate choices with functions, not as approximations or historical oddities.
Uses precise technical vocabulary (iambic, trochaic, couplet, tercet, slant rhyme, eye rhyme, internal rhyme) embedded in claims about meaning, not as labels floating free of interpretation.
notes: Vertical ramp: English I 7B asks students to analyze the effects of sound devices (alliteration, assonance, consonance) and rhyme in poetry. English II adds metrics and a taxonomized set of rhyme types (end, internal, slant, eye) with an explicit cross-form scope. English III 7B will push toward evaluating how poetic conventions contribute to the poem's argument or theme at a higher level of abstraction.
DOK 3 justification: students must apply knowledge of formal conventions to explain effects, requiring reasoning about how structural decisions produce meaning — not mere identification.

The substrand label "Informational & Argumentative" is technically the Standard 7 substrand for this code grouping in the parsed index, though poetry sits in the literary tradition. Note this classification artifact when teaching; the skills here are literary analysis skills.

STAAR does test poetic conventions directly — released items ask students to identify the effect of meter shifts, explain how rhyme scheme contributes to theme, and compare formal choices across paired poems. The stems in this entry mirror that released-item structure.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze the Function of Dramatic Conventions
expectation: Analyze the function of dramatic conventions such as asides, soliloquies, dramatic irony, and satire.
overview: This TEK asks students to move past identification — "that's an aside" — and into function: what does the convention DO for the audience, and why would a playwright choose it here? The analytical target is purpose and effect, not label recognition.
studentFriendly: You can read a play and explain exactly why a character steps forward to whisper something to the audience, or why the audience knows something the characters don't — and what each of those moves is designed to make you feel or understand.
questionStems: Read paragraphs X–Y. The aside in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Which statement BEST explains how the soliloquy in paragraph X develops the character's internal conflict?
The dramatic irony in paragraphs X–Y is created MOST directly by —
How does the author's use of satire in the excerpt contribute to the play's central argument?
Based on paragraph X, what is the MOST likely effect of the aside on the audience's understanding of events?
Which sentence from the excerpt BEST supports the inference that the playwright uses dramatic irony to create tension?
The soliloquy in paragraph X MOST nearly functions as —
Read paragraphs X–Z. How does the playwright use satire in this passage to comment on [social institution/behavior]?
Which detail from the excerpt BEST illustrates how the dramatic convention in paragraph X shapes the audience's perspective?
The playwright's use of [aside/soliloquy/dramatic irony] in paragraphs X–Y MOST likely emphasizes —

misconceptions.title: Naming the Convention Is the Same as Analyzing It
misconceptions.body: Students write "this is an aside" and consider the analysis done. The expectation is function — why the playwright used it, what it accomplishes for the audience at this exact moment in the play. Teach them the two-step: name it, then ask "so what does that DO?" If they can't answer the second question, they haven't analyzed anything.

misconceptions.title: Dramatic Irony Belongs to the Characters, Not the Audience
misconceptions.body: Students say "the character feels irony" or "the character uses irony." Dramatic irony is an audience experience — it's the gap between what the audience knows and what the character knows. Reframe it every time: who holds the knowledge? The moment students locate the irony in the audience, not the character, they've got it.

misconceptions.title: Soliloquies Are Just Monologues
misconceptions.body: Students flatten the soliloquy into "a long speech" and miss its specific function: a character speaking their true, unguarded interior state to the audience, alone on stage. The distinction matters because a soliloquy is by definition honest in a way a speech to other characters often isn't. Push them to ask: what can we learn here that we could not learn anywhere else in the play?

misconceptions.title: Satire Means Making Fun of Something
misconceptions.body: Students reduce satire to mockery and miss its argumentative spine. Satire doesn't just ridicule — it exposes, critiques, and often prescribes by implication. Watch for students who describe satire as "the playwright was joking about X" without identifying what critique the joke is making. The target of the satire always points toward the text's larger argument.

misconceptions.title: Conventions Are Decorative, Not Structural
misconceptions.body: Students treat asides, soliloquies, and irony as flourishes — stylistic extras on top of the "real" story. Teach them that dramatic conventions ARE structure: they control information flow, shape audience allegiance, and create the conditions for dramatic tension. A play without its conventions is a different play. Ask them: if you cut the asides, what does the audience lose that it can't get anywhere else?

assignments.name: Convention Function Chart
assignments.body: Students select three dramatic conventions from a shared text and complete a three-column chart: convention, location, and function. The function column must answer "what does this make the audience know, feel, or expect?" No column can say "makes it interesting."

assignments.name: Rewrite the Scene
assignments.body: Students take a scene that relies on dramatic irony or an aside and rewrite it without the convention. They then write a paragraph explaining what the audience loses and what the playwright gained by using the original technique.

assignments.name: Satire Deconstruction
assignments.body: Students read a satirical scene (Shakespeare, Moliere, Oscar Wilde, or a contemporary equivalent) and identify the target, the exaggeration, and the implied critique. They present their argument in a structured paragraph with textual evidence.

assignments.name: Soliloquy vs. Dialogue Comparison
assignments.body: Students compare what a character reveals in a soliloquy with what the same character says to others in adjacent scenes. They write one page on how the gap between the two creates meaning.

mastery: Names the specific dramatic convention in use AND explains its function at the chosen moment — not just what it is, but what it accomplishes.
Locates dramatic irony in the audience's position, not the character's speech or behavior alone.
Reads a soliloquy as an access point to unguarded interiority and distinguishes it from ordinary monologue.
Identifies what a satirical passage is critiquing, not just what it is mocking.
Connects the convention's local function to the play's larger thematic or argumentative purpose.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I introduces dramatic conventions at the identification level — students recognize and define asides, soliloquies, and dramatic irony. English II pushes into function analysis: why the playwright deploys each convention and what effect it produces on the audience. English III continues into evaluation and critique of convention use across multiple works and periods.

DOK 3 justification: analyzing function requires students to move beyond retrieval and definition into strategic thinking — explaining relationships between technique and effect across a complete dramatic text.

This TEK sits under the Informational & Argumentative substrand in the TEKS document, which initially seems counterintuitive for a drama skill. The rationale is that playwrights use dramatic conventions argumentatively — satire in particular is a form of persuasion. Treat drama analysis here as argument analysis with theatrical tools.

STAAR does not test drama directly as a genre, but released passages have included dramatic excerpts read as literary prose. Stems are written to mirror how STAAR would frame dramatic convention analysis when presented in that format.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Characteristics and Structural Elements of Informational Texts
expectation: Analyze characteristics and structural elements of informational texts such as clear thesis, relevant supporting evidence, pertinent examples, and conclusion.
overview: This TEK asks students to read informational texts the way a builder reads blueprints — not just absorbing the content but naming the load-bearing elements: where the thesis lives, what evidence is doing real structural work, which examples are pertinent versus decorative, and how the conclusion seals the argument. The move is diagnosis, not summary.
studentFriendly: You can read an informational text and identify exactly where the author states their central claim, which evidence actually supports it, and how the piece closes — and you can say whether each piece is doing its job.
questionStems: Which sentence from the selection MOST clearly states the author's central thesis?
The evidence in paragraph X BEST supports the author's claim that —
Which detail from paragraph X is MOST relevant to the author's argument?
How does the example in paragraph X contribute to the development of the author's thesis?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which piece of evidence BEST supports the inference that the author believes —
The author's conclusion in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates the relationship between the author's thesis and supporting evidence?
The author includes the example in paragraph X MOST likely to —
Which organizational choice MOST effectively develops the central argument of the selection?
Based on the structure of the selection, the author MOST likely organized paragraphs X–Y in order to —

misconceptions.title: The First Sentence Is Always the Thesis
misconceptions.body: Students anchor on opening sentences and label whatever comes first as the thesis — even when it's background, a hook, or an anecdote. Teach them that a thesis is a claim with arguable weight, not just a topic sentence. Have them ask: could a reasonable person disagree with this statement? If not, it's probably setup, not stance.

misconceptions.title: All Evidence Is Equal
misconceptions.body: Students treat every sentence in the body as "supporting evidence" without distinguishing between data, anecdote, expert testimony, and filler. Push them to evaluate pertinence — does this piece of evidence actually support the stated thesis, or is it loosely adjacent? Watch for students who highlight liberally and think coverage equals analysis.

misconceptions.title: A Conclusion Just Repeats the Introduction
misconceptions.body: Students expect the conclusion to be a word-for-word echo of the opening and mark it "present" when it more or less repeats. Teach them that strong conclusions do something the introduction couldn't — synthesize, extend, call to action, reframe the stakes. A conclusion that only restates is structurally weak, and students should be able to name that.

misconceptions.title: Examples and Evidence Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students conflate examples with evidence, treating any concrete detail as proof. Reframe: evidence supports the claim with data or authority; examples illustrate what the claim looks like in practice. A pertinent example is one that genuinely illuminates the thesis — an impertinent one is anecdote that sounds relevant but doesn't advance the argument. This distinction is the difference between a 3 and a 4 on any analytical rubric.

misconceptions.title: Structural Analysis Means Labeling Sections
misconceptions.body: Students think they've analyzed structure when they've written "introduction, body, conclusion" in the margin. The real move is explaining the WHY — why does the author place this evidence before that one? Why does this example appear in the third paragraph rather than the first? Structure is argument; teach them to treat organizational choices as rhetorical decisions, not default formatting.

assignments.name: Structural X-Ray
assignments.body: Students annotate a two-page informational text, labeling each paragraph's function (thesis, supporting evidence, pertinent example, transition, conclusion) and writing one sentence per paragraph explaining WHY it belongs where it does — not just what it says.

assignments.name: Evidence Triage
assignments.body: Students are given a list of eight potential pieces of evidence for a provided thesis. They must rank them from most to least relevant, writing one sentence of justification per ranking. Discussion follows: where did the class diverge, and what does that reveal about the thesis's scope?

assignments.name: Conclusion Autopsy
assignments.body: Students read three conclusions from different informational texts and evaluate each: Does it merely restate? Does it synthesize? Does it extend the argument? They write a one-paragraph report naming which conclusion is strongest and why, citing specific textual evidence.

assignments.name: Build and Break
assignments.body: Students write a short informational paragraph (5–7 sentences) with a clear thesis, two pieces of supporting evidence, and a pertinent example. Then they swap with a partner who must identify which element is weakest and explain why in writing. Teach the class that building reveals what analyzing requires.

mastery: Locates the thesis in a text and distinguishes it from background context or topic sentences, explaining what makes it a claim rather than a fact.
Evaluates each piece of evidence for its relevance to the stated thesis, not merely its presence in the text.
Distinguishes between examples that illuminate a claim and details that are loosely adjacent to it.
Describes the structural purpose of the conclusion — whether it restates, synthesizes, extends, or fails — with textual support.
Explains why the author's organizational choices serve (or undermine) the central argument, not just what those choices are.
notes: Vertical ramp: English I (7)(D)(i) introduces the same structural elements — thesis, evidence, examples, conclusion — at an identification level. English II adds *analyze*, pushing students past locating to evaluating whether each element is doing its job. English III will extend this to critique and cross-text comparison.

The parenthetical examples (clear thesis, relevant supporting evidence, pertinent examples, conclusion) are not an exhaustive list — they are diagnostic anchors. Instruction should surface texts where one or more elements is weak or absent so students have something to actually analyze.
DOK 3 justification: students must reason about the function and quality of structural elements, not just locate them — that requires strategic thinking beyond retrieval.
STAAR tests this TEK primarily through literary nonfiction and paired informational passages; stems referencing paragraph location and author's organizational choices are the most common vehicle.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Organizational Design and Thesis in Informational Texts
expectation: Analyze characteristics and structural elements of informational texts such as the relationship between organizational design and thesis.
overview: This TEK asks students to read structure as argument — to see how the sequence, grouping, and arrangement of an informational text isn't neutral, but is actively doing the work of advancing or obscuring the thesis. The question isn't just "what does this text say?" but "why is it built this way?"
studentFriendly: You can look at how an informational text is organized — its sections, sequence, and layout — and explain how that structure supports, complicates, or even contradicts the writer's central claim.
questionStems: Which organizational pattern BEST supports the author's central argument in paragraphs X–Y?
How does the structure of paragraphs X–Y contribute to the development of the author's thesis?
The author MOST likely chose to present information in chronological order in paragraphs X–Y in order to —
Based on the organization of the text, the author's central claim is BEST described as —
Which sentence from the selection BEST states the thesis the organizational structure is designed to support?
The author's decision to place the counterargument in paragraph X rather than paragraph Y MOST likely serves to —
How does the author's use of a problem-solution structure in paragraphs X–Y affect the reader's understanding of the thesis?
Which statement BEST describes the relationship between the organizational design and the central argument of the text?
The shift in organization between paragraphs X and Y MOST likely indicates that the author —
Based on the text's structure, the reader can conclude that the author's primary purpose is to —

misconceptions.title: Thesis Lives in the First Paragraph
misconceptions.body: Students are conditioned to hunt for the thesis in the opening paragraph and stop looking once they find a candidate. In sophisticated informational texts, the thesis may be delayed, distributed, or implied through accumulation. Teach them to read the whole text before declaring what the thesis is — then work backward to see how the structure led them there.

misconceptions.title: Organization Is a Container, Not an Argument
misconceptions.body: Students treat structure as a neutral packaging decision — the content is the argument, the structure just holds it. Push them to see that a comparison-contrast structure implicitly argues that two things are meaningfully different; a cause-effect structure argues that events are not random. The organization is a claim about how the world works. Watch for students who describe structure without explaining what the structural choice asserts.

misconceptions.title: Every Informational Text Has One Clear Structure
misconceptions.body: Students assume texts follow a single, labeled organizational pattern — cause-effect OR problem-solution OR chronological, full stop. Real informational texts layer and shift. An article might open with narrative, pivot to comparison, and close with call to action. Teach students to track where the structure changes and what the change signals about the argument's movement.

misconceptions.title: A Strong Thesis Means a Strong Text
misconceptions.body: When students identify a clear thesis, they often assume the text works. But a well-stated thesis can be undermined by organizational choices that bury evidence, front-load concessions, or sequence examples in an order that weakens rather than builds. Teach them to ask: does the structure actually deliver on the thesis, or does it promise something the text doesn't complete?

misconceptions.title: Organizational Analysis Means Labeling the Pattern
misconceptions.body: Students believe they've analyzed structure when they write "this text uses a problem-solution structure." That's identification, not analysis. Push them to the next move: what does this structure do to the thesis? Does it make the problem feel urgent? Does it make the solution feel inevitable? The label is the starting line, not the finish.

assignments.name: Structural X-Ray
assignments.body: Students read a two-to-three-page informational text and produce a visual outline showing the organizational pattern of each section. Then, in a paragraph, they explain how the sequence of sections advances, delays, or complicates the thesis. They must cite at least two specific structural choices.

assignments.name: Reorganize the Argument
assignments.body: Students receive a scrambled informational text — sections cut apart and shuffled. They reassemble it in the order they believe best serves the thesis, then write a defense of their sequencing. Compare different groups' reconstructions; where students disagree is where the teaching lives.

assignments.name: Same Thesis, Different Structure
assignments.body: Students read two short informational texts on the same topic with different organizational approaches. They analyze how each structure shapes the reader's experience of the thesis differently, and argue which structure is more effective for the intended audience.

assignments.name: Structural Revision Memo
assignments.body: Students write a memo to the author of an informational text they've read, identifying one organizational decision that weakens the thesis and proposing a specific restructuring — with a rationale grounded in how the change would better serve the central claim.

mastery: Identifies the text's organizational pattern and articulates what that pattern argues about the subject, not just what it contains.
Locates the thesis — whether stated, delayed, or implied — and traces how the structure leads the reader toward or away from it.
Explains specific structural choices (section order, placement of evidence, positioning of counterargument) and their effect on the thesis.
Recognizes when organizational design undermines or complicates the thesis rather than simply supporting it.
Produces written analysis that moves past labeling into explaining — naming what the structure does, not just what it is.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I 7D(ii) asks students to analyze the relationship between organizational design and thesis; English II holds the same expectation but applies it to more complex texts with less explicit structure — the skill is the same, the texts are harder and the organization is more layered. English III will add evaluation of the effectiveness of that relationship, pushing from analysis to judgment.
DOK 3 justification: students must reason across multiple structural elements to draw conclusions about how organization shapes argument — this is strategic thinking, not recall or skill application.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is tested through paired or extended reading passages in which students must infer or evaluate the author's organizational choices and their effect on meaning. Question Stems above reflect that testing context.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Characteristics and Structural Elements of Argumentative Texts
expectation: Analyze characteristics and structural elements of argumentative texts such as: clear arguable claim, appeals, and convincing conclusion.
overview: This TEK asks students to move beyond identifying what an argument says to examining how it is built — whether the claim is genuinely arguable, whether the appeals are doing real rhetorical work, and whether the conclusion lands or merely stops. The structural analysis here is the scaffolding for everything students will eventually do as argumentative writers.
studentFriendly: You can read an argumentative text and explain exactly how the author built the argument — where the claim is, what kind of appeals hold it up, and whether the conclusion actually earns the ending.
questionStems: Which sentence from the passage BEST states the author's central claim?
The author's use of [statistic/example] in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Which sentence from the passage BEST supports the conclusion that the author's argument is effectively structured?
The appeal in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which statement BEST describes how the author develops the argument?
The author's conclusion in paragraph X differs from the introduction because the author —
Which evidence from the passage MOST strongly supports the author's central claim?
The author MOST likely includes [quotation/data] in paragraph X in order to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates a logical appeal rather than an emotional one?
Based on the passage, which statement BEST describes a weakness in the author's argument?

misconceptions.title: A Claim Is Just the Topic
misconceptions.body: Students routinely write — and misidentify — topic statements as claims. "Climate change is a serious issue" is a topic; "The federal government must mandate carbon taxes to address climate change" is a claim. Watch for students who cannot distinguish the two when analyzing a mentor text. Teach them to ask: could someone reasonably disagree with this sentence? If not, it isn't a claim — it's a setup.

misconceptions.title: All Appeals Are Equal
misconceptions.body: Students assume that identifying an appeal — "this is pathos" — completes the analysis. Push them deeper: is the emotional appeal earned or manipulative? Is the logical appeal supported with reliable evidence or correlation dressed as causation? The label is the beginning of analysis, not the end. An appeal's presence does not make it effective.

misconceptions.title: The Conclusion Is Just a Summary
misconceptions.body: Students read conclusions as restatements and stop there. A convincing conclusion does more — it synthesizes, extends, or reframes the argument in light of the evidence presented. Teach them to ask: what does the reader know at the end that they didn't know at the beginning, and how does the conclusion use that? If the answer is "nothing new," the conclusion isn't doing its job.

misconceptions.title: Arguable Means Controversial
misconceptions.body: Students conflate "arguable" with "edgy" or "politically charged." An arguable claim is one that can be supported with evidence and reasoning — not just one that makes people uncomfortable. Texts arguing about school lunch menus or library hours are just as structurally arguable as texts about national policy. The analysis should focus on the structure of the claim, not its subject matter.

misconceptions.title: Structure Analysis Means Outline the Paragraphs
misconceptions.body: Students reduce structural analysis to labeling sections: intro, body, conclusion. That's organization, not structure. Structural analysis asks whether the order of elements serves the argument — does the strongest evidence come where the reader most needs it? Does the author address counterarguments before or after the main case, and why? Teach them to read structure as a rhetorical choice, not a checklist.

assignments.name: Claim Autopsy
assignments.body: Students receive three opening paragraphs from different argumentative texts. They identify the claim in each, rate its arguability on a 1–3 scale, and explain their rating in two sentences. Class debriefs disagreements — where students disagree on arguability, that's the rich instructional space.

assignments.name: Appeals Forensics
assignments.body: Students annotate a full argumentative essay, color-coding logical, emotional, and ethical appeals. For each highlighted passage, they write a one-sentence explanation of the appeal's intended effect. The final step: they assess whether each appeal strengthens or weakens the overall argument.

assignments.name: Conclusion Reconstruction
assignments.body: Provide students with an argumentative essay with the conclusion removed. They write a concluding paragraph, then compare theirs to the original. Discussion: which one is more convincing? What does "convincing" mean structurally, not just emotionally?

assignments.name: Strong Claim vs. Weak Claim Revision
assignments.body: Give students a set of weak claims (topic sentences masquerading as claims). They rewrite each as a genuinely arguable claim, then justify the revision. This bridges analysis to production.

mastery: Identifies the claim in an argumentative text and distinguishes it from a topic statement or general observation.
Names the type of appeal used in a specific passage and explains the rhetorical function — not just the label.
Evaluates whether structural choices (placement of evidence, handling of counterargument, shape of conclusion) serve or undermine the argument.
Reads the conclusion as a structural decision, explaining what it accomplishes beyond summarizing the preceding paragraphs.
Applies consistent analytical vocabulary — claim, appeal, warrant, conclusion — with precision across multiple texts.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I 7(E)(i) establishes the same characteristics — claim, appeals, conclusion — but the expectation in English II is analytical depth, not just identification. Students should be explaining how the structure functions, not just naming its parts.

Vertical ramp to English III: English III will ask students to evaluate argumentative texts for logical fallacies and rhetorical devices (8G), extending this structural analysis into explicit critique of reasoning quality.
DOK 3 justification: structural analysis requires students to apply conceptual understanding to text-specific choices and explain relationships among elements — beyond recall or skill application.

STAAR analog note: STAAR tests this TEK through passages where students identify evidence, evaluate appeals, and assess argument structure — stems using "central claim," "BEST supports," and "develops the argument" map directly to released items.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Evidence and Counterargument in Argumentative Texts
expectation: Analyze characteristics and structural elements of argumentative texts, including various types of evidence and treatment of counterarguments, including concessions and rebuttals.
overview: This is where argument analysis gets real — students move past identifying a claim and start examining HOW a writer defends it. The quality of evidence and what a writer does with opposition (ignore it, concede it, dismantle it) tells you almost everything about how honest and rigorous the argument actually is.
studentFriendly: You can read an argument and tell not just what the writer believes, but how solid their evidence is and whether they're playing fair when they deal with the people who disagree with them.
questionStems: Which sentence from paragraph X BEST supports the author's central claim?
The author includes the information in paragraph X MOST likely to —
How does the evidence in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the development of the author's argument?
The author's use of [type of evidence] in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Which statement BEST describes how the author addresses a counterargument in paragraph X?
The author concedes in paragraph X that [claim]. This concession MOST likely serves to —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the author's rebuttal to the opposing view is MOST likely intended to —
Which of the following BEST describes the relationship between the evidence in paragraph X and the evidence in paragraph Y?
A reader who disagrees with the author's position would MOST likely point to which weakness in the argument?
The author's treatment of opposing views in paragraphs X–Y suggests that —

misconceptions.title: More Evidence Automatically Means a Stronger Argument
misconceptions.body: Students pile-rank arguments by evidence count — the more citations, the better the case. Teach them that a single precise statistic or well-chosen example can outperform three vague anecdotes. Push them to evaluate the TYPE of evidence (statistical, anecdotal, expert testimony, logical reasoning) and ask whether the type fits the claim it's supposed to prove. Volume is not rigor.

misconceptions.title: A Concession is a Sign the Writer is Losing
misconceptions.body: When students see an author acknowledge an opposing point, they read it as a crack in the argument. Flip this: a well-placed concession signals intellectual honesty and actually strengthens the writer's credibility. Teach them to distinguish between a concession that weakens (the writer gives up too much) and one that strengthens (the writer neutralizes the strongest objection before it lands).

misconceptions.title: Rebuttal Means Saying the Other Side Is Wrong
misconceptions.body: Students treat rebuttal as flat contradiction — "the other side says X, but X is false." The more sophisticated move is showing WHY the opposing evidence is insufficient, irrelevant, or based on flawed assumptions without dismissing it entirely. Watch for students who write "however, this is incorrect" as a substitute for actual analysis.

misconceptions.title: Anecdote Is Not Real Evidence
misconceptions.body: Students have been told so many times to use "statistics and facts" that they automatically rank personal stories below data. But anecdote is a legitimate evidence type — it humanizes and contextualizes claims. The issue is when it bears weight it can't hold: a single story generalizing to all cases. Teach them to ask not "is this anecdote?" but "is this anecdote doing work the argument needs, and can it carry that weight alone?"

misconceptions.title: The Counterargument Section Is Just a Box to Check
misconceptions.body: Students — and many writers — treat the acknowledgment of opposing views as a procedural formality: say something charitable, pivot back. Teach them to look at how much space the author gives the counterargument, how fairly it's represented, and whether the rebuttal actually addresses what was raised or sneaks past it. A writer who strawmans the opposition and then "defeats" it hasn't made an argument — they've made theater.

assignments.name: Evidence Audit
assignments.body: Students read a short argumentative editorial and categorize every piece of evidence by type (statistical, anecdotal, expert, logical). They then write a one-paragraph evaluation: which evidence type does the most work, and which does the least? They must cite specific passages.

assignments.name: Concession or Collapse?
assignments.body: Students read two excerpts — one where a concession strengthens the argument, one where it undercuts it. In pairs, they draft a two-column analysis explaining what makes each concession land differently. Groups share and build class criteria for what a productive concession looks like.

assignments.name: Rebuttal Surgery
assignments.body: Students receive an argumentative passage where the rebuttal is weak or evasive. They rewrite the rebuttal paragraph so it actually engages the counterargument while preserving the author's original position. Compare the original and revision: what changed structurally?

assignments.name: Argument Skeleton
assignments.body: Students map a full argumentative text — claim, evidence types, counterargument location, concession language, rebuttal strategy — onto a structured graphic organizer. Then they write a paragraph evaluating whether the structure serves the argument or exposes its weaknesses.

assignments.name: Steel Man, Then Dismantle
assignments.body: Students identify the strongest counterargument to a text's central claim (not the one the author chose to address) and write a rebuttal that honestly engages it. This forces them to understand both the argument and its opposition at their best.

mastery: Identifies multiple evidence types within a single text and explains why each type does or does not fit the claim it supports.
Distinguishes between a concession that strengthens credibility and one that undermines the argument's coherence.
Evaluates whether a rebuttal actually responds to the counterargument raised or deflects from it.
Explains the structural function of counterargument treatment — where it appears, how much space it gets, how fairly it represents the opposing position.

Produces written analysis that moves beyond labeling ("the author uses statistics") to explaining rhetorical effect ("the statistical evidence in paragraph 3 neutralizes the anecdotal counterexample by shifting the frame from individual cases to aggregate outcomes").
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I (7)(E)(ii) introduces the same structural elements — evidence types and counterargument treatment — but at an identification level. English II pushes to analysis: students must explain WHY evidence choices and counterargument strategies work or fail, not just name them. English III extends this to evaluation and critique.

This sub-expectation is a companion to (7)(E)(i), which covers claim and organizational logic. The two work together — evidence and counterargument are how the claim gets defended; organization is how the defense is arranged.

DOK 3 justification: analysis of how multiple elements interact within a text requires reasoning beyond retrieval or skill application; students must explain the relationship between structural choices and argumentative effect.

STAAR tests this TEK primarily through argument-analysis passages where students must evaluate the function of specific paragraphs or evidence. Stems about "why does the author include" and "how does paragraph X contribute to the argument" are the most common surface forms.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Argumentative Texts: Identifiable Audience
expectation: Analyze characteristics and structural elements of argumentative texts, including identifiable audience or reader.
overview: Before students can evaluate whether an argument works, they need to know who it's working on. This TEK focuses the analytical lens on audience — how a writer constructs a text with a specific reader in mind and how that targeting shapes every structural and stylistic choice in the argument.
studentFriendly: You can read an argumentative text and identify exactly who it was written for, then explain how the writer's choices — what they assume you know, what they leave out, what language they use — are tailored to that specific audience.
questionStems: Based on paragraph X, the author MOST likely wrote this text for an audience that —
Which detail from the selection BEST supports the inference that the intended audience already believes —
The author's use of [term/phrase] in paragraph X suggests the intended reader is MOST likely —
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates how the author appeals to a specific audience?
How does the author's choice to include [example/evidence] in paragraphs X–Y contribute to reaching the intended audience?
The author MOST likely addresses the reader directly in paragraph X in order to —
Based on the selection, with which statement about the intended audience would the author MOST likely agree?
Which detail from paragraph X suggests the author assumes the audience has prior knowledge of —
The author's tone in paragraph X is MOST likely shaped by the assumption that the reader —
Which structural choice in the selection BEST demonstrates that the author is writing for a specific type of reader?

misconceptions.title: "The audience is whoever reads it"
misconceptions.body: Students default to "everyone" or "the general public" when asked to identify an audience, treating audience as an abstract given rather than a deliberate construction. Teach them that writers make specific assumptions — about what the reader already believes, what they fear, what they want — and those assumptions are embedded in the text's choices. Push them to find the evidence: what does the author assume you already know?

misconceptions.title: Audience is mentioned in the introduction and nowhere else
misconceptions.body: Students identify the intended audience from a surface signal — the publication name, a stated purpose, a title — and stop there. They don't see audience as something woven through every paragraph. Watch for students who write one sentence about audience and then analyze tone, structure, and evidence as if audience no longer applies. The stronger move is tracking how audience shapes each structural choice throughout the text.

misconceptions.title: A persuasive tone means the audience is hostile
misconceptions.body: Students equate strong persuasion with an adversarial audience and soft persuasion with a friendly one. In reality, some of the most aggressive rhetoric targets true believers who need to be energized, not converted. Teach them to distinguish between texts written to convert skeptics, reinforce believers, and mobilize the already-committed — each requires a different structural approach even when the argument looks similar.

misconceptions.title: Identifying the audience is the same as analyzing the audience
misconceptions.body: Students name the audience ("this is for environmentalists") and consider the task complete. The analytical move is explaining HOW the writer tailors the argument — what evidence they select, what they assume rather than prove, what emotional registers they activate — because of that audience. Identification is the entry point; the analysis is in the because.

misconceptions.title: Writers always get their audience right
misconceptions.body: Students read the text as successfully targeted because it exists in print. Push them to consider mismatches: moments when the writer assumes knowledge the audience may not have, or when the appeal might alienate the very reader it's trying to reach. Noticing where audience targeting breaks down is a more sophisticated analytical move than confirming where it works.

assignments.name: Audience Identification with Evidence
assignments.body: Students read two argumentative texts on the same topic written for different audiences (e.g., a scientific journal editorial vs. a community newspaper op-ed). For each, they write a paragraph identifying the intended audience and citing at least three textual choices that reveal that targeting — vocabulary, assumed knowledge, evidence selection, or structural emphasis.

assignments.name: Same Claim, Two Audiences
assignments.body: Students write two short argumentative paragraphs making the same claim but targeting explicitly different audiences (e.g., school board members vs. students). Then they annotate their own drafts identifying where they adjusted their choices for each reader. The reflection surfaces how audience actually functions in construction, not just in analysis.

assignments.name: Structural Audit
assignments.body: Students mark up a full argumentative text, annotating each paragraph for how it serves the intended audience specifically — what it assumes, what it explains, what it appeals to. Final discussion: which structural choices would have to change if the audience were different?

mastery: Names the intended audience with specificity — not "adults" but "adults who already distrust [X] and need a reason to act."
Cites specific textual choices — diction, assumed background knowledge, evidence selection — as evidence of audience targeting rather than stating the audience abstractly.
Traces how audience shapes structural decisions across the full text, not just the introduction.
Distinguishes between texts designed to convert, reinforce, or mobilize — and connects that purpose to structural choices.
Identifies moments where audience targeting is effective AND moments where it may misfire or exclude an unintended reader.
notes: Vertical ramp: English I introduces audience as a characteristic to identify; English II requires analysis — students must explain how audience shapes structural and stylistic choices throughout the text, not just name who the reader is. English III extends this into cross-text comparison of how different authors target different audiences for the same argumentative purpose.
This sub-element (iii) is one of several structural elements listed under 7(E); entries 7E.i and 7E.ii should be read alongside this one to see the full scope of the argumentative analysis standard.

DOK 3 justification: requires students to go beyond identification to explanation of how a structural feature operates within and across a text — inferential reasoning with textual support, not retrieval.

STAAR note: this TEK maps directly to how STAAR frames audience-purpose questions on informational/argumentative passages — the stems above mirror the typical "MOST likely wrote this for" and "BEST illustrates how the author appeals to" frames students encounter on the assessment.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Characteristics of Multimodal and Digital Texts
expectation: Analyze characteristics of multimodal and digital texts.
overview: This TEK asks students to treat media design as a system of choices — not decoration. Every hyperlink, embedded video, infographic, and audio clip is doing argumentative or informational work, and students need to be able to name what that work is and whether it succeeds.
studentFriendly: You can look at a website, a documentary, a digital article, or a social media post and explain how the combination of words, images, sound, and layout is shaping what you understand and believe.
questionStems: Which feature of the digital text MOST likely helps the reader understand the central idea?
How does the infographic in paragraph X contribute to the author's argument?
Based on the text and the embedded image, the reader can MOST likely infer that —
Which element of the multimodal text BEST supports the claim made in paragraph X?
The author's decision to use a hyperlink in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
How does the layout of the digital text affect the reader's understanding of the information presented?
Which sentence from the text BEST explains the relationship between the written content and the visual element?
The use of [audio/video/image] in the text MOST likely emphasizes —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, what conclusion can the reader draw about how the digital format shapes the author's message?
The author MOST likely includes the [chart/graph/photograph] in paragraph X to —

misconceptions.title: Images Are Just Decoration
misconceptions.body: Students scroll past visuals as if they're filler between the "real" text. Teach them that in a multimodal piece, the image is an argument — it selects a perspective, frames a subject, and excludes alternatives. Ask: why this image and not another? What would change if the image were removed? Watch for students who summarize the written text without once mentioning the visual.

misconceptions.title: Digital = Online = Informal
misconceptions.body: Students assume that because a text lives on a screen, it operates by looser rules than print. Push them to notice that digital texts have structural conventions just as rigid as academic essays — scroll hierarchy, above-the-fold emphasis, call-to-action placement. Teach them to read a webpage the way they'd read a formal argument: where does the author put the claim, and what does the design do to support it?

misconceptions.title: "Multimodal" Means Video
misconceptions.body: Students flatten "multimodal" to mean either video or slide presentations. Reframe this: a single newspaper article with a pull-quote, a bar graph, and a photograph is multimodal. A Twitter thread with embedded links is multimodal. The word names a relationship between modes, not a delivery platform.

misconceptions.title: More Modes = More Effective
misconceptions.body: Students default to praising complexity — "they used images AND video AND audio, so it's very effective." Teach them that modes can also undermine each other. An image that contradicts the written claim, a video that slows a digital reader before they've bought in, a font choice that signals informality in a formal argument — these are design failures, and spotting them is the analysis.

misconceptions.title: Analysis Stops at Identification
misconceptions.body: Students list what they see — "there is a chart here" — and call it done. That's inventory, not analysis. Push them past identification to function: what does this chart do that words couldn't? How does its placement affect what the reader believes before reading the paragraph that follows it? Watch for students who translate the visual into words without ever explaining why the author chose a visual mode in the first place.

assignments.name: Mode Audit
assignments.body: Students select a digital article or news feature and produce a two-column annotation: left column identifies each mode present (written text, image, caption, hyperlink, embedded media, pull-quote); right column explains the rhetorical function of each. Final step is a paragraph evaluating whether the modes work together or against each other.

assignments.name: Same Story, Two Formats
assignments.body: Pair a print news article with its digital counterpart on the same story. Students write a comparative analysis: what does the digital version add, and what does it cost? Grade for specificity — "the digital version has pictures" is insufficient.

assignments.name: Redesign with Justification
assignments.body: Students select one multimodal text they find ineffective and propose three specific design changes, explaining the function of each proposed change. They must name the mode they are adding, removing, or relocating and why.

assignments.name: Digital Text Fishbowl
assignments.body: Project a multimodal text (digital magazine feature, advocacy website, interactive infographic). One group narrates their analysis aloud while the class listens. Rotate groups. After two rounds, the class builds a shared list of analytical moves they used — these become a student-generated protocol for future analysis.

mastery: Identifies specific modes in a multimodal text (image, hyperlink, audio, video, layout, caption) and names the function each serves, not just its presence.
Explains how the interaction between two or more modes shapes the reader's understanding or acceptance of a claim — not what each mode says separately.
Evaluates whether the multimodal choices strengthen or undermine the text's purpose, with textual and visual evidence to support the judgment.
Distinguishes between mode (image, audio, layout) and platform (website, app, social media) when analyzing how a text works.
Produces written analysis that moves from identification through function to evaluation without collapsing any of the three steps.
notes: Vertical ramp: English I introduces multimodal and digital text characteristics as recognition tasks — students identify and describe. English II sharpens to analysis: not just "what is here" but "what is it doing and why did the author choose it." English III adds evaluation against rhetorical purpose, asking whether the multimodal choices are effective.

DOK 3 justification: analysis requires students to move beyond surface identification into the functional relationship between modes, which demands strategic reasoning — not retrieval or skill application alone.

STAAR analog note: STAAR regularly embeds visual elements (charts, infographics, photographs) alongside informational passages and asks how they interact with the written text. Stems in this entry mirror that pattern. The broader multimodal analysis skill (audio, hyperlinks, interactive features) is tested less directly but appears in digital testing environments where layout itself is a variable.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Author's Purpose, Audience, and Message
expectation: Analyze the author's purpose, audience, and message within a text.
overview: This is the hinge standard of Author's Purpose and Craft — before students can evaluate rhetorical moves, they have to understand what the author was trying to do, who they were trying to reach, and what they actually said. English II ramps the complexity: students are analyzing, not just identifying, which means explaining the relationship between purpose, audience, and message as a system.
studentFriendly: You can read any text — an article, a story, a speech, an ad — and explain not just what the author said but why they said it, who they were writing for, and how those choices shaped the message you received.
questionStems: Based on the selection, the author's MOST likely purpose in writing this text is —
Which detail from paragraphs X–Y BEST supports the conclusion that the author wrote this text primarily to —
The author MOST likely included paragraph X in order to —
Based on the selection, the intended audience for this text is MOST likely —
Which sentence from the selection BEST reveals the author's message?
How does the information in paragraphs X–Y develop the author's message about —
The author's purpose in paragraph X is MOST nearly —
Which statement BEST describes how the author's intended audience shapes the content of this selection?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, what conclusion can the reader draw about the author's central message?
The author MOST likely structures the selection around [event/idea] in order to —

misconceptions.title: Purpose Is Just "To Inform, Persuade, or Entertain"
misconceptions.body: Students collapse every text into one of three boxes and call it done. Watch for students who write "the purpose is to inform" and stop there — they've named a category, not analyzed a text. Teach them that purpose is specific: this author, in this text, is trying to do THIS particular thing to THIS particular reader. "To challenge the assumption that hard work alone guarantees success" is analysis; "to inform" is a placeholder.

misconceptions.title: Audience Is Whoever Read the Book
misconceptions.body: Students confuse intended audience with actual readership. A text written for wealthy policy-makers in 1900 was not written for a tenth-grader in 2026 — even if both end up reading it. Push them to read for evidence of audience: what does the author assume the reader already knows? What tone signals who is welcome? What vocabulary implies who the author expects to be reading?

misconceptions.title: Message and Main Idea Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students paste in the main idea and label it the message. The distinction matters: main idea is what the text is about; message is what the author wants the reader to take away and believe or feel. "Toni Morrison writes about racial violence in the American South" is a main idea. "Morrison argues that the violence wasn't exceptional — it was the logic of the system" is a message. Teach them to ask: what does the author want me to DO with this information?

misconceptions.title: Purpose, Audience, and Message Operate Independently
misconceptions.body: Students analyze purpose in one paragraph, audience in another, message in a third — and never connect them. The real move is seeing how they form a system: the intended audience shapes the choices the author makes, and those choices carry the message toward a purpose. A text aimed at skeptics looks different than the same argument aimed at believers — same author, same event, different everything else.

misconceptions.title: If the Text Is Fiction, It Doesn't Have a Purpose
misconceptions.body: Students apply "purpose, audience, message" only to nonfiction and switch to theme analysis the moment the text is a story. Reframe: every text has a maker who made choices for reasons. A novelist deciding to end a chapter on a character's silence has a purpose. Teach students to read fiction the same way — not "what is the theme" as a fixed extraction, but "what is this author trying to make me understand, and who do they think I am?"

assignments.name: Purpose, Audience, Message Triangle
assignments.body: Students receive two texts on the same topic — one for a general audience, one for a specialist or advocacy audience. They analyze how purpose, audience, and message differ across the two texts and write a comparative paragraph explaining which choices each author made and why.

assignments.name: Reverse-Engineer the Audience
assignments.body: Students read an unfamiliar historical or cross-cultural text and, WITHOUT being told anything about its context, reconstruct the likely intended audience from internal evidence alone. They cite specific words, assumptions, examples, and tone cues to build their case. Afterward, reveal the actual context and discuss what they got right and wrong.

assignments.name: Message vs. Main Idea Sort
assignments.body: Give students 10 one-paragraph summaries of texts they've read. They sort each into "main idea" or "message" — then write one sentence converting each main idea into a message statement. Discuss the moves required to make that shift.

assignments.name: Same Event, Different Authors
assignments.body: Two texts cover the same historical event or social issue from different authorial angles. Students write a structured analysis: same event, how do the purposes differ, how does each author's intended audience explain their framing, and what different messages emerge as a result?

mastery: Names the author's specific purpose beyond inform/persuade/entertain — with textual evidence that supports the specificity.
Identifies the intended audience using internal text evidence (assumed knowledge, tone, vocabulary, examples) rather than guessing.
Distinguishes the author's message from the main idea and can articulate what the author wants the reader to believe, feel, or do differently.
Explains how purpose, audience, and message interact — how the intended audience shapes the choices that carry the message toward the purpose.
Applies the same analytical framework to both literary and informational texts without treating fiction as purpose-free.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I 8A asks students to analyze author's purpose and audience; English II adds *message* as an explicit third target and raises the expectation from identification toward full analysis of how the three elements function as a system.
Vertical ramp to English III: English III 8A extends this to evaluating how purpose and message are achieved — judgment about effectiveness is added on top of analysis.

DOK 3 justification: students must move beyond retrieval to explain the relationship between purpose, audience, and message using evidence from the text — reasoning about how the elements connect qualifies as strategic thinking.

STAAR tests this standard heavily in the literary and informational reading passages; stems in the form "The author MOST likely included paragraph X in order to —" are among the most common item types across all grade-level STAAR ELA assessments.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Use of Text Structure to Achieve the Author's Purpose
expectation: Analyze use of text structure to achieve the author's purpose.
overview: Text structure is not decoration — it is a deliberate rhetorical decision. This TEK asks students to move past naming structures ("this is compare-contrast") and into explaining why the author built the text that way and what the structure makes possible that another structure wouldn't.
studentFriendly: You can read a text and explain not just how it's organized, but why the author arranged it that way and what that arrangement does to you as a reader.
questionStems: The author's decision to organize paragraphs X–Y using a problem-and-solution structure MOST likely serves to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the idea that the author uses a cause-and-effect structure to argue that —
How does the author's use of chronological order in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the development of the central idea?
The shift in structure between paragraph X and paragraph Y MOST likely signals that the author intends to —
Based on the structure of paragraphs X–Y, the reader can infer that the author's primary purpose is to —
Which organizational choice BEST reveals the author's perspective on [topic]?
The author's use of compare-and-contrast structure in paragraphs X–Y MOST likely emphasizes —
How does the structure of the final paragraph contribute to the author's overall purpose in the selection?
The author MOST likely places [detail/section] at the beginning of the selection in order to —
Which statement BEST describes how the structure of the selection supports the author's argument?

misconceptions.title: Naming the Structure IS the Analysis
misconceptions.body: Students write "the author uses cause and effect" and stop there, treating identification as completion. Teach them that naming is the start, not the finish line. The real question is always: what does this structure let the author do that a different structure wouldn't? Push them to add a "so that" clause to every structural observation they make.

misconceptions.title: All Text Structures Are Equally Neutral
misconceptions.body: Students tend to treat structures like interchangeable containers — the author could have used any structure and arrived at the same effect. Show them counterexamples: a persuasive essay built around problem-solution positions the author as the solver; the same argument built as compare-contrast positions the author as the arbiter. Structure encodes perspective. Teach them to ask whose interests the structure serves.

misconceptions.title: Structure Only Exists at the Paragraph Level
misconceptions.body: Students scan for topic sentences and call it structure analysis. But structure operates at multiple scales simultaneously — sentence to paragraph to section to whole text. Watch for students who miss macro-level architecture because they're stuck at the paragraph level, or who miss sentence-level syntactic patterning because they're only looking at headings and topic sentences.

misconceptions.title: The Author's Purpose Is Always One Thing
misconceptions.body: Students reach for "to inform" or "to persuade" and stop, as if purpose is a single switch. Teach them that purpose can be layered — a text can simultaneously inform AND position the reader to distrust a competing source. The structure often reveals the secondary purpose that the author isn't announcing explicitly. Watch for flat purpose statements that ignore what the structure is actually doing beneath the surface argument.

misconceptions.title: Longer = More Complex = Better Structure
misconceptions.body: Students assume texts with more elaborate structures are doing more sophisticated work. Teach them that elegance matters — a single-thread chronological narrative can be devastating if its sequence is carefully chosen, while a sprawling multi-section comparison can obscure more than it reveals. The question is always whether the structure serves the purpose efficiently, not whether it looks complicated.

assignments.name: Structure Swap
assignments.body: Students take a short published argument (8–12 paragraphs) and rewrite one section using a different organizational structure. They then write a paragraph explaining what changed — what the original structure made possible and what their rewrite lost or gained.

assignments.name: Reverse Engineer the Blueprint
assignments.body: Students receive a transcript-style text with all formatting stripped. Working in pairs, they map the structure, identify the purpose, and write a claim arguing that the structure and purpose are or aren't well matched. Evidence must be cited by paragraph.

assignments.name: Two Texts, One Topic, Different Structures
assignments.body: Students read two texts on the same subject that use different organizational strategies (e.g., one chronological, one problem-solution). They write a comparative analysis explaining what each structure enables and what each sacrifices, and which author's purpose is better served by their chosen structure.

mastery: Identifies the organizational structure operating at both local (paragraph) and global (whole-text) levels without conflating the two.
Explains the connection between a specific structural choice and its rhetorical effect — not just what structure is present, but what it enables the author to accomplish.
Distinguishes between what the author states explicitly as their purpose and what the structure reveals about the actual or secondary purpose.
Produces written analysis that cites specific structural features (sequencing decisions, transitional logic, placement of evidence) as evidence for claims about purpose.
Transfers the analysis across genres — recognizes that structure-as-purpose operates in literary texts, argumentative essays, and informational writing through different but comparable mechanisms.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I 8B asks students to analyze text structure, with the emphasis on recognition and basic explanation of effect. English II holds the same standard language but expects more sophisticated analysis of how structural choices serve layered or implicit purposes — students should be distinguishing what structure does from what the author says it's doing.
Vertical ramp to English III: English III 8B extends this to evaluation — students are expected to judge whether structural choices are effective, not merely explain them.

DOK 3 justification: students must move beyond retrieval and into reasoning about the relationship between structure and purpose, requiring strategic thinking about how multiple structural decisions work together.

STAAR tests this TEK through informational and argumentative paired passages, frequently asking about why an author chose a specific organizational move at a particular point in the text rather than asking students to name the structure.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Evaluate the Author's Use of Print and Graphic Features
expectation: Evaluate the author's use of print and graphic features to achieve specific purposes.
overview: This TEK asks students to move past noticing that a feature exists — a sidebar, a boldface term, a photograph with a caption — and into arguing why an author chose it and whether it actually worked. The evaluative turn is the whole point: did the design decision serve the purpose it was supposed to serve?
studentFriendly: You can look at how a text is laid out — its headings, photos, callout boxes, bold text, charts — and judge whether those design choices actually help the author do what they're trying to do, or whether they get in the way.
questionStems: The author MOST likely includes the photograph in paragraph X to —
How does the use of boldface text in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the reader's understanding of the topic?
Based on the sidebar in paragraph X, the author MOST likely intends to —
Which graphic feature from the selection BEST supports the author's central argument?
The author's use of headers throughout the text MOST likely serves to —
Which statement BEST describes the effect of the caption beneath the image in paragraph X?
The author includes the chart in paragraph X MOST likely because —
How does the author's use of [print/graphic feature] in paragraph X develop the reader's understanding of [concept]?
Based on the layout of paragraphs X–Y, the reader can MOST likely conclude that the author's purpose is to —
The author's decision to place the [feature] alongside the text in paragraph X suggests that —

misconceptions.title: Graphic Features Are Decoration, Not Argument
misconceptions.body: Students treat images, charts, and callout boxes as filler — something the publisher added to break up the wall of text. Teach them that in a professionally produced text, every design choice costs money and earns its spot. Push students to ask: what would be lost if this image weren't here? If the answer is "nothing," they should say so — but that conclusion needs to be earned, not assumed.

misconceptions.title: Evaluation Means Praise
misconceptions.body: When students are asked to "evaluate" a feature, they default to "this was a great choice because it helps the reader." That's not evaluation — that's a compliment. Teach them that evaluating means judging effectiveness against a claimed purpose: the chart SHOULD help readers compare data, but does it? Is it labeled clearly? Does the scale mislead? Watch for students who praise features without evidence.

misconceptions.title: Print Features Are Just Vocabulary Support
misconceptions.body: Students assume boldface and headings exist only to help struggling readers find words or sections. Push them deeper — boldface can signal ideological priority, not just difficulty. A word in bold is a word the author is claiming matters. A heading is an argument about how the topic is organized. Ask: what does this formatting say about what the author thinks is important?

misconceptions.title: All Graphic Features Work the Same Way
misconceptions.body: Students lump photographs, diagrams, maps, timelines, and infographics into one category and apply the same generic analysis ("it gives a visual"). Each type does different rhetorical work. A photograph appeals to emotion and establishes credibility; a diagram shows process; a map grounds argument in geography. Teach them to name the type and name what that type does that others can't.

misconceptions.title: The Feature and the Text Always Agree
misconceptions.body: Students assume print and graphic features simply echo what the text already says. The more interesting cases are when they don't — when a caption reframes a photograph, when a chart reveals a trend the body text downplays, when a callout quote pulls a line out of context. Teach them to read features against the prose, not just alongside it.

assignments.name: Feature Audit
assignments.body: Students select a two-page spread from a nonfiction text or magazine article and catalog every print and graphic feature present. For each feature, they write one sentence stating its apparent purpose and one sentence evaluating whether it achieves that purpose. Discuss as a class which features earned their space.

assignments.name: Removal Argument
assignments.body: Students choose one graphic or print feature from an assigned text and write a short argument (one paragraph) for why the text would be stronger — or weaker — without it. They must cite the author's evident purpose and the feature's effect on the reader.

assignments.name: Redesign with Justification
assignments.body: Students propose replacing one existing feature in a text with a different type (swap a photograph for a diagram, a table for a timeline) and write a paragraph justifying the swap in terms of the author's purpose. This forces them to articulate what each feature type can and cannot do.

assignments.name: Side-by-Side Comparison
assignments.body: Provide two texts covering the same topic — one text-only, one with rich graphic and print features. Students evaluate which achieves the shared purpose more effectively and defend their judgment with specific textual evidence.

mastery: Names the specific type of print or graphic feature (caption, subheading, pull quote, diagram, etc.) rather than using generic language like "a picture" or "the bold part."
Identifies the author's evident purpose for a feature and evaluates whether that purpose is achieved, with evidence from the text and the feature itself.
Distinguishes between features that add meaning and features that restate or merely decorate, and can articulate the difference.
Reads features in tension with the prose — notices when a visual or print choice complicates, reframes, or contradicts the surrounding text.
Produces evaluative claims with qualifying language that reflects genuine judgment rather than reflexive praise or dismissal.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I Std 8C asks students to analyze the author's use of print and graphic features — English II adds the evaluative layer, requiring students to judge whether those features achieve their intended purposes, not merely describe them.

Vertical ramp to English III: English III Std 8C will push students toward evaluating how authors manipulate these features across genres and in texts that embed competing purposes, adding a layer of rhetorical complexity.
DOK 3 justification: evaluation of effectiveness requires students to apply criteria for success, weigh evidence, and produce a reasoned judgment — extending beyond identification or description.

This TEK is STAAR-testable primarily in informational and argumentative reading passages; stems referencing paragraph numbers, image captions, and text layout are common in released items. The stems above reflect that testing context.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze How an Author's Language Shapes Reader Perception
expectation: Analyze how the author's use of language informs and shapes the perception of readers.
overview: This TEK asks students to move past "what does this text say" into "what does this text do to me — and why." The target is the machinery: how specific word choices, syntactic patterns, figurative language, and tone create a particular version of reality in the reader's mind. The author is not neutral, and the reader is not passive; this standard asks students to see both.
studentFriendly: You can read a text and identify the specific language moves an author makes — particular word choices, tones, or structures — and explain how those moves shape what readers think, feel, or believe about the subject.
questionStems: The author's use of the word [word] in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to make the reader feel —
