Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates the author's attempt to shape the reader's perception of [subject]?
How does the author's use of [figurative language/diction/syntax] in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the reader's understanding of —
The author MOST likely chooses to describe [subject] as [phrase] in order to —
Based on paragraph X, what is the MOST likely effect of the author's language on the reader?
The author's word choice in paragraph X suggests that the reader is MOST likely expected to view [subject] as —
Which detail from the selection BEST supports the inference that the author wants the reader to —
The author's use of [repetition/imagery/sentence structure] throughout the selection is MOST likely intended to —
How does the language in paragraph X inform the reader's perception of [character/event/idea]?
The tone established by the author's language in paragraphs X–Y MOST nearly suggests that —

misconceptions.title: Language Is Just Decoration
misconceptions.body: Students treat word choice as ornament — the author picked "devastating" instead of "bad" to sound smart, full stop. Push them further: "devastating" positions the reader to feel loss and scale, not just negativity. Teach them that every significant word choice is also a perception choice — it tells the reader what emotional and intellectual frame to bring.

misconceptions.title: "The Author Wanted Us to Feel Sad" Is an Analysis
misconceptions.body: Students confuse naming an effect with analyzing it. Saying the author "makes the reader feel sad" is a starting point, not an endpoint. Require the next sentence: which specific language creates that effect, and how does it work mechanically? The effect is the claim; the language is the evidence; the how is the analysis.

misconceptions.title: Neutral Language Exists
misconceptions.body: Students assume that plain, unadorned prose is unbiased and that rhetorical language is the only kind that manipulates perception. Challenge this directly: a news article that calls protesters "rioters" versus "demonstrators" is doing perception work in apparently neutral register. Watch for students who only flag flowery or figurative language and miss the quiet freight carried by everyday word selection.

misconceptions.title: Analyzing Language Means Agreeing With the Effect
misconceptions.body: Students sometimes resist analyzing language that works on them — if a passage's framing matches their views, they'll say "the author is just telling the truth." Teach them that analysis and agreement are separate acts. The author could be completely right AND still be using language strategically to shape perception. Both things can be true.

misconceptions.title: Every Word in the Text Is Worth Analyzing
misconceptions.body: Students either over-analyze — finding hidden meaning in conjunctions — or under-analyze — stopping at the most obvious example and calling it done. Teach triage: the language worth analyzing is language that does unexpected or disproportionate work. A single adjective that reframes an entire noun phrase is worth more than a paragraph of neutral exposition.

assignments.name: Side-by-Side Perception Map
assignments.body: Present two texts on the same subject (e.g., two accounts of the same historical event, or two descriptions of the same person). Students identify five specific language choices in each text and chart how each choice shifts the reader's perception. Discussion: could both texts be "accurate" while constructing entirely different realities?

assignments.name: Rewrite to Reframe
assignments.body: Students take a paragraph from a text studied in class and rewrite it using opposite perception goals — if the original language builds sympathy, rewrite it to build suspicion; if it builds authority, rewrite it to undermine. Students then annotate their revision, naming each language swap and its intended perceptual effect.

assignments.name: The Invisible Word
assignments.body: Students select one word or phrase from an assigned text that seems neutral but is doing significant perceptual work. They write a one-page argument explaining what perception the word creates, why that word (rather than an obvious synonym) was chosen, and what the reader would perceive differently if the author had chosen otherwise.

mastery: Identifies specific language choices — diction, syntax, figurative language, tone — and explains the perceptual effect of each, not just its presence.
Distinguishes between what a text states and what a text constructs in the reader — can articulate the gap between literal content and perceived meaning.
Recognizes perception-shaping language in both rhetorically charged and apparently neutral prose.
Separates personal agreement or disagreement with an author's position from analysis of how the author's language operates on a reader.
Supports claims about perceptual effect with direct textual evidence and precise explanation of the mechanism — not just assertion.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I 8D asks students to analyze how language informs and shapes perception — English II holds the same standard language but expects greater sophistication in the texts analyzed and greater precision in the explanation of mechanism. The ramp is in complexity of text and depth of reasoning, not in the framing of the skill itself.

Vertical ramp to English III: English III 8D adds the expectation that students evaluate the effect of language — moving from analysis of how language shapes perception to judgment about whether those effects are achieved or appropriate.

DOK 3 justification: the skill requires students to explain relationships between specific textual choices and cognitive/emotional effects on readers — going beyond retrieval (DOK 1–2) into strategic analysis of mechanism.

This standard sits at the intersection of literary analysis and rhetorical awareness; it is taught in both fiction and nonfiction contexts and links directly to composition standards (students who understand how language shapes perception in reading are better positioned to make intentional language choices in writing).
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Literary Devices: Irony, Sarcasm, and Motif
expectation: Analyze the use of literary devices such as irony, sarcasm, and motif to achieve specific purposes.
overview: This TEK asks students to move past naming devices and into explaining what those devices are doing — why an author reaches for irony here, how the recurring motif is accumulating meaning, what the sarcasm is costing the speaker in terms of credibility or tone. The work is purposive: every device is in service of something, and students have to name that something.
studentFriendly: You can read a story, essay, or speech and explain not just when an author uses irony, sarcasm, or a repeated image — but why it's there and what it accomplishes that a straight statement couldn't.
questionStems: The author's use of irony in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates dramatic irony?
The sarcasm in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
How does the motif of [image/symbol] develop across the selection?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the author uses irony to create which of the following effects?
The repetition of [motif] throughout the selection MOST likely contributes to —
Which statement BEST describes the purpose of the sarcasm in lines X–Y?
The author's use of situational irony in paragraph X suggests that —
How does the motif introduced in paragraph X change in meaning by paragraph Y?
The literary devices used in paragraphs X–Y MOST likely help the author achieve which purpose?

misconceptions.title: Irony = Anything Surprising
misconceptions.body: Students slap the word "irony" onto any twist, coincidence, or reversal that catches them off guard. Teach them the distinctions matter: verbal irony is a gap between what is said and what is meant; situational irony is a gap between what is expected and what happens; dramatic irony is a gap between what the character knows and what the reader knows. Watch for students who call every surprise "ironic" — push them to name which gap they're actually seeing and where the text establishes the expectation that makes the gap land.

misconceptions.title: Sarcasm Is Just Rudeness on the Page
misconceptions.body: Students read sarcasm as tone-deaf or mean and stop there — they note it exists but can't say what it's doing. Reframe this: sarcasm is a rhetorical choice that costs the speaker something (likability, directness, credibility) while gaining something else (force, wit, distance, critique). Teach them to ask what the author trades away by choosing sarcasm instead of a sincere statement, and what they gain. That trade-off is the analysis.

misconceptions.title: Motif Equals Symbol
misconceptions.body: Students conflate motif with symbol and treat the two as interchangeable. The difference is structural: a symbol is a single image that carries meaning; a motif is a recurring element whose meaning accumulates and shifts across the work. Push them to track how the motif changes — a bird in chapter one is not doing the same work as a bird in chapter twelve. Teach them motif analysis as a timeline, not a definition.

misconceptions.title: The Device Exists; Therefore the Purpose Is "Emphasis"
misconceptions.body: When students can't name a specific purpose, they default to "to emphasize" or "to show." These are not purposes — they're placeholders. Watch for this in short-answer responses. Train them to complete the move: emphasize WHAT? Show the reader WHAT? The analysis isn't done until the device is connected to a specific idea the text is building or a specific effect on the reader.

misconceptions.title: All Three Devices Work the Same Way
misconceptions.body: Students who understand one device well will misapply its logic to the others. A student who nails irony analysis will sometimes treat motif like a one-time signal rather than a pattern, or mistake sarcastic tone for dramatic irony. Teach the three as distinct tools with distinct jobs, and build comparison tasks that require distinguishing between them — not just identifying all three in isolation.

assignments.name: Device Audit
assignments.body: Students select one passage (8–12 paragraphs) and produce a three-column annotation: column one for irony, column two for sarcasm, column three for motif. For each marked moment, they write one sentence explaining the effect — not naming the device, but naming what the device accomplishes.

assignments.name: Motif Tracker
assignments.body: Students read a full short story or a chapter-length excerpt and chart every appearance of a central motif. For each entry they note: what form does it take, what does it mean here, how has its meaning shifted from the last appearance? Final step: a one-paragraph synthesis of what the motif reveals by the end.

assignments.name: Rewrite Without the Device
assignments.body: Students take a passage that deploys irony or sarcasm and rewrite it sincerely. Then they compare: what is lost? What is gained? What does the original say about the author's relationship to the reader? This comparative task makes the rhetorical choice visible.

assignments.name: Purpose Defense
assignments.body: Students write a constructed response arguing for a specific purpose behind one literary device in a shared text. They must cite at least two moments from the text and explain the connection between the device and the author's larger aims. Responses are shared and challenged in small groups.

mastery: Names the specific type of irony present and explains the gap (expectation vs. reality, statement vs. meaning, or character knowledge vs. reader knowledge) that creates it.
Identifies sarcasm and explains the rhetorical trade-off — what the author gains by choosing sarcasm over sincerity in that moment.
Tracks a motif across multiple moments in a text and articulates how its meaning accumulates or shifts rather than treating it as a static symbol.
Connects each literary device to a specific authorial purpose — not "to emphasize" or "to show," but a named idea or effect the text is building.
Distinguishes among all three devices when they appear in proximity, without collapsing them into each other.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I 8E asks students to analyze literary devices including irony, sarcasm, and motif — English II holds the same device list, so the ramp is in depth and independence. English II students are expected to move from recognition plus surface-level purpose into sustained purposive analysis: not just "the author uses irony," but what specific work that irony is doing in context of the whole.

Vertical ramp to English III: English III 8E extends into more complex rhetorical and structural craft (parallelism, juxtaposition, rhetorical devices in argument). The motif-tracking and purposive-analysis habits built here transfer directly.

DOK 3 justification: purposive analysis requires strategic thinking — students must draw inferences about authorial intent that go beyond text-level identification and require synthesis across multiple moments in the text.

STAAR tests this TEK through literary and expository reading passages. Stems typically embed the device name or present a line that enacts the device and ask about effect or purpose. Motif questions may reference multiple paragraphs explicitly.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze How Diction and Syntax Contribute to Mood, Voice, and Tone
expectation: Analyze how the author's diction and syntax contribute to the mood, voice, and tone of a text.
overview: This TEK asks students to move below the surface of what a text says and into how the text feels — and who is doing the feeling. Diction and syntax are the two primary levers authors pull to create atmosphere (mood), personality (voice), and attitude (tone), and English II students need to name what's being pulled and why it matters.
studentFriendly: You can look at a writer's specific word choices and sentence structures and explain how they create the emotional atmosphere of the text, the writer's personality coming through the page, and the writer's attitude toward the subject.
questionStems: The author's use of [word/phrase] in paragraph X MOST likely contributes to a mood of —
Which sentence from paragraph X BEST illustrates the narrator's voice?
The author's diction in paragraphs X–Y is MOST nearly described as —
How does the syntax in paragraph X contribute to the tone of the passage?
The shift in the author's diction between paragraphs X and Y MOST likely signals —
Which phrase from the selection BEST supports the inference that the narrator's tone is [tone]?
The author's use of [syntactic structure — e.g., short declarative sentences / a series of questions] in paragraph X contributes to the mood by —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the author's voice is BEST described as —
The repetition of [word/phrase] throughout the passage MOST likely affects the mood by —
Which detail from paragraph X BEST develops the author's tone toward [subject/character]?

misconceptions.title: Mood, Tone, and Voice Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students conflate all three and use them interchangeably, which collapses three distinct analytical tools into one vague gesture. Teach them the clean distinction: mood is what the reader feels, tone is the author's attitude toward the subject, and voice is the author's personality in the prose. A text can have a melancholy mood, a sardonic tone, and a clipped, spare voice — all three coexisting and all three worth naming separately. Run the same paragraph through all three lenses until students stop defaulting to "the tone is sad."

misconceptions.title: Diction Analysis Means Finding the Hardest Words
misconceptions.body: Students think analyzing diction means circling the longest or most unusual vocabulary. Push them toward the words doing quiet, structural work — definite vs. indefinite articles, the choice of "child" vs. "kid" vs. "boy," the selection of "walked" vs. "shuffled" vs. "crept." The most revealing diction is often invisible until someone points at it. Teach them: the word that didn't have to be chosen this way is the one worth examining.

misconceptions.title: Syntax Is Only About Sentence Length
misconceptions.body: When asked about syntax, students write "the sentences are short" or "the author uses long sentences" and stop. That's a description, not an analysis. Push deeper: does a series of short declaratives create a sense of control or of numbness? Does a long, subordinated sentence mimic the character's overwhelming thought? Where does the grammatical subject appear — and is agency being assigned or avoided? Syntax is architecture; teach them to read the structure, not just measure it.

misconceptions.title: Tone Is What the Author Feels About the Story Generally
misconceptions.body: Students read tone as a global, static property — "the tone of this book is dark" — rather than a precise, local, shifting quality. Tone can change paragraph to paragraph, scene to scene, even sentence to sentence. Watch for students who assign one adjective to the whole text and never revisit it. Teach them to track tone as a moving target: where does it shift, what diction or syntax triggers the shift, and what does that reveal about the author's relationship to the material at that moment?

misconceptions.title: Voice Is the Narrator's Personality, Not the Author's Choice
misconceptions.body: Students attribute voice to character as if voice just happens — as if the narrator "sounds like that" organically. Reframe this as a craft decision: the author chose every contraction, every sentence fragment, every moment of direct address. Voice is not personality that leaked onto the page; it is a construction. Watch for students who write "the narrator has a casual voice" without connecting that casualness to any specific lexical or syntactic choice. The analysis lives in the specifics of how that voice was built.

assignments.name: Three-Lens Annotation
assignments.body: Students read a single page of prose and annotate with three colors: one for diction contributing to mood, one for diction contributing to tone, and one for syntactic choices contributing to voice. They write a one-paragraph synthesis explaining how the three interact in that passage.

assignments.name: Author Autopsy
assignments.body: Students select a passage of 150–250 words from a class text and rewrite it twice — once with diction shifted toward a different tone, once with syntax restructured to create a different mood — without changing the plot events. Then they write a short explanation of what changed and why.

assignments.name: Side-by-Side Voice Study
assignments.body: Students read two passages on the same subject from two different authors (e.g., two war memoirs, two nature essays). They produce a written comparison identifying the specific diction and syntactic choices that create each author's distinct voice and tone. Minimum two textual examples per author.

assignments.name: Tone Shift Tracker
assignments.body: Students chart the tone of a longer text chapter-by-chapter or section-by-section, identifying the exact sentence or moment where the tone shifts and the specific language feature that signals the change. Final product: a visual tracker plus a paragraph explaining the most significant shift.

mastery: Distinguishes consistently among mood, tone, and voice as three separate analytical categories and applies each to specific textual evidence.
Identifies diction choices — including ordinary, quiet words — and connects them directly to a named effect on mood, tone, or voice.
Analyzes syntactic features beyond sentence length: subject placement, clause order, use of fragments, parallelism, passive construction, and their rhetorical effects.
Tracks tone as a shifting quality across a text rather than a fixed descriptor applied globally.
Explains how diction and syntax work together — not separately — to produce a unified effect in a passage.
notes: Vertical ramp: English I 8(F) introduces the concept that diction and syntax shape meaning. English II narrows the target — students now name the specific effects (mood, voice, tone) rather than meaning in general. English III extends this into evaluative and rhetorical territory, asking students to assess how effectively these choices work and to critique them. The English II expectation sits squarely at DOK 3: students must analyze relationships between specific language features and named affective/rhetorical outcomes.

DOK 3 justification: analysis requires students to move beyond identifying diction/syntax features (DOK 2) to explaining how those features produce effects across three distinct dimensions (mood, voice, tone) — a multi-causal relationship requiring extended reasoning.

STAAR regularly tests this TEK through questions that ask students to explain how a specific word or sentence contributes to tone, infer mood from descriptive language, or identify a shift in the narrator's attitude. Stems in this entry mirror that assessment pattern.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Rhetorical Devices and Logical Fallacies
expectation: Analyze the purpose of rhetorical devices such as appeals, antithesis, parallelism, and shifts and the effects of logical fallacies.
overview: This is where argument analysis gets precise. Students move from identifying rhetorical moves to explaining WHY a writer deploys them — and from nodding along with a text to recognizing when its reasoning has quietly collapsed. The payoff is readers who can't be fooled by a well-dressed bad argument.
studentFriendly: You can read a persuasive speech or essay and explain exactly what rhetorical moves the writer is making, why those moves work on an audience, and where the argument breaks down into manipulation or faulty reasoning.
questionStems: The author's use of parallelism in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates the author's use of antithesis?
The author's shift in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the author primarily appeals to the audience's sense of —
The reasoning in paragraph X MOST nearly represents which logical fallacy?
How does the author's use of parallelism in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the development of the central argument?
Which statement BEST describes the effect of the emotional appeal in paragraph X?
The author uses antithesis in paragraph X MOST likely to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST demonstrates a logical fallacy that weakens the author's argument?
Based on the selection, the author's rhetorical strategy in paragraph X suggests that the intended audience —

misconceptions.title: Appeals Are Always Emotional — the Other Kinds Don't Count
misconceptions.body: Students default to "they used emotion" for every rhetorical device and forget that logos and ethos are doing just as much work. Watch for students who label every appeal as pathos and leave the analysis shallow. Teach them to ask: is the writer building credibility, building logic, or building feeling — and then to find evidence for whichever it actually is. All three types show up on the same page; only one label is usually right.

misconceptions.title: Parallelism and Repetition Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students conflate these because both involve repeated elements. Push them to see the difference: repetition is the same word or phrase coming back; parallelism is the same grammatical structure carrying different content. The purpose shifts accordingly — repetition hammers a single idea, parallelism creates the feeling that ideas of equal weight are being balanced. Sentences that look similar are not automatically parallel; the grammar has to match.

misconceptions.title: Antithesis Just Means "Opposite"
misconceptions.body: Students hear "antithesis" and write "the author uses opposites." That's not the move — antithesis is a deliberate structural contrast placed side by side so the difference lands harder than either idea alone would. Teach them to look for the construction: not just that two contrasting ideas appear in the same text, but that they are deliberately set against each other in tight grammatical proximity to produce a specific rhetorical effect. "Ask not what your country can do for you" is not just about opposites; the structure IS the argument.

misconceptions.title: Logical Fallacies Only Matter When the Writer Is Wrong
misconceptions.body: Students assume that if they agree with the conclusion, the reasoning must be fine. The most important skill here is flagging fallacies in arguments whose conclusions are appealing — that's where readers are most vulnerable. Teach them that a fallacy is a flaw in the reasoning, not a flaw in the belief. A writer can reach a true conclusion through terrible logic, and a reader who can't see that is easy to manipulate.

misconceptions.title: Shifts Are Just Transitions
misconceptions.body: Students treat shifts as formatting moves — "here the author changes topics." Reframe this: a shift is a deliberate tonal, structural, or rhetorical gear change that signals something important about where the argument is going. A shift from logos to pathos near the end of a speech is a strategic choice, not a paragraph break. Teach them to ask: what changed, why here, and what does the writer want the reader to feel at the moment of the shift?

assignments.name: Rhetorical Device Hunt
assignments.body: Students receive a one-page excerpt from a political speech or op-ed and annotate it for all four device types — appeals, antithesis, parallelism, shifts. For each annotation, they write one sentence explaining the PURPOSE of the device, not just naming it. Annotation + brief written summary.

assignments.name: Fallacy Dossier
assignments.body: Students collect three examples of logical fallacies from current media (ads, op-eds, social media posts). For each, they identify the fallacy type, quote the specific language, and explain in two sentences what the fallacy assumes the reader won't notice. Class shares and debriefs.

assignments.name: Same Claim, Different Devices
assignments.body: Give students a simple argument ("This school should change its schedule"). Students must write three versions of one paragraph advancing the same claim — one using parallelism, one using antithesis, one using an appeal of their choice. Then they analyze which version is most persuasive and why.

assignments.name: Fallacy vs. Device Showdown
assignments.body: Give students two speeches on the same topic — one rhetorically sophisticated, one riddled with fallacies. Students write a comparative analysis: which persuades better, which persuades more honestly, and what the difference tells us about the writer's assumptions about the audience.

mastery: Names the specific rhetorical device used and explains its purpose in the argument — not just its presence.
Distinguishes between appeals (ethos, logos, pathos) and identifies which type is operative in a given passage, with textual evidence.
Identifies a logical fallacy by type and explains how it undermines the reasoning, independent of whether the conclusion is appealing.
Recognizes antithesis as a structural move, not just the presence of contrasting ideas.
Tracks rhetorical shifts across a text and explains what strategic work the shift is doing at that moment in the argument.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I 8G asks students to analyze rhetorical devices and their effects. English II adds explicit vocabulary (antithesis, shifts) and introduces logical fallacies as a named counterpart to intentional rhetorical craft — the skill ramps from "what does this device do?" to "is this device doing honest argumentative work or covering for weak reasoning?"

Vertical ramp to English III: English III 8G adds critique of rhetorical choices and extended evaluation of how devices interact across a full text. English II builds the analytical vocabulary; English III expects students to evaluate it.
DOK 3 justification: students must reason about purpose and effect within and across texts, not just identify or define devices — requires strategic thinking rather than recall.

STAAR tests this TEK through argumentative and informational paired selections; stems typically ask students to analyze the purpose or effect of a specific passage, identify the appeal type operative in a paragraph, or choose which sentence best illustrates a named device.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Plan a Piece of Writing Appropriate for Various Purposes and Audiences
expectation: Plan a piece of writing appropriate for various purposes and audiences by generating ideas through a range of strategies such as brainstorming, journaling, reading, or discussing.
overview: Planning is where most sophomore writing actually falls apart — students skip it or do it in thirty seconds, then wonder why the draft feels aimless. This TEK insists that idea generation is a real step, not a formality, and that the plan must be tethered to both purpose (what the writing is supposed to do) and audience (who it's supposed to do it to).
studentFriendly: You can figure out what you want to say, why you're saying it, and who you're saying it to — before you write a single real sentence — using strategies like brainstorming, journaling, reading around the topic, or talking it through with someone.
questionStems: Read the brainstorming notes in the box. Which idea from the notes would BEST support an argumentative essay written for a school board audience?
A student is planning an essay about [topic]. Which prewriting strategy would MOST likely help the student identify the strongest evidence for a general audience?
Based on the planning notes in paragraph 1, what is the student's MOST likely purpose for writing?
Which revision to the student's outline would BEST align the plan with the intended audience of younger students?
The student's journal entry in paragraph 2 suggests that the MOST appropriate form for this piece of writing would be —
Which detail from the brainstorming list would MOST likely be cut when writing for a formal academic audience rather than peers?
A student wants to persuade a city council to fund a community program. Which planning step would MOST likely strengthen the argument before drafting begins?
Based on the notes in paragraphs 1–3, what gap in the student's planning would MOST likely weaken the final draft?
Which sentence from the student's journal notes BEST indicates a clear purpose for the piece?
The student's outline includes details appropriate for an expert audience. Which change would MOST likely make the plan better suited for a general reader?

misconceptions.title: Planning Means Making an Outline — Full Stop
misconceptions.body: Students treat "prewriting" and "outline" as synonyms, so they jot a quick Roman-numeral list and consider themselves done. The TEK names brainstorming, journaling, reading, and discussing for a reason — the outline is one possible output of planning, not planning itself. Push students to actually generate ideas before organizing them; the outline should be built from material, not used as a substitute for it.

misconceptions.title: Purpose and Audience Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: When asked to consider their audience, students often restate their purpose ("I'm writing to inform people about climate change"), and vice versa. Teach them the distinction cleanly: purpose answers "what should this writing do?" and audience answers "who is it doing it to, and what do they already know?" A student writing to inform a five-year-old and writing to inform a scientist have the same purpose but wildly different plans.

misconceptions.title: Good Ideas Come Fast or They Don't Come at All
misconceptions.body: Students who stall during brainstorming conclude they have nothing to say and stop generating. Watch for the ten-words-and-done problem — students who treat the first ideas as the real ideas because more effort feels like evidence of weakness. Teach them that brainstorming is a volume game: the good idea is usually buried under the obvious ones, and you have to write through the obvious ones to reach it.

misconceptions.title: Discussing Counts Only If You Write It Down Immediately
misconceptions.body: Some students talk productively with a partner and then leave the conversation in the air — nothing recorded, nothing to draft from. Others dismiss discussion entirely as "not real work." Teach them that a strong planning conversation is generative but perishable: the move is to annotate, jot, or voice-memo during or immediately after. The strategy isn't the talking; it's what you capture from it.

misconceptions.title: The Plan Is Sacred — Changing It Means You Failed
misconceptions.body: Once students commit to a plan, many treat any departure from it as failure rather than discovery. They'll force a draft to match a bad plan rather than revise the plan when the draft shows them something better. Teach them early: the plan exists to serve the writing, not the other way around. A plan you outgrow is a plan that did its job.

assignments.name: Idea Excavation
assignments.body: Students are given a broad topic and must generate ideas using three different strategies — brainstorming, a ten-minute journal entry, and a five-minute discussion with a partner — then compare what each strategy surfaced. They write a one-paragraph reflection on which strategy produced the most usable material and why.

assignments.name: Audience Pivot
assignments.body: Students take a single set of brainstorming notes and produce two different planning outlines: one aimed at peers their own age and one aimed at a formal adult audience (a principal, an editor, a city council). They annotate the differences in detail selection and tone — then explain which details they cut and why.

assignments.name: Plan Autopsy
assignments.body: Students receive a weak student-written essay (teacher-generated or anonymized) and work backward to reconstruct what the planning must have looked like. They identify where the planning broke down — unclear purpose, wrong audience assumptions, underdeveloped ideas — and propose a stronger plan the student could have used instead.

mastery: Produces planning materials (brainstorm, journal, notes, or outline) that reflect genuine idea generation — not a three-word list dressed up as prewriting.
Identifies a clear purpose for the piece and makes decisions about detail, tone, and structure that are consistent with that purpose.
Adjusts the plan's content and framing based on who the audience is — what they know, what they need, and what would persuade or engage them.
Uses at least one idea-generation strategy beyond outlining and can articulate why that strategy helped or what it surfaced.
Treats the plan as a working document — willing to revise or abandon sections when drafting reveals a better direction.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I 9A requires the same planning and idea-generation strategies — English II holds the same expectation but the implicit bar rises. By English II, students are expected to handle a wider range of purposes and audiences, including argumentative writing with more complex audience awareness. The skill is the same; the demand on judgment increases.

Vertical ramp to English III: English III 9A adds explicit expectation of rhetorical sophistication in planning — students are expected to anticipate counterarguments and plan accordingly. English II is the bridge between basic prewriting (English I) and rhetorically purposeful planning (English III).

DOK 2 justification: planning requires students to apply known strategies to new writing contexts, selecting and organizing ideas in response to purpose and audience — consistent with DOK 2 skill/concept application.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is tested indirectly through Composition questions that ask students to evaluate or improve planning decisions embedded in a student draft scenario. The stems above mirror that framing.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Develop Drafts Using an Organizing Structure
expectation: Develop drafts into a focused, structured, and coherent piece of writing in timed and open-ended situations by using an organizing structure appropriate to purpose, audience, topic, and context.
overview: This TEK is where students stop writing whatever comes to mind and start making architectural decisions — choosing a structure because it fits the purpose and the reader, not because it's the one they always use. The challenge is getting students to match form to function before they start drafting, not after.
studentFriendly: You can look at a writing task, decide which organizational structure actually fits what you're trying to do and who you're writing for, and then build your draft around that structure from the start.
questionStems: Which organizational structure BEST supports the purpose of the student's draft?
Read paragraph X. The student MOST likely organized this paragraph using a [comparison/problem-solution/chronological] structure in order to —
Which revision to the draft's structure would MOST improve its coherence?
Based on the draft, which organizing strategy does the student MOST likely use to develop the central idea?
The student wants to revise paragraph X to better address the intended audience. Which revision BEST accomplishes this goal?
Which sentence BEST serves as a transition that supports the draft's organizational structure?
The student wants to reorganize paragraphs X–Y so that the argument develops more logically. Which sequence BEST accomplishes this goal?
Read the draft. Which organizational structure does the student use in paragraphs X–Z, and how does it contribute to the overall focus of the piece?
The student's draft addresses [topic]. Which organizing structure would MOST effectively serve the student's purpose of [informing/persuading/narrating]?
Which sentence, if added to paragraph X, would BEST reinforce the organizational structure the student has established?

misconceptions.title: The Five-Paragraph Form Fits Every Task
misconceptions.body: Students treat the five-paragraph essay as a universal container and pour every task into it regardless of purpose or context. They write a five-paragraph narrative, a five-paragraph persuasion, a five-paragraph analysis — same shell, different labels. Teach them that structure is a decision, not a template: a problem-solution op-ed reads nothing like a personal narrative, and the difference isn't in word count — it's in how the parts relate to each other.

misconceptions.title: "Organized" Means "Has an Introduction and Conclusion"
misconceptions.body: When students hear "organized structure," they check for an intro and conclusion and call it done. Watch for drafts with a recognizable opening and closing but a middle section that drifts — three paragraphs that could appear in any order without losing meaning. Real structure means each section does a specific job and the sequence is not interchangeable.

misconceptions.title: Audience Is a Politeness Variable, Not a Structural One
misconceptions.body: Students think "appropriate for audience" means adjusting vocabulary or tone — drop the slang, use formal language. They rarely consider that audience determines what needs to be explained, in what order, and at what level of detail. Push them to ask: what does THIS reader already know? What does THIS reader need before they can follow the next move? Those answers change structure, not just diction.

misconceptions.title: Structure Is Chosen Before Drafting, Not Discovered After
misconceptions.body: Students draft freely, then look for structure in what they've already written. The result is post-hoc rationalization — they label what they have rather than build what works. Teach them to name the structure before the first sentence: "I'm going to use cause-effect because I'm explaining why something happened, not just that it happened." That decision shapes everything that comes after.

misconceptions.title: Timed Writing Gets a Pass on Structure
misconceptions.body: In timed situations — a 45-minute class write, an on-demand prompt — students treat structure as a luxury they don't have time for. They start with "In today's society" and sprint to the end. Teach them that a two-minute planning step (claim, two or three moves, connective logic) produces a more coherent draft than six minutes of unplanned prose. Structure under time pressure is a skill, not a contradiction.

assignments.name: Structure Before You Draft
assignments.body: Students receive a writing prompt and spend the first five minutes completing a structural blueprint: purpose, audience, organizational strategy, and a one-line description of what each body section will do. Drafting begins only after the blueprint is complete. Debrief: did the draft follow the plan? Where did it drift?

assignments.name: Scrambled Draft Sort
assignments.body: Students receive a well-organized published piece cut into paragraphs, scrambled. They must reassemble it and label the organizational structure used. Then they write a short paragraph explaining WHY they believe the author chose that structure for that purpose and audience.

assignments.name: Same Topic, Two Structures
assignments.body: Students write two short drafts (one page each) on the same topic — one using chronological order, one using problem-solution. They then write a paragraph arguing which structure better serves the purpose. Forces explicit thinking about the match between form and function.

assignments.name: Timed Structure Practice
assignments.body: Students complete a timed write (40 minutes) on a released prompt. Before they write a single sentence, they must submit a written structural plan (three lines maximum). Plans are collected. After the timed write, students compare their plan to their draft and note where the structure held and where it collapsed.

mastery: Identifies the purpose, audience, and context of a writing task and selects an organizational structure that fits those conditions before drafting.
Produces a draft in which each section performs a distinct function and the sequence is not interchangeable — moving a body paragraph would visibly break the logic.
Makes structural decisions under timed conditions, using a brief planning step to establish a coherent organizational framework before writing.
Explains in revision notes or a writer's memo why the chosen structure serves the piece's purpose, not just that a structure exists.
Adjusts structure across genres — narrates chronologically, argues by claim-and-evidence, informs by category or cause-effect — rather than applying one default form to every task.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I 9B(i) establishes the same skill — organizing structure appropriate to purpose, audience, topic, and context. English II expects greater independence in selecting and sustaining structure across longer, more complex pieces, and in timed conditions without teacher scaffolding.

Vertical ramp to English III: English III 9B(i) extends the same expectation into more sophisticated genres and rhetorical contexts, with increasing demands on structural complexity and argumentation.

DOK 3 justification: selecting an organizing structure requires strategic thinking — students must evaluate options against multiple variables (purpose, audience, topic, context) and apply the decision to produce a coherent draft. This is beyond recall or skill application in isolation.

STAAR testing note: this sub-skill is tested most directly through the STAAR Writing revising task — students are asked which revision to a draft's structure, sequence, or organizational strategy best serves the stated purpose or audience. Stems in this entry mirror that testing context.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Develop Drafts with Engaging Ideas, Specific Details, and Commentary
expectation: Develop drafts into a focused, structured, and coherent piece of writing in timed and open-ended situations by developing an engaging idea reflecting depth of thought with specific details, examples, and commentary.
overview: This is where student writing either goes shallow or goes somewhere. The skill is not "include details" — it is developing one engaging idea deeply enough that the thinking behind it shows. Teachers should watch for the difference between a draft that mentions details and a draft that uses them to push an argument or insight forward.
studentFriendly: You can take a draft idea and develop it into something that actually goes somewhere — using specific details, real examples, and your own commentary to show that you've thought about it past the surface level.
questionStems: Which sentence from the draft BEST develops the writer's central idea with specific detail?
The writer includes the example in paragraph X MOST likely to —
Which revision of the underlined sentence would MOST effectively add depth to the writer's argument?
The commentary in paragraph X contributes to the draft's development MOST by —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the writer's central idea is BEST supported by —
Which sentence MOST clearly reflects the writer's own thinking rather than a restatement of fact?
The writer's use of the detail in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Which addition to paragraph X would MOST strengthen the writer's claim with specific evidence?
The writer's commentary following the example in paragraph X serves MOST to —
Which sentence from the draft BEST demonstrates depth of thought about the writer's central idea?

misconceptions.title: Details Are Decoration, Not Evidence
misconceptions.body: Students treat specific details as garnish — they drop in a statistic or a scene and move on, expecting the detail to do the argumentative work by itself. Teach them that a detail without commentary is just information; the sentence after the evidence is where the real writing happens. Push them to ask "so what?" every time they cite an example.

misconceptions.title: "Engaging" Means Emotional or Dramatic
misconceptions.body: Students conflate an engaging idea with a dramatic hook or a heart-tugging story. Watch for opening paragraphs that perform emotion but then flatten into generic claims. An engaging idea is one that has a surprising angle, a genuine tension, or an insight the reader hasn't already assumed — not necessarily one with a high emotional temperature.

misconceptions.title: Depth Means Length
misconceptions.body: When told their draft lacks depth, students add paragraphs. The result is a longer draft with the same shallow thinking spread thinner. Teach them that depth lives in the quality of the connection between a claim, its evidence, and the commentary — one developed paragraph beats three underdeveloped ones every time.

misconceptions.title: Commentary Means Opinion
misconceptions.body: Students confuse commentary with personal reaction ("I think this is important") and assume any first-person sentence counts. Real commentary explains the significance of the detail in relation to the argument — it answers "what does this example prove about the idea?" Teach them to test each commentary sentence: if it could be deleted without losing any logic, it's not doing the job.

misconceptions.title: Specific Details Mean Lots of Details
misconceptions.body: Students interpret "specific" as "many" and load their drafts with lists of examples that never connect. Watch for the student who gives five examples in a paragraph and analyzes none of them. Teach them that one fully developed example with sustained commentary does more work than five examples strung together by "also" and "another example of this is."

assignments.name: The So-What Chain
assignments.body: Students write one claim sentence, then follow it with a detail, then a commentary sentence, then a second commentary sentence that goes one step further. They may not move to a new paragraph until the chain has at least three links. Share and discuss where the thinking actually deepened.

assignments.name: Detail Audit
assignments.body: Students highlight every example or detail in their draft in one color, then highlight every commentary sentence in another. If any detail has no following commentary, they must add it before the draft moves forward. Teachers collect drafts to check the ratio.

assignments.name: One Idea, Full Depth
assignments.body: Students take a single paragraph from a weak draft and expand it into a full page — same idea, same example, but developed with layers of commentary and additional specific evidence. They then compare the original and the revision and write two sentences explaining what changed.

assignments.name: Commentary Swap
assignments.body: Partners exchange drafts and write commentary sentences for each other's details. The writer then reads the partner's commentary and decides: is that what I meant? If not, they revise their own commentary to say it more precisely.

mastery: Selects one central idea and develops it with multiple layers of evidence and commentary rather than spreading across several underdeveloped claims.
Writes commentary that explains the relationship between a detail and the central argument — not just reaction or restatement.
Chooses specific, concrete examples rather than vague references, and identifies precisely what each example is meant to prove.
Produces drafts where depth is visible within paragraphs, not just across the length of the piece.
Revises independently toward greater specificity and insight when given feedback that a draft is "thin."
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I 9(B)(ii) asks students to develop an engaging idea with specific details and examples. English II adds "commentary" and "depth of thought" as explicit targets — the skill moves from populating a draft with evidence to analyzing what that evidence means within the argument.

Vertical ramp to English III: English III 9(B)(ii) holds the same expectation but the context is increasingly complex texts and more sophisticated argumentative tasks; the depth of thought demanded rises with the reading difficulty of the source material.

DOK 3 justification: students must apply drafting strategies and analyze the relationship between evidence and claim — not just recall or reproduce; the commentary requirement demands strategic thinking about how ideas connect.

STAAR analog note: this sub-skill surfaces on the STAAR Expository and Persuasive writing prompts as holistic scoring for "development of ideas" — the question stems above mirror how revision and editing tasks on the STAAR writing section assess whether a student can identify strong versus weak development within a sample draft.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Revise Drafts for Clarity, Development, Organization, Style, and Sentence Effectiveness
expectation: Revise drafts to improve clarity, development, organization, style, diction, and sentence effectiveness, including use of parallel constructions and placement of phrases and dependent clauses.
overview: Revision at this level is not cleanup — it is architectural. English II students are expected to return to a draft and make substantive decisions about what the reader needs to understand, where the argument is thin, and whether the sentences are doing real grammatical and rhetorical work. Parallel constructions and clause placement are not grammar drills here; they are revision tools.
studentFriendly: You can take a draft you've already written and make it genuinely better — not by fixing typos, but by rethinking how ideas are ordered, where sentences need more power, and whether your word choices and sentence structures are actually doing what you intended.
questionStems: Which revision of the underlined sentence in paragraph X BEST improves the clarity of the writer's main idea?
A student wants to revise paragraph X to better develop the central argument. Which sentence, if added, would MOST effectively strengthen that paragraph?
The writer is considering deleting the underlined sentence from paragraph X. Should the writer keep or delete it, and why?
Which revision of the sentence in paragraph X MOST effectively uses parallel construction to improve its style?
The writer wants to combine the two underlined sentences in paragraph X into one sentence using a dependent clause. Which revision BEST accomplishes this goal?
Which organizational change would MOST improve the logical flow of paragraphs X and Y?
A student wrote the following sentence: [sentence]. Which revision MOST improves the placement of the underlined phrase?
The writer notices that paragraph X lacks development. Which of the following revisions would MOST effectively address this weakness?
Which revision of the underlined sentence in paragraph X BEST improves sentence effectiveness while maintaining the writer's original meaning?
Based on the draft, which transition added between paragraphs X and Y would MOST improve the organization and clarity of the essay?

misconceptions.title: Revision Means Proofreading
misconceptions.body: Students treat revision as a search-and-destroy mission for spelling errors and missing commas. Watch for students who return a "revised" draft that is word-for-word identical in structure and content but has corrected punctuation. Teach them that revision happens above the sentence level first — does the argument hold? Is each paragraph doing a job? Proofreading comes later, in editing. If students can't name ONE structural or developmental change they made, they didn't revise.

misconceptions.title: Adding More Words Equals Better Development
misconceptions.body: When students know a paragraph is underdeveloped, their fix is to pad — repeating the same idea in a new sentence, adding filler transitions, or inflating examples with vague language. Teach them that development means giving the reader something NEW: a warrant, a counterexample, a specification. Push them to ask "What does my reader not yet know here?" rather than "How do I make this longer?"

misconceptions.title: Parallel Construction Is a Grammar Rule, Not a Writing Move
misconceptions.body: Students learn parallel structure as a mechanics concept and file it away with comma rules — something to check at the end. Reframe it as a revision strategy with rhetorical teeth. Parallelism controls emphasis, creates rhythm, and signals that two ideas carry equal weight. Show them a sentence before and after parallelism is applied and ask what the reader's experience is, not just whether the grammar is correct.

misconceptions.title: Organization Is Fixed After the First Draft
misconceptions.body: Students assume that once they've written the essay in a certain order, that order is settled. Moving a paragraph feels like starting over. Teach them to physically cut and rearrange — whether on paper or in a document — and read the result cold. The question is always: does the reader have what they need, WHEN they need it? Sometimes the best introduction is the third paragraph a student wrote.

misconceptions.title: Clause Placement Is Just a Style Preference
misconceptions.body: When students place introductory phrases or dependent clauses, they treat the choice as arbitrary — "both are fine." Push them to see that front-loaded clauses create anticipation and establish context before the main claim lands, while rear-loaded clauses add information after the punch. Neither is always better, but neither is neutral. Ask them: what do you want your reader to be thinking when they hit the main clause?

assignments.name: Color-Coded Revision Pass
assignments.body: Students read their drafts and highlight each revision target in a different color: yellow for clarity issues, green for underdeveloped ideas, blue for organizational problems, pink for sentence-level weakness. They must make at least two changes in each category before the draft is considered revised.

assignments.name: Clause Surgery
assignments.body: Give students a paragraph of intentionally flat, front-heavy sentences. They revise every sentence by relocating at least one phrase or dependent clause and write a one-sentence explanation of why the placement they chose serves the reader better.

assignments.name: Parallelism Before and After
assignments.body: Students identify three non-parallel constructions in their own draft — lists, paired ideas, or series — and revise each. They present the before-and-after pairs to a partner, who identifies which revision is stronger and why.

assignments.name: Reverse Outline
assignments.body: After a full draft is written, students outline what they actually wrote (not what they planned to write) paragraph by paragraph. They compare the reverse outline to their original plan and identify at least one organizational move — reordering, cutting, or splitting — that would improve the essay's logic.

assignments.name: Development Interrogation
assignments.body: A partner reads one body paragraph from the writer's draft and writes three questions the paragraph left unanswered. The writer must revise the paragraph to answer at least two of them without inflating word count by more than 20%.

mastery: Returns a revised draft with demonstrable changes at the structural and developmental level, not just at the surface level.
Correctly applies parallel construction to revise sentences where elements are mismatched in form, and can explain the rhetorical effect of doing so.
Repositions phrases and dependent clauses to improve sentence flow and emphasis, with choices that are deliberate and defensible.
Identifies underdeveloped paragraphs and adds specific, substantive content — not filler — that advances the argument or deepens the analysis.
Produces a draft-to-revision comparison that a reader could use to trace what changed and why each change improved the writing.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I 9(C) targets the same revision categories, but English II explicitly names parallel constructions and phrase/clause placement as revision tools — signaling that syntactic control is now a required dimension of revision, not a bonus.

Vertical ramp to English III: English III 9(C) adds the expectation of revising for rhetorical effectiveness and audience awareness, building on the syntactic and structural revision skills solidified here.

DOK 3 justification: students must apply revision strategies across multiple dimensions simultaneously — clarity, development, organization, style, diction, and syntax — while making strategic decisions about which changes improve the draft and why. The work requires genuine transfer, not recall.
STAAR analog note: this TEK appears on STAAR Revision passages, where students read a student draft and select revisions; stems in this entry mirror that format directly.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Edit Drafts for Complete and Controlled Sentences
expectation: Edit drafts using standard English conventions, including a variety of complete, controlled sentences and avoidance of unintentional splices, run-ons, and fragments.
overview: This TEK is about sentence-level control as a revision discipline — not grammar drills in isolation, but the editing judgment students must exercise on their own drafts. By English II, students should be moving beyond accidental errors into deliberate construction: knowing what a complete sentence does, and knowing when they've let one collapse or bleed into the next.
studentFriendly: You can read your own draft and catch the places where a sentence falls apart — where it starts and never finishes, or where two ideas smash together without the punctuation or structure to hold them apart.
questionStems: Which revision of the underlined sentence in paragraph X BEST corrects the run-on without changing the writer's meaning?
The sentence in paragraph X is a fragment. Which revision MOST effectively makes it a complete sentence?
Which edit BEST corrects the comma splice in lines X–Y while preserving the relationship between the two ideas?
Read the sentence in paragraph X. Which of the following revisions creates the MOST controlled and complete sentence?
A student wants to combine the two sentences in paragraph X into one controlled sentence. Which revision BEST accomplishes this goal?
Which of the following sentences from the draft contains an unintentional fragment?
Which revision of paragraph X MOST effectively eliminates the run-on sentences while maintaining the writer's voice?
The writer wants to vary sentence structure in paragraph X. Which revision BEST introduces sentence variety without creating a fragment or splice?
Which sentence from the draft is punctuated correctly as a complete, controlled sentence?
A student revised the underlined portion of paragraph X. Which version MOST clearly corrects the comma splice and improves sentence control?

misconceptions.title: Long Sentences Are Run-Ons and Short Sentences Are Fragments
misconceptions.body: Students flag any long sentence as a run-on and any short sentence as a fragment — neither is true. Teach them that sentence completeness is about grammatical structure, not word count. A three-word sentence can be complete; a forty-word sentence can be perfectly controlled. Push them to identify the subject and verb before they make a judgment call.

misconceptions.title: A Comma Always Fixes a Run-On
misconceptions.body: Students reach for a comma as a universal patch — two ideas running together, throw a comma in and move on. That creates a splice, not a fix. Teach them the actual options: add a coordinating conjunction, use a semicolon, subordinate one clause, or write two sentences. The comma alone is not enough and treating it as a cure is the most common splice-generating habit in English II writing.

misconceptions.title: Fragments Are Always Mistakes
misconceptions.body: Students who've seen intentional fragments in published prose — and they have — assume fragments are a stylistic option they can deploy freely. The TEK targets *unintentional* splices and fragments, which is the key word. Teach them the difference: a writer who knows how to write a complete sentence and chooses a fragment for effect is in control; a writer who doesn't know their sentence is broken is not. Control comes before choice.

misconceptions.title: Editing Means Reading for Meaning, Not Structure
misconceptions.body: Students read their drafts for whether the ideas sound right, not whether the sentences are grammatically complete. They hear the meaning and miss the structure. Teach them to read editing passes differently from reading for content — specifically: read for structure by isolating each sentence, finding its subject and verb, and asking whether it can stand alone before moving on. A separate read-through devoted only to sentence boundaries changes what students catch.

misconceptions.title: Combining Sentences Always Improves Writing
misconceptions.body: When students learn to combine sentences, they overcorrect — stringing independent clauses together with "and" or "but" until a paragraph becomes one long chain. Teach them that sentence variety means some sentences stay short and some get combined, not that every pair of sentences should be merged. Uncontrolled combination is just a slower kind of run-on.

assignments.name: Sentence Autopsy
assignments.body: Students select one paragraph from their current draft and number every sentence. For each, they identify the subject and verb, label it complete or broken, and note the type of error if one exists. Then they revise. The discipline is in the annotation — not just fixing but naming what broke.

assignments.name: Splice, Run-On, Fragment Sort
assignments.body: Provide students with 15 sentences pulled from anonymous student drafts (or fabricated examples). Students sort them: complete, comma splice, run-on, fragment — then revise each broken sentence three different ways and choose the strongest revision. The three-revision rule forces genuine engagement with options rather than defaulting to the easiest fix.

assignments.name: Controlled Variety Revision
assignments.body: Students take a paragraph where all their sentences follow the same structure (SVO, SVO, SVO) and revise it for deliberate variety — one short simple sentence, one compound, one complex — while keeping every sentence complete and controlled. Final paragraph is submitted with sentence types labeled in the margin.

mastery: Identifies comma splices, run-ons, and fragments in their own drafts — not just in isolated exercises — and names the error type before correcting it.
Selects from multiple correction strategies (coordination, subordination, separation, revision of the whole) rather than defaulting to a single fix.
Produces paragraphs with a visible variety of sentence structures, all of which are grammatically complete.
Distinguishes between intentional fragments used for stylistic effect in mentor texts and unintentional fragments in their own drafts.
Completes a focused editing pass — separate from content revision — devoted specifically to sentence-level correctness.
notes: Vertical ramp: English I 9D.i establishes the baseline expectation for complete sentences. English II keeps the same skill but raises the bar with the phrase "variety of complete, controlled sentences" — students are no longer just avoiding errors, they are expected to demonstrate range across sentence types. English III adds further precision and requires control at the level of stylistic effect.

The word "unintentional" in the TEA expectation is load-bearing. Stem 8 and the third misconception both hinge on this: the standard does not forbid intentional fragments; it targets the ones students don't know they have.

DOK 2 justification: applying editing conventions to student-produced text requires skill application, not mere recall — but the judgment is procedural rather than evaluative across multiple criteria, which places it firmly at DOK 2.

This TEK has a strong STAAR analog: editing-in-context items test comma splices, run-ons, and fragments directly using student-draft passages. Stems mirror the "which revision BEST corrects" frame used in STAAR Revision and Editing passages.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Edit for Verb Tense and Voice Consistency
expectation: Edit drafts using standard English conventions, including consistent, appropriate use of verb tense and active and passive voice.
overview: Verb tense and voice aren't decorative — they control how time moves through a piece and who bears responsibility for each action. This TEK asks students to edit with intention, not just instinct: to hold tense consistent across a draft and to deploy passive voice as a deliberate tool, not a grammatical accident.
studentFriendly: You can read your own draft and catch places where the verb tense shifts without reason or where passive voice muddles who's doing what — then fix both with purpose.
questionStems: Read this paragraph from the draft. Which revision BEST corrects the inconsistent verb tense?
Which sentence from the draft uses passive voice in a way that MOST likely obscures the subject's responsibility?
The writer uses the past perfect tense in paragraph 3. This choice MOST likely serves to —
Which revision of the underlined sentence BEST establishes active voice without changing the meaning?
Read paragraphs 2 and 4. The shift in verb tense between these paragraphs MOST likely —
Which sentence from the draft would BEST be revised to passive voice to emphasize the action over the actor?
The writer changes from present tense to past tense in paragraph 5. What is the MOST likely effect of this shift on the reader?
Which of the following revisions MOST effectively corrects the verb tense error in the underlined sentence?
Based on the draft, which sentence BEST demonstrates consistent and purposeful use of verb tense throughout the paragraph?
The writer uses passive voice in the final paragraph. This choice MOST nearly suggests that —

misconceptions.title: Passive Voice Is Always Wrong
misconceptions.body: Students arrive having been told "never use passive voice" — and they take this as law. The real skill is knowing when passive voice serves the writing: scientific reports, moments when the actor is unknown or irrelevant, places where the receiver of the action deserves emphasis. Teach them that passive voice is a tool, not a crime, and that editing means asking WHY passive voice is here, not automatically deleting it.

misconceptions.title: Tense Consistency Means Using One Tense Forever
misconceptions.body: Students often interpret "consistent tense" as a prohibition on all tense variation. In reality, a literary analysis in present tense still uses past tense for historical context; a narrative in past tense still uses past perfect to signal earlier events. Watch for students who overcorrect into a monotone — flattening all tense variation in the name of consistency and losing the logical structure of time.

misconceptions.title: If It "Sounds Right," the Tense Is Correct
misconceptions.body: Students edit by ear, and their ear is calibrated to speech patterns that tolerate tense shifts AAVE, code-switching, and informal registers can normalize. They'll read a draft aloud, hear no problem, and mark it clean. Teach them to trace each verb explicitly — read the draft verb by verb, tag each one, and check the sequence against the timeline the piece is building.

misconceptions.title: Active Voice Makes Every Sentence Better
misconceptions.body: Students who have been drilled on active voice apply it indiscriminately, rewriting passive constructions even when passive voice is precise and appropriate. "The law was passed in 1965" is correct and purposeful — converting it to "Congress passed the law in 1965" may shift the emphasis entirely. Push them to ask what the sentence is ABOUT before deciding which voice serves it.

misconceptions.title: Editing Verb Issues Is a One-Pass Job
misconceptions.body: Students treat editing as a single sweep and assume they've caught everything. Verb tense and voice problems often travel in clusters — one tense shift signals a zone where the writer lost track. Teach them to mark the FIRST verb error, then slow down and scrutinize the surrounding sentences, because the problem is rarely isolated.

assignments.name: Verb Audit
assignments.body: Students exchange drafts and highlight every verb in a partner's essay. They label each one: tense, active or passive, and whether the choice is consistent and appropriate. They return the marked draft with a one-paragraph explanation of the two most significant verb issues they found.

assignments.name: Passive-to-Active, Active-to-Passive
assignments.body: Give students a paragraph written entirely in passive voice and one written entirely in active voice. They revise each — converting passive to active where it improves clarity, and converting active to passive where it shifts emphasis purposefully. Discussion follows: which revisions actually helped?

assignments.name: Tense Timeline
assignments.body: Students create a visual timeline of the events in their own draft and map each paragraph's dominant tense against it. Where the tense doesn't match the timeline logic, they revise and annotate why.

assignments.name: Before/After Edit
assignments.body: Students submit a "before" draft alongside their revised "after" draft with marginal annotations on every verb tense or voice change they made and a one-sentence justification for each revision.

mastery: Maintains a consistent governing tense across a draft and shifts tense only when the logic of time or sequence requires it, with the shift clearly signaled.
Distinguishes between passive voice as error and passive voice as deliberate choice, and can defend the latter in a revision note.
Reads a draft verb by verb during editing rather than reading for general "sound."
Identifies tense shift clusters — places where multiple verb errors appear together — and revises the zone, not just the single flagged sentence.
Produces a revised draft in which every verb choice is traceable to a purposeful decision about time, emphasis, or agency.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I 9D(ii) introduces verb tense and voice as editing targets; English II holds students accountable for *consistency* and *appropriateness* — both deliberate, principled choices rather than error correction alone. English III adds complexity of syntax and extended argument structures where tense management becomes more demanding.

DOK 2 justification: the primary cognitive move is applying grammatical knowledge to a draft — identifying and correcting errors and making purposeful choices — which fits DOK 2 (Skill/Concept). A student who can explain WHY a passive construction is appropriate edges toward DOK 3, which can be reached in discussion or annotation tasks.

STAAR analog note: STAAR Revision and Editing passages directly test verb tense consistency and active/passive voice in the context of a student draft — these stems map directly to the Editing strand of the STAAR Writing component.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: 110.37(9)(D)(iv) — Edit Drafts for Correct Capitalization
expectation: Edit drafts using standard English conventions, including correct capitalization.
overview: Capitalization errors are invisible to students who have stopped reading their own work. This TEK is not about teaching students what to capitalize — they've heard the rules since third grade — it's about building the editorial habit of actually looking for capitalization as a discrete pass through a draft.
studentFriendly: You can read your own draft with a focused eye and catch every capitalization error — proper nouns, titles, first words of sentences, and the tricky cases that trip writers up.
questionStems: Which edit to the underlined sentence correctly applies standard capitalization rules?
In sentence 8, the writer needs to correct a capitalization error. Which revision BEST fixes the error?
Read this sentence from the draft: [sentence]. Which word should be capitalized?
The student wants to correct the capitalization in paragraph 3. Which revision BEST fixes the error?
Which of the following correctly capitalizes the title referenced in paragraph 2?
In the sentence below, which word is capitalized incorrectly?
The writer uses the word [word] in paragraph 4. Based on context, should it be capitalized? Which answer BEST supports this conclusion?
Which sentence from the draft contains a capitalization error that needs to be corrected?
Read paragraph 5. Which revision corrects all capitalization errors in the paragraph?
The student wants to revise sentence 12 so that it follows standard capitalization conventions. Which revision BEST accomplishes this?

misconceptions.title: Capitalization Rules Were Mastered in Fourth Grade — They're Done
misconceptions.body: Students (and some teachers) treat capitalization as closed content, something learned and filed away. But the errors that show up in high school writing — lowercase "i" as a pronoun in informal registers, uncapitalized proper adjectives, inconsistent treatment of titles — are not beginner errors. Teach students that capitalization has genuinely tricky corners, and that catching errors requires a dedicated editing pass, not a vague read-through.

misconceptions.title: Everything Important Gets a Capital
misconceptions.body: Watch for overcapitalization: students who capitalize "Government," "Society," "Nature," and "Justice" whenever they feel the word deserves weight. This is especially common in literary analysis. The rule is not about importance — it is about proper nouns, specific named entities, and grammatical convention. Reframe: a capital letter is a proper noun signal, not an emphasis tool.

misconceptions.title: Titles Are Just Initial Caps on Every Word
misconceptions.body: Students apply "every word gets capitalized" to all titles, missing the actual rule (articles, prepositions, and coordinating conjunctions lowercase unless they're the first word). Then some students overcorrect the other direction and leave everything lowercase. Teach them the rule explicitly with examples, then give them a set of malformed titles and make them fix each one with a reason.

misconceptions.title: Spell-Check Will Catch It
misconceptions.body: Autocorrect and spell-checkers catch very little capitalization error — they don't flag a lowercase proper noun if the word exists as a common noun ("the president" vs. "President Adams"), and they don't know whether your student meant a title or a generic noun. Teach students that capitalization editing is a manual pass that software cannot substitute for. Have them turn off autocorrect for one assignment and feel the difference.

misconceptions.title: Fixing Capitalization Is the Last Thing — or an Afterthought
misconceptions.body: Students often fold capitalization into a single "proofread" sweep that is already doing too much. They are simultaneously checking for clarity, grammar, and mechanics, and so capitalization errors slide through. Teach them the multi-pass editing model: one pass is nothing but capitalization, moving sentence by sentence, word by word. That isolation is what surfaces errors that a general read misses.

assignments.name: Targeted Edit Pass
assignments.body: Students take a previously submitted draft and run a capitalization-only editing pass, highlighting every word they evaluate and marking corrections in a different color. They submit both the marked draft and a brief note explaining three decisions they made.

assignments.name: Title Correction Set
assignments.body: Provide 15 malformed book, film, and article titles (a mix of overcapitalization and undercapitalization). Students correct each title and write a one-sentence justification for each correction.

assignments.name: Error Hunt in Published Text
assignments.body: Students find a piece of informal online writing (a blog post, a comment thread, a fan wiki) and identify every capitalization error. They classify each error by type and propose a correction. Discuss: how does the medium change our expectations?

assignments.name: Peer Edit with a Capitalization Lens
assignments.body: Students swap drafts and read their partner's work with a single focus: capitalization only. They annotate every error found. Partners then discuss whether they agree. Disagreements become a class discussion about the rules in play.

mastery: Runs a discrete capitalization pass on drafts rather than folding it into a general proofread.
Correctly capitalizes proper nouns, proper adjectives, the pronoun "I," titles, and the first word of sentences — including edge cases.
Explains the rule behind each capitalization decision when challenged, rather than relying on instinct or feel.
Identifies and corrects both overcapitalization and undercapitalization in their own writing and peers' writing.
Demonstrates consistent application across multiple drafts, not just after targeted instruction.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I introduces editing for correct capitalization as part of the same conventions cluster. English II is not introducing the concept but is raising the expectation for independence and accuracy in increasingly complex writing contexts.
