English III and IV expect the same skill to operate without scaffolding in multi-genre and research writing.

DOK 2 justification: applying a known convention to a novel draft requires skill application and recognition of error, not simply recall — but the rules themselves are determinate, placing this squarely at DOK 2.
STAAR analog note: capitalization editing items appear on STAAR Revision and Editing passages as discrete sentence-level correction tasks. The stems in this entry mirror that format directly.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: 110.37(9)(D)(v) — Edit Drafts Using Punctuation to Set Off Phrases and Clauses
expectation: Edit drafts using standard English conventions, including punctuation — commas, semicolons, colons, dashes, and parentheses — to set off phrases and clauses as appropriate.
overview: This TEK is where punctuation becomes a rhetorical decision, not a rule to memorize. Students learn that commas, dashes, colons, semicolons, and parentheses aren't interchangeable — each one signals a distinct relationship between ideas, and choosing the wrong one misleads the reader even when the grammar is technically defensible.
studentFriendly: You know which punctuation mark to use when you're setting off extra information, and you can explain why that mark — not a different one — fits the sentence.
questionStems: Which edit BEST corrects the punctuation error in the underlined portion of paragraph X?
Which sentence from the draft uses a dash MOST effectively to set off a phrase?
The writer wants to add the following phrase to sentence X. Which punctuation MOST clearly sets it off from the main clause?
Which revision BEST corrects the use of the comma in sentence X?
Read the sentence from paragraph X. Which edit would MOST improve the clarity of the punctuation in this sentence?
The writer is considering replacing the comma in sentence X with a dash. Which statement BEST explains whether this change should be made?
Which sentence from paragraph X uses parentheses in a way that MOST effectively sets off supplementary information?
Read sentences X and Y. Which edit would MOST effectively combine the information in these sentences using a semicolon?
Which revision of sentence X BEST uses a colon to introduce the phrase that follows?
The writer wants to revise the underlined phrase in sentence X to make the relationship between the main clause and the modifier clearer. Which edit BEST achieves this?

misconceptions.title: Dashes and Commas Are Just Stronger and Weaker Versions of the Same Mark
misconceptions.body: Students treat dashes as "emphatic commas" and swap them in whenever they want to sound dramatic. Teach them that a dash signals a break — a sharp pivot, a sudden addition, a deliberate interruption — while a comma signals a softer, more expected pause. The choice isn't about intensity; it's about the structural relationship between the ideas. Show them pairs of sentences where swapping the marks changes what the reader expects next.

misconceptions.title: Parentheses Mean "Extra, Unimportant"
misconceptions.body: Students learn that parentheses hold supplementary information and conclude that anything inside them doesn't matter. That misreading produces drafts where the parenthetical undercuts the sentence's credibility — if you've buried a key qualifier in parentheses, readers may skip it. Teach them: parentheses don't make information unimportant, they make it subordinate. Watch for students who parenthesize their best evidence.

misconceptions.title: A Comma Before "Which" Is Always Right
misconceptions.body: Students who've learned the "which" vs. "that" rule mechanically slap a comma before every "which" without asking whether the clause is actually nonrestrictive. Push them to ask: does the clause identify the noun, or describe it? If removing the clause changes which thing is being talked about, the comma doesn't belong — and neither does "which." The convention and the punctuation are inseparable; teach both at once.

misconceptions.title: Semicolons Join Any Two Long Sentences
misconceptions.body: Students think semicolons are for long sentences that seem too close to split. They connect "The cafeteria was loud; she had forgotten her lunch" and don't notice that the relationship between the ideas is doing no work at all. Teach them that a semicolon signals that the two independent clauses are in meaningful dialogue — contrast, consequence, parallel — and that a well-placed semicolon should make the reader feel the connection without needing a conjunction to name it.

misconceptions.title: Colons Introduce Lists, Full Stop
misconceptions.body: Students treat the colon as a list-launcher and nothing else. They miss that a colon can introduce a single word, a clause, an appositive, or a restatement — and that when it does, it's promising the reader: "What follows explains or fulfills what I just said." Watch for students who write colons after verbs ("The ingredients are: flour, eggs, butter") — a colon never interrupts a grammatical structure mid-stream. Reframe it: a colon is a formal handoff, not a bullet-point starter.

assignments.name: Punctuation Swap Analysis
assignments.body: Students take a mentor-text paragraph and replace every comma, dash, semicolon, and colon with a different mark. They then explain in writing why each original choice was more effective (or, occasionally, why it wasn't). This forces articulation, not just recognition.

assignments.name: Sentence Surgery
assignments.body: Give students five run-on or choppy passages and three punctuation options for each. They must select one mark, revise the sentence, and write one sentence defending their choice. Grade for reasoning, not just correctness.

assignments.name: Editing Rounds
assignments.body: Students exchange drafts and mark every punctuation error they can find in their partner's attempt to set off phrases or clauses. They must distinguish between "this is technically wrong" and "this is technically defensible but rhetorically weak." Partners discuss disagreements before the teacher adjudicates.

assignments.name: Build a Rule Card
assignments.body: Students generate their own reference card summarizing when to use each of the five marks, written in their own language, with one original example per mark. They use this card during peer editing for the rest of the unit.

assignments.name: Before/After Gallery
assignments.body: Students revise three of their own sentences from earlier drafts — one using a dash for effect, one using a colon to introduce a restatement, one using parentheses to subordinate a detail. The before and after are displayed side by side with a brief annotation.

mastery: Correctly selects from among commas, semicolons, colons, dashes, and parentheses when setting off phrases and clauses, without defaulting to commas for every pause.
Explains — not just applies — why a specific punctuation mark fits a specific sentence, using the structural relationship between clauses as the justification.
Catches their own punctuation errors during revision by asking what relationship the mark is signaling, not just whether a mark is present.
Distinguishes between grammatically incorrect punctuation and rhetorically weak punctuation, and can revise for both.
Uses parentheses and dashes deliberately for effect, not as decorative variation.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I introduces the same punctuation inventory, but the emphasis is on basic correctness — is the comma there or not? English II lifts the expectation to appropriateness — students must now select the right mark for the structural relationship the sentence is creating. English III and IV build toward using these marks as rhetorical tools in sustained argumentative and analytical writing.

DOK 2 justification: the primary cognitive demand is skillful application of conventions in context — students apply rules to novel sentences and drafts, not merely recall definitions. Defending punctuation choices requires interpretation, but the anchor task is accurate editing.

This TEK has a strong STAAR editing-task analog. The "which edit BEST corrects" and "which revision MOST clearly" stems are the exact form students will encounter. Stems here mirror that format directly.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: 110.37(9)(D)(vi) — Edit Drafts for Correct Spelling
expectation: Edit drafts using standard English conventions, including correct spelling.
overview: Spelling instruction at the secondary level is not about memorizing word lists — it's about teaching students to edit with enough pattern recognition and self-awareness to catch their own errors before a reader has to. By English II, the expectation is that students are applying orthographic knowledge during revision, not relying on autocorrect as a first pass.
studentFriendly: You can read your own draft with a proofreader's eye, catching misspelled words by recognizing which patterns look wrong and using the tools and strategies you know to fix them before your writing goes out into the world.
questionStems: Which of the following sentences from the draft contains a spelling error that should be corrected?
A student writes the sentence: "[sentence with error]." Which revision BEST corrects the spelling error in the sentence?
In paragraph X, which underlined word is spelled incorrectly?
A student wants to revise paragraph X to correct all spelling errors. Which version BEST accomplishes this goal?
Which of the following is the correct spelling of the underlined word in paragraph X?
Read paragraph X from a student's draft. Which sentence contains a spelling error?
A student revises the following sentence: "[sentence]." Which word in the revised sentence is MOST likely to contain a spelling error that spellcheck would NOT catch?
Which sentence, if inserted into paragraph X, would introduce a spelling error inconsistent with standard English conventions?
A student edits the following passage for spelling. Which revision MOST accurately corrects all spelling errors in the passage?
In the student's draft, the word "[homophones/near-homophone]" is used in paragraph X. Which revision BEST corrects the error?

misconceptions.title: Spellcheck Is Editing
misconceptions.body: Students submit drafts assuming that because the word processor didn't flag anything, the spelling is correct. Teach them that spellcheck catches non-words — it cannot catch correctly spelled words used wrongly: "their/there/they're," "affect/effect," "loose/lose," "compliment/complement." Require a manual read-through pass specifically for homophones and frequently confused pairs as a non-negotiable editing step.

misconceptions.title: Good Writers Don't Make Spelling Errors in Drafts
misconceptions.body: Students who misspell words in drafts often feel shame, as if spelling errors signal low ability. Reframe this: spelling errors in a draft are a normal artifact of drafting fast. The failure isn't making the error — it's not catching it in editing. The skill lives in the editing pass, not in the first draft.

misconceptions.title: Spelling Rules Are Too Complicated to Be Useful
misconceptions.body: Students give up on rules like "i before e" the moment they encounter an exception and decide the whole enterprise of rule-based spelling is a wash. Teach them that most rules hold for large classes of words and that the exceptions are usually learnable as a short list. The goal isn't perfect recall of every rule — it's developing enough pattern-sensitivity to know when a word needs a second look.

misconceptions.title: If I Can Read It, It's Fine
misconceptions.body: Students equate legibility with correctness and assume that because the reader can guess the intended word, the spelling error doesn't matter. Push them to understand that spelling errors signal carelessness to academic and professional audiences regardless of whether the word is decipherable — and that in high-stakes writing, one visible misspelling can undermine an otherwise strong piece.

misconceptions.title: Spelling Instruction Is Only for Elementary School
misconceptions.body: Secondary students often resist spelling-focused editing as beneath them. Watch for this attitude especially in students who are otherwise strong writers. The words that trip up strong writers are different from those that trip up early readers — they're multisyllabic, Latinate, and domain-specific — but the editing discipline is the same. Treat it as a professional craft expectation, not a remedial skill.

assignments.name: Homophone Hunt
assignments.body: Distribute a student-written paragraph (anonymized or teacher-fabricated) with five correctly spelled but contextually wrong words — homophones and near-homophones. Students identify each error, name the confusion pair, and write the correct version in context. Debrief by listing the pairs on the board as a class reference.

assignments.name: Spelling Autopsy
assignments.body: Students review a returned essay and categorize every spelling error they made: (a) homophone confusion, (b) wrong suffix or prefix, (c) unfamiliar word, (d) careless transposition. They write a one-paragraph reflection on which category dominates their errors and what specific strategy they will apply in their next editing pass.

assignments.name: Peer Edit with a Spelling-Only Pass
assignments.body: During peer editing, designate one pass specifically for spelling — nothing else. Partners read slowly, word by word, and circle any word that looks uncertain. The goal is to separate the spelling-edit from the ideas-edit so neither gets crowded out by the other.

assignments.name: Domain Word Log
assignments.body: For each unit's writing assignment, students maintain a running log of the domain-specific or high-frequency academic words they misspell. At the end of the unit, they produce a personal spelling reference card to consult during final editing. The log becomes a revision tool, not a punishment.

mastery: Conducts a deliberate, word-level editing pass specifically for spelling, separate from other editing concerns.
Identifies and correctly resolves homophone and commonly confused word errors that automated tools would miss.
Uses knowledge of spelling patterns, word families, and morphology to evaluate whether an unfamiliar word looks correct.
Produces a final draft in which spelling errors do not appear — or, if working under timed conditions, catches and corrects the majority of errors before submission.
Can explain the category of a spelling error (homophone, suffix, transposition, etc.) rather than treating all errors as equally mysterious.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I (9)(D)(vi) establishes correct spelling as a convention expectation in editing. English II holds the same expectation but operates across more complex vocabulary — multisyllabic, domain-specific, and Latinate words appear more frequently in the writing tasks at this level. English III and IV carry the same standard forward with an expectation that spelling control is fully internalized and requires no separate instructional focus; errors are addressed in conferencing rather than direct instruction.

DOK 2 justification: applying orthographic knowledge to identify and correct errors in context is a skill-application task. The complexity is in recognizing error patterns, not in constructing original arguments.

STAAR note: STAAR Revision and Editing passages test spelling errors directly — students identify the misspelled word among underlined options or select the revision that corrects a spelling error. Stems in this entry mirror that format closely.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Publish Written Work for Appropriate Audiences
expectation: Publish written work for appropriate audiences.
overview: Publishing is where audience awareness becomes real: students stop writing for a grade and start writing for someone who didn't have to read it. The TEK is deceptively simple — the hard part is teaching students to make deliberate decisions about format, platform, and presentation based on who will actually receive the work.
studentFriendly: You can take a finished piece of writing, decide who needs to read it and how, and get it in front of that audience in a form they'll actually engage with.
questionStems: The student revised her essay before submitting it to the school newspaper. Which change MOST likely reflects her awareness of the newspaper's audience?
Read the following two versions of the same paragraph. Which version is BEST suited for publication in a community newsletter rather than an academic journal, and why?
Based on the passage, which decision about format MOST likely helped the author reach the intended audience?
The author chose to publish his argument as an open letter rather than a formal essay. Which sentence from the text BEST explains this choice?
Which revision to the student's draft would MOST likely make it appropriate for publication in a general-audience magazine?
The student published her personal narrative on a public blog. Which element of her writing MOST clearly demonstrates awareness of an online audience?
Which sentence from the draft BEST indicates the writer considered the expectations of the publication's readers?
Read the following draft and the editor's note. Which suggested revision MOST directly addresses the mismatch between the draft's tone and the publication's audience?
The student submitted the same piece to two different publications. Which change between the two versions MOST likely reflects the difference in each publication's intended audience?
Based on the passage, what can the reader infer about the author's awareness of audience from the word choice in paragraph 3?

misconceptions.title: "Publishing" Means Posting It Online
misconceptions.body: Students treat publishing as a synonym for uploading — slap it on Google Docs, share the link, done. Teach them that publishing is a decision that involves format, platform, voice, and presentation, all calibrated to a specific audience. A piece published in a literary magazine looks different from the same content posted on a class blog, and both look different from a letter to the editor. The platform is part of the message.

misconceptions.title: The Work Is Done After Editing
misconceptions.body: Students believe publishing is what happens after writing, not part of it. Watch for drafts that were revised for correctness but never reconsidered for audience fit — the grammar is clean but the tone is wrong for where it's going. Push them to ask: "Does this feel right for the people who will read it in this context?" That question belongs to the publishing stage, not just the editing stage.

misconceptions.title: Appropriate Audience Means Any Audience
misconceptions.body: Students confuse "appropriate audience" with "anyone who might read it." An appropriate audience is a specific one — and matching form to that audience is a craft move. Teach them to identify the audience before publishing, not after. If they can't name who the audience is and what that audience expects, they're not ready to publish.

misconceptions.title: A Formal Essay Is Always the Most "Published" Form
misconceptions.body: Students who've been trained on five-paragraph essays assume formal academic structure is the most legitimate published form. Reframe this: a personal essay in a community newspaper, a well-crafted op-ed, a digital portfolio entry, a letter to a school board — these are published forms that demand just as much craft, just differently. Watch for students who dismiss lower-stakes publications as "not real writing."

misconceptions.title: Publishing Is a One-Time Decision
misconceptions.body: Students treat the publishing choice as arbitrary or automatic ("I'll just turn it in"). Teach them that writers make intentional decisions about where and how their work appears — and that those decisions can be revised too. If a piece lands wrong, the answer might be a different venue or format, not a different draft. The relationship between writing and publication is ongoing, not one-directional.

assignments.name: Audience-Match Analysis
assignments.body: Students select one completed piece and identify three possible venues for publication (a school publication, a community outlet, a public digital platform). For each, they write one paragraph explaining what revisions the piece would need and why. Grade for specificity of audience analysis, not for the revisions themselves.

assignments.name: Live Submission
assignments.body: Students select a finished piece, identify a real external publication that accepts student work (literary magazine, local newspaper, contest, school journal), revise for that audience, and submit. The grade is on the preparation and submission process — acceptance is not the metric.

assignments.name: Before and After: Same Piece, Two Audiences
assignments.body: Students take one draft and produce two published versions for two different audiences (e.g., academic peers vs. general public). They write a brief reflection explaining every change they made and why. Comparing the two versions is the class discussion.

mastery: Identifies a specific audience before finalizing a draft and can articulate what that audience expects in terms of tone, format, and structure.
Makes deliberate formatting and presentation decisions that reflect the publication context, not just personal preference.
Revises word choice, sentence structure, and organizational features to match the conventions of the publication venue.
Can explain why a piece is or is not appropriate for a given audience, with reference to specific choices in the text.
Completes the full cycle from draft to published form, including any formatting, titling, or platform-specific requirements the venue demands.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I 9E establishes the same expectation — publish written work for appropriate audiences. English II is not a ramp in the verb but in the sophistication of the audience analysis and the range of publication contexts students navigate. By English III, the expectation carries into more complex genre and rhetorical decisions around publication. Teachers should not treat this as a repeat of English I; the work in English II is broader publication contexts and sharper audience calibration.

DOK 3 justification: Publishing requires strategic thinking — students must apply knowledge of audience, genre, and convention to make decisions that aren't rule-bound. The right choice depends on context, not formula.

STAAR analog note: This TEK is not directly assessed on STAAR as a discrete skill, but audience awareness surfaces throughout the writing-task prompts and in author's-purpose questions on the reading sections. Stems above mirror the STAAR framing for audience-craft questions.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Compose Informational Texts Using Genre Characteristics and Craft
expectation: Compose informational texts such as explanatory essays, reports, and personal essays using genre characteristics and craft.
overview: This is the workhorse composition standard for English II — students don't just explain a topic, they make deliberate genre decisions about structure, voice, and craft. The move from English I is accountability to genre: a report reads like a report, an explanatory essay carries an organizing logic, and a personal essay uses a real writerly voice, not just a personal opinion stapled to paragraphs.
studentFriendly: You can write explanatory essays, reports, and personal essays that don't just cover information — they use the specific moves that make each genre work.
questionStems: Which organizational structure BEST supports the writer's purpose in paragraphs X–Y?
The writer uses [specific transition/phrase] in paragraph X MOST likely to —
Which sentence from the essay BEST establishes the writer's central idea?
How does the evidence in paragraph X contribute to the development of the writer's claim?
The writer's use of [specific detail/example] in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Which revision to paragraph X would MOST improve the clarity of the writer's explanation?
Based on paragraph X, the writer's organizational choice is MOST likely meant to —
Which sentence BEST concludes the writer's explanation by synthesizing the information presented?
The writer MOST likely includes paragraph X in order to —
Which of the following revisions to the underlined sentence in paragraph X would MOST strengthen the report?

misconceptions.title: Informational Means Objective, So Voice Disappears
misconceptions.body: Students flatten their informational writing into a bloodless, voiceless recitation of facts — as if "informational" means "remove all personality." Teach them that craft is what separates a readable explanation from a Wikipedia stub. A personal essay about a historical event still has a distinct writerly presence; a report still makes choices about what to foreground. Voice doesn't vanish — it sharpens.

misconceptions.title: Genre Is Just a Container, Not a Set of Moves
misconceptions.body: Students treat genre as a label they slap on after writing: "I wrote about vaccines, so it's a report." Push them to understand that genre is a set of structural and rhetorical moves — a report uses source attribution and domain vocabulary, an explanatory essay drives toward a thesis and develops it logically, a personal essay anchors abstraction in specific scene and reflection. Watch for papers that could be any genre because they've made no genre-specific decisions.

misconceptions.title: A Strong Introduction Means the Rest Can Coast
misconceptions.body: Students front-load all their effort — a polished hook, a clear thesis — and then dump the remaining paragraphs as loose, unsequenced information. Teach them that the introduction sets a contract: the body has to fulfill it. Every paragraph in an explanatory essay should be traceable back to the thesis, and the conclusion should do more than restate it. Grade the middle, not just the opening.

misconceptions.title: Longer Is More Developed
misconceptions.body: Students pad informational writing with synonyms, restatements, and transitional filler to hit a page count. They mistake volume for substance. Real development in informational writing means adding a new layer of understanding — a clarifying example, a qualification, a domain-specific term explained — not restating the same idea in three different phrasings. Teach them to cut the fourth sentence of every paragraph and see if anything is lost.

misconceptions.title: Personal Essays Are Just Opinion Pieces
misconceptions.body: Students confuse the personal essay with the five-paragraph persuasive essay and produce an argument in first person, minus any craft. A personal essay is a literary genre: it grounds ideas in lived experience, uses scene and reflection, and earns its claims through specificity rather than assertion. Watch for personal essays that are all thesis and no texture — no moment, no image, no surprise. The genre requires the writer to be present, not just opinionated.

assignments.name: Genre Dissection Before Composition
assignments.body: Before students write, give them three mentor texts — one explanatory essay, one report, one personal essay — on the same general topic. Students annotate the genre moves in each (organizational structure, voice, evidence type, diction). Then they choose one genre and write their own piece, citing at least two genre moves from the mentor text they emulated.

assignments.name: Explanation Audit
assignments.body: Students submit a draft of an explanatory essay. In a required revision step, they highlight every sentence that introduces a new idea in one color and every sentence that restates an existing idea in another. They must then cut or transform at least two restatement sentences into genuine development before the final draft.

assignments.name: Personal Essay Workshop: The Specific Moment
assignments.body: Students write a personal essay that opens with a scene of no more than one paragraph — a specific moment, not a generalization — and then earns its central idea through reflection on that scene. Graded on specificity of scene and quality of reflection, not on whether the reader agrees with the idea.

assignments.name: Report with Genre Justification
assignments.body: Students write a short report (two to three pages) on a topic from another class. With the final draft, they attach a one-page genre memo: what organizational structure did they use, what evidence choices did they make, and why do those choices serve a report audience rather than an essay audience?

mastery: Selects a genre and applies its structural and craft conventions consistently throughout the piece — the text is recognizably an explanatory essay, a report, or a personal essay, not an undifferentiated composition.
Develops ideas with genuine elaboration — new examples, domain-specific language, qualifications — rather than restatement or padding.
Uses voice appropriate to the genre: writerly presence in a personal essay, credible authority in a report, clear organizing logic in an explanatory essay.
Writes conclusions that synthesize rather than merely summarize — they add a layer of understanding or implication.
Can articulate, when asked, at least two specific genre decisions they made and why those decisions serve the audience and purpose of the piece.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I 10B asks students to compose informational texts using genre characteristics — the same standard. English II's move is implicitly one of independence and craft control: students should now be selecting and sustaining genre conventions with less scaffolding, and the quality of writerly craft (not just genre compliance) becomes the evaluative target.

Vertical ramp to English III: English III 10B adds an explicit research or synthesis dimension — students compose informational texts that integrate and attribute multiple sources with increasing sophistication. English II is the last station before research writing becomes the dominant mode.

DOK 3 justification: composing a genre-appropriate piece requires strategic application of multiple conventions and craft decisions; DOK 4 elements emerge in revision tasks that ask students to evaluate and defend their choices.

These question stems are written as STAAR-style revision-and-craft prompts, the primary mode through which informational composition TEKs appear on the assessment. The TEK itself is a production standard, not a reading standard, so STAAR tests it through revision scenarios — selecting, improving, or justifying a writing move.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Compose Argumentative Texts Using Genre Characteristics and Craft
expectation: Compose argumentative texts using genre characteristics and craft.
overview: This TEK is where students stop analyzing other people's arguments and build their own from the ground up — with full command of the conventions that make argumentative writing recognizable and persuasive. The real test is whether students understand that genre shapes argument: the claim, evidence, counterargument, and call-to-action aren't decorations, they're load-bearing walls.
studentFriendly: You can write a full argumentative piece — editorial, research-based essay, op-ed — that makes a real claim, backs it with evidence, handles the opposition, and uses the craft moves that make readers take you seriously.
questionStems: Which sentence from the student's draft BEST states a defensible claim?
The writer includes paragraph X MOST likely to —
Which revision to paragraph X would MOST strengthen the argument's logical structure?
Which evidence from the passage BEST supports the claim made in paragraph X?
The writer's use of concession in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which statement BEST describes how the writer addresses a counterargument?
Which sentence, if added to paragraph X, would MOST effectively develop the writer's reasoning?
The transition between paragraphs X and Y MOST likely serves to —
Which revision BEST improves the clarity and persuasiveness of the writer's conclusion?
The writer's word choice in paragraph X MOST likely signals to the reader that —

misconceptions.title: A Strong Opinion Is a Strong Argument
misconceptions.body: Students confuse conviction with craft. They write paragraphs dense with personal feeling and light on evidence, reasoning, or structure — and then resist revision because "that's what I believe." Teach them that an argument is not a container for opinion; it's a structure that moves a skeptical reader. Their belief is the starting point, not the product.

misconceptions.title: Counterargument Is Optional Charity
misconceptions.body: Students treat the counterargument paragraph like a bonus round — something to include if there's space or to look fair-minded. In reality, the counterargument is structural. Without it, the writer appears unaware that opposition exists. Watch for students who include counterargument at the end, after the conclusion — a signal they think it's decorative rather than load-bearing.

misconceptions.title: Evidence Speaks for Itself
misconceptions.body: Students drop a quote or statistic and move immediately to their next claim, assuming the evidence did the work. It didn't. Teach them the three-part move: introduce the evidence, present it, then explain precisely how it supports the claim. The explanation is where the argument lives. If the explanation isn't there, the evidence is just decoration.

misconceptions.title: Genre Means Format, Not Craft
misconceptions.body: Students learn "argumentative essay" as a template — intro, three body paragraphs, conclusion — and fill it in mechanically. They don't ask: What kind of argumentative piece is this? An op-ed moves differently than a research essay. A letter to a board moves differently than a policy brief. Push them to make conscious genre decisions: What does MY audience expect? What conventions does THIS argument form require?

misconceptions.title: Conceding Weakens the Argument
misconceptions.body: When students discover they must acknowledge the other side, they write the weakest possible version of the counterargument, then demolish it — a textbook strawman. Teach them that conceding a genuine point and then pivoting strengthens credibility; it signals to the reader that the writer has thought carefully and is still convinced. Concession done well wins trust; strawman done sloppily loses it.

assignments.name: Op-Ed Workshop
assignments.body: Students write a 400–600 word op-ed on a school or community issue. They must include an arguable claim, at least two pieces of evidence with explained reasoning, an honest counterargument with rebuttal, and a call to action. Peer editing focuses on one question: does the structure move a skeptical reader?

assignments.name: Claim Stress Test
assignments.body: Before drafting, students submit their claim sentence alone. Teacher and class interrogate it: Is it arguable? Is it specific? Could a reasonable person disagree? Students revise until the claim can hold weight, then draft from that foundation.

assignments.name: Argument Autopsy
assignments.body: Students read a published argumentative piece (editorial, letter to the editor, policy statement) and reverse-engineer it — labeling claim, evidence, reasoning, counterargument, and conclusion. Then they write their own piece mirroring the same structural logic on a different topic.

assignments.name: Live Revision Round
assignments.body: Students draft a body paragraph in class. The teacher projects three anonymous samples. Class identifies where the reasoning is explicit, where it's implied, and where it's absent. Students revise their own paragraph using the discussion.

mastery: States a specific, arguable claim that is neither a statement of fact nor a vague preference.
Selects evidence purposefully and explains — in their own words — precisely how that evidence supports the claim.
Addresses a genuine version of the counterargument and either refutes or concedes it with clear reasoning.
Controls genre conventions consciously — the piece reads as the intended form (essay, editorial, letter), not as a template filled in.
Uses transitional logic and sentence-level craft (not just connectors) to move the reader from claim through evidence to conclusion.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I 10C asks students to compose argumentative texts — English II holds that expectation but raises the bar on craft precision, counterargument handling, and genre awareness. By English III, students will be expected to incorporate research and sustain argument across longer, more complex structures.

DOK 4 justification: extended composition requiring original synthesis, structural decision-making, and defense of a position across a complete genre-appropriate text. Students must apply multiple criteria simultaneously — claim quality, evidence selection, reasoning, counterargument, craft — not execute a single procedure.

STAAR analog: this TEK is tested through the STAAR Expository/Argumentative writing prompt (Expository in earlier grades; Argumentative in high school). Stems above reflect the revision-task strand of STAAR Writing as well as the analytical reading of argumentative mentor texts.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Compose Correspondence in a Professional or Friendly Structure
expectation: Compose correspondence in a professional or friendly structure.
overview: This TEK is about knowing that the envelope changes the letter — that the same information written to a friend and written to a college admissions office must look, sound, and sit on the page differently. Students learn to control structural convention as a form of audience awareness, not as a bureaucratic formality.
studentFriendly: You can write a letter or email — whether to a future employer, a college, or a friend — and make choices about format, tone, and language that fit the situation and signal that you know what you're doing.
questionStems: The student's letter MOST likely uses the formal greeting "Dear Ms. Reyes:" rather than "Hey" to —
Which revision of the closing in the letter BEST maintains a professional tone throughout?
Read the letter in paragraphs 1–3. The structure the writer uses MOST likely signals that the audience is —
Which sentence from the letter BEST demonstrates that the writer understands the conventions of professional correspondence?
The writer's choice to include a subject line MOST likely serves to —
Based on the letter, the writer's use of first-person language in paragraph 2 is MOST likely intended to —
Which revision of paragraph 3 BEST corrects a shift from professional to informal register?
The friendly letter's structure differs from the formal letter's structure MOST significantly in —
Which sentence from the draft MOST clearly indicates the writer's purpose for writing?
A student revising a professional letter notices that one paragraph includes slang. The revision that BEST maintains the appropriate register is —

misconceptions.title: Formal Means Stiff
misconceptions.body: Students treat professional correspondence like a legal deposition — no personality, no warmth, no first person. They write in passive constructions and strip out anything that sounds human, then wonder why the letter feels lifeless. Teach them that professionalism is about precision and structure, not about eliminating the human voice. A professional letter can be warm, direct, and specific without crossing into casual territory.

misconceptions.title: Friendly Letters Don't Have Rules
misconceptions.body: Students assume the friendly format is just "write however you want." They skip structural elements — date, salutation, body with a clear purpose, closing — because they think informality means formlessness. Watch for students who turn friendly correspondence into a text message with paragraph breaks. The friendly format has conventions; they're just warmer ones.

misconceptions.title: The Greeting and Closing Are Decorations
misconceptions.body: Students bolt on "Dear Sir or Madam" and "Sincerely" without understanding that these elements encode relationship and register. They'll mix a casual opening with a stiff closing, or open formally and close with "Thanks!!" Push them to treat the greeting and closing as tonal bookends — the reader gets their first and last impression of your register from those two lines.

misconceptions.title: One Draft Fits Both Purposes
misconceptions.body: Students write one version of a letter and assume light editing — swapping a few words — is enough to shift it between professional and friendly contexts. Teach them that the two formats require different organizational logic, not just different vocabulary. A professional letter often leads with purpose; a friendly letter might open with connection. The underlying architecture is different.

misconceptions.title: Audience Is Just About Word Choice
misconceptions.body: When students think about audience, they go to vocabulary — they'll swap "use" for "utilize" and call it professional. They miss the structural signals that do just as much audience work: indentation vs. block format, whether to include a subject line, how much context to provide before making a request. Teach them that structure is itself a message — before the reader reads a word, the layout tells them who they're dealing with.

assignments.name: Side-by-Side Correspondence
assignments.body: Students write two letters about the same situation — for example, requesting a letter of recommendation — once in professional format to a teacher and once in friendly format to a mentor they know well. They annotate each letter identifying three structural and tonal differences they made and why.

assignments.name: Correspondence Audit
assignments.body: Students receive a set of four scrambled letters — two professional, two friendly, with headers and closings removed. They sort them, label the format, and write a paragraph explaining the specific structural evidence that told them which was which.

assignments.name: Revision with a Reason
assignments.body: Students receive a draft professional letter that has three deliberate errors: one structural (wrong block format), one tonal (slang in the body), and one register mismatch in the closing. They identify each error, revise the letter, and write one sentence per correction explaining what convention was violated.

mastery: Selects the correct structural format — block or indented, subject line or no, colon or comma after salutation — based on whether the correspondence is professional or friendly.
Maintains consistent register throughout a piece of correspondence, including opening, body, and closing.
Identifies an intended audience and makes deliberate structural and tonal choices that reflect that audience, not generic defaults.
Can revise a correspondence sample to correct format errors, tonal shifts, or structural omissions without prompting.
Explains in writing why specific structural choices (greeting, layout, closing) are appropriate for the genre and audience.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I introduces correspondence conventions at the same DOK 2 level; English III and IV extend the skill by requiring more sophisticated audience analysis, multi-purpose correspondence, and possibly correspondence within research or argumentative writing contexts. The English II entry consolidates format knowledge and begins asking students to make purposeful tonal choices, not just reproduce structural templates.

DOK 2 justification: the skill requires applying established structural conventions to a specific purpose and audience — a procedural skill with decision-making embedded, not pure recall, but not yet extended analysis or critique.

STAAR analog note: correspondence as a standalone genre does not appear as a reading passage on STAAR, but writing-prompt scenarios often require students to recognize appropriate register and structure; the question stems above are modeled on how STAAR tests genre awareness through revision tasks and author's-purpose questions applied to student-produced texts.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Develop Questions for Formal and Informal Inquiry
expectation: Develop questions for formal and informal inquiry.
overview: The research process lives or dies at the question stage. English II students often treat inquiry as a search task — they think the research begins when they open a browser. This standard insists otherwise: before sources, before evidence, before claims, there must be a question worth asking. The skill is in knowing the difference between a question that drives a project and one that just fills a box on the research form.
studentFriendly: You can craft questions that are focused enough to actually research — knowing when a question is too broad, too narrow, or too easy to look up, and understanding that formal and informal inquiry call for different kinds of questions.
questionStems: Based on paragraphs X–Y, which question BEST reflects the author's central inquiry?
Which question from the list below is MOST appropriate for a formal research project on [topic]?
Read this student's research question: [question]. Which revision BEST improves its focus and researchability?
The author MOST likely developed the question in paragraph X in order to —
Which question would BEST guide a reader seeking to understand the author's purpose in the selection?
Based on the passage, which question could be MOST effectively answered using the author's evidence?
Which question is MOST likely too broad to serve as the guiding question for a focused research project?
The research question in paragraph X contributes to the argument by —
Which question from the passage BEST distinguishes between a factual inquiry and an interpretive inquiry?
Based on the selection, which question MOST likely shaped the author's approach to gathering evidence?

misconceptions.title: A Good Research Question Is One You Don't Already Know the Answer To
misconceptions.body: Students think any question they personally can't answer counts as a research question. But "How many bones are in the human body?" is unknown to them and useless as an inquiry driver — it has a single lookable answer. Teach them the difference between a reference question and a research question: research questions require interpretation, synthesis, or argument. A good question doesn't just have an answer; it has competing answers worth weighing.

misconceptions.title: "Formal" Means Harder and "Informal" Means Easier
misconceptions.body: Students read "formal inquiry" as a synonym for "big assignment" and "informal inquiry" as "quick look it up." The actual distinction is about rigor of process and audience expectation — formal inquiry involves structured sources, documented methods, and accountable claims; informal inquiry might be exploratory, conversational, or personal. Teach them that informal inquiry can be intellectually demanding and that the label signals process, not difficulty.

misconceptions.title: Narrowing the Question Means Shrinking the Topic
misconceptions.body: When students are told their question is too broad, they respond by adding a qualifier — "How does social media affect teenagers in Texas?" instead of "How does social media affect teenagers?" — and consider the job done. Push them deeper: narrowing means sharpening the KIND of claim the question invites, not just shrinking geography or population. The test is whether the question tells you what kind of evidence would actually answer it.

misconceptions.title: Questions Come Before the Research, So They Can't Be Revised
misconceptions.body: Students treat the initial inquiry question as locked once the project begins. They resist revising it even when their reading reveals that the original question was misframed, unanswerable, or already settled. Teach them that question revision is a sign of intellectual growth, not failure — good researchers change their question when the evidence demands it.

misconceptions.title: All Questions Are Equally Neutral
misconceptions.body: Students assume that asking a question is the same as being unbiased, because questions don't make claims. But questions carry embedded assumptions — "Why is [X] failing?" presupposes failure; "Which approach is better?" presupposes comparability. Watch for students whose questions pre-answer themselves. Teach them to audit their own questions for hidden premises before they commit to a research direction.

assignments.name: Question Triage
assignments.body: Give students a list of 12–15 questions on a shared topic (some too broad, some too narrow, some factual dead-ends, some genuinely researchable). Students sort them into categories and write one sentence defending each placement. Debrief as a class on the hardest calls.

assignments.name: Formal vs. Informal Question Pair
assignments.body: Students pick a topic they're curious about and write two versions of their inquiry question — one calibrated for a formal research project, one for an exploratory personal essay. They then explain in writing what changed and why. Length: one paragraph per question.

assignments.name: Question Audit
assignments.body: Students bring their current research question to class and swap with a partner. Partners interrogate the question: What assumption does it make? What would actually answer it? What kind of evidence would you need? Writers revise based on feedback and submit both versions with a reflection.

assignments.name: Research Question Evolution Log
assignments.body: During a multi-week research project, students document their guiding question at three checkpoints — beginning, midpoint, and end — and write a paragraph at the end explaining how and why the question shifted.

mastery: Generates questions that require interpretation or synthesis, not just retrieval of a single fact.
Distinguishes between questions suited to formal inquiry (structured, source-dependent, claim-driven) and informal inquiry (exploratory, open-ended, audience-flexible).
Revises initial questions when research reveals the original framing was too broad, too narrow, or based on a false premise.
Audits questions for embedded assumptions before committing to a research direction.
Matches the scope and type of question to the time, sources, and genre the project allows.
notes: Vertical ramp: English I introduces inquiry questions at a foundational level — identifying researchable questions from given scenarios. English II asks students to DEVELOP those questions themselves across both formal and informal contexts, shifting from recognition to generation. English III will push toward refining questions iteratively in response to emerging evidence, adding a recursive dimension.

DOK 3 justification: generating and evaluating inquiry questions requires strategic thinking — students must reason about what makes a question researchable, anticipate what kinds of evidence it invites, and revise based on intellectual feedback. This is above recall or application.

This TEK has limited direct STAAR testing but surfaces in research-embedded on-demand tasks; the question stems above mirror how STAAR tests research process through paired-passage and author-purpose frames. Note in planning that the standard's work is best assessed through the research project itself, not a standalone passage response.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: 110.37(11)(A.i) — Use an Appropriate Mode of Delivery to Present Research Results
expectation: Use an appropriate mode of delivery, whether written, oral, or multimodal, to present results.
overview: This is the capstone move of the inquiry cycle: students don't just complete research, they choose HOW to release it into the world. The TEK forces a rhetorical decision — written, oral, or multimodal — and the word "appropriate" is where all the thinking lives. Purpose, audience, and content should drive the choice, not habit or convenience.
studentFriendly: You decide whether your research should be a paper, a presentation, a video, a podcast, or some combination — and that choice has to make sense for your topic, your audience, and what you're actually trying to do with the information.
questionStems: A student wants to present research about noise pollution in urban schools to a school board that will vote on the issue next week. Which mode of delivery would MOST likely be the BEST choice?

Read the following passage from a student's research presentation. The student chose a multimodal format for this project. Which detail from the passage BEST supports the conclusion that this choice was appropriate for the audience?
Based on paragraphs 1–3 of the student's written report, which revision would MOST likely improve the clarity of the research findings for a general audience?
Which sentence from the student's oral presentation BEST establishes the credibility of the research?
A student researching the effects of social media on teen sleep is deciding between a written report and a video essay. Which consideration MOST directly should influence this decision?

The student chose to present findings in a multimodal format that combined a live demonstration with a written handout. Which of the following BEST explains why this combination of modes is effective?

Read the following excerpt from a student's research conclusion. The student delivered this section orally rather than in writing. Based on the excerpt, which claim about the delivery choice can MOST reasonably be inferred?
Which sentence from the research report BEST demonstrates that the student has matched the formality of the writing to the intended audience?

A student presenting original research to peers used charts, recorded interviews, and a brief spoken narration. Based on this description, which statement MOST accurately characterizes the student's delivery mode?
Which revision to the student's written research summary would MOST likely make the presentation of results clearer to a reader unfamiliar with the topic?

misconceptions.title: "Multimodal" Just Means a PowerPoint
misconceptions.body: Students think checking the multimodal box means adding a few slides. Push them to see multimodal as a genuine integration of modes — where the visual, the verbal, and the aural each carry different parts of the meaning and couldn't be stripped out without losing something. A slideshow that just mirrors spoken words isn't multimodal; it's an oral report with a backdrop.

misconceptions.title: The Written Report Is Always the Default
misconceptions.body: When students aren't sure what to do, they default to a five-paragraph paper regardless of topic or audience. Watch for research about urgent local issues landing as dry essays that will never reach anyone who needs the information. Teach them that the default should be a question: who needs to act on this, and how do they receive information? The answer to that determines the mode.

misconceptions.title: "Appropriate" Means Whatever Is Easiest
misconceptions.body: Students read "appropriate" as permission to choose whatever they're most comfortable with. Reframe it as a constraint: appropriate means the mode actually serves the content and audience — which sometimes means stretching into a format they haven't used before. Comfort is a reason to practice a mode, not a reason to pick it.

misconceptions.title: Oral Delivery Doesn't Need the Same Rigor as Writing
misconceptions.body: Students treat oral presentations as informal — less structured, less sourced, easier to wing. But presenting research orally demands the same evidentiary discipline as a written report. Watch for students who cite everything in the paper and then gestures vaguely at "scientists" when speaking. The mode changes; the intellectual standards don't.

misconceptions.title: Choosing the Mode Is the Last Step
misconceptions.body: Students often pick their delivery format after the research is done, treating it as a packaging decision. Teach them that mode should inform how they gather and organize information. A researcher planning a podcast interviews sources; one planning a data visualization prioritizes quantitative evidence. The mode shapes the inquiry, not just the output.

assignments.name: Mode Pitch
assignments.body: Before students begin presenting, they must write a one-paragraph rationale for their chosen delivery mode — identifying their audience, explaining why this format serves both the content and that audience, and naming one format they rejected and why. Evaluated before the presentation.

assignments.name: Same Research, Two Modes
assignments.body: Pairs of students take the same body of research and each present it in a different mode — one written, one oral or multimodal. They then compare: what did each mode allow? What did each lose? Class debrief on the implications.

assignments.name: Audience Alignment Audit
assignments.body: Students are assigned a specific real-world audience for their research results (a city council, a middle school class, a college admissions committee, a local nonprofit). They must select the delivery mode that fits and justify the decision in a brief cover memo before presenting.

assignments.name: Multimodal Deconstruction
assignments.body: Provide students with three examples of research presented in different modes on the same topic. Students analyze which mode is most effective and why, using specific evidence from each example. Written analysis, one page.

mastery: Selects a delivery mode based on explicit reasoning about audience, purpose, and content — not habit or familiarity.
Executes the chosen mode with the same rigor as any other: sourced, structured, and calibrated for the intended reader or listener.
In multimodal work, demonstrates genuine integration of modes — each element carries meaning the others don't fully carry alone.
Can articulate, when asked, why a different mode would have been less effective for this particular research and audience.
Adjusts register and formality within the chosen mode to match the context of the presentation.
notes: Vertical ramp: English I establishes the same expectation — students choose written, oral, or multimodal for presenting inquiry results. English III holds the same language but within a research cycle that demands greater complexity of sources and synthesis. The skill itself doesn't change across grades; what changes is the sophistication of the research being delivered and the independence of the mode decision.

DOK 3 / Apply-Evaluate: selecting and executing an appropriate mode requires strategic thinking about rhetorical context, not just task completion; Evaluate enters when students must justify the choice against alternatives.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog as a standalone performance standard, but the reasoning it demands — matching form to purpose and audience — maps closely to author's craft questions on STAAR (e.g., why does the author present this information visually rather than in prose?). Stems written to mirror that analog. See Notes in HANDOFF.md on oral/performance TEKs.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Critique the Research Process at Each Step
expectation: Critique the research process at each step to implement changes as needs occur and are identified.
overview: This TEK is about metacognition inside the research process — students don't just do research, they audit it as they go. The failure mode this standard targets is the student who collects whatever shows up first, never checks whether the process is working, and submits without questioning what they skipped, missed, or botched. The skill is recursive self-critique baked into the workflow itself.
studentFriendly: You can stop at any point during a research project, honestly evaluate whether what you've done so far is actually working, and make real changes — not just push through to the finish line.
questionStems: Read the passage. The researcher in paragraph X MOST likely revises the research question because —
Which detail from paragraphs X–Y BEST supports the conclusion that the researcher's initial source selection was inadequate?
Based on paragraph X, what change to the research process would MOST likely improve the quality of the evidence gathered?
The researcher's decision in paragraph X to consult additional sources MOST nearly suggests that —
Which sentence from the passage BEST illustrates the researcher identifying a flaw in the research process?
Based on the information in paragraphs X–Y, the researcher's adjustment to the search strategy MOST likely indicates —
Which of the following BEST describes how the researcher in paragraph X responds to gaps in the evidence?
The researcher's critique of source selection in paragraph X contributes MOST to —
Based on paragraph X, what inference can the reader make about the effectiveness of the researcher's initial approach?
Which sentence from the passage BEST demonstrates the researcher implementing a change after identifying a problem in the process?

misconceptions.title: Critiquing the Process Means Critiquing the Sources
misconceptions.body: Students think evaluating their research means deciding whether their sources are credible — so they run a quick CRAAP-test checklist and call it done. Teach them that process critique is wider than that: it means asking whether their research question was the right question, whether their search strategy surfaced the most useful material, whether they've triangulated claims, and whether gaps in the evidence are the gap or their gap. Source evaluation is one gear in a larger machine.

misconceptions.title: Changes Mean Starting Over
misconceptions.body: When students find a problem mid-process — a research question that's too narrow, a source type they neglected, a claim they can't support — they interpret critique as failure and shut down rather than adjust. Teach them that implementing changes is the point of critique, not a sign the project is broken. Reframing a question in week two is good research, not wasted work. Watch for students who turn in a weaker paper rather than acknowledge mid-project that something needs to shift.

misconceptions.title: Self-Critique Happens at the End
misconceptions.body: Most students treat process critique as a reflection prompt they fill out after submitting — a postmortem, not a live intervention. This TEK specifies "at each step," which means the critique is concurrent with the work. Push them to build in formal checkpoints: after source collection, after outlining, after drafting — not just at submission. The student who only reflects after the fact can name the problem but can't fix it.

misconceptions.title: A Good Researcher Gets It Right the First Time
misconceptions.body: Students romanticize the idea of a clean, linear research process and feel embarrassed when their first direction proves unproductive. Teach them that professional researchers pivot constantly — narrowing questions, abandoning dead ends, backfilling sources. The willingness to redirect is a mark of sophistication, not weakness. Watch for students who stay on a bad track because they've already invested time in it; sunk-cost thinking kills research quality.

misconceptions.title: Critique Requires Certainty About What Went Wrong
misconceptions.body: Students stall on self-critique because they feel they can't name what's wrong without already knowing the answer. Teach them that productive critique is often phrased as a question, not a verdict: "Is this the right scope?" "Am I missing a perspective?" "Does this evidence actually prove what I think it does?" Uncertainty is the starting point for critique, not evidence that critique isn't possible yet.

assignments.name: Research Process Journal
assignments.body: Students keep a running log — one entry per major step — documenting what they did, what they expected to find, and what they actually found. At each entry they must name one thing they would change and explain the adjustment. Graded for honesty and specificity, not for having a "clean" process.

assignments.name: Red-Light Checkpoint Protocol
assignments.body: At three assigned moments in a research unit (after question-setting, after source collection, after outlining), students pause and complete a structured self-critique form: What was the plan? What happened? What needs to change? They submit the forms with the final project so the teacher can see whether changes were actually implemented.

assignments.name: Process Autopsy Presentation
assignments.body: After submitting a research paper, students give a 3-minute talk on what went wrong and what they changed — not the content of the paper but the decisions inside the research process. Students who had a smooth process must still find three genuine moments of adjustment. Grade the quality of the critique, not the smoothness of the story.

mastery: Identifies specific problems at each stage of the research process — not just at submission — and names changes made in response.
Distinguishes between critiquing sources and critiquing the process itself, addressing scope, strategy, and question framing as well as source quality.
Demonstrates willingness to redirect mid-process, including revising the research question or search strategy when the evidence demands it.
Produces written or oral reflection that names concrete adjustments and explains why each change improved the research, not just that a change was made.
Treats process critique as iterative and concurrent rather than a one-time end-of-project reflection.
notes: Vertical ramp: English I Std 11(B) asks students to "follow a research plan" and make adjustments; English II upgrades that to active *critique* at each step — the move is from reactive adjustment to deliberate, ongoing evaluation. English III will ask students to synthesize across sources and develop original arguments, which depends on the recursive self-monitoring this standard builds.

DOK 4 justification: the standard requires extended, self-directed evaluation across multiple steps with no single correct answer — students must apply criteria they develop against their own process, then act on judgments they reach.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog as a standalone tested expectation, but the skill surfaces in research-scenario reading passages where students evaluate a fictional researcher's decision-making. Question stems above mirror that STAAR framing.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Develop and Revise a Research Plan
expectation: Develop and revise a plan.
overview: Research fails before it starts when students treat the plan as a formality they complete once and ignore. This TEK is about building a live document — a plan that changes as the research changes — so that inquiry is genuinely recursive rather than a one-shot outline dressed up as a process.
studentFriendly: You can build a research plan that actually guides your work — and change it when your sources, your question, or your thinking shifts.
questionStems: Which revision to the student's research plan MOST likely improved the focus of the inquiry?
Based on the research plan excerpt in paragraph X, what is the MOST likely next step the student should take?
Which sentence from the research plan BEST supports the claim that the student has narrowed the topic appropriately?
The student revised the research question in paragraph X MOST likely because —
Which change to the research plan would MOST effectively address the gap identified in paragraph Y?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, what does the student's revised plan suggest about the direction of the inquiry?
Which of the following additions to the research plan would BEST help the student locate credible sources?
The student's decision to revise the timeline in paragraph X MOST likely reflects —
Which sentence from paragraph X BEST identifies the weakness in the original research plan?
Based on the information in paragraphs X and Y, which revision to the plan would MOST likely lead to a stronger final product?

misconceptions.title: The Plan Is Something You Turn In, Not Something You Use
misconceptions.body: Students write a research plan to satisfy the assignment, then set it aside and research however they want. Teach them that the plan is a working tool — it should be visibly marked up, annotated, revised. If it looks pristine at the end of the project, they didn't use it.

misconceptions.title: Revising the Plan Means You Failed the First Time
misconceptions.body: Students resist revision because they read it as correction of error — they did it wrong and now they have to redo it. Reframe this explicitly: revision is evidence that the research is working. A plan that doesn't change means the sources didn't teach you anything. Push them to name WHAT changed in their thinking, not just WHAT changed in the document.

misconceptions.title: A Narrower Topic Is Always Better
misconceptions.body: Students conflate "focused" with "tiny." They compress a topic so aggressively that there's nothing to find, then give up when one search returns three results. Teach them that scope is a dial — turn it until you have a workable question, not until you've eliminated all the interesting parts. Watch for topics that look specific but are actually just obscure.

misconceptions.title: The Research Question Can't Change Once It's Approved
misconceptions.body: Students lock the question in the moment a teacher signs off on it and treat any deviation as cheating. In reality, the question should evolve as sources reveal what's actually arguable and what the evidence actually supports. Teach them to document the evolution — "my original question was X, but I revised it to Y because Z" is stronger academic thinking than a static question answered mechanically.

misconceptions.title: More Sources in the Plan Means a Better Plan
misconceptions.body: Students pad the plan with a long source list to look thorough, without verifying relevance or credibility. The plan's value isn't in its length — it's in the thinking it records. A plan with six well-evaluated sources and clear rationale beats a plan with twenty links and no annotation. Watch for students who copy-paste URLs and call it research planning.

assignments.name: Plan Archaeology
assignments.body: Give students a research plan from a fictional student who is three weeks into a project and has clearly gone off the rails. Students diagnose what went wrong in the plan, write a revised version, and explain the changes they made and why.

assignments.name: Live Revision Log
assignments.body: Students maintain a running revision log alongside their research plan — a parallel document where they record every change, why they made it, and what source or discovery triggered it. The log is submitted with the final project and graded separately.

assignments.name: Plan Peer Review Protocol
assignments.body: Pairs exchange research plans at the midpoint of a project. Each student writes a one-page structured response identifying one strength, one gap in scope, one potential source the partner missed, and one revision recommendation. Partners use the feedback to update their plans before continuing.

mastery: Produces a research plan that includes a focused question, a proposed line of inquiry, identified source types, and a realistic timeline — and can explain the reasoning behind each element.
Revises the plan at least once based on what sources reveal, and can articulate specifically what changed and why.
Distinguishes between a plan that is too broad, too narrow, and appropriately scoped — and can adjust scope without abandoning the central inquiry.
Treats the plan as a thinking document, with visible evidence of annotation, revision, and decision-making rather than a static form.
notes: Vertical ramp: English I 11(C) establishes the concept of planning as part of inquiry. English II adds revision as an explicit, named expectation — the plan is not a static artifact. English III pushes further into synthesizing across multiple sources and refining the inquiry in response to complexity, not just new information.

DOK 3 justification: developing and revising a plan requires strategic reasoning — students must evaluate their current direction, identify gaps or misalignments, and redesign accordingly. It is not recall (DOK 1) or application of a fixed procedure (DOK 2).

TEA expectation is minimal ("develop and revise a plan") — scope, revision criteria, and plan components are teacher-defined. Align plan requirements with the paired composition strand (Std 9) so research planning and writing process reinforce each other.

STAAR does not test this TEK directly; stems above mirror how released STAAR items frame research-process scenarios (student plans, research excerpts, revision decisions) in the Expository and Argumentative composition contexts.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Modify the Major Research Question to Refocus the Research Plan
expectation: Modify the major research question as necessary to refocus the research plan.
overview: Real research doesn't proceed in a straight line — the question that launches a project is rarely the question that ends it. This TEK asks students to recognize when the evidence they're finding is pulling them somewhere the original question didn't anticipate, and to make the harder move: revise the question rather than force the evidence to fit.
studentFriendly: You know how to look at what you've found so far in a research project and decide whether your original question still makes sense — or whether the evidence is telling you to ask something sharper, narrower, or completely different.
questionStems: Based on the information in paragraphs X–Y, which research question would MOST likely need to be modified to reflect the evidence gathered?
A student researching [topic] finds that most sources address [subtopic] instead of [original focus]. Which revised question BEST refocuses the student's research plan?
Which piece of evidence from the passage would MOST likely cause a researcher to narrow the original question?
Based on the passage, the researcher's original question is too broad because —
Which revision of the research question BEST accounts for the evidence presented in paragraphs X–Y?
The researcher in paragraph X modifies the research question in order to —
Which statement BEST explains why the researcher refocuses the research plan in paragraph Y?
Based on the sources gathered, which research question would produce the MOST focused and answerable investigation?
The evidence in paragraphs X–Z suggests that the original research question should be revised to address —
Which of the following MOST likely explains why a researcher would modify a major research question midway through the research process?

misconceptions.title: Changing the Question Means Starting Over
misconceptions.body: Students treat the original research question as a contract — if they change it, they've wasted everything that came before. Reframe this immediately: a revised question doesn't discard prior research; it sharpens the lens on what they've already found. Teach them that the most productive moments in research are often the ones where the question has to move.

misconceptions.title: A Good Research Question Stays Fixed
misconceptions.body: Students (and some teachers) conflate a focused question with a fixed one. Push them to see these as different qualities: focus is about precision, not rigidity. A question can be highly focused and still require revision when the evidence reveals a more productive angle. Watch for students who resist modification as a point of pride — they think discipline means sticking to the original plan.

misconceptions.title: The Question Only Needs to Get Narrower
misconceptions.body: Students default to narrowing as the only legitimate form of modification — they assume they're correcting a question that was too broad. But questions can also need to broaden, reframe, or pivot entirely. Teach them to ask: is my evidence telling me the question is too big, too small, or simply pointed at the wrong thing?

misconceptions.title: Modifying the Question Is Admitting Failure
misconceptions.body: Some students treat research plan revisions the way they treat wrong answers on a test — evidence of inadequacy. They'll contort their argument to fit the original question rather than acknowledge the question needs work. Teach them that professional researchers revise their questions constantly; the failure is not in revising, it's in refusing to.

misconceptions.title: Any New Question Will Do
misconceptions.body: When students do accept that the question needs changing, they sometimes replace it too casually — swapping one vague question for another. The point of modification is precision: the revised question should be more answerable, more grounded in the actual evidence, and narrower in scope than the original. Watch for revisions that just restate the topic with different words.

assignments.name: Research Pivot Log
assignments.body: As students work through a multi-source research project, they keep a running log documenting their original question, what each major source revealed, and any point at which the question needed modification. Final submission includes both the original and revised question with a written justification for any changes.

assignments.name: The Question Autopsy
assignments.body: Give students a short passage describing a fictional researcher's process, including the evidence they found and the question they started with. Students evaluate whether the original question should be modified, write a revised version, and explain specifically what in the evidence prompted the change.

assignments.name: Peer Question Review
assignments.body: At the midpoint of a research unit, students swap research plans with a partner. Each student reads the partner's sources and writes a one-paragraph response addressing whether the original question still fits the evidence — and what a sharper question might look like. Writers then decide whether to accept the suggested revision, modify it, or defend the original with evidence.

mastery: Reads across gathered sources and identifies when the evidence is pointing toward a different question than the one originally posed.
Writes a revised research question that is more precise, more answerable, and more grounded in the evidence than the original.
Articulates a clear rationale for any modification — names specifically what the evidence revealed that made the original question insufficient.
Distinguishes between a question that needs narrowing, broadening, or full reframing — and applies the appropriate revision.
Treats research plan modification as a normal, productive step rather than an indication of prior error.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I Std 11D asks students to modify the research question as necessary; English II holds the same expectation but within a more demanding research context — longer projects, more sources, greater source complexity — meaning the judgment required to recognize when revision is needed is proportionally harder. English III and IV continue to build on this metacognitive flexibility within increasingly independent research structures.
