DOK 3 justification: Students must evaluate the fit between evidence and question, then produce a revised question — this requires strategic thinking and self-monitoring, not just recall or skill application.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog in the tested genres; question stems above mirror how STAAR would frame research-process evaluation through a passage about a researcher's methodology or through a multi-source research simulation prompt.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Locate Relevant Sources
expectation: Locate relevant sources.
overview: This expectation is deceptively simple. The move isn't just finding sources — it's finding the RIGHT ones for a specific research purpose, which requires students to apply judgment about relevance before they've fully formed their argument. Most students treat source-location as a Google problem; teach them it's a thinking problem.
studentFriendly: You can find sources that are actually useful to your specific research question — not just sources that mention the topic, but sources that provide the kind of evidence your argument actually needs.
questionStems: A student is researching [topic] for an argumentative essay. Which source would MOST likely provide the BEST evidence for the student's claim?
Based on the research question in paragraph X, which source would be MOST relevant to the student's inquiry?
Which detail from the source list BEST indicates that a source is relevant to [specific research focus]?
A student wants to support the claim in paragraph X. Which source would MOST likely contain relevant evidence for that claim?
Which of the following BEST describes why Source Y would NOT be relevant to a research question about [topic]?
A student is narrowing a research topic from [broad subject] to [specific focus]. Which source would MOST likely remain relevant after the narrowing?
Based on the information in paragraph X, the student should MOST likely search for sources that —
Which sentence from the student's research notes BEST explains why Source Z was set aside as irrelevant?
A student finds two sources that both address [topic]. Which detail would BEST help the student determine which source is more relevant to the research question?
The student in paragraph X is conducting research on [topic]. Which search strategy would MOST likely locate the most relevant sources?

misconceptions.title: Relevance Means "About the Same Topic"
misconceptions.body: Students equate relevance with topical overlap — if the source mentions the subject, it's relevant. Teach them relevance is about fit: does this source address the specific angle, time period, audience, or claim the research requires? A source about climate change is not automatically relevant to a paper about climate policy in Texas in 2020. Push them to articulate exactly HOW a source serves their argument before they log it.

misconceptions.title: The First Three Results Are Good Enough
misconceptions.body: Students treat search results like vending machines — whatever comes out first is what they use. Watch for students who cite the same handful of top-ranking sites across every paper because they stopped looking after page one. Teach them that relevance is discovered through a search process, not a single query, and that the most useful sources are often buried behind better search terms.

misconceptions.title: More Sources Mean Better Research
misconceptions.body: Students pad bibliographies to hit a source count, treating quantity as a proxy for thoroughness. The result is research that buries one or two genuinely relevant sources under six that do nothing. Teach them that a tightly relevant source list is a mark of intellectual control, not laziness. Three sources that speak directly to the claim beat eight that vaguely orbit it.

misconceptions.title: A Source's Credibility Determines Its Relevance
misconceptions.body: Students conflate credibility and relevance, assuming a peer-reviewed article is automatically relevant because it's trustworthy, or dismissing a primary source because it "looks old." Reframe this: credibility asks whether to trust a source; relevance asks whether the source answers the specific question. A highly credible source about the wrong subtopic is still the wrong source.

misconceptions.title: Relevance Is Settled at the Start of Research
misconceptions.body: Students lock in their source list early and stop looking, even when their argument evolves. Teach them that relevance is a moving target — as the thesis sharpens, sources that seemed useful may drop out and sources initially overlooked may become central. Source-location isn't a one-time task at the beginning of a research project; it recurs every time the argument shifts.

assignments.name: Relevance Sorting
assignments.body: Give students a stack of 10 pre-selected sources (mix of relevant and irrelevant) on a common topic. They must sort them into "relevant," "possibly relevant," and "not relevant" piles and write one sentence justifying each placement. Discuss disagreements as a class.

assignments.name: Narrowing the Net
assignments.body: Students begin with a broad topic and a list of initial sources, then narrow their research question twice. After each narrowing, they must cull their source list and explain in writing which sources survive the cut and why. The goal is to show that relevance is question-dependent, not topic-dependent.

assignments.name: Search Log and Reflection
assignments.body: Students document three separate search attempts for the same research question — using different databases, search terms, or filters each time — and record which attempt yielded the most relevant results. They write a one-paragraph reflection explaining what they learned about locating sources that the first attempt couldn't have taught them.

mastery: Articulates why a specific source is relevant to their particular research question rather than to the general topic.
Adjusts search terms and databases based on what a research question actually requires — not just what the topic is called.
Culls source lists when the argument narrows or shifts, rather than carrying forward every source gathered early in the process.
Distinguishes between a source being credible and a source being relevant, and applies both criteria independently.
Locates sources across multiple databases or repositories rather than defaulting to the same two or three starting points.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I establishes the basic habit of locating sources for a research question. English II maintains the same expectation but within a more complex inquiry context — students are expected to locate sources that serve increasingly specific and arguable claims, not just gather background information. English III sharpens this further by pairing source location with synthesis and evaluation as integrated, concurrent skills.

DOK 2 justification: locating relevant sources requires applying criteria (relevance, fit to question) to new situations — beyond recall, but the judgment is skill-based rather than extended analytical reasoning. If paired with evaluation or synthesis in a task, the composite DOK rises to 3 or 4.

STAAR analog note: this TEK does not appear as a standalone tested standard but is embedded in research task performance. Question stems above mirror how STAAR assesses research process skills — source selection embedded in a student research scenario passage.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Synthesize Information from a Variety of Sources
expectation: Synthesize information from a variety of sources.
overview: Synthesis is the move that separates research from summarizing — students must take what multiple sources say, find the relationships between them, and produce a new, unified claim. This is the hardest cognitive step in the inquiry strand, and most students avoid it by writing a series of source summaries and calling it done.
studentFriendly: You can read several different sources on the same topic, figure out what they agree and disagree about, and build your own new understanding that goes beyond what any single source says.
questionStems: Which statement BEST synthesizes the information presented in both passages?
Based on paragraphs X–Y of both sources, what conclusion can MOST likely be drawn about —
The author of Source 2 would MOST likely agree with which claim made in Source 1?
Which sentence from Source 3 BEST supports the central idea shared across all three passages?
How does the information in Source 2 contribute to a deeper understanding of the argument made in Source 1?
Based on the information in Sources 1 and 2, which inference about [topic] is MOST strongly supported?
A student is writing a research paper about [topic]. Which statement BEST combines the evidence from all three sources?
Which detail from Source 3 MOST directly challenges a claim made in Source 1?
The information in paragraphs X–Y of Source 2 develops the central idea of Source 1 by —
Based on all three sources, what is the MOST likely reason that —

misconceptions.title: Synthesis Means "Now I'll Summarize Each Source in Order"
misconceptions.body: Students produce three consecutive paragraphs — one per source — and submit it as synthesis. Watch for this pattern: the sources never talk to each other. Real synthesis names the relationship first (these sources agree, contradict, complicate each other) and then pulls evidence from multiple sources to build a single unified point. Teach them to draft their claim before they start writing, not from the sources, but across them.

misconceptions.title: The Source with the Most Pages Is the Most Important
misconceptions.body: Students weight sources by volume — longer article, more credible, more space in the paper. Push them to read for relevance and specificity, not length. A two-paragraph policy brief can outweigh a 20-page academic article if it directly addresses the student's claim. The question is always: what does this source contribute to MY argument, not how big is it?

misconceptions.title: All Sources Must Agree for Synthesis to Work
misconceptions.body: When sources contradict each other, students panic and either drop one or pretend the disagreement doesn't exist. Teach them that contradiction is the richest research material they have — it signals a live debate, an unsettled question, or a perspective gap. The synthesis move is to name the tension and take a position on it, not to paper over it.

misconceptions.title: Synthesis Is Something You Do at the End
misconceptions.body: Students collect and annotate sources, then try to synthesize in the final draft — at which point they have no time. Synthesis is a reading process, not a writing process. Teach them to begin noticing relationships between sources as they read: annotate with margin notes like "source 2 complicates this" or "both agree here." The writing is just externalizing what synthesis has already done in their heads.

misconceptions.title: Your Own Voice Disappears in Synthesis
misconceptions.body: Students assume research writing means disappearing behind the sources. They write "Source 1 says... Source 2 says... Source 3 says..." with no governing claim. The student's voice is the synthesis — it decides what the sources mean together, what matters, what to argue. Watch for papers where you can delete every sentence that isn't a quotation or paraphrase and nothing is lost. That's the signal that the student hasn't synthesized anything.

assignments.name: Source Relationship Map
assignments.body: Before drafting, students create a visual map of three sources showing where they agree, disagree, and leave gaps. Each connection on the map must be labeled with the relationship type (confirms, contradicts, extends, complicates). The map is graded before the paper is written.

assignments.name: Single-Paragraph Synthesis Sprint
assignments.body: Students receive three short excerpts on the same topic and have 20 minutes to write one paragraph that synthesizes all three — no summaries, no "Source 1 says." They must produce a claim and weave evidence from all three sources to support it.

assignments.name: Debate the Sources
assignments.body: Two students are assigned opposing sources on a contested topic; a third student is assigned a third, neutral source. All three must produce a synthesis paragraph that accounts for all three sources' perspectives. The class reads the resulting paragraphs aloud and identifies which one most effectively integrates the tension.

assignments.name: Find the Gap
assignments.body: Students identify something none of their sources fully answers and write a synthesis that names the gap explicitly as part of the argument — what do these sources collectively still leave unresolved, and why does it matter?

mastery: Produces a governing claim that goes beyond any single source and can only be supported by drawing from multiple sources together.
Moves fluidly between sources within a single paragraph, using transitional framing that names the relationships between them (confirms, complicates, extends, contradicts).
Accounts for conflicting source material rather than suppressing it — names the tension and takes a position.
Maintains a distinct authorial voice that governs what the sources mean, rather than letting the sources speak for themselves in sequence.
Identifies gaps in the collective body of sources and acknowledges the limits of the synthesis.
notes: Vertical ramp: English I standard 11F establishes synthesis as a concept — students pull information from sources and combine it into a coherent product. English II expects students to manage the relationships between sources explicitly, including contradiction and tension. English III pushes further into argumentative synthesis where the student's position must be defended against competing evidence.

DOK 3 justification: synthesis at this level requires students to go beyond identifying what each source says (DOK 2) and produce novel understanding by recognizing relationships across sources. Extended reasoning is required; no single correct answer exists, which is why STAAR tests this through paired/tripled passages with integration questions.

STAAR analog: this TEK is heavily tested through multi-source reading sets — the "Sources" format on STAAR EOC — where students read 2–3 passages and answer questions requiring integrated inference. Question stems above mirror that format.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Examine Sources for Credibility and Bias
expectation: Examine sources for credibility and bias, including omission.
overview: This TEK is where research becomes genuinely critical. Students don't just locate sources — they interrogate them, looking not only at what a source claims but at who made it, what motives shape it, and what it conspicuously leaves out. Omission is the hardest piece: what a source doesn't say is often more telling than what it does.
studentFriendly: You can read any source — a news article, a website, a study — and figure out whether it's trustworthy, whose interests it serves, and what it's hiding by not saying.
questionStems: Which detail from the source MOST clearly indicates a potential bias in the author's perspective?
Based on the information in the article, the author MOST likely omits details about [topic] in order to —
A student researching [topic] would find Source 1 LEAST credible because —
Which sentence from the passage BEST supports the inference that the author has a particular point of view about [topic]?
The author's decision to include only [type of evidence] in paragraph X MOST likely —
Which statement BEST describes how the author's affiliation with [organization] affects the credibility of the source?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, what information does the author omit that would MOST likely change the reader's interpretation?
A reader evaluating the credibility of this source would MOST likely question —
Which revision to the source would MOST effectively address its bias?
The language in paragraph X suggests the author's purpose is MOST likely to —

misconceptions.title: Published Means Credible
misconceptions.body: Students treat publication — whether in print or online — as automatic proof of reliability. Teach them that publication is a filter, not a guarantee: self-publishing, sponsored content, and advocacy organizations all produce polished, widely circulated material with clear agendas. Watch for students citing ".org" sources as automatically neutral; some of the most strategic bias lives behind nonprofit branding.

misconceptions.title: Bias Is Always Obvious and Always Bad
misconceptions.body: Students expect biased sources to announce themselves with extreme language, and they assume any bias disqualifies a source entirely. Push them deeper: bias exists on a spectrum, and a source with a clear point of view can still contain accurate, useful information — the skill is accounting for the lens, not discarding the source. Teach them to name the slant and adjust, not to throw out everything that tilts.

misconceptions.title: Omission Is Accidental
misconceptions.body: Students rarely consider that what a source leaves out might be a deliberate rhetorical move. They read what's there and assume the picture is complete. Teach them to ask: What would a reader need to know to evaluate this claim fully? Is that information present? If not, whose interests does the gap serve? That's the omission question — and it's the hardest one to learn to ask.

misconceptions.title: Credibility Is Fixed — Either a Source Has It or It Doesn't
misconceptions.body: Students want a binary verdict: trustworthy or not, use it or don't. But credibility is contextual. A sports medicine journal is credible on injury data and irrelevant on climate policy. Watch for students applying one-time credibility assessments ("This site is reliable") across entirely different topics. The question is always: credible for THIS claim, on THIS subject, at THIS moment.

misconceptions.title: Checking the Author Is the Whole Job
misconceptions.body: Students often stop at "who wrote this?" and call the evaluation done. Teach them that authorship is one node in a larger web: who published it, who funded the research, what the publication's editorial stance is, when it was written, and whether the evidence cited is itself credible. A credentialed author working for a think tank with a documented funding agenda is not the same as a credentialed author publishing in a peer-reviewed journal with no financial interest in the outcome.

assignments.name: Side-by-Side Source Audit
assignments.body: Give students two sources covering the same event or topic — one with clear bias, one more neutral. Students produce a written comparison identifying credibility markers, bias indicators, and what each source omits. The goal is not to declare a winner but to map both sources' limitations.

assignments.name: The Hole in the Story
assignments.body: Students select a source on a contested topic and research what it leaves out. They write a one-page analysis naming the omission, explaining why it matters, and speculating — with textual evidence — about why the author may have excluded it.

assignments.name: Credibility Criteria Gallery Walk
assignments.body: Post six to eight sources around the room — varying in credibility, bias, and completeness. Students rotate with a rubric and score each source, then defend their ratings in a whole-class debrief. Disagreements between students generate the richest discussion.

assignments.name: Funding Follows the Argument
assignments.body: Students trace the funding behind a research source or advocacy document — who paid for it, what the funder's interests are, and whether the conclusions conveniently align with those interests. Brief written reflection (one page) on what they found and how it affects their use of the source.

mastery: Identifies specific features of a source — author credentials, publication venue, funding, date, language choices — and explains how each bears on credibility.
Distinguishes between bias that distorts and bias that merely reflects perspective, and adjusts interpretation accordingly rather than discarding the source wholesale.
Articulates what a source omits, explains why the omission matters to the argument, and identifies whose interests the gap may serve.
Applies credibility evaluation contextually — recognizing that a source's reliability depends on the specific claim being evaluated, not on a blanket judgment of the outlet.
Produces written source evaluations that are specific, evidence-based, and calibrated — neither naively trusting nor reflexively dismissive.
notes: Vertical ramp from English I: English I (11)(G)(i) introduces source credibility and bias as a concept. English II adds *omission* as an explicit lens — students are now expected to interrogate not just what a source says but what it strategically withholds.

Vertical ramp to English III: English III (11)(G)(i) extends this work into more complex multi-source synthesis, where omissions across sources compound and students must triangulate across conflicting accounts.

DOK 3 justification: evaluating credibility and identifying bias requires strategic thinking across multiple criteria; students must apply a framework of analysis, not simply recall or locate information.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is most directly tested through paired-passage or multi-source research simulation items, where students evaluate the appropriateness of a source for a given research task or identify how an author's affiliation or word choice signals bias. The stems above are modeled on that item type.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Display Academic Citations and Use Source Materials Ethically
expectation: Display academic citations, including for paraphrased and quoted text, and use source materials ethically to avoid plagiarism.
overview: This TEK is where research skills meet integrity — students must not only produce technically correct citations but understand why attribution is the baseline of academic honesty. The harder fight is paraphrase: students can dress plagiarism in their own sentence structure and not see it as theft.
studentFriendly: You can correctly cite any source — whether you're quoting it directly or putting it in your own words — because you understand that both moves require attribution and neither one is optional.
questionStems: Which citation BEST follows standard academic formatting for a direct quotation from a web source?
A student writes the following passage without attribution. Which statement MOST accurately describes the ethical problem with this approach?
Read the original source and the student's paraphrase in paragraph X. The student's version MOST likely —
Which sentence from the student's research paper requires a citation that is NOT currently provided?
A student quotes a source directly in paragraph X but does not include a page number. This omission MOST likely —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which source citation MOST clearly supports the student's central claim without misrepresenting the original author's meaning?
The student paraphrases the source in paragraph X. Compared to the original, the student's version MOST nearly —
Which revision of the in-text citation in paragraph X BEST corrects the formatting error?
A student uses the same source three times without re-citing it after the first reference. This practice MOST likely results in —
Based on the research paper as a whole, which source does the student MOST likely use to support the argument in paragraph X?

misconceptions.title: Paraphrasing Means Swapping Out Words
misconceptions.body: Students believe that if they replace enough vocabulary with synonyms, they've produced original work. Teach them that paraphrase is a restatement of an idea in a genuinely different structure — if the logic moves in the same direction, in the same order, with a synonym in every third slot, it's still plagiarism. Have them close the source before writing and reconstruct the idea from memory; if they can't, they didn't understand it.

misconceptions.title: You Only Cite Quotes
misconceptions.body: When students paraphrase — and especially when they lift a statistic or a specific claim — they assume no citation is needed because "it's in my own words." The rule is simple: if the idea didn't originate with you, you attribute it. Watch for research papers where only the blockquotes get footnotes and every paraphrased paragraph is floating free.

misconceptions.title: Citation Format Is Bureaucratic Busywork
misconceptions.body: Students treat formatting rules (hanging indents, italics, punctuation order) as arbitrary hazing. Reframe this: a properly formatted citation is a set of directions that lets the reader find the exact source. Every element is there because it helps the reader navigate. When a citation is wrong, the reader gets lost. That's not a style error — it's a failure of communication.

misconceptions.title: Summarizing a Source You Haven't Read Is Fine If You Cite It
misconceptions.body: Students will lift a summary of a source from a second article and then cite the original as if they read it — a ghost citation. They don't see this as dishonest because the citation is technically present. Teach them: citing a source you haven't read misrepresents your research process and risks misrepresenting the original argument. This is called secondary sourcing, and when it happens it should be labeled as such.

misconceptions.title: Once You Cite It Once, You're Covered for the Whole Paper
misconceptions.body: Some students believe that citing a source in their introduction or works-cited list exempts them from in-text attribution throughout. Watch for papers that introduce a source with a proper citation in paragraph two, then pull from it repeatedly with no further attribution for six more pages. Each use — each quote, each paraphrase, each borrowed statistic — needs its own acknowledgment.

assignments.name: Plagiarism Spectrum Gallery Walk
assignments.body: Present five student-written passages side by side with their source texts — ranging from verbatim copying to proper paraphrase. Students identify which constitute plagiarism, which are acceptable paraphrase, and which are direct quotes requiring quotation marks. Discuss where the line falls and why.

assignments.name: Cite It Three Ways
assignments.body: Give students a single source passage. They must produce three versions: (a) a proper direct quote with citation, (b) a proper paraphrase with citation, and (c) a summary with citation. Each version must be formatted in the course's designated citation style. Grade for both accuracy and formatting.

assignments.name: Citation Repair Shop
assignments.body: Provide a student research paper excerpt containing eight intentional citation errors — missing page numbers, improperly formatted titles, uncited paraphrases, incorrect author-date order. Students diagnose and correct each error in writing, identifying what rule was violated and why it matters.

assignments.name: Source Ethics Panel
assignments.body: Students bring in one example each of a published citation error or retraction (from academic retractions, journalism corrections, or plagiarism scandals). They present: what happened, what rule was broken, and what the professional consequence was. Anchors citation ethics in real stakes.

mastery: Produces correctly formatted in-text citations and works-cited entries for both quoted and paraphrased material without prompting.
Distinguishes between paraphrase that requires citation and original analysis that does not, and can explain the distinction aloud.
Identifies citation errors in sample texts — including missing attribution for paraphrased passages — and corrects them accurately.
Closes the source before paraphrasing and reconstructs the idea in genuinely restructured prose, not synonym substitution.
Applies the same citation standards to online sources, databases, and print materials without treating each as a separate system.
notes: Vertical ramp: English I Std 11(H) introduces citation and plagiarism avoidance at the level of basic formatting and acknowledgment. English II holds the same expectation but sits inside increasingly complex research tasks — students are pulling from more sources and producing longer papers, so the citation burden grows proportionally. English III adds synthesis and evaluation of source reliability, making English II's citation accuracy the assumed floor.

DOK 2 justification: the core skill is applying citation conventions correctly — a procedural application task. The ethical reasoning layer (what counts as plagiarism, why attribution matters) touches DOK 3, but the testable behavior is DOK 2.

STAAR testing note: this TEK is not directly tested as a discrete citation-formatting item on STAAR; instead it surfaces in revision/editing passages where students identify uncited material or improperly attributed quotes. Stems are written to mirror that context. Note this in instruction: the skill is assessed through editing judgment, not citation-format recall.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Critique a speaker's clarity, diction, and syntax
expectation: Engage in meaningful and respectful discourse when evaluating the clarity and coherence of a speaker's message and critiquing the impact of a speaker's use of diction and syntax.
overview: By English III, listening is no longer enough — students are now critics of oral argument. They evaluate whether a speaker is actually clear, whether the sentences are doing real work, and whether word choice is shaping the listener's thinking. The job is to listen analytically without being a jerk about it.
studentFriendly: You can listen to someone speak — a politician, a podcaster, a classmate giving a presentation — and tell whether their message actually holds together, whether their word choice is doing what they think it's doing, and whether their sentences are helping or hurting their point.
questionStems: The speaker's use of [word/phrase] in paragraph X MOST likely emphasizes —
Which sentence from the speech BEST supports the speaker's main argument?
The speaker's word choice in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
How does the syntax in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the clarity of the speaker's message?
Which line from the speech BEST illustrates a shift in the speaker's diction?
The speaker MOST likely uses the phrase [phrase] in paragraph X to —
How does the speaker's repetition of [word/phrase] affect the listener?
Which sentence BEST captures the speaker's tone in paragraph X?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, what is the MOST likely effect of the speaker's syntax on the audience?
The speaker's diction in paragraph X suggests that —

misconceptions.title: "Critiquing" means "poking holes"
misconceptions.body: Students collapse critique into attack. They listen for flaws and ignore strengths, or they ignore flaws to be polite. Real critique names what worked AND what didn't, with equal precision. Teach them: if you can't articulate what the speaker did well, you haven't actually been listening — you've been waiting for your turn.

misconceptions.title: Clarity equals simplicity
misconceptions.body: Students assume the clearest message is the simplest one. But a complex idea stated too simply becomes wrong. Show them speakers who are clear AND nuanced — the clarity is in the structure of the argument, not the size of the words. Watch for students who downgrade every speech to "they should've said it more simply" without asking whether simplicity would have lost the point.

misconceptions.title: Diction analysis = looking for fancy words
misconceptions.body: Students think analyzing diction means finding SAT vocabulary. The richer move is noticing the everyday words that carry hidden weight — "natural," "common-sense," "obviously," "real Americans." Teach them that the most loaded diction is often the most invisible.

misconceptions.title: Syntax is just sentence length
misconceptions.body: Students reduce syntactic critique to "their sentences are too long" or "too short." Push them deeper: where does the subject sit? Are passive constructions hiding agency? Are parallel structures creating false equivalence? Syntax is architecture; teach them to read the building, not measure the rooms.

misconceptions.title: You can't critique someone you agree with
misconceptions.body: When students share the speaker's politics or position, they go soft. They'll praise weak arguments because the conclusion lines up. The harder skill is critiquing your own side — finding the moments your team's speaker overstated, hand-waved, or dodged. That's where the real listening lives.

assignments.name: Speech Autopsy
assignments.body: Students pick a 5–10 minute speech (TED talk, political address, commencement). They produce a written critique with one paragraph each on clarity, diction, syntax, and one paragraph naming what the speaker DID well. Length: 2 pages, evidence-based.

assignments.name: Same Speech, Two Critics
assignments.body: Two students critique the same speech independently, then compare. Where did they agree? Where did they diverge — and was the divergence about the speech, or about themselves?

assignments.name: Diction Heat Map
assignments.body: Project the transcript of a speech. Students mark every word doing rhetorical work in one color and every neutral word in another. Discuss the pattern.

assignments.name: Critique Without Dismissing
assignments.body: Students critique a speech they personally agree with. They must find at least three specific weaknesses without softening their tone. Grade for honesty as much as accuracy.

assignments.name: Live Listening Lab
assignments.body: A guest speaker (teacher, admin, alum) gives a short talk. Students critique in real time on note cards. After the talk, the speaker reads the critiques aloud and responds to a few. Students see what real critique-reception looks like.

mastery: Distinguishes between agreeing/disagreeing with a speaker's position and evaluating the quality of their argument.
Identifies specific syntactic and lexical choices and explains their rhetorical impact, not just their presence.
Holds critique and respect simultaneously — names weaknesses without becoming dismissive, names strengths without becoming flattering.
Notices loaded diction even when it aligns with their own position.
Produces written critique with structure: clarity, evidence, fairness, specificity.
notes: Vertical jump from English II: Std 1A in English II is "engage in meaningful and respectful discourse when evaluating speakers' messages." English III adds *diction and syntax* as targets of critique — the skill ramps from "did the message land?" to "WHY did the message land or fail?"
DOK 4 justification: evaluation against multiple criteria with extended reasoning required to defend judgments.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Clarify meaning through pertinent questioning
expectation: Follow and give complex instructions, clarify meaning by asking pertinent questions, and respond appropriately.
overview: The English III upgrade to procedural listening: it's not just whether you followed the directions, it's whether you knew when to interrupt and what to ask. A pertinent question is the one that unlocks the whole task — and asking it well is a craft most adults still haven't mastered.
studentFriendly: You can follow complex directions, but you also know when something doesn't add up — and you can ask the kind of question that gets you the missing piece without making the speaker start over.
questionStems: Which question would BEST clarify the meaning of paragraph X?
To better understand [topic], a listener should MOST likely ask —
Which question MOST clearly seeks to clarify the speaker's instructions?
After hearing paragraph X, which question would BEST help a listener understand [topic]?
Which question is MOST relevant to clarifying the speaker's claim in paragraph X?
To clarify the speaker's main point, a listener should ask —
Which question would BEST help the listener follow the speaker's directions?
Which question would MOST likely lead to a deeper understanding of [topic]?
A listener who is unsure about [step/instruction] should MOST likely ask —
Which clarifying question would BEST help the listener evaluate the speaker's argument?

misconceptions.title: Asking "what?" counts as a clarifying question
misconceptions.body: Students default to "what?" or "huh?" when they're lost — but that just makes the speaker repeat the whole thing. A pertinent question names the specific gap: "you said put it in the folder — which folder?" Teach them: if your question makes the speaker start over, it wasn't a question, it was a confession.

misconceptions.title: Asking questions is a sign you weren't paying attention
misconceptions.body: Many students stay silent because asking feels like exposure. Reframe it explicitly: the listener who asks one sharp question is doing more work than the one who nods through and gets it wrong. Model your own clarifying questions in front of them, especially in subjects where you're less expert — they need to see adults ask things.

misconceptions.title: Following instructions = doing exactly what was said
misconceptions.body: Students execute literally and miss intent. If you say "put your work in the folder" and you meant "the green folder," a student will put it in any folder and feel they followed directions. Teach them that following complex instructions includes detecting where the speaker assumed shared knowledge — and asking before guessing.

misconceptions.title: A "pertinent" question is just any on-topic question
misconceptions.body: Students hear "pertinent" and aim for "related." But pertinent means it actually moves the work forward. Push them to rank questions: which one, asked first, would unlock the most other questions? That's the pertinent one. The others can wait or resolve themselves.

misconceptions.title: "Respond appropriately" means agree politely
misconceptions.body: In group instruction settings, students confuse appropriate response with compliance. But responding appropriately to a flawed direction might mean pushing back — "before I start, can I check whether step three should come before step two?" Teach them that appropriate response is calibrated to the situation, not always submissive.

assignments.name: The One-Question Game
assignments.body: Teacher gives a complex multi-step direction with one ambiguity baked in. Students get ONE question. Whoever's question best unlocks the task wins. Discuss why their question worked.

assignments.name: Question Triage
assignments.body: Hand out a procedure with three deliberate ambiguities. Students must identify all three AND rank them: which question, asked first, would resolve the most downstream confusion? Defend the ranking.

assignments.name: Boss / Worker Roleplay
assignments.body: Pairs simulate workplace scenarios — a manager giving instructions to a new hire. The "worker" must ask pertinent questions calibrated to power dynamics. Coach on tone and timing.

assignments.name: Bad Direction Diagnosis
assignments.body: Watch a recorded person giving instructions badly. Students write three pertinent questions they'd ask AND identify which sentence in the original triggered each question. Builds the habit of locating ambiguity precisely.

assignments.name: Translate Without Asking
assignments.body: Pairs receive a complex direction; one partner restates it for someone who wasn't there, WITHOUT asking the original speaker for clarification. Compare the result to a version where they could ask. What broke?

mastery: Asks targeted clarifying questions that name specific gaps rather than requesting wholesale repetition.
Distinguishes between misheard, misunderstood, and missing information when seeking clarification.
Calibrates question phrasing to the speaker (peer, teacher, employer) without losing the substantive ask.
Anticipates ambiguity in their OWN instructions and structures speech to preempt it.
Responds to instructions appropriately — including pushing back when an instruction is internally inconsistent — without defaulting to compliance or refusal.
notes: Vertical jump from English I: English I 1B = "follow and give complex oral instructions to perform specific tasks." English III 1B explicitly adds *clarify meaning by asking pertinent questions* — the questioning skill becomes named, not just implied. Misconception 1 here ("what?" doesn't count) is engineered to catch the gap between English I procedural listening and English III analytical listening.
DOK 3 justification: strategic thinking required to formulate effective clarifying questions in real time.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Deliver a formal presentation with rhetorical control
expectation: Give a formal presentation that exhibits a logical structure, smooth transitions, accurate evidence, well-chosen details, and rhetorical devices and that employs eye contact, speaking rate such as pauses for effect, volume, enunciation, purposeful gestures, and conventions of language to communicate ideas effectively.
overview: This is the TEK that turns "speaking" into "presenting." Structure, evidence, rhetorical devices, AND physical delivery — all at once. By English III, students should be making rhetorical choices on purpose, not by accident, and they should know which choice they made and why.
studentFriendly: You can plan and deliver a formal presentation that has a clear structure, real evidence, intentional rhetorical moves, and physical delivery — eye contact, pacing, gestures — that you actually controlled instead of letting it happen to you.
questionStems: Which strategy would BEST help the speaker convey [message] to the audience?
The speaker's MOST effective rhetorical move in paragraph X is —
Which sentence BEST demonstrates the speaker's control of formal register?
How does the organization of paragraphs X–Y support the speaker's argument?
The speaker's use of [device] in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Which transition would BEST connect paragraphs X and Y?
Which evidence from the presentation BEST supports the speaker's claim?
The speaker's tone in paragraph X is BEST described as —
Which sentence would BEST conclude the speaker's argument?
How does the speaker's use of [rhetorical device] in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the formality of the presentation?

misconceptions.title: Reading from notes counts as presenting
misconceptions.body: Students conflate reading aloud with presenting. They write a paper, print it out, and read it. The audience hears a paragraph, not a person. Teach the move: notes are landmarks, not lines. They should be able to lose their place and recover by talking, not by finding their spot.

misconceptions.title: Smooth transitions are about transition phrases
misconceptions.body: Students drop in "next, I'll talk about..." and call it a transition. Real transition is logical — the previous point creates a need for the next one. Teach them: if I removed your transition phrase entirely, would the listener still feel the move from point A to point B? If no, the architecture is the problem, not the wording.

misconceptions.title: Rhetorical devices are decorations
misconceptions.body: Students sprinkle in alliteration or a metaphor and feel they've "used rhetoric." Push them: every rhetorical device should be earning its place. Why anaphora here and not there? Why a rhetorical question on this point and not the next? Devices without intent are just noise.

misconceptions.title: Confidence is fake-it-til-you-make-it
misconceptions.body: Students think nervous = bad and they should pretend not to be. The result is a stiff, performative confidence the audience can smell. Real presentation skill is making the nervousness work: slowing down on purpose, owning the pause, letting your hands rest. Teach them to use the nerves, not deny them.

misconceptions.title: Eye contact = staring at one person
misconceptions.body: Students take "make eye contact" too literally and lock onto one terrified peer for ninety seconds. Teach the move: scan in groups, hold contact for one full thought, then move. If a section of the room hasn't been looked at in a minute, that section is checking out — eye contact is how you keep the room awake.

assignments.name: The One-Sentence Test
assignments.body: Before drafting, students must write the single sentence they want their audience to remember. The full presentation is built backward from that sentence. Defend it on the way out.

assignments.name: Same Talk, Three Audiences
assignments.body: Students write a 5-minute presentation and deliver three opens — one for peers, one for parents, one for industry pros. Reflect on what changed and what didn't.

assignments.name: Silent Tape
assignments.body: Record presentations, then watch the playback with the audio off. Students analyze their physical delivery — eye contact, gestures, posture — separated from what they said. Then watch with audio only. The two channels usually don't match.

assignments.name: Device Justification
assignments.body: Students annotate their final script: every rhetorical device gets a marginal note explaining why it's there. Devices without justification get cut.

assignments.name: Surprise Cut
assignments.body: Twenty-four hours before final delivery, the teacher tells students they have 60 fewer seconds. They must cut, not rush. What goes? What gets sharper as a result?

mastery: Designs presentations from a single anchor claim outward, with structure that serves the claim rather than decorating it.
Uses rhetorical devices intentionally — can articulate why each one is where it is.
Controls pace, pauses, and volume as deliberate tools, not symptoms of nerves or rote delivery.
Adjusts delivery in real time based on audience signals (engagement, confusion, attention drift).
Recovers from mistakes without breaking the frame — handles a forgotten line, a tech failure, or a hard question without losing presence.
notes: Vertical alignment: English I 1C and English III 1C share nearly identical TEA language — the rigor jump is implicit in audience expectations and content complexity, not in the verb. Distinguish the two by what students PRESENT ON, not how they're scored.
DOK 4 justification: extended planning, integration of multiple subskills, original synthesis under live conditions.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Move teams forward through purposeful collaboration
expectation: Participate collaboratively, offering ideas or judgments that are purposeful in moving the team toward goals, asking relevant and insightful questions, tolerating a range of positions and ambiguity in decision making, and evaluating the work of the group based on agreed-upon criteria.
overview: English III collaboration is a step up from "play nice in groups." It's about offering ideas that actually move the work, sitting with disagreement without flinching, and evaluating the group's output against criteria you agreed on — not against vibes. This is the TEK that prepares students for actual professional team dynamics.
studentFriendly: You can be the person in the group who actually pushes the work forward — contributing ideas that matter, asking the question nobody else thought to ask, sitting with disagreement instead of running from it, and judging the group's work honestly against the criteria you set.
questionStems: Which response from a group member would BEST move the discussion forward?
Which question from a group member would MOST likely surface a hidden assumption?
The speaker's contribution in paragraph X MOST likely supports the group's goal of —
Which statement BEST evaluates the group's work against the agreed-upon criteria?
Which response would BEST address the disagreement in paragraphs X–Y?
The group member's question in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Which contribution from the discussion BEST demonstrates tolerance for ambiguity?
To advance the group's project, the team member should MOST likely —
Which statement BEST captures the group's collective decision in paragraph X?
How does the group member's response in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the team's progress?

misconceptions.title: Collaboration means consensus
misconceptions.body: Students think a successful group meeting ends with everyone agreeing. But forced consensus often means the loudest voice won and the quiet ones folded. Teach the move: a healthy disagreement that gets named is more productive than a unanimous vote that hides three holdouts. Watch for groups that look "happy" but aren't actually deciding.

misconceptions.title: Tolerating ambiguity = being indecisive
misconceptions.body: Students confuse sitting with uncertainty for failing to commit. The skill is the opposite — you can hold two unresolved possibilities in your head AND keep working. Show them what this looks like: "I don't know yet which option is right, so let's draft toward both and decide on Friday." That's ambiguity tolerance, not indecision.

misconceptions.title: A group leader is the person who talks most
misconceptions.body: Students associate leadership with airtime. But the real moves are often quieter — the person who asks the question that reframes the meeting, the one who notices a quiet member is being overlooked, the one who paraphrases two opposing positions accurately. Teach them: leadership is what the group needs, not what looks like leadership.

misconceptions.title: Evaluating the group means rating people
misconceptions.body: When students "evaluate group work," they default to ranking teammates ("Sarah was great, Marcus didn't do anything"). The TEK calls for evaluating the work against criteria — process, output, decisions made. Push past blame: "what did our process produce, and where did the criteria we set actually hold us?"

misconceptions.title: Insightful questions are smart-sounding questions
misconceptions.body: Students will ask "what's the deeper meaning of...?" and feel insightful. The more useful question is often the simpler one nobody asked: "wait, who exactly is this for?" Teach them that insightful = surfaces a hidden assumption, not = uses big vocabulary.

assignments.name: Criteria Before Content
assignments.body: Before any group project starts, the group must spend a full class period writing the criteria they'll be evaluated against. Submit those criteria for approval. Project grade is partly based on how well the final work hits the criteria they themselves wrote.

assignments.name: The Devil's Advocate Rotation
assignments.body: Each group meeting, one student is assigned to take the opposite position from the emerging consensus — regardless of personal opinion. Reflect afterward on what got surfaced.

assignments.name: Silent Disagreement
assignments.body: Group members write their position on a contested decision on paper before discussing. Compare written-vs-spoken positions after. Where did people fold? Where did they hold?

assignments.name: Self-Evaluation, Group-Witnessed
assignments.body: Each student writes their own contribution evaluation — what they did well, where they fell short. Read aloud to the group. Group members can correct, not vote.

assignments.name: The Question Log
assignments.body: During a group project, every member keeps a running list of every question they asked the group. At the end, sort by which questions actually moved the work and which were noise. Discuss patterns.

mastery: Contributes ideas calibrated to the group's current need (more synthesis vs. more dissent vs. more direction).
Holds disagreement without dissolving it — names tensions, lets them sit, returns to them.
Asks questions that surface hidden assumptions rather than display intellectual range.
Evaluates group output against agreed criteria, not against personal preferences or interpersonal dynamics.
Functions effectively in groups where the leader role rotates, is unclear, or is held by a peer.
notes: Vertical jump: English I 1D establishes collaborative discussion mechanics (agendas, note-taking, voting). English III 1D moves into adult-team dynamics — purposeful contribution, ambiguity tolerance, criteria-based evaluation. Same letter, very different ceiling.
Strongest cross-strand connection is to Inquiry & Research (Std 11) — the collaboration TEK is implicitly the substrate for any group research project.
DOK 4 justification: extended evaluation against multiple criteria, requires sustained metacognition about own contributions and group dynamics.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Validate multiple meanings of advanced vocabulary
expectation: Use print or digital resources to clarify and validate understanding of multiple meanings of advanced vocabulary.
overview: Looking up a word is the easy part. The English III move is recognizing that the dictionary entry has *four* numbered meanings and figuring out which one this writer meant — then validating that guess against the sentence around it. Half the misreads in junior-year reading come from students locking onto the first definition they see.
studentFriendly: When a word has more than one meaning, you can use a dictionary or other reference to figure out which meaning the writer actually intended — and check it against the sentence to make sure you got it right.
questionStems: Which definition BEST matches the use of the word [word] in paragraph X?
What does the word [word] MOST nearly mean as it is used in paragraph X?
In paragraph X, the word [word] means —
Which dictionary definition BEST fits the use of [word] in paragraph X?
Read this dictionary entry for [word]. Which numbered definition BEST matches the word's use in paragraph X?
The word [word] in paragraph X MOST likely means —
Which meaning of [word] is MOST appropriate for the way the word is used in the selection?
As used in paragraph X, [word] means —
Which definition of [word] BEST helps the reader understand its meaning in paragraph X?
Read this entry from a dictionary. Which numbered definition BEST matches the use of [word] in the selection?

misconceptions.title: The first definition is the right definition
misconceptions.body: Students treat dictionary entries like a single answer. They grab definition 1 and move on, even when 1 is the most common usage rather than the contextually correct one. Teach them to read ALL the numbered senses before choosing — and to plug each candidate back into the sentence as a sanity check.

misconceptions.title: Looking it up is the whole job
misconceptions.body: Students think the move ends at "I looked it up." But validation is the second half: does the meaning you picked actually make the sentence work? Build the habit of re-reading the original sentence with the chosen meaning explicit. If the sentence is still murky, you picked wrong or there's something else going on.

misconceptions.title: Digital reference equals reliable reference
misconceptions.body: Students grab the first hit from Google or a phone autocomplete and call it done. Teach them which references actually do the job — Merriam-Webster, OED, dictionary.com vs. random "what does X mean" sites. The difference shows up most when the word has technical, archaic, or discipline-specific senses.

misconceptions.title: "Advanced" means "long"
misconceptions.body: Students assume advanced vocabulary means SAT-style obscure words. But advanced often means familiar words doing unfamiliar work — "subject" in a philosophy text, "credit" in an economics text, "argument" in a math text. The lookup skill applies to those too, and they're the ones students skip because they think they already know them.

misconceptions.title: One lookup, one meaning, forever
misconceptions.body: Students look up a word once and assume the meaning sticks across every text they read it in. Teach them that the SAME word in a different writer's hands carries a different meaning — and "validating" means doing the work each time, not banking the meaning from last week's reading.

assignments.name: The Wrong Definition
assignments.body: Hand students a passage with five flagged words. For each, list THREE candidate dictionary meanings. Students pick one and defend it from the surrounding text — and explain why the other two fail.

assignments.name: Same Word, Different Text
assignments.body: Pick a word that appears in three texts students have read — same word, different senses. Students annotate each occurrence with the dictionary sense that fits, and write one paragraph on what they had to know to tell them apart.

assignments.name: Reference Smackdown
assignments.body: Same word, three sources: Merriam-Webster, an online dictionary aggregator, and a discipline-specific glossary. Compare entries. Which one would they trust for this passage and why?

assignments.name: Validate or Bust
assignments.body: Students submit five vocabulary lookups from independent reading with: word, sentence in context, chosen dictionary sense, and ONE-sentence justification using the surrounding text. Grade for the justification, not the lookup.

assignments.name: Connotation Hunt
assignments.body: Students find a word where the dictionary definition is technically right but misses what the writer is doing. Write a paragraph explaining what the dictionary missed and how they figured it out.

mastery: Recognizes when a word has multiple senses BEFORE assuming the obvious one applies.
Uses surrounding text to choose among dictionary senses rather than defaulting to the first listed.
Re-reads the sentence with the chosen meaning explicit as a validation step, not as a teacher-pleasing ritual.
Distinguishes between reliable and unreliable reference sources and chooses based on the text type.
Catches familiar words doing unfamiliar work — the "advanced" use of common vocabulary — and looks them up anyway.
notes: Vertical jump from English II: English II 2A focuses on technical/discipline-specific vocabulary; English III shifts to *multiple meanings of advanced vocabulary* — the polysemy skill is now the named target. Students need to be reading texts where the same word does different work in different sentences.
DOK 2 justification: skill-based application with multiple steps — locate, choose, validate. Pushes into DOK 3 when students must defend the choice against alternative senses.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Draw conclusions about nuanced meanings such as imagery
expectation: Analyze context to draw conclusions about nuanced meanings such as in imagery.
overview: This is where vocabulary stops being lookup and becomes interpretation. Students stop asking "what does this word mean?" and start asking "what is this word DOING here?" Imagery is the test case — the word's literal meaning is rarely the point. The job is reading the sensory, emotional, and associative cargo a word is carrying.
studentFriendly: Words don't just mean things — they make you see things, feel things, and react in particular ways. You can use the surrounding text to figure out what a word is making you experience and why the writer chose THAT word instead of one with the same dictionary meaning.
questionStems: Which word from paragraph X BEST supports the meaning of [word/image]?
The author MOST likely uses the word [word] in paragraph X to —
What is the effect of the imagery in paragraphs X–Y?
Which sentence from the selection contains an example of imagery that emphasizes [emotion/idea]?
The image of [image] in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Which word from the selection BEST creates a feeling of [mood]?
The author's use of sensory details in paragraphs X–Y MOST likely contributes to —
Which line from the poem BEST conveys [mood]?
What does the imagery in paragraph X suggest about [character/situation]?
How does the author's word choice in paragraph X contribute to the tone of the selection?

misconceptions.title: Imagery means "language about pictures"
misconceptions.body: Students restrict imagery to visual description and miss everything else. But imagery is sensory — auditory, tactile, olfactory, kinesthetic. The line "the silence was wet" isn't visual at all. Teach them to track ALL five senses when reading for imagery, and to flag synesthesia (mixing senses) as a high-craft move.

misconceptions.title: If I felt something, I read it right
misconceptions.body: Students conflate emotional reaction with analysis. "It made me feel sad" is a starting point, not a finish. Push them to identify WHICH word, image, or pattern produced the feeling — and to consider whether a different reader would feel the same thing. The work is in tracing the cause, not just naming the effect.

misconceptions.title: Connotation is just "the feeling a word has"
misconceptions.body: Students treat connotation as vibes. But connotation is concrete: it's the cluster of associations a word drags in from its history of use. "Childlike" and "childish" have the same dictionary core; their connotations are opposite. Make them list the associations explicitly — what does this word remind you of, where do you usually hear it, who uses it about whom?

misconceptions.title: Nuanced reading means finding hidden meanings
misconceptions.body: Students think nuance is decoding — finding the secret message. But nuanced meaning is on the surface; it's just not the literal meaning. The writer isn't hiding it, the reader is just refusing to read it. Reframe: nuance is the meaning that's right there in front of you that you're trained to ignore.

misconceptions.title: Every word is doing rhetorical work
misconceptions.body: The opposite trap: students start over-reading and find symbolism in every comma. Teach them to distinguish working language from connective tissue. The writer's verbs, key adjectives, and metaphors carry weight; "the" and "and" don't. If everything matters, nothing does.

assignments.name: Synonym Swap
assignments.body: Give students a passage and replace 5–8 carefully chosen words with their dictionary-equivalent synonyms. Students compare the two versions and write what was lost. Forces them to articulate what the original word was carrying.

assignments.name: Imagery Inventory
assignments.body: Students log every sensory image in a chapter by sense (sight/sound/smell/taste/touch) and pattern. After logging, they write one paragraph on what the patterns reveal — repeated senses, missing senses, dominant emotional register.

assignments.name: Connotation Web
assignments.body: Pick five loaded words from a text (e.g., "freedom," "natural," "primitive," "civilized," "real"). For each, students list 5–10 connotations and the contexts where they pick them up. Discuss which connotations the writer is invoking and which they're trying to dodge.

assignments.name: Sentence Surgery
assignments.body: Students rewrite a vivid passage in flat, denotative prose. Then they reverse-engineer back to the original — guessing where the writer added charge and how. Compare guesses to the actual original.

assignments.name: Image Tracker
assignments.body: Across a full text (novel, play, long essay), students track ONE recurring image — water, light, hands, etc. They produce a timeline showing every appearance and write a closing paragraph on how the image evolved and what it tells them about the work.

mastery: Reads images as carriers of meaning, not just decorative description.
Names connotations specifically — what associations the word brings, from where, used by whom.
Distinguishes language doing rhetorical work from language doing connective work, and focuses analysis on the former.
Tracks sensory imagery across all five senses, not just the visual.
Articulates WHY a writer chose this word over a near-synonym, with specific reference to surrounding text.
notes: This is a bridge TEK — it lives in Vocabulary but does most of its work in literary analysis (Std 6) and craft (Std 8). When teaching, build it alongside imagery work in fiction and poetry rather than as standalone vocab.

Vertical jump from English II: English II 2B asks students to distinguish denotation and connotation; English III pushes into *imagery and nuance*, which is connotation-in-action across whole passages.
DOK 3 justification: strategic thinking required to read context for cumulative effect rather than single-word lookup.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Determine meanings of foreign words used in English
expectation: Determine the meaning of foreign words or phrases used frequently in English such as ad hoc, faux pas, non sequitur, and modus operandi.
overview: These phrases show up in journalism, legal writing, opinion pieces, and academic argument — and they're not optional vocabulary. A student who skips "ad hoc" loses the sentence; a student who reads "non sequitur" as a vibe rather than a claim misses the writer's actual move. The English III bar is recognizing them, knowing what they mean, and reading them as the precision tools they are.
studentFriendly: Educated writing in English borrows phrases from other languages — Latin, French, Italian — and uses them as shortcuts for ideas English doesn't have a clean word for. You can recognize these phrases, know what they mean, and read writers who use them without getting tripped up.
questionStems: What does the phrase [foreign phrase] MOST nearly mean as it is used in paragraph X?
The phrase [foreign phrase] in paragraph X MOST likely means —
As used in paragraph X, [phrase] means —
Which definition BEST matches the use of [foreign phrase] in paragraph X?
The author uses the phrase [phrase] in paragraph X to mean —
Which word or phrase from paragraph X helps the reader understand the meaning of [foreign phrase]?
In paragraph X, [foreign phrase] is MOST likely used to describe —
Read this dictionary entry. Which definition BEST matches the use of [phrase] in paragraph X?
The phrase [foreign phrase] in paragraph X suggests that —
What is the MOST likely meaning of [foreign phrase] as used in the selection?

misconceptions.title: Foreign phrases are decoration
misconceptions.body: Students treat them like a writer's Latin showing off. But the phrases survive in English because they pack precision: "ad hoc" doesn't mean "informal" — it means "for this specific purpose, not part of a system." Teach the actual semantic distinction, not the vibe. If a synonym would work, the writer wouldn't have reached for the borrowed phrase.

misconceptions.title: "I get the feeling" is enough
misconceptions.body: Students approximate meaning from context and call it done. But these phrases are technical — used wrong, they mark a writer as careless. "Begs the question" misused, "literally" misused, "non sequitur" misused. Hold students to the actual definition, not the contextual guess.

misconceptions.title: They're all Latin
misconceptions.body: Students lump every foreign-looking phrase under "Latin." But English borrows from French ("faux pas," "raison d'être"), Italian ("a cappella"), German ("schadenfreude"), Japanese ("zeitgeist" — wait, that's German; "tsunami" is Japanese). Pronunciation cues, register, and connotation differ by source language. Teach the families, not just the phrases.

misconceptions.title: Using them makes your writing sound smart
misconceptions.body: Students hear teachers value these phrases and stuff them into essays. The result reads like a thesaurus accident. Coach: use the phrase only when its precision is needed. Otherwise, it's pretension. The mark of a strong writer is knowing which phrase to keep and which to translate to plain English.

misconceptions.title: Pronunciation doesn't matter for reading
misconceptions.body: Students skip pronunciation because they're reading silently — but if they ever say it aloud, it shows. "Tête-à-tête" pronounced as "tet-a-tet" or "Bona fide" pronounced as "bona fyde" — these are mistakes that mark a reader as having only seen the phrase, never heard it. Pair the phrase with the pronunciation when you teach it.

assignments.name: The Phrase Hunt
assignments.body: Students find one foreign-borrowed phrase in each of three different text types — a news article, an opinion column, a literary passage — and explain how the phrase is functioning in each. Same phrase, different contexts? Different phrases entirely? Discuss the patterns.

assignments.name: Translation Test
assignments.body: Pick ten phrases (ad hoc, bona fide, carte blanche, faux pas, non sequitur, modus operandi, quid pro quo, prima facie, status quo, ipso facto). Students translate each into clear English AND write a sentence using each correctly. Grade for precision, not just translation.

assignments.name: Misuse Detective
assignments.body: Compile real examples of misused borrowed phrases from print and online writing (often "begs the question," "literally," "ironic"). Students diagnose the misuse and rewrite the sentence correctly.

assignments.name: Register Sort
assignments.body: Give students a mixed list of borrowed phrases and three contexts (legal brief, casual blog post, academic paper). Sort each phrase into the contexts where it would and wouldn't fit. Defend the sorting.

assignments.name: Rewrite Without It
assignments.body: Students take an article that uses three or more borrowed phrases and rewrite it in plain English. They submit both versions plus a paragraph on what was lost or gained. Sometimes the rewrite is better. Discuss when.

mastery: Recognizes common borrowed phrases on sight in journalism, opinion writing, and academic prose.
Knows the precise semantic value of each phrase, not just an approximate vibe.
Reads writers who use borrowed phrases without skipping over the phrase or losing the sentence.
Distinguishes phrases that have become part of working English (déjà vu, ad hoc, status quo) from phrases that are still register-marked (raison d'être, tête-à-tête, ipso facto).
Uses borrowed phrases sparingly in their own writing — when precision requires it, not when impressiveness is the goal.
notes: The TEK lists *ad hoc, faux pas, non sequitur, and modus operandi* as examples — these aren't the whole list. Build your own classroom corpus from the texts students are actually reading. Different readings yield different phrases.

Vertical jump from English II: same skill, harder corpus. English II covered phrases like "bona fide" and "carte blanche"; English III pushes into legal/academic territory ("ad hoc," "modus operandi," "non sequitur") where the precision matters more.
DOK 2 justification: identify and apply meanings in context. Pushes to DOK 3 when students evaluate whether a writer used a phrase correctly.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: 110.38(3) — Self-Select Text and Read Independently
expectation: Self-select text and read independently for a sustained period of time.
overview: This standard is about building the habit that makes everything else in ELA work — reading a lot, by choice, over time. The teacher's job is to protect the time, stock the shelves, and get out of the way.
studentFriendly: You choose what you want to read and actually read it — for long enough that it becomes something you do, not something you survive.
questionStems: Based on paragraphs X–Y, the narrator's MOST likely reason for continuing to read [type of text] is —
Which detail from the passage BEST supports the inference that the reader finds the subject matter personally meaningful?
The author's use of [genre feature] in paragraph X would MOST likely appeal to a reader who prefers —
A student selects a novel after reading the back cover. Which detail from the back cover MOST likely influenced the student's choice?
Based on the passage, a reader interested in [topic] would MOST likely read this text for —
Which sentence from the passage BEST explains why a reader might abandon a text before finishing it?
The structure of paragraphs X–Y contributes MOST to a reader's ability to —
A student reading independently for thirty minutes encounters an unfamiliar word in paragraph X. Which strategy would MOST likely help the student maintain reading momentum?
Based on the passage, the MOST likely effect of reading widely across genres is —
Which statement BEST describes how a reader's self-selected text differs in purpose from a teacher-assigned text?

misconceptions.title: "Free Reading" Means No Accountability
misconceptions.body: Teachers sometimes treat independent reading as a break rather than a standard — something students do when the real work is done. Reframe it: this is the practice reps. A musician who only performs in class lessons but never plays at home does not improve at the rate of one who does both. The standard exists because reading volume predicts reading ability, and class time alone cannot produce that volume.

misconceptions.title: Any Book in Their Hands Is Success
misconceptions.body: When students select texts dramatically below their reading level for every session, every day, the selection habit is not transferring to growth. Watch for students who default to the same comfortable book or series for an entire semester without stretching. Self-selection is about ownership, not avoidance — teach them to name what they want to get better at, then help them find a text that requires something from them.

misconceptions.title: Sustained Means Silent and Still
misconceptions.body: Students conflate sustained independent reading with a specific posture rather than a specific cognitive state. The standard requires sustained engagement with a self-selected text — not a particular seating arrangement. Watch for the student who looks compliant (head down, book open) but is not reading, and for the student who seems restless but is deeply hooked on every page. Measure actual reading behavior, not performance of reading behavior.

misconceptions.title: Self-Selection Only Works for Students Who Already Like Reading
misconceptions.body: Teachers sometimes assign independent reading choice only to strong readers and quietly direct reluctant readers toward specific "approved" books. This inverts the purpose. Reluctant readers are exactly the students who need to discover that something in the world of text is for them. Teach students who claim to hate reading to name what they do like — cars, crime, a specific athlete, horror — and put that thing in their hands in text form.

misconceptions.title: Finishing the Book Is the Goal
misconceptions.body: When students see completion as the measure of success, they stop selecting texts honestly — they pick short books to rack up finishes or abandon hard books to avoid the appearance of failure. Push them deeper: the goal is sustained engagement over time, not a count of back covers reached. Teach them to track minutes read and honest engagement, not page counts.

assignments.name: Reading Identity Statement
assignments.body: At the start of each semester, students write a one-paragraph reading identity statement: what genres they gravitate toward, what they've abandoned and why, what they want to read next and why. Revisit at the end of the semester — what changed?

assignments.name: Choice Shelf Audit
assignments.body: Students spend ten minutes browsing the classroom library or a curated digital list, choose three texts they might want to read, and write one sentence per text explaining what drew them to it. The teacher conferences briefly with students whose choices reveal a pattern worth redirecting.

assignments.name: Reading Log with Reflection
assignments.body: Students track daily independent reading in a simple log (title, minutes, pages, one-sentence honest reaction). At the end of each week, they write two sentences: what kept them reading, or what made them want to stop. The reflection is the assessment — not the page count.

assignments.name: Recommend It Forward
assignments.body: After finishing a self-selected text, students write a 150-word recommendation addressed to a specific classmate by name, explaining why that person in particular should read it. Post recommendations to a class board. Watching classmates pick up books on a friend's recommendation is the point.

mastery: Selects texts with genuine personal investment — can articulate why the specific book, not just why the genre.
Reads for the full sustained period without redirection, restlessness, or performance of reading behavior.
Adjusts selections across the semester — moves across genres, authors, and difficulty levels rather than orbiting the same comfort zone.
Responds honestly in reading logs or conferences — names what is working, what isn't, and why, without performing enthusiasm.
Carries reading volume beyond the classroom — references books in discussion, makes connections to other texts, and occasionally reads past the assigned period.
notes: This standard carries no sub-letters and is not divided into discrete skills — it is a practice standard, not a knowledge standard. DOK 2 reflects the application of a habit rather than recall or simple comprehension.

Vertical continuity: the expectation is identical in phrasing across English I–IV. The ramp is in what self-selection looks like at increasing sophistication — the complexity of texts students reach for, the range of genres they explore, and the stamina they sustain. English III students should be choosing texts that demand something from them, not just texts that confirm what they already know they like.

STAAR does not directly assess this standard. Question Stems above mirror how STAAR tests the analog skills — inferring a reader's purpose, analyzing text features that drive engagement, evaluating reading strategies — rather than assessing the act of independent reading itself. This is noted so teachers do not deprioritize the standard because it lacks a direct test item; the standard feeds everything that IS tested.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Establish purpose for reading assigned and self-selected texts
expectation: Establish purpose for reading assigned and self-selected texts.
overview: Most high schoolers read the same way regardless of why they're reading — eyes on words, pages turning, retain what sticks. The English III move is reading on purpose: knowing before page one whether they're reading to argue, to find information, to enjoy, to model their own writing, to prepare for a test, or to push back against a claim. The same passage rewards different readings depending on what you came to it for.
studentFriendly: Before you start reading, you can decide WHY you're reading — what you want to know, what you want to use, what question you want answered — and that decision changes how you read.
questionStems: The author's MOST likely purpose for writing the selection is to —
The reader's MOST likely purpose for reading the selection is to —
Which strategy would BEST help a reader who is reading the selection to [purpose]?
A reader reading the selection to [purpose] should MOST likely focus on paragraph —
Which paragraph would be MOST useful to a reader who is researching [topic]?
To find information about [topic], the reader should focus on —
Which detail from the selection would be MOST important to a reader who is studying [topic]?
The selection would be MOST useful for a reader who wants to —
Based on the title and headings, the reader can predict that the selection will MOST likely —
Which section of the selection BEST helps a reader who wants to [purpose]?

misconceptions.title: Purpose just means "to understand"
