misconceptions.body: Students give "to understand the text" as their purpose for every reading and think they've answered the question. But "understand" is what reading does; it's not why you're doing it. Push them: understand WHAT for WHAT? Are you reading to write a response, build an argument, learn a procedure, study craft, or something else? "To understand" is the floor, not the goal.

misconceptions.title: Purpose is what the teacher told me
misconceptions.body: Students externalize purpose entirely — purpose is whatever the assignment said. But assigned reading still requires them to internalize a purpose. "Read chapter four" is a task; the student's job is to translate it into a reading stance: am I reading this to extract themes, to follow plot, to mine for evidence, to study technique? If they can't articulate that translation, they're reading on autopilot.

misconceptions.title: Self-selected reading needs no purpose
misconceptions.body: Students think purpose is only for school reading. But picking up a book at the library is itself an act of purpose-setting — escapism, learning, professional growth, taste-development. Teach them to make it conscious. The reader who knows why they picked the book finishes more books and remembers more from them.

misconceptions.title: One purpose per text
misconceptions.body: Students treat purpose like a single switch. But strong readers shift purpose mid-text — start reading for entertainment, hit a passage worth studying for craft, slow down, then resume the entertainment read. Teach them that purpose can be layered and can change. The shift itself is a sign of a developing reader.

misconceptions.title: If you didn't get what you came for, you read it wrong
misconceptions.body: Students assume mismatch between purpose and outcome means a reading failure. Often it means the text was wrong for the purpose, or that the reading revealed a better question than the one they walked in with. Reframe: a purpose that doesn't survive contact with the text is data about either the text or the reader. Both are worth knowing.

assignments.name: Pre-Read Stance
assignments.body: Before reading any assigned text, students write 2–3 sentences: what they expect to get from it, what they'll mark, how they'll know when they've succeeded. After reading, return to the stance and assess. Does this twice a week. Build the habit.

assignments.name: One Text, Three Purposes
assignments.body: Give the whole class the same article. Assign each student one of three purposes (find the strongest evidence, model the introduction style, identify the weakest point in the argument). Compare what each group noticed. Same text, three readings.

assignments.name: Self-Selected Justification
assignments.body: Students bring a self-selected book. They write a one-paragraph justification: why this book, what they're after, what they'll know when they got it. Re-write the justification after finishing. Compare. Discuss.

assignments.name: Purpose Mid-Stream
assignments.body: Students annotate a chapter and mark every place their purpose shifted — where they slowed down, where they sped up, where their question changed. Submit annotations + a paragraph on what triggered each shift.

assignments.name: Reading the Wrong Text
assignments.body: Students attempt to fulfill a research purpose using a text that doesn't quite fit. They document the mismatch and decide whether to abandon, adjust, or supplement. The skill is recognizing the mismatch quickly and deciding well.

mastery: Articulates a specific reading purpose before starting an assigned text — not just "to understand" but what they want from this particular text for what particular use.
Translates teacher-given assignments into operational reading stances (slow here, mark this, ignore that).
Distinguishes the purpose for self-selected reading from the purpose for assigned reading and adjusts accordingly.
Notices and adjusts when a text isn't serving the purpose — either changing the purpose or supplementing the text.
Reflects after reading: did the purpose survive the text? What did the reading reveal that the purpose didn't anticipate?
notes: This TEK is the metacognitive front-end of all reading instruction. It's tempting to skip — "of course they have a purpose" — but most high schoolers don't, not consciously. Build a 90-second pre-read ritual into your routine and the rest of Std 4 gets easier.

Vertical jump from English II: same skill, but English III students are reading texts where purpose-setting matters more — denser informational texts, multi-text research, and self-selected reading that's now adjacent to college-level material.
DOK 2 justification: skill-based with planning. The judgment work pushes into DOK 3 when students evaluate text-purpose fit and adjust mid-read.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Generate questions before, during, and after reading
expectation: Generate questions about text before, during, and after reading to deepen understanding and gain information.
overview: The reader who asks questions out-reads the reader who waits to be asked. This TEK pushes students past comprehension-check questions ("what color was her dress?") into the questions a thinking reader actually asks — the ones that name what's missing, what's strange, what doesn't add up, what the writer left out. The skill ramps across the reading: pre-read questions are predictive, mid-read questions are reactive, post-read questions are evaluative.
studentFriendly: Good readers don't just read — they ask questions while they read. Before, during, and after, you can ask the kind of question that pulls more out of the text than the text seemed willing to give.
questionStems: Which question would BEST help the reader understand the central idea of the selection before reading?
After reading paragraph X, which question would BEST help the reader understand the selection?
Which question is MOST clearly answered by the selection?
Which question would BEST help the reader understand the author's purpose?
Which question BEST captures the focus of paragraph X?
Which question would help the reader BEST evaluate the author's argument?
After finishing the selection, the reader would MOST likely have which question?
Before reading paragraph X, which question would BEST prepare the reader to understand it?
Which question does the selection raise but NOT answer?
Which question would BEST guide the reader through paragraphs X–Y?

misconceptions.title: Questions are what you ask when you're confused
misconceptions.body: Students treat questions as a confession of failure — only ask if you got lost. But the strongest questions come from readers who are tracking the text closely enough to notice what's missing. Reframe: questions are what skilled readers DO, not what struggling readers retreat to. The skilled reader has more questions, not fewer.

misconceptions.title: A good question has a clear answer in the text
misconceptions.body: Students think questions should be answerable from the reading. But the best comprehension questions are often the ones the text DOESN'T answer — the gaps, the choices the writer made, the things assumed. Teach them to value those questions equally. Sometimes the most important question is "why didn't the writer talk about X?"

misconceptions.title: Pre-reading questions are predictions
misconceptions.body: Students collapse pre-reading questions into "guess what'll happen." But pre-reading questions are wider than prediction — what do I already think about this topic, what do I want from this text, what do I expect to be challenged on, what am I hoping to learn? Predicting plot is one tiny corner of pre-reading.

misconceptions.title: Post-reading questions go in a journal entry
misconceptions.body: Students treat post-reading questions as a writing exercise rather than the start of further inquiry. But the question that surfaces AFTER you've finished often points to what's worth reading next, what to discuss, what to research. Treat post-reading questions as live — they're the launch pad for the next text, not a graveyard for what you didn't get.

misconceptions.title: Generating questions slows reading down
misconceptions.body: Students resist active questioning because it interrupts flow. But unasked questions slow reading down MORE — every confusion ignored becomes a comprehension debt that compounds. Teach them to ask the question fast and either resolve it or note it and keep moving. The question isn't the friction; the unasked question is.

assignments.name: The Pre/Mid/Post Stack
assignments.body: Before reading: write three questions. Mid-read: stop at predetermined points and write one question each. After: write three more. Submit the full stack with annotations. Review which questions got answered, which evolved, which dropped, and which became the ones worth keeping.

assignments.name: Question Upgrade
assignments.body: Pair work. Each student writes a comprehension question about the same text. They trade and rewrite their partner's question to be stronger — deeper, more specific, more textually grounded. Discuss what the upgrade did.

assignments.name: Gap Hunting
assignments.body: Students annotate a nonfiction article and mark every place the author asserted something without supporting it. For each gap, write the question they'd ask the author if they could. Submit five strongest. Discuss which are unanswered and why.

assignments.name: Question Trees
assignments.body: Pick a complex question raised by a text. Students branch it into 3–5 sub-questions, then 3–5 sub-sub-questions. The exercise is in the branching — which questions open up the topic, which close it down. Map the tree on paper.

assignments.name: Answer-First Reverse
assignments.body: Give students a paragraph (an answer) without context. They generate the question(s) the paragraph is responding to. Compare across the class. Discuss what makes the same paragraph readable as an answer to multiple different questions.

mastery: Generates questions across all three phases — predictive before, reactive during, evaluative after — without prompting.
Distinguishes literal-comprehension questions from interpretive, evaluative, and inquiry-driving questions, and uses each appropriately.
Notices what a text DIDN'T address and frames the absence as a question worth asking.
Treats pre-reading questions as planning tools that shape how they read, not just guesses about content.
Uses post-reading questions to drive next steps — further reading, discussion, writing — rather than treating them as a wrap-up activity.
notes: The same questioning skill lives in 1B (oral) and 11A (research). Cross-reference them when teaching — the same student who's learning to ask pertinent questions in conversation is learning to ask pertinent questions of a text.

Vertical jump from English II: students should now be generating questions that anticipate authorial choices, not just plot beats. The questioning gets metacognitive — about the writer's moves, not just the content.
DOK 3 justification: strategic thinking required to generate questions that drive inference, evaluation, and inquiry rather than literal recall.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Make and confirm predictions using text features and structures
expectation: Make and correct or confirm predictions using text features, characteristics of genre, and structures.
overview: Prediction sounds elementary — "what will happen next?" — but the English III version is about reading the architecture of a text fast enough to anticipate where the writer is going. A reader who knows that a five-paragraph op-ed will end with a call to action reads it differently than one who doesn't. The skill is recognizing the form, predicting the moves, and adjusting when the writer breaks pattern.
studentFriendly: The features and structure of a text — headings, genre conventions, paragraph patterns — let you predict what's coming. Then you read on and either confirm what you expected or correct your prediction based on what you actually find.
questionStems: Based on the title and headings, the reader can predict that the selection will MOST likely —
Which feature of the selection BEST helps the reader predict its content?
Based on paragraph X, the reader can predict that —
Based on the structure of the selection, the reader can predict that paragraph X will —
Based on the genre of the selection, the reader can MOST likely predict —
Which detail from the selection BEST confirms the prediction that [outcome]?
After reading paragraphs X–Y, what can the reader predict about [outcome]?
The first paragraph of the selection MOST likely suggests that —
Which text feature BEST helps the reader predict the focus of the selection?
Based on the introduction, the reader can predict that the rest of the selection will —

misconceptions.title: Prediction is for fiction
misconceptions.body: Students think predicting belongs in novels — what'll happen next? But informational and argumentative texts reward prediction even more: what's the writer going to claim, what counterargument will they raise, where's the call to action. Teach prediction across genres, and emphasize that strong predictive readers anticipate writers' moves, not just plot.

misconceptions.title: A good prediction is one that comes true
misconceptions.body: Students treat prediction as a guessing game with right and wrong answers. But the value is in the prediction process — what cues did you read, what convention did you assume, what made you guess that? A wrong prediction reveals more than a right one if the reader actually examines why they were wrong.

misconceptions.title: Text features are for kids
misconceptions.body: Students dismiss attention to titles, subheadings, captions, and bold print as elementary-school work. But high-level readers use these features constantly to navigate dense informational texts. The textbook has subheadings for a reason; the article has a pull-quote for a reason. Teach students to read the architecture before reading the prose.

misconceptions.title: "Confirming" a prediction means it was right
misconceptions.body: Students think confirmation requires a match. But "confirm" can mean partial — predicted the right move but wrong timing, predicted the right structure but wrong content. Teach the gradations: was your prediction right in shape, in detail, in spirit, or none of the above? Each level of fit teaches something different.

misconceptions.title: Genre conventions are rules
misconceptions.body: Students treat genre as a checklist — sonnet has 14 lines, op-ed argues a position, mystery reveals the killer. But genre is a set of expectations writers can fulfill, modify, or subvert. The skilled reader uses genre to predict, then notices when the writer deviates and asks why. Convention isn't constraint; it's a starting position to read against.

assignments.name: Read the Architecture First
assignments.body: Hand out an article. Students read ONLY the title, subheadings, first sentences of each paragraph, and any pull-quotes — then write their predictions about the article's structure and argument. Then they read the full article and assess. Discuss the gap between what the architecture promised and what the prose delivered.

assignments.name: Genre Bingo
assignments.body: Students get a card with five genre conventions for the form they're reading (op-ed, personal essay, scientific abstract, literary criticism). They mark each convention as it appears. Discuss which were predictable, which were broken, which were subverted.

assignments.name: Stop and Predict
assignments.body: During a long reading, the teacher signals stop points. At each stop, students predict the next move (next paragraph, next section, next chapter). After reading on, they assess. Patterns emerge across the class about which kinds of predictions tend to be reliable.

assignments.name: Prediction Audit
assignments.body: After finishing a longer text (story, essay, novel chapter), students list five predictions they made along the way. For each: what was the prediction, what was the actual outcome, what cue did they read, and was the cue trustworthy? Discuss the calibration.

assignments.name: Subverted Conventions
assignments.body: Students find a published text that BREAKS the conventions of its genre. They diagnose where it broke, why the writer broke it, and whether the break worked. Builds the habit of reading convention as a frame to push against.

mastery: Uses text features (titles, headings, captions, pull-quotes) and genre conventions to anticipate text structure and content before reading.
Updates predictions actively as new evidence arrives — not just at the end, but throughout the reading.
Distinguishes between a writer breaking convention strategically and the reader misreading the convention.
Confirms or corrects predictions explicitly — names what was right, what was off, and what cue led them astray.
Uses prediction across genres — applies the skill to argument, exposition, narrative, and poetry, not just plot-driven fiction.
notes: The TEK explicitly covers "correct OR confirm" — the corrective work is half the skill. Build the habit of returning to predictions to assess them; otherwise students predict and forget. The assessment is where the learning lives.

Vertical jump from English II: predictions in English II focus on plot and main idea; English III pushes to predicting *authorial moves* — argument turns, structural pivots, rhetorical strategies. The skill ramps from "what happens" to "what the writer does."
DOK 2 justification: applying knowledge of structure to predict; pushes to DOK 3 when students evaluate why predictions failed and what convention was at play.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Create mental images to deepen understanding
expectation: Create mental images to deepen understanding.
overview: Mental imaging looks like an elementary skill — "picture it in your head" — but in English III it's how readers turn dense, abstract, or unfamiliar prose into something they actually understand. A reader who builds a mental image of a courtroom they've never seen, a battlefield they've never walked, or a chemical reaction they've never witnessed can hold the prose. A reader who skims past visualization loses fidelity fast. The skill matters more in junior year, not less, because the texts are harder.
studentFriendly: When you read, your brain can build a movie of what you're reading — what things look like, sound like, feel like. The clearer the movie, the better you understand what's actually on the page.
questionStems: Which detail in paragraph X BEST helps the reader visualize [setting/event]?
The author's description in paragraph X helps the reader to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST creates a mental image of [scene]?
The imagery in paragraph X MOST likely helps the reader understand —
Which sensory detail BEST helps the reader picture [setting/character]?
Based on the description in paragraph X, the reader can MOST likely picture —
Which words from the selection BEST help the reader form a mental image of [topic]?
The author MOST likely uses sensory language in paragraph X to —
The description in paragraphs X–Y BEST helps the reader visualize —
Which detail from the selection contributes MOST to the reader's mental picture of [setting]?

misconceptions.title: Visualization is just for novels
misconceptions.body: Students think mental imaging belongs to fiction. But informational, scientific, and historical texts reward visualization too — picturing a courtroom, a watershed, a chemical lab, a 1920s factory floor. Pushing students to visualize unfamiliar settings is often the difference between reading the words and understanding the content.

misconceptions.title: Either you see it or you don't
misconceptions.body: Some students claim they "can't visualize." Often they're imaging differently — kinesthetically, spatially, narratively — and don't recognize it as imaging. Reframe: mental image isn't always a picture. It can be a sense of where things are, what's happening, who's near whom. Coach them to notice what their brain IS building, even if it's not a movie.

misconceptions.title: A mental image is decorative
misconceptions.body: Students treat imaging as an extra — nice if you can, not essential. But mental images are how memory consolidates. The student who pictures the scene remembers the chapter. The student who reads through visual prose flat retains less. Make this explicit: imaging isn't a flourish, it's a memory tool.

misconceptions.title: Vivid images come from vivid writing only
misconceptions.body: Students think they need writers like Dickens or Cormac McCarthy to visualize. But spare prose can produce sharp images IF the reader is doing the work. Hemingway's iceberg principle requires the reader to fill in. Teach students to visualize even when the writer didn't lay it out — especially then.

misconceptions.title: My image has to match what the writer saw
misconceptions.body: Students think there's a correct mental image and they're trying to nail it. But mental images are negotiations between text and reader; two readers can image the same passage differently, and both can be defensible if the text supports them. Teach them to defend their image from the text rather than worry about matching some teacher's version.

assignments.name: Read and Sketch
assignments.body: Students read a vivid descriptive passage and produce a quick sketch — figures, layout, key objects. The sketch isn't graded for art; it's graded for textual fidelity. What did the text tell them? What did they add? Discuss in pairs.

assignments.name: Movie of the Mind
assignments.body: After reading a chapter, students cast it like a movie — describe the actors, the lighting, the camera angles, the soundtrack. Forces them to articulate what their imaging produced. Discuss what their casting reveals about how they read.

assignments.name: The Unwritten Detail
assignments.body: Students annotate a passage and mark every place they had to fill in detail the writer left out. Was the writer trusting the reader, lazy, deliberately ambiguous, or something else? Compare students' fill-ins.

assignments.name: Visualize the Abstract
assignments.body: Students take a paragraph from a science, history, or philosophy text — abstract, idea-heavy, low on visual cues — and build a metaphor or mental model that lets them hold it. Share models in class. Discuss which models work and why.

assignments.name: Soundtrack the Passage
assignments.body: Students read a passage of prose or poetry and create a short audio playlist (3–4 songs) that matches its mood, pace, and emotional shape. Defend the playlist from the text. The exercise forces them to articulate what they sensed.

mastery: Builds mental images while reading without prompting, across genres — fiction, poetry, informational, technical.
Distinguishes what the text gave from what the reader supplied, and can articulate the difference.
Uses mental imaging to navigate unfamiliar settings, processes, and concepts that lie outside personal experience.
Defends their mental image from textual evidence rather than treating it as private or ineffable.
Recognizes when a flat or absent image is a sign the prose is losing them, and uses imaging as a comprehension repair strategy.
notes: This TEK is a strong pairing with 2B (imagery as a vocabulary skill) and 8D (language shaping perception). The same student doing imaging work in 4D is reading imagery in 2B and analyzing diction in 8D — the three skills compound. Teach them in proximity.

Vertical jump from English II: same skill, more challenging texts. By English III the visualization work happens against denser informational and literary prose where the imaging is the comprehension move, not an enrichment activity.
DOK 2 justification: applying knowledge to construct meaning; pushes to DOK 3 when students defend their image against alternative imagings using textual evidence.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Make connections to personal experience, other texts, and society
expectation: Make connections to personal experiences, ideas in other texts, and society.
overview: The classic three-bucket move — text-to-self, text-to-text, text-to-world — but the English III version refuses sentimentality. The point isn't to slap a personal anecdote on the page; it's to use the connection to deepen the read. A connection that doesn't change how you understand the text is filler. A connection that DOES change the read is analysis.
studentFriendly: What you read connects to your life, to other things you've read, and to what's happening in the world. The strongest connections are the ones that change how you understand the text — not the ones that just add a personal story.
questionStems: The author of this selection would MOST likely agree with the author of —
Which idea in the selection BEST connects to [topic] in society today?
Which event in the selection MOST closely connects to [historical/cultural event]?
The reader's experience with [topic] would MOST likely help the reader understand —
Which detail from the selection BEST connects to a similar idea in [paired text]?
The selection's central idea is MOST similar to the central idea of —
Which connection between the selection and [topic] is BEST supported by the text?
Based on the selection, the reader can MOST likely connect [idea in text] to —
Which broader social pattern is BEST illustrated by the events in paragraphs X–Y?
The author MOST likely expects the reader to connect [event in text] to —

misconceptions.title: Personal connection means personal anecdote
misconceptions.body: Students slap a story from their own lives onto an essay and call it analysis. But personal connection is only useful if it sharpens the read — illuminates a character's motive, makes a theme concrete, names a feeling the writer was reaching for. Teach them: if the personal story doesn't change how you read the next paragraph, leave it out.

misconceptions.title: Text-to-text means "this reminds me of another book"
misconceptions.body: Students name a vague resemblance and stop. Push deeper: what are the two texts actually doing similarly, and where do they diverge? A useful text-to-text connection treats the comparison as analytic — same theme, different treatment? Same structure, different content? Same era, different stance? The connection should produce a question worth pursuing, not a checkbox.

misconceptions.title: Connections require shared experience
misconceptions.body: Students think they can't connect to texts about lives unlike theirs. But the bridge is rarely identical experience — it's analogous emotion, parallel structure, recognizable dilemma. Teach them that a reader can connect to a 19th-century enslaved person's narrative without having lived it; the connection is to the human reality of the work, not the surface details.

misconceptions.title: "Society" connections mean current events
misconceptions.body: Students reduce text-to-world to "what's in the news." But society connections are wider: structures (class, power, gender), institutions (school, family, work), patterns (migration, surveillance, debt). A novel set in 1850 connects to society through what it shows about labor, gender, faith — not just whether labor today is the same.

misconceptions.title: A good connection is one I feel
misconceptions.body: Students assume emotional resonance is the marker of a good connection. But the strongest connections are often the ones that produce intellectual recognition — "oh, this is the same problem in a different costume." Teach them to value cold connections as much as warm ones. Sometimes the best connection is a structural parallel that surprises you.

assignments.name: The Cold Connection
assignments.body: Students make a text-to-text or text-to-world connection that ISN'T emotional — purely structural, thematic, or historical. They defend it from both texts. Forces them past sentimental connection-making.

assignments.name: Connection Audit
assignments.body: Students annotate a chapter with three connections (one self, one text, one world). For each, they write one sentence on what the connection LET them notice in the original text that they wouldn't have otherwise. Submit. Grade for the second sentence.

assignments.name: Bad Connections
assignments.body: Students write three deliberately bad connections (vague, irrelevant, sentimental) and three good ones for the same passage. Discuss the difference. Builds metacognition about what makes a connection earn its place.

assignments.name: The Distortion Question
assignments.body: When students make a text-to-world connection, they ALSO write what the connection distorts — what it gets wrong about either the text or the world. Forces care about the analogy's limits.

assignments.name: Connection Conversation
assignments.body: Pairs of students discuss a text. One student's job: make connections. Other student's job: push back when the connection doesn't earn its place. Switch roles. Builds the muscle of distinguishing connection-as-analysis from connection-as-filler.

mastery: Makes connections that change the reading — produce new noticing, new questions, new interpretation — rather than connections that sit alongside the text.
Distinguishes weak personal-anecdote connections from strong analogical or structural ones.
Connects across texts in ways that frame comparison as a question rather than a resemblance check.
Reads texts about unfamiliar lives, eras, and contexts without claiming that lack of shared experience prevents connection.
Names what a connection illuminates AND what it distorts; uses the analogy critically.
notes: 4E and 5A overlap heavily. 4E is the cognitive move during reading; 5A is the response move after. When teaching, sequence them: 4E happens in margins and discussion; 5A happens in writing. Don't double-teach as if they were separate skills.

Vertical jump from English II: English II students made connections; English III students *evaluate* their own connections. The metacognitive layer — is this connection earning its place? — is the new bar.
DOK 3 justification: strategic thinking required to evaluate connections for analytical value rather than surface resemblance. The judgment work distinguishes English III from earlier grades.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Make inferences and use evidence to support understanding
expectation: Make inferences and use evidence to support understanding.
overview: Inference is the move beyond what the text literally says — what it suggests, implies, or builds toward. The English III bar is two-part: not just "what can you infer" but "what evidence anchors the inference." Students need to stop floating claims and start locking them to specific lines.
studentFriendly: You can read between the lines — figure out what the text is saying without saying it — and back up your reading with specific words from the page.
questionStems: Based on information in paragraph X, the reader can infer that —
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the inference that [character/idea]?
The reader can MOST likely conclude from paragraphs X–Y that —
Which detail from the selection supports the conclusion that [statement]?
Which quotation from the selection BEST supports the idea that —
What does the description in paragraph X suggest about [character/situation]?
The author MOST likely includes [detail] to show that —
Which statement is BEST supported by the information in the selection?
Read this sentence from paragraph X. What does this sentence suggest about [topic]?
Based on the selection, what can the reader infer about [character's] feelings toward [topic]?

misconceptions.title: Inference is just a fancy word for guess
misconceptions.body: Students treat inference as imagination — whatever feels true about the text. But an inference is a claim with evidence; if they can't point to a line, they're guessing. Teach them: every inference must answer the follow-up question "where do you see that?" If the answer is "I just feel it," it's not an inference yet.

misconceptions.title: The longest quote is the strongest evidence
misconceptions.body: Students grab massive blocks and call it support. The strongest evidence is the most specific, not the most. One precise sentence beats a paragraph dumped wholesale. Watch for students who quote a full chunk because they don't know which sentence inside it is doing the work — that's a comprehension issue masquerading as a citation issue.

misconceptions.title: If the text doesn't contradict it, it's a valid reading
misconceptions.body: Students think any inference not directly disproven by the text is fair game. But inference is constrained — some readings are better-supported than others. Teach them to rank competing readings by evidence weight, not by which one feels right. A defensible inference is the one with the most lines pointing at it.

misconceptions.title: Evidence comes after the inference
misconceptions.body: Students write the claim first, then hunt for support — the worst possible order. Stronger move: read for evidence FIRST, let the inference emerge from the cluster of details. Reframe inference as pattern recognition, not opinion-defending. The evidence should be doing the work.

misconceptions.title: Quoting the obvious sentence counts as inference
misconceptions.body: When the author flat-out states something, quoting it isn't an inference — it's a citation. Inference lives in what the author shows but doesn't say. Watch for students who answer "what can you infer" by lifting the most explicit line in the passage. Push them to the sentences that imply rather than state.

assignments.name: One-Line Evidence
assignments.body: Students make 5 inferences from a chapter. For each, they support with ONE sentence from the text — not a paragraph, not a chunk. Forces precision and rewards readers who can locate the load-bearing line.

assignments.name: The Best Evidence
assignments.body: Give students an inference. They find three different lines that could support it. Rank the lines strongest to weakest and defend the ranking. Builds the muscle of evidence quality, not just evidence presence.

assignments.name: Inference vs. Stated Sort
assignments.body: Mixed list of statements about a text — some are inferences, some are direct quotes. Students sort each. Discuss the borderlines. Surfaces the difference between "the text says" and "the text implies."

assignments.name: Reverse Inference
assignments.body: Give students a single sentence from a text. They write three different inferences it could support. Discuss which is most defensible and why. Surfaces how the same evidence can point in multiple directions.

assignments.name: Layered Inference
assignments.body: Students make a basic inference from one detail, then a deeper inference from three details together. Show that combining evidence produces stronger reads than single-line reads.

mastery: Distinguishes between an inference (claim + textual support) and a guess (claim alone) without prompting.
Selects evidence with precision — one strong sentence, not a quoted block.
Ranks competing readings by the weight of evidence rather than personal preference.
Builds layered inferences from clusters of details rather than single lines.
Names what the text shows vs. what it states — and treats the gap as the work of inference.
notes: 4F is the most heavily STAAR-tested skill in Std 4. Inference + evidence questions appear on every released test in multiple forms — direct inference, BEST support, MOST likely conclusion. Worth front-loading the testing vocabulary.

Vertical jump from English II: same skill name, but English III texts are longer and inferences are more layered. The stretch is making inferences from patterns across paragraphs, not single sentences.
DOK 3 justification: strategic thinking — students must select among competing inferences and weigh evidence quality, not just produce a claim.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Evaluate details read to understand key ideas
expectation: Evaluate details read to understand key ideas.
overview: Not all details carry equal weight. Students often read every sentence as if it mattered the same, then drown in the text. The English III move is learning to weigh — which details are load-bearing for the central idea, which support, which decorate. Reading well is reading as ranking, not just collecting.
studentFriendly: You can tell which details in a text are doing the heavy lifting — building the main idea — and which are just adding flavor. Not every sentence matters the same.
questionStems: Which sentence from the selection BEST states the central idea?
Which detail is MOST important to the development of [topic]?
What is the BEST summary of the selection?
Which idea from paragraph X is MOST important to understanding the selection as a whole?
Which detail BEST supports the central idea of the selection?
Which sentence is LEAST important to the development of the central idea?
How does paragraph X contribute to the development of the central idea?
Which statement BEST captures the central idea of the selection?
The author MOST likely includes the details in paragraph X to —
Which paragraph BEST develops the idea that [claim]?

misconceptions.title: Every detail matters equally
misconceptions.body: Students treat all details as equally important and end up writing summaries that are just shorter retellings of the text. Teach them to rank: load-bearing for the main idea, supporting, decorative. The skill of evaluating details is a skill of distinguishing between them, not collecting all of them.

misconceptions.title: The first sentence is always the main idea
misconceptions.body: Topic-sentence dogma left over from middle school. Push them to test it: is the first sentence actually the main idea, or is it just the entry point? Sometimes the central idea emerges across paragraphs, sometimes it lands at the end, sometimes it's never explicitly stated. Teach them to look for it, not to assume it.

misconceptions.title: Summary equals compression
misconceptions.body: Students compress a text by trimming length and call it a summary. But real summary requires choosing which ideas to keep — and which to cut. The skill is selection, not abbreviation. Watch for summaries that read like the original text shrunk; those are compressions, not summaries.

misconceptions.title: Important means most-mentioned
misconceptions.body: Students assume the most-frequently-stated detail is the most important. Sometimes it is. Sometimes the most important detail appears once but underlies everything else. Teach them to ask: what would the text lose if this were removed? That question separates frequency from function.

misconceptions.title: Details are just facts
misconceptions.body: Students use "detail" to mean "concrete statement" and ignore description, comparison, anecdote, and quotation as if those weren't details. Teach them: a detail is anything specific the author chose to include. The metaphor in paragraph 3 is a detail. The quoted dialogue is a detail. The author's word choice is a detail.

assignments.name: Three-Tier Detail Sort
assignments.body: Students read a chapter and sort the details into three tiers: load-bearing for the central idea, supporting, decorative. Defend each placement with one sentence. Surfaces which students can already evaluate vs. which still treat all details as equal.

assignments.name: Cut to Half
assignments.body: Give students a passage and tell them to cut it to half its length without losing the central idea. Forces them to evaluate which details survive — and which they thought mattered but actually don't.

assignments.name: The Missing Detail
assignments.body: Students identify ONE detail that, if removed, would weaken the central idea most. They defend the choice. Builds the muscle of pinpointing the load-bearing line rather than naming the most obvious one.

assignments.name: Different Summaries
assignments.body: Same text, different lengths — students write a 1-sentence, a 3-sentence, and a 1-paragraph summary. Compare which details survive each cut. Reveals the hierarchy of importance the student is operating with.

assignments.name: Reverse Outline
assignments.body: Students read a finished essay or chapter and write the implicit outline the author followed. Identifies which details serve which structural function — introduce, develop, complicate, conclude.

mastery: Distinguishes load-bearing details from decorative ones and defends the distinction with reasoning, not impression.
Articulates the central idea of a selection using language not lifted from a single sentence — synthesizing across the text.
Writes summaries that select rather than compress — leaving details out deliberately, not by accident.
Identifies which paragraphs do which work in a longer text (introduce, develop, complicate, conclude).
Names the function of a detail (support, contrast, exemplify, qualify) rather than just naming the detail itself.
notes: 4G is the central-idea + key-detail skill that STAAR tests heavily — usually 2–3 questions per passage. "BEST summary," "MOST important detail," "BEST supports the central idea" all live here. Worth teaching the testing language explicitly.
Vertical jump from English II: students moved from identifying main ideas to evaluating which details build them. The evaluative move is the distinguishing English III layer.
DOK 3 justification: requires strategic comparison of details for relative importance, not just identification or recall.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Synthesize information from a variety of text types to create new understanding
expectation: Synthesize information from a variety of text types to create new understanding.
overview: Synthesis is more than summary across multiple texts. Students read two articles and produce a "they both said this" report — that's compilation, not synthesis. Real synthesis builds something neither text contained alone. By English III, students should be reading multiple texts as a conversation and producing the third position the conversation creates.
studentFriendly: When you read two or more texts together, you can build an understanding that's BIGGER than either text alone — not just a list of what each said, but something new the texts together let you see.
questionStems: What is one similarity between the two selections?
What is one difference between the two selections?
With which statement would the authors of both selections MOST likely agree?
How are the authors' opinions about [topic] similar in the two selections?
How are the authors' opinions about [topic] different in the two selections?
Both selections support the idea that —
Which idea is presented in Selection 1 but NOT in Selection 2?
Read this sentence from Selection 1 and this sentence from Selection 2. What is the MOST significant difference between the views expressed?
Based on both selections, the reader can conclude that —
The authors of both selections would MOST likely agree that —

misconceptions.title: Synthesis is side-by-side summary
misconceptions.body: Students produce two paragraphs ("Article 1 says… Article 2 says…") and call it synthesis. The synthesis move is the THIRD paragraph — the one that builds something neither text said. Teach them: if your writing reports on the texts in sequence, you've summarized; if your writing makes a claim the texts together support, you've synthesized.

misconceptions.title: Texts have to agree to be synthesized
misconceptions.body: Students think synthesis requires the texts to agree. The opposite is often true: the friction between texts is the raw material for synthesis. Teach them to USE disagreement — the question becomes "what does the disagreement reveal?" Two texts arguing past each other can produce a sharper third position than two texts nodding along.

misconceptions.title: Different genres can't be synthesized
misconceptions.body: Students refuse to put a poem and an essay in conversation, or a memoir and a news article. They see genre boundaries as walls. Teach them: different genres treating the same theme produce richer synthesis than two essays on the topic, because each genre lights up a different dimension. The genre difference IS the affordance.

misconceptions.title: Synthesis means picking a side
misconceptions.body: Students read two opposing texts and feel forced to choose a winner. But synthesis is often the position that contains both, qualifies both, or finds the level at which both are partially right. Teach them: synthesis can be agreement-with-qualification, not just verdict.

misconceptions.title: Stitching quotes equals synthesis
misconceptions.body: Students paste together quotes from each text and call the result a synthesis. But quotes are evidence, not structure. Real synthesis is the student's own claim that the texts together support — quotes do the supporting, not the speaking. Watch for paragraphs that are 70% quotation; those are usually unsynthesized.

assignments.name: Three Texts, One Question
assignments.body: Students read three short pieces on the same topic (one informational, one literary, one personal essay). They produce one paragraph that synthesizes — not summarizes — across all three. Length cap forces them past sequential reporting.

assignments.name: The Conversation Map
assignments.body: Students map two texts as a conversation. What does Text 1 say to Text 2? Where do they agree? Disagree? What would each say to the other if they could? Visual structure surfaces synthesis moves the prose hides.

assignments.name: Find the Third Position
assignments.body: Two texts disagree on a topic. Students articulate the third position — the one neither text takes but both partially support. Forces them off the binary and into synthesis as construction.

assignments.name: Synthesis vs. Summary Sort
assignments.body: Give students sample student paragraphs that purport to synthesize. They sort: real synthesis, or just side-by-side summary? Defend. Builds the metacognitive ability to recognize the move in their own writing.

assignments.name: Genre Pairing
assignments.body: Students pair two texts of different genres on the same theme (a poem + a news article, a memoir + an academic essay). Write a synthesis paragraph that uses BOTH genres' affordances rather than flattening either.

mastery: Produces synthesis that builds new understanding rather than reporting on both texts in sequence.
Treats agreement and disagreement between texts as raw material, not obstacles to overcome.
Synthesizes across genres without flattening either text's distinctive voice.
Articulates a claim the texts together support that neither states explicitly.
Uses quotes as evidence inside synthesis, not as structure for it.
notes: STAAR's paired-passage section lives here. "Both selections," "MOST likely agree," "one difference," "one similarity" — those are 4H questions disguised. Worth teaching as a unit with the testing language exposed up front.
4H and 11D are nearly identical skills with different framing — 4H during reading, 11D during inquiry. Don't double-teach as if they were separate.
Vertical jump from English II: same skill name, but English III synthesis is expected across more text types and longer passages. The stretch is volume + diversity, not the synthesis move itself.
DOK 4 justification: extended reasoning across multiple sources to produce new understanding — the highest cognitive band in Std 4.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Monitor comprehension and make adjustments when understanding breaks down
expectation: Monitor comprehension and make adjustments such as re-reading, using background knowledge, asking questions, annotating, and using outside sources when understanding breaks down.
overview: The metacognitive backbone of all reading — knowing when you're lost, knowing what to do about it. Most struggling readers can't name the moment comprehension broke. By English III, students should be self-aware enough to catch the breakdown in real time and choose a fix-it move from a kit they actually know how to use.
studentFriendly: When you stop understanding what you're reading, you can NOTICE that you stopped — and you have a set of moves (re-read, annotate, look it up, ask a question) you can use to get back on track.
questionStems: Which strategy would BEST help a reader understand paragraph X?
If a reader does not understand the meaning of [term/concept] in paragraph X, what should the reader do?
To clarify the meaning of paragraph X, the reader should —
Which question would BEST help a reader understand the author's purpose in the selection?
Re-reading paragraph X would MOST likely help the reader understand —
Which background information would BEST help a reader understand [concept] in the selection?
To understand the connection between paragraphs X and Y, a reader should —
Which outside source would BEST help a reader understand [topic] in the selection?
After reading paragraph X, the reader is MOST likely to be confused about —
Which strategy would BEST help a reader determine the meaning of [unfamiliar term] in paragraph X?

misconceptions.title: Confusion means the text is bad
misconceptions.body: Students externalize breakdowns: the writer is unclear, the topic is dumb, the assignment is unfair. Sometimes true. Often the reader has a comprehension move available that they're not deploying. Teach them to ask "is it the text or is it me?" — and to know that both are possible answers, and either one points to a different fix.

misconceptions.title: Re-reading is what bad readers do
misconceptions.body: Students treat re-reading as a sign of weakness or stupidity. Strong readers re-read constantly. Re-frame: re-reading is the cheapest, fastest fix-it move available — and it's not the same read twice. Strong re-reading is slower, sentence-by-sentence, with a different angle (what's the subject of this sentence? what does this pronoun refer to?).

misconceptions.title: I'll figure it out as I keep reading
misconceptions.body: Students push through breakdowns hoping clarity returns later. Sometimes it does. Often the breakdown compounds — paragraph 4's confusion makes paragraph 5 incomprehensible. Teach them to STOP at the moment of confusion, name it, and choose a move. The reader who stops to fix the breakdown finishes faster than the one who powers through.

misconceptions.title: Annotation means highlighting
misconceptions.body: Students yellow out half a page and call it annotation. But highlighting is recognition without engagement. Real annotation is conversation — questions in the margin, summaries at section breaks, links between passages, reactions to claims. If they're not writing words, they're not annotating.

misconceptions.title: Looking things up is cheating
misconceptions.body: Students think reaching for a dictionary or encyclopedia undermines their reading. The opposite: a reader who closes the gap with a quick lookup understands the text MORE deeply, not less. Teach the move as part of strong reading, not a workaround. The shame about lookups is leftover from elementary "use context clues" instruction; in English III, lookups are a tool.

assignments.name: The Breakdown Log
assignments.body: Students read a chapter and mark every spot where comprehension broke down. For each breakdown, they name the fix-it move they used (or should have used). Submit annotations + a one-paragraph reflection. Surfaces the moments students don't usually catch.

assignments.name: Strategy Match
assignments.body: Give students 5 short passages, each with a different kind of breakdown (unfamiliar word, dense syntax, missing background, ambiguous reference, abstract concept). They match each breakdown to the best strategy and defend the match.

assignments.name: Annotation Audit
assignments.body: Students hand in their annotations from a recent reading. Grade for VARIETY — questions, summaries, links, reactions, predictions — not for quantity. Highlights aren't annotations.

assignments.name: Lookup Receipts
assignments.body: Students keep a log of every term, name, or concept they looked up during a week of reading. End of week: which lookups paid off most? Which were unnecessary? Which should they have done that they skipped? Builds judgment about when to deploy the move.

assignments.name: The Hardest Paragraph
assignments.body: Students bring the hardest paragraph from a recent reading. Class works through fix-it moves together — re-read, look up, ask, annotate. Demystifies the strategies by showing them in action on real student-found difficulty.

mastery: Catches comprehension breakdowns in the moment they happen rather than at the end of a section.
Names the kind of breakdown (vocabulary, syntax, background, reference, abstraction) and chooses a strategy that fits.
Re-reads strategically — not just again, but with a different angle (slower, sentence-by-sentence, aloud, with annotation).
Treats outside sources (dictionary, encyclopedia, prior reading) as integrated reading tools, not as workarounds or shortcuts.
Reflects on what kinds of texts cause what kinds of breakdowns and pre-deploys strategies on similar future reads.
notes: 4I is the closing TEK in Std 4 because everything else in the standard depends on it. A student who doesn't monitor comprehension can't establish purpose, generate useful questions, or synthesize. Teach this one explicitly even though it feels obvious — most students don't have it.
STAAR tests this less frequently than 4F or 4G but does ask "which strategy" questions on most administrations. Worth recognizing the testing language even if it's not the heaviest assessment target.
Vertical jump from English II: same skill, but English III texts are denser — the breakdowns are different (abstraction, layered argument, unfamiliar register) and the strategy choices have to match.

DOK 2 justification: skill-based with selection — students apply known strategies to known kinds of breakdowns. Pushes to DOK 3 when students must choose among competing strategies for a novel breakdown.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Describe personal connections to a variety of sources
expectation: Describe personal connections to a variety of sources, including self-selected texts.
overview: This is the entry point to response skills — students naming why a text matters to them, in a way that's actually about the text and not just about themselves. By English III, "I liked it" is no longer the bar; students should be able to anchor a personal reaction to specific moments on the page. Self-selection is a quiet but real lift: choosing matters, and so does justifying the choice.
studentFriendly: You can read something — a book, a poem, an article you picked yourself — and explain what it stirred up in you and why, with the "why" pointing at specific lines.
questionStems: Which detail from the selection MOST likely connects to the reader's own experience?
Based on the selection, the reader can MOST likely relate to [character] because —
The author's description in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to make the reader feel —
Which line from the selection BEST captures an emotion the reader might share with [character]?
The reader's response to paragraph X is MOST likely shaped by —
Why might a reader find the events in paragraphs X–Y meaningful?
Which statement BEST describes how the selection might affect a reader who has experienced [situation]?
The narrator's reflection in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to invite the reader to —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which idea is the reader MOST likely to recognize from their own life?
How does the selection's structure encourage the reader to make a personal connection?

misconceptions.title: Personal connection means writing about yourself
misconceptions.body: Students hear "personal" and slide off the text into autobiography. Three paragraphs about their grandma, one sentence about the story. Reframe this: the connection is the bridge — text on one side, reader on the other — and the bridge has to load on both ends. If they aren't quoting the text, they aren't connecting to it.

misconceptions.title: Any reaction counts
misconceptions.body: Students will offer "this was sad" or "I liked the main character" and call it a connection. That's a reaction, not a connection. A connection names a SPECIFIC moment in the text and a SPECIFIC moment in the reader's life or values. Push them to name both halves.

misconceptions.title: Self-selection is a freebie
misconceptions.body: When students choose their own text, they often choose the easiest thing on the shelf and write a thin response. Treat self-selection as a higher bar, not a lower one — they picked it, so they should have more to say. Ask why this text and not another. The choice itself is data.

misconceptions.title: Connections must be positive
misconceptions.body: Students think personal connection means "I related to this." But strong connections include resistance — the parts they pushed back on, the parts that didn't ring true. Disagreement IS connection. Watch for the student who only writes about what aligned with them and missed where the text challenged them.

misconceptions.title: The connection is the analysis
misconceptions.body: Students treat "this reminded me of X" as the whole job and stop there. The connection is the entry, not the destination — once they've made it, the next move is analytical: what does the connection reveal about the text, the author's craft, or the reader's lens? If it doesn't open into something larger, it's a journal entry.

assignments.name: Two-Column Connection
assignments.body: Students pick a passage. Left column: the text, quoted. Right column: their connection, with specific personal detail. The columns must mirror — every text moment gets a connection, every connection points back to a text moment.

assignments.name: Why This Book
assignments.body: For self-selected reading, students write 250 words on why they picked this text and not another. They must reference at least two passages from the first 30 pages that confirm or complicate the choice.

assignments.name: Connection Across Three Texts
assignments.body: Students respond to three different sources (poem, essay, podcast clip) on a shared theme. Each response must name a personal connection AND a craft observation. Surfaces the difference between "I felt seen" and "I see what the author did."

assignments.name: Resistance Log
assignments.body: Students track moments in a text where they DISAGREED with the author or character. They write a connection rooted in pushback, not alignment. Builds the habit of critical reading without breaking engagement.

assignments.name: Connection Reframed as Analysis
assignments.body: Students draft a personal-connection paragraph, then revise it so every "I" sentence is followed by a "the author" sentence. Forces the move from reader-centered to text-centered without losing the reader.

mastery: Names a specific moment in the text AND a specific moment in their experience — both halves load-bearing.
Includes connections that complicate or resist the text, not just connections that affirm.
Justifies self-selection with reference to specific passages, not generic appeal.
Moves from "I felt" to "the author made me feel — by doing X" — connection as a doorway to craft analysis.
Treats personal response as evidence about the reader, not a substitute for evidence about the text.
notes: STAAR rarely tests "personal connection" directly — multiple-choice can't measure a reader's interior. Stems above are the closest analog: questions about how a passage might affect a reader, what a reader might recognize, what the author invites the reader to feel. Treat this TEK as primarily a discussion and writing skill, with STAAR exposure indirect.
Vertical jump from English II: English II asks students to "describe" connections; English III adds "self-selected" — a shift toward independent reading identity and reader-as-curator.
DOK 2 justification: skill of conceptual articulation — students apply a frame (connection) and explain reasoning, but the cognitive demand is below evaluation or extended analysis.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Write responses that demonstrate analysis of texts, including comparing texts within and across genres
expectation: Write responses that demonstrate analysis of texts, including comparing texts within and across genres.
overview: This is the response skill that scales — students moving from "what does this text do" to "what do these two texts do, and what does the comparison reveal?" The hard part is keeping both texts equally on the table; most student comparisons collapse into a long discussion of one text and a paragraph about the other. The point of the move is not to list overlaps but to use one text as a lens that sharpens reading of the other.
studentFriendly: You can write a response that puts two or more texts side-by-side — same genre or different — and shows how reading them together reveals something neither one shows alone.
questionStems: With which statement would the authors of both selections MOST likely agree?
How does the structure of Selection 1 compare to the structure of Selection 2?
Which idea is developed in BOTH selections?
The authors of both selections MOST likely include [detail/strategy] to —
How does the author of Selection 2 challenge or extend an idea presented in Selection 1?
Which sentence from Selection 1 BEST connects to the central idea of Selection 2?
The authors' purposes in Selections 1 and 2 differ MOST in that —
Based on both selections, the reader can MOST likely conclude that —
Which technique is used in Selection 1 but NOT in Selection 2?
How does the genre of each selection shape the way each author develops [theme/argument]?

misconceptions.title: Compare-and-contrast means a list
misconceptions.body: Students reduce comparison to a Venn diagram on the page — three things alike, three things different, done. But comparative analysis is not bookkeeping. The point is to USE one text to read the other better. Reframe: comparison is a tool, not a structure. The essay shouldn't read like an inventory.

misconceptions.title: Same-topic equals comparable
misconceptions.body: Students grab two texts on the same subject and assume they're paired. But two texts about war can be incomparable if their genres, audiences, or purposes don't intersect meaningfully. Teach them to ask "what's the comparison FOR?" before the comparison starts. If the only link is topic, the comparison will be shallow.

misconceptions.title: Split the response in halves
misconceptions.body: Students write one paragraph about Text 1, one paragraph about Text 2, then a paragraph claiming both are interesting. That's two responses stapled together. Push them toward integrated structure: each body paragraph weaves both texts around a single analytic claim. If you can read Text 1 paragraphs without ever needing Text 2, the structure has failed.

misconceptions.title: Genre is just label, not lens
misconceptions.body: When the prompt says "across genres," students note "this is a poem and that's an essay" and move on. But genre is a reading instruction — a poem invites a different kind of attention than an op-ed. Teach them that the cross-genre comparison is most revealing when it asks how each genre's CONVENTIONS shape what each text can and can't do.

misconceptions.title: Analysis is just observation in formal clothes
misconceptions.body: Students will write "Selection 1 uses imagery, and Selection 2 also uses imagery" and call it analysis. That's pairing, not analyzing. Analysis names WHAT each text does, WHY each does it, and what's revealed by putting them together. Watch for the response that's all surface-feature spotting and no consequence.

assignments.name: Lens Pairing
assignments.body: Students pair two texts and write a response in which one text serves as the lens for reading the other. The response must articulate why this pairing — what one text reveals about the other that wouldn't be visible alone. 2 pages.

assignments.name: Cross-Genre Cluster
assignments.body: Give students a poem, an essay, and a news article on the same theme. They write a comparative response analyzing how each genre constrains and enables the author's argument. Forces genre-as-lens, not genre-as-label.

assignments.name: Integrated Paragraph Drill
assignments.body: Students write three body paragraphs comparing two texts. Constraint: every paragraph must reference both texts at least twice. Surfaces students who default to two separate analyses.

assignments.name: Disagreeing Authors
assignments.body: Students find two texts whose authors would disagree on a specific question. They write a response that lays out the disagreement precisely AND uses each author's argument to identify a weakness in the other's. Builds dialectical thinking, not just contrast.

assignments.name: One Claim, Two Texts
assignments.body: Students write a single thesis that requires both texts to support it. If the thesis can be defended using only one text, the thesis isn't comparative enough yet. Iterate until the comparison is structural.

mastery: Writes responses where both texts function load-bearing throughout — neither is decorative.
Treats genre as a lens that shapes what each text can do, not just a label.
Develops a single analytic claim that REQUIRES the comparison — not two parallel claims.
Uses one text to surface what's invisible in the other, rather than just listing overlaps.
Names disagreement and tension between texts as productive, not as a problem to resolve.
notes: STAAR tests this skill heavily through paired-passage items — "with which statement would both authors most likely agree" is the canonical stem. Strong stems above mirror the released-test bank.

Vertical jump from English II: English II asks for written analysis "including" comparison; English III sharpens the comparison demand to within-and-across genres, expecting students to handle a poem next to an essay, not just two short stories.
DOK 3 justification: strategic thinking — students must select the analytic frame, sustain it across two texts, and produce extended writing that integrates rather than parallels.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Use text evidence and original commentary to support an analytic response
expectation: Use text evidence and original commentary to support an analytic response.
overview: This is the engine room of analytic writing — students learning that a quote alone proves nothing and a claim alone is just opinion. The English III move is to weave them: short, precise evidence followed by commentary that does interpretive work the quote can't do by itself. Most student writing fails here at the seam — they drop a quote and assume the reader sees what they see. This TEK is the seam.
studentFriendly: You can write a paragraph that grounds your thinking in specific words from the text and adds your own analysis — your reader can see both the page and your mind on the page.
questionStems: Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the writer's claim that —
Which quotation from the selection BEST illustrates [theme/idea]?
Which detail from paragraph X provides the strongest support for the conclusion that —
Which line from the selection MOST clearly develops [argument/character trait]?
The writer's analytic point in paragraph X is BEST supported by which sentence from the source?
Which evidence from the selection MOST directly contradicts [claim]?
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the central idea that —
The author's use of [specific phrase] in paragraph X is BEST understood as evidence of —
Which quotation MOST clearly captures the shift in [tone/argument/perspective]?
Read this sentence from the selection. Which interpretation is BEST supported by the sentence?

misconceptions.title: The quote speaks for itself
misconceptions.body: Students drop a quote and move on, assuming the reader will see what they see. They won't. Commentary is the part where the writer says "and here's why this line matters." Watch for the body paragraph that ends with a quotation mark — that's the writer abandoning the reader. Teach the rule: never let a quote close a paragraph.

misconceptions.title: Commentary means restating the quote
misconceptions.body: Students paraphrase the quote and call it analysis. "The text says the sun was setting. This shows that it was sunset." That's translation, not commentary. Commentary should add interpretation the quote alone doesn't make explicit — what's implied, what's at stake, what the choice of THIS detail reveals. Push them past restatement.

misconceptions.title: Longer quotes are stronger
misconceptions.body: Students grab paragraph-length quotes because more text feels like more proof. The opposite is true — the strongest evidence is surgical. One precise phrase often does more work than a six-line block. Teach the embedded fragment: lift the load-bearing words, weave them into your sentence. Block quotes should be rare and earned.

misconceptions.title: Evidence comes after the claim, always
misconceptions.body: Students learn one structure (claim, evidence, commentary) and apply it everywhere, even when it deadens the writing. Strong analytic prose moves more flexibly — sometimes the evidence opens the paragraph, sometimes the analysis precedes the quote that earns it. Treat the formula as scaffolding, not a final structure.

misconceptions.title: Original commentary means stating opinions
misconceptions.body: Students hear "original" and start writing reactions. "I think this is sad" is original but not analytic. Original commentary is original READING — interpretation grounded in the text but not stated by the text. The originality lives in the connection between evidence and claim, not in personal feeling. Reframe it: original = what only YOU saw in the line, not what only YOU felt.

assignments.name: Quote Sandwich Drill
assignments.body: Students write five short paragraphs on a single text. Each paragraph: a setup sentence, an embedded quotation, and TWO sentences of commentary minimum. Forces them to extend analysis past the surface.

assignments.name: Surgical Evidence
assignments.body: Students write a response in which every quotation is six words or fewer. Constraint forces precision — they have to find the load-bearing phrase, not the impressive-looking sentence. Discuss which paragraphs got stronger from the constraint.

assignments.name: Commentary Layers
assignments.body: Students draft a paragraph with one quote and one sentence of commentary. Then they revise: add a SECOND layer of commentary that goes further than the first. Surfaces the difference between observation and analysis.

assignments.name: Evidence Rejection
assignments.body: Give students a claim and ten possible quotes from the text. They pick the BEST three and explain why the other seven were rejected. Builds the muscle of evidence selection — what makes evidence strong is what it BEATS, not just what it does.

assignments.name: Paragraph Surgery
assignments.body: Students take a body paragraph and circle the original commentary in one color and the borrowed-from-the-text in another. If commentary is less than half the paragraph, revise. Visual diagnostic for evidence-heavy, analysis-thin writing.

mastery: Embeds short, precise evidence into their own sentences rather than dropping block quotes.
Writes commentary that interprets rather than restates — always answering "so what?"
Selects evidence based on what it does for the argument, not what it sounds like.
Sustains commentary for at least two sentences after every quotation.
Distinguishes between original commentary (interpretive) and personal opinion (reactive).
notes: STAAR tests evidence-selection directly through "BEST supports" stems — these are the most common analytic items on every released test. The commentary half of the TEK is tested via short and extended constructed response, where rubrics weight integration of evidence with student reasoning.

Vertical jump from English II: English II requires evidence and commentary; English III raises the floor on commentary quality — restatement no longer scores. The shift is from "quote present" to "quote interpreted."
DOK 3 justification: strategic thinking — students must select among possible evidence, determine its analytic weight, and produce extended interpretive writing.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Paraphrase and summarize texts in ways that maintain meaning and logical order
expectation: Paraphrase and summarize texts in ways that maintain meaning and logical order.
overview: Paraphrasing and summarizing look like beginner moves but they're where comprehension gets exposed. A bad paraphrase changes meaning by accident; a bad summary loses the argument's spine. By English III, the bar is fidelity — students must reproduce the source's idea AND its logical shape using their own words, without distortion. This is also the integrity floor: weak paraphrasing is one drift step from plagiarism.
studentFriendly: You can rewrite what a text says in your own words — short or long — without changing what it means or messing up the order of its ideas.
questionStems: Which sentence BEST summarizes the main idea of paragraphs X–Y?
Which statement BEST paraphrases the meaning of paragraph X?
Which is the BEST summary of the selection?
Which sentence BEST captures the central argument of the selection?
Which statement BEST restates the conclusion drawn in paragraphs X–Y?
The author's main point in the selection can BEST be summarized as —
Which is the MOST objective summary of the selection?
Which order BEST reflects the sequence of ideas in the selection?
Which statement BEST reflects what the author argues in paragraph X?
Which sentence BEST captures the relationship between the ideas in paragraphs X and Y?

misconceptions.title: Paraphrasing means swapping words for synonyms
misconceptions.body: Students think paraphrasing is a thesaurus exercise — change every fifth word, leave the structure intact. That's "patchwriting" and it's plagiarism's cousin. True paraphrasing rebuilds the sentence from the meaning up — different syntax, different framing, same idea. Watch for student paraphrases that mirror the source's sentence shape; that's the tell.

misconceptions.title: Summary means short version
misconceptions.body: Students compress by deleting examples and keeping the topic sentences. That can produce a summary that's accurate but skeletal — missing the WHY. Strong summary preserves the ARGUMENT, not just the headline. Teach them to summarize the moves the author makes (claims, evidence, turns), not just the topics covered.

misconceptions.title: Logical order means same order
misconceptions.body: Students assume "maintain logical order" means follow the original's paragraph sequence. But sometimes the source's structure is rhetorical, not logical — buried lede, building to a punchline. The logical order is the order of REASONING. A faithful summary may compress, but it should never invert the cause-effect or claim-evidence relationships in the source.

misconceptions.title: A neutral summary is an empty summary
misconceptions.body: Students hear "objective summary" and strip out anything specific, leaving generic statements. "The author talks about issues with technology" — that summary fits a thousand articles. Objective doesn't mean vague; it means accurate without inserting the writer's opinion. Push for summaries with specific verbs and specific claims.

misconceptions.title: Paraphrasing is a low-stakes skill
misconceptions.body: Students treat paraphrasing as a copy-without-quotes maneuver — fast, mechanical, beneath them. But paraphrasing is the skill that exposes whether they understood. If they can't paraphrase the paragraph, they didn't read it. Reframe paraphrasing as a comprehension test, not a citation workaround.

assignments.name: Source vs. Paraphrase Compare
assignments.body: Students paraphrase a difficult paragraph, then compare their version to the original side-by-side. They must identify any places their paraphrase changes meaning, even slightly. Surfaces unintentional drift.

assignments.name: One-Sentence Summary, Three Versions
assignments.body: Students write three different one-sentence summaries of the same article. Each must be accurate. Discuss what each version emphasizes and what it loses. Builds awareness of summary as a choice.

assignments.name: Logical-Order Reconstruction
assignments.body: Cut a short article into its sentences and shuffle them. Students reorder using only logic, not memory. Surfaces how arguments are built sentence-to-sentence.

assignments.name: Patchwriting Detection
assignments.body: Show students a source paragraph and three "paraphrases" — one is patchwritten, one is fair, one is accurate but distorts meaning. They identify each and explain the distinction. Direct teaching for the integrity edge.

assignments.name: Argument-Spine Summary
assignments.body: Students summarize an essay in three sentences: claim, key evidence, conclusion. Strips away example-stacking and forces them to identify the load-bearing moves of the source.

mastery: Paraphrases by rebuilding the sentence rather than swapping synonyms.
Preserves the author's logic and emphasis, not just the topics covered.
Distinguishes objective summary from vague summary — accurate without opinion, but specific.
Recognizes when a paraphrase has drifted from the source's meaning and revises.
Treats paraphrasing as a comprehension check — if they can't do it, they reread.
notes: STAAR tests summary directly via "BEST summary of the selection" — usually four answer choices ranging from too narrow to too broad to too biased to correct. Stems above match the released-test pattern.
Paraphrase is tested less frequently as a discrete item but is foundational for the extended-response rubric (students who can't paraphrase end up either copying or distorting source material).

Vertical jump from English II: English II requires faithful paraphrasing and summary; English III adds "logical order" — meaning students are now responsible for preserving the structure of the argument, not just its content.
DOK 2 justification: skill of accurate restatement and selective compression — cognitive demand is conceptual but not strategic.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Interact with sources in meaningful ways such as notetaking, annotating, freewriting, or illustrating
expectation: Interact with sources in meaningful ways such as notetaking, annotating, freewriting, or illustrating.
overview: This is the back-stage skill — what students DO with a text while they're reading it, before any response is due. Strong readers leave a trail; weak readers leave a clean book. By English III, the bar isn't just that students annotate, but that the annotations function — that the marks help them think rather than perform compliance. The teacher move is to stop grading the highlighter and start grading the brain on the page.
studentFriendly: You can leave evidence of your reading on the page — notes, marks, sketches, freewrites — that helps you think while you read and remember what you thought when you come back to it.
