questionStems: Which detail from the selection would MOST likely deserve a reader's annotation as evidence of [theme]?
Which sentence from the selection would BEST be marked as a turning point in the author's argument?
Based on paragraph X, which question would a thoughtful reader MOST likely write in the margin?
Which detail from the selection would BEST be linked to a connection from outside the text?
Which line from the selection MOST likely deserves a question mark from a careful reader?
Which detail from paragraph X would a reader MOST likely highlight as evidence of —
The reader can MOST clearly track the author's argument by noting which sentences in paragraphs X–Y?
Which idea from the selection would BEST be summarized as a margin note?
Which sentence from the selection MOST clearly signals a shift the reader should mark?
A reader notetaking on this selection should MOST emphasize which of the following?

misconceptions.title: Annotation means highlighting
misconceptions.body: Students bought yellow highlighters and assume their job is now to pick the "important" parts. But pure highlighting is a passive move — by the end of the text the whole page is yellow and nothing stands out. Teach the rule: marks must DO something. A bracket asks a question, an arrow connects ideas, a margin note names a move. If the highlight isn't followed by a sentence, the highlight wasn't reading.

misconceptions.title: More notes = better notes
misconceptions.body: Students fill the margins with full-sentence summaries of every paragraph and call it active reading. That's transcription, not interaction. Strong notetaking is selective — short, sharp, only where the reading triggered something. Watch for students who annotate everything; they're hiding from the choice of what mattered.

misconceptions.title: Annotation is for the teacher
misconceptions.body: When students annotate to be graded, they perform — neat handwriting, full sentences, evenly distributed marks. Real annotation is messy because real reading is messy. Reframe annotation as a note to their FUTURE SELF: when they come back to write the response paper, will the annotations help them remember what they thought? If not, the annotations failed.

misconceptions.title: Freewriting is a warm-up exercise
misconceptions.body: Students think freewriting is the thing you do for five minutes before "real" writing starts. But freewriting in response to a text is a comprehension move — it surfaces what the reader noticed before they edit themselves. Teach them to freewrite WHILE reading, not just before writing. The goal is unfiltered thinking, not polished thinking.

misconceptions.title: Illustration is for younger students
misconceptions.body: Students dismiss sketching as elementary-school stuff. But a quick visual — a flowchart of an argument, a diagram of a character's relationships — can surface structural reading that prose notes can't. Watch for the student who claims they "don't draw"; they may be missing a tool that would unlock their analytic reading.

assignments.name: Annotation Tax
assignments.body: For one chapter, students must produce at least three margin notes of EACH type: question, connection, summary, disagreement. Forces variety and surfaces the type they default to (usually summary).

assignments.name: Future-Self Test
assignments.body: Students annotate a chapter, then put the book away for a week. They return and try to write a response using only their annotations as memory aid. The gap between what they remember and what they marked is the diagnostic.

assignments.name: Sketch the Argument
assignments.body: Students read an essay and produce a one-page visual representation — flowchart, concept map, diagram of any kind — that shows the structure of the argument. Compare different sketches; the disagreements reveal different readings.

assignments.name: Five-Minute Source Freewrite
assignments.body: After reading a difficult passage, students freewrite for five minutes without lifting the pen. No editing, no organization, just react. Surfaces what they noticed before they decided what they should have noticed.

assignments.name: Annotation Audit
assignments.body: Students swap annotated texts with a partner. Each writes a one-paragraph "review" of the other's annotation: what worked, what was performative, what would be useful for writing a response. Builds metacognition through peer mirroring.

mastery: Produces selective, purposeful annotations rather than uniform highlighting.
Uses multiple modes — verbal, visual, structural — based on what the text rewards.
Treats annotations as notes to a future self, not as performance for a grader.
Returns to annotations during writing and finds them useful, not decorative.
Adjusts notetaking strategy based on the genre and difficulty of the source.
notes: STAAR rarely tests notetaking and annotation as a discrete skill — these are process skills, not product skills, and multiple-choice can't watch a reader work. Stems above are the closest analog: questions about what a thoughtful reader WOULD mark or note.
This TEK is foundational scaffolding for 5C (evidence + commentary) and 11C (research). A student who can't annotate a source can't evidence-cite it later.

Vertical jump from English II: same skill name, but English III sources are denser and longer — annotations must be more selective to remain useful. Students who used heavy annotation in lower grades may need to be retrained for editorial restraint.
DOK 2 justification: skill of applying notetaking and annotation strategies — cognitive demand is procedural and selective, but not extended-reasoning.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Respond using acquired content and academic vocabulary as appropriate
expectation: Respond using acquired content and academic vocabulary as appropriate.
overview: Vocabulary acquisition is hollow if students hoard the words and never spend them. This TEK is the spending skill — students transferring the academic and content-specific terms they've learned into their own analytic responses, accurately and at the right moment. The "as appropriate" qualifier matters: jamming SAT words into a freewrite is not the goal. Precision is. Use the term when the term is the most precise tool, and not before.
studentFriendly: You can use the academic and subject-area words you've learned — diction, foreshadowing, syntax, motif, allusion — when they're the right tool for what you're saying, not just to sound smart.
questionStems: The author's use of the word [term] in paragraph X MOST nearly means —
Which word from paragraph X BEST describes [character/idea]?
Which literary term BEST applies to the author's technique in paragraph X?
The phrase [phrase] in paragraph X is an example of —
Which word would BEST replace [word] in paragraph X without changing the author's meaning?
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates the author's use of [literary device]?
The word [term] is used in paragraph X MOST likely to —
Which response uses the academic term [term] MOST accurately?
The author's repetition of [word/term] in paragraphs X–Y serves to —
Which statement BEST captures the rhetorical function of [device] in paragraph X?

misconceptions.title: Big words mean smart writing
misconceptions.body: Students think academic vocabulary is what you reach for when you want to sound impressive. The result is overdressed prose — three-syllable words doing one-syllable work. Reframe: academic words are TOOLS, not COSTUME. Use the term when it captures something a plainer word can't. Otherwise the plain word wins.

misconceptions.title: Academic vocabulary equals literary vocabulary
misconceptions.body: Students associate "academic vocabulary" with terms like motif, juxtaposition, hyperbole — the literary toolbox. But academic vocabulary also includes content-area terms (genre, register, agency, premise) and analytic verbs (suggest, imply, illustrate, complicate). Watch for students whose vocabulary is rich on devices and thin on the verbs that drive analytic sentences.

misconceptions.title: Knowing the term means using the term
misconceptions.body: Students can define "metonymy" on a vocabulary quiz but never use it in a response. Vocabulary acquisition isn't measured by recall; it's measured by spontaneous use. Treat the response paper as the assessment, not the quiz. If the words don't appear in the response, they weren't really acquired.

misconceptions.title: Use the term once and you're done
misconceptions.body: Students drop a vocabulary word into a paragraph like a flag — there, I used it — and don't extend the analysis the term opens up. Saying "the author uses synecdoche" without explaining what the synecdoche DOES is just labeling. Push them past identification. The term should be a doorway into deeper analysis, not a checkpoint.

misconceptions.title: Wrong-but-confident is no risk
misconceptions.body: Students misuse academic terms with full confidence — calling something a metaphor that's a simile, calling tone what's actually mood. Confident misuse is worse than not using the word at all because it tells the reader the writer doesn't really know the tool. Watch for vocabulary that's plausibly placed but slightly off; that's the most common kind of error and the hardest to spot.

assignments.name: Term in Service
assignments.body: Students respond to a text and must use five academic terms from a class list. Constraint: each use must be followed by a sentence explaining what the term reveals about the text. No term-as-flag allowed.

assignments.name: Replace and Sharpen
assignments.body: Students take a draft paragraph and find three places where a plain word could be replaced with a more precise academic term — only if the swap makes the analysis sharper. If it doesn't, leave it. Builds the habit of asking "is this the right word?" rather than "is this the bigger word?"

assignments.name: Vocabulary Stress Test
assignments.body: Quiz students by giving them sentences with academic vocabulary used in misuses and accurate uses. They identify which is which and explain why. Trains the diagnostic eye for confident misuse.

assignments.name: Analytic Verb Bank
assignments.body: Students keep a running list of analytic verbs (suggests, complicates, undermines, illustrates, foregrounds, destabilizes) and must vary their verb use across a response — no verb used twice. Surfaces how much of student writing leans on "shows" and "tells."

assignments.name: Term-Driven Body Paragraph
assignments.body: Students write a body paragraph where one academic term is the controlling concept of the paragraph — the term opens it, organizes the evidence, and closes it. Forces the term to be load-bearing rather than decorative.

mastery: Uses academic vocabulary when it sharpens the analysis, not to elevate tone.
Distinguishes between literary terms, content-area terms, and analytic verbs — and uses all three.
Extends every term-use into analysis rather than treating identification as the endpoint.
Recognizes and corrects confident misuses of academic vocabulary in their own drafts.
Adjusts vocabulary register based on audience — heavier in formal analysis, lighter in informal response.
notes: STAAR tests vocabulary directly through "MOST nearly mean" stems, which are some of the most common items on every released test. Stems above mirror the released-test bank for both context-driven word meaning and identification of literary/rhetorical devices.
This TEK overlaps with 2A/2B (vocabulary acquisition) but the response-skill framing is the key — 2A is about LEARNING words, 5F is about USING them in analytic writing. Don't conflate the two.

Vertical jump from English II: same skill name, but English III expectation includes "content vocabulary" — terms specific to literary analysis, rhetoric, and informational text — at higher density than English II.
DOK 2 justification: skill of applying acquired vocabulary in context — cognitive demand is procedural and selective.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Discuss and write about the explicit and implicit meanings of text
expectation: Discuss and write about the explicit and implicit meanings of text.
overview: Every text says something and suggests something. The English III move is for students to handle BOTH layers in the same response — what the text states on the surface and what it implies underneath, without collapsing one into the other. This is where strong analytic readers are made: students who can name the literal claim, name the unstated claim, and explain the relationship between the two without skipping the surface to score points on the depth.
studentFriendly: You can read a text on two levels at once — what it directly says AND what it quietly suggests — and write or talk about both without losing either.
questionStems: Based on paragraphs X–Y, the reader can MOST likely infer that —
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the inference that —
The author MOST likely includes [detail] in paragraph X to suggest that —
The author's description of [thing] in paragraph X implies that —
Which statement reflects an idea that is implied but NOT directly stated in the selection?
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates the difference between what is said and what is meant?
The reader can MOST clearly conclude from paragraph X that —
What does the author suggest about [topic] without stating it explicitly?
The phrase [phrase] in paragraph X MOST nearly means —
Which sentence from the selection states an idea that paragraph Y only implies?

misconceptions.title: Implicit means hidden
misconceptions.body: Students treat implicit meaning like a code to crack — secret messages buried in the text. But implicit meaning isn't hidden, it's UNSTATED. The text shows it without saying it, often through detail selection, juxtaposition, or what the author chose NOT to say. Reframe: implicit isn't sneakier than explicit, it's just not on the literal surface.

misconceptions.title: Surface reading is for younger students
misconceptions.body: Students in English III sometimes skip the explicit meaning entirely, racing to symbolic interpretation because they think that's where the points are. But strong analytic reading rests on accurate surface reading first. Watch for the student who reads "A Rose for Emily" and starts on the symbolism before they've gotten Emily's actual age right. Subtext without surface is just guessing.

misconceptions.title: Implicit meaning is opinion
misconceptions.body: Students think the implicit reading is wherever the reader's opinion goes. But implicit meaning is still constrained by the text — it has to be supportable from specific details, even though the text doesn't state it directly. The line "you can read between the lines but not OUTSIDE them" is a useful frame. Implicit reading isn't free; it's just less obvious.

misconceptions.title: The author always means something deeper
misconceptions.body: Students assume every text is metaphorical, every detail is symbolic. Sometimes a sandwich is a sandwich. Forcing implicit readings onto every detail produces the kind of analysis that gives English class a bad name. Teach them that part of strong reading is knowing when the explicit reading IS the analysis, and the implicit move is unnecessary.

misconceptions.title: Explicit-then-implicit is a two-paragraph structure
misconceptions.body: Students hear "discuss both meanings" and write one paragraph on the surface and one paragraph on the depth — disconnected. Strong response weaves the two. The surface reading gives the implicit reading something to push off of, and the implicit reading reframes the surface. Treat them as one analytic motion, not two stops on a tour.

assignments.name: Two-Layer Paragraph
assignments.body: Students respond to a passage with one paragraph that does both layers — names the explicit claim, names the implicit claim, and explains the relationship. Constraint: no transition word between the layers. Forces integration, not stacking.

assignments.name: What's NOT Said
assignments.body: Students read a passage and write a one-page response on the implicit meaning — but the response must be grounded in specific details. They must list five textual details supporting the implicit reading. Builds evidence-anchored subtext analysis.

assignments.name: Surface Defense
assignments.body: Students argue that the explicit meaning of a passage IS the meaning — that no implicit reading is needed. They must defend their reading against three possible implicit interpretations. Builds the muscle of restraint and surface fidelity.

assignments.name: Author's Selection
assignments.body: Students identify three details the author included AND three details the author plausibly could have included but didn't. They argue what the omissions imply. Surfaces implicit meaning through what's absent rather than what's present.

assignments.name: Surface vs. Depth Sort
assignments.body: Mixed list of statements about a text — some explicit, some implicit, some unsupported. Students sort each into one of three categories and explain. Direct training for the difference between "the text says," "the text implies," and "the reader is reaching."

mastery: Names both the explicit and implicit meaning of a passage with equal precision.
Anchors implicit readings in specific textual details, not personal interpretation alone.
Distinguishes when the explicit reading IS the analysis versus when the implicit move adds something.
Integrates surface and depth in a single analytic move rather than treating them as separate paragraphs.
Identifies what the author chose not to say and treats absence as evidence.
notes: STAAR tests this skill heavily — both halves. "BEST supports the inference" stems target implicit meaning; "MOST nearly means" and "the author MOST likely includes [X] to" stems target the move from surface to subtext. Stems above mirror the released-test bank.

Significant overlap with 4F (inference) — the distinction is that 4F is a comprehension skill (READING what's implied), 5G is a response skill (WRITING/TALKING about both layers). Treat them as the same cognitive move framed for different output.

Vertical jump from English II: same skill name, but English III texts more often layer subtext through what's unsaid (omissions, juxtapositions) rather than through symbolism alone. Students need to shift from symbol-hunting to absence-reading.
DOK 3 justification: strategic thinking — students must hold two readings simultaneously and explain the relationship between them.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Respond orally or in writing with appropriate register and effective vocabulary, tone, and voice
expectation: Respond orally or in writing with appropriate register and effective vocabulary, tone, and voice.
overview: This is the audience-awareness TEK — students producing responses whose surface qualities (formality, attitude, personality) match what the moment calls for. By English III, the bar is conscious code-switching: students should be able to write a literary analysis in formal register and a podcast review in conversational register, deliberately, without one bleeding into the other. The hard part is that students often have ONE voice and try to wear it everywhere.
studentFriendly: You can shift the way you sound on the page or out loud — formal for an essay, looser for a discussion, sharper for an argument — based on who you're talking to and what you're trying to do.
questionStems: The author's tone in paragraph X can BEST be described as —
Which word BEST describes the speaker's voice in paragraphs X–Y?
Which sentence from the selection BEST captures a shift in the author's tone?
The author's word choice in paragraph X creates a tone of —
Which response would BEST suit a formal academic audience?
The speaker's register shifts MOST clearly between paragraphs X and Y. Which sentence signals the shift?
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates the author's voice?
The author's tone in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Which response BEST balances analytic register with the writer's individual voice?
How does the author's choice of [formal/informal] vocabulary in paragraph X affect the reader?

misconceptions.title: Formal means stiff
misconceptions.body: Students hear "academic register" and write like a tax form — long sentences, no contractions, every verb passive. Formal register isn't lifeless; it's controlled. The strongest analytic writing reads like a thoughtful person talking, just dressed appropriately. Watch for the student whose formal voice has no detectable human behind it; that's the goal misread.

misconceptions.title: Voice equals personality
misconceptions.body: Students confuse "voice" with "being yourself." But voice is the specific texture of the writer on the page — pacing, sentence rhythm, characteristic moves, signature word choices. A writer can have voice in a formal analytic essay and a casual blog post; both will sound like THEM. Reframe: voice is what survives across registers. Personality is what the writer can't suppress; voice is what they can shape.

misconceptions.title: Tone is one thing per paragraph
misconceptions.body: Students label a paragraph's tone with a single word — "the tone is reflective" — and stop there. But strong texts shift tone WITHIN paragraphs, sometimes within sentences. Teach them to track tone like temperature: where does it warm, where does it cool, what triggers the shift? Single-label tone analysis is high-school-essay tone analysis. Push past it.

misconceptions.title: Casual means easy
misconceptions.body: When a prompt allows informal register (a podcast, a class discussion, a freewrite), students sometimes get sloppy — assuming "casual" gives them permission to be unstructured. Strong informal response is just as deliberately crafted as formal response. Casual register has its own discipline — quick pacing, surprising specificity, the implied confidence of someone who chose the casual mode for a reason.

misconceptions.title: Vocabulary controls register on its own
misconceptions.body: Students think register is about word choice — pick fancier words for academic, plainer words for casual, done. But register lives in syntax (sentence length and structure), pacing, and stance toward the reader as much as in vocabulary. A response can have SAT words and still read informally if the sentences are short and the stance is conversational. Push them to track register at multiple levels.

assignments.name: Same Idea, Three Registers
assignments.body: Students take a single analytic claim about a text and write three short responses: one for academic submission, one for a class discussion post, one for a podcast script. Compare. The exercise surfaces which features (vocabulary, syntax, pacing, stance) shift and which stay the same.

assignments.name: Tone Map
assignments.body: Students take a passage and mark each sentence with a tone-temperature note (warmer/cooler/sharper/softer). Discuss the patterns. Builds finer-grained tone awareness than single-paragraph labels.

assignments.name: Voice Imitation
assignments.body: Students read three paragraphs by three different writers (Joan Didion, Hanif Abdurraqib, Anthony Lane). They write a fourth paragraph on a new topic that imitates ONE of the voices. Identifies the surface markers of voice as craft choices, not personality.

assignments.name: Register Mismatch Diagnostic
assignments.body: Show students a piece of writing where register is wrong for the audience (a too-casual cover letter, a too-formal text message). They identify the specific words, syntax, or stance that creates the mismatch and revise. Builds diagnostic eye for register failure.

assignments.name: Voice on Demand
assignments.body: Students write the SAME response in two registers, two audiences, two days apart. Compare what they kept (their voice) and what they shifted (register and tone). Surfaces what's stylistic identity vs. what's situational dressing.

mastery: Code-switches register deliberately based on audience and purpose.
Maintains a consistent personal voice across multiple registers — formal and casual both sound like the writer.
Tracks tone at the sentence level, not just the paragraph level.
Adjusts vocabulary, syntax, pacing, AND stance when shifting register, not vocabulary alone.
Recognizes when a register choice is failing the audience and revises before submission.
notes: STAAR tests tone and word-choice register through "tone in paragraph X is BEST described as" and "the author's word choice creates a tone of" stems. Voice as a discrete element is rarely tested in multiple choice — it's a rubric criterion on extended constructed response.

This TEK is the public-facing layer of writing — the part the audience notices first. Even students with strong analytic reading can lose points if their register is wrong for the prompt. Make it a revision pass: read the draft once asking "is this the right voice for this audience?"

Vertical jump from English II: English II names the same elements; English III raises the demand on conscious deployment — students should now be able to NAME the register choice they're making, not just produce it intuitively.
DOK 3 justification: strategic thinking — students must select stylistic features deliberately and sustain them across an extended response while also handling content.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Reflect on and Adjust Responses When Valid Evidence Warrants
expectation: Reflect on and adjust responses when valid evidence warrants.
overview: This TEK is about intellectual honesty under pressure — the capacity to change your answer when the text forces you to, not just when it's comfortable. It lives at the intersection of reading comprehension and self-monitoring: students have to know what they said, evaluate new or missed evidence against it, and update with precision rather than capitulation.
studentFriendly: You can read a text, form a response, then genuinely reconsider and revise that response when better evidence shows up — not because someone pushed back, but because the text earns it.
questionStems: A student initially claimed that the narrator in paragraph 3 feels regret. Which evidence from paragraphs 5–7 MOST likely requires the student to adjust that claim?
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports a revision to the inference that the speaker supports the proposed policy?
After reading paragraphs 8–10, which statement MOST accurately updates the conclusion reached in paragraph 4?
A reader concludes that the author agrees with the critics. Which detail from the final paragraph MOST directly challenges that conclusion?
Which evidence from the selection would MOST likely cause a reader to reconsider the inference that the character acts out of loyalty rather than fear?
Based on paragraphs 6 and 9, which statement BEST reflects how a reader should revise an initial interpretation of the author's purpose?
A student argues that the central claim of the essay is stated in paragraph 2. Which sentence from the selection MOST directly requires that argument to be reconsidered?
Which pair of details from the selection BEST supports revising the conclusion that the resolution benefits both parties?
The author introduces new information in paragraph 11. How does this information MOST likely affect a reader's earlier interpretation of the narrator's motives?
Which statement MOST accurately reflects how evidence in paragraphs 7–9 complicates the inference drawn from paragraph 3?

misconceptions.title: Changing Your Mind Means You Were Wrong
misconceptions.body: Students treat revision as failure rather than as a sign of close reading. When a teacher asks them to revisit a claim, they hear criticism, not an invitation. Teach them explicitly: adjusting a response when evidence warrants it IS the skill — the original answer was a checkpoint, not a verdict. Writers revise; readers revise too.

misconceptions.title: Any Pushback Is Valid Evidence
misconceptions.body: Students capitulate to social pressure — a partner's confident objection, a teacher's raised eyebrow — and call it "adjusting based on evidence." The word "valid" in this TEK is load-bearing. Push them to distinguish between peer opinion, emotional appeal, and textual evidence that actually contradicts the claim. Not every challenge to your reading earns a revision.

misconceptions.title: Adjusting Means Abandoning the Original Position Entirely
misconceptions.body: Students who do revise often overcorrect — they wholesale discard their first reading and adopt whatever the new evidence implies, losing the nuance. Teach them that adjustment can be partial: "My original claim holds for paragraphs 1–4 but needs to be qualified once the author introduces the counter-evidence in paragraph 9." Precision, not collapse.

misconceptions.title: The Text Always Has One Right Answer to Land On
misconceptions.body: Students revise toward what they think the teacher wants, rather than toward what the evidence actually supports. Watch for students who "adjust" their responses in whichever direction earns approval rather than in the direction the textual evidence actually points. The target is fidelity to the text, not fidelity to consensus.

misconceptions.title: Reflection Happens After You're Done Reading
misconceptions.body: Students treat reflection as a post-reading debrief — something that happens when the test is over or the discussion ends. Effective readers monitor continuously: they flag contradictions as they appear, hold working interpretations loosely, and update in-process. Teach them to annotate moments of revision mid-reading, not just summarize their final position at the end.

assignments.name: Claim-Evidence-Revision Log
assignments.body: Students record an initial claim about a text after the first half, then return after completing the full reading to note whether the second half confirmed, complicated, or required revision of that claim. They must cite specific evidence for each stage and explain the logic of any adjustment.

assignments.name: The Valid Evidence Test
assignments.body: After a small-group discussion, students write a reflection identifying one moment they changed their interpretation. They must label whether the change was driven by textual evidence, peer pressure, or teacher cue — and evaluate whether the revision was warranted. Grade for honesty and precision equally.

assignments.name: Annotated Revision Draft
assignments.body: Students submit a first-draft written response with a highlighted sentence they revised after rereading, accompanied by a one-paragraph note explaining what evidence forced the change and what specifically changed in their claim. Forces them to name the mechanism of revision, not just produce the cleaner draft.

mastery: Identifies specific textual evidence — not peer opinion or teacher feedback — as the warrant for revising a reading.
Adjusts claims with precision: can articulate what changed, what stayed the same, and why the new evidence outweighs or qualifies the original.
Maintains intellectual independence — updates in response to the text, not in response to social or evaluative pressure.
Monitors interpretation continuously during reading rather than treating reflection as a single endpoint.
Recognizes when evidence complicates rather than fully overturns a claim and frames the revision accordingly.
notes: Vertical alignment: English II Std 5(I) asks students to "reflect on and adjust responses when valid evidence warrants" — the language is identical across the vertical band. The ramp is in the complexity of the texts and arguments students are responding to, not in the procedural skill itself. By English III, students are working with more layered argumentative and literary texts where the evidence that warrants revision is more ambiguous and requires more sophisticated evaluation.

DOK 3 justification: the skill requires strategic thinking — students must evaluate the relationship between prior claims and new evidence, judge whether evidence is valid, and decide how to calibrate the adjustment. That's reasoning beyond retrieval or skill application.

STAAR analog note: this TEK most commonly surfaces on STAAR through questions that present a student's initial interpretation and ask which textual evidence most directly requires revision — stems 1, 4, and 5 above model this pattern directly.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Defend or Challenge Authors' Claims Using Text Evidence
expectation: Defend or challenge the authors' claims using relevant text evidence.
overview: This TEK is where literary and informational analysis meets argument — students don't just identify what an author claims, they take a position on it and back it up with the text itself. The key word is "relevant": students must select evidence that actually bears on the claim, not evidence that's merely nearby.
studentFriendly: You can read what an author argues, decide whether they've made a solid case or a weak one, and use specific passages from the text to defend your position or dismantle theirs.
questionStems: Which piece of evidence from the selection BEST supports the author's claim in paragraph X?
A student argues that the author's claim in paragraph X is not fully supported. Which sentence from the selection MOST effectively challenges that argument?
Which detail from the selection would MOST likely weaken the author's central claim?
Based on the selection, which statement BEST defends the author's assertion that —
The author claims in paragraph X that [claim]. Which sentence from the selection provides the MOST relevant evidence to support this claim?
Which statement BEST describes a limitation of the evidence the author uses to support the claim in paragraphs X–Y?
A reader challenges the author's claim in paragraph X by arguing [counterargument]. Which detail from the selection MOST directly refutes this challenge?
Based on the selection, the author's argument in paragraph X is MOST weakened by —
Which quotation from the selection would BEST support a student's written defense of the author's position?
The author's claim in paragraph X is MOST likely supported by which of the following pieces of evidence from the selection?

misconceptions.title: "Defending" Means Agreeing With the Author
misconceptions.body: Students read "defend the author's claim" as "say the author is right because you like the conclusion." Teach them that defending a claim is a structural move — you marshal textual evidence to show the claim holds up — entirely separate from whether you personally buy it. Push them to defend claims they find debatable: that's where the skill actually lives.

misconceptions.title: Any Sentence Near the Claim Counts as Evidence
misconceptions.body: Students pull quotes that appear in the same paragraph as a claim and call it a day. Watch for this: proximity is not relevance. A relevant piece of evidence must directly bear on the specific claim being evaluated — it must answer "how does this prove or disprove that?" Teach them to articulate the logical connection every time, or the quote doesn't make the cut.

misconceptions.title: Challenging Means Finding Typos or Tone Problems
misconceptions.body: When students challenge an author's claim, they go after surface features — the author "sounds biased," the essay "feels one-sided," or they disagree with the author's word choice. Reframe this: a valid challenge requires a specific claim the author made plus a specific piece of evidence (from the text or from what's conspicuously absent) that shows the claim doesn't hold. Attitude is not argument.

misconceptions.title: Strong Opinions Substitute for Strong Evidence
misconceptions.body: Students with a firm view on the topic write passionate responses that circle back to their own beliefs rather than anchoring in the text. The evidence standard is not "what do I know about this topic" — it's "what does THIS text say, and how does that specific passage function in the argument?" Grade for textual grounding, not conviction.

misconceptions.title: Defending and Challenging Are Mutually Exclusive Moves
misconceptions.body: Students think they must pick a side and stick to it — either the author is right or wrong. In practice, the most sophisticated responses defend some claims and challenge others within the same text. Teach them that a nuanced response to a complex argument distinguishes between the claims that hold and the claims that don't, and supplies evidence for each position.

assignments.name: Claim Audit
assignments.body: Students read an argumentative or informational text and identify three to five distinct claims the author makes. For each claim, they write one sentence either defending or challenging it and cite the most relevant sentence from the text. The exercise forces claim-level precision before full-paragraph writing begins.

assignments.name: The Steelman and the Takedown
assignments.body: Students write two short responses to the same text — one that defends the author's central claim as strongly as possible using only textual evidence, and one that challenges it using the same constraint. Comparing both reveals how evidence selection shapes argument.

assignments.name: Evidence Court
assignments.body: In pairs or small groups, students take opposing positions on an author's claim and present their textual evidence to the class, which votes on whose evidence was more relevant and logically connected — not whose conclusion they agree with. Debrief focuses on why some evidence is stronger than others.

assignments.name: Annotated Challenge
assignments.body: Students read a text and annotate it in two colors: one for evidence that supports the author's claims and one for evidence that complicates or undermines them. They then write a response that synthesizes both sets of annotations into a nuanced evaluation.

mastery: Identifies a specific claim within a text and takes a clear, arguable position on whether the evidence in the text supports it.
Selects textual evidence that is logically connected to the claim under evaluation — not merely adjacent to it.
Articulates the connection between the evidence and the position explicitly, rather than assuming the quote speaks for itself.
Distinguishes between defending or challenging different claims within a single text, producing a response that is nuanced rather than uniformly for or against.
Sustains textual grounding throughout the response — the argument stays anchored in the text, not in outside knowledge or personal opinion alone.
notes: Vertical ramp: English II Std 5J asks students to defend or challenge authors' claims — English III carries the same expectation, but the surrounding Standards 5 context in III foregrounds synthesis across multiple texts and nuanced genre awareness, meaning the claim-and-evidence move must now operate on more complex source material and within a richer argumentative frame.
DOK 3 justification: students must apply reasoning to evaluate textual evidence and construct a strategic response — not recall or identify, but justify a position against textual criteria.

This TEK is a direct STAAR target in the literary and informational response sections; question stems above mirror the evidence-selection and claim-support patterns STAAR uses for on-demand argumentative writing prompts and multiple-choice passage sets.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Relationships Among Literary Elements
expectation: Analyze relationships among thematic development, characterization, point of view, significance of setting, and plot in a variety of literary texts.
overview: English III raises the literary analysis bar from identifying elements to mapping how they work on each other — how a setting shapes a character's choices, how a narrator's limited perspective bends the theme, how plot structure can confirm or undercut what the text seems to be saying. The verb is "relationships," not "identify" or "describe." That word is doing the real work of this standard.
studentFriendly: You can read a story or novel and explain how its setting, characters, narrator, and plot events are all pushing on the same theme at the same time — and show how each piece changes what the others mean.
questionStems: Which statement BEST describes how the setting in paragraphs X–Y contributes to the development of the story's central theme?
How does the narrator's point of view in paragraph X MOST likely affect the reader's understanding of the protagonist's motivations?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which statement BEST describes how the plot event in paragraph Z changes the characterization of [character]?
The shift in setting from paragraph X to paragraph Y MOST likely serves to —
Which detail from the passage BEST supports the inference that the narrator's perspective limits the reader's understanding of [character]?
How do the events in paragraphs X–Y MOST likely develop the story's theme about [concept]?
Read the following passage from paragraph X. The author's characterization of [character] in this passage MOST likely contributes to the theme by —
Which statement BEST describes the relationship between the significance of the setting and the choices made by [character] in paragraphs X–Y?
Based on the passage, with which statement about the story's central theme would the narrator MOST likely agree?
How does the plot structure across paragraphs X–Z MOST likely shape the reader's understanding of [character]'s development?

misconceptions.title: Theme Is the Topic, and That's Enough
misconceptions.body: Students write "the theme is friendship" and stop. They haven't identified a theme — they've named a subject. Teach them that a theme is a claim the text makes about friendship, loss, power, or whatever the topic is. Watch for thesis statements that could apply to any story ever written; those are topic labels dressed up as analysis. Push them to complete the sentence: "The text argues that friendship ___."

misconceptions.title: Elements Are Separate Buckets to Fill
misconceptions.body: Students treat characterization, setting, and theme like checklist items — describe the character here, describe the setting there, name the theme somewhere else. Real literary analysis in English III requires the student to show how these elements act on one another. Reframe every analysis paragraph around a relationship: what does this setting do to this character, and what does that interaction reveal about the theme?

misconceptions.title: Point of View Is Just First-Person vs. Third-Person
misconceptions.body: Students reduce point of view to a grammar observation and move on. The higher-order move is asking what the narrator knows, what the narrator can't know, and what the narrator chooses not to say — and then asking how those gaps shape meaning. Teach them that a first-person narrator is as much a literary construction as any other character, with biases and blind spots that do rhetorical work.

misconceptions.title: Setting Is Backdrop, Not Engine
misconceptions.body: Students mention the setting in their introduction and never return to it. For English III, setting should be doing visible work — generating conflict, limiting characters' options, embodying the theme through physical detail. When a student says "the story is set in the American South during the 1930s" and that's the last you hear of the setting, that's the misconception in action. Ask them: what could the characters NOT do because of where and when they were?

misconceptions.title: Plot Summary Substitutes for Thematic Tracking
misconceptions.body: When asked to analyze how plot develops theme, students retell the story. Retelling isn't analysis — it's inventory. Teach them to move from event to implication: not "then X happened" but "X happened, which reveals / complicates / confirms the text's claim that ___." The test of analysis is whether a reader who already knows the plot would learn something new from reading the student's paragraph.

assignments.name: Element Collision Diagram
assignments.body: Students select a pivotal scene and map it visually: four spokes radiating from the scene — characterization, setting, point of view, theme — with arrows showing which elements are driving which. Written annotations explain each arrow in 1–2 sentences.

assignments.name: Narrator Swap
assignments.body: Students rewrite a key scene from a different point of view (e.g., from the antagonist's perspective, or in third-person omniscient if the original is first-person). A one-page reflection follows: how did the change alter what the reader knows about character motivation and theme?

assignments.name: Setting Significance Essay
assignments.body: Students write a focused 2-page essay arguing that the setting in a chosen text is not decorative — it actively determines the shape of the plot and the possibilities open to characters. Minimum three pieces of textual evidence.

assignments.name: Cross-Text Thematic Comparison
assignments.body: Students read two literary texts with related themes but different settings, narrators, or plot structures. They write a comparative analysis of how each text's structural choices lead to different thematic conclusions despite similar subject matter.

assignments.name: Annotated Close Read
assignments.body: Students annotate a 1–2 page excerpt with color-coded margin notes tracking each literary element and drawing lines where the elements visibly interact. Discussion follows: which relationship in the text surprised you most, and what does it reveal?

mastery: States a specific, arguable theme (a claim, not a topic) and traces how at least two other literary elements jointly develop it across the text.
Explains how point of view creates gaps or distortions in characterization, not just identifies the grammatical person of the narrator.
Connects setting to plot causally — shows how setting enables or forecloses specific events, rather than describing it as context.
Moves from plot event to thematic implication in written analysis, without stopping at summary.
Produces multi-element analysis in which the literary elements are in conversation with each other, not catalogued separately.
notes: Vertical ramp: English II 6A asks students to analyze how elements "interact and build on one another." English III sharpens that into relationships specifically among the five named elements — theme, characterization, point of view, setting, and plot — making the analysis both more targeted and more demanding. English IV will extend this to stylistic and structural choices across literary periods.

DOK 3 justification: students must go beyond identification to explain causal and reciprocal relationships among multiple literary elements within and across texts. Strategic thinking across the whole text is required, not just retrieval from a single passage.

STAAR tests this TEK heavily in the literary passages of the Reading section; stems will focus on "BEST describes how" and "MOST likely contributes to" phrasing around characterization and theme. No special analog needed — this is core STAAR territory.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze How Characters' Behaviors and Motivations Create Moral Dilemmas
expectation: Analyze how characters' behaviors and underlying motivations contribute to moral dilemmas that influence the plot and theme.
overview: This TEK asks students to read character psychology as structural — not just "what kind of person is this?" but "how does this person's interior logic produce the impossible choices the plot turns on, and what does the text ultimately say about that?" The move is from character empathy to causal analysis.
studentFriendly: You can look at a character's wants, fears, and values and explain how those inner drives create the impossible situations in the story — situations that shape what happens and what the story is really about.
questionStems: Which detail from paragraph X BEST reveals the underlying motivation that leads the character to make the decision described in paragraph Y?
The moral dilemma the character faces in paragraph X is MOST likely a result of —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which statement BEST describes how the character's motivation contributes to the central conflict?
How does the character's behavior in paragraph X MOST likely influence the development of the plot?
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that the character's decision reflects a moral dilemma?
The theme of the selection is MOST likely developed through the character's —
Read paragraph X. The character's behavior in this paragraph suggests that his/her underlying motivation is to —
Which event in the plot is MOST directly caused by the character's conflicting motivations as described in paragraphs X–Y?
Based on the selection, with which statement about the character's moral dilemma would the author MOST likely agree?
How does the resolution of the moral dilemma in paragraph X contribute to the development of the theme?

misconceptions.title: Motivation Is Whatever the Character Says They Want
misconceptions.body: Students take characters at their word — if a character says they want justice, that's the motivation, full stop. Teach them to triangulate: what does the character say, what do they do, and what do they avoid? Stated goals and underlying motivations are often in direct conflict, and the gap between them is where the moral dilemma actually lives.

misconceptions.title: A Moral Dilemma Is Just a Tough Decision
misconceptions.body: Students flatten moral dilemmas into "hard choices" — picking between two bad options — without asking WHY both options are bad. The richer question is what values or loyalties are in collision. Push them: what would have to be true about the world for this choice to be easy? Whatever the character can't accept is the heart of the dilemma.

misconceptions.title: The Moral Dilemma Is Separate from the Plot
misconceptions.body: Students treat character psychology as backstory and plot as "what happens." They analyze motivation in one paragraph and plot in another and never connect them. The TEK requires the causal chain: motivation produces behavior, behavior creates or escalates the dilemma, the dilemma drives plot turns. If a student can't draw that chain, they haven't yet done the analysis.

misconceptions.title: Theme Is What the Author Believes About the Dilemma
misconceptions.body: Students want to assign the author a verdict: "Steinbeck thinks loyalty is more important than honesty." But theme is rarely that clean. Watch for students who collapse the complexity of the dilemma into a bumper sticker. The more honest move is showing how the text holds the tension without resolving it cleanly — and naming what that irresolution means.

misconceptions.title: Any Conflict Involving a Character Qualifies as a Moral Dilemma
misconceptions.body: Not every conflict is a moral dilemma. A character running from a bear isn't in a moral dilemma — they're in danger. A moral dilemma requires competing values or obligations that can't all be honored simultaneously. Watch for students who cite any character difficulty as a dilemma. Teach them the test: does the character's choice require sacrificing something that also has genuine moral weight?

assignments.name: Motivation Archaeology
assignments.body: Students select a pivotal scene and work backward: what behavior is visible on the surface, what goal drives that behavior, and what deeper fear or value drives the goal? Students produce a three-layer diagram and a paragraph explaining how the deepest layer generates the moral dilemma.

assignments.name: Dilemma Anatomy
assignments.body: Students identify the central moral dilemma and map both sides: what values, relationships, or obligations pull the character toward each option. Then they write a paragraph explaining which side the character chooses and what the text suggests about that choice through plot consequences.

assignments.name: Alternative Outcome Analysis
assignments.body: Students rewrite a single decision point — the character chooses differently — and trace how that change would alter at least two subsequent plot events and shift the thematic statement. Pairs share and discuss what the original ending reveals that their revision obscures.

assignments.name: Cross-Text Dilemma Comparison
assignments.body: Students read two texts where characters face structurally similar moral dilemmas but with different underlying motivations. They write a comparative paragraph arguing which character's motivation makes their dilemma more thematically resonant and why.

mastery: Distinguishes between a character's stated goal and their underlying motivation, using textual evidence for both.
Explains how the underlying motivation, not just external circumstances, generates the specific moral dilemma in the text.
Traces the causal chain from motivation to behavior to dilemma to plot consequence with precision, not generality.
Articulates the theme as the text's position on the moral tension — without flattening it into a simple lesson or falsely assigning the author a verdict.
Evaluates whether a given conflict in the text qualifies as a moral dilemma by identifying the competing values in collision.
notes: Vertical ramp from English II: English II 6(B) asks students to analyze how characters' motivations create internal and external conflict. English III sharpens the lens — the target is specifically the *moral* dimension of the dilemma (competing values, not just competing desires) and requires students to connect it explicitly to both plot causation and thematic development.

DOK 3 justification: students must construct a causal analysis across multiple textual elements — motivation, behavior, dilemma, plot, theme — requiring reasoning beyond retrieval or summarizing. DOK 4 is achievable if the task asks students to apply the framework across multiple texts or defend a thematic interpretation against an alternative.

This TEK pairs naturally with any canonical American literature text that turns on moral ambiguity: *The Great Gatsby*, *Death of a Salesman*, *The Crucible*, *Their Eyes Were Watching God*. The richest dilemmas are those where the text refuses to exonerate or condemn.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Evaluate How Literary Elements Shape the Author's Portrayal of Plot
expectation: Evaluate how different literary elements shape the author's portrayal of the plot.
overview: This TEK asks students to move beyond identifying literary elements and evaluate how those elements — characterization, point of view, tone, conflict, setting, irony, and others — actively construct the plot rather than simply accompany it. The question is not "what is the conflict?" but "what does this conflict make possible in the story, and how does it interact with everything else?"
studentFriendly: You can read a story and explain not just what happens but how the author's choices — whose perspective we're in, how the setting is framed, how characters are developed — are the actual machinery driving the plot forward.
questionStems: Which statement BEST explains how the setting in paragraphs X–Y contributes to the development of the central conflict?
The author's use of [literary element] in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Based on the events in paragraphs X–Y, the reader can conclude that the narrator's point of view —
How does the characterization of [character] in paragraphs X–Y shape the reader's understanding of the plot's resolution?
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the inference that the conflict in paragraph X is driven by —
The author's decision to withhold information about [event] until paragraph X MOST likely affects the plot by —
Read paragraphs X–Y. How does the author use [literary element] to develop the tension in this section of the text?
Which of the following BEST describes how the tone shifts between paragraph X and paragraph Y, and what effect does that shift have on the plot?
The author uses irony in paragraph X MOST likely to —
Based on the selection as a whole, which statement MOST accurately evaluates the role of [literary element] in shaping the plot?

misconceptions.title: Literary Elements Are Decoration, Not Architecture
misconceptions.body: Students treat setting, tone, and point of view as descriptive details layered on top of a plot that would exist without them. Push them to test this: if the story were told from a different point of view, would the same events be possible? Would they mean the same thing? The literary elements are not dressing — they are structural. Teach them to ask, "What could NOT happen if this element changed?"

misconceptions.title: "Evaluate" Means "List and Label"
misconceptions.body: Students read "evaluate" and produce an inventory: "The story uses foreshadowing, imagery, and irony." That is identification, not evaluation. Evaluation requires a judgment about HOW and HOW WELL the element does its job in shaping the plot. Watch for responses that describe elements without ever arguing for their effect or significance.

misconceptions.title: Conflict Is the Only Element That Drives Plot
misconceptions.body: Students understand that conflict moves plot forward and then stop there, ignoring how characterization creates the conditions for conflict, how setting constrains the options available to characters, or how irony reframes the meaning of resolution. Teach them that conflict is the engine but the other elements are the fuel, the road, and the steering — none of which is optional.

misconceptions.title: Resolution Tells You Everything About How the Plot Was Shaped
misconceptions.body: Students read backwards from the ending and assume the ending explains the whole plot. They miss how earlier literary choices — a narrator's blind spot, a setting detail introduced in chapter one, a foil character introduced quietly — were shaping reader expectation and narrative possibility long before the resolution arrived. Teach them to read for the architecture, not just the endpoint.

misconceptions.title: Every Literary Element Carries Equal Weight in Every Text
misconceptions.body: Students apply a checklist mentality: find setting, find tone, find point of view, discuss each for equal time. The skill is discriminating — in THIS text, which element is doing the heaviest lifting on the plot? In some texts it's point of view; in others it's setting; in others it's the pacing of revelation. Teach them that evaluation means ranking and defending, not covering the list.

assignments.name: Element Removal Test
assignments.body: Students identify the two or three literary elements doing the most work in a short story. For each, they write a paragraph arguing what would be lost from the plot — not just from the "feel" — if that element were changed or removed. The test is structural, not emotional.

assignments.name: Comparative Plot Autopsy
assignments.body: Students read two versions or adaptations of the same story (a short story and its film adaptation, or two different translations of a classic). They identify how differences in literary elements between versions produce different plots — even when the "events" are nominally the same.

assignments.name: Single-Element Deep Dive
assignments.body: Students select one literary element from a novel unit and track its behavior across the entire text, annotating how it shapes plot at the beginning, middle, and end. They present their findings as an argument: "This element is the controlling force of the plot because..."

assignments.name: Constructed Response Evaluation
assignments.body: Students respond to a prompt that gives them a claim about how a literary element shapes the plot and asks them to evaluate whether that claim is accurate, overstated, or incomplete — using evidence from the text. This mirrors the evaluation demand of the TEK without asking students to generate the claim from scratch.

mastery: Names specific literary elements and articulates — with textual evidence — how each shapes specific plot events, not just the overall "feel" of the story.
Evaluates elements comparatively, distinguishing which do more structural work in a given text rather than treating all elements as equally present.
Argues for causal relationships between literary elements and plot outcomes: "Because the narrator withholds X, the reader cannot anticipate Y, which makes Z's revelation structurally necessary."
Reads the ending as a product of earlier architectural choices, not as a self-explaining conclusion.
Produces written evaluation that includes a claim, evidence, and a judgment about significance — not just a list of observed features.
notes: Vertical ramp: English II Std 6C asks students to "analyze" how literary elements shape plot — English III adds the evaluative demand, requiring students to weigh and argue the relative significance of elements, not merely identify their presence and function. The move is from analysis to judgment.

DOK 4 justification: extended reasoning required to evaluate how multiple interacting elements construct a narrative, defend judgments about relative significance, and connect local textual choices to macro-level plot architecture. No single-step retrieval answer is possible.

STAAR analog: this TEK maps cleanly onto literary fiction passages on STAAR, where questions target how craft choices (point of view, characterization, setting, irony) produce specific plot effects. Stems in this entry reflect those established STAAR question types.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze How Setting Context Influences Plot, Characterization, and Theme
expectation: Analyze how the historical, social, and economic context of setting(s) influences the plot, characterization, and theme.
overview: This TEK is the engine behind close reading in any era-grounded text. Students who can't situate a work historically are reading characters in a vacuum — they explain Gatsby's obsession as a personality flaw instead of a symptom of the 1920s, or read Helga Crane's paralysis as weakness instead of a response to the crushing limits of the Harlem Renaissance's expectations for Black women. The skill here is causal: not "what is the setting" but "what does the setting make possible, impossible, or inevitable."
studentFriendly: You can read a novel or story and explain specifically how the time period, social rules, and economic conditions force characters into the choices they make and push the plot in directions it couldn't go any other way.
questionStems: Which statement BEST explains how the economic conditions described in paragraphs X–Y influence the protagonist's decision to —
The historical context established in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, what does the social structure of the setting suggest about the character's MOST likely motivation for —
How does the setting's historical context in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the development of the theme of —
Which detail from paragraph X BEST supports the inference that the character's options are limited by the social conditions of the time?
The author's description of [economic detail] in paragraph X MOST likely foreshadows —
What can the reader conclude about how the historical setting influences the conflict between [characters] in paragraphs X–Y?
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates how the social context of the setting shapes the protagonist's characterization?
How does the shift in setting described in paragraphs X–Y affect the development of the plot?
The author's use of [historical/social detail] in paragraph X MOST nearly suggests that the theme of [theme] develops because —

misconceptions.title: Setting Is Just the Backdrop
misconceptions.body: Students treat setting as a stage direction — they note that a story takes place during the Great Depression or under Jim Crow and then proceed to analyze character as if those conditions don't constrain every choice on the page. Teach them to flip the question: if you moved this exact character into a different era, what would change? The answer reveals how much structural work the setting is doing.

misconceptions.title: Historical Context Means Historical Summary
misconceptions.body: Students front-load their essays with a paragraph of Wikipedia-style history that never touches the text. They've absorbed the facts but haven't applied them causally. Push them to follow every historical claim with "and therefore, in the story..." — the connective tissue between outside context and textual evidence is the whole move, not a bonus step.

misconceptions.title: Social Context Only Applies to Characters Who Are Marginalized
misconceptions.body: Students apply social context selectively — they'll note how race or class constrains one character but read powerful characters as simply "acting on their own." Teach them that social structures shape everyone, including those they benefit. The wealthy character's confidence, the white character's assumptions, the male character's freedom of movement — all of those are social context working silently.

misconceptions.title: Economic Context Means the Characters Are Poor
misconceptions.body: Students equate "economic context" with poverty or financial hardship, ignoring the way economic systems — inheritance laws, labor markets, property rights, credit access — shape characters across the class spectrum. Watch for students who skip the economic lens on texts set in middle- or upper-class worlds. Teach them that wealth is an economic condition too, and the rules that maintain it drive plot just as hard as the rules that produce scarcity.

misconceptions.title: Theme Is Discovered in the Text, Not Shaped by History
misconceptions.body: Students treat theme as something buried inside the story, independent of when it was written or what the author was responding to. They locate "the theme of freedom" without asking what specific unfreedom the author was writing against. Push them to ask: what historical moment made THIS theme urgent? What does the author need readers to understand that they might not understand if they'd lived differently?

assignments.name: Context-to-Consequence Map
assignments.body: Students identify three specific historical, social, or economic conditions from the text's setting and trace each one to a concrete plot event, a character decision, or a thematic statement. All three chains must be supported with quoted textual evidence.

assignments.name: Counterfactual Analysis
assignments.body: Students write a one-page argument responding to the prompt: "If this story took place in [alternate era or context], what could NOT happen?" They must identify at least two plot events or character choices that are products of the original setting's constraints, and explain why removing those constraints changes the story's meaning.

assignments.name: Author's Historical Moment
assignments.body: Students research the year and place the text was written (not set), then write a short paragraph explaining what the author was responding to. They connect that historical moment to the text's theme using at least two pieces of textual evidence. The goal is to distinguish between the historical setting of the story and the historical moment of its composition.

assignments.name: Close Reading: One Paragraph, Three Lenses
assignments.body: Give students a single dense paragraph from the anchor text. They annotate it three times: once for historical context, once for social context, once for economic context. In a follow-up discussion or written reflection, they explain which lens illuminates the most and why.

mastery: Identifies specific historical, social, or economic conditions in a text and names how each one shapes a discrete plot event, character decision, or thematic development — not in general terms but with textual evidence.

Articulates causality: not "the setting is the 1930s" but "the scarcity produced by the Depression makes [character]'s refusal to accept help not stubbornness but pride shaped by economic humiliation."
Applies context analysis to powerful and marginalized characters equally, recognizing that social structures operate on everyone in the text.
Distinguishes between the setting's historical moment and the author's historical moment, and can explain how both shape the text's meaning.
Reads theme as historically contingent — able to name what specific conditions the theme is a response to, not just what abstract idea the story "explores."
notes: Vertical ramp: English II Std 6D asks students to "analyze how the setting influences the plot and characters." English III adds the explicit breakdown of setting into its historical, social, and economic dimensions — the move from "setting matters" to "here are the specific mechanisms by which setting constrains." English IV extends this to evaluating how multiple settings across a work interact.

DOK 3 justification: students must use contextual knowledge drawn from outside the text to explain causal relationships within it — that's application of conceptual understanding to produce analysis, not retrieval.

Anchor texts where this TEK lands well: *The Great Gatsby*, *Their Eyes Were Watching God*, *Death of a Salesman*, *The Grapes of Wrath*, *Passing*, *A Raisin in the Sun* — any text where the era is doing heavy structural work on what characters can want or reach.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Read and Analyze American Literature Across Literary Periods
expectation: Read and analyze American literature across literary periods.
overview: English III is the American literature course, and this standard is its spine. Students are not just reading American texts — they are reading them as a sequence, tracing how form, subject, and ideology shift from one period to the next. The job is to teach period as context, not decoration.
studentFriendly: You can read American texts from different eras — Puritan sermons, Transcendentalist essays, Harlem Renaissance poetry, postmodern fiction — and explain how when and where a piece was written shapes what it says and how it says it.
questionStems: Which statement BEST describes how the author's historical context influences the central argument of the passage?
Based on the passage, the author's perspective MOST likely reflects which period in American literary history?
Read this sentence from paragraph X. The author's word choice in this sentence MOST likely reflects the values of —
How does the structure of the passage contribute to its reflection of the literary period in which it was written?
Which detail from the passage BEST supports the inference that the author was responding to a specific historical or cultural moment?
The author's treatment of [theme] in this passage is MOST similar to which of the following ideas common to [literary period] writers?
Based on paragraphs X and Y, what shift in the author's perspective MOST likely reflects a change in the cultural or historical landscape?
Which sentence from the passage BEST illustrates the tension between individual identity and collective values that characterizes [literary period] writing?
Read the following passage from two different literary periods. In what way does the author of Passage 2 MOST likely revise or challenge the assumptions found in Passage 1?
The author's use of [literary device] in paragraph X MOST closely aligns with conventions associated with which American literary movement?

misconceptions.title: Literary Periods Are Just Date Ranges
misconceptions.body: Students treat periods as timelines to memorize — Romanticism ends here, Realism begins there — rather than as clusters of shared ideas, anxieties, and formal choices. Teach them that a period is an argument writers were having with each other, and that knowing the argument is the point. The dates are handles, not content.

misconceptions.title: "American" Means the Same Thing in Every Period
misconceptions.body: Students flatten "American" into a single idea and miss how radically contested that identity has been across centuries. Watch for students who read every text as though it's speaking to the same America. Push them to ask: whose America is this author describing, who is included in it, and who is arguing back?

misconceptions.title: Historical Context Is the Biography of the Author
misconceptions.body: Students reduce historical context to biographical trivia — Fitzgerald was rich, Thoreau went to the woods — rather than the social and ideological forces shaping what could be written and how. Reframe context as the set of pressures the text is either submitting to or resisting. The author's life is a footnote; the culture is the context.

misconceptions.title: Older Texts Are Less Complex
misconceptions.body: Students assume difficulty equals proximity — that contemporary texts are more sophisticated and earlier texts (especially pre-20th century) are simpler. The opposite trap is common too: students overcorrect and treat older texts as difficult because of archaic language, missing the ideas entirely. Teach them that complexity is not a function of date; a Bradford narrative and a Toni Morrison novel both demand close reading, just different keys.

misconceptions.title: Analyzing Period Means Paraphrasing the Era
misconceptions.body: Students summarize historical background instead of using it to read the text. They write paragraphs about what was happening in America instead of explaining how what was happening in America shows up in this sentence, this image, this structural choice. Period is a lens, not a topic. Watch for analysis that could be cut-and-pasted from a textbook without ever quoting the actual passage.

assignments.name: Period Lens Essay
assignments.body: Students select a passage from two different American literary periods on a shared theme (nature, freedom, identity, justice). They write a comparative essay arguing how each text reflects its period — using specific textual evidence, not background summary. The claim must be about the texts, not the history.

assignments.name: Then and Now Conversation
assignments.body: Students pair a canonical American text with a contemporary American text on the same subject. They annotate both for period markers — formal choices, assumptions, silences — and present a 3-minute argument about what changed and what didn't.

assignments.name: The Canon Problem
assignments.body: Students research which texts were added to the American literary canon in which decades and by whom. They then read one canonical and one historically excluded text from the same period and argue, in writing, what you miss when only one of them is taught.

assignments.name: Period Annotation Lab
assignments.body: Students receive an unmarked passage with the author's name and date removed. They annotate it for period markers — thematic content, formal choices, diction, assumptions — and argue in a paragraph which literary period it belongs to and why. Then they reveal the source and evaluate their reasoning.

mastery: Reads a passage and identifies specific formal and thematic markers that connect it to a literary period, rather than stating the period from memory.
Uses historical and cultural context to explain why a text makes specific choices — structural, tonal, thematic — not just what those choices are.
Compares texts across periods and can articulate what changed, what persisted, and what the shift itself reveals about American culture.
Avoids biographical reduction — grounds analysis in cultural and ideological context rather than in facts about the author's life.
Reads excluded or marginalized American texts alongside canonical ones and recognizes how periodization itself reflects choices about whose voices were centered.
notes: Vertical jump from English II: English II reads world literature broadly; English III narrows to American literature specifically and adds the through-line of literary period as an analytic frame. The skill moves from "read and analyze across genres" to "read and analyze across time within a national tradition" — a tighter historical lens on a more defined corpus.

Vertical jump to English IV: English IV returns to world literature, expecting students to apply the period-analysis habits built in English III to a global canon. Students who can read American periods can transfer that lens to British Romanticism, postcolonial African literature, etc.

DOK 3 justification: analyzing how period context shapes textual meaning requires students to apply a conceptual framework (literary period as cultural argument) to specific textual evidence — it goes beyond retrieval or skill application into strategic thinking across multiple texts and contexts.

STAAR note: this standard underlies most released English III STAAR passages — nearly every literary selection is anchored to an American period, and test items consistently ask students to connect author's purpose, word choice, and structure to historical or cultural context. The stems above mirror that pattern directly.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Relationships Among Characteristics of Poetry
expectation: Analyze relationships among characteristics of poetry, including stanzas, line breaks, speaker, and sound devices in poems across a variety of poetic forms.
overview: This TEK asks students to move past identifying poetic devices and into explaining how those devices work together — how a line break enacts the meaning it describes, how sound devices hold a stanza's argument together, how the speaker's distance from the subject shapes everything. The payoff is a reader who hears what a poem is doing structurally, not just thematically.
studentFriendly: You can read a poem across different forms — sonnet, elegy, free verse, ode — and explain how the way it's built (its stanzas, line breaks, sound choices, and speaker) creates its effect on the reader.
questionStems: Read lines X–Y. The line break after "[word]" MOST likely emphasizes —
How does the stanza structure in the poem contribute to the development of the speaker's perspective?
Which line from the poem BEST supports the conclusion that the speaker is —
The sound devices in stanza X are MOST likely intended to —
How does the shift in stanza X compared to stanza Y affect the poem's meaning?
The speaker's tone in lines X–Y is MOST nearly —
Which of the following BEST describes how the line breaks in lines X–Y contribute to the poem's rhythm?
The repetition of [sound/word] in stanzas X and Y MOST likely serves to —
Based on lines X–Y, what can the reader infer about the speaker's relationship to —
How does the poetic form of this poem support the development of its central idea?

misconceptions.title: Line Breaks Are Just Where the Poet Ran Out of Room
misconceptions.body: Students treat line breaks as formatting accidents rather than deliberate decisions. They read through enjambment without pausing, and they don't ask why the break lands where it does. Teach them to stop at every line ending and ask what the poet gained — or gave up — by cutting the line there. A break mid-sentence is a held breath; a period-ended line is a door closing.

misconceptions.title: Stanzas Are Just Paragraphs With a Different Name
misconceptions.body: Students map stanza logic onto paragraph logic and expect each one to carry a single topic sentence.
