Stanzas don't always work that way — they can reverse a premise, accelerate a feeling, or hold a silence. Watch for students who write stanza summaries instead of stanza analyses. Push them to name what changed between stanzas, not what each one "is about."

misconceptions.title: Sound Devices Are Decoration
misconceptions.body: When students encounter alliteration or assonance, they name it and move on, treating the sound like a garnish. The real move is asking what the sound does to pace, to tone, to the weight of a phrase. A cluster of hard consonants does something different than a run of open vowels. Teach them: if you can swap in different sounds without changing the effect, you haven't understood the sound device.

misconceptions.title: The Speaker Is Always the Poet
misconceptions.body: Students collapse speaker and author without a second thought, especially in lyric poetry. They write "the poet feels" when they should be writing "the speaker." More importantly, they miss what happens when the speaker is clearly not the poet — a persona, a historical voice, a fictional consciousness. Push them to ask: what does this poem gain by not being spoken in the poet's own name?

misconceptions.title: Poetic Form Is Just a Label
misconceptions.body: Students learn that a poem is a sonnet or a villanelle and stop there — the form becomes a category, not a set of constraints that create meaning. Teach them that form is a promise the poem makes and then either keeps or breaks. A sonnet that blows past the volta is doing something. A free verse poem that suddenly rhymes is doing something. The form only matters if students ask what it costs the poem to use it.

assignments.name: Form-to-Function Chart
assignments.body: Students read three poems in different forms (a fixed form, a free verse, and a hybrid). For each, they complete a two-column chart: left column names the structural characteristic (stanza count, line break pattern, dominant sound device, speaker stance); right column explains what that characteristic contributes to the poem's meaning. Final column: one sentence on how the characteristics work together.

assignments.name: The Same 14 Lines
assignments.body: Give students a sonnet and a 14-line free verse poem on a related subject. Students write a comparative paragraph focused exclusively on how structure — not content — shapes the reader's experience differently in each.

assignments.name: Break the Line Differently
assignments.body: Students take a stanza of a published free verse poem and re-lineate it three different ways. For each version, they write one sentence explaining what is gained or lost by that line break choice. Then they write a final paragraph explaining why the original version is (or isn't) the best choice.

assignments.name: Speaker Audit
assignments.body: Students annotate a poem for every clue about who the speaker is, what the speaker knows, and what the speaker can't see. They then write a paragraph arguing for a specific speaker characterization — using evidence from stanza structure, sound, and word choice — not just from what the poem says.

mastery: Explains how a specific line break or stanza boundary creates an effect — not just that it exists.
Connects sound devices (alliteration, assonance, consonance, repetition) to tone, pace, or emphasis rather than simply labeling them.
Maintains a consistent distinction between speaker and poet when analyzing lyric poems.
Identifies how the conventions of a specific poetic form shape meaning, not just how the poem fits the form.
Produces analysis that moves across multiple characteristics simultaneously — showing how stanza, line, sound, and speaker decisions work together rather than in isolation.
notes: Vertical ramp: English II Std 7B asks students to analyze the effects of poetic characteristics including stanzas, line breaks, speaker, and sound devices. English III adds the word "relationships" — students must now show how those features interact with each other across a variety of forms, not just describe each one in turn. English IV will push this into evaluation of how a poet's choices reflect or challenge genre conventions.

DOK 3 justification: the "relationships among characteristics" framing requires students to construct explanations that integrate multiple textual elements — not retrieve or identify them independently. This is strategic thinking, not recall.

This TEK sits in the Informational & Argumentative substrand per the TEKS framework, though its subject matter is literary. Question stems are written to reflect STAAR's literary analysis phrasing, which is how poetry is tested in practice.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze How Dramatic Elements Advance the Plot
expectation: Analyze how the relationships among dramatic elements advance the plot.
overview: Drama is a machine — every element (conflict, dialogue, stage directions, soliloquy, aside, scene breaks, dramatic irony) is a gear, and students need to trace how the gears mesh. The move here is not identifying what's present but explaining what changes when one element pushes against another.
studentFriendly: You can read a play and explain how specific dramatic tools — conflict, irony, dialogue, stage directions — don't just decorate the story but actually force the plot to move in the direction it does.
questionStems: In paragraphs X–Y, the conflict between [character A] and [character B] MOST likely advances the plot by —
Which line of dialogue from scene X BEST demonstrates how [character's] internal conflict contributes to the rising action?
The stage directions in paragraph X MOST likely serve to —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the dramatic irony created when [event] occurs MOST likely contributes to the plot by —
Which of the following BEST describes how the soliloquy in scene X develops the conflict?
The relationship between [character A]'s motivation in scene X and [character B]'s action in scene Y MOST likely suggests —
How does the aside delivered by [character] in paragraph X affect the audience's understanding of subsequent events?
Which sentence from scene X BEST supports the conclusion that the climax is driven by [specific dramatic element]?
The playwright's use of [dramatic element] in paragraphs X–Y MOST likely affects the pacing of the plot by —
Based on the events in scenes X through Y, what is the MOST likely reason the playwright resolves the conflict through [specific technique] rather than [alternative]?

misconceptions.title: Dramatic Elements Are a Checklist, Not a System
misconceptions.body: Students identify conflict, irony, or dialogue as isolated features and stop there — logging what they found rather than tracing what it caused. Push them further: once you name the element, the real work is explaining what the plot could NOT do without it. If they can't answer that, they've catalogued, not analyzed.

misconceptions.title: Conflict Is Only Between Characters
misconceptions.body: Students default to character-versus-character conflict and miss the structural work done by internal conflict, conflict with fate, or conflict with social order. Teach them that in drama, the most plot-driving conflicts are often the ones that can't be resolved by a single conversation — watch for students who reduce every play to "they argued and then X happened."

misconceptions.title: Stage Directions Are Just Instructions for Actors
misconceptions.body: Students treat stage directions as production notes irrelevant to literary analysis. Reframe this fast: stage directions are an author's tool for controlling what the audience knows and when. A character who exits in silence after a confrontation is doing plot work. A stage direction that draws attention to a prop is seeding something. Train them to read every stage direction as a deliberate narrative choice.

misconceptions.title: Dramatic Irony Only Matters to the Audience
misconceptions.body: Students acknowledge that the audience knows something a character doesn't, then fail to connect that gap to plot movement. The irony isn't decorative — it creates tension that makes later events land differently, changes how we read a character's decisions, and shapes our anticipation of the resolution. Watch for students who describe irony correctly but can't explain how it changes the stakes.

misconceptions.title: The Climax Is Where the Plot "Gets Good"
misconceptions.body: Students locate climax by emotional intensity — the loudest argument, the biggest revelation — rather than by structural function. Teach them that climax is the moment the central conflict reaches its point of no return, which may be quiet. Misidentifying the climax cascades into misreading the falling action and resolution. Press them: after this moment, what can no longer be undone?

assignments.name: Element Tracing Chart
assignments.body: Students select three dramatic elements from a play and map each one forward through the plot: where is the element introduced, where does it shift, and what does that shift cause? Completed in two columns — "What the element does" and "What the plot does next."

assignments.name: Removed Element Rewrite
assignments.body: Students choose one scene and rewrite a brief summary of what would happen to the next two scenes if one dramatic element (a piece of dramatic irony, a soliloquy, a key stage direction) were removed. The goal is proving the element's causal role by demonstrating its absence.

assignments.name: Comparative Structure Analysis
assignments.body: Students read two scenes from the same play — one early, one late — and analyze how the same dramatic element functions differently at each point in the plot. Written response, one paragraph per scene, one synthesis paragraph connecting both.

assignments.name: Dramatic Gear Map
assignments.body: Small groups diagram the act or scene as a system: dramatic elements are nodes, causal relationships are arrows. Each arrow must be labeled with the mechanism ("creates tension that forces X to act," "reveals information that redefines Y's motivation"). Groups present and defend one disputed arrow.

assignments.name: Director's Cut
assignments.body: Students write a brief director's note for a scene, arguing for a specific staging choice based on how a dramatic element should be emphasized to make its plot function legible to the audience. This pushes them to articulate the element's role precisely enough to give a real instruction.

mastery: Identifies a dramatic element and traces a specific causal chain from that element to a plot event — not just correlation but mechanism.
Analyzes relationships between at least two dramatic elements, explaining how their interaction produces a particular plot outcome.
Distinguishes between elements that decorate a scene and elements that are structurally necessary to the plot's movement.
Accurately identifies structural plot points (inciting incident, climax, resolution) based on function, not emotional intensity.
Supports analysis with specific textual evidence from dialogue, stage directions, and scene structure — not from plot summary alone.
notes: Vertical alignment: English II Std 7(C) targets explaining how dramatic elements function within a text. English III ramps to analyzing *relationships among* elements and their combined effect on plot advancement — the shift is from single-element function to systemic interaction. English IV pushes further into evaluating how a playwright's choices across the full work reflect theme and argument.

DOK 3 justification: the core move is strategic thinking — students must explain causal relationships between elements, not merely identify or describe them. Extended analysis of how two or more elements interact to produce plot movement is inherently DOK 3.
Drama falls under the Informational & Argumentative substrand in the TEKS strand architecture, which may feel counterintuitive; note this when teaching strand taxonomy.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Characteristics and Structural Elements of Informational Texts
expectation: Analyze characteristics and structural elements of informational texts such as clear thesis, strong supporting evidence, pertinent examples, commentary, summary, and conclusion.
overview: This TEK asks students to move past "what does this text say" into "how is this text built." The job is structural: find the thesis, trace how evidence is marshaled, assess whether examples are actually pertinent, and evaluate the logic that ties commentary and conclusion back to the opening claim.
studentFriendly: You can read an informational text — an editorial, a research report, an essay — and identify how the author structured it to make their argument land, from the thesis all the way through to the conclusion.
questionStems: Which sentence from the selection MOST clearly states the author's thesis?
How does the evidence in paragraph X BEST support the author's central claim?
The author includes the example in paragraph X MOST likely to —
Which of the following BEST describes the function of paragraph X in the overall structure of the selection?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, what is the MOST likely purpose of the author's commentary following the evidence?
Which sentence from the selection BEST represents the author's conclusion?
How does the organizational structure of paragraphs X–Y contribute to the development of the author's argument?
The author's use of summary in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Which piece of evidence from the selection MOST strongly supports the inference that the author believes —
Based on the selection, which statement BEST describes how the author transitions from supporting evidence to conclusion?

misconceptions.title: The Thesis Is Always the First Sentence
misconceptions.body: Students hunt for the thesis in the opening line and stop looking if it isn't there. Real informational texts — especially those written for general audiences — often defer the thesis, build context first, or embed it mid-introduction. Teach them to look for the claim that the rest of the text is organized to prove, not just the first declarative sentence they find.

misconceptions.title: Any Fact Counts as Strong Evidence
misconceptions.body: Students treat evidence as a checklist item: if there's a statistic or a quote, the evidence is strong. Push them to ask whether the evidence actually connects to the claim being made. A fact can be real and still be irrelevant. The question is not "does this evidence exist?" but "does this evidence do the work the thesis requires?"

misconceptions.title: Examples and Evidence Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students collapse these categories. An example illustrates — it makes an abstract claim concrete. Evidence proves — it provides verifiable support. A text can use both, and often the power is in understanding how they work together: the evidence establishes the claim, the example makes it stick. When students can't distinguish these moves, their analysis flattens everything to "the author gives examples to support the main idea."

misconceptions.title: Commentary Is Just Filler Between Quotes
misconceptions.body: Students skip over the author's explanatory prose to get to the next piece of evidence. This is backwards. The commentary is where the author's reasoning lives — it's where they explain WHY the evidence means what they claim. Teach them that the ratio of evidence to commentary is a window into the author's confidence and logic: lots of evidence, no explanation often signals hand-waving.

misconceptions.title: A Good Conclusion Just Repeats the Thesis
misconceptions.body: Students evaluate conclusions by whether they "remind you of the thesis," which means they reward restating and ignore synthesis. A strong conclusion does something — it widens scope, acknowledges implications, or reframes the stakes. Watch for students who grade conclusions on familiarity rather than rhetorical effect.

assignments.name: Anatomy of an Argument
assignments.body: Students annotate a one-page editorial or op-ed, labeling each sentence or paragraph with its structural function: thesis, evidence, example, commentary, summary, conclusion. In a brief write-up, they explain one structural choice the author made well and one they would revise.

assignments.name: Structural Comparison
assignments.body: Students read two informational texts on the same topic — one structured tightly, one loosely. They map both structures and write a paragraph explaining which organizational approach is more effective and why, using evidence from both texts.

assignments.name: Build-a-Breakdown
assignments.body: Students receive a scrambled informational text — paragraphs cut apart and shuffled. They reassemble it in logical order, then write a short explanation defending their sequencing by naming the structural elements they used as clues.

assignments.name: Commentary Spotlight
assignments.body: Students locate every instance of author commentary in a selected text (distinct from evidence and examples) and evaluate in writing whether the commentary adequately explains the evidence or whether it relies on the reader to do interpretive work the author should have done.

mastery: Accurately identifies the thesis — including when it is deferred, implied, or embedded mid-text — and distinguishes it from background information.
Differentiates among evidence, examples, and commentary by naming the function each performs in the text, not just pointing to their presence.
Evaluates whether supporting evidence is pertinent — that is, whether it actually connects to and advances the central claim.
Describes how structural choices (sequence, proportion, placement of summary) shape the reader's experience of the argument.
Assesses the conclusion's rhetorical function beyond whether it restates the thesis — identifying whether it synthesizes, extends, or undercuts the argument.
notes: Vertical ramp: English II's version of this standard asks students to analyze informational texts using the same structural features. English III sharpens the expectation toward evaluation — not just identifying a thesis but assessing whether it is clear, whether evidence is strong rather than merely present, whether examples are pertinent rather than merely included. The shift is from recognition to critique.

DOK 3 justification: the task requires students to apply analytical categories (thesis, evidence, example, commentary, summary, conclusion) to novel texts and reason about how structural choices produce rhetorical effects — this goes beyond retrieval and simple application.

For STAAR purposes: this TEK is tested directly through informational reading passages. Stems focus on structural function of specific paragraphs, strength of evidence, and purpose of authorial moves. The examples listed in the standard (thesis, evidence, examples, commentary, summary, conclusion) are the exact vocabulary STAAR stems will use.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze How Organizational Design Serves Author's Purpose
expectation: Analyze characteristics and structural elements of informational texts such as the relationship between organizational design and author's purpose.
overview: This TEK moves students past identification — they already know what compare-contrast and cause-effect look like. Now the question is why: why did this author make this structural choice at this moment, and what does it accomplish that a different structure could not? The relationship between architecture and intent is the whole game.
studentFriendly: You can read an informational text and explain not just how it's organized, but why that particular structure is the exact move the author needed to make to accomplish their purpose.
questionStems: The author's decision to organize paragraphs X–Y using a problem-solution structure MOST likely serves to —
Which organizational pattern BEST describes the structure of paragraphs X through Y?
How does the author's use of [chronological/compare-contrast/cause-effect] order in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the central argument?
Based on the structure of the selection, the author MOST likely organized the text to —
The shift in organizational design between paragraph X and paragraph Y MOST likely signals that the author intends to —
Which statement BEST explains why the author introduces [specific element] before presenting the counterargument in paragraph X?
The author MOST likely chose to end the selection with [structural element] in order to —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, what is the MOST likely reason the author uses parallel structure across multiple sections of the text?
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that the author's organizational choices reflect a persuasive rather than informational purpose?
The arrangement of information in paragraphs X–Z suggests that the author's PRIMARY purpose is to —

misconceptions.title: Structure Is Decoration, Not Argument
misconceptions.body: Students treat organizational patterns as containers that hold content rather than as choices that make meaning. They'll correctly label a text as compare-contrast and stop there, never asking what the comparison is designed to make the reader believe. Teach them: every structural decision is a rhetorical one. The author who compares A to B first, rather than B to A, has already started arguing.

misconceptions.title: One Text, One Structure
misconceptions.body: Students learn the canonical patterns — chronological, cause-effect, compare-contrast, problem-solution — and assume a text commits to one. Real informational writing layers and switches. A feature article might open with narrative, pivot to cause-effect, and close with problem-solution. Watch for students who mislabel mixed-structure texts by forcing them into a single category; the better question is which structure dominates and where the pivots happen.

misconceptions.title: Purpose Is Obvious from the Title or Introduction
misconceptions.body: When asked about author's purpose, students quote the introduction or assume the purpose is stated somewhere in the first paragraph. The relationship between organizational design and purpose requires them to look at the whole text — what gets foregrounded, what gets buried, what gets repeated, what's withheld until the end. Push them to treat the structure itself as evidence about purpose, not just the content.

misconceptions.title: Informational Means Neutral
misconceptions.body: Students conflate informational genre with objective intent. They assume that if a text looks like a report or article, the author has no angle. Teach them to notice whose data appears first, which organizational pattern frames the issue, and what the closing section asks the reader to accept. Purpose lives in structure even when tone is measured.

misconceptions.title: Naming the Pattern IS the Analysis
misconceptions.body: Students write "the author uses a problem-solution structure" as if the sentence is complete. It isn't. The analysis lives in what that structure accomplishes: the problem gets three paragraphs, the solution gets one — what does that asymmetry tell you about what the author wants the reader to feel by the end? Require them to connect the pattern to the effect on the reader every time.

assignments.name: Structure Audit
assignments.body: Students select two informational texts on the same topic — one journalistic, one academic or policy-based. They map the organizational structure of each in a visual outline, then write a paragraph explaining how the structural differences reveal different purposes, even when the subject is identical.

assignments.name: Reverse Engineering
assignments.body: Give students an informational text with the section headers removed. They identify the organizational logic, label the pattern, and write one paragraph explaining what the structure tells them about the author's purpose — before they see the original headers. Then reveal the headers and discuss alignment or discrepancy.

assignments.name: Structural Rewrite
assignments.body: Students take one informational passage organized around cause-effect and rewrite it in problem-solution order, then explain in writing what the reorganization does to the author's apparent purpose. The act of rewiring the structure makes the original choice visible.

assignments.name: Annotated Structure Map
assignments.body: Students produce a section-by-section map of a longer informational text (1,000+ words), labeling the organizational pattern of each section AND writing one sentence per section connecting the structure to the author's purpose. Final product is a margin-annotated printout plus a one-paragraph synthesis.

mastery: Identifies the dominant organizational pattern and explains where and why the structure shifts within a single text.
Connects specific structural choices — what comes first, what is repeated, what is withheld — to the author's demonstrable purpose.
Distinguishes between organizational patterns that inform and those that persuade, even when the surface genre appears neutral.
Produces textual evidence from structure itself (sequence, proportion, placement) rather than from content alone.
Evaluates whether the organizational design is effective for the stated purpose, and can articulate what a different structure would have gained or lost.
notes: Vertical ramp: English II (7D.ii) asks students to analyze organizational patterns in informational texts. English III sharpens the lens from pattern recognition to purpose relationship — the structural choice is now evidence of rhetorical intent, not just a textual feature. English IV extends this into cross-text analysis and evaluation of how structure shapes argument across a genre or discipline.
DOK 3 justification: requires students to use organizational pattern as evidence for a claim about author's purpose — multi-step reasoning across the full text, not retrieval or recall.

STAAR note: this sub-expectation is regularly tested through author's-craft questions that ask why an author organized a section a particular way or why a shift occurs between paragraphs. The "MOST likely" and "contributes to" stems in the Question Stems section directly mirror released-item phrasing for this skill cluster.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Characteristics and Structural Elements of Argumentative Texts
expectation: Analyze characteristics and structural elements of argumentative texts such as clear arguable thesis, appeals, structure of the argument, convincing conclusion, and call to action.
overview: This TEK asks students to read an argumentative text the way a builder reads blueprints — not just "did it convince me?" but "what is this thing made of, and does the construction hold?" By English III, students should be able to name every load-bearing element of an argument and explain how it functions, not merely whether they agree with the conclusion.
studentFriendly: You can read a persuasive essay, speech, or editorial and identify exactly how it is built — where the thesis sits, what kind of appeals it uses, how the argument is sequenced, and whether the conclusion actually closes the case or just stops talking.
questionStems: Which sentence from the selection MOST clearly states the author's central arguable claim?
The author's use of statistics in paragraph X is BEST described as an appeal to —
How does the structure of paragraphs X–Y contribute to the development of the author's argument?
Which sentence from the selection BEST functions as a call to action?
The author MOST likely organizes the argument in paragraphs X–Y by —
Based on paragraph X, the author's appeal to [shared values / emotion / authority] is intended to —
Which detail from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that the author's thesis is arguable rather than factual?
How does the concluding paragraph contribute to the effectiveness of the author's argument?
The author includes [counterargument / concession] in paragraph X MOST likely in order to —
Which statement BEST describes how the author transitions from presenting evidence to drawing a conclusion in paragraphs X–Y?

misconceptions.title: A Strong Thesis Is Just a Topic Statement
misconceptions.body: Students routinely identify the topic sentence of an introduction as the thesis. Push them past this: a thesis must be *arguable* — someone could reasonably disagree with it. Watch for students who call "Social media is popular" a thesis. Teach them to apply the disagreement test: if a reasonable, informed reader cannot push back, it is a fact, not a claim. English III students should be able to distinguish within a single reading.

misconceptions.title: Appeals Are Decoration, Not Architecture
misconceptions.body: Students treat ethos, pathos, and logos as labels to stick on passages like Post-it notes, rather than as structural moves the author is making to do specific work. The richer question is not "what appeal is this?" but "what is this appeal accomplishing at this point in the argument — and would the argument collapse without it?" Teach them to ask what the appeal is doing, not just what it is called.

misconceptions.title: The Conclusion Is Just a Summary
misconceptions.body: Students consistently read the final paragraph as a repeat of everything already said. In strong argumentative writing, the conclusion does something the body cannot — it widens the stakes, delivers the emotional payoff, or issues a specific call to action. Watch for students who mark a conclusion "effective" simply because it restates the thesis; push them to identify the specific move the author makes to close, not just echo.

misconceptions.title: "Structure of the Argument" Means Order of Paragraphs
misconceptions.body: When asked about argument structure, students list the number of body paragraphs and call it done. Structure analysis means tracking the logical sequence of moves: claim, warrant, evidence, concession, rebuttal, conclusion — and naming how each move serves the next. Teach them to map the argument as a chain of reasoning, not a table of contents.

misconceptions.title: A Call to Action Is Always Explicit
misconceptions.body: Students look for the sentence that says "you should do X" and, when it isn't there, conclude there is no call to action. Many calls to action in sophisticated argumentative texts are implied — the author builds urgency and positions the reader to feel the obvious next step without stating it directly. Teach them to ask: after reading this, what does the author want me to do or believe differently, even if those words never appear?

assignments.name: Blueprint Markup
assignments.body: Students annotate a 400–600 word argumentative editorial by labeling each structural element (thesis, each type of appeal, concession/rebuttal, conclusion move, call to action) directly on the text. They then write one paragraph explaining whether the structure is effective — not whether they agree with the argument.

assignments.name: Thesis or Fact?
assignments.body: Give students 10 sentences from real op-eds and editorials. They classify each as arguable thesis, factual claim, or topic statement, and justify each classification in writing. Discuss where the class disagrees.

assignments.name: The Autopsy
assignments.body: Students choose an argument they find unconvincing and diagnose exactly where the structural breakdown occurs — weak thesis, misused appeal, abrupt conclusion, no call to action. They must name the specific element and explain how it fails, not just say "I didn't agree."

assignments.name: Side-by-Side Structure Comparison
assignments.body: Students read two arguments on the same topic with different structural approaches (e.g., one that opens with a narrative appeal, one that opens with a data-driven claim). They map each argument's structure visually and write a paragraph analyzing which structure is better suited to its audience and purpose.

mastery: Identifies the arguable thesis in an unfamiliar text and can explain why it qualifies as a claim rather than a fact.
Names the specific appeal used in a passage and explains what rhetorical work it is performing at that point in the argument.
Traces the logical sequence of the argument — claim through conclusion — and evaluates whether each structural move supports the next.
Distinguishes between a conclusion that closes the argument and one that merely ends it, with textual evidence.
Identifies a call to action whether it is stated explicitly or implied through accumulating urgency.
notes: Vertical ramp: English II introduces argumentation structure at the recognition level — students identify elements present in a text. English III shifts to *analysis* — students explain how each element functions and whether the structure holds. English IV extends to evaluation and production of argument at greater rhetorical sophistication.
DOK 3 justification: students must apply knowledge of argumentation structure to unfamiliar texts and explain the function of each element in context — not merely identify it.

This entry covers the structural/characteristic inventory sub-skill (7)(E)(i); the companion entry 110.38(7)(E)(ii) covers rhetorical devices and logical fallacies specifically. Keep stems and misconceptions distinct across the two entries.

No direct STAAR passage type is labeled "argumentative," but released STAAR literary nonfiction and expository passages regularly require students to analyze claim structure, appeal type, and conclusion effectiveness — stems above reflect that testing context.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Evidence and Counterargument Treatment in Argumentative Texts
expectation: Analyze characteristics and structural elements of argumentative texts, including various types of evidence and treatment of counterarguments, including concessions and rebuttals.
overview: This is where argument analysis gets structural. Students aren't just identifying a claim — they're mapping how an author builds the case: what kinds of evidence appear, how much work each type actually does, and whether the author handles opposition honestly or dodges it. The concession/rebuttal move is the most revealing diagnostic of argumentative sophistication, and it's also where student analysis most often stalls out.
studentFriendly: You can read an argument and explain not just what evidence the author uses but how they handle the other side — whether they acknowledge objections fairly, grant what's true in them, and then push back with precision.
questionStems: Which sentence from paragraph X BEST supports the conclusion that the author anticipates a counterargument?
The author's use of [phrase/evidence] in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Which type of evidence does the author rely on MOST heavily to support the central claim?
In paragraphs X–Y, the author concedes that [position] in order to —
The rebuttal in paragraph X is MOST effective because it —
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates the author's treatment of an opposing viewpoint?
Based on paragraph X, the author would MOST likely agree that —
The author's decision to include [statistic/anecdote/expert testimony] in paragraph X contributes to the argument by —
Which statement BEST describes how the author responds to the counterargument introduced in paragraph X?
The evidence in paragraphs X–Y is MOST nearly characterized as —

misconceptions.title: A Concession Is a Sign of Weakness
misconceptions.body: Students read concession as the author "admitting they're wrong" and conclude that any argument with a concession is less convincing. Flip this: a well-placed concession is the mark of an author confident enough to acknowledge complexity without losing the thread. Teach them to ask not just "does the author concede?" but "does the concession strengthen or expose the argument?" — those are different questions.

misconceptions.title: All Evidence Is Equivalent
misconceptions.body: Students treat a personal anecdote and a peer-reviewed study as interchangeable "evidence" because both support the claim. Push them to distinguish: what does each type of evidence actually prove, and what can't it prove? An anecdote proves something happened; it doesn't prove it's representative. Watch for students who call evidence "strong" based entirely on whether they found it persuasive rather than whether it's logically sufficient.

misconceptions.title: Rebuttal Means Saying the Other Side Is Wrong
misconceptions.body: Students think a rebuttal is simply "the author says the counterargument is bad." Real rebuttal is surgical — it acknowledges what the opposition has right, then identifies the specific flaw in their reasoning, the missing evidence, or the limits of their conclusion. Teach them: a rebuttal that doesn't engage the strongest version of the opposition's claim is a dodge, not a refutation.

misconceptions.title: The Counterargument Is Always in One Paragraph
misconceptions.body: Students expect the author to signal "here is the counterargument" in an obvious block of text, then move on. Argumentation doesn't always work that way — opposition can be threaded through concessive clauses, hedged language, or strategic ordering. Watch for students who miss embedded counterarguments because they're looking for a clearly labeled section rather than reading for logical structure.

misconceptions.title: More Evidence Means a Stronger Argument
misconceptions.body: Students count evidence items and assume the argument with more citations or examples wins. The deeper analysis is about variety, relevance, and integration — does the author use multiple types of evidence? Does each piece connect directly to the claim it's supposed to support? Teach them that five weak examples don't add up to one strong one, and that evidence variety signals an author who understands the burden of proof has multiple dimensions.

assignments.name: Evidence Taxonomy Chart
assignments.body: Students read a two-page argumentative text and categorize every piece of evidence by type (anecdote, statistic, expert testimony, analogy, historical precedent). Then they write a paragraph evaluating whether the mix is appropriate for the claim and audience.

assignments.name: Concession-Rebuttal Dissection
assignments.body: Students isolate the passage where the author handles an opposing view. They annotate: what exactly is conceded, what is retained, and how the rebuttal targets the opposition's logic rather than its conclusion. Final product is a one-paragraph written analysis with line-level evidence.

assignments.name: Steel-Man the Opposition
assignments.body: Before analyzing how an author handles counterarguments, students write the strongest possible version of the counterargument themselves. Then they return to the text and evaluate whether the author engaged that strong version or a weaker substitute.

assignments.name: Argument Map
assignments.body: Students produce a visual diagram of the argument's structure: central claim, supporting evidence by type, counterargument, concession, rebuttal. The map makes architectural choices visible that prose reading often obscures.

assignments.name: Comparative Treatment
assignments.body: Two texts argue opposing positions on the same issue. Students analyze how each author handles the other side — which is fairer, which is more evasive, and how the quality of counterargument treatment affects overall persuasiveness.

mastery: Identifies specific types of evidence (anecdote, statistics, expert testimony, analogy) and explains what each type can and cannot logically establish.
Distinguishes between a concession and a full agreement — can articulate what the author grants without surrendering the central claim.
Evaluates whether a rebuttal actually addresses the strongest version of the counterargument or deflects to a weaker one.
Locates counterargument treatment that is embedded or threaded, not just block-paragraphed.
Produces written analysis that moves from identification to evaluation — not just "the author uses statistics" but "these statistics establish X, which directly addresses Y objection."
notes: Vertical ramp from English II: English II 7E(ii) establishes the same targets (evidence types, counterargument treatment, concessions, rebuttals). English III sharpens the expectation from identification toward analytical evaluation — students must not only locate these moves but assess their logical adequacy and rhetorical effect.

DOK 3 justification: students must reason across multiple structural elements simultaneously, evaluating relationships between claim, evidence type, and counterargument handling — this is strategic thinking, not retrieval.

The concession/rebuttal distinction is the highest-leverage sub-skill here; it's where STAAR argumentation items most frequently require students to distinguish between an author acknowledging the opposition and an author actually refuting it.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Argumentative Texts: Identifiable Audience
expectation: Analyze characteristics and structural elements of argumentative texts such as an identifiable audience or reader.
overview: Every argumentative text is written FOR someone — and that targeting shapes everything: diction, evidence choices, assumed knowledge, emotional appeals. English III students move past "who is the audience?" and into "how does the audience shape the argument's construction?" The real skill is reverse-engineering the text to surface the imagined reader.
studentFriendly: You can read an argumentative text and figure out who the writer is trying to reach — and explain how that intended audience drives the specific choices the writer made throughout the piece.
questionStems: Based on the evidence in paragraphs X–Y, the author's intended audience is MOST likely —
Which detail from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that the author wrote this text for a specific audience?
The author's use of [term/phrase] in paragraph X suggests the intended reader is MOST likely —
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates how the author adapts the argument for a particular audience?
The author MOST likely includes [example/statistic] in paragraph X to appeal to readers who —
Based on paragraph X, the author assumes the audience already knows —
Which of the following BEST describes how the author's awareness of audience affects the structure of the argument?
The author's decision to address [counterargument/concern] in paragraph X suggests the intended audience MOST likely —
How does the author's word choice in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the appeal for a specific type of reader?
With which statement about the author's intended audience would a careful reader of this selection MOST likely agree?

misconceptions.title: "The Audience" Is Just a Demographic Label
misconceptions.body: Students identify the audience as "adults" or "teenagers" and consider the analysis done. Push them to name the audience's VALUES, assumptions, prior knowledge, and anxieties — the characteristics that actually drive the argument's choices. A label is the starting point, not the conclusion. Teach them: if you can name the audience but can't explain one specific thing the author did because of that audience, you haven't analyzed anything yet.

misconceptions.title: Audience Is Stated Somewhere in the Text
misconceptions.body: Students hunt for a sentence that announces the audience rather than inferring it from the text's evidence choices, vocabulary, tone, and structural decisions. Real audience identification is an inference built from accumulation — teach them to read for what the author assumes the reader already believes, already values, already knows, and who the author imagines pushing back.

misconceptions.title: If the Audience Isn't Mentioned, It Doesn't Matter
misconceptions.body: When a text doesn't name its audience explicitly, students conclude audience analysis doesn't apply. This is exactly backwards — the most rhetorically sophisticated texts embed their audience assumptions invisibly. Watch for students who skip audience questions on unlabeled op-eds and essays. The absence of explicit address is itself a rhetorical choice.

misconceptions.title: Audience and Purpose Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students collapse audience into purpose ("the author wrote this to persuade"), missing that audience and purpose interact. The same purpose — convince readers to support a policy — produces radically different texts depending on whether the audience is skeptical, sympathetic, or uninformed. Teach them to hold the two questions separately: Who is this for? What does the author want from them? Then ask how the first answer shapes every decision made in service of the second.

misconceptions.title: Any Reader Who Reads It Is the Intended Audience
misconceptions.body: Students reason that since they read the text, they are the audience — and their own reaction becomes the evidence for audience analysis. Redirect: intended audience is constructed before the text is published, not assembled from whoever happens to pick it up. Teach them to find evidence of design — the assumptions baked in before any real reader showed up.

assignments.name: Audience Autopsy
assignments.body: Students read two versions of an argument on the same topic written for different audiences (e.g., a scientific journal abstract vs. a public-facing op-ed on the same study). For each, they identify the intended audience and write one paragraph explaining three specific textual choices that reveal it. Comparison sharpens the inference.

assignments.name: Reverse-Engineer the Reader
assignments.body: Students receive an argumentative text with all explicit audience markers removed (publication name, byline, dateline). Working only from diction, evidence selection, assumed knowledge, and tone, they construct a profile of the intended reader — then compare their profiles with the actual source context. Discussion focuses on which textual signals were most reliable.

assignments.name: Audience Drift Analysis
assignments.body: Students track a recurring phrase or piece of evidence across three different argumentative texts on the same issue, each aimed at a different audience. They write a short analysis explaining how the author's framing of that same evidence shifts depending on who's reading — and what that reveals about audience's role in shaping argument.

mastery: Infers the intended audience from textual evidence — diction, evidence choices, assumed knowledge, tone — rather than from explicit labels or demographic guesses.
Connects specific structural and stylistic decisions in the text to the characteristics, values, or assumptions of the inferred audience.
Distinguishes between the intended audience and incidental readers, and explains how the text is constructed with the former in mind.
Holds audience and purpose as related but distinct analytical lenses — identifies each separately, then explains their interaction within the argument.
Supports audience claims with multiple pieces of evidence, not a single moment of diction or one labeled example.
notes: Vertical ramp: English II 7E(iii) introduces audience identification as a structural element of argumentative texts. English III raises the expectation to analysis — students must explain how the identified audience shapes construction, not merely name who the audience is. English IV will push toward evaluation of how effectively the argument reaches its audience.

DOK 3 justification: audience identification requires inference across multiple textual features; explaining how audience shapes structural and stylistic choices requires strategic thinking beyond retrieval or skill application.

This sub-element pairs tightly with 7E(i) (logical structures) and 8D (language and perception) — in strong arguments, audience awareness and argumentative structure are inseparable. Treat them as co-curricular rather than sequential.

STAAR analog note: STAAR tests audience inference through author's-craft and purpose questions — "the author MOST likely includes X to appeal to readers who" and "the author assumes the audience already knows." Stems above mirror that framing directly.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze the Effectiveness of Characteristics of Multimodal and Digital Texts
expectation: Analyze the effectiveness of characteristics of multimodal and digital texts.
overview: This TEK asks students to move past simply identifying multimodal elements — images, hyperlinks, embedded video, infographics, audio — and evaluate whether those elements are actually doing rhetorical work. The question is never "is there a graphic here?" but "does this graphic do anything the prose couldn't do alone?"
studentFriendly: You can read a website, documentary, podcast, or digital article and judge whether the images, sound, layout, and links genuinely strengthen the message or just decorate it.
questionStems: The infographic in paragraph 4 MOST likely serves to —
Based on the information in paragraphs 2 and 3, how does the embedded video BEST contribute to the author's argument?
Which characteristic of the digital text is MOST effective in conveying the complexity of the data presented in paragraph 6?
The author's decision to include hyperlinks in paragraph 5 MOST likely allows the reader to —
How does the layout of the webpage MOST effectively support the author's purpose in paragraphs 1–3?
The photograph placed alongside paragraph 7 MOST likely affects the reader by —
Based on the multimodal elements in paragraphs 4–6, the author MOST likely includes audio excerpts in order to —
Which statement BEST describes how the interactive timeline contributes to the development of the central idea?
The author's use of bold headings and sidebar graphics in paragraphs 2–5 is MOST effective because —
A reader can conclude from the combination of text and images in paragraphs 8–10 that the author's MOST likely purpose is to —

misconceptions.title: More Modes = More Effective
misconceptions.body: Students assume that a text with more images, videos, and links is automatically more effective than a text with fewer. Teach them that effectiveness is about purposeful integration — a single well-chosen photograph can do more work than six stock images scattered across a page. Push them to ask: does each element add meaning the verbal text couldn't carry alone?

misconceptions.title: The Image Just "Shows" What the Text Says
misconceptions.body: Students treat visuals as illustrations rather than arguments. They'll note that a graph "shows the data from paragraph 3" without asking whether the graph makes the data more persuasive, more accessible, or more misleading. Watch for this; a redundant image doesn't prove effectiveness, and an image that contradicts or overcomplicates the text is a flaw worth naming.

misconceptions.title: Digital Format Is Itself the Analysis
misconceptions.body: Students conflate the medium with the characteristics. They'll write "it's a website so it's multimodal" and stop there. Reframe this: the analysis is about specific decisions — the choice of color palette, the placement of a pull quote, the decision to embed a chart rather than describe one. The format is the starting point, not the conclusion.

misconceptions.title: Hyperlinks Are Always Helpful
misconceptions.body: Students see hyperlinks as inherently positive — "you can learn more!" Teach them that links can fragment a reader's attention, create dependency on sources the author doesn't control, or give the appearance of evidence without the substance of it. An effective hyperlink leads somewhere that genuinely extends the argument; an ineffective one leads to a homepage or a paywalled article.

misconceptions.title: Effectiveness Means the Student Liked It
misconceptions.body: Students rate effectiveness by personal taste — "I liked the design" or "the colors were distracting to me." Push them toward rhetorical criteria: Does the element serve the audience and purpose? Does it strengthen the argument? Does it make the central idea clearer or more credible? Their preferences are not the standard; the text's own purpose is.

assignments.name: Effectiveness Audit
assignments.body: Students select a digital text (news site feature, advocacy webpage, explainer article) and produce a two-page analysis evaluating three multimodal characteristics. For each, they must argue whether it is effective or ineffective and supply specific textual evidence. Vague praise or vague criticism is returned ungraded.

assignments.name: Strip It Down
assignments.body: Students read a multimodal text, then read a plain-prose version of the same content (teacher-provided or student-created). They compare: what did the images, layout, or embedded media add — or what did it obscure? The written comparison must take a position on which version is more effective for the stated audience and purpose.

assignments.name: Redesign Critique
assignments.body: Students identify one multimodal characteristic they argue is ineffective and propose a specific redesign with a written rationale. The redesign must explain the rhetorical reasoning, not just aesthetic preference — why would this change serve the audience and purpose better?

assignments.name: Side-by-Side Comparison
assignments.body: Two digital texts on the same topic, one multimodally rich and one spare. Students analyze how each text's characteristics serve (or undermine) its purpose, then argue which makes the stronger case and why.

mastery: Names specific multimodal or digital characteristics (layout, embedded media, hyperlinking, data visualization, image placement) rather than gesturing at "pictures and stuff."
Evaluates effectiveness against the text's own stated or implied purpose and audience — not personal taste.
Distinguishes between redundant elements (the visual repeats what the text already says) and additive elements (the visual extends or complicates the text's argument).
Identifies when a multimodal element weakens the text — fragmented reading path, misleading data visualization, emotionally manipulative imagery — and explains the rhetorical consequence.
Produces written analysis that makes a claim about effectiveness, supplies specific evidence from the text, and reasons through the connection between the two.
notes: Vertical ramp: English II introduces characteristics of multimodal and digital texts; English III adds the *effectiveness* judgment — students are no longer just identifying what's there but evaluating whether it works. English IV extends this toward cross-genre synthesis with multiple digital sources.

DOK 3 justification: analyzing effectiveness requires strategic thinking — students must apply evaluative criteria to specific textual features and reason about rhetorical impact, not just recall or describe.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is tested indirectly through author's craft questions on paired or multimodal passages; stems here mirror that framing, treating embedded graphics and digital features as rhetorical choices subject to the same analysis as word choice or structure.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze the Author's Purpose, Audience, and Message
expectation: Analyze the author's purpose, audience, and message within a text.
overview: This is the load-bearing TEK for all of Author's Purpose and Craft — the three terms here (purpose, audience, message) form the analytical triangle everything else in Strand 8 is built on. The work in English III is to hold all three variables simultaneously and explain how they interact, not treat them as separate checklists.
studentFriendly: You can read any text — a novel, an editorial, a speech transcript — and explain why it was written, who it was written for, and what it's actually trying to say, and show how those three things shape each other.
questionStems: Read the following paragraph from the selection. Which statement BEST describes the author's purpose in this paragraph?
Based on the selection, the author's intended audience is MOST likely —
Which detail from the selection BEST supports the inference that the author's purpose is to persuade?
The author includes the information in paragraphs X–Y MOST likely to —
Which statement MOST accurately describes the central message of the selection?
How does the author's choice of audience affect the message presented in the selection?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the author's purpose shifts from informing to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST reveals the author's underlying message about [topic]?
The author's use of [detail/example] in paragraph X contributes MOST to which purpose?
With which statement would the author of this selection MOST likely agree?

misconceptions.title: Purpose Is Just "To Entertain, Inform, or Persuade"
misconceptions.body: Students reach for the three-point list and stop there, as if naming the category is the same as analyzing the purpose. Push them deeper: persuade toward WHAT? Inform an audience to feel HOW? The three-category framework is a starting place, not an answer. Watch for students who label a satirical essay "to entertain" because it's funny, missing entirely that the entertainment is the vehicle, not the destination.

misconceptions.title: The Message Is Whatever the Text Is About
misconceptions.body: Students conflate topic with message — "the text is about war" does not mean "the message is about war." Teach them that the message is the author's claim or stance about the topic, the thing the author wants the reader to believe, feel, or do differently after reading. Topic is the noun; message is the argument.

misconceptions.title: Audience Analysis Means Guessing Demographics
misconceptions.body: When asked about audience, students list "adults" or "high school students" and move on. Teach them that identifying the audience means specifying what the audience is assumed to already believe, know, and value — because that shapes every decision the author makes about diction, evidence, and structure. A demographic label is the start of an audience analysis, not the end of one.

misconceptions.title: Purpose, Audience, and Message Are Three Separate Questions
misconceptions.body: Students answer each as its own isolated prompt, then stop. The analytical move English III requires is triangulating — showing how the intended audience constrains what the message can be, and how the purpose determines how the message is packaged. Train them to write sentences like "Because the author is writing for X, the message is framed as Y rather than Z."

misconceptions.title: The Author Always Means What the Text Says Literally
misconceptions.body: Students assume the stated purpose is the real purpose. They miss irony, understated critique, and strategic omission. Teach them to ask: what is this text doing that it isn't saying? A corporate memo "informing" employees of a policy shift may be doing the work of preempting dissent. The stated purpose and the actual purpose are worth comparing.

assignments.name: Purpose Shift Tracker
assignments.body: Students read a multi-section text (long editorial, essay with distinct parts) and mark where the author's purpose shifts. They annotate each section with the purpose and a one-sentence rationale, then write a paragraph explaining how the shifts serve the overall message.

assignments.name: Two Audiences, One Message
assignments.body: Students take a single op-ed and rewrite the opening paragraph twice — once for the original intended audience, once for a different audience. They then write a short analysis explaining which choices changed and why.

assignments.name: Triangle Diagram + Evidence
assignments.body: For any text studied, students build a three-node diagram (purpose, audience, message) with arrows between nodes and one piece of textual evidence attached to each arrow, showing how the nodes interact. Written summary paragraph required.

assignments.name: Author Intent vs. Actual Effect
assignments.body: Students write a split analysis: one paragraph arguing what the author intended, one paragraph arguing what the text actually does to a modern reader. They conclude with a sentence on whether intent and effect align.

mastery: Names the author's purpose with specificity beyond the three-category label and grounds it in textual evidence.
Describes the intended audience in terms of assumed values, knowledge, and beliefs — not just demographic labels.
Distinguishes the text's topic from the author's message and states the message as a claim.
Explains the relationship between at least two of the three variables (purpose, audience, message) using evidence from the text.
Recognizes when stated purpose and actual rhetorical effect diverge, and names what the text is doing beneath its surface.
notes: Vertical ramp: English II Std 8A asks students to analyze author's purpose and audience within a text. English III adds *message* as an explicit third variable and raises the expectation from analysis of individual elements to analysis of how the three interact. English IV will ask students to evaluate the effectiveness of the author's choices — the move from analyze to evaluate.

DOK 3 justification: students must use evidence from text to construct an explanation of how three interacting variables (purpose, audience, message) shape each other — strategic thinking beyond simple retrieval or skill application.
This TEK has strong STAAR representation; purpose/message stems are among the highest-frequency item types on the English III STAAR reading component.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Evaluate Use of Text Structure to Achieve the Author's Purpose
expectation: Evaluate use of text structure to achieve the author's purpose.
overview: This TEK asks students to move past identifying structure — compare-contrast, problem-solution, chronological, and so on — and into asking whether the structure is doing the right job. The real question is: did the author's organizational choices actually serve what they were trying to accomplish, or did the structure work against them?
studentFriendly: You can look at how a text is organized — the order of ideas, the way sections are arranged, how the argument builds — and judge whether those structural choices actually helped the author make their point or undermined it.
questionStems: The author's decision to begin the text with [opening section/paragraph] MOST likely serves to —
How does the organizational structure of paragraphs X–Y contribute to the author's purpose?
Which statement BEST explains how the author's use of [chronological order/problem-solution/comparison] supports the central argument?
The author's choice to place [detail/section/anecdote] in paragraph X rather than earlier in the text MOST likely —
Based on the structure of the selection, which of the following BEST describes the author's purpose?
How does the structure of paragraph X differ from the structure of paragraph Y, and what does this difference suggest about the author's intent?
The author concludes the text with [final paragraph detail]. This structural choice MOST likely —
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates the author's use of [cause-and-effect/compare-contrast] structure to support the claim in paragraph X?
Which of the following MOST accurately evaluates the effectiveness of the author's organizational strategy?
The transition from paragraph X to paragraph Y suggests that the author's PRIMARY purpose shifts from —

misconceptions.title: Naming the Structure Is the Whole Job
misconceptions.body: Students identify "compare-contrast" or "problem-solution" and consider the analysis complete. Teach them that naming a structure is the floor, not the ceiling — the evaluative move is asking whether the structure was the right tool for the job. A compare-contrast structure can be brilliantly chosen or lazily imposed; the structure's name tells you nothing about whether it worked.

misconceptions.title: All Structures Are Equally Valid for Any Purpose
misconceptions.body: Students assume that as long as a text has an identifiable structure, the author's choice was intentional and effective. Watch for this especially in struggling readers, who will give credit for any structure that exists. Push them to ask: would a different structure have made this argument stronger? Could the author have built to the central claim more effectively another way?

misconceptions.title: Text Structure Only Applies to Nonfiction
misconceptions.body: Students will apply structural analysis only to essays and articles, then go blank when asked to evaluate structure in literary nonfiction, memoir, or hybrid texts. Remind them that a personal essay is making choices about what to narrate first, what to embed as backstory, and where to land — those are structural decisions with purposes, too. The same evaluation lens applies.

misconceptions.title: The Author's Purpose Is Always What They State Explicitly
misconceptions.body: When students evaluate structure against purpose, they use only the stated thesis or the surface-level topic as "the purpose." The richer analysis requires them to infer the full purpose — persuade, challenge assumptions, build empathy, expose contradiction — and then ask whether the structure serves that inferred goal. Teach them to read purpose from tone and effect, not just from thesis statements.

misconceptions.title: Effective Structure Means No Confusion
misconceptions.body: Students equate a "good" structure with one that is easy to follow, penalizing intentionally fragmented or nonlinear texts. Teach them that some authors disrupt chronology or fracture sequence deliberately — to create disorientation, to mirror a character's mental state, to force re-reading. Effectiveness is measured against the author's purpose, not against ease of comprehension.

assignments.name: Structure Swap
assignments.body: Students take a short argumentative or informational text and rewrite the same content using a different organizational structure. They then write a paragraph comparing the two versions: which structure better serves the author's apparent purpose, and why?

assignments.name: Structure Audit
assignments.body: Students annotate a full-length nonfiction text, labeling each section's structural function (establishes context, introduces counterargument, pivots to evidence, etc.), then write a one-page evaluation arguing whether the overall structure was effective or whether a different arrangement would have served the author's purpose better.

assignments.name: Two Texts, One Topic
assignments.body: Provide two texts on the same topic organized differently — one chronological, one analytical, for example. Students write a comparative evaluation: which author's structural choices better achieve their stated or inferred purpose?

assignments.name: Structure and Stakes
assignments.body: After reading a complex text (literary nonfiction, hybrid essay), students identify the moment where the structure most surprised them and write a paragraph evaluating whether that surprise served the author's purpose or worked against it.

mastery: Identifies the organizational structure of a text AND articulates the specific purpose the structure was designed to serve — not just what the text is about.
Evaluates the effectiveness of structural choices by weighing evidence for and against, not simply asserting that the structure "worked" or "didn't work."
Distinguishes between an author's stated purpose and an inferred purpose, and evaluates structural choices against the deeper goal.
Recognizes that unconventional or fragmented structures can be purposeful, and applies the same evaluative rigor to experimental texts as to conventional ones.
Produces written evaluation with specific textual evidence tied to structural claims — not summary or paraphrase.
notes: Vertical ramp: English II Std 8B asks students to "analyze" use of text structure — English III elevates the verb to "evaluate," requiring students to make and defend a judgment about effectiveness, not just describe the structure's presence and function. This is a meaningful DOK jump.

DOK 3 justification: evaluation requires students to apply criteria, weigh evidence, and defend a reasoned judgment — not retrieve or demonstrate a procedure, and not the extended multi-source reasoning that would push to DOK 4.

English III context: by this point students have encountered a range of complex texts across genres; the expectation is that structural evaluation is applied to genuinely difficult texts, including hybrid and literary nonfiction forms, not just clear-cut five-paragraph essays.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Evaluate an Author's Use of Print and Graphic Features
expectation: Evaluate the author's use of print and graphic features to achieve specific purposes.
overview: This TEK asks students to move past noticing — "there's a sidebar here" — into evaluating whether design choices actually accomplish something. The real work is connecting visual and typographic decisions to rhetorical intent: why bold THIS word, why include THAT chart, and did it work?
studentFriendly: You can look at how a text is designed — the headings, the images, the callout boxes, the fonts — and judge whether those choices actually do what the author needed them to do.
questionStems: The author MOST likely includes the diagram in paragraph X to —
Based on the information in the graph, which conclusion is BEST supported?
How does the use of bold text in paragraph X contribute to the author's purpose?
The author's use of the sidebar in section X MOST likely serves to —
Which statement BEST explains why the author includes the photograph alongside paragraph X?
The caption beneath the image in paragraph X MOST nearly suggests that —
How does the heading structure throughout the article help the reader understand the author's argument?
The author MOST likely uses the pull quote in section X in order to —
Based on the chart in paragraph X and the information in paragraph Y, what conclusion can the reader MOST logically draw?
Which graphic feature in the text BEST supports the author's central claim, and how does it develop that claim?

misconceptions.title: Graphic Features Are Decoration, Not Argument
misconceptions.body: Students treat images, charts, and callout boxes as filler the editor dropped in — things to glance at and skip. Push them to treat every design element as a deliberate choice with rhetorical stakes. Teach them: if the author (or editor, or designer) put it there, it is doing a job. The question is what job, and whether it succeeds.

misconceptions.title: "It Helps the Reader Understand" Is a Complete Answer
misconceptions.body: Students default to the same thin explanation — the chart "makes it easier to understand" — for every graphic feature in every text. That answer is almost never wrong, and almost never enough. Watch for it and push harder: easier to understand WHAT, exactly? Does the visual add information the prose doesn't carry, or does it just restate it? Does it persuade when the words can't?

misconceptions.title: Evaluation Means Praise
misconceptions.body: When the task says "evaluate," students assume they're supposed to confirm that the author's choices were good ones. Real evaluation includes the possibility of failure — a graph that misleads, a photograph that pulls emotional weight the argument doesn't actually earn, a callout box that buries the most important caveat. Teach them that an author's design choice can be strategic AND flawed at the same time.

misconceptions.title: Print Features and Graphic Features Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students conflate the two categories and end up analyzing fonts and photographs as if they work the same way. Print features — bold, italics, headers, bullet points — shape how the reader processes the prose itself. Graphic features — images, charts, maps, diagrams — carry information or emotional weight outside the prose. Each category has its own rhetorical logic; teach students to name which type they're analyzing before they analyze it.

misconceptions.title: The Purpose Is Always "To Inform"
misconceptions.body: Because these features appear most often in informational texts, students lock onto "inform" as the catch-all purpose and stop there. But a graph can persuade; a photograph can provoke; a bold header can create urgency or authority. Teach them to distinguish among informing, persuading, engaging, organizing, and emphasizing — and to name which purpose a specific feature is actually serving, not just the genre's default.

assignments.name: Feature Audit
assignments.body: Students select a nonfiction article with multiple graphic and print features. They build a two-column table: left column names the feature and its location; right column evaluates its purpose and effectiveness in 2–3 sentences. At least one entry must argue the feature does NOT fully succeed.

assignments.name: Design the Same Article Two Ways
assignments.body: Give students a plain-text version of a short informational piece. They redesign it twice — once to persuade, once to inform — choosing different graphic and print features for each version. Written reflection explains every choice.

assignments.name: Comparative Feature Analysis
assignments.body: Two articles cover the same topic but are designed differently (e.g., a news article vs. a magazine feature). Students write a focused comparison: which text's design choices better serve its stated purpose, and why? Evidence must cite specific features by name and location.

assignments.name: Evaluate a Misleading Visual
assignments.body: Students examine a graph or infographic that distorts data through design (truncated axis, cherry-picked scale, etc.). They write a paragraph identifying the design choice, explaining its effect on the reader, and evaluating whether the distortion is accidental or strategic.

mastery: Distinguishes between print features (bold, headers, bullet points) and graphic features (images, charts, diagrams) and applies distinct analytical moves to each.
Names a specific design feature, identifies its intended purpose, and renders a judgment about whether it achieves that purpose — with textual evidence.
Recognizes that the same feature (e.g., a photograph) can serve different purposes in different contexts and adjusts the evaluation accordingly.
Produces written evaluation that goes beyond "it helps the reader understand" to specify what understanding is built, or what persuasion is attempted, and whether it succeeds.
Considers the possibility that a design choice fails or partially fails — and can articulate why without dismissing the author's intent.
notes: Vertical ramp: English II Std 8C asks students to "analyze" the author's use of print and graphic features. English III upgrades the verb to "evaluate" — students must now render a judgment about effectiveness, not just describe function. English IV holds at evaluate but applies it to more complex and multimodal texts.

DOK 3 justification: evaluation requires strategic thinking — students must select criteria, apply them to specific features, and defend a judgment. This sits at the upper edge of DOK 3 rather than DOK 4 because the evaluation is bounded by the text; students are not constructing their own framework across multiple texts.

STAAR tests this TEK through informational and argumentative passages with embedded visuals; questions ask about purpose and effect of specific features, often pairing the graphic with surrounding prose to test integrated reading.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Evaluate How an Author's Language Shapes Reader Perception
expectation: Evaluate how the author's use of language informs and shapes the perception of readers.
overview: This is the capstone craft standard — students don't just identify language choices, they assess whether those choices are working and how they are bending the reader's reality. The move is from noticing to evaluating: not "the author used loaded diction" but "the author's loaded diction made the reader more likely to accept an incomplete argument without questioning it."
studentFriendly: You can read a text and explain not just what the author said but how the specific words and structures they chose led you — or other readers — to see the subject in a particular way, and whether that shaping was fair.
questionStems: The author's use of [word/phrase] in paragraph X MOST likely causes the reader to perceive the subject as —
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates how the author's language influences the reader's attitude toward [character/topic]?
How does the author's word choice in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the reader's understanding of [concept]?
The author's use of [rhetorical device] in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the author's language suggests that readers should conclude —
Which statement BEST describes how the author's diction in paragraph X shapes the reader's perception of [subject]?
The author MOST likely includes the phrase [phrase] in paragraph X in order to —
How does the shift in the author's language between paragraphs X and Y affect the reader's perception of [subject]?
The author's repeated use of [word/phrase/pattern] throughout the selection MOST nearly suggests that —
Based on the selection, with which statement about the author's use of language would a careful reader MOST likely agree?

misconceptions.title: "Shapes perception" just means tone
misconceptions.body: Students collapse this standard into "the author's tone is hopeful/ominous/critical" and stop there. Tone is an entry point, not the destination. Push them to trace how specific language choices produce that tone and then ask what the reader is led to believe as a result — what assumptions are being seeded, what alternatives are being quietly ruled out. If a student stops at "the tone is urgent," they haven't evaluated anything yet.

misconceptions.title: Evaluating language means judging the author's quality as a writer
misconceptions.body: Students hear "evaluate" and grade the author: "good word choice," "bad sentence structure." Redirect immediately. The evaluation question is not "Is this well-written?" but "What is this language doing to me as a reader, and is that effect legitimate?" A text can be beautifully crafted and profoundly manipulative — both things are equally worth evaluating.

misconceptions.title: Neutral language doesn't shape perception
misconceptions.body: Students assume that when an author isn't "trying to persuade," the language is neutral and therefore not shaping anything. Teach them that every lexical choice — including the choice to use passive constructions, to name some groups and not others, to quantify some claims and leave others vague — is a perceptual decision. There is no zero-degree language. Watch for students who only analyze explicitly persuasive texts and dismiss journalism or "objective" writing as not doing anything.

misconceptions.title: Perception-shaping is always manipulative and therefore bad
misconceptions.body: Students who catch an author shaping perception often jump straight to accusation: the author is being dishonest or propagandistic. Reframe: all effective writing shapes perception — that's the job.
