The evaluation question is whether the language illuminates or distorts, whether it expands or narrows the reader's sense of the subject. A good author shapes perception toward accuracy. A bad one shapes it away from it.

misconceptions.title: One example is enough to make the evaluation
misconceptions.body: Students find a single strong example of loaded diction or a striking metaphor and treat the analysis as done. Push them toward the pattern: how does this choice connect to others across the text? Does the same logic run through the syntax, the examples selected, the sources cited? A single example identifies; a pattern evaluates. Teach them that evaluation requires seeing the whole design, not the most dramatic part of it.

assignments.name: Perception Audit
assignments.body: Students read two texts on the same subject — one informational, one argumentative — and produce a side-by-side analysis of how the language in each shapes reader perception differently. They must name at least three specific language moves per text and explain the perceptual effect of each. Length: 2 pages.

assignments.name: Before and After
assignments.body: Give students a paragraph with strong perception-shaping language. They rewrite it using different diction and syntax to create the opposite perception without changing the core facts. Then they write a short reflection: what did you have to change, and what does that tell you about the original author's choices?

assignments.name: Manipulation or Illumination?
assignments.body: Students evaluate a nonfiction excerpt and argue — with specific textual evidence — whether the author's language shapes perception toward a more accurate or less accurate understanding of the subject. They must acknowledge the strongest counterargument before landing their judgment.

assignments.name: Pattern Map
assignments.body: Students annotate a full-length text (editorial, speech transcript, or essay) marking every language choice that shapes perception. They then write a thesis that characterizes the pattern across the whole text, not just individual moments.

mastery: Identifies specific language choices — diction, syntax, figurative language, selection of detail — and traces each one's effect on how readers perceive the subject.
Distinguishes between legitimate rhetorical shaping (building toward accurate understanding) and distortion (narrowing or misrepresenting the subject).
Evaluates perception-shaping across a whole text, identifying the pattern of choices rather than isolated examples.
Connects language analysis to ideological or argumentative stakes — explains not just what the reader feels but what the reader is being positioned to believe or discount.
Writes evaluations that are specific, evidence-based, and honest about the author's effectiveness even when the student disagrees with the author's position.
notes: Vertical ramp: English II Std 8(D) asks students to "analyze" how language informs and shapes perception; English III upgrades to "evaluate" — the shift demands a judgment about whether and how well the language is doing that work, not just a description that it is. English IV will push toward independent synthesis across multiple texts.

DOK 4 justification: evaluation requires applying criteria across a complete text, producing extended reasoning about the author's design, and defending judgments against counterclaims — not retrievable from the text alone.
This standard is among the most heavily tested on STAAR in English III; stems 1, 6, and 7 above reflect the exact phrasings that appear most frequently in released items.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Evaluate the Use of Literary Devices to Achieve Specific Purposes
expectation: Evaluate the use of literary devices such as paradox, satire, and allegory to achieve specific purposes.
overview: This TEK asks students to move past identification — not "this is a paradox" but "here is what the paradox is doing and whether it works." The three named devices are not decorative; they are structural arguments dressed in literary clothing, and evaluating them means understanding the claim the author is making through the form itself.
studentFriendly: You can read a text, identify when an author is using a device like paradox, satire, or allegory, and explain exactly what that device is doing and whether it accomplishes what the author intended.
questionStems: The paradox in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Which statement BEST describes the purpose of the satire in paragraphs X–Y?
Read paragraph X. The author's use of allegory in this passage MOST likely serves to —
Which line from the text BEST illustrates how the author uses paradox to develop the central theme?
The satirical tone in paragraphs X–Y contributes to the author's purpose MOST by —
Based on the allegory developed throughout the selection, which statement BEST captures the author's implied argument?
The author includes the paradox in paragraph X MOST likely to suggest that —
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that the author uses satire to critique —
How does the author's use of allegory in paragraphs X–Y develop the theme of —
The author's use of [device] in paragraph X is MOST effective in achieving which purpose?

misconceptions.title: Identifying the Device IS the Analysis
misconceptions.body: Students name the device and stop. They write "This is a paradox" or "The author uses satire" as if the label completes the work. Teach them that naming is the starting line, not the finish. The evaluation question is always: what is the device doing, and is it doing it well? If a student can't answer those two things, they haven't analyzed — they've catalogued.

misconceptions.title: Satire Means Making Fun of Something
misconceptions.body: Students reduce satire to mockery and miss its argumentative structure. They'll note that a text is funny or mean and leave it there. Push them to ask what the exaggeration or irony is exposing — what real-world target is being illuminated, and what is the author's implied corrective? Satire without a critique underneath it isn't satire; it's just snark.

misconceptions.title: Allegory Is Always One-to-One
misconceptions.body: Students lock into rigid symbol mapping — this character equals this idea, full stop. They miss that allegory works in layers and that the mapping is often imprecise by design. Teach them that a productive allegorical reading asks where the correspondence holds AND where it breaks down, because the breakdowns are often where the author's actual argument lives.

misconceptions.title: Paradox Means the Author Made a Mistake
misconceptions.body: When students encounter a genuine paradox — a statement that seems to contradict itself — they assume the author slipped up. They'll flag it as a logic error rather than a deliberate rhetorical move. Reframe this: paradox is a controlled collision between two truths that can't both be right but both are. The contradiction is the point. Watch for students who "correct" paradoxes in their annotations.

misconceptions.title: "Evaluate" Means Deciding Whether You Liked It
misconceptions.body: Students treat evaluation as personal taste — "I liked the satire" or "the allegory felt confusing." That's response, not evaluation. Evaluation means applying criteria: Did the device accomplish its stated or implied purpose? Did it clarify, persuade, illuminate, or undercut the text's argument? Teach them to build a case with evidence, not a preference with an adjective.

assignments.name: Device Autopsy
assignments.body: Students select one passage in which paradox, satire, or allegory is central and write a 1-page evaluation: What is the device? What purpose does it serve? What evidence supports that reading? Does it succeed? Require a claim, not just a description.

assignments.name: Satire Target Analysis
assignments.body: Students read a satirical excerpt (Swift, Twain, Orwell, or a contemporary equivalent) and identify the real-world target, the technique used to critique it, and whether the satire achieves its purpose. They must defend their evaluation with at least three specific textual details.

assignments.name: Allegory Layer Map
assignments.body: Working with a short allegorical text, students create a two-column map: left column tracks literal events/characters; right column tracks allegorical meanings. Then they write a paragraph explaining where the allegory is precise and where it is deliberately imprecise — and what the imprecision reveals.

assignments.name: Compare the Device Across Texts
assignments.body: Two authors use the same device (e.g., paradox) to different ends. Students write a comparative evaluation: which use is more effective, and by what criteria? The criteria must be stated explicitly before the verdict is delivered.

mastery: Names the literary device, explains its function in the specific passage, and evaluates whether it achieves its purpose — in that sequence, without skipping steps.
Distinguishes between what a satirical, paradoxical, or allegorical text says literally and what it argues rhetorically.
Applies consistent evaluative criteria (clarity, effectiveness, thematic fit) rather than substituting personal preference for analysis.
Recognizes that allegory is layered, that satire has a real-world target, and that paradox is controlled rather than accidental.
Produces written evaluation that makes a claim, cites specific textual evidence, and defends the claim against a plausible counterreading.
notes: Vertical ramp: English II introduces literary devices and asks students to analyze their effects; English III adds the evaluative layer — not just "what does this do" but "does it work, and by what measure." English IV extends this toward comparative and cross-cultural analysis of how the same devices function across traditions.
DOK 3 justification: evaluation requires students to apply criteria, weigh evidence, and construct a reasoned judgment — this is strategic thinking, not recall or skill application.

The three exemplar devices (paradox, satire, allegory) are not exhaustive; the TEK's "such as" opens the door to others (irony, extended metaphor, mock-epic), but these three are the highest-yield for STAAR and merit explicit instructional focus.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Evaluate How Diction and Syntax Contribute to Mood, Voice, and Tone
expectation: Evaluate how the author's diction and syntax contribute to the mood, voice, and tone of a text.
overview: This TEK is where word-level and sentence-level craft decisions get connected to the emotional and rhetorical atmosphere of a text. Students must do more than label tone — they must trace exactly which lexical and syntactic choices produce it, and explain why altering one would change the whole room's temperature.
studentFriendly: You can read a passage and explain not just what the mood or tone is, but which specific words and sentence structures created it — and why those choices matter.
questionStems: Read paragraph X. The author's use of [word/phrase] MOST likely contributes to which mood in the passage?
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates how the author's syntax establishes a tense tone?
The author's diction in paragraph X — particularly the words [word] and [word] — MOST likely conveys a tone of —
How does the author's use of short, fragmented sentences in paragraph X contribute to the mood of the passage?
Which sentence from paragraphs X–Y BEST reveals the narrator's voice?
The shift in diction between paragraphs X and Y MOST likely signals —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the author's syntax MOST nearly creates a mood of —
The author's repetition of [word/phrase] throughout the passage MOST likely contributes to which of the following?
Which of the following BEST describes how the author's diction in paragraph X differs from the diction in paragraph Y, and what effect does that shift produce?
The author's choice to use [passive/active/inverted] sentence structure in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —

misconceptions.title: Tone Is Just the Author's Attitude — Full Stop
misconceptions.body: Students learn the one-liner definition ("tone is the author's attitude toward the subject") and never move past it. Watch for responses that name a tone word — "sarcastic," "somber," "hopeful" — and stop there, as if labeling were the analysis. Push them to ask: what words, what sentence shapes, what rhythms built that attitude? The label is the starting line, not the finish.

misconceptions.title: Mood and Tone Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students conflate mood (what the reader feels) and tone (what the author projects). A passage can carry a cold, clinical tone from the author while producing dread in the reader — those are distinct effects worth distinguishing. Teach them to test the difference: "How does the author sound?" versus "How does the passage make me feel?" If their answers are identical every time, they're not making the distinction.

misconceptions.title: Voice Is Just Personality
misconceptions.body: Students treat voice as an impressionistic gut-feel — "the author sounds sarcastic" or "the author seems like someone I'd like." Real voice analysis locates specific syntactic habits: sentence length patterns, the ratio of abstract to concrete diction, the position of modifying phrases, the degree of hedging or certainty. Teach them that voice is a fingerprint made of consistent craft decisions, not a vibe.

misconceptions.title: Diction Analysis Ends at the Word Level
misconceptions.body: Students identify one charged word and call it done. The richer analysis is cumulative — noticing that an author reaches for latinate abstractions when describing institutions but Anglo-Saxon concrete words when describing individuals, or that the adjective load spikes in emotionally heightened moments. Watch for students who cherry-pick a single "good word" and never ask what the surrounding diction field is doing.

misconceptions.title: Syntax Critique Means Counting Sentence Length
misconceptions.body: Students who are trying to analyze syntax often count syllables or words and report "the sentences get shorter." That observation is the entry point, not the destination. Where does the subject land? Are modifying phrases piled before or after the main clause? Are parallel structures creating equivalence or irony? Teach them that sentence architecture determines what the reader processes first, last, and hardest — and that's where syntax becomes craft.

assignments.name: Diction Swap
assignments.body: Give students a paragraph from a text they've read and a list of synonyms for five key words. Students substitute the synonyms and then write a paragraph explaining how the mood and tone shifted — or didn't — and why.

assignments.name: Voice Fingerprint
assignments.body: Students select three passages from three different authors writing in the same genre (e.g., three personal essays, three war narratives). They identify at least three syntactic and two lexical patterns that distinguish each writer's voice, then write a brief comparative analysis naming what produces each distinct fingerprint.

assignments.name: Tone Reversal
assignments.body: Students take a passage with a clearly established tone (e.g., elegiac, satirical, urgent) and rewrite one paragraph with the opposite tone, preserving the content but altering diction and syntax. They then annotate every change they made and explain the craft decision behind it.

assignments.name: Syntax Heat Map
assignments.body: Project a passage. Students mark every sentence that does something architecturally notable — inverted order, deliberate fragment, long cumulative structure, parallel list — and explain the tonal or mood effect of each. Debrief as a class on which syntactic moves carry the most emotional weight.

mastery: Names a specific mood, voice, or tone and traces it to at least two distinct lexical or syntactic choices, explaining the mechanism of effect rather than just the label.
Distinguishes between mood and tone in a passage, attributing each to different craft features.
Analyzes syntax beyond sentence length — addresses word order, clause structure, modification patterns, and parallelism.
Identifies cumulative diction patterns across a passage rather than isolating a single word.
Explains how a shift in diction or syntax signals a shift in tone or mood, connecting the craft decision to its rhetorical and emotional consequence.
notes: Vertical ramp: English II (8F) asks students to analyze how diction and syntax contribute to mood, voice, and tone. English III elevates the verb to *evaluate* — students must now make a judgment about the effectiveness of those choices, not merely describe their presence. English IV will ask students to synthesize this analysis across multiple texts and authorial traditions.

DOK 3 justification: moving from identification of craft features to evaluation of their combined tonal and atmospheric effect requires strategic thinking — students must reason about the relationship between discrete choices and a whole-text quality.

This TEK has a natural STAAR analog in literary analysis passages, where released items regularly ask how diction or sentence structure produces an author's tone or a narrator's voice. Stems in this entry mirror that released-item pattern.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze the Effects of Rhetorical Devices and Logical Fallacies
expectation: Analyze the effects of rhetorical devices and logical fallacies on the way the text is read and understood.
overview: This TEK is where argument analysis gets teeth. Students don't just identify an anaphora or slap the label "ad hominem" on something — they explain what that device or fallacy actually does to a reader's thinking: what it opens up, what it papers over, what it makes the audience feel before they've had a chance to reason. The target is effect, not taxonomy.
studentFriendly: You can read a persuasive text and explain not just what rhetorical moves the author is making, but what those moves do to the way a reader receives and thinks about the argument — including when a move is hiding a weak claim.
questionStems: The author's use of anaphora in paragraphs X–Y MOST likely affects the reader by —
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates a logical fallacy that weakens the author's argument?
The rhetorical question in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
The author's appeal to authority in paragraph X contributes to the argument by —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, how does the author's use of [rhetorical device] affect the reader's understanding of the central claim?
Which statement BEST describes the effect of the slippery slope reasoning in paragraph X?
The author's use of [device] in paragraph X suggests that the author MOST likely assumes the audience —
Which sentence from the selection BEST explains how a logical fallacy undermines the author's credibility?
The repetition of [phrase] throughout the selection MOST nearly functions to —
Based on the selection, with which statement about the author's use of rhetorical devices would a careful reader MOST likely agree?

misconceptions.title: Identifying the Device IS the Analysis
misconceptions.body: Students write "the author uses pathos" and stop there, as if naming the move completes the job. Teach them that labeling is the entrance, not the destination — the real question is what the device does to a reader who encounters it. Push them to finish the sentence: "…which causes the reader to feel/assume/overlook…" If they can't complete it, they haven't analyzed anything yet.

misconceptions.title: Fallacies Only Appear in Bad Writing
misconceptions.body: Students are taught fallacy hunting as a debunking exercise, so they assume strong texts are fallacy-free. Show them how skilled writers deploy moves that technically qualify as emotional appeals or overgeneralizations — and how those moves can still be effective, even persuasive to careful readers. The analytical question isn't just "is this a fallacy?" but "does the fallacy work on the intended audience, and why?"

misconceptions.title: Rhetorical Devices and Fallacies Are Separate Categories
misconceptions.body: Students keep two separate lists — devices in one column, fallacies in another — and never connect them. In practice, a rhetorical device can function as a fallacy: an extended analogy can commit false equivalence, an appeal to tradition can be circular reasoning. Teach them to read for what the move is doing argumentatively, not just what it is taxonomically.

misconceptions.title: Every Strong Emotion Is Manipulative
misconceptions.body: When students spot emotional appeals, they reflexively call them manipulative and downgrade the author's credibility. Teach them the difference between illegitimate emotional manipulation (using fear to bypass evidence) and legitimate pathos (using a story to make evidence land). Not every appeal to feeling is a fallacy — the question is whether the emotion substitutes for reasoning or supports it.

misconceptions.title: The Effect of a Device Is Always Positive for the Author
misconceptions.body: Students assume rhetorical devices always help the author's case. A device can backfire — overwrought anaphora can read as bombastic, a false analogy can make the author look careless, excessive hedging can undermine authority. Teach them that analyzing effect means asking whether the device strengthens or weakens the reader's confidence in the argument, not just whether the author intended it to help.

assignments.name: Device-Fallacy Crosswalk
assignments.body: Students annotate a speech transcript, marking each rhetorical device and each logical fallacy. For each mark, they write one sentence explaining the effect on the reader. Final step: they identify two places where a device and a fallacy overlap in the same move.

assignments.name: The Backfire File
assignments.body: Students find an example of a rhetorical device that undermined rather than strengthened an argument — in an editorial, political speech, or advertisement. They write a paragraph explaining what the author intended and what the device actually did to a careful reader.

assignments.name: Effect Chain
assignments.body: Students pick one logical fallacy from a paired text and diagram the effect chain: what the author asserts, what the fallacy assumes the reader will accept without evidence, and what a skeptical reader would notice instead.

assignments.name: Paired Argument Comparison
assignments.body: Two authors argue opposite sides of the same issue. Students identify one rhetorical device from each and compare the effects — not whose position is right, but whose argumentative moves work better on the intended audience and why.

assignments.name: Revision for Effect
assignments.body: Students take a paragraph of their own argumentative writing and identify every rhetorical device they used, intentionally or not. They then revise one device to make its effect more precise and flag any move that accidentally functions as a fallacy.

mastery: Names a rhetorical device or logical fallacy and immediately explains the specific effect it produces on how a reader receives or processes the argument — not just what it is.
Distinguishes between rhetorical moves that legitimately strengthen an argument and those that substitute for evidence or reasoning.
Identifies when a single textual move functions simultaneously as a rhetorical device and a logical fallacy.
Evaluates whether a rhetorical device achieves its intended effect or backfires against the author's credibility.
Produces written analysis with specific textual evidence, named moves, and explained effects — not just a catalog of labels.
notes: Vertical ramp: English II Std 8G targets recognizing rhetorical devices and fallacies; English III shifts to analyzing their effects on how the text is read and understood — the move is from identification to explanation of consequence. English IV extends this to evaluation across multiple texts and traditions.
DOK 3 justification: students must reason about causal relationships between textual choices and reader response, requiring strategic thinking rather than recall or skill application.

STAAR regularly tests this TEK through argument analysis passages in both literary and informational reading sections — stems asking how a device "affects the reader" or "contributes to the argument" are direct analogs.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Plan a Piece of Writing Through Generative Strategies
expectation: Plan a piece of writing appropriate for various purposes and audiences by generating ideas through a range of strategies such as brainstorming, journaling, reading, or discussing.
overview: Planning is where writing actually starts, and English III students who treat it as optional produce work that shows it. The real skill here is not picking a strategy — it is choosing a strategy that fits the purpose, the audience, and the writer's own thinking process, and then generating material rich enough to sustain a full draft.
studentFriendly: You know how to start a piece of writing before you start writing it — choosing the right strategy for the job, whether that is freewriting, talking it through, reading into the topic, or mapping ideas — so that when you sit down to draft, you actually have something to say.
questionStems: Read the brainstorming notes in the box. Which idea from the notes BEST supports the development of an argument about —
A student is planning an essay for an audience of local officials. Which planning strategy would MOST likely help the student identify the audience's concerns?
Based on the journal entry in paragraph 1, which claim would the student MOST likely develop in a formal essay?
Which sentence from the student's notes BEST demonstrates an understanding of the intended purpose of the writing?
A student wants to write a persuasive piece about [topic]. Which of the following planning steps would MOST likely strengthen the student's argument before drafting?
Read the student's freewrite in the box. Which idea from the freewrite would need the MOST development before it could serve as a central claim?
The student's notes in paragraph X reveal a tension between two possible arguments. Which revision to the plan would MOST likely resolve this tension?
Which planning strategy would be MOST appropriate for a student who needs to narrow a broad topic before drafting a literary analysis?
Based on the planning notes, the student's intended audience is MOST likely —
Which detail from the student's brainstorming list MOST directly supports the stated purpose of the piece?

misconceptions.title: Planning Means Writing the Introduction First
misconceptions.body: Students conflate drafting with planning — they sit down, write an opener, and call it prewriting. Watch for students who submit a rough first paragraph as their "plan." Teach them that planning is generative and messy by design; it produces material that may never appear in the final draft but shapes everything that does. The introduction comes last when you know what you actually argued.

misconceptions.title: Brainstorming is a Warm-Up, Not Real Work
misconceptions.body: Students treat prewriting as the thing you do before the real thing starts — a formality to get through. Reframe it: the planning stage is where the writer's actual thinking happens. A draft with nothing behind it is just transcription. Push them to treat their brainstorming notes as intellectual work product, worth revising and returning to throughout the process.

misconceptions.title: One Strategy Fits All Assignments
misconceptions.body: Students who discover freewriting apply it to every task, including assignments that require research synthesis or audience analysis where it is nearly useless without reading first. Teach them that strategy selection is itself a skill — you read into an unfamiliar topic, you talk through a structural problem, you freewrite when you know the subject and need to unlock your own thinking. Matching the strategy to the task is the move.

misconceptions.title: More Ideas Always Means a Better Plan
misconceptions.body: Students generate long idea lists and assume quantity equals readiness. The harder skill is culling — identifying which ideas are actually arguable, which serve the stated purpose, and which belong to a different essay entirely. Teach them that a plan with twenty weak ideas is less useful than a plan with four strong ones and a clear sense of how they connect.

misconceptions.title: Audience Awareness Happens at Revision, Not Planning
misconceptions.body: Students treat audience as something to consider after the draft is written — they plan for themselves and adjust for the reader later. This produces writing that is structurally sound for the writer but alienating for everyone else. Teach them to name the audience explicitly during planning and to ask, before a single sentence is drafted, what that audience already knows, what they are skeptical of, and what they need to be convinced.

assignments.name: Strategy Audit
assignments.body: Students complete the same planning task using two different strategies (e.g., brainstorming list AND journaling) for the same prompt. They then write a one-paragraph reflection identifying which strategy produced more usable material and why, given the purpose and audience.

assignments.name: Plan-to-Draft Traceability
assignments.body: After completing a full draft, students annotate their planning notes to show which ideas made it into the draft, which were cut, and which evolved. The goal is to make the planning-to-drafting pipeline visible and discussable.

assignments.name: Audience-First Planning
assignments.body: Before generating any ideas, students write a one-paragraph profile of their intended audience — what the audience knows, believes, and needs. They then generate a brainstorming list and highlight every idea that directly addresses that profile. Anything not highlighted gets defended or cut.

assignments.name: Cold Start Planning Lab
assignments.body: Give students an unfamiliar topic and 20 minutes to produce a planning document using any strategy they choose. Then debrief as a class: which strategies surfaced the most usable material for this particular topic? Which would have worked better?

mastery: Selects a planning strategy deliberately, naming why it fits the assigned purpose and audience rather than defaulting to habit.
Produces planning notes that are generative enough to sustain a full draft — not a single-sentence outline, but developed ideas with enough specificity to argue from.
Names the intended audience explicitly in the plan and generates ideas shaped by what that audience knows, doubts, or needs.
Demonstrates the ability to cull a brainstorming list — identifying which ideas are central, which are peripheral, and which belong elsewhere.
Returns to planning notes during drafting and revision, treating them as a live resource rather than a completed task.
notes: Vertical ramp from English II: The planning expectation in English II is identical in structure but carries less weight in terms of purpose and audience complexity. English III writers are expected to plan for audiences beyond peers and teachers — public, civic, academic — and to select strategies that fit the rhetorical situation, not just the assignment type.

DOK 2 justification: The core task is applying known strategies to a new writing situation. The intellectual demand is procedural-with-judgment — students must select and execute, not just recall. Extended reasoning is reserved for the drafting and revision standards that follow.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog in the traditional sense — planning itself is not tested as a discrete item. However, STAAR revision and editing passages frequently embed planning decisions as revision choices (e.g., a student's draft missing audience focus or lacking a clear central idea). Question stems above mirror how STAAR tests planning through the revision-passage format.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Develop Drafts Using Strategic Organizational Structures
expectation: Develop drafts into a focused, structured, and coherent piece of writing in timed and open-ended situations by using strategic organizational structures appropriate to purpose, audience, topic, and context.
overview: This is where students stop thinking about organization as a formula and start treating it as a decision. The skill isn't selecting a structure — it's selecting the RIGHT structure and knowing why that choice serves this piece, for this audience, in this situation.
studentFriendly: You can look at what you're writing, who you're writing for, and what you're trying to do — and choose an organizational structure that actually fits, rather than defaulting to the five-paragraph template every time.
questionStems: Which organizational structure does the writer use MOST effectively to develop the central argument in paragraphs X–Y?
The writer MOST likely chose to present information in chronological order in paragraph X in order to —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, how does the writer's use of compare-and-contrast structure contribute to the development of the central idea?
Which sentence from the selection BEST demonstrates the writer's awareness of audience in the organization of ideas?
The transition at the beginning of paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Which organizational choice in the selection MOST clearly supports the writer's purpose?
How does the structure of paragraphs X–Y contribute to the coherence of the piece as a whole?
The writer's decision to place the counterclaim in paragraph X rather than at the end MOST likely —
Based on the selection, which statement BEST describes the relationship between the writer's organizational structure and the intended audience?
Which revision to paragraph X would MOST improve the structural coherence of the piece?

misconceptions.title: Organization Is a Template, Not a Tool
misconceptions.body: Students arrive in English III having been rewarded for five-paragraph essays so consistently that they treat it as the universal structure for all writing. Watch for students who plug every prompt into introduction-three-body-paragraphs-conclusion regardless of purpose or genre — they're not making an organizational decision, they're executing a habit. Teach them that structure is a rhetorical choice: a problem-solution frame serves a proposal, a narrative arc serves a personal essay, a compare-contrast grid serves an analysis. The template follows the task, not the other way around.

misconceptions.title: "Focused" Means Narrow
misconceptions.body: Students interpret focus as restriction — fewer ideas, shorter scope, less complexity. The result is writing that's thin rather than tight. Reframe focus as precision: a focused piece pursues one controlling idea relentlessly, from every angle, through every piece of evidence. A narrow piece just avoids things. Push them to ask not "am I saying enough?" but "does everything I'm saying serve the same destination?"

misconceptions.title: Audience Awareness Happens at the Vocabulary Level Only
misconceptions.body: When students think about audience, they adjust word choice — they write "use" instead of "utilize" for a general audience, or add more formal language for a teacher. That's surface-level audience awareness. Teach them that real audience awareness shapes structure: a skeptical audience needs the counterargument addressed early; an uninformed audience needs context before claims; a hostile audience needs common ground before persuasion. If the structure doesn't change when the audience changes, they haven't really adjusted for audience.

misconceptions.title: Context and Purpose Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students collapse context and purpose into one variable, usually purpose. But context includes the occasion, the platform, the prior knowledge of the reader, and the conventions of the form — all of which shape structural choices independently of purpose. A speech delivered at a memorial has a different structural logic than an op-ed arguing the same position, even if the purpose is identical. Teach students to hold all four variables — purpose, audience, topic, context — as separate levers that can pull structure in different directions simultaneously.

misconceptions.title: Revision Means Sentence-Level Editing
misconceptions.body: In timed writing situations especially, students treat revision as proofreading. They find comma errors. They fix spelling. They do not look at whether the paragraphs are in the right order, whether the opening sets up the structure that follows, or whether the conclusion does more than restate. Teach them to revise structurally first — move paragraphs, cut sections that drift, reorder for impact — before touching individual sentences. A polished sentence in the wrong paragraph is still in the wrong paragraph.

assignments.name: Structure Swap
assignments.body: Students write a short piece (one page) on the same topic twice — once using a chronological structure and once using a problem-solution structure. They write a one-paragraph reflection explaining which structure better served the purpose and why.

assignments.name: Audience Stress Test
assignments.body: Students take a draft they've already written and rewrite the organizational outline for three different audiences: a peer, a skeptic, and an expert in the field. They don't rewrite the full draft — just the structural plan. Discussion follows: what changed, and what stayed constant?

assignments.name: Timed Draft + Structure Debrief
assignments.body: Students complete a timed writing (40 minutes) on a given prompt. After time is called, they annotate their own draft: bracket each section, label the organizational move it makes, and write one sentence explaining why they made that structural choice. The debrief is metacognitive — not about the quality of the writing, but the intentionality of the structure.

assignments.name: Structure Autopsy
assignments.body: Students receive two published pieces on the same topic — one that uses its organizational structure well and one that muddles it. They produce a side-by-side structural outline of both and write a paragraph explaining how organizational choice affected each piece's effectiveness.

mastery: Selects an organizational structure and can articulate why that structure fits the specific purpose, audience, topic, and context of the piece — not just "I used cause-and-effect because the prompt was about causes."
Produces drafts where paragraphs and sections are sequenced with visible logic — each unit of meaning connects to the next, and the overall shape is recognizable and intentional.
Adjusts structural choices when purpose or audience shifts, producing noticeably different organizational plans for the same topic under different conditions.
In timed writing situations, commits to a structure early and executes it consistently rather than drifting mid-draft into a different organizational logic.
Revises at the structural level — reorders, adds transitions, cuts off-topic sections — before editing at the sentence level.
notes: Vertical ramp from English II: English II 9B(i) asks students to use organizational structures appropriate to purpose and audience. English III adds *topic* and *context* as explicit variables — the structural decision now has four inputs instead of two. The jump is from "choose a structure that fits your goal" to "choose a structure that fits all of the situational demands simultaneously."

DOK 3 justification: strategic selection and application of organizational structures in response to multi-variable situational demands requires reasoning beyond recall or skill application; the writer must evaluate how purpose, audience, topic, and context interact before committing to a plan.

This TEK governs the drafting sub-skill of organizational structure; 9B(ii) governs sentence-level drafting strategies. Both are sub-expectations of 9B — they are typically taught in tandem but assessed differently.

STAAR tests the organizational component of this TEK through revision-strand items asking students to identify the most effective structural revision, the function of a transitional move, or the relationship between organization and purpose in a draft.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Develop Drafts with Engaging Ideas, Rhetorical Devices, and Commentary
expectation: Develop drafts into a focused, structured, and coherent piece of writing in timed and open-ended situations by developing an engaging idea reflecting depth of thought with effective use of rhetorical devices, details, examples, and commentary.
overview: This is where English III writing separates from English II: students aren't just expected to make a point — they're expected to make it interesting, layered, and rhetorically deliberate. The move is from "I have an argument" to "I know how to make an audience feel the weight of it."
studentFriendly: You can take an idea that matters to you and develop it in writing with enough depth, detail, and rhetorical craft that a reader who didn't already agree with you might actually be moved.
questionStems: Which sentence from the student essay BEST reflects an engaging central idea developed with depth of thought?
The writer's use of [rhetorical device] in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Which detail in paragraph X MOST effectively supports the writer's central argument?
The commentary in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Which sentence from the essay BEST illustrates the writer moving beyond summary into original analysis?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which revision would MOST strengthen the development of the writer's central idea?
The writer includes the example in paragraph X MOST likely to —
Which of the following BEST describes the relationship between the detail in paragraph X and the commentary that follows?
The rhetorical device used in paragraph X contributes to the essay's development by —
Which sentence from the essay BEST demonstrates that the writer has moved from assertion to substantiated argument?

misconceptions.title: More Details Automatically Means More Depth
misconceptions.body: Students pile on details — statistics, quotes, anecdotes — and assume the accumulation equals development. What's missing is the commentary: the writer's voice explaining what the details mean and why they matter. Teach them that an example without commentary is just a body left at the crime scene; the analysis is the investigation.

misconceptions.title: Rhetorical Devices Are Decorations You Add at the End
misconceptions.body: Students treat rhetorical devices like garnish — metaphor sprinkled in after the real writing is done. Push them to see rhetorical choices as structural decisions that shape how a reader moves through an idea. A well-placed anaphora or extended metaphor doesn't decorate the argument; it IS the argument taking a particular form. Ask them: what does this device let you say that a plain sentence can't?

misconceptions.title: "Engaging" Means Dramatic or Emotional
misconceptions.body: When students hear "engaging idea," they reach for the sentimental or the sensational. Watch for essays that open with sweeping declarations ("Since the dawn of time...") or pivot into melodrama when the argument gets hard. Engaging writing at this level reflects genuine intellectual curiosity — a specific, surprising, or counterintuitive angle on the subject. Teach them: the most engaging move is often a well-placed admission that the question is harder than it looks.

misconceptions.title: Commentary Is Restating the Evidence
misconceptions.body: Students write "This shows that..." and then repeat the example in slightly different words. That's summary, not commentary. Commentary explains WHY the evidence matters, connects it back to the central idea, and pushes the reader's thinking forward. Show them the difference by projecting two versions of the same paragraph — one with restatement, one with genuine analysis — and asking them to name what the second writer does that the first doesn't.

misconceptions.title: Depth of Thought Means Length of Writing
misconceptions.body: Students conflate a long response with a thoughtful one, adding sentences until a paragraph feels heavy enough. The opposite failure also appears in timed writing: brevity mistaken for efficiency. Teach them that depth is measured by how much the essay moves the reader's understanding, not by word count. A single paragraph that genuinely complicates an assumption can reflect more depth than three paragraphs of assertion-and-summary.

assignments.name: Commentary Surgery
assignments.body: Give students a draft paragraph with four strong details and zero commentary. Their only job is to write the commentary — not add more details, not revise the details, just respond to them. Compare results: whose commentary pushed the argument forward? Whose restated it?

assignments.name: Device-Driven Draft
assignments.body: Students choose one rhetorical device (anaphora, antithesis, extended metaphor, rhetorical question) and draft a paragraph in which that device is load-bearing — not decorative. They then annotate the draft explaining what the device is doing structurally, not just naming it.

assignments.name: The Hard Angle
assignments.body: Students take a familiar argument (one they've written before or one the class has discussed) and find the most counterintuitive entry point they can — the angle a lazy writer would skip. They draft an opening paragraph from that angle and workshop whether it deepens the argument or derails it.

assignments.name: Timed vs. Open-Ended Parallel
assignments.body: Students write on the same prompt twice: once in 25 minutes, once with a full week. After both are done, they compare their own drafts. Where did depth suffer under time pressure? Where did the timed draft actually force more directness? The goal is metacognitive awareness of how process shapes product.

assignments.name: Depth Audit
assignments.body: Students exchange drafts and mark every sentence as one of three types: Assertion, Evidence, or Commentary. When they tally the results, the pattern usually shows itself — most drafts are lopsided. The exercise names the problem without the teacher having to.

mastery: Identifies and pursues a central idea that is specific and arguable rather than obvious or sweeping.
Selects details and examples that do genuine work for the argument — not every available fact, but the right ones.
Writes commentary that moves the reader's understanding forward rather than restating what was just quoted or cited.
Makes at least one deliberate rhetorical device choice and can explain the structural function it serves.
Maintains idea development and focus across the full draft, including under timed conditions — the argument doesn't dissolve in the final paragraph.
notes: Vertical ramp from English II: English II 9(B)(ii) asks students to develop an engaging idea with details, examples, and commentary. English III adds the explicit demand for rhetorical devices and depth of thought — the shift is from "include the moves" to "deploy them deliberately and explain their effect."

Vertical ramp to English IV: English IV 9(B)(ii) holds the same language but raises the bar through the complexity of texts and arguments students are expected to engage — the moves are the same; the intellectual terrain is harder.

DOK 3 justification: students must apply rhetorical knowledge strategically and analyze their own developing arguments — not retrieve or recall, but construct and evaluate choices within the act of writing.

This TEK has no STAAR passage-reading analog in the traditional sense, but STAAR's expository and argumentative writing prompts directly assess these sub-skills. Question stems here are framed around student essay excerpts as the "text," mirroring how STAAR assesses written revision and editing tasks.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Revise Drafts for Clarity, Development, Organization, Style, and Fluency
expectation: Revise drafts to improve clarity, development, organization, style, diction, and sentence fluency, both within and between sentences.
overview: Revision is the hardest writing skill to teach because students confuse it with proofreading. This TEK demands structural, rhetorical, and stylistic reconsideration — not spell-check. The six targets (clarity, development, organization, style, diction, sentence fluency) give English III writers a diagnostic framework for interrogating their own drafts with precision.
studentFriendly: You can read your own draft like a stranger would — spotting where your argument loses grip, where sentences stumble, and where a single word swap would make the whole paragraph land harder.
questionStems: Which revision of the underlined sentence in paragraph X BEST improves the clarity of the writer's claim?
A student wants to add a sentence to paragraph X to develop the central idea more fully. Which sentence BEST accomplishes this goal?
Read paragraph X. Which revision MOST effectively improves the organization of the paragraph?
The writer wants to combine the two sentences in paragraph X to improve sentence fluency. Which revision BEST accomplishes this goal?
Which sentence from the draft MOST clearly needs revision because it does not support the main idea of paragraph X?
The writer is considering replacing the underlined word in paragraph X. Which word MOST effectively improves the precision of the writer's diction?
Which revision of paragraphs X and Y BEST improves the organizational structure of the draft as a whole?
Read the following sentence from paragraph X. Which revision MOST effectively improves the style and tone of the sentence?
The writer wants to revise paragraph X to improve the development of the argument. Which sentence, if added, would MOST strengthen the paragraph?
Which revision of the underlined section in paragraph X BEST improves the fluency between sentences?

misconceptions.title: Revision Means Reading It Again and Fixing Typos
misconceptions.body: Students treat revision as a one-pass proofreading sweep — they catch a misspelled word, move a comma, and hand the draft back in. Teach them the difference explicitly: editing is surface, revision is substance. Make them revise before they edit by physically taking their pen away from commas and handing them a revision lens — one per pass. Clarity only. Then development only. The separation forces them to see the draft as a structure, not a finished product.

misconceptions.title: A Draft Is Already Good If It Makes Sense to the Writer
misconceptions.body: Students validate their own drafts by asking "Do I know what I mean?" instead of "Would a reader who doesn't know me know what I mean?" The writer always fills in missing logic automatically. Push them to read as strangers: cover up their name, swap papers with someone outside their friend group, or read the draft aloud to someone who hasn't been in class. The confusion that surfaces belongs in the revision.

misconceptions.title: More Words Equals Better Development
misconceptions.body: When students are told to develop an idea, they pad — extra adjectives, repeated points, sentence stretching. Real development means adding evidence, unpacking reasoning, or showing how one idea causes or complicates another. Watch for drafts that grew from one page to two but got thinner, not richer. Teach them to add a "because" or "which means" after every major claim and see whether they can actually complete the sentence.

misconceptions.title: Sentence Fluency Is About Making Sentences Sound Nice
misconceptions.body: Students chase rhythm by smoothing every sentence to the same length and shape — they produce a draft with no rough edges and no energy. Fluency is not uniformity; it is purposeful variation. Teach them to look at their sentence-length pattern the way a musician looks at a score — the short punchy sentence after three long ones is doing rhetorical work. Watch for drafts where every sentence is 18–22 words and everything sounds exactly the same.

misconceptions.title: Diction Revision Means Swapping in Bigger Words
misconceptions.body: Students equate strong diction with elevated vocabulary — they find a thesaurus and replace "shows" with "demonstrates" without asking whether the new word actually fits. The real move in diction revision is precision: does this word mean exactly what I need, or does it gesture in the right direction? Teach them to challenge the five most important nouns and five most important verbs in their draft. Not the longest words — the most load-bearing ones.

assignments.name: Six-Lens Revision Protocol
assignments.body: Students take a completed draft and revise it in six deliberate passes — one per target (clarity, development, organization, style, diction, sentence fluency). Each pass is tracked in a different color on a printed copy. Students submit both the color-coded draft and a final clean version, along with a one-paragraph reflection naming the two lens passes that changed the draft most significantly.

assignments.name: Before / After Gallery
assignments.body: Students select one paragraph from their draft that they revised most substantially, then display the before and after versions on a shared document. Classmates label what changed and which of the six targets was addressed. The class discusses which revisions made the most impact and why — the goal is building a shared vocabulary for revision decisions.

assignments.name: Sentence Fluency Autopsy
assignments.body: Students paste a paragraph of their draft into a table and label every sentence with its length in words and its syntactic structure (simple, compound, complex, compound-complex). They then map whether their variation is purposeful or accidental, and revise the paragraph to make every length and structure choice intentional. Written reflection: what was the effect of the pattern they started with, and what is the effect of the pattern they ended with?

mastery: Produces a second draft with substantive changes to at least three of the six targets — not surface corrections, but structural and rhetorical moves that alter how the reader experiences the text.

Articulates why a specific revision improves the draft, naming the target (e.g., "I moved this paragraph because it disrupted the organizational logic — the reader needs the context before the argument").
Varies sentence length and structure within paragraphs in ways that reflect rhetorical intent, not accident.
Selects words in the final draft that are precise rather than approximate — can defend specific word choices under questioning.
Identifies and resolves gaps in development by adding reasoning or evidence, not by inflating word count.
notes: Vertical ramp from English II: English II 9C requires the same six revision targets. English III escalates the expectation through the sophistication of the composition context — analytical and argumentative writing in English III demands that revision work harder across all six dimensions simultaneously, particularly development and organization.

DOK 3 justification: revision requires students to apply criteria across a complete draft, make strategic decisions about restructuring, and explain the reasoning behind changes — this is sustained strategic thinking, not a single-step task.

STAAR analog: this TEK maps directly to the STAAR Revision strand (Questions 21–30 on the Writing portion). Question stems mirror the six targets; students who understand the six-target framework can decode most STAAR revision questions by identifying which target the question is probing.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Edit Drafts to Demonstrate Command of Standard English Conventions
expectation: Edit drafts to demonstrate a command of standard English conventions using a style guide as appropriate.
overview: Editing is not proofreading. This TEK asks students to exercise disciplined command — not just catching comma splices, but making principled decisions about usage, mechanics, and documentation, guided by the authority of a style guide rather than gut feel. By English III, students should be editing with intention, not just with spell-check.
studentFriendly: You can move through a draft and fix every sentence-level problem — punctuation, grammar, usage, documentation — consulting a real style guide (MLA, APA, Chicago) the way a professional writer actually does.
questionStems: The author of the draft needs to edit sentence 4 to correct a grammatical error. Which revision BEST corrects the error while maintaining the author's intended meaning?
Read this sentence from the draft: [sentence]. The MOST likely reason an editor would flag this sentence is —
Which revision of paragraph X BEST demonstrates command of standard English conventions?
A student wants to use a style guide to document the source cited in paragraph X. Which citation MOST likely follows MLA conventions?
Which sentence in the draft contains a punctuation error that could MOST easily confuse the reader?
The editor's note reads: "This passage needs revision for subject-verb agreement." Which revision BEST addresses that concern?
Read sentences 6 and 7. Which edit would MOST improve the clarity and grammatical correctness of these sentences?
A student revises paragraph X and produces the following draft. Which version MOST effectively demonstrates command of standard English conventions?
Which sentence from the draft BEST illustrates the writer's control of standard English conventions?
Based on the context of paragraph X, the underlined word should MOST likely be changed to —

misconceptions.title: Editing Is the Last Step, Not an Ongoing One
misconceptions.body: Students treat editing as a single final sweep before turning something in, so they rush it and miss layered errors. Teach them that editing is iterative — one pass for grammar, one for punctuation, one for documentation — not one panicked read-through the night before. Watch for students who edit and revise at the same time; they almost always do both badly.

misconceptions.title: A Style Guide Is for Citations Only
misconceptions.body: Students pull out MLA to format a Works Cited page and then put it down. A style guide governs far more — punctuation of titles, use of abbreviations, handling of numbers, formatting of quotations — and English III students are expected to know this. Push them to use the index of their style guide, not just the bibliography section.

misconceptions.title: "Correct" Means Whatever Sounds Right to Them
misconceptions.body: Students trust their ear over the rule, which works when their reading diet has given them a good ear and fails catastrophically when it hasn't. Teach them that "command" means they can cite a rule, not just feel a preference. When they flag something as wrong, ask them where the rule lives — if they can't point to it, they're guessing.

misconceptions.title: Editing Flattens Voice
misconceptions.body: Students resist correcting a sentence because they think it'll stop sounding like them. The resistance is real and worth taking seriously — but the fix is teaching precision, not permissiveness. Show them how a well-edited sentence can be MORE vivid than the original: the writer's voice survives grammar; it doesn't survive confusion.

misconceptions.title: Standard Conventions Are Fixed and Universal
misconceptions.body: Students assume every convention is a law that applies to every genre and every audience. Teach them that "as appropriate" in the TEK language is doing real work — a personal essay and a research report have different conventions, and a style guide helps writers navigate those differences, not ignore them. Watch for students who mechanically apply MLA to informal writing or who refuse to use MLA for a research paper because they find it limiting.

assignments.name: Error Autopsy
assignments.body: Students receive a two-page student-written draft seeded with 15 deliberate errors across grammar, punctuation, and usage. They identify and correct each one, citing the rule from the class style guide by page number or section heading. The citation requirement is graded — naming the error is not enough.

assignments.name: One Sentence, Three Style Guides
assignments.body: Students take a single quoted source and format the citation three ways: MLA, APA, and Chicago. They annotate the differences. Objective: demystify the style guide as an authority structure, not a random formatting tyranny.

assignments.name: Before and After Audit
assignments.body: Students submit a rough draft alongside a final edited draft and produce a changelog: a numbered list of every edit made, with a one-sentence rationale for each. Grade the changelog as much as the draft — the reasoning is the skill.

assignments.name: Peer Edit With Citation
assignments.body: Students edit a partner's draft using their style guide as a reference. Every correction must cite a rule source. This slows down careless editing and makes the style guide a working tool rather than a decorative shelf item.

assignments.name: Editing Sprint
assignments.body: Students are given 20 minutes to edit a 300-word passage. They annotate directly on the text. Debrief focuses on which errors they caught first, which they missed, and what a second pass would have changed.

mastery: Identifies and corrects errors in grammar, punctuation, mechanics, and usage with specific rule citations from the appropriate style guide.
Uses a style guide correctly for documentation — not just Works Cited, but in-text citation conventions, title formatting, and other genre-specific guidance.
Edits iteratively across multiple passes with distinct objectives, rather than making one undifferentiated sweep.
Explains every editorial decision with a principle, not just a preference — can say WHY a sentence needed changing.
Maintains the author's voice and intent while correcting surface-level errors, without flattening the prose in the name of convention.
notes: Vertical ramp: English II Std 9D expects students to edit with attention to conventions. English III adds "command" — a higher bar that implies consistent, rule-governed, purposeful control across multiple convention domains — and makes explicit the expectation that students use a style guide as an authoritative reference, not just as a gesture toward academic legitimacy. English IV extends into more complex and varied publication contexts.

DOK 2 justification: the core cognitive work is applying known rules to novel sentences, which is skill-level application. When students must diagnose an error they haven't seen before and retrieve the correct rule, this can reach DOK 3, but the TEK's baseline is DOK 2.

STAAR note: editing conventions are tested directly in the Revision and Editing strand of STAAR Writing. Stems for this TEK closely mirror the STAAR Editing passage format; students should recognize this testing frame as a direct match to what they practice in class.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Publish Written Work for Appropriate Audiences
expectation: Publish written work for appropriate audiences.
overview: Publishing is where audience awareness stops being theoretical. Students have been revising for a reader; now they have to identify an actual reader and make deliberate choices about platform, format, and voice based on who that reader is and what they need from the text.
studentFriendly: You can take a finished piece of writing and make smart decisions about where it should live, what form it should take, and how it should be shaped for the specific people who will read it.
questionStems: The author revised the introduction before submitting it to the school newspaper. Which change MOST likely reflects an adjustment made for that specific audience?
Based on the passage, which revision BEST demonstrates the writer's awareness of a public audience?
The student chose to publish the piece as an open letter rather than a personal essay. How does this choice MOST likely affect the reader's expectations?
Which sentence from the draft would MOST likely need to be revised before publishing in a professional context?
The writer's decision to include [specific detail] in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Which change to the conclusion would BEST prepare the essay for publication in a general-interest magazine?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the writer's tone suggests the intended audience is MOST likely —
A student wants to submit the essay to a literary journal instead of a community newsletter. Which revision would BEST address the different audience's expectations?
The writer removed paragraph X before publishing. Which statement BEST explains why this revision improves the piece for a public audience?
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates a deliberate stylistic choice made for the benefit of the intended reader?

misconceptions.title: "Publish" Means Post It Online
misconceptions.body: Students treat publishing as synonymous with uploading — slap it on a blog, drop it in a Google Doc share, done. Teach them that publishing is a decision with consequences: the platform shapes how the piece is read, by whom, and with what expectations attached. A school literary magazine, a community op-ed, a class zine, and a personal website are all publications — but they are not the same publication, and they demand different versions of the same piece.

misconceptions.title: Audience Is Already Handled During Drafting
misconceptions.body: Students assume that because they thought about audience while writing, publication is just a formatting step. Watch for pieces that read fine in the classroom but would land wrong in a real context — too much inside vocabulary, too many assumed references, tone that works for peers but alienates strangers. The act of publishing forces a harder, more specific version of the audience question: not "who might read this?" but "who WILL read this, and what do they already know?"

misconceptions.title: Good Writing Publishes as Written
misconceptions.body: Students who have done strong revision work often resist further changes at the publishing stage. Teach them that revision for the writing process and revision for publication are two different acts. The piece may be well-crafted and still need a different title, a tighter opening, or a cut paragraph when it moves into a real venue. Publication is its own editorial moment — not a punishment for imperfect drafts, but a distinct professional skill.

misconceptions.title: Longer and More Formal Is Always More Publishable
misconceptions.body: Students equate publishing with formality — they pad, elevate diction, and add hedges because they think real publications sound like that. Push them toward examples of published work that is clean, direct, and conversational. The real question is register, not length: does the voice fit where this piece is going? A compelling personal essay for a literary magazine and a tightly argued op-ed for a city paper demand different registers, neither of which is "more formal" in any simple sense.

misconceptions.title: The Audience Is Whoever Grades It
misconceptions.body: This is the deepest misconception and the hardest to break. Students have spent years writing for teachers, and "publishing" within a classroom can reinforce that instinct if the teacher is still the only real reader. Watch for students who talk about audience in their reflections but have made no actual changes based on that audience. Real publication requires a real reader who isn't obligated to be kind — that shift in stakes is the whole point of the assignment.

assignments.name: Venue Match
assignments.body: Students select a finished draft and research three real publication venues (school paper, local paper, a relevant online journal, a themed anthology). For each venue, they write one paragraph describing what revisions the piece would need. Then they choose one and make those revisions. The reflection is as important as the revision.

assignments.name: Class Publication
assignments.body: The class produces one curated publication — a zine, a literary magazine, a class blog with editorial standards, a community newsletter insert. Students submit pieces, go through a peer editorial process, and produce a final edited volume. The editorial decisions (what to accept, what to ask writers to revise, what layout signals to different readers) are part of the assignment, not just decoration.

assignments.name: Before and After
assignments.body: Students document the changes they made to a piece between the final draft and the published version. They write a short editorial memo explaining each significant change and the audience-based reasoning behind it. This makes the invisible publishing revision visible and assessable.

assignments.name: Real Submission
assignments.body: Students identify an actual external publication that accepts student or youth writing and submit a piece. Whether accepted or not, the process of reading submission guidelines, formatting correctly, and writing a cover letter is the assignment. Debrief the experience — what did the submission guidelines tell them about the audience?

mastery: Identifies a specific, real audience for a finished piece and articulates what that audience needs from the text.
Makes deliberate, explainable revisions between final draft and published version based on venue and audience — not cosmetic changes, not padding.
Selects an appropriate publication platform or format and can justify the choice in terms of who will read it and how.
Distinguishes between writing-process revision and publication revision as two different editorial acts.
Produces a published piece that reads as though it was written for its actual audience, not just completed for a teacher.
notes: Vertical ramp: English II 9(E) establishes the publishing expectation at the level of selecting an appropriate format and audience. English III pushes students to make and justify substantive revisions at the publishing stage — the audience is more specific, the venues more demanding, and the editorial reasoning more explicit. English IV will extend this toward sustained independent publication and professional-context awareness.
DOK 3 justification: publishing requires strategic thinking — selecting venue, applying audience-based criteria, making revision decisions — beyond simple recall or single-skill application.

STAAR does not directly test this TEK as a discrete skill; stems above mirror how STAAR tests audience awareness and revision-for-purpose within writing passages. Note this in instruction: the testing analog is revision-with-audience-purpose, not publication mechanics per se.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Compose Literary Texts Using Genre Characteristics and Craft
expectation: Compose literary texts such as fiction and poetry using genre characteristics and craft.
overview: This is the TEK where students stop analyzing what authors do and start doing it themselves — deliberately, not accidentally. The demand is craft: genre awareness applied to intentional choices about structure, voice, image, and form. The risk is students producing competent summaries of genre features rather than actual literary texts.
studentFriendly: You can write fiction and poetry that shows you understand how the genre works — not just filling in the form, but making deliberate choices about structure, language, and form the way real writers do.
questionStems: The narrator's shift from past tense to present tense in paragraph X MOST likely creates —
Which line from the poem BEST illustrates the speaker's use of imagery to develop the central idea?
The author's choice to begin the story in medias res in paragraph X contributes to the overall effect by —
Which detail from paragraph X BEST supports the inference that the narrator is an unreliable narrator?
The structure of the poem — moving from [image A] to [image B] — MOST likely suggests —
How does the author's use of [literary device] in paragraphs X–Y develop the story's central conflict?
The shift in the speaker's tone between stanza X and stanza Y MOST likely indicates —
Which sentence from paragraph X BEST demonstrates the author's use of [craft element] to reveal character?
Based on the details in paragraphs X–Y, the author MOST likely uses the setting to —
The author's decision to end the story with [detail/image] MOST nearly suggests that —

misconceptions.title: Genre Characteristics Are a Checklist, Not a Compass
misconceptions.body: Students treat genre features as boxes to check — "I added a metaphor, so this is poetry now." Teach them that genre characteristics are tools serving meaning, not requirements for certification. Ask them: why THIS device HERE? What would the poem lose if you cut it? If they can't answer, the craft isn't doing work yet.

misconceptions.title: "Creative" Means Anything Goes
misconceptions.body: Students hear "creative writing" and abandon every structural decision to instinct, assuming that deliberateness kills authenticity. Push back hard. Every skilled author makes conscious choices — about where a scene starts, where a line breaks, what the speaker never says. Watch for students who defend every weak draft choice with "that's how I felt it." Feeling and craft are not enemies; one without the other produces noise.

misconceptions.title: Fiction Requires Plot, Poetry Requires Rhyme
misconceptions.body: Students anchor to the most surface-level genre features: fiction needs something to happen, poetry needs to rhyme. Show them flash fiction with almost no external action and poems with no end-rhyme that land harder than anything with a twist ending or a couplet. The goal is effect, and effect comes from precision — not from hitting the most obvious formal targets.

misconceptions.title: Voice Is Personality, Not a Craft Choice
misconceptions.body: Students conflate "my voice" with whatever comes out naturally, assuming voice is innate and unchangeable. Teach them that voice is built — through syntax, diction, distance, perspective, and pace — and that skilled writers construct different voices for different texts. Watch for students who can't explain why their narrator sounds the way they do. If they can't articulate it, they're not controlling it.

misconceptions.title: Revision Means Editing, Not Rethinking
misconceptions.body: Students revise by fixing surface errors — comma splices, spelling, word repetition — and consider the draft finished. In literary composition, revision often means structural overhaul: moving where the story enters, cutting the first stanza entirely, collapsing two characters into one. Teach them to hold the draft loosely and ask first whether the architecture is doing its job before they polish the furniture.

assignments.name: Imitation with Commentary
assignments.body: Students choose a published short story or poem they admire and write an original piece imitating its structure, point of view, or form — NOT its content. Alongside the draft, they submit a one-page annotation identifying at least five specific craft choices they replicated and why each was deliberate.

assignments.name: One Moment, Two Genres
assignments.body: Students write the same emotional or thematic content twice — once as a scene of flash fiction (400–600 words), once as a poem (20–30 lines). They then write a short reflection on what each genre could do that the other couldn't. Discussion follows on what gets lost in translation and what each form uniquely enables.

assignments.name: Workshop with Diagnostic Feedback
assignments.body: Students bring a draft to small-group workshop. Readers identify one specific place where a craft choice is working and one where it isn't, citing the text. Writers may not speak during feedback. After workshop, each writer drafts a revision plan naming at least three structural changes — not edits, changes — before touching the draft again.

mastery: Makes deliberate, articulable craft choices — can name what a structural or linguistic decision accomplishes and why it belongs in this piece.
Produces fiction that moves through scene with purposeful pacing and a clear controlling tension, not just plot summary in prose clothing.
Produces poetry in which line breaks, imagery, and form serve the speaker's central concern — not decoration layered over a prose statement.
Revises structurally — relocating entry points, cutting entire sections, shifting perspective — not only correcting surface errors.
Reads published literary texts as a writer, noticing craft moves and naming why they work rather than just whether the text is "good."
notes: Vertical ramp: English II 10(A) requires composing literary texts using genre characteristics and craft — the same language. English III's escalation is implicit: the craft expectations in Std 8 (author's purpose, rhetorical choices, use of language) are considerably higher, and students writing literary texts in English III are expected to demonstrate the analytical vocabulary they've built. English IV continues this pattern with longer and more complex original works.

DOK 4 justification: students must make original, extended creative decisions and defend craft choices — this is not reproduction of a form but construction of a text, requiring synthesis of multiple genre, craft, and thematic considerations.

For STAAR analog: this TEK is assessed primarily through on-demand literary writing prompts, not multiple-choice. The question stems above mirror how craft choices in published literary texts are tested — STAAR treats the stimulus text as a model for what student writers should be able to do. Note this in instruction: the released literary writing prompts and the companion stimulus passages are the most direct assessment evidence for this TEK.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Compose Informational Texts Using Genre Characteristics and Craft
expectation: Compose informational texts such as explanatory essays, reports, resumes, and personal essays using genre characteristics and craft.
overview: This TEK is where students stop writing "school essays" and start writing real genres that exist in the world. The leap in English III is craft — not just hitting genre features as a checklist, but controlling tone, structure, evidence selection, and voice in ways that make the piece do something for an actual reader.
studentFriendly: You can write informational texts — explanatory essays, reports, resumes, personal essays — that look and work like the real thing, not like an assignment.
questionStems: Read the report excerpt in paragraphs X–Y. The writer MOST likely organized the information in this order to —
Which sentence from the explanatory essay BEST introduces the central idea of the paragraph?
The writer's word choice in paragraph X suggests that the intended audience is MOST likely —
Which revision to the resume entry in paragraph X would MOST improve its clarity and professionalism?
The writer of the personal essay uses the anecdote in paragraph X MOST likely to —
Which sentence from the report BEST supports the conclusion the writer draws in paragraph Y?
How does the organizational structure of paragraphs X–Z contribute to the reader's understanding of the topic?
