The transition the writer uses between paragraphs X and Y MOST likely serves to —
Which detail from the personal essay MOST effectively develops the writer's central claim?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the writer's use of [specific technique] in the explanatory essay MOST nearly functions as —

misconceptions.title: Informational Means Neutral
misconceptions.body: Students assume informational writing has no voice, that every sentence should read like an encyclopedia entry. Watch for writing that's technically accurate but so flat it says nothing. Teach them that informational writing still has a controlling intelligence behind it — a writer making choices about what to include, what to emphasize, and how to sequence evidence. Neutral and voiceless are not the same thing.

misconceptions.title: The Resume Is Just a Form to Fill Out
misconceptions.body: Students treat the resume as a template exercise, dropping in their real or imagined credentials without considering how each word is doing rhetorical work. Teach them that every verb choice, every quantifier, every formatting decision is a persuasive act. A resume is informational AND argumentative — it's making a case for a specific reader with specific expectations.

misconceptions.title: Genre Features Are the Destination
misconceptions.body: Students hit the checklist — thesis, three body paragraphs, works cited — and stop there. They conflate meeting the requirements with writing well. Push them deeper: genre features are the floor, not the ceiling. The craft is in what you do once you've built the structure — how you open, how you pace evidence, when you zoom in and when you step back.

misconceptions.title: Reports and Essays Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students flatten all informational prose into one gray mass. A report aggregates and presents evidence for a reader who needs to make a decision; an explanatory essay unpacks an idea for a reader who needs to understand something. Teach them that the genre determines the writer's job, the tone, and the relationship with the reader. Misread the genre and you've misread the task.

misconceptions.title: Personal Essays Are Just Personal Narratives
misconceptions.body: Students confuse the personal essay with a memoir or a creative narrative — they write a story and call it done. The personal essay uses personal experience to move toward an insight or idea that extends beyond the writer's own life. Watch for pieces where the "so what" is entirely private ("this taught me to be brave") rather than reaching outward into something universal or arguable.

assignments.name: Genre Swap
assignments.body: Students write about the same subject twice — once as a report (for a civic body or decision-maker) and once as a personal essay (for a general reader). They then write a one-paragraph reflection on how the genre changed what they had to do with the same material.

assignments.name: Resume Audit
assignments.body: Students draft a resume for their current qualifications, then for a job they want in ten years. They annotate every verb choice and every omission, explaining the rhetorical decision behind it. Class shares the most debated choices.

assignments.name: Explanatory Essay with a Real Audience
assignments.body: Students write an explanatory essay on a topic relevant to a specific real audience — a letter-to-the-editor format, a piece for the school newspaper, a brief for a community organization. The audience must be named in the piece and must shape its structure and diction.

assignments.name: Craft Autopsy
assignments.body: Before drafting, students read two professional examples of the genre they'll be writing (selected by the teacher). They identify three specific craft moves — not genre features, but actual writerly decisions — and must imitate at least one in their own draft. The imitation is cited in a footnote.

assignments.name: Cold Read Revision
assignments.body: Students exchange drafts with a partner who was writing in a different genre. The reader's job is to name the genre, describe what they understand the central idea to be, and identify the single moment where the craft is working best. Writers revise based on at least one insight from the cold read.

mastery: Produces a draft that is recognizably in the target genre — not because it lists genre features, but because it does the work that genre does for a real reader.
Makes purposeful craft decisions (opening strategy, evidence sequencing, sentence-level control) that serve the genre's purpose rather than defaulting to the five-paragraph template.
Distinguishes between genres in the same family — can articulate why a report and an explanatory essay make different demands on the writer.
Produces a resume that controls every word as a rhetorical act, not as a form-fill.
Writes a personal essay that reaches from the personal toward an insight with broader resonance.
notes: Vertical ramp: English II requires students to compose informational texts using genre characteristics and craft at the same code; English III holds the same expectation but at greater sophistication of audience awareness, craft intentionality, and genre range — the resume appears at this level as a college-and-career-readiness marker.

DOK 3 justification: students must apply genre knowledge to new writing tasks and make strategic craft decisions, requiring more than recall or skill application — the task requires judgment about audience, purpose, and form working simultaneously.

STAAR tests informational writing most directly on the revising/editing portions of the exam and through the short-answer composition prompt; stems above are modeled on how STAAR frames revision tasks and asks students to evaluate writer choices in published and student texts.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Compose Argumentative Texts Using Genre Characteristics and Craft
expectation: Compose argumentative texts using genre characteristics and craft.
overview: This is the payoff standard for English III writing — students don't just argue, they argue with craft, which means understanding what argumentation as a genre actually demands: a claim that can be disputed, evidence that is selected rather than dumped, warrants that connect the two, and rhetorical choices that work on a reader. The word "craft" is doing real work here; it separates competent argument from powerful one.
studentFriendly: You can write an argument that doesn't just state an opinion but actually builds a case — using the structural moves and stylistic choices that make readers take your position seriously.
questionStems: Which sentence from the student's essay BEST establishes the writer's central claim?
The writer includes the information in paragraph X MOST likely to —
Which revision to paragraph X would MOST strengthen the writer's argument?
How does the writer's use of [rhetorical device] in paragraph X contribute to the effectiveness of the argument?
The writer's word choice in paragraph X suggests that the intended audience is MOST likely —
Which sentence from the essay BEST provides a counterclaim the writer addresses?
Based on paragraphs X and Y, the writer's organizational structure is MOST likely intended to —
Which piece of evidence from paragraph X BEST supports the writer's claim that —
The writer concludes with paragraph X MOST likely in order to —
Which transition in the essay MOST effectively signals a shift from evidence to the writer's analysis?

misconceptions.title: Argument Means Disagreement, Not Structure
misconceptions.body: Students think "argumentative" means picking a fight — they lead with attitude and treat forcefulness as a substitute for logic. Teach them that argument is a genre with architecture: claim, grounds, warrant, acknowledgment of opposition, rebuttal. The passion is fine; it has to run on structure or it goes nowhere.

misconceptions.title: Evidence Is the Argument
misconceptions.body: Students load paragraphs with quotes and statistics and assume the evidence speaks for itself. Watch for body paragraphs that are 80% source material and 20% the writer's voice. The argument lives in what the writer DOES with the evidence — the analysis, the connection back to the claim, the warrant that explains why this evidence means what they say it means. Push them to flip the ratio.

misconceptions.title: The Counterargument Paragraph Is a Formality to Check Off
misconceptions.body: Students drop a single sentence ("Some people might think X, but they're wrong") and consider the counterargument handled. Teach them that the counterargument section is actually where the argument gets stronger — by taking the opposing view seriously, steelmanning it, and then showing precisely where and why it falls short. A writer who dismisses opposition looks scared of it.

misconceptions.title: Craft Is Just Style, Not Argument
misconceptions.body: Students treat rhetorical craft — parallel structure, strategic repetition, varied syntax, diction choices — as decoration applied after the argument is "done." Reframe this as architecture: the craft IS part of the argument. The way a sentence is built shapes whether a reader trusts the claim. Show them mentor texts where the argument would collapse if you flattened the style.

misconceptions.title: A Longer Essay Is a Better Argument
misconceptions.body: Students equate length with thoroughness and pad every paragraph to hit a page count. Watch for repeated points phrased differently, over-explained transitions, and conclusions that restate the entire essay. Teach them that argumentative craft includes knowing what to cut — an argument that circles back on itself loses the reader's trust faster than a short one that stays tight.

assignments.name: The Steelman Draft
assignments.body: Before writing their own argument, students write the strongest possible version of the opposing position — one that a real opponent would recognize as fair. Then they write their own argument knowing exactly what they're up against. Compare the two drafts: does their argument actually address the steelman they built?

assignments.name: Warrant Workshop
assignments.body: Give students a claim and a piece of evidence. They must write only the warrant — the sentence that explains why that evidence proves that claim. No evidence-dumping, no restating the claim. Share and compare: different students will write different warrants for the same claim/evidence pair, revealing that warrants are choices, not givens.

assignments.name: Revision by Audience Shift
assignments.body: Students write a complete argumentative draft, then revise it for a different target audience — same claim, same evidence, new rhetorical framing. Debrief: what changed? What genre conventions stayed the same regardless of audience?

assignments.name: Craft Annotation
assignments.body: Students find a published op-ed or editorial and annotate every rhetorical craft move in the margins — parallel structure, diction choices, strategic concessions, sentence rhythm changes. Then they revise one paragraph of their own essay to steal one technique.

assignments.name: Public Argument
assignments.body: Students compose a 600-word letter-to-the-editor or op-ed for a real outlet (school paper, local newspaper, a publication that accepts student submissions) on an issue they have actual standing to address. Real audience; real genre constraints.

mastery: Produces a disputable, specific claim — not a fact, not a preference, but a position that can be argued against.
Selects and integrates evidence with explicit warrants that connect evidence to claim, rather than presenting evidence as self-explanatory.
Addresses and genuinely engages opposing positions rather than dismissing them in a sentence.
Makes intentional craft decisions — diction, syntax, structure — that serve the argument's rhetorical purpose, not just its page count.
Sustains argument logic across multiple paragraphs without restating the claim repeatedly or losing the through-line.
notes: Vertical ramp from English II: English II Std 10C establishes the same expectation, but English III raises the bar on craft complexity — students are expected to command rhetorical choices consciously, not just produce structurally sound argument. The DOK-4 floor means the writing task itself must require extended reasoning, not template-filling.

DOK 4 justification: producing an original argumentative text integrating genre knowledge, rhetorical craft, evidence selection, and responsive audience awareness requires sustained higher-order thinking across a full composing process — not a single skill performed in isolation.

STAAR connection: argumentative writing is assessed directly on the STAAR Writing test; these stems mirror the revision-and-editing frames STAAR uses to evaluate student argumentation at the passage level.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Compose Correspondence in a Professional or Friendly Structure
expectation: Compose correspondence in a professional or friendly structure.
overview: This TEK is deceptively simple — the challenge is not that students can't write letters, it's that they don't know when to switch registers. Professional correspondence demands structural discipline and tonal control that informal communication has made invisible to many students. The real work is teaching them that choosing the wrong structure isn't a style error — it's a credibility problem.
studentFriendly: You can write letters, emails, or other correspondence that match the right tone and structure — formal when the situation demands it, personal when it calls for that — and the reader knows immediately that you understood the room.
questionStems: Which sentence from the letter BEST demonstrates that the writer understands the professional context of the correspondence?
The writer MOST likely begins the letter with a formal salutation in order to —
Based on paragraphs 2–3, which phrase from the letter BEST establishes the writer's credibility with the reader?
The closing of the letter contributes to its professional tone MOST likely by —
Which revision to the underlined sentence in paragraph 1 would BEST improve the letter's formal register?
The writer's word choice throughout the letter suggests that the intended audience is MOST likely —
How does the organizational structure of the letter in paragraphs 1–3 contribute to the clarity of the writer's purpose?
Which sentence from the letter MOST clearly signals a shift from professional context to a personal appeal?
The writer MOST likely includes specific details in paragraph 2 in order to —
Based on the letter as a whole, with which statement about effective professional correspondence would the writer MOST likely agree?

misconceptions.title: Casual Tone Is Just a Style Choice
misconceptions.body: Students treat formality as preference rather than situational requirement — they write casual emails to employers or formal letters to friends and call both acceptable. Teach them that register mismatch is a communication failure, not a personality expression. Walk them through real-world consequences: the internship application that reads like a text message, the cover letter that sounds like a legal brief. Structure is audience awareness made visible.

misconceptions.title: Friendly Means Unstructured
misconceptions.body: When students are told a letter can be "friendly," they take that as permission to abandon organization entirely. A friendly letter still has a greeting, a body with a logical sequence, and a close — the warmth lives in the word choice, not in the chaos of the paragraphs. Push them to find letters between real people that are both warm AND organized, then ask: what would be lost if those writers had just written stream-of-consciousness?

misconceptions.title: Professional Means Stiff and Impersonal
misconceptions.body: The other failure mode: students overcorrect into stilted, impersonal prose they believe sounds formal. They write "I am writing to you today for the purpose of requesting..." when "I am requesting..." would do exactly the same work with more confidence. Teach them that professional writing is clear and direct, not verbose and distant. Watch for passive constructions and throat-clearing openers — those are the signals of a student who has confused length with formality.

misconceptions.title: The Format Is the Assignment
misconceptions.body: Students who learn the template treat it as the whole task — they reproduce the correct heading, salutation, and close while writing a body paragraph that says nothing. The structural elements exist to support the message; without a clear purpose in the body, the format is costume without content. Grade the thinking inside the structure, not just the structure itself, and require students to articulate the purpose of their letter in one sentence before they draft.

misconceptions.title: All Emails Are Informal Correspondence
misconceptions.body: Students conflate the medium with the register — because email is a casual platform in their experience, they assume all emails are informal. A professional email follows the same conventions as a formal letter: a clear subject line, formal salutation, structured body, and appropriate close. Teach them that register is determined by context and relationship, not by the app they're using to send it.

assignments.name: Side-by-Side Drafts
assignments.body: Students write two letters on the same topic — one professional (to an employer, editor, or official) and one friendly (to a peer or family member). They then annotate both drafts, marking every place where structural or tonal choices differ and explaining why. The comparison makes register choices visible rather than intuitive.

assignments.name: Register Rescue
assignments.body: Provide students with four poorly matched letters — a casual email to a college admissions office, an over-formal letter to a grandparent, etc. Students revise each letter to correct the register, then write a one-paragraph explanation of what was wrong and why it matters.

assignments.name: Real Stakes Correspondence
assignments.body: Students identify an actual letter they need to write — a scholarship application, a letter to a local official, a thank-you to a mentor — and draft it for real submission. Peer review is structured around the professional conventions checklist before final draft. The external audience raises the stakes for structural accuracy.

assignments.name: Correspondence Analysis
assignments.body: Students read two published letters (one professional, one friendly — historical or contemporary) and identify the structural markers that signal each register: salutation form, sentence-level formality, body organization, and close. They produce a brief written analysis connecting structure to purpose.

mastery: Selects the appropriate register — professional or friendly — based on context, audience, and purpose without being prompted.
Executes the structural conventions of both formats correctly: heading, salutation, organized body, and close appropriate to each type.
Sustains tonal consistency throughout the letter — neither slipping into casual phrasing in a professional piece nor stiffening unnecessarily in a friendly one.
Writes body paragraphs with a clear, communicable purpose — not just structural placeholders that fulfill the form without delivering a message.
Revises correspondence for register accuracy, catching mismatches between tone and context during the editing process.
notes: Vertical alignment: English II establishes correspondence as a genre expectation; English III holds the same expectation, which means the ramp is not in the standard itself but in the quality of execution — greater control of register, more deliberate structural choices, stronger integration of purpose and tone. English IV maintains this, adding complexity of context (professional correspondence for increasingly high-stakes audiences). Teachers should not re-teach the format — they should push at the register question, which is where English III-level thinking lives.

DOK 2 justification: the primary cognitive demand is applying known structural conventions to a new context; the deeper tonal control required for register matching edges toward DOK 3 for stronger writers. Assign the Side-by-Side draft to surface DOK 3 thinking.

STAAR note: correspondence is not a direct STAAR composition prompt genre, but its conventions surface in paired-passage analysis items (author's purpose, audience awareness, tonal choices) and in editing items where register-appropriate word choice is tested. Question stems above mirror STAAR's phrasing for reading a correspondent's choices as craft decisions.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Compose Literary Analysis Using Genre Characteristics and Craft
expectation: Compose literary analysis using genre characteristics and craft.
overview: Literary analysis in English III is not a book report with opinions — it is an evidence-driven argument about how a text works. Students must move beyond what a text says to explain how genre conventions and specific craft decisions produce meaning, and they must produce that explanation in a form that itself demonstrates the conventions of analytical writing.
studentFriendly: You can write a structured, evidence-based argument that explains not just what a literary text means, but how its genre, form, and specific craft choices — diction, structure, point of view, imagery — create that meaning.
questionStems: Which sentence from the student's essay BEST states a claim about how the author's craft contributes to the meaning of the text?
In paragraph X of the essay, the writer uses evidence from the poem MOST likely to —
Which revision would MOST improve the literary analysis in paragraph X?
The student's use of the phrase [phrase] in paragraph X suggests the writer MOST likely intends to —
Which sentence from the essay BEST demonstrates the writer's analysis of a genre characteristic?
Read paragraphs X and Y from the student essay. How does the organizational structure MOST contribute to the argument the writer is building?
Which of the following would BEST strengthen the analysis in paragraph X of the essay?
The writer includes the detail from paragraph X of the source text MOST likely to support the claim that —
Which sentence from the essay BEST shows the student distinguishing between what the text says and how the author achieves that effect?
Based on the student's essay, the writer would MOST likely agree that the author's use of [craft element] in the text —

misconceptions.title: Summary Is Not Analysis
misconceptions.body: Students retell the plot or paraphrase the poem and call it analysis. Watch for paragraphs that begin with "In this story..." and proceed to narrate events without making a claim about craft. Teach them the diagnostic question: "Am I telling my reader WHAT happens, or am I telling them HOW and WHY the author built it that way?" If the answer is WHAT, they're summarizing. Analysis requires a claim about craft, evidence, and an explanation of the connection — all three, every time.

misconceptions.title: The Thesis Doesn't Need to Be an Argument
misconceptions.body: Students write thesis statements like "The author uses imagery, symbolism, and diction in this story." That's a table of contents, not a claim. Teach them that a literary analysis thesis must assert a relationship between craft and meaning — something a reasonable reader could dispute. "The author's use of fragmented syntax mirrors the narrator's psychological unraveling, forcing the reader to experience disorientation rather than observe it" is an argument. A list of devices is not.

misconceptions.title: Quoting Is the Same as Proving
misconceptions.body: Students drop a quotation and move on, treating the text as self-explanatory. They've cited evidence; they've done analysis. Wrong. Teach them that the quotation is not the argument — the explanation of the quotation is. The sentence AFTER the quote does the analytical work: what is this doing, how is it doing it, and why does it matter for the meaning of the whole? If that sentence is missing, the analysis isn't there yet.

misconceptions.title: Genre Characteristics Are Just Background Information
misconceptions.body: Students mention that the text is a sonnet or a short story as orientation for the reader, then proceed to ignore how those genre conventions shape meaning. Push them deeper: the volta in a Shakespearean sonnet is not trivia — it is an architectural decision. The compression of flash fiction is not a fact about word count — it is a constraint that produces specific effects. Genre is an analytical lens, not a label.

misconceptions.title: Literary Analysis Is About What the Text Means to You
misconceptions.body: When students learned personal response in earlier grades, they learned that their interpretation matters. Now that's working against them. They write "I think the author is saying..." or "This poem made me feel..." and treat their emotional response as the endpoint of analysis. Reframe it: their interpretation must be grounded in and limited by the text's craft decisions. The analysis is not about what the student feels — it is about what the text does, and how, and why.

assignments.name: Craft Claim Ladder
assignments.body: Students read a short poem or prose excerpt (1–2 pages) and write a series of three thesis statements in escalating specificity: one vague ("uses imagery effectively"), one better ("uses imagery to establish tone"), one analytical ("uses images of mechanical repetition to suggest the speaker's alienation has become indistinguishable from routine"). Discuss as a class which thesis would produce the strongest full essay and why.

assignments.name: Reverse-Engineer a Published Analysis
assignments.body: Students read a short published literary analysis (from a journal, a quality review, or a model essay). They identify: the thesis, the genre characteristics named, the craft elements analyzed, the ratio of summary to analysis in each paragraph. Then they write a one-page meta-analysis of how the writer does the job. This teaches them to see the architecture before they build their own.

assignments.name: Full Literary Analysis Essay
assignments.body: Students compose a full literary analysis (3–5 pages) of a text read in class. Requirements: arguable thesis about how genre characteristics and craft produce meaning, minimum four body paragraphs each anchored to a specific craft element, evidence embedded and explained (not dropped), conclusion that extends the argument rather than repeating it. Grade with a rubric that weights claim quality and analytical depth over correctness of interpretation.

assignments.name: Paragraph Autopsy
assignments.body: Teacher presents three student body paragraphs (anonymized, from previous years or fabricated) ranging from summary to partial analysis to full analysis. Students diagnose each paragraph's analytical depth and prescribe specific revisions for the weaker ones. This surfaces the spectrum before students draft their own.

mastery: Produces an arguable thesis that asserts a specific relationship between craft decisions and textual meaning — not a list of devices and not a plot summary.
Integrates quoted evidence with explanation that identifies the craft at work and connects it to the essay's central claim.
Analyzes genre characteristics (structural conventions, formal constraints, narrative modes) as meaning-making choices, not background context.
Maintains analytical voice throughout — does not slide into personal response, summary, or device-labeling without explanation.
Organizes the essay so each paragraph builds the argument forward rather than presenting isolated observations.
notes: Vertical ramp from English II: English II Std 10(E) also requires literary analysis, but English III raises the ceiling by pairing it explicitly with genre characteristics as an analytical lens — students must name and deploy conventions of the genre as part of the argument, not merely produce analysis of a literary text in general.

Vertical ramp to English IV: English IV Std 10(E) continues to require literary analysis and is expected to produce writing at a level of sophistication commensurate with college readiness — English III is the critical preparation year.

DOK 4 justification: composing original literary analysis requires sustained, evidence-based argumentation that synthesizes close reading with genre knowledge and is evaluated against disciplinary standards — this is extended thinking, not retrieval.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog in the sense of a standalone passage-based question; STAAR assesses writing holistically on the composition portion. Question stems above are modeled on revision/editing tasks and analytical reading tasks that mirror the sub-skills this TEK demands.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Compose Rhetorical Analysis Using Genre Characteristics and Craft
expectation: Compose rhetorical analysis using genre characteristics and craft.
overview: This is where reading analytically and writing analytically become the same act. Students aren't just identifying devices — they're building a sustained written argument about how a text's choices create meaning, using the rhetorical analysis essay as its own disciplined genre with its own conventions and demands.
studentFriendly: You write a formal essay that argues HOW and WHY a text works — not just what it says, but how the author's specific choices of language, structure, and appeal shape what the audience thinks and feels.
questionStems: Which sentence from the student essay BEST establishes the writer's central claim about the author's rhetorical strategy?
The writer's use of textual evidence in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Which revision to the underlined sentence in paragraph X would MOST improve the clarity of the rhetorical analysis?
The student's analysis in paragraph X is MOST limited because it —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the writer's organizational structure MOST effectively contributes to —
Which sentence BEST supports the inference that the writer understands the author's intended audience?
The writer's conclusion in paragraph X suggests that the rhetorical strategy analyzed is MOST likely intended to —
Which detail from the selection BEST develops the writer's claim that the author uses [device] to establish credibility?
Read the following sentences from the student essay. The transition between paragraphs X and Y MOST nearly suggests that —
Which revision would MOST strengthen the writer's analysis of the author's use of diction in paragraph X?

misconceptions.title: Rhetorical Analysis Is Just Finding Devices
misconceptions.body: Students learn to name — ethos, anaphora, parallelism — and think that naming is analyzing. A list of devices is not an argument. Teach them that every device identification must answer the question "so what does this DO to the audience?" — if the sentence stops at the label, it's a scavenger hunt, not an analysis.

misconceptions.title: Summary Sneaks In When Argument Gets Hard
misconceptions.body: When students can't articulate HOW a move works, they retreat to what the text SAYS. Watch for paragraphs that begin with "In this passage, the author talks about…" — that's the tell. The analysis has collapsed into paraphrase. Push them to move the verb: not "the author talks about" but "the author frames X as Y in order to…"

misconceptions.title: The Genre Has No Rules So Anything Goes
misconceptions.body: Students treat rhetorical analysis as a free-form response because it doesn't have a rigid five-paragraph skeleton. In fact, the genre has tight conventions: a claim about a rhetorical choice, evidence from the text, analysis of effect, connection to purpose and audience. Teach them the genre is structured — just not pre-stamped.

misconceptions.title: Every Essay Needs a Thesis That Says "The Author Uses Three Devices"
misconceptions.body: Students over-apply the five-paragraph template and produce theses that read as a device checklist ("Smith uses ethos, pathos, and logos to persuade"). This produces an outline, not a claim. The thesis of a rhetorical analysis makes an argument about the dominant rhetorical effect of the text and the choices that produce it — not a promise to enumerate three tools.

misconceptions.title: Quoting More Proves More
misconceptions.body: Students equate long quotations with strong evidence. They drop a six-line block quote and then move on, as if the text analyzed itself. Teach them that analysis is the ratio that matters: a short, precise quotation followed by three times as much close unpacking is stronger than a long quote with one thin sentence behind it.

assignments.name: The Single-Move Analysis
assignments.body: Students select ONE rhetorical move from a published speech or essay — one metaphor, one structural shift, one appeal — and write a single tight paragraph that fully unpacks its effect. This isolates analysis from the pressure of the full essay and forces depth over breadth.

assignments.name: Argument Before Draft
assignments.body: Before drafting, students write a one-paragraph memo to themselves: What is my central claim about this text's rhetoric? Who is the author's audience? What is the dominant effect? Only after the memo is approved do they begin drafting. Cuts summary-as-analysis before it starts.

assignments.name: Full Rhetorical Analysis Essay
assignments.body: Students compose a 3–5 page analysis of a speech, editorial, or public argument of their choosing. Thesis states a claim about rhetorical effect. Body paragraphs are organized around rhetorical moves, not text chronology. Works-cited or in-text citation required. Peer review focused on the analysis-to-quote ratio.

assignments.name: Analysis Swap and Annotate
assignments.body: Students exchange drafts with a partner, who reads and highlights every place where the writer names a device but does not analyze its effect. Highlighted sentences get a margin note: "Name → Effect?" Writer revises using the annotations. Class debrief on the most common gaps.

assignments.name: Mentor Text Deconstruction
assignments.body: Students read a published rhetorical analysis (AP Lang sample, Atlantic essay, scholarly close reading) and annotate it for the genre moves: Where is the claim? How is evidence introduced? How is effect explained? How does the writer connect device to purpose? Students then model one paragraph of their own essay on the structure they found.

mastery: Produces a thesis that makes an arguable claim about the text's dominant rhetorical effect — not a list of devices present.
Integrates textual evidence as anchor for analysis rather than as substitute for it; quotations are precise and the analysis that follows them is longer than the quote itself.
Moves from device identification to audience effect to authorial purpose within the same body paragraph — all three levels present in each analytical move.
Maintains the conventions of the rhetorical analysis genre: consistent analytical stance, text-based reasoning, organized around rhetorical choices rather than text sequence.
Distinguishes between what the text says and what the text does — summary never substitutes for analysis in the final draft.
notes: Vertical alignment: English II Std 10F asks students to compose a rhetorical analysis — English III sustains and deepens the same expectation with increased complexity of source texts and rhetorical situation. English IV is expected to extend this skill to comparative rhetorical analysis and longer-form scholarly writing. The ramp is in text complexity and argumentative sophistication, not genre newness.

DOK 4 justification: students must construct an original, sustained written argument that evaluates rhetorical choices across a whole text, defend that argument with textual evidence, and demonstrate command of a disciplined analytical genre — extended reasoning across all four DOK levels embedded.
The genre conventions of rhetorical analysis are themselves a TEKS target; instruction must treat the form explicitly, not assume students will absorb it from reading models alone.

STAAR directly tests the reading side of rhetorical analysis (Author's Purpose and Craft, Strand 8); the writing of analysis appears most visibly in the expository and argumentative writing prompts on the on-demand assessment. Stems above mirror how STAAR frames essay revision tasks and author's-craft questions on the reading section.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Develop Questions for Formal and Informal Inquiry
expectation: Develop questions for formal and informal inquiry.
overview: Question development is the hinge on which all research turns — if the question is bad, nothing downstream can rescue it. English III students need to understand the difference between a question that drives a genuine investigation and one that merely confirms what they already believe, and to calibrate that question appropriately for the scope and register of the research task.
studentFriendly: You can look at a topic you don't fully understand and generate a research question that is specific enough to actually answer, honest enough to follow wherever the evidence goes, and matched to the kind of research — quick background check or formal paper — you actually need to do.
questionStems: Which research question would BEST guide an investigation into the effects of [topic] on [subject]?
A student wants to write a formal research paper about [topic]. Which question would MOST likely lead to a focused, arguable thesis?
Read the following background paragraph. Which question is BEST supported by the information provided and would MOST effectively extend the inquiry?
Which research question is TOO broad to answer in a single paper about [topic]?
A student conducting informal inquiry about [topic] develops the following question. Which revision MOST improves the question's specificity and researchability?
Based on paragraph X, which question would MOST likely guide the next stage of the student's research?
Which question requires the researcher to MOST likely synthesize multiple sources rather than rely on a single reference?
A student's initial research question is [question]. After reviewing preliminary sources, which revised question BEST reflects a more focused and analytical inquiry?
Which question BEST distinguishes between a topic and a researchable problem?
The student in paragraph X develops a research question based on a personal observation. Which revision would MOST effectively make the question suitable for formal academic inquiry?

misconceptions.title: A Research Question Is Just a Narrowed Topic
misconceptions.body: Students conflate narrowing a topic with forming a question. They write "My research is about the opioid crisis in rural Texas" and think they've done it. Teach them that a research question demands a gap — something unknown, contested, or causally murky. The question has to be answerable through investigation, not just description. Push them: what do you NOT yet know about this, and why does that matter?

misconceptions.title: Formal and Informal Inquiry Use the Same Kind of Question
misconceptions.body: Students treat a Google-search curiosity question and a thesis-driving research question as interchangeable. They aren't. Informal inquiry questions are often open, exploratory, and broad by design — they help you figure out what you're looking at. Formal inquiry questions are precise, debatable, and scoped to what a paper can actually prove. Teach them to name which mode they're operating in before they write a single question.

misconceptions.title: A Good Research Question Already Has an Answer
misconceptions.body: Students draft questions they already know the answer to — or worse, questions they need to come out a certain way. Watch for questions like "Doesn't social media cause depression in teenagers?" These are arguments wearing question clothes. The question has to be genuinely open to them before they start. If they already know the answer, it's a thesis, not a question.

misconceptions.title: Broader Questions Are More Impressive
misconceptions.body: Students equate scope with rigor. They think "What caused all war throughout human history?" is more intellectually serious than "How did the U.S. government's framing of the War on Terror shift between 2001 and 2008?" Flip this: narrow questions are harder to write and more honest about what one researcher can actually do. Grade specificity as a virtue, not a limitation.

misconceptions.title: You Ask the Question Once and Then You're Done
misconceptions.body: Students treat question development as a one-time event at the start of a project. In reality, preliminary research should revise the question — sometimes dramatically. Teach them that discovering your first question was too broad, too narrow, or already answered is progress, not failure. Build in a formal question-revision checkpoint partway through the research process so students see this as expected, not as a sign they got it wrong.

assignments.name: Question Ladder
assignments.body: Students receive a broad topic and must generate five questions of increasing specificity, from "encyclopedia-level" to "researchable paper-level." They annotate each rung with what kind of source would answer it and approximately how long the answer would take to produce. The activity makes scope visible.

assignments.name: Question Autopsy
assignments.body: Students receive four student-generated research questions — one too broad, one too narrow, one already-answered, and one genuinely researchable — without labels. They diagnose each, explain what's wrong or right, and revise the weak ones. Teaches diagnostic precision before they write their own.

assignments.name: Formal vs. Informal Side-by-Side
assignments.body: Students pick one topic and write two questions: one for informal inquiry (background exploration, no paper required) and one for formal inquiry (thesis-driving, paper-ready). They write a paragraph explaining what changes between the two and why. Surfaces the register distinction explicitly.

assignments.name: Question Revision Log
assignments.body: After initial research, students submit their original question alongside a revised version and a one-paragraph explanation of what they found that made them revise. Graded on honesty and specificity of the explanation — not on whether the original question was "good."

assignments.name: Peer Question Workshop
assignments.body: Students exchange research questions in pairs and ask each other three questions: Can this be answered in the length of the assigned paper? Is there a genuine debate here, or is the answer already settled? What would count as evidence for or against this? The conversation often does more diagnostic work than any rubric.

mastery: Produces a research question that is specific enough to answer within the assigned scope and open enough to require genuine investigation.
Distinguishes between a question appropriate for informal background inquiry and one that drives a formal, arguable research paper.
Revises an initial research question based on preliminary source reading, explaining what the sources revealed that required the revision.
Identifies when a question is actually an argument in disguise and rewrites it to restore genuine openness.
Articulates what kind of evidence would be needed to answer the question — before research begins — as a test of whether the question is workable.
notes: Vertical ramp: English II Std 11A asks students to develop questions for inquiry; English III holds the same expectation but within a course that demands sustained formal research across complex informational and argumentative texts — the rigor comes from the surrounding context, not a word change in the standard. English IV continues the same expectation with the additional weight of college-readiness stakes. Watch for teachers who treat this as a repeat skill and skip the instruction; the student population in English III needs explicit re-teaching of question quality, not a reminder that they learned it before.

DOK 3 justification: developing a workable research question requires students to assess the scope, debatability, and answerability of a question — a strategic and abstract cognitive task that extends beyond recall or skill application.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog in the traditional sense; however, STAAR tests research-adjacent reasoning in the Expository and Argumentative composition prompts, where students implicitly frame a researchable claim. Question stems above are modeled on how STAAR embeds research process reasoning in reading and writing contexts.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Critique the Research Process at Each Step
expectation: Critique the research process at each step to implement changes as needs occur and are identified.
overview: Most students treat research as a march from assignment to bibliography — they do not revisit, recalibrate, or rethink. This TEK demands something harder: metacognitive audit at every stage, with the authority to actually change course. The skill is not just finding good sources; it is catching the moment your question, your search, or your synthesis went sideways — and fixing it before the paper is due.
studentFriendly: You can stop at any point in a research project, evaluate what is and is not working, and make real changes to your question, your sources, or your approach — instead of pushing forward with a plan that is already broken.
questionStems: Read the excerpt from a student's research log. Which revision BEST reflects an appropriate response to the problem the student identified at this stage of the research process?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which step in the research process MOST likely needs to be revisited before the student can proceed?
The student's note in paragraph X suggests that the original research question was —
Which of the following changes would MOST effectively address the gap in evidence identified in paragraph X?
A student discovers midway through research that two of her primary sources contradict each other. Which action MOST likely represents an appropriate next step in the research process?
Based on the research log entry in paragraph X, the student MOST likely needs to —
Which sentence from the research notes BEST supports the conclusion that the student's search strategy required revision?
The researcher's decision described in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
A student realizes his thesis cannot be supported by the sources he has collected. Which revision to his research process would MOST likely resolve this problem?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which action BEST demonstrates that a researcher is effectively critiquing her own process?

misconceptions.title: Research Is a Straight Line
misconceptions.body: Students believe that research moves in one direction — question, search, sources, draft, done. Watch for students who collect five sources and immediately start writing, regardless of whether those sources actually address the question they ended up with. Teach them that the research process is recursive by design: the answer you find changes the question you should be asking, and that is not failure — that is how inquiry actually works.

misconceptions.title: Changing Course Means Starting Over
misconceptions.body: When students recognize a problem with their process, they panic and either push forward with what they have or scrap everything. Neither response is the right one. Teach them that a targeted correction — narrowing a search term, swapping one weak source for a stronger one, refocusing a thesis — is a sign of rigor, not a sign of failure. Critique without paralysis is the skill.

misconceptions.title: Self-Critique Is Only for the End
misconceptions.body: Students treat reflection as a final-step activity: they evaluate their project after it is submitted, not while it is still open. This TEK requires critique at EACH step — before the search, during source evaluation, while drafting, and while synthesizing. Push them to build a standing habit of asking "Is this still serving my question?" every time they shift tasks, not just once at the end.

misconceptions.title: A Good Source Is Enough Justification to Keep It
misconceptions.body: Students resist cutting sources they worked hard to find, even when the source no longer fits the evolved project. Watch for bibliographies that are padded with sources that do not actually appear in the paper — students are keeping them to justify their effort. Teach them that a source that does not serve the argument is dead weight, and removing it is a research decision, not a loss.

misconceptions.title: Critique Means Criticism of External Materials Only
misconceptions.body: Students apply analytical scrutiny outward — to their sources, to other people's arguments — but rarely turn it on their own process. They will happily identify flaws in a published article but have no vocabulary for describing what went wrong in their own search strategy. Teach them process-audit language: "My initial question was too broad," "I anchored on the first three sources I found," "I did not check for counterarguments until the draft stage." Naming the misstep precisely is the first move toward fixing it.

assignments.name: Research Process Log
assignments.body: Students keep a running log across the full research unit, recording at each step what they set out to do, what they actually found, and what they decided to change and why. The log is graded alongside the final product — a strong paper with a thin log scores lower than a strong paper with documented, honest process critique.

assignments.name: Mid-Project Audit Conference
assignments.body: Halfway through the research unit, students meet individually with the teacher for a five-minute conference. They bring their current question, source list, and one written sentence explaining what is not working yet. The teacher asks one question; the student must name a specific change they will make before the next class.

assignments.name: Pivot Memo
assignments.body: When a student changes direction in their research — shifts the question, drops a source, reconceives the thesis — they write a one-paragraph memo explaining what triggered the change, what evidence led them there, and what the new direction is. These memos become part of the process portfolio.

mastery: Identifies specific problems at each stage of research — vague question, weak source, unsupported claim — and names them precisely rather than generally.
Makes targeted, documented adjustments to the research process in response to identified gaps, without abandoning the project or ignoring the problem.
Distinguishes between a research plan that needs a small correction and one that needs a substantive reconsideration of the central question.
Produces a research log or process document that shows real decision-making over time, not a retroactive summary written after the fact.
Evaluates their own search strategy and source selection with the same critical lens they apply to external sources.
notes: Vertical ramp from English II: English II Std 11B asks students to "modify the research plan as needed." English III sharpens the demand — "critique" replaces "modify," requiring evaluation against standards at each step, not just responsive adjustment when something obviously breaks.
Vertical ramp to English IV: English IV Std 11B is expected to extend this same iterative critique to independent, self-directed inquiry, where no external checkpoints are built in.

DOK 4 justification: students must evaluate their own process against implicit standards for sound inquiry, identify where and why a step failed, and design a response — extended reasoning across a metacognitive task.

No direct STAAR analog: this TEK operates at the process level and is not tested in isolation on STAAR. Question stems above mirror how STAAR tests research-process reasoning through scenarios — research log excerpts, student decision descriptions, and process-correction questions. Named here per doctrine.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Develop and Revise a Research Plan
expectation: Develop and revise a plan.
overview: Research fails at the planning stage more than the writing stage. This TEK is about teaching students that a research plan is a living document — built with intention, then broken and rebuilt as new evidence reshapes the question. The point is not to produce a plan; it's to practice disciplined revision of one.
studentFriendly: You can build a clear, workable research plan — with a focused question, a realistic strategy for finding sources, and a timeline — and then actually revise it when the evidence takes you somewhere you didn't expect.
questionStems: Read paragraphs X–Y. Which revision to the student's research plan MOST likely improves the focus of the inquiry?
Based on the information in paragraph X, which change to the research question would BEST narrow the scope of the investigation?
The student revises the research plan in paragraph X MOST likely because —
Which sentence from the passage BEST supports the conclusion that the student's original plan was too broad?
How does the revision described in paragraph X contribute to the overall effectiveness of the research plan?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the student's decision to change the research focus MOST likely suggests that —
Which detail from the passage MOST clearly indicates that the original research plan needed revision?
The student adds a new step to the research plan in paragraph X in order to —
Which sentence BEST describes the relationship between the revised research question and the sources identified in paragraph Y?
Based on the passage, what is the MOST likely effect of revising the research plan before beginning data collection?

misconceptions.title: The Plan Is Just a Box to Check Before the Real Work
misconceptions.body: Students treat the research plan as a bureaucratic hurdle — something you submit so the teacher sees you started. Teach them that the plan IS research: the decisions made during planning (scope, angle, source types, timeline) determine whether the inquiry is survivable. A student who phones in the plan will hit a wall mid-project with no way to diagnose why.

misconceptions.title: Revision Means the First Plan Was Wrong
misconceptions.body: When students are told to revise their plan, they feel like they failed. They interpret revision as correction rather than refinement. Push them to reframe: a plan that needed no revision is a plan that didn't encounter real research. If new evidence didn't shift anything, they weren't reading carefully enough.

misconceptions.title: Narrowing Is Always the Right Move
misconceptions.body: Students default to narrowing the research question whenever they feel stuck, assuming specificity is always better. Watch for students who narrow themselves into a dead end — a question so specific that no credible sources exist. Teach them that revision sometimes means broadening the angle or pivoting the framing entirely, not just slicing the topic thinner.

misconceptions.title: A Good Plan Has No Gaps
misconceptions.body: Students try to plan for everything before they begin, filling in every cell of the template to look thorough. Real planning tolerates uncertainty. Teach them that identifying what you don't know yet — and naming it explicitly in the plan — is itself a research skill. The gap is not a weakness; it's the next question.

misconceptions.title: Revising the Plan and Revising the Paper Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students conflate plan revision with late-stage drafting decisions and blend the two together. Teach them that plan revision happens BEFORE significant writing begins — it's about adjusting the inquiry framework, not cleaning up the prose. Watch for students who produce a "revised plan" that is actually a description of what they already wrote.

assignments.name: Plan Draft + Stress Test
assignments.body: Students submit an initial research plan. Pairs swap plans and try to identify three places where the plan would break down in the real world — insufficient sources, scope too wide, unclear guiding question. Each partner writes a revision memo, then authors revise their own plan in response.

assignments.name: Revision Log
assignments.body: Students maintain a running log alongside their research plan documenting every change they make and why — what evidence or dead end triggered it. At the end of the unit, they write a one-page reflection on how the final plan differs from the first draft and what that difference reveals about their inquiry.

assignments.name: Before and After
assignments.body: Teacher provides three anonymous "before" research plans (weak, mid, strong — from previous years or fabricated). Students revise the weak plan as a class, the mid plan in pairs, and the strong plan individually, each time writing a sentence explaining the rationale for each revision.

mastery: Produces an initial research plan with a focused guiding question, identified source types, and a realistic timeline — not a vague list of topics.
Revises the plan in response to specific evidence or dead ends, and can articulate what triggered each revision rather than revising arbitrarily.
Distinguishes between narrowing, broadening, and pivoting as revision strategies — and chooses among them based on what the evidence is actually doing.
Treats gaps and unknowns in the plan as navigable rather than disqualifying — names them and builds steps to address them.
Demonstrates that the final plan is a substantively different document from the first draft, with revision decisions traceable to the inquiry process.
notes: Vertical alignment: English II Std 11C asks students to develop a research plan; English III adds *revise* as an explicit requirement — the skill ramps from producing a plan to treating it as iterative. English IV will push students toward independent, self-directed inquiry with minimal scaffolding.

The TEA expectation is compressed ("develop and revise a plan") but implies the full arc of research planning: guiding question formation, source strategy, timeline, and iterative adjustment as inquiry progresses.

DOK 3 justification: strategic thinking is required — students must make decisions about scope, sequence, and revision direction that are not procedurally determined. DOK 4 is possible when plan revision is tied to an extended inquiry defense.

STAAR does not test research planning directly as a performance task; stems above mirror how STAAR tests planning and revision reasoning through embedded nonfiction passages describing student research scenarios.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Modify the Major Research Question to Refocus the Research Plan
expectation: Modify the major research question as necessary to refocus the research plan.
overview: Research rarely goes where you planned. This TEK asks students to do something counterintuitive — to recognize when their original question is no longer serving the evidence and to revise it deliberately rather than forcing the sources to answer the wrong question. It's a metacognitive checkpoint built into the research process itself.
studentFriendly: You know how to tell when your original research question isn't working anymore — and you can rewrite it so your whole project gets back on track instead of grinding forward on a broken path.
questionStems: A student conducting research revises her major question from "How does social media affect teenagers?" to "How does social media use during school hours affect academic performance in high school students?" Which of the following BEST describes the purpose of this revision?
Based on the information in paragraphs X–Y, a researcher would MOST likely need to modify the major research question because —
A student's original research question is no longer supported by the available sources. Which revision to the research plan would MOST likely help refocus the inquiry?
Which statement BEST explains why a researcher might narrow a broad research question after beginning the research process?
Read the following research scenario in paragraphs X–Y. The researcher's decision to revise her question MOST likely reflects —
A student discovers that most available sources address a different aspect of his topic than his original question anticipated. Which action would MOST likely help him refocus his research plan?
Based on paragraphs X–Z, which revised research question would BEST align with the evidence the student has gathered?
The student's decision to broaden his research question in paragraph X suggests that he —
Which of the following MOST nearly describes the difference between a focused research question and one that is too broad to answer effectively?
A researcher realizes her question has shifted in scope after reviewing her sources. Which step would MOST likely come next in her research plan?

misconceptions.title: Changing the Question Means Starting Over
misconceptions.body: Students treat question revision as failure and panic — they've already written things, they've already found sources, changing now seems catastrophic. Teach them that revision is built into the research process on purpose; a question that grows with the evidence is a sign of real inquiry, not a sign of incompetence. Push them to see the difference between abandoning research and steering it.

misconceptions.title: The Original Question Is Always Too Broad
misconceptions.body: Students assume revision always means narrowing, so they reflexively tighten every question the first time they hit a bump. Watch for students who narrow themselves into a corner — a question so specific that no sources exist to answer it. Revision can go in either direction; the goal is fit between question and evidence, not automatic narrowing.

misconceptions.title: Once You Have Sources, You're Committed to Your Question
misconceptions.body: When students have a stack of sources, they resist changing the question because they're afraid the sources will "not count anymore." Reframe this: the sources drove the revision — they're still part of the project. A refined question usually keeps most of the original sources and adds focus, not a whole new bibliography.

misconceptions.title: Modifying the Question Is a One-Time Event
misconceptions.body: Students think research works linearly — question, then sources, then paper. Teach them that the question may shift more than once as research deepens. The first revision is rarely the last. A researcher who checks their question against accumulating evidence throughout the process is working the way real researchers actually work.

misconceptions.title: A Good Revised Question Looks Completely Different from the Original
misconceptions.body: Students sometimes overcorrect and rewrite the question so drastically that it bears no resemblance to the original topic — they've changed subjects, not refined focus. Watch for scope drift masquerading as revision. A well-modified question tightens or adjusts the angle of inquiry; it doesn't abandon the intellectual territory the student chose in the first place.

assignments.name: Question Audit
assignments.body: At the midpoint of a research unit, students pull out their original research question and write a one-page memo evaluating it against the sources they've gathered. They must identify one thing the question does well and one thing the evidence is forcing them to reconsider, then produce a revised question with a one-paragraph rationale.

assignments.name: Side-by-Side Comparison
assignments.body: Students submit both their original and revised research questions with annotations explaining what changed, why, and what it means for the direction of their project. Evaluated on clarity of reasoning, not on which version is "better."

assignments.name: Research Plan Redirect
assignments.body: Given a pre-written research scenario (student, topic, gathered sources), students diagnose what's wrong with the original question and write a modified version that better fits the available evidence. This works well as a quick formative task before students apply the skill to their own projects.

mastery: Identifies specific tension between an original research question and the evidence available, and articulates that tension in writing before revising.
Produces a modified question that demonstrably improves the fit between question and evidence — not just a cosmetic rewrite.
Adjusts the research plan (source list, angle, scope) to match the revised question rather than leaving the plan misaligned with the new direction.
Treats revision as a normal, recurring step in the research process rather than an emergency correction.
Distinguishes between a question that needs narrowing, one that needs broadening, and one that needs a different angle entirely.
notes: Vertical alignment: English II Std 11D asks students to refine the major research question as necessary. English III uses "modify" — a more deliberate, structurally-aware act — and ties it explicitly to refocusing the whole research plan, not just rewording the question. English IV 11D will expect students to apply this skill within more complex, discipline-specific inquiry contexts.

DOK 3 justification: students must evaluate the relationship between an evolving question and accumulated evidence, then make a reasoned decision about direction — strategic thinking beyond simple recall or skill application.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog in the reading/writing assessment; stems above mirror how STAAR tests research process skills through scenario-based questions, typically embedded in the writing or revision strands of the test.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Locate Relevant Sources
expectation: Locate relevant sources.
overview: Short on words, long on stakes. "Locate relevant sources" sounds mechanical, but the real skill is discriminating — knowing what counts as relevant to THIS inquiry, not just what's findable. Students who can run a Google search have not necessarily learned this TEK; students who can explain why a source earns a place in their research have.
studentFriendly: You can find sources that actually connect to your research question — not just sources about the same topic, but sources that give you what you specifically need to make your argument or answer your question.
questionStems: A student is researching the long-term economic effects of Hurricane Katrina. Which source would MOST likely be relevant to this inquiry?
Based on the research question in paragraph 1, which source listed in the bibliography BEST supports the student's central claim?
Which detail from Source 2 is MOST relevant to the student's argument in paragraph 3?
A student wants to locate sources about the health effects of microplastics on marine life. Which search strategy would MOST likely yield relevant results?
The student includes Source 4 in her research. Based on paragraphs 2–4, which statement BEST explains why this source is relevant to her inquiry?
Which source would MOST likely provide relevant evidence for a claim about federal housing policy after World War II?
Based on the student's thesis in paragraph 1, which of the following sources is LEAST relevant to her research?
A student conducting research on the effects of social media on adolescent mental health locates four sources. Which source is MOST relevant to the specific focus of her inquiry?
Which sentence from Source 3 provides evidence MOST directly relevant to the student's research question?
The student revises her research question in paragraph 5. Based on the revised question, which previously located source is no longer relevant?

misconceptions.title: "Relevant" Means "About the Same Topic"
misconceptions.body: Students treat relevance as a topic-matching exercise: if the source mentions climate change and the paper is about climate change, it counts. Teach them that relevance is relational — a source is relevant only when it connects to the specific angle of the inquiry. Watch for bibliographies packed with on-topic sources that never actually appear in the paper; that's the diagnostic.

misconceptions.title: More Sources Means Better Research
misconceptions.body: Students believe a longer works cited list signals thoroughness. The result is bibliographic padding — credible-looking sources that do no work in the paper. Reframe it: a relevant source earns its place by doing something in the argument. If they can't name what a source contributes, it's not relevant yet — it's just located.

misconceptions.title: The First Credible Result Is the Right Result
misconceptions.body: Students stop searching when they find something usable. The better move is triangulating — locating multiple relevant sources and choosing among them deliberately. Push them to explain not just why a source is relevant, but why it's MORE relevant than the alternatives they found and passed over.

misconceptions.title: Database Search and Research Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students conflate navigating a database with conducting research. Running a search and clicking on results is a logistics task; locating relevant sources requires evaluating what the search returns against the specific needs of the inquiry. Teach them that the search is the beginning — relevance is determined after reading, not after clicking.

misconceptions.title: Relevance Is Fixed Once You Locate the Source
misconceptions.body: Students lock their source list early and stop evaluating. As the inquiry develops, the research question sharpens — and a source that looked relevant at the start may no longer fit, while something initially skipped may now be exactly right. Teach them to revisit relevance judgments as the paper takes shape, not just at the moment of discovery.

assignments.name: Relevance Justification Memo
assignments.body: Students locate five sources for a research question and write a one-paragraph justification for each, explaining specifically how it connects to their inquiry — not just what it's about. Sources they cannot justify get cut.

assignments.name: The Relevance Audit
assignments.body: Students exchange research source lists with a partner. The partner reads each source title and abstract and identifies which sources they believe are relevant, which are marginal, and which seem off-target — without seeing the original justifications. Pairs then compare judgments and reconcile disagreements.

assignments.name: Shifting Focus, Shifting Sources
assignments.body: Give students a broad research question and a set of pre-located sources. Progressively narrow the question through three iterations. Students must re-evaluate which sources remain relevant after each narrowing and explain what changed.

assignments.name: Search Strategy Documentation
assignments.body: Before locating sources, students write down their search terms, databases, and the logic behind each choice. After locating, they reflect: which strategies yielded the most relevant results and why? The documentation becomes part of the research submission.

mastery: Identifies sources as relevant based on their connection to the specific inquiry — not just their subject area — and can articulate that connection explicitly.
Distinguishes between sources that are topically related and sources that are functionally relevant to the argument being built.
Revises source selections as the research question evolves, dropping sources that no longer fit and locating new ones that do.
Evaluates search results deliberately rather than defaulting to the first usable result, comparing relevance across multiple options.
Produces a source list in which every entry has a visible function in the finished work — no padding, no placeholders.
notes: Vertical alignment: English II Std 11E establishes the practice of locating relevant sources. English III holds the same expectation but embeds it in more complex, self-directed inquiry — students are expected to apply relevance judgment with greater independence and to revise source selections as the inquiry develops. English IV continues this trajectory toward sustained, original research.

DOK 2 justification: locating relevant sources requires applying a criterion (relevance to the inquiry) to evaluate and select from available options — beyond recall, but not yet the synthesis or evaluation that characterizes DOK 3 or 4. The higher-order work lives in adjacent TEKs (11C, 11D).

STAAR does not test source location directly as a performance task, but released research simulations embed relevance judgments in source-selection scenarios and bibliography evaluation items. Question stems above mirror that embedded testing format.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Synthesize Information from a Variety of Sources
expectation: Synthesize information from a variety of sources.
overview: Synthesis is the move that separates research from report-writing. Students don't stack quotes from different sources — they construct a new understanding that no single source contains. The hard part isn't finding the sources; it's subordinating them to a controlling idea the student built.
studentFriendly: You can read multiple sources on the same topic, find where they connect, contradict, and complement each other, and build one coherent argument or explanation that goes beyond what any single source says on its own.
questionStems: Which statement BEST synthesizes the central arguments presented in both selections?
Based on the information in paragraphs X and Y, what conclusion about [topic] can MOST likely be drawn from both sources?
The author of Selection 1 and the author of Selection 2 would MOST likely agree that —
Which sentence from Selection 2 BEST supports the claim made in paragraph X of Selection 1?
How does the evidence in paragraphs X–Y of Selection 2 contribute to an understanding of the argument in Selection 1?
With which statement would the authors of both selections MOST likely agree?
Based on both selections, what is the MOST likely reason researchers have [reached a conclusion/taken a position] about [topic]?
Which of the following BEST represents a new understanding that can be developed by reading both selections together?
The information in paragraph X of Selection 2 challenges the claim in paragraph Y of Selection 1 by —
Which sentence from Selection 1 or Selection 2 provides the MOST relevant evidence to support the inference that [synthesized idea]?

misconceptions.title: Synthesis Is Just Summary with Multiple Sources
misconceptions.body: Students treat synthesis as "summarize Source A, then summarize Source B, then summarize Source C." Watch for the paragraph that reads like a relay race — each source gets its lap, then passes to the next. Teach them: if you can delete one source and the paragraph still makes the same move, you haven't synthesized anything. True synthesis produces a claim that requires all the sources to hold.

misconceptions.title: The Sources Have to Agree
misconceptions.body: Students assume synthesis means finding consensus — they hunt for the point where all their sources overlap and camp there. Push them to work with tension. Two sources disagreeing on a mechanism while agreeing on a conclusion is more useful than three sources saying the same thing. Conflict between sources isn't a problem to resolve; it's evidence to explain.

misconceptions.title: More Sources Means Better Synthesis
misconceptions.body: Students pad bibliographies to signal thoroughness. A research essay with eight sources loosely referenced beats nothing, but a tight synthesis of three sources that the student actually understood is stronger. Teach them that synthesis is a relationship they build between sources — and you can't build a relationship with sources you've skimmed.

misconceptions.title: Their Own Thinking Doesn't Count
misconceptions.body: When students synthesize, they often vanish from their own paper. They arrange the sources and write transitions, but no original claim ever appears — just reported positions. Reframe: the student's job is to be the theorist. The sources are evidence for a claim the student is making, not a conversation the student is merely transcribing. If you can't find the student's voice in a synthesis, the assignment isn't done.

misconceptions.title: Cross-Source Connections Have to Be Obvious
misconceptions.body: Students only note the connections the sources name explicitly — when Author B cites Author A, students notice; when they share an underlying assumption neither names, students miss it. Train them to look for what sources take for granted. The invisible agreement between sources — the shared premise no one states — is often the most interesting synthesis move available.

assignments.name: Three-Source Fusion
assignments.body: Students read three curated sources on a contested issue. They produce a one-page synthesis that advances a claim no single source makes. The claim must appear in the first paragraph, and every source must contribute evidence — not just be mentioned. Graded on whether the claim requires all three sources to stand.

assignments.name: The Disagreement Map
assignments.body: Before writing, students complete a graphic organizer: one column per source, one row per key claim in each source. They mark agreements (same), disagreements (conflict), and gaps (one source addresses, others ignore). The synthesis must address at least one conflict and one gap. The map is turned in with the draft.

assignments.name: Source Autopsy
assignments.body: Students evaluate four sources on the same topic and decide which three to use for a synthesis essay — and which to cut, with a written rationale. The exercise forces them to think about what each source contributes and whether it contributes something different.

assignments.name: Synthesis Revision Lab
assignments.body: Students bring a draft research paragraph that is currently "source stacking." Pairs identify where synthesis is missing and rewrite one paragraph collaboratively. The revised paragraph must contain a student-generated claim that requires both sources.

mastery: Produces a controlling claim that cannot be found verbatim in any single source — it is generated by reading sources against each other.
Integrates evidence from multiple sources within a single paragraph or argument, rather than assigning one source to each paragraph.
Explains how sources relate — where they agree, disagree, or fill gaps in each other — rather than simply reporting each source's position.
Maintains a visible authorial voice that subordinates the sources rather than transcribing them.
Handles source conflict productively: names the disagreement, accounts for it, and uses it to complicate or strengthen the synthesis claim.
notes: Vertical ramp: English II requires students to synthesize sources into a cohesive argument; English III holds the same expectation but in the context of more complex and varied sources — primary documents, conflicting studies, cross-disciplinary texts. English IV will add the expectation that synthesis is sustained and evaluated across a full independent research project.
