DOK 4 justification: synthesis across a variety of sources requires extended reasoning, original claim construction, and cross-source evaluation — none of which can be accomplished through recall or single-source application.

This TEK has no direct STAAR passage-set analog at the item level, but paired-passage items on STAAR Grades 7–11 regularly test synthesis logic — "with which statement would both authors agree," "which evidence from Selection 2 best supports the claim in Selection 1." Those stem patterns reflect how synthesis is operationalized in standardized testing and are used here accordingly.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Examine Sources for Credibility, Bias, and Accuracy
expectation: Examine sources for credibility, bias, and accuracy.
overview: This is the gatekeeping TEK of the research strand — before students quote anything, they have to earn the right to use it. The job is teaching them to read a source as a critic, not a stenographer: who made this, what do they want, and how do we know whether any of it is true?
studentFriendly: You can pick up any source — a website, a study, an article — and figure out whether the author is trustworthy, whether they have an agenda shaping the information, and whether the facts they're presenting actually hold up.
questionStems: Based on the information in paragraph X, the author of Source 1 MOST likely has a background in —
Which detail from Source 2 BEST supports the conclusion that the source is biased toward —
A student researching [topic] would MOST likely question the credibility of Source 3 because —
Which sentence from Source 1 BEST reveals the author's purpose in presenting the information?
The author of Source 2 includes [statistic/claim] in paragraph X MOST likely to —
Based on paragraphs X–Y of Source 3, which statement BEST describes a limitation of this source's accuracy?
Which piece of evidence from Source 1 would MOST likely be questioned by a reader evaluating the source's credibility?
A student notices that Source 2 omits information about [counterargument/data]. This omission MOST likely suggests —
Which source would BEST support an argument about [topic] with the LEAST amount of potential bias?
The use of [word/phrase] in paragraph X of Source 1 MOST likely indicates that the author —

misconceptions.title: Published Means Credible
misconceptions.body: Students assume that if something was printed, posted on an official-looking website, or cited by another source, it passes the credibility test. Watch for students who list ".org" or ".edu" as automatic proof of reliability — teach them that domain extensions describe ownership, not rigor. Walk them through a partisan think-tank site with a .org suffix and ask them to find where the funding comes from.

misconceptions.title: Bias Makes a Source Worthless
misconceptions.body: When students identify bias, they often throw the source out entirely. Reframe this: a biased source is still evidence — of perspective, of argument, of what one side claims. Teach them to use biased sources as primary evidence of a viewpoint rather than as authoritative fact, and to acknowledge the bias explicitly in their writing rather than pretending it disqualifies the source from existing.

misconceptions.title: Accuracy Is Binary — Either Right or Wrong
misconceptions.body: Students treat accuracy like a light switch: a source is either accurate or it isn't. The harder lesson is that accuracy exists on a spectrum — outdated statistics were once accurate, cherry-picked data is technically correct but misleading, anecdotal evidence isn't false but isn't generalizable. Push them to ask not just "Is this true?" but "Is this complete, current, and representative?"

misconceptions.title: The Author's Credentials Are All That Matter
misconceptions.body: Students over-rely on author credentials as the sole marker of credibility — if the person has a degree or a title, the source clears the bar. Teach them that credentials are one signal, not the whole picture: a credentialed author writing outside their field, publishing in a non-peer-reviewed outlet, or funded by an interested party still requires scrutiny. The question is always whether the expertise, the venue, and the evidence align.

misconceptions.title: Evaluating Sources Is a One-Time Step Before Writing
misconceptions.body: Students treat source evaluation as a checkbox done at the start of research — they vet sources once, then move on and trust them completely. Watch for students who quote a source in draft five that they've stopped interrogating since day one. Teach them that new claims inside a source require renewed scrutiny: a generally credible source can still include a single unsupported assertion that needs to be flagged.

assignments.name: Source Autopsy
assignments.body: Students receive three sources on the same topic — one highly credible, one biased, one inaccurate — without labels. They must write a one-page evaluation of each, identifying specific textual evidence for each dimension (credibility, bias, accuracy) before the class compares findings.

assignments.name: The Funding Trail
assignments.body: Students pick a source from their current research project and trace its funding, affiliation, and publication history. They write a paragraph explaining how that context should affect how a reader uses the source — and whether they are still using it in their own paper and why.

assignments.name: Bias Spectrum Sort
assignments.body: Provide five sources on a contested topic across the political or ideological spectrum. Students place them on a bias spectrum with written justification for each placement, then discuss: which sources could work together in an argument, and what would need to be disclosed about each?

assignments.name: Credibility Cross-Check
assignments.body: Students find a specific claim from a source in their research and attempt to verify it using two independent sources. They write a brief report: Did the claim hold up? Was it nuanced differently in other sources? What did the cross-check reveal?

mastery: Identifies specific textual evidence — author credentials, publication venue, funding source, language choices — to support a judgment about credibility rather than relying on surface markers like domain name or appearance.
Distinguishes between a source that is biased and a source that is useless, and explains how a biased source can serve a legitimate research purpose when handled transparently.
Evaluates accuracy by checking claims across multiple independent sources and flags information that is outdated, cherry-picked, or unsupported rather than simply labeling it true or false.
Revisits source evaluations at multiple stages of the research process rather than treating initial vetting as final.
Articulates in writing how the credibility, bias, and accuracy of a source affects how — and whether — it should be used in their argument.
notes: Vertical ramp: English II establishes that students can examine sources for credibility, bias, and accuracy at a foundational level. English III expects that evaluation to be more deliberate — students are building extended research arguments, so the stakes of a weak source are higher and the scrutiny should be proportionally deeper. English IV moves toward independent scholarly research norms.

DOK 3 justification: evaluating a source against multiple criteria simultaneously — credibility, bias, AND accuracy — requires strategic reasoning and the ability to weigh conflicting evidence, pushing beyond simple recall or skill application.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is tested indirectly on STAAR through paired-passage research scenarios in which students must evaluate which source is more reliable, identify an author's purpose or bias, or select the BEST source for a given research question. Question stems above mirror that format.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Examine Sources for Faulty Reasoning
expectation: Examine sources for faulty reasoning such as post hoc-ad hoc, circular reasoning, red herring, and assumptions.
overview: Research literacy does not end at finding sources — students must audit the reasoning inside them. This TEK targets the logical scaffolding holding a source's argument together: if the reasoning is structurally broken, the conclusion collapses regardless of how credible the publication looks. Students learn to read as logicians, not just as consumers.
studentFriendly: When you evaluate a source for research, you check not just whether the facts check out, but whether the argument connecting those facts actually holds — because a source can be full of true statements and still reason its way to a wrong conclusion.
questionStems: Which sentence from the passage BEST illustrates an example of circular reasoning?
The author's claim in paragraph X relies MOST heavily on which assumption?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the author's argument is MOST weakened by —
Which statement from the passage is MOST likely an example of a red herring?
The author's conclusion in paragraph X MOST likely results from which logical error?
Which detail from the passage BEST supports the inference that the author's argument depends on an unexamined assumption?
In paragraph X, the author argues that because [Event A] occurred before [Event B], [Event A] caused [Event B]. This reasoning is MOST accurately described as —
Which sentence from the passage MOST clearly introduces information unrelated to the author's central argument?
The reasoning in paragraphs X–Y is MOST flawed because the author —
Which statement BEST identifies a weakness in the author's argument in paragraph X?

misconceptions.title: If the Source Is Published, the Logic Must Be Sound
misconceptions.body: Students treat publication as a proxy for valid reasoning — if it made it into a journal, a newspaper, or a book, someone must have checked it. Teach them that editorial review catches factual errors, not logical ones; peer review can miss structural fallacies entirely. The name on the masthead is not a substitute for reading the argument.

misconceptions.title: Post Hoc Is Just "Confusing Correlation and Causation"
misconceptions.body: Students learn the correlation-causation rule in science class and assume it covers post hoc reasoning entirely. It doesn't. Post hoc is specifically about sequence — the claim that A caused B because A came first. Push them to distinguish: the fallacy isn't that two things happen together, it's that one thing happening earlier is treated as proof that it caused the other. The difference matters when they write research.

misconceptions.title: Circular Reasoning Is Easy to Spot
misconceptions.body: Students think circular arguments are obvious — the author just restates the premise as the conclusion. The harder cases are the ones where the circle is large: the author builds through several paragraphs before landing back at the starting assumption, and the distance makes the loop invisible. Teach them to trace the argument backward: what is the conclusion resting on, and is that support independent of the conclusion?

misconceptions.title: A Red Herring Is Always Intentional Deception
misconceptions.body: Students assume red herrings are planted deliberately to mislead. Often they aren't — authors genuinely lose track of the argument and pursue interesting-but-unrelated points without realizing they've changed the subject. Watch for students who go looking for bad-faith manipulation; redirect them to the structural question: does this paragraph actually connect to the claim? Motive is irrelevant.

misconceptions.title: Assumptions Are Always a Flaw
misconceptions.body: Students see "assumption" on a list of logical fallacies and conclude that all assumptions are problems to eliminate. Teach them the distinction: every argument rests on assumptions, and reasonable ones are fine — the flaw is when the assumption is contested, hidden, or implausible and the author never acknowledges it. The skill is identifying which assumptions are doing unearned work, not cataloguing every unstated premise.

assignments.name: Fallacy Forensics
assignments.body: Students select two opinion pieces on the same topic from different sources. For each, they identify at least two logical errors by name, quote the passage where the fallacy appears, and explain in one sentence why the reasoning fails. The final paragraph compares which source reasons more soundly — and why that matters for research use.

assignments.name: Build the Bad Argument
assignments.body: Students write a short (one-page) persuasive piece that deliberately embeds one each of post hoc, circular reasoning, and a red herring. They then trade with a partner who must identify all three by name and location. The exercise of constructing fallacies builds recognition faster than just spotting them.

assignments.name: Source Audit Annotation
assignments.body: Students annotate a provided research-style article (teacher-selected for embedded logical errors) using a three-column margin key: quote the passage, name the fallacy, explain the error. Minimum five annotations. Debrief as a class — fallacies are often contested, and the discussion is the skill.

assignments.name: The Assumption Hunt
assignments.body: Students take any argument they've already accepted — something they believe — and excavate its assumptions. They list the three to five things the argument takes for granted without proving, then assess whether each assumption is reasonable or unexamined. The goal is transferring the skill from other people's arguments to their own.

mastery: Correctly identifies post hoc, circular reasoning, red herring, and unexamined assumptions by name in unfamiliar sources, with textual evidence.
Distinguishes between a weak argument and a fallacious one — and can explain why the distinction matters for research credibility.
Recognizes fallacies even when they are embedded in otherwise credible, well-sourced prose.
Applies the same scrutiny to sources that confirm their own position as to sources that challenge it.
Articulates why a specific logical error undermines a source's usefulness for research, not just that the error exists.
notes: Vertical alignment: English II introduces source evaluation broadly; English III specifies the fallacy types students must be able to name and locate. English IV expects students to address fallacies in their own research arguments — the skill moves from identification to self-correction across the progression.
DOK 3 justification: students must apply knowledge of fallacy types to novel sources, then reason about how a specific error undermines a specific argument — strategic thinking, not recall.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog in the oral language sense, but faulty reasoning in written argument passages is tested regularly on STAAR via author's purpose and craft questions; stems above reflect that framing.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Display Academic Citations and Use Source Materials Ethically
expectation: Display academic citations, including for paraphrased and quoted text, and use source materials ethically to avoid plagiarism.
overview: Citation is the technical surface of a deeper obligation: intellectual honesty. This TEK asks students to handle both — the mechanics of formatting and the ethics of attribution — because a student who can format a Works Cited page but still passes off paraphrased thinking as their own has learned the costume without the principle.
studentFriendly: You know how to give proper credit for both direct quotes and paraphrased ideas in a research paper, and you understand why that matters beyond just following a rule.
questionStems: A student writes the following passage without a citation. Based on the information in the selection, which detail from the passage MOST likely requires attribution to a source?
Which of the following citations BEST demonstrates correct academic formatting for a paraphrased idea taken from a secondary source?
Read the student's paragraph in lines X–Y. The student has used information from a source without attribution. Which revision MOST accurately corrects this error?
Which sentence from the student's draft MOST clearly indicates that the writer paraphrased rather than directly quoted a source?
Based on the research guidelines in the passage, a student who copies sentence structure from a source while changing only individual words is MOST likely guilty of —
Which of the following in-text citations BEST follows the conventions described in paragraph X of the style guide?
The student's Works Cited entry in lines X–Y is incomplete. Which element is MOST likely missing from this citation?
A student wants to use the idea expressed in paragraph X without quoting it directly. Which revision of the student's notes MOST effectively paraphrases this idea while avoiding plagiarism?
Read lines X–Y of the student's research paper. The writer's use of a direct quotation without a signal phrase MOST likely —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which statement BEST describes the difference between quoting and paraphrasing a source in academic writing?

misconceptions.title: Paraphrasing Means Changing a Few Words
misconceptions.body: Students believe that swapping out synonyms and scrambling sentence order constitutes paraphrasing. Watch for papers where every sentence maps directly onto a source sentence with vocabulary substitutions — this is patchwriting, and it is plagiarism regardless of whether a citation appears. Teach them that a real paraphrase starts with closing the source, processing the idea, and then writing from understanding.

misconceptions.title: A Citation at the End of the Paragraph Covers Everything
misconceptions.body: Students will drop one parenthetical citation at the end of a paragraph that contains four borrowed ideas and assume the debt is paid. Teach them that attribution must be localized — each borrowed fact, idea, or phrase needs its own anchor, whether that's a signal phrase, an in-text citation, or both. One citation for six sentences of borrowed thinking is still plagiarism.

misconceptions.title: If It's on the Internet, It's Free to Use Without Citing
misconceptions.body: Students conflate "publicly accessible" with "public domain" and skip citations for websites, social media posts, and news articles. Reframe this as: any idea that isn't yours requires a source, regardless of the medium. The format of the citation changes; the obligation does not.

misconceptions.title: Formatting a Works Cited Page Is the Hard Part
misconceptions.body: Students spend outsized energy getting the punctuation of a Works Cited entry exactly right and treat ethical source use as a formality. Push them to see the citation as the record of an intellectual transaction — the formatting signals to readers where to go to check the work, and that accountability function is what matters. A badly formatted citation that credits the right source is less problematic than a perfectly formatted entry for a source that wasn't actually used.

misconceptions.title: You Only Need to Cite Quotes
misconceptions.body: Students treat in-text citation as something triggered only by quotation marks. They paraphrase freely without attribution because "I put it in my own words." Teach them explicitly: the citation obligation is attached to the idea, not to the words. If the thought came from a source, the source gets credited — every time, for paraphrase and summary as much as for direct quotation.

assignments.name: Citation Audit
assignments.body: Students receive a one-page student-written research excerpt with six errors: uncited paraphrases, a missing in-text citation, a patchwritten sentence, and one Works Cited formatting error. They identify each error, name its type, and write a corrected version.

assignments.name: Source to Paragraph
assignments.body: Students are given a primary source excerpt and must produce three versions of a body paragraph: one using a direct quote with correct citation, one using a paraphrase with correct citation, and one using a summary with correct citation. They annotate how each version handles attribution differently.

assignments.name: Plagiarism Spectrum
assignments.body: Present students with five examples of source use on a continuum — from clean original thought to verbatim copying — and have them place each on the spectrum with a written justification. Include at least two ambiguous middle cases to force honest conversation about patchwriting.

assignments.name: Works Cited Reconstruction
assignments.body: Students are given raw source information (author, title, publication, URL, date accessed, etc.) in random order and must construct correctly formatted entries in the required citation style. Include at least one source type they haven't cited before — a podcast, a government document, a tweet.

mastery: Produces in-text citations for both direct quotations and paraphrased ideas with consistent accuracy and without prompting.
Distinguishes between patchwriting and genuine paraphrase, and can revise patchwritten sentences into legitimate paraphrase with attribution.
Constructs a Works Cited or bibliography with correct formatting across multiple source types, including digital sources.
Explains the ethical rationale for citation beyond rule-following — understands attribution as an obligation to the originating thinker, not just a formatting convention.
Identifies citation errors in peer work and names the specific type of error (missing attribution, patchwriting, incomplete entry, wrong format) rather than just flagging that "something is wrong."
notes: Vertical ramp: English II introduces citation and plagiarism avoidance as foundational behaviors; English III expects students to display citations with consistency and apply source-use ethics across a full research project, including for paraphrased material as a distinct obligation from quoted material. English IV extends this toward discipline-specific citation conventions and increasingly complex multi-source synthesis.

DOK 2 justification: the core demand is accurate application of a known procedure (citation formatting and attribution) to student-generated or source-based writing — skill execution rather than analysis or evaluation.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is not directly tested on STAAR as a standalone skill but appears embedded in Composition tasks where proper source integration is evaluated. Question stems above are written in the style STAAR uses for research/composition passages and editing tasks, which is the closest testing analog.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Use an Appropriate Mode of Delivery to Present Research Results
expectation: Use an appropriate mode of delivery, whether written, oral, or multimodal, to present results.
overview: This is the final gate of the research process: students don't just produce findings — they decide HOW to deliver them. The decision between written, oral, and multimodal isn't cosmetic; it's an argument about what the audience needs and what the content demands. A student who defaults to a slide deck without thinking has skipped the intellectual work this TEK is asking for.
studentFriendly: You know how to look at your research findings and make a real decision about whether to write them up, present them out loud, or combine text, images, and audio — based on what would actually communicate best to your audience.
questionStems: A student has completed research on a topic and needs to present findings to a community board unfamiliar with the subject. Which mode of delivery would MOST likely be effective, and what evidence from the research supports that choice?
Read the following excerpt from a student's research presentation. The student chose a multimodal format. Which element of the presentation BEST supports the claim made in paragraph 2?
Based on the passage, which sentence BEST explains why the researcher chose an oral delivery over a written report?
The student's decision to use a written mode of delivery in this excerpt is MOST likely intended to —
Which sentence from the presentation BEST demonstrates that the student matched the mode of delivery to the intended audience?
The student's use of visual data in paragraphs 3–4 MOST likely serves to —
Based on paragraphs 1–2, what can the reader MOST likely infer about why the student selected a multimodal format rather than a written-only report?
Which revision to the student's oral presentation script would MOST effectively improve the clarity of the findings in paragraph 5?
The transition between the visual component and the written summary in this multimodal presentation MOST nearly functions to —
Which sentence from the written research report BEST supports the conclusion presented in the final paragraph?

misconceptions.title: The Slide Deck Is Always the Answer
misconceptions.body: Students treat multimodal as the safe default and produce a PowerPoint for every inquiry project without asking whether it's the right choice. Watch for students who build slides first and then reverse-engineer their research into them — the tail wagging the dog. Teach them that mode selection is an argument: what does THIS audience need, from THIS content, in THIS context?

misconceptions.title: "Multimodal" Means More Impressive, Not More Appropriate
misconceptions.body: Students conflate complexity with quality and assume a presentation with audio, images, and animation always outranks a clean written report. Push them to justify: what does the visual component add that words cannot? If they can't answer that, they haven't made a mode decision — they've made a decoration decision.

misconceptions.title: Written Mode Is the Easy Option
misconceptions.body: When students feel uncertain, they retreat to a written report because it feels safer and more familiar. The problem is they often choose it passively, not intentionally. The written mode is powerful — but only when it's chosen because the content calls for precision, permanence, or an audience that needs to reference the material over time. Teach them to name WHY they're writing, not just that they are.

misconceptions.title: Oral Delivery Only Means Talking Through Slides
misconceptions.body: Students think oral presentation is synonymous with reading bullet points aloud. The actual skill is synthesizing research into spoken argument — structuring claims, handling transitions, managing pacing — without the crutch of a text they're reading off a screen. Watch for students who submit a slide deck and call it an oral presentation.

misconceptions.title: Mode Choice Is Made Once and Never Revisited
misconceptions.body: Students lock in a mode at the start of a project and treat it as fixed. The richer move is reconsidering mode as findings develop: did the data get complex enough to need visualization? Did the audience change? Teach them that mode selection is part of the revision process, not just the planning stage.

assignments.name: Mode Justification Memo
assignments.body: Before students begin their final research presentation, they write a one-page memo arguing for their chosen mode of delivery. They must address the audience, the nature of the content, and why the alternative modes would be less effective. Grade the argument, not just the choice.

assignments.name: Same Research, Three Formats
assignments.body: Students take a single set of findings and present them in all three modes — written executive summary, oral briefing, and one multimodal artifact (infographic, annotated slideshow, or video). They then write a short reflection on what each mode required them to sacrifice or emphasize.

assignments.name: Audience Swap
assignments.body: Give students a fixed research topic and two different audiences (e.g., a peer panel and a school board). They produce the same findings for both audiences in different modes and explain the switch. Debrief focuses on how audience shapes delivery.

assignments.name: Critique the Mode
assignments.body: Provide students with a sample research presentation (written OR oral OR multimodal) that uses the wrong mode for its audience and purpose. Students write a critique identifying specifically where the mismatch breaks down and what a better-matched mode would look like.

mastery: Articulates a specific rationale for mode selection — naming audience, content type, and context — before beginning the presentation.

Executes the chosen mode with the craft it demands: a written report that is structured and precise, an oral presentation that is rehearsed and fluent, a multimodal artifact where every element earns its place.
Integrates research findings coherently within the chosen mode rather than dumping raw sources or unprocessed data into the deliverable.
Revisits and defends mode choice during revision rather than treating it as a fixed early decision.
Recognizes when a mode is mismatched to the audience or purpose and can articulate what a better-aligned choice would look like.
notes: Vertical alignment: English II Std 11(I) asks students to select and use an appropriate mode to present results. English III maintains the same expectation but operates in the context of increasingly complex, self-directed inquiry — the sophistication expected in the research itself raises the stakes for delivery mode decisions. English IV moves toward sustained, original research and publication-ready presentation, where mode choice carries professional-register expectations.

DOK 3 justification: students must apply strategic thinking to select and execute a mode of delivery, then evaluate whether the choice served the audience and purpose — not a recall or single-step task.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog since it governs a performance product (research presentation). Question stems model how STAAR tests analogous skills through research simulation passages and student-written excerpts, which is consistent with STAAR's Research strand format.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Critique a Speaker's Clarity, Diction, Syntax, and Rhetorical Strategies
expectation: Engage in meaningful and respectful discourse when evaluating the clarity and coherence of a speaker's message and critiquing the impact of a speaker's use of diction, syntax, and rhetorical strategies.
overview: English IV is the capstone — students don't just assess whether a speaker was clear or chose good words; they now name the rhetorical machinery underneath: the appeals, the structural moves, the strategic silences. The expectation is that this critique happens inside real discourse, which means it must be reasoned, civil, and defensible under pushback.
studentFriendly: You can listen to a speaker — a candidate, a CEO, a classmate — and explain not just what they said and how they said it, but what rhetorical moves they were making and whether those moves actually worked.
questionStems: The speaker's use of [rhetorical strategy] in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Which sentence from the speech BEST illustrates the speaker's primary rhetorical appeal?
How does the syntax in paragraphs X–Y MOST likely contribute to the speaker's persuasive effect?
The speaker's word choice in paragraph X suggests that the intended audience is MOST likely —
Based on paragraph X, which statement BEST describes the impact of the speaker's diction on the clarity of the message?
Which of the following BEST explains how the speaker's rhetorical strategy in paragraph X advances the argument?
The speaker's shift in tone between paragraphs X and Y MOST likely serves to —
Which sentence from the speech BEST supports the conclusion that the speaker is attempting to establish credibility with the audience?
The speaker's repetition of [word/phrase] throughout the speech MOST nearly functions to —
Based on the speech, with which statement would the speaker MOST likely agree about the relationship between clarity and persuasion?

misconceptions.title: Rhetorical Strategy Is a Fancy Name for "Tricks"
misconceptions.body: Students often treat rhetorical strategies as manipulation tactics rather than legitimate tools of argument. They dismiss effective rhetoric as deceptive and effective speakers as slick. Teach them that rhetoric is the discipline of matching your communication choices to your audience and purpose — naming a strategy does not invalidate it, and not every appeal to emotion is a fallacy.

misconceptions.title: Adding More Rhetorical Devices Makes a Speech Better
misconceptions.body: Students conflate density of devices with quality of argument. They praise speeches loaded with anaphora and allusions while missing that the underlying claim is thin. Reframe this: rhetorical strategies are load-bearing only when they're doing real work. A speech stuffed with appeals but missing a clear warrant is still a weak argument — it's just a well-dressed one.

misconceptions.title: Critiquing Rhetoric Means Proving the Speaker Was Wrong
misconceptions.body: Students collapse rhetorical critique into fact-checking. They spend their energy disproving claims rather than analyzing how the speaker constructed the message. Redirect them: you can fully believe a speaker's conclusion and still find their rhetorical choices weak, evasive, or manipulative — and vice versa. Critique is about the mechanism, not the verdict.

misconceptions.title: Syntax and Diction Are Separate from Rhetoric
misconceptions.body: Students treat the five-paragraph critique as three independent buckets — clarity, diction, syntax — then bolt "rhetorical strategies" on as a fourth. Watch for critiques that discuss word choice and sentence structure without connecting them to rhetorical function. Push them to ask: what is this syntactic choice doing in service of the speaker's larger persuasive goal?

misconceptions.title: Respectful Discourse Means Softening the Critique
misconceptions.body: Students in senior year often have the politeness instinct well-trained but apply it as a muzzle. They hedge every judgment, qualify every weakness, and dilute the critique until it says nothing. Teach them the distinction between tone and content: respectful discourse means you don't attack the person, but it does not mean you soften your assessment of the argument. Precise, honest, and courteous are not in conflict.

assignments.name: Rhetorical Autopsy
assignments.body: Students select a speech from a major public address (presidential, judicial, activist). In a 2–3 page written critique, they analyze clarity, diction, syntax, and at least two identifiable rhetorical strategies, citing specific passages as evidence. The final paragraph names what the speaker did well without softening the critique of weaknesses.

assignments.name: Side-by-Side Strategists
assignments.body: Students compare two speeches addressing the same topic — one effective, one widely criticized. They map the rhetorical strategies each speaker deploys, identify where the strategies succeed or fail, and present their findings in a structured class discussion where they must respond to at least one challenge from a peer.

assignments.name: The Uncomfortable Critique
assignments.body: Students critique a speech they personally find persuasive. They must identify at least two moments where the speaker's rhetorical strategy is weak, evasive, or manipulative — and they must name them clearly, without hedging. Grade on specificity and honesty, not on whether the student "likes" the speech.

assignments.name: Live Transcript Analysis
assignments.body: Provide students with a lightly edited transcript of a real spoken address. Students annotate the transcript for diction, syntactic choices, and rhetorical strategies, then write a one-paragraph critique per category. Debrief by projecting the transcript and having students defend their annotations against the class.

assignments.name: Critique Gallery Walk
assignments.body: Each student posts a one-paragraph critique of a different speech on butcher paper around the room. Students rotate, read, and leave a sticky note either extending or challenging one claim in each critique — practicing respectful discourse on others' critical thinking, not just on the original speaker.

mastery: Names specific rhetorical strategies — not just "they used emotion" but identifies the appeal, the structural move, and what the speaker was trying to accomplish with it.
Connects diction and syntactic choices to rhetorical function rather than analyzing them as isolated stylistic features.
Maintains honest, precise critique of speeches they agree with and disagrees fairly with speeches they find unconvincing.
Participates in discourse about a speaker's choices without conflating disagreement with the rhetoric and disagreement with the speaker's position.
Produces critique that is structured, evidence-grounded, and specific enough that a reader unfamiliar with the speech could understand exactly what the speaker did and why it worked or failed.
notes: Vertical jump from English III: 110.38(1)(A) targets clarity, diction, and syntax. English IV adds *rhetorical strategies* as an explicit object of critique — the skill moves from analyzing the surface features of a speaker's language to naming the persuasive architecture underneath. Students arriving from English III should already be able to discuss diction and syntax with specificity; the English IV challenge is connecting those features to intentional rhetorical design.

DOK 4 justification: students must evaluate a speaker across multiple simultaneous criteria — clarity, diction, syntax, and rhetorical strategy — synthesize those judgments into a defensible critique, and sustain that critique under the pressure of respectful discourse with peers. No algorithmic path exists; extended reasoning and evidence selection are required throughout.

This is the terminal course expectation for oral language critique across the English I–IV sequence. English I establishes basic discourse norms; English II adds message evaluation; English III adds diction and syntax as critique targets; English IV integrates rhetorical strategy as the capstone analytical layer.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog — oral language standards are not tested as listening tasks. Question stems are written to mirror how STAAR would test the equivalent skills via speech transcript or written argument passages.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Follow and Give Complex Instructions, Clarify Meaning, and Respond Appropriately
expectation: Follow and give complex instructions, clarify meaning by asking pertinent questions, and respond appropriately.
overview: This is the capstone oral language skill before students leave secondary ELA — the ability to operate in a high-stakes, multi-step spoken exchange without losing the thread, without pretending to understand, and without responding off-target. At English IV, complexity is the operative word: the instructions aren't simple, the questions have to be precise, and "appropriate" response means calibrated to audience and context.
studentFriendly: You can receive complicated spoken directions, ask smart follow-up questions when something isn't clear, and give a response that actually fits the moment — skills that matter in college orientation, job training, and every professional setting after graduation.
questionStems: Read the following transcript of a workplace training session. Which question asked by the trainee BEST clarifies the meaning of the supervisor's instructions in paragraph 3?
Based on paragraphs 2–4 of the transcript, which step in the instructions would MOST likely be misunderstood without the speaker's follow-up explanation?
Which response from the listener in paragraph 5 BEST demonstrates an appropriate reply to the speaker's directions?
The speaker in paragraph 2 revises her original instructions. What does this revision MOST likely suggest about the complexity of the original directions?
Which question from the exchange MOST nearly reflects a pertinent clarifying question rather than an off-topic one?
Based on the transcript, the listener's response in paragraph 6 suggests that he —
Which sentence from the instructions BEST explains why the listener asks for repetition in paragraph 4?
The listener's follow-up question in paragraph 3 MOST likely serves to —
Read the multi-step instructions in paragraphs 1–3. Which detail would a listener MOST likely need to clarify before completing the task correctly?
Based on the exchange in paragraphs 4–6, what can the reader infer about the listener's understanding of the original instructions?

misconceptions.title: Asking Questions Means You Weren't Listening
misconceptions.body: Students treat clarifying questions as admissions of failure — if they understood, they'd already know. Reframe this immediately: a pertinent question is evidence of active processing, not passive confusion. Teach them the difference between a question that reveals they missed the whole point and a question that pinpoints the one ambiguous step. That distinction is the skill.

misconceptions.title: "Respond Appropriately" Just Means Be Polite
misconceptions.body: Students reduce appropriate response to tone — they say "thank you" and think they've done it. Push them deeper: appropriate response means answering what was actually asked, at the right length, in the right register, with the right level of detail for the audience. A one-word answer to a complex prompt is not polite — it's incomplete.

misconceptions.title: Complex Instructions Can Be Reconstructed Later
misconceptions.body: Students assume they can piece together multi-step oral instructions from memory or from asking a peer afterward. Watch for students who nod through a complex set of directions and then flounder. The cognitive work happens in the moment — real-time tracking of sequence, conditionals, and dependencies. Teach them to listen structurally, not just for content.

misconceptions.title: The Last Instruction Is the Most Important
misconceptions.body: Students tend to weight the final step most heavily because it's freshest in working memory. But complex instructions often front-load the most critical information — conditions, exceptions, definitions of terms. Teach them to listen across the whole sequence before deciding what matters most, and to flag the front-end information before it fades.

misconceptions.title: Clarifying Questions Can Wait Until the End
misconceptions.body: Students hold their questions until the speaker finishes, then find they've lost the exact phrasing they needed to ask about. In complex oral exchanges, the window for a pertinent question is often during a natural pause — not after. Teach them to recognize those pauses as licensed openings, not interruptions, and to frame questions precisely enough that they'd still make sense out of context.

assignments.name: Instruction Relay
assignments.body: One student reads a complex, multi-step procedure (assembling furniture, operating equipment, filing a form) aloud once. The listener may ask exactly two clarifying questions, then must perform or describe the steps in order. Debrief: which questions were pertinent? Which steps were still unclear?

assignments.name: Transcript Annotation
assignments.body: Provide students a printed transcript of a recorded oral exchange (workplace, academic, or public context). Students annotate: highlight every clarifying question, mark whether it was pertinent or off-topic, and identify moments where the listener's response was or wasn't appropriate to the instruction given.

assignments.name: Mock Onboarding
assignments.body: Simulate a first-day job scenario: a "supervisor" (teacher or student) delivers complex onboarding instructions verbally. The listener must ask at least two pertinent questions, then summarize the instructions back in the correct sequence. Grade on accuracy of the summary, quality of the questions, and appropriateness of tone.

assignments.name: Revision Round
assignments.body: After a mock exchange, students watch a recording or read a transcript of their own clarifying questions and identify which were pertinent and which were vague, redundant, or off-target — then rewrite the weak ones in writing before trying the exchange again.

mastery: Tracks multi-step oral instructions in real time, including sequence, conditions, and exceptions, without needing repetition of the full set.
Asks clarifying questions that target the specific point of ambiguity — not general confusion, not rephrasing of what the speaker already said.
Responds in a way that is calibrated to the complexity of the original instructions — neither over-brief nor bloated with unnecessary hedging.
Distinguishes between a question that reflects active comprehension and one that signals they tuned out.
Adjusts register and level of formality in both questions and responses to match the audience and context of the exchange.
notes: Vertical ramp from English III: 110.38(1)(B) targets clarifying meaning through pertinent questioning in the context of discourse and listening. English IV adds *following and giving* complex instructions as the paired skill — the student is now both receiver and sender of high-stakes oral information, not just a critical listener. The complexity threshold also raises: multi-step, conditional, and context-dependent instructions are the expected input.

DOK 3 justification: following complex instructions requires strategic use of comprehension in unpredictable real-time conditions; formulating pertinent questions requires analysis of what is and isn't yet understood; appropriate response requires situational judgment, not recall.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog since oral language performance is not tested on the written assessment. Question stems above use speech transcripts read as prose — the standard STAAR workaround for oral language TEKs. Note this for teachers so they don't expect released items that match this TEK directly.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Formulate and Deliver Sound Arguments Using Classical Speech Structure
expectation: Formulate sound arguments and present using elements of classical speeches such as introduction, first and second transitions, body, conclusion, the art of persuasion, rhetorical devices, employing eye contact, speaking rate such as pauses for effect, volume, enunciation, purposeful gestures, and conventions of language to communicate ideas effectively.
overview: This is the capstone oral language standard — English IV asks students to synthesize argument, classical structure, rhetorical craft, and physical delivery into a single coherent speech. The skill is not just performing; it is building an argument that can stand on its own and then delivering it so the argument lands.
studentFriendly: You can construct a real argument from scratch using the structural moves of classical rhetoric — introduction, transitions, body, conclusion — and deliver it with the vocal and physical control that makes an audience actually listen.
questionStems: Read the speech transcript. The speaker's introduction MOST likely serves to —
Which sentence from the body of the speech BEST supports the speaker's central argument?
The speaker's use of [rhetorical device] in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the first transition MOST nearly functions to —
Which line from the conclusion BEST reinforces the persuasive purpose established in the introduction?
The speaker's word choice in paragraph X suggests that the intended audience is MOST likely —
How does the speaker's use of [rhetorical device] in paragraph X contribute to the overall persuasiveness of the argument?
Based on the speech as a whole, which statement BEST describes the relationship between the speaker's claim and the evidence presented in the body?
The speaker MOST likely pauses after the phrase [phrase] in paragraph X in order to —
Which organizational choice in the speech MOST effectively develops the speaker's argument from introduction to conclusion?

misconceptions.title: Argument Is the Same as Opinion
misconceptions.body: Students confuse having a position with having an argument. They open with a claim, assert it repeatedly, and call it persuasion. Teach them: an argument is a claim plus reasoning plus evidence — and the evidence has to do real work, not just sit there as decoration. Watch for speeches where every body paragraph ends with "and that's why I believe..." instead of advancing the logic.

misconceptions.title: Classical Structure Is a Formula, Not a Tool
misconceptions.body: Students treat introduction-body-conclusion as a box-checking exercise — say hello, make three points, say goodbye. Push them to understand why classical structure exists: the introduction builds ethos and primes the audience's frame; transitions do the argumentative pivot work; the conclusion doesn't summarize, it closes the case. If a student's transitions could be deleted without anyone noticing, they haven't written transitions — they've written headings.

misconceptions.title: Delivery Is Decoration, Not Argument
misconceptions.body: Students treat eye contact, gesture, and pacing as the polish you add after the real work is done. Reframe this hard: delivery IS rhetoric. A pause after a statistic forces the audience to sit with it. A gesture that contradicts the spoken words undermines the argument. Purposeful delivery amplifies the logic; careless delivery can cancel it. Grade delivery as rhetorical choice, not performance grade.

misconceptions.title: Rhetorical Devices Are Ornamental
misconceptions.body: Students sprinkle in anaphora or chiasmus because the rubric said "rhetorical devices," not because the device is doing argumentative work. Teach them: a rhetorical device earns its place only if removing it would weaken the argument or the audience's emotional engagement. If you can delete the device and the speech is just as strong, the device was furniture, not architecture.

misconceptions.title: The Conclusion Just Restates the Introduction
misconceptions.body: Students write conclusions that are shorter introductions — they restate the claim, list the three points again, and sit down. The classical conclusion is a peroration: it crystallizes the emotional and logical stakes, names what the audience should DO or THINK differently, and closes with the phrase or image that will follow the listener out of the room. Teach them: if your conclusion could swap positions with your introduction, you haven't written a conclusion.

assignments.name: Classical Blueprint Before Draft
assignments.body: Before students write a word of their speech, they must submit a one-page structural blueprint labeling every section — introduction, first transition, body paragraphs with claim/evidence/warrant labeled, second transition, conclusion — and naming the rhetorical device used in each major move. No blueprint approved, no draft started.

assignments.name: Delivery Autopsy
assignments.body: Students record their practice delivery and watch it back with the sound off, then with sound only, then full. For each pass they annotate: what is my body doing, what is my voice doing, what is the audience feeling? Written reflection turned in with the final speech.

assignments.name: Argument Stress Test
assignments.body: Pairs of students exchange speech drafts and write the strongest possible steelman of the opposing position. The original speaker must then revise to address the steelman before final delivery. The goal is finding the holes before the audience does.

assignments.name: Historical Model Dissection
assignments.body: Students select a canonical speech (Lincoln's Second Inaugural, Churchill's "We Shall Fight on the Beaches," Barbara Jordan's 1976 DNC keynote) and annotate it for every classical element the TEK names. They then write one page explaining which structural and rhetorical choice most accounts for the speech's persuasive durability.

assignments.name: Live Delivery with Critic Panel
assignments.body: After final speeches, three classmates serve as critics — each assigned one dimension (argument soundness, classical structure fidelity, delivery effectiveness). Critics give one-minute oral feedback per speech using specific evidence from the text. Speaker may respond for thirty seconds.

mastery: Constructs a speech in which the introduction establishes ethos and primes the argument, each transition performs explicit logical work, and the conclusion closes the case rather than restating it.
Deploys rhetorical devices that are argumentatively functional — the device changes what the audience feels or believes, not just how the speech sounds.
Calibrates delivery choices — pause, volume, rate, gesture, eye contact — to serve the argument at specific moments, not uniformly across the speech.
Produces a sound argument: claim is clearly stated, evidence is specific and sourced, warrants connect evidence to claim without logical gaps.
Receives critique and can identify, by structural label, which element of the classical framework the critique targets and what the revision should address.
notes: Vertical ramp from English III: 110.38(1)(C) requires students to deliver a formal presentation with rhetorical control. English IV adds the full classical framework — named structural elements, the art of persuasion, and the complete inventory of delivery variables — making this a synthesis standard, not just an increment.

DOK 4 justification: students must design and execute an original extended argument, making structural and rhetorical decisions across multiple dimensions simultaneously and defending those decisions under critique.

No direct STAAR analog for live delivery performance; question stems above test the analog via speech transcript analysis — how STAAR approaches oral language TEKs when testing them as prose. Noted per HANDOFF doctrine.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Participate Collaboratively with Purpose and Evaluative Rigor
expectation: Participate collaboratively, offering ideas or judgments that are purposeful in moving the team toward goals, asking relevant and insightful questions, tolerating a range of positions and ambiguity in decision making, and evaluating the work of the group based on agreed-upon criteria.
overview: This is the capstone version of collaborative participation — English IV asks students not just to contribute, but to contribute purposefully, hold ambiguity without shutting down, and apply shared criteria when evaluating their group's work. The move is from participation to stewardship of the group's intellectual progress.
studentFriendly: You can push a group forward — not just by talking, but by asking the right questions, sitting with uncertainty long enough to reach a better decision, and measuring the group's work against standards everyone agreed to upfront.
questionStems: Based on paragraphs X–Y, which statement from a group member MOST likely moved the discussion toward the group's goal?
The participant's question in paragraph X is BEST described as —
Which response from a group member MOST effectively addresses the ambiguity introduced in paragraph X?
Based on the discussion in paragraphs X–Y, which judgment offered by a participant is MOST purposeful in advancing the group's work?
The group member's comment in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Which question asked during the discussion in paragraph X is MOST relevant to the group's agreed-upon criteria?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, how does the participant's tolerance of opposing positions contribute to the group's decision-making process?
Which statement from the discussion BEST demonstrates a participant evaluating the group's work against established criteria?
The participant in paragraph X responds to disagreement by — which MOST likely has what effect on the group's progress?
Based on the discussion transcript in paragraphs X–Y, which participant's contribution BEST balances offering a personal judgment with moving the group toward its goal?

misconceptions.title: Purposeful Means Talking the Most
misconceptions.body: Students mistake volume for contribution. They speak frequently but reactively — responding to the last thing said rather than steering toward the goal. Teach them the difference: purposeful participation tracks the group's destination and asks "does this move us closer?" before speaking. Watch for students who dominate airtime but leave the group no further along than when they started.

misconceptions.title: Tolerating Ambiguity Means Avoiding a Decision
misconceptions.body: When students hear "tolerate ambiguity," they use it as permission to stay stuck. They confuse withholding judgment with never making one. Reframe it: tolerating ambiguity means staying in the productive discomfort of multiple valid positions long enough to make a better decision — not camping there permanently. Push them to name when the ambiguity has been useful and when it's become avoidance.

misconceptions.title: Agreed-Upon Criteria Are Obvious and Don't Need to Be Named
misconceptions.body: Groups assume shared criteria exist without establishing them. Then they evaluate each other's work using completely different standards and are surprised by conflict. Teach them that "agreed-upon" is active — the group must surface, negotiate, and state the criteria before evaluation begins. Evaluation without criteria is just preference with extra steps.

misconceptions.title: Insightful Questions Are Long or Complex
misconceptions.body: Students think a sophisticated question requires elaborate setup. In practice, the most insightful collaborative questions are often sharp and brief — they expose a gap the group hasn't noticed, or reframe the problem cleanly. Watch for students who confuse question length with question quality, and model the short, disruptive question that changes the room's direction.

misconceptions.title: Evaluating the Group's Work Means Grading Teammates
misconceptions.body: Students conflate group evaluation with judging individuals — and either pull punches to protect relationships or use evaluation as cover for grievances. Redirect them: evaluation targets the work against criteria, not the person behind the work. Teach them to ask "does this product meet the standard we agreed to?" rather than "did so-and-so do enough?"

assignments.name: Criteria-First Discussion
assignments.body: Before any collaborative task, groups spend five minutes establishing written criteria for success. After the task, each member evaluates the group's output against those criteria in a short written debrief. Compare individual evaluations and discuss where they diverged.

assignments.name: Purposeful Contribution Log
assignments.body: During a Socratic seminar or group project, students track their own contributions in a log: what they said, whether it moved the group toward the goal, and what they would say differently. Debrief as a class — not to shame, but to build self-awareness.

assignments.name: Ambiguity Hold
assignments.body: Present a complex, genuinely open-ended scenario (ethical dilemma, policy problem, disputed interpretation). Groups are prohibited from reaching a final position for the first fifteen minutes. After the hold, they decide. Debrief: did sitting with ambiguity change the decision? How?

assignments.name: Transcript Critique
assignments.body: Students read a lightly edited transcript of a real group discussion (prior class, public deliberation, or published record). They annotate each contribution: purposeful/reactive, question type, tolerance of ambiguity, criteria-based or preference-based. Write a one-page evaluation of the group's collaborative effectiveness.

assignments.name: Stakeholder Roundtable
assignments.body: Students take assigned roles representing different stakeholders on a contested issue. After deliberation, they must reach consensus and present both the decision and the agreed-upon criteria that drove it. Post-roundtable reflection asks: which contributions moved us, and which stalled us?

mastery: Tracks the group's stated goal during discussion and explicitly connects contributions to it rather than responding only to the last comment.
Asks questions that expose gaps, reframe problems, or surface unexamined assumptions — not questions that merely confirm what the group already believes.
Articulates competing positions accurately before pushing toward resolution, demonstrating genuine tolerance rather than performed neutrality.
References agreed-upon criteria when evaluating the group's work and distinguishes between personal preference and criteria-based judgment.
Adjusts their role dynamically — pushing forward when the group stalls, pulling back when others need space — based on what the group needs to reach its goal.
notes: Vertical jump from English III: Std 1D in English III asks students to "participate collaboratively in discussions." English IV adds four explicit performance targets — purposeful contribution, insightful questioning, tolerance of ambiguity, and criteria-based evaluation. The jump is from participation as presence to participation as leadership of the group's intellectual work.

DOK 3 justification: students must apply criteria to evaluate group performance, reason through ambiguity, and make strategic decisions about when and how to contribute — all of which require strategic thinking beyond recall or skill application.

No direct STAAR analog: this TEK lives in oral performance. Question stems are written as if a discussion transcript has been provided as a reading passage, consistent with how STAAR handles oral language analogs elsewhere in the test design. Note this when using stems with students.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Use Resources to Clarify and Validate Advanced Vocabulary
expectation: Use print or digital resources to clarify and validate understanding of multiple meanings of advanced vocabulary.
overview: This is the culminating vocabulary-tools standard — by English IV, students should know which resource to reach for, how to read it critically, and how to navigate the problem of a word that means three things depending on context. The skill isn't "look it up"; it's "look it up correctly and know when the first definition isn't the right one."
studentFriendly: You can pick up a dictionary, thesaurus, or credible digital resource, find a complex word with multiple meanings, and figure out which meaning actually fits the context you're reading or writing in.
questionStems: Based on context in paragraph X, the word [word] MOST nearly means —
Which definition of [word] BEST matches its use in paragraph X?
The author's use of [word] in paragraph X suggests that the word means —
In paragraph X, the word [word] is used to convey which of the following?
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that [word] is used to mean [definition A] rather than [definition B]?
The meaning of the word [word] in paragraph X is MOST closely related to which of the following words?
Read this dictionary entry for [word]. Based on the entry and paragraph X, which definition BEST fits the author's use of the word?
Which detail from paragraphs X–Y BEST clarifies the meaning of [word] as it is used in the selection?
Based on paragraph X, the phrase [phrase] MOST likely means —
How does the author's use of [word] in paragraph X contribute to the development of the central idea?

misconceptions.title: The First Definition Is the Right Definition
misconceptions.body: Students open a dictionary, read the first entry, and stop. Advanced vocabulary frequently carries a primary definition that is obsolete, overly broad, or simply wrong for the context at hand. Teach them to read all the definitions, check the part of speech, and compare against context before settling. Watch for students who confidently quote definition 1 for a word being used in sense 4.

misconceptions.title: Digital = Less Reliable, Print = More Reliable
misconceptions.body: Students have absorbed the vague warning that the internet is untrustworthy and overcorrect by distrusting all digital resources. Push them to distinguish between resource quality and resource format — a subscription OED online is more reliable than a forty-year-old desk dictionary with no updates. The question is always which resource, not which format.

misconceptions.title: Synonyms Are Interchangeable
misconceptions.body: When students use a thesaurus to replace a word, they treat every synonym as a perfect swap. Teach them that synonym lists record words with overlapping — not identical — meanings, and that connotation, register, and collocation all differ. "Destitute," "impoverished," "broke," and "penurious" are not the same word; using any one in place of another changes the sentence.

misconceptions.title: Looking It Up Once Settles the Question
misconceptions.body: Students validate a word's meaning once and move on, even when the same word appears three more times in the same text with what might be a slightly different inflection. Advanced vocabulary often shifts in meaning across a text as the author applies it in extended or figurative ways. Teach them to re-examine each instance rather than applying their initial lookup uniformly.

misconceptions.title: A Word with Multiple Meanings Is Just Confusing
misconceptions.body: When students hit a word with five definitions, they treat multiplicity as a problem rather than information. Reframe this: a word with rich definitional history is telling you something about the author's register, the word's field of origin, or the precision the author is chasing. The multiple meanings are context clues about what ISN'T meant as much as what is.

assignments.name: Definition Audit
assignments.body: Students select ten advanced vocabulary words from a current reading and look each one up in two different resources — one print or subscription database, one freely available digital source. They record all definitions, identify discrepancies, and write a sentence explaining which definition fits the text and why.

assignments.name: Multiple-Meanings Close Read
assignments.body: Students receive a passage in which the same word appears three times with subtly different semantic weight each time. They use reference resources to map all relevant definitions of the word and then write one paragraph per instance explaining which definition applies and what evidence in the surrounding text confirms it.

assignments.name: Thesaurus Stress Test
assignments.body: Students take a mentor sentence with a precisely chosen word, replace it with three different synonyms from a thesaurus, and evaluate how each swap changes the sentence's tone, meaning, and effectiveness. The goal is to show that word choice is never arbitrary and that reference tools require critical judgment, not blind application.

mastery: Selects the appropriate resource for the task — recognizes when context demands a specialized dictionary, an etymological reference, or a general thesaurus, rather than defaulting to the nearest tab.
Reads multiple definitions for a word and uses surrounding context to identify which definition the author is applying, rather than defaulting to the first or most familiar entry.
Explains the difference between near-synonyms in terms of connotation, register, and context — not just denotation.
Re-examines a word's meaning across multiple instances within the same text, tracking whether the author's usage shifts or remains consistent.
Produces written analysis that cites specific resource entries alongside specific textual evidence when validating a vocabulary interpretation.
notes: Vertical alignment: English III 2A carries the same expectation at the same DOK level. The ramp into English IV is implicit — this is now the final formal vocabulary-tools standard before college and career, so precision and independence of resource use should be held to a higher standard than in previous courses. Where English III students may still be guided to resources, English IV students should be selecting them autonomously.

DOK 2 justification: the core skill is interpretation and comparison of definitional information against textual context — a comprehension-and-application move that doesn't yet require the extended analysis of DOK 3. When students write multi-paragraph definition audits, the task as a whole can reach DOK 3, but the TEK itself targets DOK 2.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is tested indirectly through vocabulary-in-context questions on STAAR — "which meaning of the word BEST fits paragraph X" is the operative stem type. Stems above reflect that testing pattern directly.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Context to Draw Conclusions About Nuanced Meanings
expectation: Analyze context to draw conclusions about nuanced meanings such as in imagery.
overview: This is the senior-year vocabulary capstone: not "what does this word mean in the dictionary" but "what does this word mean here, in this sentence, next to those other words, in this genre, in this moment." Imagery is the named example, but the real target is the student who can read a charged phrase and trace exactly how context bends its meaning.
studentFriendly: You can read a phrase — especially a vivid image — and explain not just what it literally says, but what it quietly means given everything around it.
questionStems: In paragraph X, the imagery of [image] MOST likely suggests —
Based on context, the phrase "[phrase]" in paragraph X MOST nearly means —
Which detail from paragraph X BEST supports the conclusion that the author uses [word] to convey —
The author's use of [image] in paragraph X contributes to the reader's understanding that —
In paragraphs X–Y, the shift in imagery from [A] to [B] MOST likely indicates —
Based on the context in paragraph X, the connotation of "[word]" is BEST described as —
Which inference about the speaker's attitude is BEST supported by the imagery in paragraph X?
The phrase "[phrase]" in paragraph X has a nuanced meaning that MOST likely conveys —
In the context of the passage, the word "[word]" in paragraph X MOST nearly means —
The imagery in paragraphs X–Y MOST likely develops the idea that —

misconceptions.title: The Literal Reading Is Good Enough
misconceptions.body: Students identify what an image depicts and stop — "it's describing a storm." Teach them that the image is never only itself. The storm is always doing something else: marking a shift in mood, externalizing a character's breakdown, prefiguring a rupture in the plot. Push them to ask not "what is this?" but "what is this standing in for, and how does the surrounding text confirm that?"

misconceptions.title: Nuance Lives in the Hard Words
misconceptions.body: Students assume nuanced meaning requires elevated vocabulary — that the image with the unusual diction is where the meaning gets complicated. The more dangerous assumption is that plain, familiar words are transparent. Watch for students who cruise past "cold," "clean," "still," or "empty" without noticing how those everyday words accumulate into something devastating. The nuance is often hiding in the common words.

misconceptions.title: Context Means the Surrounding Sentences
misconceptions.body: Students treat context as the two sentences before and after the phrase. Reframe this: context includes genre, speaker, moment in the narrative arc, the author's established patterns, the cultural or historical references the text has already activated. When a student claims a phrase means X, ask them: what ELSE in this text supports that reading? If the answer is only "the sentence before it," they haven't read the context — they've read a window.

misconceptions.title: Every Image Has One Right Answer
misconceptions.body: Students hunt for the singular correct meaning and panic when multiple readings seem defensible. Teach them that nuanced meaning is evidence-dependent, not answer-key-dependent. Two readings can both be valid if both are supported by textual evidence — and the richer reader is the one who can hold both, explain the tension, and decide which is better supported. That's the senior skill.

misconceptions.title: Drawing Conclusions Means Stating the Theme
misconceptions.body: When asked to draw a conclusion about imagery, students default to thematic restatement: "this shows the theme of loss." Push them deeper: what specific aspect of "loss" does this image render that a plain statement couldn't? What does the image make the reader feel or see that the theme sentence flattens? Conclusions about nuanced meaning should be more precise than the theme, not a repackaging of it.

assignments.name: Context Collapse
assignments.body: Students take a passage and replace three images with their literal equivalents ("the dying light of the afternoon" → "it was getting dark"). They write one paragraph explaining what the original image did that the replacement can't — forcing them to name nuance by absence.

assignments.name: Competing Readings
assignments.body: Students produce two textually supported interpretations of the same image. They then write a third paragraph arguing which reading is better supported and why. Grade for the quality of the argument, not for picking the "right" answer.

assignments.name: Image Annotation Chain
assignments.body: Students annotate a poem or dense prose passage in three passes: (1) literal description only, (2) connotative associations, (3) context-driven conclusion about nuanced meaning. The three layers are submitted together so the teacher can see the reasoning progression.

assignments.name: Cross-Text Image Audit
assignments.body: Students find the same image type (water, fire, light, hands) appearing in two different texts and analyze how context makes its meaning different in each — same image, different conclusion because different context. Forces them to see that meaning is relational, not fixed.

mastery: Moves beyond literal identification of imagery to explain what the image is doing rhetorically or thematically.
Accounts for multiple layers of context — not just adjacent sentences but genre, speaker, and textual patterns — when drawing conclusions about meaning.
Articulates why a nuanced meaning cannot be reduced to a simpler paraphrase without loss.
Defends a conclusion about meaning with specific textual evidence and can adjudicate between competing readings.
Identifies nuance in common, plain-language imagery — not just in elevated or unfamiliar vocabulary.
notes: Vertical ramp: English III (2)(B) targets the same skill but leans on denotative/connotative distinction and context-derived meaning generally. English IV's named example — "imagery" — signals the expectation that students are now working in figurative, compressed, and densely layered texts where context is doing the heaviest lifting. The skill culminates here.

DOK 3 justification: students must use strategic thinking to synthesize contextual information and construct a meaning that is not directly stated — analysis and conclusion-drawing across multiple textual variables.

STAAR analog: this TEK maps directly to STAAR's vocabulary-in-context items and to figurative-language questions asking students to infer meaning from imagery — both well-represented question types on released exams.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Determine the Meaning of Foreign Words and Phrases Used in English
expectation: Determine the meaning of foreign words or phrases used frequently in English such as ad nauseum, in loco parentis, laissez-faire, and caveat emptor.
overview: English has never been a closed system — it borrows constantly from Latin, French, and Greek, and a significant layer of academic, legal, and political discourse runs on phrases most students have never been taught to decode. This TEK asks students to do more than memorize translations; they have to understand why these phrases entered English and what they're doing in the texts where they appear.
studentFriendly: You can encounter a Latin phrase in a legal document, a French term in an economics article, or a borrowed expression in an op-ed and figure out what it means from context, roots, and the job it's doing in the sentence.
questionStems: In paragraph X, the phrase "caveat emptor" MOST nearly means —
Based on the context of paragraph X, the author's use of "laissez-faire" suggests that —
Which phrase from the article BEST conveys the author's attitude toward government regulation?
The author uses the term "in loco parentis" in paragraph X to suggest that —
The Latin phrase in paragraph X contributes to the author's argument MOST by —
In paragraph X, "ad nauseam" is used to indicate that a point has been —
Based on the context clues in paragraph X, the foreign phrase used by the author MOST likely refers to —
Which definition BEST matches the meaning of "caveat emptor" as it is used in paragraph X?
The author's choice to use a Latin phrase rather than an English equivalent in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST helps the reader infer the meaning of the foreign phrase in paragraph X?

misconceptions.title: Foreign Phrases Are Just Decoration
misconceptions.body: Students assume writers drop Latin or French phrases to sound impressive, and therefore treat them as noise to skip over. Push back hard: these phrases almost always carry a precise legal, philosophical, or rhetorical load that a simple English equivalent would blur. Teach them that when a careful writer reaches for "caveat emptor" instead of "buyer beware," something about precision or register is at stake — their job is to figure out what.

misconceptions.title: Memorizing the Translation Is the Finish Line
misconceptions.body: Students learn that "ad nauseam" means "to a sickening degree" and consider themselves done. The richer move is understanding what the phrase is doing in context: is the author using it approvingly, critically, ironically? Translation without contextual analysis is vocabulary study, not reading. Watch for students who can define the phrase in isolation but can't explain why the author chose it.

misconceptions.title: You Either Know It or You Don't
misconceptions.body: Students treat borrowed phrases as a closed club — either you've been taught the phrase or you're locked out of the meaning. Reframe this: root knowledge, context, and the sentence's logic can unlock most of these expressions even without prior exposure. Teach them to work the surrounding text the way they would any unfamiliar word; the same strategies transfer.

misconceptions.title: These Phrases Only Appear in Old or Formal Writing
misconceptions.body: Students mentally file Latin and French borrowings under "irrelevant to anything I'll actually read." Disabuse them with current examples: "laissez-faire" appears in contemporary economic journalism, "in loco parentis" appears in school policy debates and real legal arguments they may encounter as adults, and "ad nauseam" turns up in op-eds and social media commentary. This is live vocabulary, not museum vocabulary.

misconceptions.title: All Foreign Phrases in English Come from Latin
misconceptions.body: Students overgeneralize and assume everything borrowed is Latin. A significant portion of the borrowed layer in English is French ("faux pas," "carte blanche," "raison d'être"), and some is Greek-rooted.
