When students default to "it must be Latin," they miss the distinct register and history that French borrowings carry — often legal, aristocratic, or culinary — versus Latin's scientific and ecclesiastical weight. Teach them to notice the difference.

assignments.name: Phrase in the Wild
assignments.body: Students locate a foreign phrase used in a current newspaper, magazine, or online article and write a paragraph explaining: the literal translation, what the phrase is doing in context, and whether an English equivalent would change the effect. One phrase, one page, real-world source required.

assignments.name: Register Swap
assignments.body: Students take a paragraph from a legal brief, academic essay, or policy document that uses one or more borrowed foreign phrases and rewrite it substituting plain English equivalents. Then they write a short reflection: what did the substitution cost? What, if anything, did it gain? This forces them to articulate what these phrases are doing beyond decoration.

assignments.name: Phrase Family Tree
assignments.body: Students are assigned one phrase from the standard's exemplar list (ad nauseam, in loco parentis, laissez-faire, caveat emptor) and trace its history: language of origin, literal translation, when it entered English, what contexts it appears in today, and one example sentence from a real source. Presented as a one-page research brief, not a poster.

assignments.name: Contextual Cold Read
assignments.body: Present students with three short passages — each containing a foreign phrase they have not been pre-taught — and ask them to derive meaning from context alone, writing a sentence explaining their reasoning. This isolates the transfer skill from rote memorization.

mastery: Derives the meaning of an unfamiliar foreign phrase from context clues and root knowledge, explaining the reasoning rather than just stating a definition.
Distinguishes between the literal translation of a phrase and its functional meaning in a specific text.
Identifies why an author chose a borrowed foreign phrase over a plain English equivalent and articulates the rhetorical or register effect of that choice.
Recognizes the language of origin (Latin, French, Greek) and uses that recognition to narrow meaning and connotation.
Applies phrase meaning accurately in analysis — neither over-inflating the phrase's significance nor dismissing it as ornamentation.
notes: Vertical alignment: English III's parallel expectation covers the same skill. English IV is the terminal course, so this is the last formal instructional anchor for borrowed foreign vocabulary — students should leave with transfer strategies they can apply independently in college, legal, and professional reading.

The exemplar phrases in the TEA expectation are carefully chosen to span registers: ad nauseam (rhetorical/colloquial), in loco parentis (legal/institutional), laissez-faire (economic/political), caveat emptor (legal/commercial). Instruction should span that same range rather than treating them as an arbitrary list.

STAAR does not test foreign phrase identification in isolation as a discrete item type, but borrowed phrases appear in informational and argumentative passages tested under vocabulary and author's craft standards. Stems here are written to reflect how STAAR embeds this skill within context-dependent vocabulary questions. Note this to students so they recognize it when it appears.
DOK 2 justification: students must apply knowledge of roots, context, and register to interpret meaning — beyond recall, but not yet requiring extended synthesis or evaluation.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: 110.39(3) — Self-Select Text and Read Independently for a Sustained Period
expectation: Self-select text and read independently for a sustained period of time.
overview: This is the capstone independent reading expectation — the last time TEKS will ask a student to sustain voluntary reading before they leave public education. The point isn't compliance with a reading log; it's that English IV is a student's last institutional invitation to become a reader on their own terms.
studentFriendly: You choose what you read, you sit with it long enough to actually go somewhere in it, and you do that again and again until reading independently is just something you do.
questionStems: Based on paragraphs X–Y, which detail MOST clearly suggests the narrator's reason for continuing to read the text?
Which sentence from the passage BEST supports the inference that the reader has sustained attention to the central idea?
The reader's selection of this text MOST likely reflects a preference for —
Which detail from paragraph X BEST illustrates the narrator's deepening engagement with the subject?
Based on the passage, which statement MOST nearly describes the effect of sustained reading on the narrator's understanding?
Which line from the text BEST supports the conclusion that the narrator has read widely in this genre?
The shift between paragraphs X and Y MOST likely reflects the reader's —
Which sentence from the passage MOST clearly demonstrates how prior reading informs the narrator's interpretation?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the narrator's choice of this text contributes MOST to —
Which detail from the passage BEST supports the inference that the narrator has developed a reading identity independent of assigned texts?

misconceptions.title: Any Reading Counts the Same
misconceptions.body: Students — and sometimes teachers — treat this standard as a checkbox: any book, any page count, any amount of distraction tolerated. The standard is about sustained engagement, not sustained page-turning. Watch for students who log time but can't say a single thing about what they read. Teach them that duration without attention is just sitting near a book.

misconceptions.title: The Teacher Picks the Right Book for Senior Year
misconceptions.body: Some teachers feel obligated to steer every student toward canonical or college-prep texts, which quietly kills the self-selection function entirely. The whole mechanism of this standard is that the student makes the call. A senior who chooses a true-crime narrative and reads it with total absorption is doing more for their reading life than one who dutifully plods through a "worthy" text they despise.

misconceptions.title: Reading Stamina Is Fixed
misconceptions.body: Students who struggle to sustain reading for more than ten minutes often believe they simply aren't readers — that stamina is a personality trait rather than a practice. Teach them that stamina builds like any other capacity: with repeated low-stakes reps over time. The student who makes it twelve minutes today and fifteen next week is doing the work.

misconceptions.title: Self-Sustained Reading Is a Break from Real Learning
misconceptions.body: When independent reading time appears on a schedule, students and parents sometimes read it as filler — something that happens when there's nothing important to do. Push back hard on this. The research on reading volume and vocabulary, fluency, and background knowledge is unambiguous. Treat this time with the same instructional seriousness as a writing workshop.

misconceptions.title: Finishing a Book Is the Goal
misconceptions.body: Students often tie their sense of success to completion: they "won" if they finished, "failed" if they didn't. This collapses the standard's actual target — sustained independent engagement — into a productivity metric. Teach them that abandoning a book that isn't working and choosing a better one is reader behavior, not quitting. English IV is a good place to explicitly teach that experienced readers quit bad books all the time.

assignments.name: Reading Life Reflection
assignments.body: At the end of each quarter, students write a one-page reflection: what they read, why they chose it, what held their attention and what didn't, and what they plan to read next. Grade for specificity and honesty, not for the titles chosen.

assignments.name: Sustained Reading Conference
assignments.body: Brief (5–7 minute) one-on-one teacher-student conference once per quarter. The student talks about their current book — what's working, what isn't, where they are in it. Teacher listens for signs of genuine engagement versus performance of engagement.

assignments.name: Reader-to-Reader Recommendation Shelf
assignments.body: Students write a 150-word recommendation card for any book they finish during the year. Cards go on a physical or digital shelf available to the class. The constraint: the card must make a specific reader-to-reader case, not a summary.

assignments.name: Genre Stretch Challenge
assignments.body: Once per semester, students are asked to self-select from a genre they haven't read before. They read for at least two sustained sessions, then write a paragraph: was it for them, and why or why not? This keeps self-selection from calcifying into habit without curiosity.

mastery: Selects texts without teacher prompting and articulates a clear rationale for the choice when asked.
Sustains focused reading across multiple class sessions without redirection.
Can speak specifically about what they've read — not just plot summary, but what held them and what didn't.
Adjusts reading selections when a text isn't working rather than abandoning reading altogether.
Describes themselves, unprompted, as someone who reads outside of school — or is working toward that.
notes: Vertical alignment: Standard 3 is identical in language across English I–IV. The expectation doesn't escalate syntactically, but the instructional weight should. By English IV, the goal isn't compliance with independent reading time — it's internalization. The student leaving this course should carry the habit past the classroom. Teachers should frame English IV's version of this standard explicitly as a handoff: no one will assign them reading time in college or the workforce.
DOK 2 justification: skill application is the core — selecting and sustaining. Reflection and conferencing can push toward DOK 3, but the standard itself sits at application of reading behavior.

STAAR analog note: Standard 3 is not directly assessed on STAAR. Question stems above mirror how STAAR tests readerly behavior and sustained engagement through the lens of narrator or character experience in prose selections — the closest analog available in a standardized testing context.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Establish Purpose for Reading Assigned and Self-Selected Texts
expectation: Establish purpose for reading assigned and self-selected texts.
overview: By English IV, students should be operating with genuine metacognitive intentionality — not just consuming a text but deciding, before and during reading, what they are there to find. The skill is the difference between a reader and a scanner.
studentFriendly: You decide — before you start and while you're reading — exactly what you're looking for in a text, and that decision shapes everything you notice, question, and remember.
questionStems: Which statement BEST describes the author's purpose in paragraphs X–Y?
A student reads this article to support an argumentative essay. Which sentence from the selection would MOST likely be useful for that purpose?
Based on the introduction, what is the MOST likely reason a reader would consult this text?
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the inference that the author intended this text for an academic audience?
A reader whose purpose is to evaluate the author's argument would MOST likely pay closest attention to which paragraph?
How does the structure of paragraph X contribute to the reader's ability to identify the author's central claim?
Which detail from the selection MOST likely signals a shift in the author's purpose?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, a reader who wanted to understand the author's stance would MOST likely conclude that —
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates the author's intent to persuade rather than inform?
A reader whose purpose is to compare two authors' perspectives would MOST likely use which piece of evidence from this selection?

misconceptions.title: "Establishing Purpose" Means Writing "I Am Reading to Learn"
misconceptions.body: Students write a vague purpose statement at the top of a graphic organizer and consider the task done. Watch for this in senior classes especially — the more familiar the routine, the more hollow it becomes. Push them: a purpose statement that could apply to any text on any day is not a purpose. Teach them that purpose must be specific enough to make some sentences matter more than others.

misconceptions.title: Self-Selected Means No Accountability
misconceptions.body: When students pick their own text, they assume they've already done the hard part and coast through the reading. Teach them that self-selection raises the stakes — you chose this, so you owe it real attention. A reader who selected a text and still can't articulate what they were looking for hasn't read; they've browsed.

misconceptions.title: Purpose Is Set Once and Never Revisited
misconceptions.body: Students establish a purpose in the pre-reading moment and treat it as closed. They don't adjust when a text surprises them or goes somewhere unexpected. The stronger habit is recursive: purpose is a hypothesis. Show them texts where the initial purpose is wrong — where what looked like a personal narrative turns out to be an argument, or where a news article is actually an opinion column — so they practice recalibrating mid-read.

misconceptions.title: Assigned Texts Don't Need a Purpose Beyond "Because the Teacher Said So"
misconceptions.body: Senior students in particular develop a compliance-only relationship with assigned reading. They complete it without interrogating why it was assigned or what they personally need from it. Reframe: even in assigned reading, the student has a purpose — to evaluate, to compare, to be persuaded or to resist persuasion. The assignment sets the text; the reader still has to set the lens.

misconceptions.title: Reading for Evidence Is the Only Valid Purpose
misconceptions.body: Students who've drilled argument writing default to evidence-hunting as their one reading mode. They skim for quotable sentences and miss structure, tone, and the author's own uncertainty. Teach them that purpose can be structural ("I'm reading to understand how this author builds a counterargument"), evaluative ("I'm reading to decide whether this source is credible"), or interpretive — not just extractive.

assignments.name: Purpose Memo
assignments.body: Before each major reading, students write a two-sentence memo: what this text is, and what they specifically need from it. After reading, they write two more sentences: did the text deliver, and did their purpose shift? Collected across a unit, these form a metacognitive reading log.

assignments.name: Same Text, Three Purposes
assignments.body: Give the whole class the same two-page excerpt. Assign a third of the class to read for argument structure, a third for author credibility, a third for emotional appeal. After reading, groups share what they noticed — and what the other groups caught that they missed. The debrief is the lesson.

assignments.name: Self-Selected Source Audit
assignments.body: As part of a research unit, students bring in one self-selected source and write a paragraph explaining (a) why they selected it, (b) what specific purpose it was meant to serve in their argument, and (c) whether it actually served that purpose after reading. This is graded on honesty and precision, not just selection quality.

mastery: Articulates a specific, text-responsive purpose before reading — one that would cause them to pay attention to different sentences than a reader with a different purpose.
Adjusts purpose mid-reading when a text's genre, tone, or argument differs from expectation, without losing comprehension momentum.
Demonstrates different reading behaviors for different purposes — annotating differently when reading for structure versus reading for evidence.
Connects self-selected texts to a clear intellectual or argumentative need, not just general interest.
Reflects after reading on whether the original purpose was met and why, naming specific moments in the text that confirmed or redirected the reading.
notes: Vertical capstone: English I–III all carry this same expectation. By English IV, "establish purpose" should be fully internalized — students are not being introduced to the skill, they are being held to a senior-level standard of metacognitive precision. The vertical ramp is from teacher-scaffolded purpose-setting (English I) to fully independent, text-responsive, multi-purpose reading (English IV).

DOK 2 justification: the core act of establishing purpose requires applying a cognitive framework to a specific text — it is a skill application, not recall. When paired with reflection or comparison across texts, the task extends toward DOK 3.

STAAR note: this TEK does not have a standalone STAAR question type; it underpins nearly every comprehension item on the assessment. Stems above are written to mirror how purpose-driven reading shows up as inference, author's intent, and evidence-selection questions.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Generate Questions About Text Before, During, and After Reading
expectation: Generate questions about text before, during, and after reading to deepen understanding and gain information.
overview: This is the capstone version of a skill students have practiced since elementary school — but by English IV, the expectation is that their questions are driving genuine intellectual work, not compliance. The job is to teach students to interrogate a text rather than receive it.
studentFriendly: You read a difficult text the way a detective works a case — asking questions before you enter, pressing harder when something doesn't add up mid-read, and circling back afterward to figure out what you still don't understand.
questionStems: Which question would BEST help a reader clarify the author's purpose in paragraph X?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which question would a reader MOST likely generate to deepen understanding of the author's central argument?
Which detail from paragraph X MOST likely prompted the question "Why does the author shift tone here?"
A student reading the passage stops after paragraph X and asks, "What evidence supports this claim?" Which sentence from the passage BEST answers that question?
Before reading a text with the title "[title]," a reader would MOST likely generate which question?
Which question BEST reflects a reader's attempt to synthesize information from both passages?
After reading both selections, a reader asks, "How do the authors differ in their assumptions about the audience?" Which evidence from the passages MOST directly answers this question?
A reader generates the question "What is left unresolved?" after finishing the passage. Which detail from the text MOST supports the idea that a central tension remains unresolved?
Which question would MOST help a reader evaluate the reliability of the evidence in paragraph X?
Based on the passage, which inference could a careful reader reach by asking "What does the author NOT say, and why?"

misconceptions.title: Questions Are for Before-Reading Only
misconceptions.body: Students treat pre-reading questions — activated from the title, the headings, the first paragraph — as the whole task, then switch to passive absorption the moment the reading starts. Watch for students who front-load good questions and then stop generating. Teach them that the most valuable questions are the ones that emerge mid-text when something unexpected happens: a turn in argument, a contradiction, a gap in the logic. Questioning is a continuous act, not a warm-up.

misconceptions.title: A Good Question Is One You Already Know the Answer To
misconceptions.body: Senior students, especially those who have been trained for surface-level quizzes, ask questions they can immediately answer — "What is the setting?" "Who is the narrator?" These are checks, not inquiries. Push them toward questions that unsettle: "Why does this author assume I already agree?" "What would have to be true for this claim to hold?" The questions that make them uncomfortable are the questions doing real comprehension work.

misconceptions.title: More Questions Means More Understanding
misconceptions.body: Students equate generating a long list of questions with deep engagement. Length is not depth. A student who generates fifteen questions about vocabulary and zero questions about structure, argument, or implication has missed the point. Teach them to audit their own question lists: Are all your questions at the same level? Are any of them asking about how the text works, not just what it says?

misconceptions.title: After-Reading Questions Are Wrap-Up, Not Follow-Through
misconceptions.body: Students treat post-reading questions as a cooling-down ritual — "What was the main idea?" "What did I like?" Reframe after-reading as the hardest phase: now that you've read the whole thing, what do you still not know? What did the author not resolve? What would you ask the author if you could? After-reading questions should open outward, not close the text down.

misconceptions.title: Questioning Is a Private Skill That Doesn't Need to Be Taught
misconceptions.body: Some teachers assume good readers naturally generate good questions and struggling readers just need more exposure. The move students need is explicit: model your own mid-reading questions aloud, mark the textual triggers that prompted them, and show what you do with a question once you have it. Questioning is a transferable technique, not a personality trait.

assignments.name: Question Log
assignments.body: Students keep a three-column reading log — Before, During, After — for a complex informational text or essay. At the end, they identify their three strongest questions and write one paragraph per question explaining what prompted it and what they did with it.

assignments.name: Question Audit
assignments.body: After students generate their question lists, they categorize each question by level: surface (what/who/when), structural (how/why the text works), evaluative (is this valid/fair?), and generative (what does this open up beyond the text). They must have at least two questions in the last two categories before the assignment is complete.

assignments.name: Dead-End vs. Generative Questions
assignments.body: Students compare two questions — one that leads them back into the text for an answer and one that leads them outward to new inquiry. In a short essay, they explain why the generative question is harder but more valuable, using evidence from their reading.

assignments.name: Socratic Seminar with Question Currency
assignments.body: Students enter a Socratic seminar with three written questions — one before-reading, one mid-reading, one post-reading. They may only speak when they are posing or developing one of their questions. At the end, they reflect in writing: which question moved the conversation most, and why?

assignments.name: Parallel Text Questioning
assignments.body: Students read two texts on the same subject. They generate separate question logs for each, then write a comparative paragraph identifying the question that ONE text answered but the other left open — and arguing why that gap matters.

mastery: Generates questions at all three stages without being prompted, and the questions at each stage are qualitatively distinct — not reruns of the same concern in different words.
Asks questions that address argument structure, authorial choices, and gaps in evidence — not only surface-level content recall.
Uses their own questions as reading tools: returns to the text to answer them, revises them when the text redirects, and carries unanswered questions into post-reading reflection.
Distinguishes between a question that the text can answer and a question the text opens but cannot close — and treats both as valuable.
In writing, demonstrates that questioning shaped their analysis: cites the question that led them to a specific textual detail, not just the detail itself.
notes: Vertical ramp from English III: the core skill (generate questions before, during, after) is identical in wording — the English IV expectation is in the sophistication of the questions produced and the independence with which students apply the strategy to complex, college-level texts. Instruction should resist re-teaching the mechanic and instead raise the bar on question quality.

DOK 3 justification: strategic thinking is required — students must select which questions are worth pursuing, evaluate their own question lists for depth, and use generated questions as interpretive tools rather than checklists.

No direct STAAR analog for "generating questions" as a discrete tested skill; STAAR tests the comprehension outcomes that questioning produces (inference, synthesis, evaluation). Question stems above mirror how STAAR tests what a careful reader would ask or conclude, framing questioning through its results on the page. Note this distinction when teaching students to connect the strategy to test performance.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Make and Correct or Confirm Predictions
expectation: Make and correct or confirm predictions using text features, characteristics of genre, and structures.
overview: By English IV, prediction is a metacognitive act — not a guess at what happens next, but a disciplined reading of how texts signal their own moves. Students at this level should be using genre knowledge and structural cues as sophisticated forecasting tools, then holding themselves accountable when the text confirms or overturns their inference.
studentFriendly: You can read a text's genre markers, structural patterns, and features to anticipate where it's going — and when it surprises you, you can revise your thinking and explain exactly what tricked you and why.
questionStems: Based on the title and subheadings in the article, a reader would MOST likely predict that the author will —
Which text feature in paragraph X BEST signals what the following section will argue?
Read paragraphs X–Y. Which prediction about the author's next move is BEST supported by the structure of the text so far?
A reader who predicted [claim] after reading paragraph X would MOST likely correct that prediction based on —
The genre characteristics of this selection suggest that the author will MOST likely resolve the central conflict by —
Which detail from paragraph X BEST supports the inference that the author's argument will shift in the following paragraphs?
Based on the organizational structure of the text, what does the reader MOST likely expect the final paragraph to accomplish?
A reader predicts after paragraph X that [outcome]. Which sentence from the text BEST confirms or overturns that prediction?
The headings and transitional language in this article MOST nearly suggest that the author will next —
Which conclusion about the text's structure is BEST supported by the evidence in paragraphs X–Y?

misconceptions.title: A Prediction Is a Guess, Not a Commitment
misconceptions.body: Students make a prediction and immediately abandon it the moment anything surprising happens, treating the prediction as throwaway. Teach them that a prediction is a contract with the text — when you revise it, you owe an explanation: what evidence did you use, what evidence proved you wrong, and what does that reversal tell you about how the text works? Watch for students who skip straight to "I was wrong" without doing the analytical work of explaining why.

misconceptions.title: Text Features Are Just Navigation Tools
misconceptions.body: Students treat headings, captions, bold text, and subheadings as skimming aids rather than rhetorical signals. They read around them rather than through them. Push them deeper: a heading doesn't just label what's coming — it frames how the reader is supposed to receive it. What does the heading assume the reader already knows? What does it promise? Is that promise kept?

misconceptions.title: Genre Knowledge Means Knowing What Genre It Is
misconceptions.body: Students think naming the genre ("it's an argumentative essay") is the analytical move. The richer move is knowing what the genre commits the author to — what structural expectations a persuasive op-ed creates, what an argument in that form must do before it can close, what a Shakespearean tragedy is forecasting from the opening scene. Teach them to use genre as a prediction engine, not a label.

misconceptions.title: Confirming a Prediction Means You Read Well
misconceptions.body: When students' predictions come true, they often stop there — they got it right, so the analysis is done. The confirmation is actually where the work begins: what textual features allowed you to see that move coming? Could another reader have missed it? What does the author gain by being predictable here — or by subverting the expectation? Confirming a prediction should open more questions, not close them.

misconceptions.title: Structure Only Matters in Informational Texts
misconceptions.body: Students reserve structural analysis for nonfiction and ignore it in literary texts. But narrative structure, dramatic arc, and poetic form are prediction engines too. A reversal at the volta of a sonnet, a shift in a short story's point of view, a sudden change in a play's pacing — all of these are structural signals. Teach students that every genre has a grammar of expectations, and skilled readers are always reading that grammar alongside the words.

assignments.name: Prediction Log with Accountability
assignments.body: Students read a complex text in three segments, pausing after each to record their current predictions and the specific textual evidence — features, genre signals, structural moves — behind them. After the final segment, they write a paragraph analyzing which predictions held, which were corrected, and what that pattern reveals about the text's strategy.

assignments.name: Genre Swap Analysis
assignments.body: Students read two texts making similar arguments — one a formal essay, one an op-ed or column. They predict, based solely on genre and structure, where each will go before reading more than the first third. After reading both fully, they compare which genre's conventions made prediction easier and why.

assignments.name: The Structural Autopsy
assignments.body: After finishing a full text, students work backward: they identify every moment the text signaled a structural move (transition, heading, genre convention, tonal shift) and assess whether those signals made the move predictable or masked it deliberately. Final question: is the text playing straight with its structure, or using it as misdirection?

mastery: Uses specific text features, genre conventions, and structural signals — not vague intuition — to justify predictions before and during reading.
When a prediction is overturned, produces a precise explanation of which evidence misled the original inference and what the correction reveals about the text's design.
Treats confirmation of a prediction as an occasion for further analysis — what did the text do to make that move visible or invisible?
Applies structural and genre-based prediction to literary texts with the same rigor applied to informational ones.
Distinguishes between a prediction the text supports and one the reader wanted — holds personal expectation separate from textual evidence.
notes: Vertical alignment: English III 4(C) carries the same expectation verbatim. The ramp at English IV is not in the standard's language but in the complexity of texts applied — senior-level literary and informational texts demand prediction work that draws on more sophisticated genre knowledge (e.g., Shakespearean tragedy, long-form journalism, philosophical argument) and denser structural layering. The accountability move — correcting OR confirming, not just predicting — is where English IV instruction should put its weight.

DOK 3 justification: using text features and genre knowledge to generate and evaluate predictions requires strategic thinking and inference, not mere recall or skill application; DOK 4 would require extended reasoning across texts, which this standard could support in assignment design but does not explicitly require.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog for oral or performance contexts; stems above model how STAAR tests comprehension-based prediction through prose passage analysis, paragraph reference, and inference-from-structure framing.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Create Mental Images to Deepen Understanding
expectation: Create mental images to deepen understanding.
overview: This TEK is easy to dismiss as a beginner skill — it's not. By English IV, visualization is the mechanism by which sophisticated readers track sensory detail, monitor gaps in their comprehension, and construct the kind of layered understanding that complex literary and argumentative texts demand. The goal is conscious, strategic imagery, not daydreaming.
studentFriendly: You read a passage and your mind builds a picture — and how well you can build that picture tells you exactly where the text is working on you and where you've lost the thread.
questionStems: Which detail from paragraph X MOST contributes to the reader's ability to visualize the setting?
The author's description in paragraph X MOST likely creates an image of —
Based on the details in paragraphs X–Y, the reader can BEST picture the narrator as —
Which sentence from the selection BEST helps the reader visualize the central conflict?
The imagery in paragraph X MOST nearly suggests that —
Which phrase from the selection MOST clearly appeals to the reader's sense of [sight/sound/touch]?
The author uses the details in paragraph X to create an image that MOST likely conveys —
A reader would MOST likely picture the mood in paragraph X as —
Based on the figurative language in paragraph X, the reader can infer that the speaker views [subject] as —
How do the visual details in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the reader's understanding of the [character's/narrator's] situation?

misconceptions.title: Visualization Is Something You Either Have or Don't
misconceptions.body: Students treat this as a natural talent and opt out when they don't automatically "see" something. Teach them that visualization is a habit built by slowing down and interrogating the text's sensory language — not a passive gift. When a student says "I just don't picture things when I read," that's a diagnostic, not an excuse. Ask them what they do notice, and build from there.

misconceptions.title: A Clear Picture Means Full Understanding
misconceptions.body: Students who can visualize a scene confidently sometimes stop there, assuming the image is the meaning. Push them deeper: the image is a doorway, not a destination. Ask what the image implies — what does it feel like, what is conspicuously absent from the picture, what would change if the author had described it differently? Visualization that doesn't generate inference is just decoration.

misconceptions.title: Only Fiction Uses This Skill
misconceptions.body: Students compartmentalize mental imagery as a literary fiction move and switch it off during argumentative or informational reading. Reframe this: visualizing the example in an op-ed, the process in an explanation, the data behind a statistic — these are comprehension acts too. A reader who can't picture what an argument is describing hasn't understood the argument.

misconceptions.title: More Detail Always Produces a Better Image
misconceptions.body: Students sometimes believe that a longer, more explicitly described passage is easier to visualize and therefore superior, while spare prose "doesn't give you enough." Teach them that minimalist writing demands more from the reader's imagination — and that productive visualization often means constructing what the author chose not to say. The most powerful mental images are often built from what's withheld.

misconceptions.title: The Mental Image Must Match What the Author "Intended"
misconceptions.body: Students worry about getting the image "wrong" and freeze. Reframe visualization as a personal and active process: the image you construct is data about your reading, and discrepancies between your image and a peer's are worth investigating — not correcting. The question is always "What in the text produces that image?" not "Is my picture correct?"

assignments.name: Image Audit
assignments.body: Students read a dense passage and stop after every paragraph to write three words capturing the image they're holding. At the end, they compare their image log to the actual textual details that generated it. Where did the text produce the image and where did they fill in gaps?

assignments.name: The Missing Frame
assignments.body: Students identify a moment in a complex text where the author deliberately withholds visual detail. They write one paragraph arguing what the author's choice to leave the image incomplete accomplishes — and what a reader who doesn't notice the gap might miss.

assignments.name: Cross-Genre Visualization
assignments.body: Students apply visualization to an argumentative text — an editorial, a Supreme Court opinion excerpt, a policy brief. They sketch (literally or in words) the scene or process the argument is describing and then evaluate how accurately the author's language supports that picture.

assignments.name: Peer Image Comparison
assignments.body: Two students read the same passage independently and write a description of the mental image they formed. They compare and use textual evidence to arbitrate differences — the goal is not consensus, but identifying which textual signals drove divergent images.

mastery: Identifies specific words and phrases in a text that generate sensory images and explains the comprehension work those images do.
Recognizes when a deliberate gap in sensory detail is a rhetorical or literary choice, not an oversight, and articulates the effect of that gap.
Applies visualization to informational and argumentative texts, not only fiction or narrative prose.
Uses discrepancies between their mental image and a peer's as an occasion for close textual rereading rather than confusion or capitulation.
Articulates what a mental image implies — inference, tone, characterization — not just what it depicts.
notes: Vertical ramp: English III 4D establishes the same expectation; English IV carries it forward in the context of more complex and genre-diverse texts — including AP-level literary and argumentative prose where the demand on visualization is higher and the payoff (inference, tone recognition, gap detection) is more consequential.
DOK 2 justification: the base skill is application of a strategy; DOK rises to 3 when students are asked to explain why an image matters or what a deliberate gap accomplishes.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog as a standalone tested item; stems above mirror how comprehension strategies surface inside literary and informational STAAR passages — through imagery-based inference and author's craft questions.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Make Connections to Experience, Other Texts, and Society
expectation: Make connections to personal experiences, ideas in other texts, and society.
overview: By English IV, connection-making is not a warmup or a comprehension check — it is an analytical move. Students are expected to read a text against something real: their own experience, another text's argument, a social pattern they can name and examine. The skill lives at the intersection of reading and critical thinking about the world.
studentFriendly: You can read a text and explain how it connects to something you have lived, something you have read, or something happening in the larger world — and that connection actually deepens your understanding of the text, not just yourself.
questionStems: Which statement BEST explains how the ideas in paragraph X connect to a pattern in contemporary society?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the author's argument is MOST similar to the argument made in which of the following scenarios?
A reader who has experienced [situation] would MOST likely interpret the narrator's reaction in paragraph X as —
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the inference that the author intends the text to reflect a broader social concern?
How does the conflict described in paragraph X connect to a theme found in other texts about [topic]?
The situation in paragraph X is MOST similar to —
Based on the selection, a reader could conclude that the author's personal experience MOST likely shaped which of the following ideas?
Which detail from paragraph X BEST illustrates a connection between the text and a real-world social issue?
The author's perspective in paragraphs X–Y is MOST nearly aligned with the argument that —
Which sentence from the selection BEST develops the idea that individual experience and larger social forces are connected?

misconceptions.title: Connections Are a Feelings Exercise, Not a Reading Skill
misconceptions.body: Students treat text-to-self connection as emotional response and stop there — "this reminded me of when my grandmother died." Push them deeper: what does the connection actually reveal about the text's meaning? The connection is evidence, not the conclusion. Teach them that a personal connection earns its place in analysis only when it illuminates something about how the text is working.

misconceptions.title: Text-to-World Means Bringing Up Current Events
misconceptions.body: Students think making a societal connection means name-dropping a news story and moving on. What they need to do is trace the logic — identify the social dynamic the text engages, explain how the text positions itself on that dynamic, and then evaluate how accurately or usefully it does so. Watch for students who mention a social issue in one sentence and never return to the text.

misconceptions.title: Any Two Texts That Share a Theme Are "Connected"
misconceptions.body: When students write text-to-text connections, they often identify surface similarities — both texts have a protagonist who loses someone — and call it done. The real move is to explain what the comparison reveals that reading either text alone would not. Teach them: if the connection doesn't change how you read one of the texts, it hasn't done anything yet.

misconceptions.title: Personal Experience Is Always Relevant
misconceptions.body: Not every text invites the same kind of personal connection, and students who reflexively insert their own experience into every analysis often bury the text under autobiography. At English IV, students must discern when their experience genuinely illuminates the text's meaning versus when it distracts from it. Watch for responses where the student's story crowds out any real textual engagement.

misconceptions.title: Society Means "Everyone" or "People in General"
misconceptions.body: Students make vague societal connections by invoking "society" as an undifferentiated mass — "this connects to how society treats people." Push them to name a specific institution, pattern, system, or historical moment. The more specific the societal anchor, the more intellectually honest and analytically useful the connection becomes. Vague social references are a hedge, not an argument.

assignments.name: The Triple-Column Connection Log
assignments.body: While reading a full text, students maintain a three-column log: one column for textual evidence, one for the type of connection (self / text / world), and one for the analytical payoff — what does this connection reveal about the text's meaning? Students bring the completed log to Socratic seminar and use it as evidence.

assignments.name: Cross-Text Argument Comparison
assignments.body: Students pair the current text with one text they have read previously this year. They write a focused two-page essay arguing what the comparison reveals that neither text reveals alone. The thesis must name the comparative insight, not just the shared topic.

assignments.name: Social System Spotlight
assignments.body: Students identify one social system, institution, or historical pattern the text engages — named specifically, not vaguely. They write a one-paragraph analysis explaining how the text responds to that system and whether the text's position is convincing. Grade for specificity and textual grounding.

assignments.name: Connection Audit
assignments.body: Students draft a body paragraph making a text-to-self or text-to-world connection. Then they swap with a partner who assesses one thing: does the connection actually change how you read the text, or is it decorative? Partners revise based on feedback before final submission.

mastery: Identifies a specific connection — to a lived experience, a named text, or a defined social pattern — and explains what it reveals about the text's meaning rather than simply stating its existence.
Selects the type of connection (self / text / world) that does the most analytical work for a given passage, rather than defaulting to the easiest or most personal.
Grounds every connection claim in textual evidence — the connection is always a route back into the text, not a detour away from it.
Names societal connections with precision: a specific institution, system, or historical context, not a general reference to "society" or "the world today."
Sustains a comparative reading across two texts, articulating what the comparison reveals that single-text reading would miss.
notes: Vertical alignment: English III 4(E) carries the same language verbatim. The ramp in English IV is expectational, not terminological — students are now in their senior year working with more complex texts, wider reading histories, and stronger capacity for social analysis. Teachers should expect more precision and intellectual risk in how connections are framed and justified, not simply the same move repeated.

DOK 3 justification: making a substantiated connection requires strategic reasoning — selecting relevant evidence, constructing a logical bridge between text and context, and defending the relevance of the connection. The skill exceeds recall or skill application and enters extended reasoning territory.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is rarely assessed as a stand-alone item but underlies inference, theme, and author's purpose questions throughout STAAR. The stems above mirror how connection-based reasoning surfaces in those item types.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Make Inferences and Use Evidence to Support Understanding
expectation: Make inferences and use evidence to support understanding.
overview: By English IV, inference is the baseline — students are expected to read gaps and silences in complex texts and ground every interpretive move in specific evidence. The skill here is not just "make a guess"; it is producing reasoning chains where evidence precedes and constrains the inference, not the other way around.
studentFriendly: You can read a difficult text, figure out what it implies but never directly states, and point to the exact words that led you there — not just what you felt or assumed.
questionStems: Based on paragraphs X–Y, the reader can infer that the narrator MOST likely —
Which piece of evidence from the selection BEST supports the inference that the character believes —
The details in paragraph X suggest that the author MOST likely wants the reader to conclude —
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the inference made in paragraph X?
Based on the evidence in paragraphs X–Y, what can the reader MOST reasonably infer about —
The reader can infer from the information in paragraph X that —
Which of the following conclusions is BEST supported by evidence from the selection?
Based on the details in paragraph X, the reader can MOST likely infer that the speaker's attitude toward [subject] is —
Which sentence from the selection provides the STRONGEST evidence that —
The evidence in paragraphs X–Y MOST strongly supports the conclusion that —

misconceptions.title: Inference Means Guessing What "Feels Right"
misconceptions.body: Students treat inference as an intuition exercise — they land on an interpretation and then hunt for evidence that confirms it after the fact. The actual move runs in the opposite direction: evidence first, inference second. Teach them to locate the specific sentences or details that force a particular conclusion before they name the conclusion. Watch for students who cite vague "tone" or "mood" as their evidence; push them to the exact word or phrase doing the work.

misconceptions.title: Any Detail From the Text Counts as Evidence
misconceptions.body: Students will cite the nearest sentence to their claim regardless of whether it actually supports it. They conflate "I found a sentence" with "I found evidence." Teach them that evidence must be both relevant to the claim AND sufficient to make the inference more likely than alternatives. If a different sentence would support the opposite inference just as well, the evidence is weak — name that distinction explicitly.

misconceptions.title: Stated Information and Inferred Information Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Some students quote what the text says explicitly and call it an inference. Teach them that a stated fact is not an inference — inference lives in the gap between what the text says and what it means or implies. Have them practice distinguishing "The text says X" from "Because the text says X, we can conclude Y, which is never stated directly."

misconceptions.title: A Strong Inference Needs Only One Piece of Evidence
misconceptions.body: Students stop at the first supporting detail and declare the case made. In complex literary and argumentative texts, single-sentence evidence is often insufficient because authors build meaning through accumulation. Push them to triangulate: two or three pieces of evidence pointing the same direction make an inference defensible; one piece makes it a possibility. Teach them the phrase "this is supported throughout the text by..." as a forcing function for thoroughness.

misconceptions.title: If the Inference Is Interesting, It Must Be Valid
misconceptions.body: Students invest in creative interpretations — which is good — but then resist revision when contradicting evidence surfaces. They've decided what the text "really means" and filter out data. Teach them that an inference that cannot survive a counterexample is a theory, not a conclusion. Model what it looks like to say, "I thought X, but paragraph Y actually contradicts that, so I'm revising to Z."

assignments.name: Evidence Ladder
assignments.body: Students select a complex passage and build an evidence ladder: list three separate pieces of textual evidence, then write the one inference all three support together. They must explain why the inference requires all three rungs — why one or two alone wouldn't get them there.

assignments.name: Two-Column Inference Log
assignments.body: Students read a full text and maintain a running two-column log: left column records the exact quoted evidence; right column records the inference drawn. At the end, they identify which inferences are strongest (most evidence, most specific) and which are weakest (one piece, vague) and revise or discard the weak ones.

assignments.name: Inference vs. Stated Fact Sort
assignments.body: Given a list of ten statements about a text — five directly stated, five inferred — students sort them and justify each placement with a citation. Discuss as a class where the hard cases fell and why.

assignments.name: Counterevidence Stress Test
assignments.body: Students share their strongest inference with a partner. The partner's job is to find one piece of textual evidence that complicates or contradicts it. The original student must either defend the inference against the challenge or revise it. Written reflection follows: what changed, and what does the final inference actually claim?

mastery: Consistently identifies what is implied rather than stated and articulates the inferential gap explicitly.
Selects evidence that is both relevant and sufficient — not just the nearest available sentence — and explains why it supports the inference.
Uses multiple pieces of evidence to triangulate a defensible conclusion rather than resting on a single detail.
Willingly revises an inference when contradicting evidence is identified, and explains the revision.
Distinguishes between a strong inference (multiple supporting details, no direct contradiction) and a weak one (single detail, alternative readings equally plausible).
notes: Vertical alignment: English III 4(F) carries the same expectation verbatim. The English IV ramp is not in the wording of the standard but in the complexity of the texts applied to — senior-level literary and informational texts, including global and cross-cultural works, demand inference chains of greater depth. Mastery at this level means sustaining the skill across extended, unfamiliar, and syntactically demanding texts without prompting.

DOK 3 justification: making and defending an inference requires strategic thinking — selecting evidence, constructing a reasoning chain, and evaluating the inference against alternatives. DOK 2 would cover locating stated information; DOK 3 covers the inferential reasoning this standard requires.

This standard has a clear STAAR analog. Inference and evidence-support questions are among the highest-frequency item types on the STAAR English I–II assessments; English IV students should be fluent in recognizing the exact phrasing ("based on paragraph X, the reader can infer," "which sentence BEST supports the inference") as testing language for this skill.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Evaluate Details to Analyze Key Ideas
expectation: Evaluate details read to analyze key ideas.
overview: This is the capstone comprehension move in English IV: students don't just identify or explain details, they weigh them — asking which details do real work, which ones are decorative or distracting, and what the selection's central ideas actually depend on. The job is to build readers who don't treat everything on the page as equally important.
studentFriendly: You can read a complex text and judge which details actually matter, which ones are just filler, and what those key details reveal about the central ideas the author is building.
questionStems: Which detail from paragraph X BEST supports the central idea of the selection?
The detail in paragraph X MOST likely develops the author's key idea by —
Based on the details in paragraphs X–Y, which statement BEST analyzes the author's central argument?
Which sentence from the selection MOST clearly contributes to the reader's understanding of the author's key idea?
The author includes the detail in paragraph X MOST likely to —
Which detail from the selection would BEST support the inference that the author believes —
How do the details in paragraphs X and Y work together to develop a key idea in the selection?
Which of the following details from the selection is LEAST relevant to the author's central claim?
Based on the details in paragraph X, the reader can conclude that —
The author's use of the detail in paragraph X MOST nearly suggests that —

misconceptions.title: Every Detail Is a Key Detail
misconceptions.body: Students treat the text as a flat list of equally important information and try to incorporate everything into their analysis. Teach them that evaluation requires judgment — they must argue for why a detail matters, not just prove it exists. Push them to ask: if this detail disappeared, would the key idea collapse or survive? That question forces prioritization.

misconceptions.title: "Analyze" Means Retell with More Words
misconceptions.body: Students write elaborate summaries and call it analysis. Watch for responses that walk through the text chronologically, adding transitions like "then the author says." Real analysis explains what a detail does — the logical or rhetorical work it performs — not what it says. Redirect them: describe the effect of the detail, not its contents.

misconceptions.title: The First Sentence of Each Paragraph Is Always the Key Idea
misconceptions.body: Students trained on topic-sentence hunting apply that shortcut universally and miss texts where the key idea emerges across multiple paragraphs or is embedded in a subordinate clause. Show them texts where the controlling idea only becomes visible through accumulation. Teach them to read the whole paragraph before crowning any sentence the "main idea."

misconceptions.title: Strong Details Are Long or Complicated
misconceptions.body: Students equate importance with complexity and overlook the single, quietly devastating detail that does more work than any paragraph around it. A statistic, a proper name, a date, an anecdote tucked into a dependent clause — any of these can be load-bearing. Teach them to slow down at small, specific things rather than scanning past them toward longer passages.

misconceptions.title: Evaluation Means Opinion, Not Argument
misconceptions.body: When asked to "evaluate," students write what they personally find interesting or moving rather than building an evidence-based case about significance. Teach them that evaluation is a claim requiring defense: not "I found this detail compelling" but "this detail is essential because without it the author's key idea has no empirical grounding." The difference is the because.

assignments.name: Detail Ranking
assignments.body: Students read a 2–3 page excerpt and rank the five most significant details in order of importance to the key idea. They write one sentence defending each ranking choice. Compare rankings across the class and debate disagreements.

assignments.name: Cut and Consequence
assignments.body: Students select one detail they argue is nonessential, remove it from a printed transcript, and write a paragraph explaining what the key idea loses — or doesn't lose — without it. The move forces them to test their own evaluation.

assignments.name: Detail vs. Key Idea Map
assignments.body: Students create a two-column document: left column lists specific textual details, right column states the key idea each detail supports, complicates, or undermines. The mapping must be argued, not assumed.

assignments.name: Cross-Text Detail Comparison
assignments.body: Students read two texts on the same topic and identify the two most significant details from each. They then write a short analysis explaining how the chosen details in each text shape different versions of a shared key idea.

assignments.name: Detail Audit
assignments.body: Students receive a sample student-written analysis full of summarized details presented as analysis. They annotate it, flagging every place where detail recitation substitutes for evaluation, and rewrite two passages to model the correct move.

mastery: Distinguishes between details that are incidental to a text and details that are structurally necessary to a key idea.
Explains the logical or rhetorical function of a detail — what it does, not merely what it says.
Defends evaluative claims about detail significance with textual evidence and explicit reasoning, not personal response.
Recognizes when a key idea depends on accumulation across multiple details rather than a single moment.
Produces written analysis in which detail selection is argued, not assumed, and where the relationship between evidence and key idea is made explicit.
notes: Vertical alignment: English III 4G asks students to evaluate details to analyze key ideas — English IV holds the same expectation but operates at senior-level text complexity, with longer and more argumentatively dense sources. The ramp is not in the cognitive verb but in the sophistication of texts against which the skill is applied and the independence expected in the analysis.

DOK 4 justification: evaluating which details are essential to key ideas requires students to synthesize across a text, build a reasoned argument about significance, and defend that argument — sustained reasoning beyond recall or skill application.

This TEK has a direct STAAR analog: released STAAR items for Comprehension frequently ask students to identify which detail BEST supports a central idea or contributes to the development of a key claim. The stems in this entry mirror that testing vocabulary directly.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Synthesize Information Across Text Types
expectation: Synthesize information from a variety of text types to create new understanding.
overview: This is the apex comprehension move — not summarizing multiple texts, not comparing them, but building something that didn't exist before reading them together. English IV students are expected to hold information from a poem, an essay, a data set, a speech, and a news article simultaneously and produce a claim or insight that none of those texts could generate alone.
studentFriendly: You can read across wildly different kinds of texts — a poem, a graph, an argument, a memoir — and build an understanding that none of them could give you on their own.
questionStems: Which statement BEST synthesizes the central ideas presented in the excerpt from Source 1 and the data in Source 2?
Based on Sources 1, 2, and 3, which conclusion is MOST strongly supported by all three texts?
The author of Source 1 and the author of Source 2 would MOST likely agree that —
Which sentence from Source 3 BEST develops an idea introduced in paragraph X of Source 1?
A student reading all three sources would MOST likely conclude that —
Which of the following statements synthesizes information from both the literary text and the informational text in a way that creates new understanding?
How does the information in Source 2 change or complicate the argument made in paragraph X of Source 1?
Which claim is supported by evidence from at least two of the three sources?
Based on all three sources, what is the MOST likely reason the authors reach different conclusions about the same topic?
Which statement presents a new understanding that could ONLY be reached by reading Sources 1, 2, and 3 together?

misconceptions.title: Synthesis Is Just a Fancier Summary
misconceptions.body: Students collect one point from each source and stack them in order — Source 1 says X, Source 2 says Y, Source 3 says Z — and call it synthesis. That's a list, not a new understanding. Teach them that synthesis requires a move across the sources: what do they produce together that none of them produces alone? If their "synthesis" could have been written after reading only one source, they haven't synthesized yet.

misconceptions.title: "A Variety of Text Types" Means More Paragraphs
misconceptions.body: Students treat a poem and an essay as the same thing with different line breaks. The power of cross-text-type synthesis is precisely that different formats carry different kinds of knowledge — a graph knows things a lyric essay doesn't, and vice versa. Push them to name what each text TYPE can and cannot show, and then ask what happens when those different kinds of knowledge are read against each other.

misconceptions.title: New Understanding Means a New Opinion
misconceptions.body: Students think synthesis produces their personal take — "Reading all this made me think we should care more about climate." That's a response, not a synthesis. New understanding is an insight that is accountable to the texts, not just triggered by them. Teach them to trace the claim back: which specific information from which sources combined to produce this understanding? If they can't trace it, they haven't built it yet.

misconceptions.title: The Strongest Source Wins
misconceptions.body: When sources conflict, students resolve the tension by picking a winner — usually the most authoritative-sounding source — and discarding the others. Real synthesis holds the tension. Conflicting sources aren't a problem to solve; they're evidence that the phenomenon is complex. Watch for students who quietly drop a source that complicates their thesis. That dropped source is usually the most important one.

misconceptions.title: Synthesis Happens at the End
misconceptions.body: Students treat synthesis as a conclusion move — they read everything, then synthesize at the last paragraph. But synthesis is a reading practice, not a writing stage. They should be noting connections, tensions, and emergent ideas as they move across texts. Teach them to annotate for synthesis: "This complicates Source 1" or "This is what Source 2 couldn't show." The written synthesis is just the articulation of work that's been happening all along.

assignments.name: Three-Source Mosaic
assignments.body: Assign students a thematic question (e.g., "What does it mean to belong to a place?"). Provide one poem, one data-driven article, and one personal essay on the topic. Students write a one-page synthesis argument — not a compare-contrast, but a new claim — with textual evidence from all three. Grade for whether the claim requires all three sources to exist.

assignments.name: The Dropped Source
assignments.body: Students draft a synthesis using three sources, then deliberately remove one source and revise. They submit both drafts and a short paragraph explaining how the synthesis changed — or arguing that it shouldn't have changed and defending why. Forces them to interrogate what each source is actually contributing.

assignments.name: Genre Switch Synthesis
assignments.body: Students synthesize information from two texts of the same type (two essays, two articles) and two texts of different types (a poem and a chart). They write one paragraph each and then compare: how did the text types change what was synthesizable? The reflection is the assignment.

assignments.name: Live Synthesis Build
assignments.body: Project three short texts (one per slide). After each text, students write one sentence capturing what they now understand that they didn't before reading that text. After all three, they write a synthesis statement. The process is visible; the teacher can see where understanding accumulates or collapses.

assignments.name: Cross-Text Panel Debate
assignments.body: Students are assigned one source each and become its "expert." In small groups, each student presents the core insight of their source. Then the group must produce a collective synthesis claim that no one person could have generated alone. Written up individually after the discussion.

mastery: Produces a claim or insight that is traceable to at least two distinct text types and could not have been generated from any single source alone.
Accounts for tensions and contradictions across sources rather than resolving them by discarding conflicting evidence.
Names what each text type uniquely contributes — what a graph can show that a poem cannot, and vice versa — and uses that awareness to build stronger synthesis.
Annotates across texts during reading, noting connections and emergent ideas before writing begins.
Distinguishes between summary (what the sources say), comparison (how sources differ), and synthesis (what they produce together) and can move deliberately between those modes.
notes: Vertical ramp: English III's 4(H) requires synthesizing "information from a variety of sources" — the same cognitive move, but English IV adds "text types" as an explicit variable. The ramp is from source quantity to source diversity; students must now reckon with what different formats of knowledge do differently, not just reconcile more of the same.

DOK 4 justification: creating new understanding that extends beyond any individual source requires extended reasoning, cross-text connection, and original intellectual construction — the hallmarks of DOK 4.

No direct STAAR analog as a standalone synthesis-across-text-types prompt, but the Paired Selections section of STAAR (paired literary + informational) is the closest testing surface. Question Stems are modeled on STAAR's cross-text synthesis items and the Expository Writing prompt, which requires synthesis across multiple provided sources.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Monitor Comprehension and Adjust When Understanding Breaks Down
expectation: Monitor comprehension and make adjustments such as re-reading, using background knowledge, asking questions, annotating, and using outside sources when understanding breaks down.
overview: This TEK is about metacognitive honesty — knowing when you've lost the thread and actually doing something about it. By English IV, students should have a repertoire of repair strategies and the discipline to deploy them without prompting, even with college-level and AP texts.
studentFriendly: You can tell when you've stopped actually understanding what you're reading — and you have a set of moves (re-reading, annotating, asking questions, pulling in outside context) that you automatically reach for instead of drifting forward in the text hoping it sorts itself out.
questionStems: The reader in paragraph X re-reads the previous paragraph before continuing. Which evidence from the selection BEST explains why this adjustment would help the reader's comprehension?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which question would MOST likely help a reader who has lost track of the author's argument?
Which sentence from the selection would MOST likely require a reader to consult an outside source to fully understand its meaning?
A student annotates the phrase [phrase] in paragraph X with a question mark. Which statement BEST explains what aspect of the text the student is flagging?
The author's use of [term/reference] in paragraph X assumes the reader brings which background knowledge to the text?
Which detail from paragraphs X–Y would MOST likely cause a careful reader to stop and re-read the preceding section?
A reader pauses after paragraph X because the argument seems to contradict an earlier claim. Which sentence from the selection BEST identifies the source of that confusion?
Based on the selection, which inference about the author's intended audience MOST likely explains why certain terms are left undefined in paragraph X?
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates a moment where a reader's background knowledge about [topic] would directly support comprehension?
A student annotating the selection marks paragraph X as unclear. Which outside source would MOST likely resolve the confusion — and which detail from the paragraph supports that conclusion?

misconceptions.title: Reading Forward Is the Same as Understanding
misconceptions.body: Students treat finishing a passage as proof they comprehended it. They move through confused text without pausing because stopping feels like failure. Teach them that confusion is diagnostic data, not a character flaw — the reader who pauses and adjusts is more sophisticated than the one who coasts forward hoping clarity arrives. Build habits early: when the page turns and they can't say what just happened, that's the signal.

misconceptions.title: Re-Reading Means Starting Over From the Top
misconceptions.body: When students do decide to re-read, they often re-read the entire piece instead of locating the exact sentence or passage where comprehension broke down. Teach them to diagnose the breakdown site first: go back to the last sentence they understood, then move forward from there. Whole-text re-reads waste time and often just reproduce the same confusion at higher speed.

misconceptions.title: Annotation Is Decoration
misconceptions.body: Students fill margins with stars, underlines, and circles that record nothing — a visual performance of reading. Watch for annotations that have no cognitive content: if a student can't say what a marking means or why it's there, it isn't annotation, it's highlight-as-avoidance. Push them to annotate the problem: "confused here," "who is 'they'?", "need to look up [term]." The mark should capture a thought, not replace one.

misconceptions.title: Background Knowledge Is Either There or It Isn't
misconceptions.body: Students treat background knowledge as a binary — you either know the context or you don't, and if you don't, the text is simply inaccessible. Reframe this: background knowledge is buildable mid-read. Teach them to identify exactly what they don't know (a historical event, a technical term, an allusion) and look it up. The skill isn't having the knowledge in advance; it's knowing what's missing and knowing how to get it.

misconceptions.title: Asking Questions Means Asking the Teacher
misconceptions.body: Students default to "I'll ask when we discuss it" as a comprehension strategy, which means they defer confusion rather than resolve it. Teach them to generate their own questions in writing — "What does the author mean by X?" "Is this claim supported later?" — and to sit with those questions as they continue reading, rather than parking them for someone else to answer. Self-questioning is a cognitive act, not a request for rescue.

assignments.name: Breakdown Log
assignments.body: As students read a challenging text (a dense nonfiction excerpt, a difficult poem, a primary source), they keep a two-column log: left column names where understanding broke down (quote the sentence), right column names which strategy they used and what happened as a result. Debrief as a class: which strategies worked for which types of breakdown?

assignments.name: Think-Aloud Transcription
assignments.body: Students read a new paragraph aloud and narrate their thinking in real time — confusion, strategy choice, result. They record and transcribe two minutes of this process, then annotate the transcript: where did they deploy a repair strategy? Where did they drift through confusion without acting on it? The gap between those moments is the learning target.

assignments.name: Annotation Audit
assignments.body: Students exchange annotated texts with a partner. The partner reads only the annotations and writes what they think the annotator was tracking. The annotator reads the partner's interpretation and evaluates: did the annotations actually communicate their comprehension process? Where did the annotations fail to capture real thinking?

assignments.name: Outside Source Integration
assignments.body: Students identify three moments in a difficult text where comprehension required outside context. They locate a source for each, document what they found, and write one sentence per moment explaining how the source changed or deepened their reading. The goal is making the research-for-comprehension habit visible and deliberate.

mastery: Identifies specific sentences or passages where comprehension broke down rather than describing confusion in vague terms ("the whole thing was hard").

Selects the appropriate repair strategy for the type of breakdown — re-reading for syntax confusion, outside sources for missing context, annotation for tracking a complex argument, background knowledge activation for allusions or references.
Generates written questions while reading that are answerable from the text or a named outside source — not vague requests for explanation.
Annotations capture cognitive moves (confusion flagged, claim questioned, connection made) rather than decorative marks with no interpretive content.
Demonstrates reading stamina with difficult texts — pauses to repair rather than drifting, and can describe the strategies used and their results after reading.
notes: Vertical alignment: English III 4(I) carries the same expectation verbatim. The English IV ramp is not in the language of the standard but in the complexity of the texts students are expected to monitor — AP-level arguments, college-preparatory nonfiction, complex literary essays. The strategies are the same; the cognitive demand of the texts where students apply them is the distinguishing factor.

DOK 3 justification: strategic deployment of repair strategies across varying text types requires students to diagnose the kind of breakdown, select an appropriate response, and evaluate whether it worked — this is strategic thinking, not simple recall or skill application.

No direct STAAR analog: this standard targets a metacognitive process that STAAR does not test explicitly. However, STAAR regularly tests its products — inference, author's purpose, synthesis, use of context — which all depend on successful comprehension monitoring. Question stems above mirror how STAAR tests the downstream behaviors that comprehension monitoring enables. See the Notes flag above.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Describe Personal Connections to a Variety of Sources
expectation: Describe personal connections to a variety of sources, including self-selected texts.
overview: By English IV, this is the capstone version of reader response — students have enough lived experience, enough accumulated reading, and enough self-awareness to articulate not just *what* a text made them feel but *why* the connection is happening and what it reveals about them as readers. The skill is about developing a reading identity, not just logging reactions.
studentFriendly: You can look at something you've read — whether your teacher assigned it or you found it yourself — and explain clearly what it connects to in your own life, experience, or prior reading, and why that connection matters to your understanding of the text.
questionStems: Based on paragraphs X–Y, the narrator's experience MOST likely connects to which universal theme?
Which detail from the selection BEST supports the inference that the speaker's personal history shapes how they interpret events?
The author's description in paragraph X would MOST likely resonate with a reader who has experienced —
Which sentence from the passage BEST illustrates how an individual's background influences their reading of a situation?
A reader who has experienced [situation] would MOST likely interpret paragraph X as —
Based on the selection, which conclusion about the relationship between personal experience and textual meaning is BEST supported?
The shift in the narrator's perspective between paragraphs X and Y MOST likely reflects —
Which statement BEST describes how the author uses personal narrative to develop the central idea?
A reader connecting this passage to their own experience would MOST likely focus on which detail in paragraph X?
Based on the selection as a whole, the author's account of [event] contributes MOST to the reader's understanding of —

misconceptions.title: "Connecting" Means Saying "I Can Relate"
misconceptions.body: Students treat personal connection as a feeling to report, not an idea to develop. They write "I can relate because I've also felt sad" and consider the job done. Teach them that a connection is only useful if it tells you something new about the text or about yourself — the connection is the starting point of analysis, not the ending point.

misconceptions.title: Self-Selected Means Self-Indulgent
misconceptions.body: When students are allowed to choose their own texts, some interpret that freedom as permission to engage on whatever terms they want, including barely at all. Push them to see that the source being self-selected raises the bar — they chose it, so they own the connection more fully and owe a more precise account of why it matters to them.

misconceptions.title: Personal Experience Competes With the Text
