misconceptions.body: Students sometimes believe that bringing in their own life means leaving the text behind — that personal response is the opposite of close reading. Reframe this: a strong personal connection is one that drives you back to the text for evidence. Watch for responses that are entirely memoir and have no textual anchor.

misconceptions.title: The Stronger the Emotion, the Better the Connection
misconceptions.body: Students mistake intensity for quality. They amplify their reactions ("this passage DESTROYED me") without explaining the mechanism. Teach them that a quiet, precise connection — "the father's silence on page 12 reminded me of the specific way my grandfather avoided certain questions" — is worth far more than a dramatic but vague one.

misconceptions.title: You Can Only Connect to Characters Who Look Like You
misconceptions.body: Students narrow their definition of personal connection to surface-level identification — same race, same gender, same hometown. The more powerful skill is cross-difference empathy: connecting to a character's emotional logic even when the context is completely foreign. Watch for students who dismiss texts with unfamiliar settings as "not relatable" without trying to find the shared human architecture underneath.

assignments.name: The Connection Audit
assignments.body: Students select a passage from a self-chosen text and write a two-column response: the left column names the specific textual moment; the right column describes the personal connection and explains what the connection reveals about the text's meaning. At least three entries required.

assignments.name: Reading Identity Portrait
assignments.body: Students curate five texts they have chosen over the past two years — any genre, any source — and write a short essay analyzing what those choices say about who they are as a reader. The essay must make an argument, not just a list.

assignments.name: Cross-Text Connection Essay
assignments.body: Students identify a theme that appears in both a course text and a self-selected text and write a focused essay tracing how their personal experience with that theme deepened their reading of both. The connection must run in both directions — from life to text AND from text back to life.

assignments.name: The Precise Moment
assignments.body: Students find the single sentence in any text they have read this semester that hit them hardest. They write one paragraph explaining the sentence and two paragraphs unpacking why it connected — tracing the connection down to its root, not just its surface.

mastery: Identifies a specific moment in a text — not a general summary — and anchors the personal connection to that exact moment with quoted or paraphrased evidence.
Articulates why the connection occurred, not just that it occurred — tracing the link between their experience and the text's meaning.
Uses the personal connection as a lens that sharpens their reading rather than a substitute for engaging with the text.
Demonstrates range: can make meaningful connections across genres, time periods, and cultures, including texts that differ significantly from their own experience.
Produces written response that moves from connection to insight — the connection changes or deepens their interpretation of the text by the end of the piece.
notes: Vertical alignment: English III 5A is the same expectation verbatim — describe personal connections to a variety of sources, including self-selected texts. The English IV version is the terminal iteration; mastery here means the habit is internalized and the reader can sustain it independently across unfamiliar texts without scaffolding. Teachers should push English IV students past the mechanics of connection-making and toward the meta-question: what kind of reader am I, and how do my connections reveal that?

DOK 2 justification: the core move is comprehension + application — producing a connection requires students to process both the text and their own experience and map one onto the other. Assessment items that ask students to evaluate the quality of a connection or generalize across multiple texts may reach DOK 3.

STAAR analog note: STAAR does not test personal connection directly as a student-response skill; the stems above mirror how STAAR tests the textual side of the same relationship — how character experience, author perspective, and reader inference intersect. Teachers should treat the stems as models for the analytical half of the skill.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Write Responses That Demonstrate Analysis of Texts
expectation: Write responses that demonstrate analysis of texts, including comparing texts within and across genres.
overview: This is the capstone response skill: students don't just comprehend or annotate — they write their way into understanding. The cross-genre comparison requirement is where it gets hard, because students have to hold two texts in mind simultaneously and explain what the collision between them reveals.
studentFriendly: You can write an analytical response that shows you've gone inside a text — or two texts from different genres — and figured out something about how they work and what they're doing together.
questionStems: Which sentence from the response BEST demonstrates the student's analysis of how the author develops the central idea in the selection?
Based on paragraphs X–Y of both selections, which statement MOST accurately describes how the authors' approaches to [topic] differ?
The student's claim in paragraph X is BEST supported by which evidence from the selections?
Which sentence from the paired selections MOST likely prompted the comparison in paragraph X of the response?
How does the student's analysis in paragraph X contribute to the development of the overall argument?
With which statement about both texts would the student MOST likely agree, based on the response?
Which detail from Selection 2 MOST directly supports the inference drawn in paragraph X of the student's response?
The student's comparison of [element] across both texts in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Which revision to paragraph X would MOST strengthen the student's cross-genre analysis?
Based on the student's response, the comparison between the two selections is MOST likely intended to demonstrate —

misconceptions.title: Comparison Means Finding Similarities
misconceptions.body: Students default to the Venn diagram move: list what's the same, list what's different, call it analysis. Real comparison is explanatory — it uses the relationship between texts to reveal something neither text makes visible on its own. Teach them that a comparison with no payoff (no "so what" that couldn't exist without both texts) isn't analysis; it's bookkeeping.

misconceptions.title: Quoting Is the Same as Analyzing
misconceptions.body: Students drop a quote, say "this shows," and move on, treating quotation as self-evident proof. Push them to stay in the sentence after the quote — what does that passage reveal about the author's choices, and what does it mean in the context of the other text? If their response could cut all the quote-explanation sentences without losing the argument, they haven't analyzed anything.

misconceptions.title: Across Genres Just Means Poem + Essay
misconceptions.body: Students treat the genre requirement as a formatting checkbox. They'll pair a poem with an article and never mention the generic difference once in the response — never ask what the poem's compression does that the article's elaboration can't, or vice versa. Teach them that genre is an argument: the form is doing rhetorical work, and a cross-genre comparison has to reckon with that.

misconceptions.title: Analysis Is Opinion With Textual Evidence
misconceptions.body: Students confuse analytical writing with supported personal response. "I think the author is saying..." followed by a quote is still just an opinion. Analysis makes a claim about HOW the text works — structure, language, form, perspective — and explains the mechanism, not just the meaning. Watch for responses that are argumentative in tone but descriptive in substance.

misconceptions.title: Longer Responses Are Better Responses
misconceptions.body: Senior students often inflate — more quotes, more summary, more restatement — under the impression that volume signals rigor. The clearest sign of mastery is a response that says exactly what it needs to say and stops. Teach them that every sentence in an analytical response should be load-bearing; if it could disappear without weakening the argument, it probably should.

assignments.name: Cross-Genre Collision
assignments.body: Pair a short lyric poem and a related op-ed or essay on the same subject (grief, justice, labor, belonging). Students write a two-page analytical response arguing what the pairing reveals that neither text reveals alone. They must name at least one specific generic difference and explain its rhetorical significance.

assignments.name: Response Autopsy
assignments.body: Give students two anonymous student responses to the same prompt — one strong, one surface-level. They annotate: where is the analysis? Where does it collapse into summary or opinion? After annotation, they revise the weaker response in place, rewriting only the analytical sentences.

assignments.name: Within-Genre, Across-Time
assignments.body: Pair two short stories or two essays from different historical periods on a shared theme. Students write a focused response comparing how each author's historical moment shapes their handling of the theme. The constraint: no more than one page. Compression forces precision.

assignments.name: The Third Text
assignments.body: After writing a cross-genre comparison, students write a one-paragraph extension arguing which text a hypothetical third author — working in a genre of the student's choosing — would be more likely to extend, and why. This tests whether the analysis actually holds outside the two texts.

assignments.name: Sentence-Level Revision Workshop
assignments.body: Students exchange drafts and mark every sentence that is purely summary or unsupported opinion. They then rewrite those sentences to make an analytical claim. Final draft is submitted alongside the marked-up peer draft to show the revision arc.

mastery: Makes an analytical claim about both texts that couldn't stand on a reading of either text alone.
Uses textual evidence as a mechanism — explains how and why quoted or paraphrased material supports the argument, not just that it does.
Addresses generic differences between compared texts explicitly and explains their rhetorical consequence.
Organizes the response so that comparison is cumulative — each paragraph builds toward a conclusion the reader couldn't have predicted from the opening claim alone.
Writes with economy: every sentence does analytical work; summary and restatement are subordinated to argument.
notes: Vertical ramp: English III 5B asks students to write responses demonstrating analysis of texts. English IV adds the explicit requirement to compare within and across genres — the cross-genre dimension is the course-level escalation. Students arriving from English III should be able to sustain analytical writing; the new task is holding genre as an analytical variable while doing it.

DOK 4 justification: cross-genre comparison requires students to apply analytical reasoning across multiple texts while constructing an extended, evidence-based written argument — this is sustained extended thinking, not retrieval or application to a single text.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog in the traditional sense, as STAAR tests reading comprehension rather than student-produced analytical writing. However, released STAAR expository and literary prompts assess the underlying analytical moves — the question stems above mirror how STAAR tests evidence use, inference, and cross-text reasoning, reframed as response evaluation rather than original composition.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Use Text Evidence and Original Commentary to Support an Evaluative Response
expectation: Use text evidence and original commentary to support an evaluative response.
overview: This is the capstone move of written response: students don't just quote and summarize — they evaluate, and they do it with their own thinking carrying equal weight as the text. The gap between English III and English IV is the word "original" — commentary that isn't just a restatement of the evidence but a genuine claim the student is advancing.
studentFriendly: You can write a response that makes a real argument about a text — choosing the right evidence AND generating your own reasoning to show why that evidence means what you say it means.
questionStems: Which quotation from the selection BEST supports the student's evaluation that the author presents grief as isolating rather than communal?
The student writes, "The author's shift in tone signals that resolution is impossible." Which evidence from the selection MOST effectively supports this claim?
Read the following student response. Which sentence provides original commentary rather than a restatement of textual evidence?
A student wants to evaluate the author's argument as deliberately incomplete. Which two pieces of evidence from the selection BEST support that evaluation when considered together?
Which sentence from the selection would MOST strengthen an evaluative claim about the narrator's reliability?
The student's commentary in paragraph 3 suggests that the author uses irony to —
Which revision to the student's response would MOST effectively develop the original commentary in paragraph 2?
Based on the selection, which evidence would BEST support the evaluation that the author prioritizes atmosphere over character development?
A student evaluates that the author's central argument depends on an unstated assumption. Which sentence from the selection MOST directly supports this inference?
Which of the following MOST accurately describes the relationship between the evidence cited in paragraph 4 and the student's evaluative claim?

misconceptions.title: Quotation Is Not Commentary
misconceptions.body: Students treat the act of quoting as the act of arguing. They drop a line from the text, write "This shows that..." and consider the paragraph done. The evidence isn't the argument — the argument is what you do with the evidence. Teach them: commentary is what the evidence can't say for itself. If the text could make your point alone, you're not needed. Make them ask, after every quotation, "So what? Why does this matter for my evaluation?"

misconceptions.title: "Evaluative" Means "Opinion" Means "Anything Goes"
misconceptions.body: When students hear "evaluative," they read it as permission to express feelings unchecked by the text. Watch for responses where the student's claim floats free of any specific textual grounding — lots of assertion, no tether. Evaluative response means a reasoned judgment anchored to evidence, not a gut reaction wearing academic language. Push them: your evaluation has to be falsifiable from the text — if the author had made a different choice, your claim would collapse.

misconceptions.title: Original Commentary Is Paraphrase in Disguise
misconceptions.body: Students who have been trained to "explain in their own words" often produce commentary that merely restates the evidence in slightly different phrasing. This is not original commentary — it's synonym substitution. Teach them the difference: paraphrase says what the text says; commentary says what the text means, or what it does, or what it costs. One test: could a student who hadn't read the evidence still understand your commentary? If yes, it's probably commentary. If it requires the evidence to make sense, it might be a paraphrase.

misconceptions.title: More Evidence Equals a Better Argument
misconceptions.body: Students stack quotations to signal effort or thoroughness, assuming volume compensates for analytical depth. Three embedded quotes with no commentary is weaker than one quote with a developed, original evaluative claim. Teach them that selection matters more than accumulation — the strongest respondents choose evidence precisely because it makes their evaluation difficult to refute, not because it fills space. Grade commentary length alongside evidence count to break this habit.

misconceptions.title: The Evaluation Has to Be Positive
misconceptions.body: Students in English IV — often writing about canonical or challenging texts — default to approving evaluations because they assume disagreement signals misreading. Teach them that the most sophisticated evaluative responses often identify what a text fails to do, where its argument breaks down, or what its form costs it thematically. Naming a weakness in a Pulitzer-winning novel, backed by specific evidence and original reasoning, is a higher-order move than confirming the text is good.

assignments.name: Evidence Audit
assignments.body: Students highlight every quotation in a draft response in one color and every sentence of original commentary in another. They must have at least as many commentary sentences as evidence sentences. Any quotation with no commentary gets cut or replaced.

assignments.name: Steal the Claim
assignments.body: Students exchange evaluative claims (thesis statements only) with a partner. Each student then locates evidence for the other's claim from the same text. In discussion: did the evidence they found match? Did different evidence support the same evaluation equally well? What does that reveal about claim construction?

assignments.name: Evaluation vs. Summary Smackdown
assignments.body: Students rewrite a weak sample response (provided) that summarizes rather than evaluates. They must transform three summary sentences into evaluative commentary using specific textual evidence. Rubric targets the ratio of original reasoning to description.

assignments.name: Counterclaim Commentary
assignments.body: Students draft an evaluative response, then write one paragraph of original commentary that acknowledges the strongest counter-reading of the same evidence. Grade for intellectual honesty alongside argumentative force.

assignments.name: The One-Quote Essay
assignments.body: Students write a 400-word evaluative response using exactly one quotation from the text. Every other sentence must be original commentary. Forces them to develop the analytical muscle rather than hiding behind evidence accumulation.

mastery: Selects evidence that is genuinely necessary for the evaluative claim — not decorative, not redundant with other evidence already cited.
Produces original commentary that advances the evaluation beyond what the evidence states — makes a causal, thematic, or structural inference the text cannot make alone.
Maintains a clear evaluative claim throughout the response — evidence and commentary both serve the same through-argument.
Distinguishes between describing what the text does and judging what the text achieves or fails to achieve.
Integrates evidence grammatically and smoothly — no dropped quotations, no quotations that interrupt rather than support the commentary surrounding them.
notes: Vertical ramp from English III: English III 5C builds the skill of supporting a response with text evidence and commentary; English IV adds the evaluative frame as the governing expectation — the student is no longer just demonstrating comprehension but rendering a judgment. The commentary must be original, not just accurate.

DOK 4 justification: producing original evaluative commentary requires extended reasoning, synthesis across textual evidence, and defense of a reasoned judgment — no single correct response exists, and quality depends on how the student's thinking connects and controls the evidence.

This TEK has a strong STAAR analog in the written composition prompts (literary and expository) where students are scored on the quality of their evidence use and development of ideas. Question stems above mirror how STAAR reading items assess the components of strong written response when student writing samples appear as stimulus texts.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Paraphrase and Summarize Texts
expectation: Paraphrase and summarize texts in ways that maintain meaning and logical order.
overview: This TEK is deceptively simple — and that's the trap. By English IV, students have been paraphrasing and summarizing since elementary school, so they assume they've got it. The standard is actually demanding that they preserve BOTH meaning AND logical order simultaneously, which requires understanding how a text's argument or narrative architecture works, not just what it says.
studentFriendly: You can restate any text — an article, a speech, a passage — in your own words without distorting what it means or scrambling the order in which its ideas move.
questionStems: Read paragraph X. Which of the following BEST paraphrases the author's central claim in that paragraph?
Which summary MOST accurately reflects the logical order of ideas presented in paragraphs X through Y?
The student wrote the following summary of paragraphs X–Y. Which detail from the summary changes the meaning of the original text?
Which sentence BEST summarizes the author's argument in the selection?
A student paraphrases paragraph X as follows: [paraphrase]. Which part of the paraphrase MOST changes the meaning of the original?
Based on the selection, which of the following summaries BEST maintains the logical progression of the author's ideas?
Which sentence from the selection would MOST likely appear in an accurate summary of the text?
The author organizes paragraphs X–Z by [structure]. Which summary BEST preserves this organizational pattern?
Which paraphrase of the underlined sentence in paragraph X MOST nearly captures the author's intended meaning?
Which of the following summaries MOST clearly distorts the logical order of the original selection?

misconceptions.title: Paraphrase Means Find-and-Replace Synonyms
misconceptions.body: Students swap out individual words while preserving the original sentence structure and call it paraphrasing. This is cosmetic — it doesn't demonstrate comprehension, and it often produces awkward or inaccurate restatements. Teach them that a genuine paraphrase rebuilds the sentence from the idea up, not from the words out.

misconceptions.title: A Summary Is Just the First and Last Sentence of Each Paragraph
misconceptions.body: Students strip-mine topic sentences and conclusions and assemble them into what looks like a summary. Push them to ask: does this capture how the argument moves? A summary that lists ideas without tracking their sequence or causal relationships misses the architecture — and loses the meaning.

misconceptions.title: Maintaining Logical Order Means Copying the Original Sequence
misconceptions.body: Students think "logical order" means they must trace the text paragraph by paragraph. That's not the job. The job is preserving the RELATIONSHIP between ideas — cause before effect, claim before evidence, problem before solution — even if the phrasing and structure shift. Watch for summaries that scramble the logic while following the paragraph numbers faithfully.

misconceptions.title: If It's Shorter, It's a Better Summary
misconceptions.body: Students equate compression with quality. A one-sentence summary that drops the text's central distinction or conflates two opposing positions is worse than a two-paragraph summary that gets it right. Teach them to ask: would someone who hasn't read the original understand the argument accurately from my summary? Brevity is a tool, not a goal.

misconceptions.title: Paraphrase and Summary Are the Same Task
misconceptions.body: Students treat paraphrase and summary as interchangeable — using summary when precision is needed or paraphrasing sentence by sentence when a global restatement is called for. They are different moves with different scopes. A paraphrase restates a specific passage at roughly the same length; a summary compresses the whole. Clarifying when to use which — and why — is a metacognitive task many seniors haven't been explicitly taught.

assignments.name: Side-by-Side Audit
assignments.body: Students paraphrase a complex paragraph from a nonfiction text and then exchange with a partner, who marks any place where meaning shifts or logical sequence breaks. Students revise based on annotations. The goal is precision, not just length reduction.

assignments.name: Compression Ladder
assignments.body: Students write three versions of a text's summary: one at 50% of the original length, one at 25%, and one in a single sentence. After each compression, they identify what was lost — and whether the loss is acceptable. Discuss where the line is between necessary compression and meaning distortion.

assignments.name: Broken Summaries
assignments.body: Provide students with deliberately flawed summaries of a text they've read — some scramble the logical order, some introduce inaccuracies, some omit essential moves. Students diagnose each flaw and rewrite the summary correctly. Grade for diagnosis as much as for the rewrite.

mastery: Restates passages in genuinely new sentence structures, not synonym substitutions, without shifting meaning.
Produces summaries that trace the logical sequence of a text's argument or narrative — not just its topics.
Distinguishes between paraphrase (passage-level restatement) and summary (whole-text compression) and selects the appropriate task for the context.
Identifies when a paraphrase or summary has distorted meaning or scrambled logic — in their own work and in examples.
Adjusts summary scope and detail level based on purpose without sacrificing accuracy.
notes: Vertical alignment: 110.38(5)(D) in English III carries the same expectation verbatim. The ramp from III to IV is not in the standard's wording — it's in the complexity of the texts students are applying it to. By English IV, the texts are longer, more argumentatively layered, or structurally complex (e.g., texts with embedded counterargument, multiple organizational schemes). Teachers should explicitly increase text complexity rather than treating this as a repeated or already-mastered skill.

DOK 2 justification: the skill requires understanding and restating content at a comprehension and skill-application level; DOK 3 would apply when students must evaluate the quality or fidelity of a paraphrase or summary, which occurs in assignments but not in the expectation itself.

STAAR note: STAAR tests the underlying skill indirectly — identifying the BEST summary or detecting which paraphrase distorts meaning are recurring item types, particularly in paired passages and informational reading. These stems directly mirror that testing pattern.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Interact with Sources in Meaningful Ways
expectation: Interact with sources in meaningful ways such as notetaking, annotating, freewriting, or illustrating.
overview: This TEK is about active reading as a thinking discipline — not highlighting for its own sake, but using the page as a workspace where a reader argues back, questions, summarizes, and connects. In English IV, the expectation is that this habit is already built and students are deploying it with college-level texts under their own direction.
studentFriendly: You engage with what you read by doing something with it on paper — marking it up, writing back to it, capturing what it made you think — so that reading becomes a conversation, not a one-way transmission.
questionStems: Read the student's annotations in the box below. Which annotation BEST demonstrates the student's understanding of the author's central argument?
A student underlines the phrase [phrase] in paragraph X and writes "contrast with paragraph Y" in the margin. This annotation MOST likely shows that the student —
Based on the freewrite excerpt, which statement BEST describes the student's interpretation of the passage?
Which of the following notes MOST effectively captures the main idea of paragraphs X–Y?
A student annotates paragraph X with the word "concession." This label MOST likely indicates that the student recognized —
Which revision to the student's notes would MOST improve their accuracy in representing the author's claim?
The student's marginal note in paragraph X reads "[claim] — but what's the evidence?" This annotation MOST nearly reflects which reading strategy?
Based on the student's freewrite in the box, what inference can BEST be drawn about which aspect of the text the student found most significant?
Which of the following annotations would MOST help a student prepare to write an analysis of the author's use of evidence in paragraphs X–Z?
A student illustrates the relationship between two concepts from paragraph X using a diagram. This strategy MOST likely supports the student's ability to —

misconceptions.title: Highlighting Is the Same as Annotating
misconceptions.body: Students treat a highlighted page as a finished task. They've marked the text but haven't yet thought about it — which means they've done the mechanical part and skipped the cognitive part. Teach them that annotation means leaving a trace of your thinking, not a trace of your attention. A highlighted sentence with no marginal note is just decoration.

misconceptions.title: Notes Should Be Complete Sentences
misconceptions.body: Students either write nothing (too formal) or transcribe whole paragraphs (no compression). The productive zone is fragments and phrases that force the student to decide what the text is actually doing — "concedes here," "weak example," "links to intro claim." Push them toward compression as interpretation: what you leave out of a note is a decision, not laziness.

misconceptions.title: Freewriting Is for Creative Writing Class
misconceptions.body: Students wall off freewriting from analytical reading because they associate it with personal expression. Show them the intellectual move: freewriting after a dense passage externalizes confusion before it calculates into a wrong interpretation. Teach them that the messiness of a freewrite is evidence of thinking, not evidence of not knowing.

misconceptions.title: Annotating Means Finding the Important Parts
misconceptions.body: Students annotate in search of the "right" things to mark, waiting for permission. The stronger reader annotates what surprises, confuses, contradicts, or confirms — not just what seems quotable. Watch for students who only mark thesis statements and definitions; they're annotating for extraction, not engagement.

misconceptions.title: This Only Matters for Assigned Reading
misconceptions.body: By English IV, students should be carrying annotation habits into self-chosen reading, independent research, and any text they'll need to return to. When students treat interaction-with-sources as a classroom performance rather than a durable skill, they drop it the moment the assignment is done. Make the habit visible across contexts — research, dual-enrollment, personal reading — not just in-class close reading.

assignments.name: Annotation Audit
assignments.body: Students bring in a text they annotated independently (from research or free reading). In pairs, they describe their system — what each mark means, what triggered each note — and identify one thing they would have annotated differently with more time.

assignments.name: Three-Method Comparison
assignments.body: Students read the same short passage three times, each time using a different method: notetaking, freewriting, and marginal annotation. They then write a paragraph explaining which method produced the most useful thinking and why.

assignments.name: Teach Your System
assignments.body: Students design a one-page annotation guide explaining their personal annotation system — what symbols they use, what triggers marginalia, how they freewrite after dense passages. They swap guides and use each other's systems on a new text, then debrief on what worked.

assignments.name: Source Interaction Portfolio
assignments.body: Over a research unit, students collect annotated copies of five sources. Final submission includes the annotated texts plus a one-page reflection on how their interaction with sources evolved across the unit.

mastery: Uses annotation to record thinking — questions, connections, pushback — not just to identify important sentences.
Produces notes and freewrites that compress and interpret rather than transcribe.
Selects the interaction method (notetaking, freewriting, annotation, illustration) based on the type of text and the reading purpose.
Sustains source-interaction habits across self-directed tasks, not only teacher-prompted ones.
Can explain specifically how their interaction with a source shaped their written response or analysis of it.
notes: Vertical alignment: this TEK is identical in wording across English I–IV. The ramp is in text complexity and the expectation of independence — in English IV, the methods should be student-chosen and habitual, not teacher-scaffolded. English III sets the expectation; English IV assumes the habit is owned.

DOK 2 justification: the skill requires applying a processing strategy to a specific text — procedural engagement with content. Higher DOK is reached when the annotations feed into an analysis task (at which point the anchor skill becomes a sub-behavior inside a DOK 3–4 demand).

STAAR does not test annotation methods directly; the question stems above mirror how STAAR tests reading comprehension by embedding student annotation samples as part of a stimulus, a format that appears in STAAR's composition and editing passages. Note this limitation when using stems for test prep.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Respond Using Acquired Content and Academic Vocabulary
expectation: Respond using acquired content and academic vocabulary as appropriate.
overview: This TEK is about whether students can actually use the language of literary and disciplinary study — not just recognize it. By English IV, students should reach for domain-specific vocabulary organically in their written and oral responses, because they've internalized it, not because they've been told to include it.
studentFriendly: You use the right language — literary terms, disciplinary vocabulary, and course-specific concepts — naturally and correctly when you write or speak about what you've read or studied.
questionStems: Which sentence from the student's response BEST demonstrates the use of academic vocabulary to support the claim?
The student's use of the term [word] in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Which revision of the underlined sentence would MOST effectively incorporate academic vocabulary appropriate to the argument?
Based on paragraph X, the student's response would be MOST strengthened by replacing [word/phrase] with —
The student uses the phrase [phrase] in paragraph X. This choice MOST likely indicates —
Which sentence from the response BEST demonstrates the student's understanding of [content concept]?
Read paragraph X. Which revision MOST clearly reflects the use of disciplinary vocabulary appropriate to the topic?
The student's word choice in paragraph X MOST nearly conveys —
Which sentence from the response BEST supports the inference that the student understands the distinction between [term A] and [term B]?
The student's response in paragraph X would MOST benefit from the addition of —

misconceptions.title: Vocabulary Is Something You Sprinkle In
misconceptions.body: Students treat academic vocabulary as a garnish — they write a response and then hunt for a place to insert a term. Teach them that the vocabulary is the thinking. If they can't write the sentence with the term naturally, they don't know the concept yet. Watch for responses that use three vocabulary words in the first paragraph and then abandon them entirely — that's decoration, not acquisition.

misconceptions.title: "Appropriate" Means "As Many Fancy Words as Possible"
misconceptions.body: Students interpret "as appropriate" as an invitation to load every sentence with domain language, producing responses that are dense but incoherent. The standard isn't quantity — it's fit. Push them to ask: does this term clarify my meaning or complicate it? A student who chooses "juxtaposition" precisely earns more credit than one who uses "foil," "contrast," "juxtaposition," and "antithesis" in the same sentence hoping one lands.

misconceptions.title: Content Vocabulary and Academic Vocabulary Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students conflate these. Content vocabulary is domain-specific (allusion, tragedy, dialectic, sublime). Academic vocabulary crosses disciplines (analyze, synthesize, infer, contend, refute). Both matter, and both belong in strong responses, but they do different work. Teach students to distinguish the terms that name the thing from the terms that describe what they're doing with it.

misconceptions.title: If You Know the Word, You've Acquired It
misconceptions.body: Students think seeing a word on a vocabulary list constitutes acquisition. Real acquisition shows up in writing — in the ability to deploy a term correctly under pressure, to modify it grammatically, to distinguish it from near-synonyms. Watch for students who define terms correctly on a test but avoid them in essays. That's recognition, not acquisition. Push them to use the term in at least three different writing contexts before calling it acquired.

misconceptions.title: Informal Language Is Never Appropriate
misconceptions.body: Some students swing the other way: they over-formalize everything, including contexts where a direct, plainspoken sentence would be clearer and stronger. "As appropriate" in the standard means students need to read the rhetorical situation. Argumentative analysis calls for disciplinary precision; a personal reflection or short answer might call for something less formal. Teach them that register awareness is part of what vocabulary acquisition enables.

assignments.name: Vocabulary in the Wild
assignments.body: Students select five terms from the course's running academic/content vocabulary list and write a single cohesive paragraph that uses all five accurately and without awkwardness. Grade on integration, not just presence — a list of five correctly spelled terms earns nothing.

assignments.name: Revision for Register
assignments.body: Provide students two versions of a sample response: one stripped of academic vocabulary, one over-loaded with it. Students write a third version that uses vocabulary appropriately, then write a one-sentence rationale for each term they kept or added.

assignments.name: Cold Response, Hot Debrief
assignments.body: Give students an on-demand prompt about a text they know well. After they submit, run a class debrief on vocabulary choices: which terms appeared, which were missing, which were forced. Students annotate their own drafts after the debrief and submit a revision with a brief reflection on what they changed and why.

mastery: Uses content and academic vocabulary correctly in written responses without being prompted to include specific terms.
Selects vocabulary that clarifies meaning rather than signals effort — uses the precise term, not the most impressive-sounding one.
Sustains appropriate vocabulary use across an entire response, not just in the opening paragraph.
Distinguishes between academic vocabulary (cross-disciplinary action verbs and analytical terms) and content vocabulary (domain-specific terminology) and deploys both correctly.
Adjusts register based on the response context — more formal and precise in argumentative essays, appropriately direct in shorter analytical tasks.
notes: Vertical alignment: This TEK runs identically worded through English I–IV, but the expectation escalates through the sophistication of the vocabulary pool. By English IV, the course content has drawn from college-level literary criticism, argument theory, and cross-disciplinary inquiry — so "acquired" vocabulary in English IV is richer and more specialized than in English I. The skill is the same; the lexical territory has expanded significantly.

DOK 3 justification: Appropriately applying acquired vocabulary across varied response contexts requires strategic reasoning, not just recall. Students must judge which terms fit which contexts, which elevates this above retrieval (DOK 1–2) without requiring the extended multi-criteria evaluation of DOK 4.

No direct STAAR analog for vocabulary-in-response as a distinct tested behavior — STAAR assesses vocabulary receptively (reading) rather than productively (writing). Question stems above mirror how STAAR frames vocabulary analysis when evaluating student-authored text or revision tasks on the writing portion.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Discuss and Write About Explicit and Implicit Meanings of Text
expectation: Discuss and write about the explicit and implicit meanings of text.
overview: This is the capstone reading-response skill for English IV: students must hold both surfaces simultaneously — what the text says outright and what it leaves beneath the surface — and articulate the relationship between them in writing. The challenge is not finding one or the other; it's teaching students that meaning lives in the tension between the two.
studentFriendly: You can read a text, identify what it states directly, dig beneath that to what it implies or leaves unstated, and write about both with precision and evidence.
questionStems: Which sentence from the selection MOST clearly states the author's explicit argument?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the reader can MOST likely infer that —
Which detail from the selection BEST supports the implicit meaning that the author —
The author states in paragraph X that [statement]. This detail MOST likely suggests —
Which inference about the narrator is BEST supported by evidence from the selection?
The author's use of [image/phrase] in paragraph X implicitly suggests that —
What can the reader conclude about [subject] based on what is NOT directly stated in paragraphs X–Y?
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates the difference between what the speaker says and what the speaker means?
Based on the selection as a whole, which statement MOST accurately captures both an explicit claim and its implied significance?
The implicit meaning of the final paragraph is BEST supported by —

misconceptions.title: "Implicit" Means Anything You Feel Like Inferring
misconceptions.body: Students treat implicit meaning as a license for unconstrained interpretation — if they can imagine it, it must be there. Teach them that implicit meaning is always tethered to textual evidence. An inference must be traceable: "the text implies X because of Y and Z in the selection." If they can't locate the anchors, they're not reading the text, they're writing their own.

misconceptions.title: Explicit Means Obvious, So It Doesn't Need Discussion
misconceptions.body: Students skip quickly past explicit content because they think stating the obvious is beneath them. Push them to see that explicit meaning is the foundation, not the floor to race past. In senior-level analysis, explicit claims must be precisely quoted and contextualized — the quality of their explicit identification shapes the quality of every inference that follows.

misconceptions.title: A Good Response Needs Only One Level
misconceptions.body: Students write either a summary (all explicit) or a speculative paragraph (all implicit) and think they've answered the prompt. Watch for essays that never quote the text or essays that never say anything the text actually states. The real move is the dialogue between levels — "the text says X, but beneath that it is doing Y."

misconceptions.title: Implicit Meaning Is Always Dark or Hidden
misconceptions.body: Students assume implicit meaning involves uncovering something sinister, ironic, or secret — the subtext is always a gotcha. Reframe this: implicit meaning includes tone, values, assumptions, emotional register, and structural choices. Sometimes what's implied is warmth, ambiguity, or ideological investment that the author didn't consciously hide — just didn't state.

misconceptions.title: Writing About Both Means Alternating Paragraphs
misconceptions.body: Students produce responses that explicitly-paragraph, then implicitly-paragraph, with no integration. Teach them that the most sophisticated responses show how explicit and implicit meanings interact — how a stated claim creates an expectation that the implicit layer then complicates, confirms, or undercuts. The goal is synthesis, not a two-column table.

assignments.name: Dual-Layer Annotation
assignments.body: Students annotate a short literary or nonfiction text in two passes — first marking every explicit statement of meaning, then marking every point where the text implies something not directly stated. Written synthesis paragraph follows: one page, must quote both layers with analysis.

assignments.name: The Two Readings
assignments.body: Students write two short paragraphs about the same passage — one describing only what the text explicitly says, one describing only what the text implies. Then they write a third paragraph arguing how the two layers together produce a unified meaning neither paragraph alone could reach.

assignments.name: Peer Inference Challenge
assignments.body: Students write three implicit-meaning claims about a shared text, then exchange with a partner who must verify each inference against textual evidence. Claims that can't be anchored are struck. Students revise based on the challenge.

assignments.name: Extended Analytical Essay
assignments.body: A full essay prompt requiring students to analyze how explicit and implicit meanings operate across at least two moments in a text, with a thesis that accounts for the relationship between them. Minimum 3 pages. Evidence required for every claim at both levels.

assignments.name: Seminar + Written Takeaway
assignments.body: Class discussion of a complex text, tracking claims as they surface. Students categorize spoken claims in real time (explicit vs. implicit). After discussion, each student writes a 1-page synthesis identifying where the seminar's understanding shifted from surface to depth.

mastery: Locates and quotes explicit textual statements accurately, contextualizing rather than merely restating them.
Constructs implicit-meaning claims that are grounded in specific textual evidence — words, images, structure, omission — not in personal feeling or assumption.
Writes responses that articulate the relationship between explicit and implicit layers, not simply listing one then the other.
Adjusts the balance of explicit and implicit analysis to fit the complexity of the text and the demands of the prompt.
Produces written responses with a clear thesis, textual evidence at both meaning levels, and analysis explaining how the evidence supports each claim.
notes: Vertical alignment: English III 5G carries the same expectation verbatim. The ramp in English IV is in the complexity of texts assigned and the sophistication of the written response expected — senior-level texts demand multi-layered analysis, not just identification. Teachers should resist treating this as review; the same skill at a higher textual register is a genuinely harder task.

DOK 3 justification: the skill requires strategic thinking to hold two meaning levels simultaneously, locate evidence for each, and synthesize them into a coherent written argument. Some written-response tasks may push to DOK 4 when extended reasoning across multiple texts is required.

Because this standard governs both discussion and writing, assessment should include both modalities — a seminar contribution without written follow-up, or an essay without discussion grounding, is partial coverage.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Respond with Appropriate Register, Vocabulary, Tone, and Voice
expectation: Respond orally or in writing with appropriate register and purposeful vocabulary, tone, and voice.
overview: This is the capstone response skill — by English IV, students are expected to make deliberate rhetorical choices every time they respond, not just produce output. Register, vocabulary, tone, and voice aren't decorations; they're the argument. The skill is getting students to treat every response as a craft decision, not a personality expression.
studentFriendly: You can shift how you write or speak — choosing words, tone, and level of formality — based on what the situation actually calls for, and those choices make your response more effective, not just more polished.
questionStems: Which revision of the underlined sentence BEST adjusts the register to match the formal audience the writer is addressing?
The writer's use of [word/phrase] in paragraph X MOST likely contributes to which of the following tones?
Which sentence from the response BEST demonstrates a purposeful shift in the writer's voice?
Based on paragraph X, the writer's word choice suggests that the intended audience is MOST likely —
How does the vocabulary in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the overall tone of the response?
Which of the following revisions would MOST effectively align the writer's register with the formal context described in paragraph X?
The writer's tone in paragraph X is MOST nearly —
Based on the response, with which statement about the writer's intended effect would a careful reader MOST likely agree?
Which sentence from the response BEST illustrates the writer's consistent maintenance of an appropriate voice throughout?
The shift in vocabulary between paragraphs X and Y MOST likely signals —

misconceptions.title: Voice Means Personality, Not Purpose
misconceptions.body: Students treat voice as a synonym for personality — they write the way they always write and call it "their voice." Push them to see that purposeful voice means selecting a register and sustaining it in service of the response's goal. Teach them: voice is not what leaks out, it's what you choose to project. A response can carry your personality AND be calibrated to the audience. Those aren't opposites.

misconceptions.title: Register Is About Formality Level Only
misconceptions.body: Students collapse register into a binary: formal or informal. In reality, register includes word complexity, sentence architecture, the level of assumed shared knowledge, and the degree of hedging or confidence the writer performs. Watch for students who throw in "utilize" instead of "use" and think they've upgraded their register — they've only changed one word without shifting the underlying rhetorical relationship to the audience.

misconceptions.title: Tone Is the Same as Mood
misconceptions.body: Students frequently conflate the writer's tone with the emotional mood of the subject matter. They describe a piece about grief as having a "sad tone" without analyzing the specific choices — understatement, diction, sentence length — that create that effect. Teach them: tone is constructed, not transmitted. A writer can address a tragic subject with a detached, clinical tone or an intimate, confessional one. The subject doesn't dictate the choice.

misconceptions.title: Vocabulary Is Decorative, Not Structural
misconceptions.body: When students get feedback to "use stronger vocabulary," they swap in bigger words without asking whether those words do different work. They add complexity without adding precision. Push them deeper: purposeful vocabulary means the word is doing something the adjacent word cannot — carrying a connotation, signaling a register, or closing off an ambiguity. If you can swap the word out without losing anything, it wasn't purposeful; it was decorative.

misconceptions.title: Responding Orally Doesn't Require the Same Discipline
misconceptions.body: Students treat written response as the "real" task and oral response as improvisation. They don't transfer register and tone awareness to spoken contexts — they slip into casual language, hedge constantly, or perform a stiff imitation of academic speech. Teach them that oral register is its own skill: the same principles apply, but the cues are different — pace, volume, word selection in real time, and the absence of revision. Raise the stakes on oral responses so students stop treating them as throwaways.

assignments.name: Register Rewrite
assignments.body: Give students a single piece of writing — a paragraph from a student essay or a passage from a published text. Ask them to rewrite it three times: once for a peer audience, once for a college admissions committee, once for a professional field journal. They annotate the choices they made in each version.

assignments.name: Tone Audit
assignments.body: Students select one of their own previously written responses and conduct a tone audit: identify every word or sentence that undermines the intended tone, every place where register slips, every moment where voice becomes inconsistent. Revise, then write a brief reflection on what they found.

assignments.name: Oral Register Lab
assignments.body: Students respond to the same prompt twice orally — once in conversation with a peer, once as a formal presentation to a panel. Both are recorded. They compare the two recordings and analyze where their word choice, sentence structure, and tone shifted, and whether those shifts were appropriate or accidental.

assignments.name: Purposeful Vocabulary Defense
assignments.body: After completing a written response, students highlight every word they consider purposeful and write a one-sentence defense of each choice. Any word they cannot defend gets replaced. This makes vocabulary selection a conscious, auditable act rather than a feeling.

mastery: Selects a register appropriate to the audience and context and sustains it across the entire response without slipping into casual or inconsistent language.
Makes deliberate vocabulary choices that carry specific connotative or rhetorical weight, not just lexical complexity for its own sake.
Constructs a consistent tone that serves the response's purpose and can articulate why that tone was chosen.
Transfers register and tone awareness to oral responses with the same discipline applied to written ones.
Revises responses by targeting register, vocabulary, tone, and voice as distinct levers — not treating "clean it up" as a unified instruction.
notes: Vertical ramp from English III: English III 5H establishes the same skill — respond with appropriate register, vocabulary, tone, and voice. English IV does not add new components to the expectation text, but the vertical demand is in the complexity of the tasks, the sophistication of the contexts, and the expectation of transfer across oral and written modes without scaffolding. By English IV, this is mastery under pressure, not introduction.
DOK 3 justification: students must apply register and tone choices to novel prompts and explain or defend the reasoning behind those choices — strategic thinking, not retrieval.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is tested most directly through revision and editing items that ask students to select the best replacement for a sentence or word given an identified audience or purpose. The stems above are calibrated to that testing context.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Reflect on and Adjust Responses When Valid Evidence Warrants
expectation: Reflect on and adjust responses when valid evidence warrants.
overview: This is the capstone metacognitive move in Response Skills — the moment a student has to decide whether new evidence actually changes what they said before, and then follow through on that change in writing or discussion. The skill separates students who can perform a reading from students who can actually think.
studentFriendly: You can read new evidence, decide whether it genuinely changes your position, and then revise your response — not because someone pushed back, but because the evidence actually warranted it.
questionStems: Based on the evidence in paragraph X, which response to the author's argument MOST needs to be revised?
Which sentence from the selection provides the MOST valid reason for a reader to reconsider the claim in paragraph Y?
A student initially concluded that [claim]. Which detail from the text BEST supports adjusting that conclusion?
The evidence in paragraphs X and Y MOST likely requires the reader to —
Which statement BEST reflects a necessary adjustment to the position expressed in paragraph X, based on the evidence in paragraph Z?
After reading paragraphs X–Y, which original claim is MOST difficult to support?
The author introduces the evidence in paragraph X in order to —
Based on the evidence in the selection, with which revision to the following claim would the author MOST likely agree?
Which detail from the text BEST indicates that a reader's initial response to the author's argument should be reconsidered?
The reader's revised interpretation of [event/claim] is BEST supported by which evidence from the selection?

misconceptions.title: Changing Your Mind Means You Were Wrong
misconceptions.body: Students treat revision as confession — if they adjust their response, they think it means their first answer was a failure. Reframe this explicitly: adjusting when evidence warrants is the skill, not the consequence of missing it the first time. A student who holds a position until real evidence moves them is demonstrating exactly what this TEK requires. Teach them that intellectual stubbornness is a different failure mode than intellectual surrender.

misconceptions.title: Any Pushback Is Valid Evidence
misconceptions.body: When a peer disagrees or a teacher asks a follow-up question, students assume they should revise — not because the evidence changed, but because someone applied social pressure. Watch for students who constantly hedge or abandon positions the moment someone raises an eyebrow. The word *valid* in this standard is doing real work: teach them to name specifically what evidence moved them and why it actually addresses the original claim.

misconceptions.title: Reflection Happens at the End
misconceptions.body: Students treat this as a finishing step — they write a response, then "reflect" by tacking on a sentence that says "I now think..." as a formality. The real skill is recursive: reflection interrupts the reading and writing process throughout. Push them to identify the exact moment in the text where their position became harder to hold, not just to report that it changed.

misconceptions.title: Adjusting a Response Means Reversing It
misconceptions.body: Students think adjustment means flipping their position — from agreement to disagreement, or vice versa. But adjustment can mean narrowing a claim, adding a qualification, identifying an exception, or acknowledging complexity while maintaining the core position. Teach them that nuance is a form of revision. Watch for all-or-nothing responses that swing from one extreme to another when the text actually calls for a more measured adjustment.

misconceptions.title: Valid Evidence Is Whatever Confirms the New Direction
misconceptions.body: Once students decide to revise, they go hunting for evidence to support the revision rather than evaluating whether the evidence is actually valid. They've replaced one bias with another. Teach them to test the new evidence with the same rigor they should have applied to the first claim: Is it specific? Is it from a credible source within the text? Does it directly address the claim? Does it outweigh counter-evidence, or just add complexity?

assignments.name: Position Trace
assignments.body: Students read a multi-source set and document their interpretation of a central claim after each source — recording what changed, what held, and what evidence triggered each shift. Final product is a one-page annotated trace of their reasoning, not just a final position.

assignments.name: Warrant the Revision
assignments.body: Students exchange written responses with a partner. Each student identifies one piece of evidence from the text that would require the other's response to be adjusted and writes a one-paragraph explanation of why that evidence is valid and what specific adjustment it warrants. The original writer then revises one sentence of their response in writing, explaining the change in a footnote.

assignments.name: The Hold-and-Fold Debate
assignments.body: Students take a position on a text-based claim. Teacher introduces two additional pieces of evidence mid-discussion — one valid, one irrelevant. Students must publicly decide whether to adjust and justify why they held or revised based on which evidence was actually valid. Grade for the quality of the justification, not the direction of the change.

assignments.name: Revision Autopsy
assignments.body: After a major written response is returned, students write a half-page reflection: What evidence in the text, if they had weighed it more carefully, should have adjusted their original claim? What would the revised claim look like? This must be text-specific — no generic "I would have included more evidence."

mastery: Identifies specific evidence in a text that is sufficient to change, narrow, or qualify a previously held position — and articulates why that evidence meets the threshold of "valid."
Distinguishes between social pressure to revise (peer disagreement, teacher questioning) and textual evidence that actually warrants revision.
Produces revised responses that show a traceable connection between the new evidence and the specific change made — not just a different conclusion, but a documented reason.
Demonstrates willingness to hold a position against weak counter-evidence while adjusting readily when strong evidence appears, within the same piece of work.
Qualifies, narrows, or complicates a response when full reversal is not warranted — resisting the binary of "same answer" versus "opposite answer."
notes: Vertical alignment: English III 5I carries the same expectation verbatim. The English IV context raises the stakes — students are now working with more complex multi-source texts, longer argument chains, and post-secondary conventions that demand explicit acknowledgment of counter-evidence. The skill is identical; the texts and the precision expected in the justification are not.

DOK 4 justification: Students must evaluate the validity of evidence against a prior claim, make a judgment about whether revision is warranted, produce the revised response, and defend the reasoning — all metacognitive moves that require extended, self-monitored thinking.

STAAR note: This TEK is rarely tested as a discrete item. Stems above mirror how STAAR tests the underlying skill through revision-of-claim and evidence-sufficiency questions embedded in on-demand writing and literary analysis prompts. Note this when using stems with students.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Defend or Challenge Authors' Claims Using Evidence
expectation: Defend or challenge the authors' claims using relevant text evidence.
overview: This is the capstone move of argumentative reading: students don't just identify what an author claims — they take a position on it and back that position with the text itself. English IV students should be able to step into a written argument as a participant, not a spectator.
studentFriendly: You can read what an author argues, decide whether that argument holds up, and then build your own case — using the author's own words and evidence — to either back them up or push back.
questionStems: Which piece of evidence from the selection BEST supports a challenge to the author's central claim?
A student argues that the author's claim in paragraph X is well-supported. Which sentence from the selection MOST effectively defends that argument?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which statement BEST describes a weakness in the author's reasoning?
Which detail from the selection MOST likely strengthens the author's position that —
The author claims in paragraph X that [claim]. Which sentence from the selection BEST contradicts this claim?
A reader challenges the author's argument by pointing out [counterclaim]. Which evidence from the selection would MOST effectively support this challenge?
Based on the selection, which conclusion about the strength of the author's evidence is BEST supported?
Which pair of details from the selection MOST clearly demonstrates that the author's claim is — or is not — supported by sufficient evidence?
The author uses the evidence in paragraph X primarily to —
With which of the following statements about the author's argument would the text evidence in paragraphs X–Y MOST strongly agree?

misconceptions.title: Defending Means Agreeing and Challenging Means Attacking
misconceptions.body: Students conflate their personal opinion with the textual task. They defend claims they like and challenge claims they dislike, regardless of what the text actually supports. Teach them the distinction is structural: the question is whether the evidence the author provides is sufficient and relevant — not whether the student finds the conclusion appealing. Push them to defend claims they personally disagree with using only the text.

misconceptions.title: "Relevant Evidence" Means "Any Quote"
misconceptions.body: Students drop quotations without explaining how the evidence bears on the specific claim. They treat selecting text as the finish line. Watch for responses where a quote floats in a paragraph with no warrant — no sentence explaining why that particular piece of evidence addresses that particular claim. Relevant evidence is evidence that actually does work; teach them to articulate the connection explicitly.

misconceptions.title: A Challenge Requires Finding Lies or Errors
misconceptions.body: Students think challenging an author means catching them in a factual mistake. In practice, the stronger challenges target insufficient evidence, overgeneralization, unexamined assumptions, or contradictions within the text itself. Reframe challenge as a logical question — "Does what the author provides actually prove what the author claims?" — rather than a fact-checking exercise.

misconceptions.title: One Piece of Evidence Is Enough
misconceptions.body: Students write a single quote and declare their defense or challenge complete. At English IV level, the standard calls for a built case — multiple pieces of relevant evidence that, together, establish the position. Teach them that one example proves one instance; multiple examples establish a pattern, and a pattern is what an argument needs to stand.

misconceptions.title: Defending the Claim Means Ignoring Counterevidence
misconceptions.body: Students who choose to defend an author skip over the moments in the text where the evidence gets thin or the reasoning shaky. Mastery-level defense actually acknowledges the text's complications and explains why the claim holds despite them. Watch for students who write airtight defenses by simply omitting the hard parts of the text — that's not defense, it's selective reading.

assignments.name: Two-Column Verdict
assignments.body: Students read a short argumentative text and divide a page into two columns: "Evidence That Defends the Claim" and "Evidence That Challenges the Claim." They populate both columns from the text, then write a paragraph arguing which side is stronger — using only material from the text.

assignments.name: Switch Sides Brief
assignments.body: Students write a one-page defense of an author's claim. Then they switch: write a one-page challenge. Both pieces must cite different evidence from the same text. Debrief focuses on which task was harder and what that reveals about the argument's structure.

assignments.name: Author on Trial
assignments.body: Frame a class discussion as a proceeding: some students serve as counsel defending the author's argument, others prosecute it, and a third group acts as jury using a rubric for evaluating text evidence. Each side must cite specific paragraphs. Jury deliberates and delivers a written verdict with reasoning.

assignments.name: Crack the Claim
assignments.body: Give students two paired texts making similar claims with different levels of support. They identify which author's claim is more defensible and write a structured response citing evidence from both texts to justify their judgment.

mastery: Identifies the author's central claim precisely — not a paraphrase so broad it could mean anything — and takes a clear, stated position on it.
Selects evidence that is genuinely relevant to the specific claim being defended or challenged, not just evidence that sounds related.
Articulates a warrant for each piece of evidence — explains explicitly how the evidence bears on the claim.
Builds a case from multiple pieces of evidence rather than resting the argument on a single quotation.
Acknowledges complicating evidence within the text and accounts for it rather than ignoring it.
notes: Vertical alignment: English III 5J carries the same expectation language. The ramp at English IV is in the sophistication of the texts — seniors are expected to apply this skill to more complex, multi-layered arguments where claims are embedded, evidence is qualified, and counterarguments are structural. Teachers should not treat this as a repeat of junior-year work; the text selection does the heavy lifting on difficulty.

DOK 3 justification: students must reason with evidence strategically — selecting, warranting, and building a case — not merely retrieve or identify. DOK 4 would apply when defending or challenging across multiple texts in an extended research context.

STAAR analog: this TEK maps directly onto the argumentative writing and reading comprehension tasks on STAAR and SAT — particularly items that ask students to evaluate the strength of an author's support or identify evidence that most weakens or strengthens a claim.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Relationships Among Literary Elements in a Variety of Texts
expectation: Analyze relationships among thematic development, characterization, point of view, significance of setting, and plot in a variety of literary texts.
overview: This is the capstone literary analysis standard — English IV students are expected to hold multiple elements simultaneously and show how they talk to each other, not just what each one does in isolation. The move is relational: how does the setting's significance pressure the plot? How does point of view constrain what characterization is even possible? The goal is synthesis, not inventory.
studentFriendly: You can read a literary text and explain not just what the characters, setting, theme, and plot ARE — but how each one shapes and is shaped by the others, so your analysis holds together as an argument, not a list.
questionStems: Which statement BEST explains how the setting in paragraphs X–Y contributes to the development of the central theme?
The narrator's point of view in paragraph X MOST likely affects the reader's understanding of [character] by —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, how does [character's] response to [plot event] develop the theme of [concept]?
Read paragraphs X–Y. The author's characterization of [character] MOST likely serves to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the inference that the setting shapes the conflict in the story?
How does the shift in point of view between paragraphs X and Y affect the reader's interpretation of [event]?
The author MOST likely includes [plot event] in paragraph X to —
Which detail from paragraph X BEST illustrates the relationship between [character's] motivations and the story's central theme?
Based on the selection, what is the MOST likely significance of the setting in paragraph X to the outcome of the plot?
The author's characterization of [character] in paragraphs X–Y MOST nearly suggests that —

misconceptions.title: Listing Elements Is Not the Same as Analyzing Their Relationships
misconceptions.body: Students produce five-paragraph essays with one paragraph per element — theme, character, setting, plot, point of view — and call it analysis. Watch for this structure because it signals the student never asked how the elements interact. Teach them to start with a relationship ("the restricted point of view produces the characterization gap that makes the theme legible") and work outward from there, not inward from a checklist.

misconceptions.title: Theme Is the Moral at the End
misconceptions.body: Students treat theme as the lesson the author is teaching, usually lifted from the final paragraph or the protagonist's last speech. Push them deeper: theme is built across the entire text through the accumulation of character choices, setting pressure, plot reversals, and narrative vantage point. If they can name the theme without citing any of those elements, they haven't found the theme — they've found the bumper sticker.

misconceptions.title: Point of View Is Just "Who Is Telling the Story"
misconceptions.body: Students identify first-person or third-person limited and move on, as if naming the narrative mode is the analysis. The richer move is asking what the chosen point of view EXCLUDES — what can the narrator not see, not know, not say? Characterization is always partial; point of view is the mechanism of that partiality. A narrator who can't access another character's interiority is a structural fact that shapes everything else.

misconceptions.title: Setting Significance Means "The Story Takes Place in a Dark, Spooky Place"
misconceptions.body: Students reduce setting to mood backdrop — dark equals scary, rural equals isolated, city equals corrupt. The harder analysis is asking what the setting makes POSSIBLE or IMPOSSIBLE in the plot, and what it reveals about the characters who are shaped by it or chafe against it. Teach them: if you swapped the setting for its opposite and the story would still work, the setting wasn't doing anything — prove it was necessary.

misconceptions.title: Characterization and Theme Are Two Separate Conversations
misconceptions.body: Students analyze character as psychology ("she's stubborn because of her childhood") and theme as abstraction ("the novel is about power") without connecting the two. Every character trait worth analyzing is also a thematic argument — the stubborn character who refuses to leave is the text's argument about loyalty, or pride, or survival, depending on what the plot does to her stubbornness. Teach them that characterization IS thematic development, not parallel to it.

assignments.name: Relational Web
assignments.body: Students choose three literary elements from the standard and map their relationships in a visual diagram — not a Venn but a directed-arrow web that shows causality and mutual influence. Each arrow must be labeled with a sentence explaining the relationship. Pairs then compare webs and defend their choices.

assignments.name: Element Swap Thought Experiment
assignments.body: Students take a passage and rewrite one element (change the point of view, relocate the setting, alter one character trait) and write a one-page analysis of how that change would cascade through the other elements. The goal is to make the original relationships visible by breaking them.

assignments.name: Comparative Analysis Across Genres
assignments.body: Students read a short story and a poem that share a theme. They analyze how each text uses characterization, setting, and point of view differently to develop the same thematic territory — and argue which is more effective and why, with textual evidence.

assignments.name: Annotated Close Reading
assignments.body: Students annotate a 3–5 page passage, using a color-coded system: one color per element. Then they identify every place where two colors overlap — those intersections become their analysis targets. Final product: a 2-page essay that builds its argument from the overlaps, not from each color separately.

assignments.name: End-of-Course Synthesis Essay
assignments.body: Using two full literary texts read during the year, students write a 4–5 page comparative analysis arguing how both authors use the relationship between [one element] and [another element] to develop a shared thematic concern. Scored on thesis specificity, relational thinking, and quality of textual evidence.

mastery: Constructs a thesis that names a relationship among at least two literary elements — not a thesis that describes what each element does separately.
Selects textual evidence that simultaneously illuminates more than one element, demonstrating relational thinking rather than element-by-element reporting.
Explains how the chosen point of view limits, enables, or shapes what characterization and thematic development are possible in the text.
Argues for the significance of the setting — not just its presence — in terms of what it allows or forecloses in the plot and character development.
Produces analysis that holds together across a full-length literary text, not just a single passage, showing sustained relational thinking over complex narrative structures.
notes: Vertical ramp from English III: English III 6A asks students to analyze the same elements but does not foreground the relational nature of that analysis as explicitly. English IV raises the bar by requiring students to demonstrate how elements interact and condition one another — synthesis is the target, not identification or isolated analysis.

DOK 4 justification: extended reasoning is required to connect multiple elements in a principled argument; there is no single correct answer and students must defend a position using evidence from across the full text.

STAAR note: these stems mirror how the exam tests literary analysis through paired-element inference and author's purpose questions, using prose fiction or narrative nonfiction passages. The relational dimension is most often tested through "how does X contribute to Y" frames — teachers should train students to recognize that phrasing as an invitation to relational analysis.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze How Characters' Behaviors and Motivations Create Moral Dilemmas
expectation: Analyze how characters' behaviors and underlying motivations contribute to moral dilemmas that influence the plot and theme.
overview: This TEK pushes literary analysis past surface-level character description into the machinery of narrative ethics. Students have to trace the chain: motivation drives behavior, behavior creates a moral dilemma, the dilemma shapes what happens and what it means. The skill is relational — not "what does this character want?" but "how does what this character wants put the plot and theme under pressure?"
studentFriendly: You can read a literary text and explain how a character's hidden reasons for acting create an impossible situation — and how that impossible situation drives both what happens in the story and what the story is ultimately saying.
questionStems: In paragraphs X–Y, which detail BEST reveals the underlying motivation that leads the character to act as he does?
The character's decision in paragraph X MOST likely creates a moral dilemma because —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, how does the character's motivation contribute to the development of the plot?
Which statement BEST describes how the moral dilemma in paragraph X influences the story's theme?
The character's behavior in paragraph X is MOST likely intended by the author to suggest that —
