Read paragraph X. Which sentence BEST supports the inference that the character's motivation is not what other characters believe it to be?
How does the conflict between the character's desire and her sense of duty in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the moral dilemma she faces?
Based on the selection, with which statement about the relationship between motivation and moral consequence would the author MOST likely agree?
The moral dilemma introduced in paragraph X MOST directly influences the plot by —
Which detail from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that the character's underlying motivation, rather than her stated reason, drives the resolution of the story?

misconceptions.title: Motivation Is Whatever the Character Says It Is
misconceptions.body: Students take characters at their word. If a character says she's acting out of loyalty, the student writes "her motivation is loyalty" and closes the book. Teach them that stated motivation and underlying motivation are often in conflict — that gap is where the moral dilemma lives. Push them to look at behavior, not just dialogue: what does the character actually do when the stated motivation costs something?

misconceptions.title: A Moral Dilemma Is Just a Hard Decision
misconceptions.body: Students conflate "difficult choice" with "moral dilemma." Not every hard decision is morally structured — some are just logistical. A moral dilemma requires competing ethical obligations, not just competing preferences. Watch for students who describe any plot complication as a dilemma. Reframe: a moral dilemma exists when every available choice requires the character to violate something they value. That's the pressure point — and the place where theme emerges.

misconceptions.title: Plot and Theme Are Separate Things to Analyze
misconceptions.body: Students silo the analysis: one paragraph on character, one on plot, one on theme, and no arrows between them. The TEK demands the arrows — the chain of causation is the analysis. If a student can't explain how the dilemma in chapter three is still exerting pressure on the theme in the final page, they haven't done the work. Teach them to think cinematically: a good story is a machine, and moral dilemmas are the engine.

misconceptions.title: The Character Who Does Wrong Is the One with the Dilemma
misconceptions.body: Students assume moral dilemmas belong to the villain or the character who makes the "bad" choice. In fact, the richest dilemmas belong to characters who are trying to do right — and failing, or succeeding at too high a cost. Point them toward protagonists who face genuine ethical conflict: the character who must lie to protect someone, who must betray one loyalty to honor another. That's where the theme gets weight.

misconceptions.title: Underlying Motivation Means Unconscious Psychology
misconceptions.body: Students drift toward armchair psychoanalysis — the character "doesn't know" she's motivated by fear, so students project clinical diagnoses onto the text. Underlying motivation in literary analysis means motivation the text gives evidence for but doesn't state outright — it's a reading inference, not a psychological interpretation. Teach them to anchor every claim about motivation in textual evidence, not in what "makes sense" given what they know about human nature.

assignments.name: Motivation Chain Map
assignments.body: Students select a pivotal scene and build a visual diagram tracing the chain: underlying motivation → behavior → moral dilemma → plot consequence → thematic implication. Each link must include a textual citation. Pairs present their chain and defend the links they drew.

assignments.name: Two-Character Dilemma Analysis
assignments.body: Students identify a moral dilemma that implicates two characters simultaneously and write a comparative analysis: how do different motivations lead different characters into the same ethical bind? How does that convergence shape the story's theme? Length: 2–3 pages, evidence-based.

assignments.name: Rewrite the Choice
assignments.body: Students take a character's critical decision at the peak of the moral dilemma and rewrite it — the character makes the opposite choice. Then they trace the consequences: how would the plot change? Would the theme change, or would a different path arrive at the same thematic statement? Discussion follows: what does that reveal about the original text's architecture?

assignments.name: Authorial Intention Argument
assignments.body: Students argue in writing whether the author designed the moral dilemma to create sympathy for the character, to critique a social system, or to resist an easy thematic resolution. They must cite at least four passages and address a counterargument to their interpretation.

mastery: Distinguishes between a character's stated motivation and their underlying motivation, citing textual evidence for both.
Explains the specific mechanism by which a character's behavior creates a moral dilemma — not just that a dilemma exists, but what competing obligations produce it.
Traces how the moral dilemma exerts pressure on plot events and on the text's thematic development, drawing the chain explicitly.
Sustains analysis across a full text rather than isolating it to a single scene — identifies how motivation and dilemma echo across chapters or sections.
Produces written analysis that is structurally causal, not descriptive: each paragraph moves from evidence to inference to significance, not from summary to observation.
notes: Vertical ramp from English III: English III 6B asks students to analyze how characters' motivations contribute to conflict and the resolution of conflict. English IV elevates the target — "moral dilemma" is a more structurally demanding object than "conflict," requiring students to articulate competing ethical obligations rather than just opposing forces. The thematic linkage is also made explicit in English IV where English III treats theme as a downstream inference.

DOK 3 justification: students must reason through multiple causal relationships simultaneously (motivation → behavior → dilemma → plot/theme) using textual evidence — this is strategic thinking, not simple recall or skill application.

This TEK is STAAR-tested through literary selection prompts; stems are written in STAAR's author's-craft and character-analysis registers, which map directly onto how this skill appears on released tests.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Critique and Evaluate Complex Plot Structures
expectation: Critique and evaluate how complex plot structures such as subplots contribute to and advance the action.
overview: This is the terminal expectation for plot analysis — English IV students don't just identify subplots, they judge whether they're working. The real move is evaluating structural decisions: does this thread sharpen the main conflict, develop theme, or complicate the protagonist, or is it distraction dressed up as depth?
studentFriendly: You can read a novel or play, track multiple storylines at once, and make a judgment call about whether those secondary plots are doing real work — deepening the story's meaning or advancing the action — or just taking up space.
questionStems: Read paragraphs X–Y. How does the subplot introduced in this section MOST likely contribute to the central conflict of the story?
Which sentence from the passage BEST supports the conclusion that the subplot involving [character] advances the main action?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the author MOST likely introduces the secondary plot line in order to —
How does the structural choice to interrupt the main plot with the events in paragraph X affect the reader's understanding of [central character's] situation?
Which detail from the subplot in paragraphs X–Y MOST directly foreshadows the resolution of the main conflict?
The author's decision to resolve the subplot before the main plot in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Read paragraphs X–Z. What is the MOST likely effect of weaving the two plot lines together at this point in the narrative?
Which sentence from the passage BEST illustrates how the subplot develops the story's central theme?
Based on the passage, what can the reader infer about the relationship between the subplot and the main conflict?
The author's use of a parallel subplot in paragraphs X–Y MOST nearly suggests that —

misconceptions.title: Subplots Are Optional Decoration
misconceptions.body: Students treat subplots like bonus content — interesting if you like the character, cuttable if you don't. Push them to ask: what does the main plot LOSE if this thread disappears? If the answer is "nothing," the subplot is failing. If the answer is "the theme collapses" or "the protagonist's decision makes no sense," the subplot is load-bearing. Teach them to locate the structural dependency before passing judgment.

misconceptions.title: "It Advances the Action" Means "Things Happen"
misconceptions.body: Students think any subplot with activity is doing its job. They'll write "the subplot advances the action because characters do things in it." The real question is directional: does this thread push the main plot forward, create pressure on the protagonist, or reframe what's at stake? Teach them the difference between motion and momentum — a subplot can be full of events and still be dead weight.

misconceptions.title: Critique Means Dismissal
misconceptions.body: When students are asked to critique a plot structure, they default to verdict: "The subplot was unnecessary" or "It was a good choice." Neither is a critique. A real critique identifies WHAT the subplot is attempting, WHERE it succeeds or fails at that attempt, and WHY the author may have made that structural call anyway. Watch for students who deliver a ruling without a trial.

misconceptions.title: Subplots Only Involve Secondary Characters
misconceptions.body: Students assume the main character belongs to the main plot and secondary characters belong to subplots. Many complex narratives plant the protagonist in both — the subplot may reveal a version of the protagonist the main action never could. Teach them to track which storyline is illuminating which aspect of character, not just who appears on which pages.

misconceptions.title: Complexity Equals Quality
misconceptions.body: By English IV, students have absorbed the idea that more structure equals more sophistication. They'll praise tangled, multi-threaded narratives just because they're tangled. Push them harder: does the complexity serve the meaning, or is it obscuring a thin central conflict? A lean two-thread structure that locks together at the climax is often more sophisticated than six threads that never resolve. Evaluate the architecture, not the blueprint's density.

assignments.name: Thread Mapping
assignments.body: Students diagram the major and minor plot threads of a complex text on a single timeline, marking every point where threads intersect, diverge, or resolve. For each intersection, they write one sentence explaining what the crossing accomplishes narratively. The map becomes the evidence base for a critique essay.

assignments.name: Subplot Autopsy
assignments.body: Students select one subplot from the course text and write a structured critique: what the subplot attempts, where it succeeds, where it falls short, and what the narrative would lose or gain without it. Minimum 2 pages. Must include textual evidence from both the subplot AND the main plot.

assignments.name: Cut and Defend
assignments.body: Students identify a subplot they believe is the weakest structural choice in the text. They write a 1-page argument for cutting it entirely, then swap with a partner who writes a 1-page counterargument for keeping it. Both drafts must be grounded in textual evidence about narrative function, not personal preference.

assignments.name: Parallel Structure Comparison
assignments.body: Students read two texts with subplots (one literary, one contemporary — novel vs. prestige TV pilot or screenplay excerpt) and compare how each uses secondary threads. Written analysis: which structural approach is more effective, and why?

mastery: Identifies specific subplot threads and traces the points at which they intersect with, pressure, or redirect the main conflict.
Evaluates subplot effectiveness using narrative criteria — theme development, character revelation, pacing, foreshadowing — not personal investment in the characters.
Distinguishes between a subplot that advances action and one that merely runs parallel to it.
Produces a critique that names what the structural choice is attempting before judging whether it succeeds.
Sustains analysis across a full complex text, not just isolated passages, to evaluate cumulative structural impact.
notes: Vertical ramp from English III: Std 6(C) in English III asks students to analyze how complex plot structures contribute to and advance the action. English IV adds *critique and evaluate* — the cognitive demand shifts from explaining what the structure does to judging whether it works and defending that judgment against textual evidence.

DOK 4 justification: students must apply multiple narrative criteria simultaneously, sustain analysis across an extended text, and produce a reasoned defense of their evaluation — not a summary or identification task.

The word "such as subplots" is illustrative, not exhaustive — in-medias-res openings, multiple climaxes, braided timelines, and frame narratives all qualify as complex plot structures worth evaluating under this TEK.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Evaluate How Context of Setting Influences Plot, Characterization, and Theme
expectation: Evaluate how the historical, social, and economic context of setting(s) influences the plot, characterization, and theme.
overview: This is the capstone move on setting: not identifying where and when, but evaluating what the forces of history, society, and economics actually do to the story. Students must argue — with evidence — that a particular context shaped character choices, drove the plot, and generated the theme. The skill is causal, not descriptive.
studentFriendly: You can read a novel or story and explain how the specific historical moment, social pressures, and economic conditions of its setting aren't just backdrop — they're what make the characters act, the plot unfold, and the theme mean what it means.
questionStems: Based on paragraphs X–Y, how does the historical context of the setting MOST likely contribute to the conflict the protagonist faces?
Which detail from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that the economic conditions of the setting drive the plot?
The social context described in paragraph X MOST likely influences the protagonist's decision to — because —
Which statement BEST evaluates the relationship between the setting's historical period and the story's central theme?
How does the economic context established in paragraphs X–Y shape the characterization of [character]?
Based on the selection, which inference about the effect of the social setting on the plot is BEST supported?
The author's use of [historical/social/economic detail] in paragraph X MOST likely develops the theme that —
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates how the setting's social conditions limit or enable [character]'s choices?
How does the historical context introduced in paragraph X contribute to the reader's understanding of [character]'s motivations?
Based on the selection as a whole, which statement MOST accurately evaluates how the setting's context shapes the story's resolution?

misconceptions.title: Setting Is the Stage, Not the Engine
misconceptions.body: Students describe setting and move on, treating it as atmosphere rather than causation. They'll write "the story takes place during the Great Depression" and never explain what the Depression actually forces anyone to do. Teach them to ask: if you drop these characters into a different economic moment, does the plot still happen? If the answer is no, they've found the causal link — now they need to argue it explicitly.

misconceptions.title: "Historical Context" Means Listing Facts from Class
misconceptions.body: Students import history-class knowledge and drop it into literary analysis as decoration — a paragraph of dates and events that never connects to a specific sentence in the text. Push them to anchor every contextual claim to a textual detail. The standard is evaluate, not report; the evidence lives in the book, not in a history textbook.

misconceptions.title: All Three Contexts Are Interchangeable
misconceptions.body: Students flatten historical, social, and economic into a single undifferentiated "context" and apply it vaguely to everything. Teach them to disaggregate: social context shapes what characters are allowed to want, economic context shapes what they're able to reach, and historical context shapes what events are even possible. Each lens produces different analytical pressure — the skill is knowing which one is doing the most work in a given passage.

misconceptions.title: Characterization Is Personality, Not Position
misconceptions.body: Students evaluate how context shapes whether a character is "kind" or "selfish" rather than how it shapes what structural position they occupy, what options they have, and what they're willing to risk. A character's courage reads differently when the historical context makes that courage cost something specific. Teach them to evaluate character choices against the constraints the context creates — not just the traits the character displays.

misconceptions.title: The Theme Is Already Inside the Text, Context or Not
misconceptions.body: Students treat theme as something the author planted independently of when and where the book is set — as if "loyalty is complicated" would mean the same thing in Elizabethan England, the Jim Crow South, and present-day Seoul. Push them to argue that context isn't incidental to the theme; in the strongest literary works, the theme is only fully legible because of the specific historical, social, or economic pressure the setting creates.

assignments.name: Context-to-Consequence Chain
assignments.body: Students select a pivotal plot event and trace it backward to its contextual origins. They must name the specific historical, social, or economic condition that made the event possible or inevitable, cite textual evidence for each link, and write a one-page argument defending the chain. No event is allowed to float free of context.

assignments.name: Parallel Text Comparison
assignments.body: Students read two short texts set in different historical or economic moments but with similar character situations (e.g., two characters facing social exclusion). They write a comparative analysis evaluating how the different contexts produce different plot trajectories and thematic stakes, using evidence from both texts.

assignments.name: What If the Context Changed?
assignments.body: Students choose one contextual element — a law, an economic condition, a social norm — and write a structured argument answering: if this one element were removed, what in the plot could not have happened, what in the characterization would shift, and what theme would become unspeakable? The point is to prove causation by negation.

assignments.name: Annotated Passage Analysis
assignments.body: Students select a 2–3 page passage dense with contextual pressure and annotate every detail that carries historical, social, or economic weight. They then write a synthesis paragraph evaluating which context — historical, social, or economic — does the most work in that passage, defending their ranking with the annotations as evidence.

mastery: Distinguishes between historical, social, and economic context and applies each analytically rather than interchangeably.
Makes explicit causal arguments — not just "the setting is X" but "because the setting is X, the character must do Y, which drives Z in the plot."
Anchors every contextual claim to specific textual evidence rather than importing general historical knowledge as a substitute for close reading.
Evaluates how context shapes the theme by arguing that the theme's meaning depends on the specific pressures the setting creates.
Sustains the analysis across plot, characterization, AND theme — not just one or two of the three.
notes: Vertical ramp: English III 6D asks students to analyze how setting influences character and plot development. English IV lifts the verb to evaluate and adds economic context explicitly as a third dimension alongside historical and social. The expectation at the senior level is a full causal argument, not an analysis of observed effects.

DOK 4 justification: evaluation across multiple interdependent dimensions (historical, social, economic) applied simultaneously to plot, characterization, and theme requires extended reasoning and defense of judgment — not retrieval or application.

STAAR typically tests setting-context skills through paired or longer prose passages where the historical or social moment is embedded rather than stated. Stems that ask students to infer causation from contextual detail (rather than identify setting description) reflect the actual assessment demand.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Read and Analyze British Literature Across Literary Periods
expectation: Read and analyze British literature across literary periods.
overview: This is the anchor TEK for English IV — the course is organized around it. Students are not just reading British texts; they are reading them as artifacts of their era, tracing how literary form, voice, and preoccupation shift from Old English through the postmodern. The analytical move is always double: what is this text doing, and what does the period it comes from explain about why it does that?
studentFriendly: You can read a British text — a medieval ballad, a Romantic poem, a modernist novel — and explain how the time and culture it came from shaped its form, its concerns, and its language.
questionStems: Based on the selection, which statement BEST describes how the author's historical context influenced the central argument of the text?
The author's use of [literary device] in paragraph X is MOST likely a reflection of which literary period's values?
Which detail from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that the text belongs to the [period] literary tradition?
How does the author's perspective in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the reader's understanding of the period in which the text was written?
The shift in tone between paragraphs X and Y MOST likely reflects —
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates a concern central to the [literary period]?
Based on the selection, the author MOST likely uses [structural feature] to —
A reader can infer from paragraphs X–Y that the social or political conditions of the period affected the text by —
How does the form of the selection reflect conventions associated with [literary period or genre]?
Which of the following BEST describes how the author's treatment of [theme] differs from or aligns with the values of the literary period in which the text was produced?

misconceptions.title: "Literary Period" Is Just a Date Range
misconceptions.body: Students treat literary periods as trivia — they memorize that Romanticism runs roughly 1785–1830 and stop there. The meaningful move is understanding what Romanticism is a RESPONSE to: industrialization, rationalism, the political upheaval of revolution. Teach them that every period is an argument — a set of writers pushing back against what came before. Knowing the date without knowing the argument makes the label useless.

misconceptions.title: British Literature Means Shakespeare and Whatever
misconceptions.body: Students arrive with a mental map of British lit that includes Shakespeare, maybe Dickens, maybe Orwell, and then fog. They underestimate the range — from Beowulf to Zadie Smith, from Donne's metaphysical conceits to Woolf's interior monologue. Watch for students who lump everything pre-1900 into one undifferentiated "old English" category. Push them to notice the vast differences between, say, a Cavalier lyric and a Victorian essay, even when both feel old.

misconceptions.title: Historical Context Is Background, Not Analysis
misconceptions.body: Students gesture at context — "this was written during World War I, so it's about war" — without using it analytically. That's not analysis; it's Wikipedia. Teach them the sharper move: how does the war change what the speaker can say, what form the poem takes, what gets left out? Context is not the answer — it's the pressure that shapes the text's choices.

misconceptions.title: Close Reading and Period Reading Are Separate Skills
misconceptions.body: Students think literary period analysis means zooming out and close reading means zooming in, and they switch between modes without connecting them. The real skill is doing both simultaneously — noticing a specific word choice and tracing how it carries period ideology. Reframe this: close reading is HOW you do period analysis, not an alternative to it.

misconceptions.title: Canonical Means Correct
misconceptions.body: When they read a text assigned as "great" — Milton, Austen, Tennyson — students defer. The text is canonical, therefore it must be doing everything right. Push them to hold both: a text can be historically significant AND ideologically complicated, formally brilliant AND morally limited by its period. The canon earns its place not by being above criticism but by rewarding it.

assignments.name: Period to Period
assignments.body: Students read two short texts from different British literary periods on a related theme (e.g., nature, duty, the individual). They write a comparative analysis explaining how the period each text came from shapes its treatment of the theme. Evidence must be textual; claims about period must be specific.

assignments.name: Text in Context
assignments.body: Given a British text, students identify three specific choices (structural, stylistic, or thematic) the author makes and argue — using historical and literary-period context — why those choices would have been available, legible, or necessary at that moment in literary history. One-page written response.

assignments.name: The Anachronism Test
assignments.body: Students take a passage from a British text and rewrite one paragraph as if a writer from a different period had written it. They then write a short explanation of every choice they changed and why. Forces them to articulate what the period actually demands from a text.

assignments.name: Period Presentation
assignments.body: Small groups each take one literary period (Old English, Medieval, Renaissance, Restoration/Augustan, Romantic, Victorian, Modernist, Postmodern). They teach the class: what is this period ARGUING for, who is it arguing against, and here is a text that proves it. Presentation must include a close reading of at least one passage.

mastery: Identifies the literary period of an unfamiliar British text using internal textual evidence — not just external labels — and explains the reasoning.
Uses historical and cultural context analytically, not decoratively: connects specific period pressures to specific textual choices.
Moves fluidly between close reading and period-level analysis in the same written response, connecting micro-level language choices to macro-level period concerns.
Holds critical distance from canonical texts — can name a text's significance AND name what the period's ideology costs it.
Distinguishes meaningfully between literary periods rather than treating pre-modern British literature as a uniform category.
notes: Vertical jump from English III: English III Std 7A requires students to read and analyze world literature across literary periods — the scope is global. English IV narrows to British literature specifically but deepens the expectation: students are now expected to command a specific canon with its own periodization, its own historiography, and its own set of ideological tensions (empire, class, religion, gender). The skill is similar; the demands of the specific tradition are much greater.

DOK 3 justification: reading across literary periods requires sustained strategic thinking — students must apply period knowledge to interpret texts and construct analytical arguments that connect form, content, and historical moment. Extended responses and comparisons push toward DOK 4 in performance tasks.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog in the sense of a single tested passage type — STAAR English IV uses British literary passages but assesses craft and structure (Strand 8) rather than period classification explicitly. Question stems above mirror how STAAR tests literary analysis of these texts in practice: author's craft, textual evidence, inference about purpose and context.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze Poetic Effects Across Literary Time Periods and Cultures
expectation: Analyze the effects of sound, form, figurative language, graphics, and dramatic structure in poetry across literary time periods and cultures.
overview: This is the capstone poetry standard — students are not just identifying devices, they are explaining what those devices DO across the full breadth of poetic tradition. The "across literary time periods and cultures" clause is not decorative; it requires genuine comparative reach, from Old English verse to contemporary spoken word, from Anglo-American formalism to postcolonial poetry in translation.
studentFriendly: You can read any poem — ancient or modern, from any tradition — and explain exactly how its sound, shape, figurative language, visuals, and structure work together to create their effects on a reader.
questionStems: Read the poem in paragraph X. The speaker's use of [device] in lines X–X MOST likely creates which of the following effects?
Which line from the poem BEST illustrates the relationship between the poem's form and its central idea?
The figurative language in stanza X is MOST likely intended to —
How does the sound device used in lines X–X contribute to the poem's meaning?
Based on the poem, which statement BEST describes the effect of the poem's structure on the reader's understanding of the speaker's situation?
The poet's use of [graphic element / white space / line breaks] in lines X–X MOST nearly suggests —
Which of the following BEST describes how the shift in form between stanzas X and X develops the poem's theme?
Read the two poems. With which statement about the use of [figurative language / sound / form] would both poets MOST likely agree?
The dramatic structure of the poem is MOST likely intended to —
Based on the poem's imagery and sound devices, the reader can infer that the speaker's attitude toward [subject] is —

misconceptions.title: "Across Cultures" Means Picking One Country Per Assignment
misconceptions.body: Students (and teachers) treat the cross-cultural requirement as a checkbox — one American poem, one British poem, done. Push them further: a poem from the Harlem Renaissance, a Sikh shabad set as verse, a praise song from the Yoruba tradition, a haiku by Bashō. The requirement exists to surface how sound, form, and structure are NOT universal — the same move means different things in different traditions. Teach them to ask what the culture assumes about what poetry IS before they evaluate what the poem does.

misconceptions.title: Sound Devices Are Just Background Music
misconceptions.body: Students identify rhyme and alliteration as decoration — pleasant extras layered on top of the "real" meaning. Teach them that sound IS argument: the catch of a hard consonant, the open vowel held across a line, the silence after an enjambment. When they hear sound as ornament rather than meaning, push back with a question: what does this poem lose if you strip the sound? If they can't answer, they haven't analyzed the effect yet.

misconceptions.title: Form Is a Container, Not a Force
misconceptions.body: Students read the sonnet as a box the poet chose to put words into. They miss that the volta isn't decoration — it's the pivot the meaning turns on. They miss that the ghazal's radif and qafia are not arbitrary; they carry cultural and spiritual weight. Teach them to ask: what does this particular form demand of a reader? What does it make possible that free verse cannot?

misconceptions.title: Figurative Language Analysis Stops at Identification
misconceptions.body: Students write "the poet uses a metaphor comparing life to a journey" and consider the job done. That's identification, not analysis. The follow-through is always: what does this comparison MAKE the reader do, feel, assume, or question? Push them with "so what?" relentlessly until the response includes a claim about effect on the reader, not just a description of the device.

misconceptions.title: Graphics and White Space Are Decorative Formatting
misconceptions.body: Students treat line breaks, stanza spacing, indentation, and visual layout as the typesetter's job, not the poet's. They skip right over Lucille Clifton's lowercase, George Herbert's shaped verse, or the strategic blankness in Claudia Rankine. Teach them to treat every spatial choice as a deliberate act: where does the eye pause? What does the gap ask the reader to supply? What does the shape on the page say before a single word is read?

assignments.name: Tradition Autopsy
assignments.body: Students select one poem from before 1800 and one from after 2000, from different cultural traditions. They write a comparative analysis explaining how sound, form, and figurative language operate differently in each — and what those differences reveal about each tradition's assumptions about what poetry should do.

assignments.name: Strip-and-Restore
assignments.body: Students take a formally complex poem (villanelle, ghazal, pantoum) and rewrite it as plain prose. Then they write one paragraph explaining exactly what was lost — naming specific sonic, structural, and figurative effects — and one paragraph on what, if anything, the prose version clarified. The exercise forces articulation of what form actually contributes.

assignments.name: Graphic Poetry Close Read
assignments.body: Students analyze a poem where visual layout is a primary device — George Herbert, E.E. Cummings, Claudia Rankine, or a contemporary concrete poem. The written analysis must account for the spatial design as explicitly as it accounts for figurative language and sound.

assignments.name: Cross-Cultural Sound Comparison
assignments.body: Students listen to recordings of two poems from different traditions — one Anglo-American, one from another language or oral tradition — and write an analysis comparing how sound operates in each. They must address what translates across traditions and what does not.

mastery: Identifies sound, form, figurative language, graphic elements, and dramatic structure as interconnected — not as a checklist of separate features.
Explains the EFFECT of each device on the reader, not merely its presence in the poem.
Contextualizes poetic choices within the literary tradition and culture from which the poem emerges.
Produces comparative analysis that draws genuine distinctions between how different traditions use the same devices.
Reads visual and spatial elements — white space, line breaks, stanza shape — as deliberate craft choices with analyzable effects.
notes: Vertical ramp from English III: English III 7B asks students to analyze the effects of poetic devices within a poem; English IV adds the cross-temporal and cross-cultural dimension — students must now situate those devices within tradition and compare across traditions. The skill moves from close reading a poem to reading a poem as a cultural artifact.

DOK 3 justification: students must use strategic thinking to explain how multiple elements interact across unfamiliar cultural contexts — not recall or basic skill application. DOK 4 would apply if the task requires extended reasoning producing a novel synthesis (appropriate for full essays, not individual stems).
Graphics and dramatic structure are often skipped in instruction; call them out explicitly in unit planning.

STAAR note: this TEK maps most directly to poetry passages on STAAR with questions targeting figurative language effect, structural shifts, and tone. Cross-cultural comparison is less likely to be tested directly but underpins the depth needed for multi-text sets.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze and Evaluate How Dramatic Elements Advance the Plot
expectation: Analyze and evaluate how the relationships among the dramatic elements advance the plot.
overview: This is the capstone move in drama analysis — not just naming dramatic elements but tracing how they interact to drive events forward and then judging how effectively they do so. By English IV, identifying conflict or stage direction is scaffolding; the real work is arguing whether the playwright's structural choices earned the outcomes they produced.
studentFriendly: You can read a play, map how its parts — dialogue, conflict, staging, acts — work together to push the story forward, and make an evidence-based argument about whether those choices actually work.
questionStems: Which of the following BEST explains how the conflict introduced in Act I contributes to the resolution in Act III?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the stage directions MOST likely serve to —
Which statement BEST evaluates the relationship between the exposition and the rising action in the selection?
The dialogue between [Character A] and [Character B] in Scene X MOST likely advances the plot by —
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that the climax is a direct result of the dramatic irony established earlier?
How does the playwright's use of [dramatic element] in Act X contribute to the development of the central conflict?
Based on the selection, which statement MOST accurately evaluates how the falling action prepares the reader for the denouement?
The shift in [Character's] motivation in Scene X MOST likely affects the plot by —
Which detail from the selection BEST supports the inference that the subplot in Act II is essential to the resolution of the main conflict?
The playwright's decision to withhold [information] until Scene X MOST nearly serves to —

misconceptions.title: Listing Elements Is the Same as Analyzing Relationships
misconceptions.body: Students catalog what they see — "there is conflict, there is dialogue, there is foreshadowing" — and call it analysis. Push them deeper: the TEK asks for relationships. Teach them to use connective language — "because," "which causes," "as a result of" — to force the argument. If there is no causal chain in their analysis, they haven't analyzed; they've inventoried.

misconceptions.title: Every Dramatic Element Matters Equally
misconceptions.body: Students treat all elements as carrying equal plot weight, spending as much time on a minor stage direction as on the central conflict. Teach them that evaluation requires hierarchy — some elements are doing the structural heavy lifting and some are texture. The evaluative move is identifying which elements are load-bearing and making the case for why.

misconceptions.title: The Plot Just Happens; Elements Are Decoration
misconceptions.body: Students write about plot as if it arrives independently of craft choices, then tack on "the stage directions show this." Reframe this explicitly: the playwright assembled these elements to manufacture the plot. Nothing in a well-constructed play is accidental. Watch for passive constructions — "the conflict occurs when" — and redirect: "the playwright builds conflict through X, which then forces Y."

misconceptions.title: "Advance" Means Only "What Happens Next"
misconceptions.body: Students reduce plot advancement to chronology — this scene happens, then this one. The richer reading is causal and thematic: an element advances the plot when it generates consequence, reveals character in ways that shift relationships, or raises the dramatic stakes. Teach them to distinguish sequence from causation, especially in complex plays where scenes don't advance events linearly.

misconceptions.title: Evaluation Means Opinion Without Evidence
misconceptions.body: When students are asked to evaluate, they slide into preference — "I think the ending was effective because it surprised me." The evaluative standard here is structural, not personal: does the element do what drama requires of it? Does the climax follow credibly from what was established? Teach them to build evaluative criteria from the text itself before rendering judgment.

assignments.name: Element Causation Map
assignments.body: Students select a play studied in class and create a structured diagram tracing how at least four dramatic elements — conflict, dialogue, staging, subplot, etc. — connect causally to produce the resolution. Each connection on the map requires a quoted line or stage direction as evidence.

assignments.name: Playwright in the Hot Seat
assignments.body: Students write a 1-page evaluative argument defending or challenging one major structural choice the playwright made — a late-act revelation, a subplot that delays the climax, a stage direction that recontextualizes dialogue. The argument must use evidence from at least two different points in the text to show the relationship held (or broke down) across the play.

assignments.name: Two-Play Comparative Evaluation
assignments.body: Students read excerpts from two plays that use the same dramatic element differently (e.g., dramatic irony in Oedipus Rex versus Death of a Salesman). They write a focused comparative evaluation: which play uses the element more effectively to advance the plot, and why? Evidence from both texts required.

assignments.name: Scene Surgery
assignments.body: Students select a pivotal scene and rewrite it with one dramatic element removed or significantly altered — then write an analysis of what that removal breaks in the plot's forward momentum. The goal is demonstrating, by subtraction, what the element was actually doing.

mastery: Explains not just what dramatic elements are present but how they cause, complicate, or resolve other elements — tracing relationships rather than listing features.
Builds a hierarchy of elements, identifying which are structurally essential to plot advancement and defending that claim with textual evidence.

Evaluates structural choices against craft criteria derived from the text itself — not personal taste or surprise — and supports the evaluation with specific evidence across multiple points in the play.
Uses causal language ("which forces," "because of this shift," "as a result of the withheld information") to make the analysis precise and testable.
Sustains the argument across the full arc of a play, not just at the climax — showing how early element relationships set up consequences that arrive later.
notes: Vertical ramp from English III: English III 7C asks students to analyze how dramatic elements advance the plot; English IV adds the evaluative layer — students must now judge how effectively and purposefully those relationships operate. The analytical foundation is assumed; the new work is argument and evidence-backed judgment.

DOK 4 justification: students must synthesize relationships among multiple elements across the full arc of a play, evaluate structural effectiveness against craft criteria, and defend that evaluation with extended textual reasoning — this exceeds retrieval, application, or single-criterion analysis.
This standard sits under the "Informational & Argumentative" substrand by TEKS taxonomy, but its content is drama — expect student work that bridges literary analysis and evaluative argument.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Critique and Evaluate Characteristics and Structural Elements of Informational Texts
expectation: Critique and evaluate characteristics and structural elements of informational texts such as clear thesis, effective supporting evidence, pertinent examples, commentary, summary, and conclusion.
overview: This is the capstone move for informational text analysis in English IV — students are not just identifying structural features, they are judging them. Is the thesis actually clear? Is the evidence actually effective? The "such as" list is a scaffold, not a checklist; the real skill is evaluating whether each element is doing the work it's supposed to do.
studentFriendly: You can read an informational text — an essay, an editorial, a report — and evaluate not just what it says but whether its structure holds up: whether the thesis is clear, the evidence is actually relevant, the examples earn their place, and the conclusion delivers.
questionStems: Which statement BEST expresses the central thesis of the selection?
The author's use of evidence in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the author's central claim?
The example in paragraph X is MOST likely included to —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the author's commentary on the evidence is intended to —
How does the conclusion in paragraph X contribute to the overall effectiveness of the text?
Which revision to the thesis statement in paragraph 1 would MOST improve the clarity of the author's argument?
The author's use of summary in paragraph X MOST likely helps the reader by —
A reader could MOST reasonably conclude that the evidence in paragraph X is —
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates a weakness in the author's support for the central claim?

misconceptions.title: A Thesis Statement Just Has to Exist
misconceptions.body: Students hunt for a claim, find one, and stop there — they check the box without evaluating whether the thesis is actually doing its job. Teach them: a thesis can be present and still fail — it can be vague, circular, or so broad that it commits to nothing. Push them to ask not "Is there a thesis?" but "Does this thesis give the rest of the essay a target to hit?"

misconceptions.title: "Supporting Evidence" Means Any Evidence
misconceptions.body: Students accept quotations, statistics, and anecdotes uncritically because the text bothered to include them. They conflate volume with validity. Teach them the difference between evidence that appears relevant and evidence that actually advances the claim — a compelling statistic about the wrong population, or a quotation pulled without context, is not effective support, no matter how authoritative it sounds.

misconceptions.title: Examples and Evidence Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students collapse "pertinent examples" into "supporting evidence" and treat them as one category. Watch for this in written critiques — they'll evaluate the examples as if they're proof and miss the fact that examples illustrate while evidence argues. Teach them: an example shows what something looks like; evidence demonstrates that something is true. Both can fail, but they fail differently.

misconceptions.title: Commentary Is Optional Filler
misconceptions.body: Students skim or skip analytical commentary in a text because it isn't the "real" content — they're looking for facts and quotes. Teach them that commentary is where the author's thinking actually lives: it explains the significance of evidence, connects examples to the thesis, and does the argumentative work. A text with strong evidence and no commentary is a body without a spine. If the commentary is weak, the argument is weak, even if the evidence is strong.

misconceptions.title: A Strong Conclusion Just Summarizes
misconceptions.body: Students evaluate conclusions by whether they restate the main points — they call it "effective" if everything got mentioned again. Push them deeper: a conclusion earns its place by landing the argument, not filing a report on it. Teach them to ask what the conclusion adds — if a reader could delete the conclusion without losing any meaning, it has failed, no matter how competently it restates the thesis.

assignments.name: Structural Autopsy
assignments.body: Students select a published informational essay (op-ed, long-form journalism, academic essay). They annotate for thesis, evidence, examples, commentary, summary, and conclusion — then write a one-page critique that evaluates the effectiveness of each element with specific textual evidence. The standard is not "does it exist" but "does it work."

assignments.name: Failure Report
assignments.body: Students receive a deliberately flawed informational text (teacher-written or an edited weak student model). They write a structured critique identifying exactly what fails and why — then revise the two weakest sections of the text, explaining in a brief note what they changed and what principle guided the revision.

assignments.name: Comparative Evaluation
assignments.body: Two informational texts on the same topic, different quality levels. Students write a critique comparing the effectiveness of each text's structural elements — thesis, evidence, and conclusion — using direct quotation and explicit evaluative language. Goal: they finish knowing which text is stronger and being able to say exactly why.

mastery: Evaluates thesis clarity using specific criteria — not just "it's vague" but identifying precisely what the claim fails to commit to or what it over-promises.
Distinguishes between evidence that is present and evidence that is effective, citing specific reasons the evidence does or does not advance the claim.
Identifies the function of examples versus the function of evidence and critiques each on appropriate terms.
Evaluates commentary as the connective tissue of the argument — recognizing when its absence or weakness undermines otherwise strong evidence.
Assesses the conclusion's contribution beyond summarization, noting whether it genuinely closes the argument or merely restates it.
notes: Vertical alignment: English III 7D(i) asks students to analyze characteristics and structural elements of informational texts. English IV escalates the verb to critique and evaluate — students are no longer describing what a text does but rendering a judgment on whether it does it well. The move is from analysis to assessment, and the bar is proportionally higher.

DOK 4 justification: sustained evaluation across multiple structural elements with evidence-based judgment required at each step; students must hold criteria, apply them to a complex text, and defend their conclusions in extended writing.

The "such as" in the expectation signals that the list (thesis, evidence, examples, commentary, summary, conclusion) is illustrative, not exhaustive — other structural elements (counterargument, transitions, framing) are fair game. Note this in instruction so students don't treat the six items as a closed checklist.

STAAR typically embeds this TEK in passages that present argumentation as informational prose — editorials, essays, reported arguments. Question stems will often ask students to evaluate a specific structural move rather than identify it.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Critique Organizational Design and Author's Purpose in Informational Texts
expectation: Critique and evaluate characteristics and structural elements of informational texts such as the relationship between organizational design and author's purpose.
overview: This is the capstone version of a skill students have been building since middle school — but the English IV move is evaluation, not just identification. Students must argue whether the organizational choices an author made actually serve the stated or implied purpose, and where they fall short.
studentFriendly: You can read an informational text — an editorial, a report, a long-form article — and explain not just how it's organized but whether that structure was the right choice for what the author was trying to do.
questionStems: The author MOST likely begins the article with the personal anecdote in paragraph 1 rather than the statistical evidence in paragraph 3 in order to —
How does the organizational structure of paragraphs 4–7 contribute to the author's purpose in this section?
Based on the text, which statement BEST describes the relationship between the author's use of a problem-solution structure and the author's central argument?
The author's decision to place the counterargument in paragraph 8 rather than earlier in the text MOST likely serves to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that the author's organizational choices reinforce the text's persuasive purpose?
A reader could MOST reasonably infer that the author chose a chronological structure in paragraphs 2–6 in order to —
How does the shift in organizational pattern between paragraphs 5 and 6 affect the development of the author's central idea?
The author's use of a compare-and-contrast structure in paragraphs 9–11 MOST likely reflects an attempt to —
Which organizational choice from the text MOST undermines the author's stated purpose? Use evidence from the selection to support your response.
Based on paragraphs 12–14, the author's decision to end with a call to action rather than a summary MOST suggests that the author's primary purpose is to —

misconceptions.title: Organization Is Decoration, Not Argument
misconceptions.body: Students treat structure as a neutral container — the author picked chronological order because that's how things happened, end of analysis. Push them to see that every structural choice is a rhetorical bet: starting with anecdote bets that emotion opens the door to evidence; using problem-solution bets that readers feel the problem before they'll buy the solution. If the bet doesn't pay off, the structure fails the purpose. Teach them to name the bet and evaluate whether it landed.

misconceptions.title: "The Author's Purpose Is to Inform" Covers Everything
misconceptions.body: When students identify purpose as "to inform," they've stopped thinking. That answer forecloses critique before it starts — if the purpose is vague enough, any structure looks fine. Require them to name a specific purpose: "to convince readers that existing climate reporting obscures individual responsibility." Now they can evaluate whether the structure actually serves that sharper claim, and they'll find cracks.

misconceptions.title: Critique Means Saying the Structure Is Bad
misconceptions.body: Students interpret critique as attack. They'll say the structure "doesn't work" without being able to say what a better structure would look like or why this one fails. Real critique is comparative — it requires knowing what the alternatives were and why the choice matters. Teach them: critique isn't a verdict, it's a reasoned argument about tradeoffs.

misconceptions.title: Longer Texts Have More Sophisticated Organization
misconceptions.body: Students assume complexity equals quality — a text with seven structural moves must be more purposeful than one with two. Some of the most powerful informational prose uses simple, relentless structure precisely because the argument demands it. Watch for students who praise texts for organizational complexity without asking whether the complexity served the reader or obscured the argument.

misconceptions.title: Organizational Design and Text Features Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students collapse structure into surface features — headers, bullet points, sidebars. Those are layout decisions, not organizational design. Organizational design is the logic of sequencing: why this section comes before that one, why evidence is delayed, why the counterargument lands where it does. Teach them to read the architecture of the argument, not just the furniture.

assignments.name: Structural Autopsy
assignments.body: Students select a long-form informational article (1,500+ words) and map its organizational structure section by section. Then they write a one-page critique: does the structure serve the author's purpose? Where does it help and where does it hurt? Evidence from the text required.

assignments.name: Reorganize and Defend
assignments.body: Students take a short informational text (600–900 words), reorder its sections into a structure they argue would better serve the author's purpose, and write a paragraph justifying each change. The assignment grades whether the justification is evidence-based, not whether the reorganization is "correct."

assignments.name: Compare the Blueprints
assignments.body: Two informational texts on the same topic use different organizational structures (e.g., one problem-solution, one thematic). Students write a comparative critique: which structure better serves its author's stated or implied purpose, and why?

mastery: Names the specific organizational structure used in a text and connects it to a precisely stated author's purpose — not "to inform," but a defensible claim about what the author is trying to make readers believe or do.
Evaluates whether the structural choice serves the purpose, identifying specific moments in the text where the organization strengthens or weakens the argument.
Distinguishes between organizational design (the logic of sequencing) and surface text features (headers, bullets) without prompting.
Writes critique that is comparative — naming alternatives and explaining the tradeoff — rather than simply delivering a verdict.
Holds evaluation and fairness simultaneously: can identify where a structure genuinely works without softening the critique of where it doesn't.
notes: Vertical ramp: English III 7(D)(ii) asks students to analyze the relationship between organizational design and author's purpose. English IV adds *critique and evaluate* — students are no longer describing the relationship; they are arguing about whether it works. The cognitive load is meaningfully higher: analysis describes, evaluation judges.

DOK 4 justification: sustained evaluation across multiple criteria (organizational logic, rhetorical purpose, effectiveness) with extended reasoning required to defend a judgment. No single "correct" answer exists; quality of argumentation determines mastery.

STAAR analog note: this TEK maps directly onto STAAR's author's purpose and craft questions. Stems using paragraph references, structural shifts, and purpose-inference frames are consistent with released STAAR item patterns for informational texts.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Critique and Evaluate Argumentative Text Structure and Elements
expectation: Critique and evaluate characteristics and structural elements of argumentative texts such as clear arguable thesis, appeals, structure of the argument, convincing conclusion, and call to action.
overview: This is the capstone evaluation move for argumentative reading in English IV — students don't just identify structural elements, they judge whether those elements are doing their job. The target is precision: can a student say not only "there is a thesis" but "this thesis is or isn't arguable, and here is why that matters for everything else in the text."
studentFriendly: You can read any argumentative text — an op-ed, a policy brief, a speech — and judge whether its thesis is actually debatable, its appeals are honest and well-placed, its argument structure holds together, and whether its conclusion actually closes the deal.
questionStems: Which of the following BEST describes how the author's thesis in paragraph 1 affects the overall structure of the argument?
The author's use of emotional appeal in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST represents a clear, arguable thesis?
Read this sentence from paragraph X: [sentence]. The author MOST likely includes this call to action to —
How does the structure of paragraphs X–Y contribute to the effectiveness of the author's argument?
Based on the selection, which statement BEST critiques the strength of the author's conclusion?
The author's appeal to authority in paragraph X is MOST effectively supported by —
Which sentence from paragraph X MOST nearly undermines the author's central claim?
The author organizes paragraphs X through Y primarily to —
With which statement about the author's argumentative structure would a careful reader MOST likely agree?

misconceptions.title: A Thesis Is Any Sentence That States the Topic
misconceptions.body: Students flag the opening sentence of every argument as the thesis, regardless of whether it takes a debatable position. Teach them the test: if no reasonable person could disagree with the statement, it is not an arguable thesis — it is a fact or a framing sentence. Push them to find the sentence a smart opponent would argue against.

misconceptions.title: Appeals Are Just "Ethos, Pathos, Logos" Labels
misconceptions.body: Students identify an appeal, slap the Greek label on it, and consider the analysis finished. The evaluation question isn't what kind of appeal it is — it's whether it works. Is the emotional appeal manipulative or earned? Is the logical appeal built on a warrant the audience would actually accept? Watch for students who treat labeling as critique and move on without ever judging effectiveness.

misconceptions.title: A Strong Conclusion Summarizes the Argument
misconceptions.body: Students assume restating the thesis is what conclusions are supposed to do. That describes a weak conclusion. A convincing conclusion synthesizes, escalates the stakes, answers the "so what," or delivers a call to action that grows naturally from the argument. Teach them to ask: what does this conclusion do that the introduction couldn't have done?

misconceptions.title: The Call to Action Is Always at the End
misconceptions.body: Students search the final paragraph for the call to action and stop looking. Many argumentative texts embed calls to action mid-argument — in an emotional appeal that nudges readers toward a behavior, or in a rhetorical question that positions a particular choice as obvious. Watch for students who miss embedded calls to action and then report that the text "doesn't have one."

misconceptions.title: Structural Evaluation Means Counting Paragraphs
misconceptions.body: When asked to evaluate argumentative structure, students count the number of counterargument paragraphs or note that the author "uses three pieces of evidence." That is inventory, not evaluation. Teach them to ask: does the structure serve the argument? Would reordering any section strengthen or weaken it? Does the evidence appear where the audience most needs to be convinced?

assignments.name: Thesis Stress Test
assignments.body: Students find the thesis of an op-ed or speech and write a one-paragraph rebuttal from the position of the strongest possible opponent. If they can't construct a real rebuttal, they argue the thesis isn't arguable enough — then revise it so it is.

assignments.name: Structure Diagram + Verdict
assignments.body: Students map the full structural skeleton of an argumentative text — thesis, evidence, appeals, counterargument, conclusion, call to action — on a single page, then write a one-paragraph verdict: what structural choice most strengthens the argument, and what most weakens it?

assignments.name: Side-by-Side Appeals Audit
assignments.body: Students compare how two different argumentative texts on the same issue use appeals. For each appeal, they rate effectiveness on a 1–4 scale and justify the score in a sentence. Final product: a comparison chart plus a short written claim about which author's appeal strategy is more honest and why.

assignments.name: Conclusion Reconstruction
assignments.body: Give students a strong argumentative text with the conclusion removed. They write their own conclusion, then read the original. They compare: what did the author do that they didn't? What did they do better? What does the difference reveal about what a convincing conclusion actually requires?

assignments.name: Critique Letter
assignments.body: Students write a formal critique letter addressed to the author of an assigned argumentative text. The letter must address thesis arguability, effectiveness of at least two appeals, structural coherence, and the conclusion's success or failure — with specific textual evidence for each judgment.

mastery: Identifies the thesis and articulates whether it is arguable — not just present — and traces how that quality (or lack of it) affects the rest of the argument.
Evaluates appeals by effectiveness and appropriateness, not just by type, naming specific textual moments and explaining why each appeal does or does not serve the argument.

Judges structural choices — ordering of evidence, placement of counterargument, position of the call to action — and explains how the arrangement strengthens or compromises the argument's persuasive force.
Assesses the conclusion's success at synthesizing, escalating stakes, or delivering a call to action, distinguishing between conclusions that merely restate and those that close the argument.
Produces written critique with specific textual evidence, a clear evaluative claim, and reasoning that applies consistent criteria across multiple structural elements.
notes: Vertical jump from English III: English III 7E(i) asks students to analyze argumentative structure and elements; English IV upgrades the verb to *critique and evaluate* — the move is from identifying what is present to rendering a judgment on whether it works and why. By senior year, the reader is positioned as a peer critic, not a decoder.

The examples in the TEA expectation — clear arguable thesis, appeals, structure of the argument, convincing conclusion, and call to action — are not a checklist. Students should evaluate how these elements interact: a strong call to action built on a weak thesis is still a structural failure.

STAAR analog: this TEK maps directly to STAAR's argumentative reading passages. Released items consistently test thesis arguability, appeal function, and structural choices using the exact stems modeled above.

DOK 4 justification: evaluating structural effectiveness requires applying multiple criteria simultaneously, defending judgments with evidence, and extending reasoning to explain how one element's weakness cascades through the full argument.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Critique Argumentative Texts: Evidence and Counterargument
expectation: Critique and evaluate characteristics and structural elements of argumentative texts, including various types of evidence and treatment of counterarguments, including concessions and rebuttals.
overview: English IV students are expected to do more than identify whether an author uses counterargument — they must evaluate how well the author handles it. The real test is whether students can distinguish a genuine concession from a rhetorical gesture, and a solid rebuttal from one that sidesteps rather than answers the opposing claim.
studentFriendly: You can read an argument, judge whether the evidence is actually doing the job the author claims it's doing, and evaluate whether the author took the opposing view seriously or just performed the appearance of fairness.
questionStems: The author's use of [type of evidence] in paragraph X MOST likely strengthens the argument by —
Which sentence from the selection BEST represents the author's concession to the opposing view?
How does the rebuttal in paragraphs X–Y affect the overall credibility of the author's argument?
The author's treatment of the counterargument in paragraph X suggests that the author MOST likely believes —
Which of the following BEST describes a weakness in the author's use of evidence in paragraph X?
The author includes the counterargument in paragraph X MOST likely in order to —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which statement BEST evaluates the effectiveness of the author's rebuttal?
The evidence presented in paragraphs X–Z MOST nearly supports the conclusion that —
With which statement would a critic of the author's argument MOST likely agree?
How does the author's concession in paragraph X contribute to the development of the central argument?

misconceptions.title: Spotting the Counterargument Is the Finish Line
misconceptions.body: Students locate the counterargument paragraph, label it, and consider the analysis complete. Push them past identification: the real question is whether the counterargument the author chose to address is the STRONGEST opposing view or a convenient strawman. Teach them to ask, "Is this the best objection, or the easiest one to knock down?"

misconceptions.title: Concession Equals Weakness
misconceptions.body: Students read any concession as the author admitting defeat. Reframe it immediately: a well-placed concession is a credibility move, not a retreat. It signals that the author has engaged with the full picture. The failure mode is a fake concession — one that acknowledges the other side in language so vague it doesn't cost the author anything. Teach students to grade the concession: does it actually give something up?

misconceptions.title: All Evidence Is Equal
misconceptions.body: Students treat a personal anecdote and a peer-reviewed study as interchangeable because both are "evidence." The critique requires them to distinguish types — anecdotal, statistical, testimonial, experimental, analogical — and evaluate whether the TYPE of evidence matches the CLAIM it's supposed to support. A statistic proving correlation doesn't prove causation; a compelling story doesn't prove a trend.

misconceptions.title: A Rebuttal Is Just Disagreeing More Loudly
misconceptions.body: Students think a rebuttal means restating the original claim with more emphasis. Show them what a structural rebuttal actually does: it identifies the logic or evidence the counterargument depends on and shows why that logic or evidence fails. A rebuttal that just says "but we still disagree" hasn't rebutted anything — it's restated. Watch for students who describe a rebuttal as effective simply because the tone is confident.

misconceptions.title: The Argument Either "Works" or "Doesn't Work"
misconceptions.body: Students collapse evaluation into a binary: the argument is good or bad. Push them toward gradient critique — the evidence in section two is strong, the rebuttal in section three dodges the real issue, the concession is genuine but the author over-concedes and doesn't recover. Mastery looks like a student who can say the argument is compelling overall AND name the specific place where it loses ground.

assignments.name: Counterargument Audit
assignments.body: Students read an argumentative essay and identify every place the opposing view appears. For each instance they classify it: is this a genuine counterargument, a strawman, a concession, or a rebuttal? They write one paragraph evaluating whether the author chose the strongest version of the opposing view.

assignments.name: Evidence Type Inventory
assignments.body: Students catalog every piece of evidence in a complex argument, label its type, and evaluate whether the type matches the claim. They flag any mismatch — where the author uses emotional evidence to support a causal claim, for example — and write a two-sentence critique of the choice.

assignments.name: Rebuttal Reconstruction
assignments.body: Students identify a rebuttal they judge to be weak, write a stronger version of the counterargument the author failed to fully answer, then draft the rebuttal the original text should have made. Debrief focuses on what made the original rebuttal insufficient.

assignments.name: Side-by-Side Concessions
assignments.body: Give students two argumentative texts on the same topic — one that concedes strategically and one that avoids the opposing view entirely. They write a comparative analysis of which argument is more credible and why, citing specific sentences.

assignments.name: Editorial Critique
assignments.body: Students write a 1-page critique of a published op-ed, evaluating its evidence and counterargument treatment. The critique must name what the author did well AND what failed, with textual evidence for both judgments.

mastery: Distinguishes between a genuine concession that gives something up and a hollow acknowledgment that costs the author nothing.
Evaluates whether the counterargument the author chose to address is the strongest available objection or a convenient alternative.
Identifies the type of evidence used for each claim and explains whether the match between evidence type and claim is logically sound.
Describes what a rebuttal does structurally — targeting the logic or evidence the counterargument depends on — rather than just noting that disagreement is expressed.
Produces gradient critique: identifies the strongest and weakest elements of an argument's evidence and counterargument handling within a single evaluative response.
notes: Vertical alignment: English III 7E(ii) asks students to analyze evidence and counterargument treatment; English IV ramps the verb to *critique and evaluate* — students must now render a judgment on quality, not just describe the structure. The move is from "what is the author doing?" to "how well is the author doing it, and where does it fail?"

The counterargument/concession/rebuttal cluster is one of the most heavily tested argumentative reading skills on STAAR in upper grades. Stems should probe whether students can evaluate effectiveness, not just locate features.

DOK 4 justification: extended evaluation against multiple criteria — evidence type, counterargument selection, rebuttal logic, concession authenticity — requires synthesis across the whole text and defense of a reasoned judgment.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Critique and Evaluate Argumentative Texts: Identifiable Audience
expectation: Critique and evaluate characteristics and structural elements of argumentative texts such as identifiable audience or reader.
overview: This is the capstone version of audience analysis — English IV students are not simply identifying who the text is written for, they are evaluating how deliberately and skillfully the writer has constructed their appeal toward that audience. The question is no longer "who is this for?" but "does the author actually know their audience, and how can you prove it?"
studentFriendly: You can read an argumentative text and evaluate how precisely the author has identified their audience — spotting every word choice, assumption, and structural decision that reveals exactly who the writer was speaking to and whether that targeting worked.
questionStems: Based on paragraphs X–Y, the author MOST likely intended this argument for an audience that —
Which detail from the selection BEST supports the inference that the author assumed the reader already believed —
The author's use of [term/phrase] in paragraph X suggests the intended audience is MOST likely —
Which sentence from the selection MOST clearly reveals an assumption the author makes about the reader's values?
How does the author's choice to address [counterargument/concern] in paragraph X contribute to the argument's effectiveness for its intended audience?
The author MOST likely includes the example in paragraph X in order to —
Based on the selection, with which statement about the intended audience would the author MOST likely agree?
Which pair of details from the selection BEST illustrates how the author tailors the argument to a specific reader?
The shift in the author's tone between paragraphs X and Y MOST likely reflects the author's awareness that the audience —
A reader unfamiliar with [assumption embedded in the text] would MOST likely conclude that the argument —

misconceptions.title: "Intended Audience" Means Whoever Could Read This
misconceptions.body: Students name the broadest possible audience — "adults," "Americans," "people who care about this issue" — and call it analysis. Push them toward specificity: What does this author assume the reader already knows? Already believes? Already cares about? The identifiable audience is constructed by the text's embedded assumptions, not by the topic. If a student can't name at least one assumption the author makes about their reader, they haven't found the audience yet.

misconceptions.title: Acknowledging the Audience Is the Same as Reaching Them
misconceptions.body: Students conflate a writer's intention with the argument's effectiveness. A writer can clearly target an audience and still fail — using jargon that alienates, making assumptions the reader rejects, or building credibility the wrong way for that crowd. Teach them that critique requires evaluating execution, not just intention: did the author's choices actually work for that audience, or did the targeting misfire?

misconceptions.title: Appeals Are Only in the Obvious Emotional Moments
misconceptions.body: Students scan for pathos in the dramatic sentences and ignore the quieter audience-targeting moves: the vocabulary level, the examples chosen, the arguments the author doesn't bother making because the audience already agrees. Teach them to read the silences — what did the author not explain, and what does that tell you about who they expected to be reading?

misconceptions.title: Critique Means Finding Fault
misconceptions.body: In English IV, students often write one-sided evaluations — either "this argument perfectly addressed its audience" or "the author totally failed." Real critique holds complexity. An argument can be skillfully targeted in one dimension and tone-deaf in another. Teach them to write evaluations that acknowledge both: where the author read their audience precisely, and where the author misjudged or underestimated them.

misconceptions.title: Audience Analysis Is Separate from Structural Analysis
misconceptions.body: Students treat audience and structure as two different checklists — they note the audience in one paragraph and then forget it when analyzing claims, evidence, and counterarguments. Push them to see audience as the organizing principle of every structural choice: the order of claims, what evidence gets foregrounded, which objections get addressed first. Teach them that every structural decision is an audience decision.

assignments.name: Audience Autopsy
assignments.body: Students select a published argumentative text (op-ed, policy brief, open letter) and produce a written critique identifying the specific assumed audience — naming at least three embedded assumptions — and evaluating how successfully each structural element serves that audience. Length: 2 pages, evidence-cited.

assignments.name: Same Argument, Different Audiences
assignments.body: Give students one argumentative claim and have them write two 1-paragraph versions targeting opposite audiences. Then swap and critique a partner's pair: where did the targeting work, and where did it break down? Discussion follows.

assignments.name: Misfire Identification
assignments.body: Provide a published argument that demonstrably misjudged its audience (a failed op-ed, a rejected proposal with context). Students identify specifically where and why the targeting failed, supported by textual evidence.

assignments.name: Cross-Text Audience Comparison
assignments.body: Students read two argumentative texts on the same issue addressed to different audiences. They write a comparative analysis evaluating how each author's structural choices reflect their respective audience — and which argument is better crafted for its intended reader.

mastery: Identifies the implied audience with specificity — names embedded assumptions, not just a demographic category.
