Distinguishes between the author's apparent intention and the argument's actual effectiveness for the identified audience.
Evaluates structural choices (claim order, evidence selection, counterargument placement) as audience-driven decisions.
Produces written critique that holds complexity — naming both successful and unsuccessful audience-targeting moves within the same text.
Supports every evaluative claim with specific textual evidence, not general impressions.
notes: Vertical alignment: English III (7)(E)(iii) asks students to critique and evaluate argumentative texts for identifiable audience at the analysis level; English IV raises the expectation to sustained evaluative critique — students must not only identify the audience but assess the precision and effectiveness of every authorial decision made in service of that audience. The skill ramps from "Can you find the audience?" to "Can you prove the argument earns or loses its audience, and why?"

DOK 4 justification: students must extend beyond the text to evaluate effectiveness against criteria (the identified audience's assumed knowledge, values, and needs), defend judgments with evidence, and hold competing interpretations simultaneously.

STAAR note: this TEK is tested through extended argumentative reading passages in which students must infer the intended audience from structural and lexical evidence rather than from explicit authorial statements. Stems emphasizing inference ("MOST likely intended for," "suggests the intended audience") reflect the assessment's expectation.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Critique and Evaluate Multimodal and Digital Texts
expectation: Critique and evaluate the effectiveness of characteristics of multimodal and digital texts.
overview: This is the capstone move for digital reading: students don't just navigate multimodal texts — they judge how well the design decisions actually work. The question shifts from "what does this text do?" to "does it do it well, and how do you know?"
studentFriendly: You can look at a website, documentary, infographic, or interactive article and explain specifically what's working, what isn't, and why the design choices either strengthen or undermine the message.
questionStems: The author's decision to include the infographic in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Based on the visual and written elements in the selection, which statement BEST describes how the two modes work together to develop the central argument?
Which detail from the digital text BEST supports the conclusion that the author intended to persuade rather than inform?
The use of hyperlinks within the article MOST likely affects the reader's experience by —
Read paragraphs X–Y. How does the placement of the video clip contribute to the development of the author's argument?
Which characteristic of the digital text MOST effectively establishes the author's credibility with the intended audience?
The author's choice to use a data visualization in paragraph X rather than written statistics MOST likely —
Based on the selection, which statement BEST evaluates the effectiveness of the text's multimodal design in achieving its purpose?
Which sentence from the selection BEST explains why the interactive element in section X strengthens the author's claim?
The shift from written text to visual elements in paragraphs X–Y MOST likely affects the reader by —

misconceptions.title: More Modes Automatically Means More Effective
misconceptions.body: Students assume that a text packed with images, video, audio, and animation is more effective than a simpler one — because complexity signals effort. Teach them that effectiveness is always measured against purpose and audience: a stunning visual that distracts from a complex argument is a failure, not a feature. Push them to ask whether each mode earns its place.

misconceptions.title: Critique Means Finding the Flaw
misconceptions.body: Students treat critique as a fault-finding mission. They scan for what's wrong and stop there, ignoring the harder judgment: which design choices worked, which didn't, and whether the text succeeds overall despite its weaknesses. Teach them that a one-sided critique — all praise or all attack — is not a critique at all. The evaluative move requires holding both.

misconceptions.title: Visual Elements Are Decoration
misconceptions.body: Students routinely treat images, charts, and video as optional extras bolted onto the "real" text. They read the prose, skip the visual, and miss the argument. Reframe it: in a multimodal text, the visual IS a paragraph. Teach them that when the author chose to show rather than tell, that was a rhetorical decision — and it deserves the same scrutiny as any sentence.

misconceptions.title: Digital Means Credible
misconceptions.body: Students extend automatic trust to polished digital texts — professional design cues trigger credibility assumptions whether or not the content deserves them. They need to learn that production quality and argumentative quality are independent variables. A beautifully designed site can carry a deeply flawed argument; a plain one can carry a rigorous one. Teach them to separate the wrapper from the contents.

misconceptions.title: Effectiveness Is Subjective, So Any Answer Works
misconceptions.body: Because aesthetic judgment feels personal, students retreat to "it depends on the reader" and refuse to commit to a specific evaluation. Push back hard: effectiveness can be argued with evidence. Show them that strong critique names specific design choices, ties each to a stated or implied purpose, and defends the judgment with concrete textual support. Vagueness isn't openness — it's avoidance.

assignments.name: Multimodal Autopsy
assignments.body: Students select a digital text (an interactive news feature, a data journalism piece, a documentary clip with accompanying article) and produce a structured critique: one section each on written content, visual/graphic elements, and the interaction between modes. Each section must name at least one specific effectiveness strength and one weakness, with evidence.

assignments.name: Redesign Proposal
assignments.body: After critiquing a multimodal text, students write a one-page memo proposing one specific design change — a different visual, a restructured layout, a removed element — and justify how the change would improve effectiveness. The constraint: they must work within the original text's purpose and audience.

assignments.name: Side-by-Side Evaluation
assignments.body: Students compare two digital texts on the same topic (e.g., two news organizations' coverage of the same event, one data-heavy and one image-heavy). They evaluate which multimodal approach is more effective for the stated purpose and audience, citing specific design characteristics from each.

assignments.name: Critique the Class
assignments.body: Students create a short multimodal text (slide deck, infographic, or multimedia post) and then exchange with a partner for written critique using a shared rubric focused on effectiveness criteria. Students respond in writing to the critique they receive — agreeing, disagreeing, or qualifying the judgment with evidence.

mastery: Identifies specific multimodal characteristics — layout, image selection, data visualization, embedded media, interactivity — and explains the rhetorical function of each, not just its presence.
Evaluates effectiveness in relation to the text's stated or implied purpose and intended audience, not against personal preference.
Holds positive and negative evaluative judgments simultaneously, producing critique that is neither flattering nor dismissive.
Distinguishes between production quality and argumentative quality, and refuses to conflate polished design with sound reasoning.
Supports evaluative claims with specific, quoted or described evidence from both the written and visual/digital elements of the text.
notes: Vertical ramp: English III (7F) asks students to analyze characteristics of multimodal and digital texts; English IV adds the evaluative layer — critique and effectiveness judgment. The conceptual shift is from description to verdict, requiring students to apply and defend criteria rather than simply identify features.

DOK 4 justification: sustained evaluation against multiple criteria (purpose, audience, design logic, mode interaction) with extended reasoning and evidence required to defend judgments — this is not a task completable at the recall or skill level.

STAAR analog note: 7F is not directly tested as a standalone oral or digital performance item on STAAR, but it maps onto Author's Purpose and Craft questions (Standard 8) that ask students to evaluate how textual and visual choices serve the author's purpose. The question stems above reflect this STAAR framing.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Evaluate the Author's Purpose, Audience, and Message
expectation: Evaluate the author's purpose, audience, and message within a text.
overview: By English IV, this is a capstone skill — students are not identifying purpose, they are evaluating it. The question is no longer "what does the author want?" but "did the author achieve it, for whom, and at what cost to the message?"
studentFriendly: You can read any text and judge whether the author's purpose fits the audience they chose, whether the message actually lands, and what gets gained or lost in the choices they made.
questionStems: The author MOST likely wrote this text in order to —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the author's message is BEST described as —
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the inference that the author intended the text for an audience of —
How does the author's use of [technique/device] in paragraph X contribute to the author's purpose?
The author's choice to include [detail/example] in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
With which statement about the author's message would a reader of this text MOST likely agree?
Based on the selection, the MOST likely reason the author addresses [counterargument/concession] in paragraph X is to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST reveals a shift in the author's purpose?
The author's use of [tone word/phrase] in paragraph X suggests that the intended audience is MOST likely —
How does the author's purpose in paragraphs X–Y affect the development of the central message?

misconceptions.title: Purpose Is Just "To Persuade, Inform, or Entertain"
misconceptions.body: Students treat PIE as a final answer rather than a starting point. They label the text "persuasive" and consider the job done, without examining what specific claim is being argued, for whom, and whether the rhetorical moves actually deliver it. Push them to write purpose statements that name the author, the specific claim or goal, the intended audience, and the strategy — not a single word pulled from a three-item list.

misconceptions.title: Audience Is Whoever Published It
misconceptions.body: Students conflate the publication venue with the intended audience. A text published in a mainstream newspaper is not necessarily written "for everyone" — the syntax, assumed knowledge, and cultural references all encode a specific reader. Teach them to read the text's assumptions backward: what does the author expect you to already know, believe, or care about? That's where the audience lives.

misconceptions.title: The Author's Message = The Author's Main Idea
misconceptions.body: Students collapse message into main idea and produce summaries dressed up as analysis. Message in this context carries evaluative weight — it's what the author wants the reader to walk away thinking, feeling, or doing, which is not always the same as the literal subject. A text about bees might have a message about collective action. Teach them to ask: what does the author want this to mean beyond what it says?

misconceptions.title: If the Purpose Seems Obvious, There's Nothing to Evaluate
misconceptions.body: When the text is openly argumentative, students assume the evaluation is easy — "the author wants to persuade, and they do." But evaluation means assessing the gap between intent and execution: Did the evidence match the claim? Did the tone alienate the stated audience? Did the author's framing obscure the message for some readers? Watch for students who praise every purposeful text as "effective" without naming what would make it MORE effective or where it falls short.

misconceptions.title: Evaluating Purpose Means Agreeing or Disagreeing with the Argument
misconceptions.body: Students confuse evaluating rhetorical effectiveness with approving or disapproving the author's position. They'll say "the author is wrong" when the task is to say whether the author is convincing — and these are completely different judgments. Reframe it this way: you can think the author is wrong AND find the argument well-constructed, or agree with the position AND find the execution weak. Separating those two moves is where real evaluation lives.

assignments.name: Purpose Audit
assignments.body: Students select a text (editorial, essay, speech, advertisement) and write a structured evaluation: one paragraph on intended audience, one on stated versus implied purpose, one on message, and one verdict — did the text succeed on its own terms? Evidence required throughout.

assignments.name: Same Message, Different Audiences
assignments.body: Give students two texts making similar arguments to different audiences (e.g., a scientific paper and a magazine feature on the same topic). Students evaluate how each author shapes purpose and message for their reader and which is more effective, and for whom.

assignments.name: Author's Chair Reverse Engineering
assignments.body: Students draft a short text with a specific audience and purpose in mind, then swap with a peer who writes an evaluation of the author's purpose, audience, and message — without being told the original intent. Debrief: where did the author's purpose come through? Where did it get lost?

assignments.name: Comparative Evaluation
assignments.body: Students compare two texts with similar purposes (e.g., two speeches arguing for the same cause) and evaluate which author's message is more clearly aligned with the intended audience. They must cite specific textual evidence for their judgment.

mastery: Articulates the author's purpose in specific terms — naming the claim, the intended audience, and the rhetorical strategy — rather than defaulting to generic PIE labels.
Identifies the intended audience from textual evidence (assumed knowledge, tone, syntax, cultural references) rather than from the publication venue alone.
Distinguishes between a text's main idea and its message, explaining what the author wants the text to mean beyond its literal subject.
Evaluates the effectiveness of the author's choices in serving the stated purpose, naming where they succeed and where they fall short.
Separates rhetorical effectiveness from personal agreement, assessing the text's construction independently from the reader's own position on the topic.
notes: Vertical ramp from English III: 110.38(8)(A) asks students to analyze the author's purpose, audience, and message. English IV elevates the verb to *evaluate* — students must now render a judgment on whether purpose is achieved and at what cost, not just identify what the purpose is. The standard completes its arc here; this is the most rigorous form of the skill in the vertical sequence.

DOK 3 justification: evaluation against multiple criteria (purpose, audience, message alignment) requires strategic thinking and reasoning across the full text; some tasks will reach DOK 4 when students must defend cross-textual judgments with extended argument.

STAAR analog: this TEK is heavily tested in STAAR's author's purpose and craft section. Stems are drawn from STAAR's standard phrasing for purpose-and-message questions across literary and informational passages.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Evaluate Use of Text Structure to Achieve the Author's Purpose
expectation: Evaluate use of text structure to achieve the author's purpose.
overview: This is the capstone move in structural analysis: not just naming the structure a writer chose, but judging whether it actually worked. By English IV, students should be asking whether the architecture of a text is serving or sabotaging the argument — and they need to be able to make that case with evidence.
studentFriendly: You can look at how a text is put together — what comes first, how sections connect, what's buried or foregrounded — and judge whether those structural choices actually helped the writer accomplish what they set out to do.
questionStems: How does the author's use of [structural feature — e.g., flashback, problem-solution, parallel sections] in paragraphs X–Y MOST likely contribute to the overall purpose of the text?
Which statement BEST evaluates how the author's decision to open with [anecdote/statistic/counterargument] supports the text's central argument?
The author places the concession in paragraph X rather than at the end of the text. This structural choice MOST likely —
Based on the overall structure of the selection, what is the MOST likely reason the author withholds [information] until paragraph X?
How does the shift in structure between paragraphs X and Y affect the reader's understanding of the author's purpose?
Which sentence from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that the author's organizational choice strengthens rather than weakens the argument?
The author uses a [compare-contrast / cause-effect / chronological] structure in paragraphs X–Y. How does this structure MOST likely serve the author's purpose?
With which statement about the effectiveness of the text's structure would the author MOST likely agree?
Based on the text, which structural choice MOST contributes to the author's ability to persuade the reader?
A student argues that placing [section/paragraph] earlier in the text would have made the argument more effective. Which evidence from the selection BEST supports or refutes this claim?

misconceptions.title: Naming the Structure IS the Analysis
misconceptions.body: Students identify the structure — "it's cause and effect" — and stop there, as if the label were the insight. Push them further: Does the cause-and-effect structure actually illuminate something, or does it oversimplify the relationship? The evaluation is in the judgment, not the taxonomy. Teach them that naming is the floor, not the ceiling.

misconceptions.title: The Author Always Got It Right
misconceptions.body: Students default to defending whatever structure the author used, treating publication as proof of quality. Show them texts where the structure is genuinely a liability — a chronological argument that buries the stakes, a problem-solution frame that never adequately defines the problem. Evaluation means the verdict could go either way. If students can't articulate a structural weakness, they're not evaluating — they're summarizing with extra steps.

misconceptions.title: Purpose Is Always "To Persuade" or "To Inform"
misconceptions.body: Students flatten purpose into a binary and then evaluate structure against it. Teach them that a writer's purpose is often multiple and layered — to persuade, yes, but also to unsettle, to reframe, to honor, to indict. A structure that serves the surface purpose might undercut the deeper one. Push them to name the specific purpose before they evaluate whether the structure serves it.

misconceptions.title: Structural Evaluation Ignores What's Missing
misconceptions.body: Students evaluate the structure they see without asking about the structure that's absent. What was left out? What would a different arrangement have foregrounded? Where did the author avoid a move they could have made — and why? Teach them that absence is also a structural choice. The most interesting evaluations often hinge on what the text refuses to do.

misconceptions.title: Text Structure Means Headings and Sections
misconceptions.body: Students think text structure lives at the level of visible formatting — headers, bullet points, labeled sections. Push them to see micro-structure: the decision to put the thesis in the second paragraph instead of the first, the choice to end a section on a question rather than a claim, the repetition of a phrase as structural scaffolding rather than accident. Structure operates at every level of the text, not just the outline.

assignments.name: Structural Verdict Essay
assignments.body: Students select a challenging nonfiction text and write a two-page evaluation arguing whether the structure advances or undermines the author's stated and unstated purposes. Claim must be explicit; evidence must reference specific structural moves (not just content).

assignments.name: Restructure and Defend
assignments.body: Students take a text they've already read and reorder three significant sections. They write a short memo explaining what their restructuring gains and what it sacrifices — and why the original author may have chosen differently.

assignments.name: Two Structures, One Argument
assignments.body: Give students a strong thesis and two different structural plans (e.g., chronological vs. problem-solution). They write the same argument both ways, then evaluate which structure better serves the purpose and why. Debrief as a class.

assignments.name: Published Critic vs. Student Evaluation
assignments.body: Pair a text with a published review that comments on the text's structure. Students write their own structural evaluation independently, then compare with the critic's. Where do they agree? Where do they diverge — and whose evaluation is better supported?

mastery: Identifies the structural choices a writer made (sequence, framing, section order, omissions) and articulates the purpose those choices were serving.
Produces a genuine verdict — structure is effective, partially effective, or counterproductive — with textual evidence anchoring each claim.
Distinguishes between the author's surface purpose and deeper purpose, evaluating structure against both where relevant.
Considers what the structure omits or deemphasizes, not just what it includes.
Uses evaluative vocabulary precisely: "this choice advances," "this choice undermines," "this arrangement prioritizes X at the cost of Y."
notes: Vertical ramp: English III 8B asks students to analyze how text structure contributes to meaning — the analytical move. English IV escalates to evaluation: Does it work? Is it the right structure for the purpose? This is a judgment call, not a description.
DOK 4 justification: Evaluation against self-generated criteria, extended reasoning required to defend the verdict, and the correct answer is not retrievable — it must be constructed from the text.

Because this is Author's Purpose and Craft (Standard 8), the evaluation must stay tethered to evidence about purpose, not just personal preference about structure. That's a distinction worth teaching explicitly.
STAAR tests this strand heavily through literary and informational analysis passages; stem 10 above mirrors the "refute or support" style appearing in recent released tests.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Evaluate the Author's Use of Print and Graphic Features
expectation: Evaluate the author's use of print and graphic features to achieve specific purposes.
overview: This is the culminating move for visual-textual analysis — seniors are not just identifying features but judging whether they work. The TEK asks students to hold the author accountable: was the pull quote earned? Does the infographic clarify or oversimplify? Does the font choice undercut the argument's seriousness? This is craft evaluation, not scavenger hunting.
studentFriendly: You can look at any designed text — a magazine spread, a research report, a political pamphlet — and judge whether the author's visual and typographic choices actually do what the author intended, or whether they backfire.
questionStems: The author's use of the sidebar in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Which graphic feature in the selection BEST supports the author's central argument?
Based on the infographic, what can the reader MOST likely conclude about the author's purpose?
How does the author's use of bold text in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the reader's understanding of —
The author includes the photograph MOST likely to —
Which statement BEST describes how the heading structure in the selection helps the reader?
The author's choice to present data as a bar graph rather than in paragraph form is MOST likely intended to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST explains the purpose of the pull quote in paragraph X?
The author's use of [graphic feature] in the selection is LEAST effective at achieving its purpose because —
Based on the selection, what is the MOST likely effect of the author's use of white space and subheadings on the reader?

misconceptions.title: Naming the Feature IS the Analysis
misconceptions.body: Students will write "The author uses a graph" and stop there, as if identifying the feature fulfills the task. Push them to the next clause: "The author uses a graph to compress five years of enrollment data into a single scannable image — but the truncated y-axis makes a modest trend look dramatic." Teach them the move is always: feature → purpose → judgment of effectiveness. Anything shorter is description, not evaluation.

misconceptions.title: Graphic Features Are Decoration, Not Argument
misconceptions.body: Students read text and treat images, callout boxes, and charts as visual filler inserted by a designer, not as argumentative choices made by the author. Disabuse them of this — in professional nonfiction, every feature is a rhetorical decision. A photograph does rhetorical work; a table of statistics does rhetorical work. Watch for students who annotate graphic features as "adds interest" without asking what claim or impression the feature is advancing.

misconceptions.title: If It Looks Professional, It Must Be Effective
misconceptions.body: Seniors are susceptible to production-value bias: polished graphics get high marks in their heads automatically. Teach them that a sophisticated-looking infographic can still misrepresent data, omit context, or create false equivalence. The evaluation has to include whether the feature achieves its purpose accurately and fairly, not just whether it looks impressive.

misconceptions.title: Print Features Only Matter for Struggling Readers
misconceptions.body: Students have absorbed the message that headers, bold text, and sidebars exist to help "bad readers" find information. In AP-level and college-level texts, print features are part of the rhetorical design — a strategically bold term frames what's important; a deliberately absent subheading forces the reader to sit with ambiguity. Teach them to read print features as authorial moves, not accessibility scaffolds.

misconceptions.title: "Specific Purposes" Means "The Thesis"
misconceptions.body: Students collapse "specific purposes" into a single global aim — "to persuade" or "to inform" — and then say every feature serves that one purpose. The richer evaluation locates the local purpose of each feature: this graph exists to make the scale of the problem visceral; this pull quote exists to humanize a statistic; this font choice exists to signal authority. Push them toward granular, feature-level purpose claims before the overall evaluation.

assignments.name: Feature Audit with Verdict
assignments.body: Students select a long-form journalistic piece with multiple print and graphic features (The Atlantic, Scientific American, a government report). For each feature, they complete a three-column log: (1) feature and location, (2) apparent purpose, (3) verdict — effective, ineffective, or mixed, with one sentence of reasoning. Final step: a paragraph ranking the three most impactful features and defending the ranking.

assignments.name: Redesign Proposal
assignments.body: Students identify one graphic or print feature in a text they believe fails its purpose. They write a one-page proposal explaining why it fails and what a more effective design choice would be, with a rationale grounded in the author's evident goals. This forces evaluation AND synthesis.

assignments.name: Same Data, Two Designs
assignments.body: Present students with the same factual information formatted two ways (e.g., a data table vs. a narrative paragraph, or two differently designed infographics). Students evaluate which design better serves a given purpose and argue their position in a structured comparison. Surfaces how design is never neutral.

assignments.name: Cross-Text Feature Comparison
assignments.body: Students read two texts on the same topic — one that uses graphic and print features extensively, one that does not. They evaluate whether the featured text achieves its purpose more effectively than the plain one, and under what circumstances the plainer format might actually be stronger.

mastery: Identifies specific print and graphic features and articulates the local purpose each is designed to serve — not just the global thesis of the text.
Produces a judgment about effectiveness that includes both what the feature accomplishes and where it falls short or misfires.
Distinguishes between a feature that is visually effective and one that is rhetorically effective — recognizes that production quality does not equal argumentative success.
Supports evaluative claims with textual and visual evidence, not impressions ("it looks good" or "it's confusing").
Considers the intended audience when evaluating whether a feature achieves its purpose — a feature that works for a general audience may be reductive for a specialist one.
notes: Vertical ramp from English III: 110.38(8)(C) asks students to "analyze" the use of print and graphic features; English IV upgrades the verb to "evaluate" — the shift from noticing how features work to judging whether they work effectively is the capstone move for this strand.
DOK 4 justification: evaluation against multiple criteria (purpose, audience, accuracy, rhetorical fit) with extended reasoning required to defend the judgment; no single correct answer.

STAAR analog note: this TEK maps most directly to informational reading passages that include embedded graphics, charts, sidebars, or visual data. STAAR tests this through questions about author's purpose for including a specific feature, the effect of a feature on the reader, and comparative judgments between graphic and verbal presentation of the same information.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Critique and Evaluate How an Author's Language Shapes Reader Perception
expectation: Critique and evaluate how the author's use of language informs and shapes the perception of readers.
overview: This is the capstone craft standard for English IV — students aren't just identifying what an author does with language, they're evaluating whether it works and critiquing the shape it gives reality. The target is the relationship between linguistic choice and constructed perception: whose version of events does the language make feel inevitable, natural, or true?
studentFriendly: You can read any text — argument, fiction, journalism, policy — and explain precisely how the author's word choices, syntax, and framing are building a version of reality in the reader's mind, and whether that construction holds up under scrutiny.
questionStems: The author's use of [word/phrase] in paragraph X MOST likely shapes the reader's perception by —
Which sentence from the selection BEST demonstrates how the author's language influences the reader's understanding of [topic/character/event]?
How does the author's diction in paragraphs X–Y contribute to the reader's perception of [subject]?
The author's use of [figurative language/syntax/tone] in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the author's language choices create the impression that —
The author MOST likely uses the phrase [phrase] in paragraph X to persuade the reader that —
Which statement BEST describes the effect of the author's language on the reader's perception of [character/event/argument]?
How does the shift in the author's diction between paragraphs X and Y affect the reader's understanding?
The author's syntax in paragraph X MOST nearly suggests that —
With which statement about the author's use of language would a critical reader MOST likely agree?

misconceptions.title: "Shaping Perception" Means the Author Is Lying
misconceptions.body: Students conflate constructed perspective with intentional deception and conclude that any authorial shaping is manipulation to be exposed and dismissed. Teach them that all language constructs — the question is not whether the author shapes perception but HOW, to WHAT end, and with what degree of transparency. Push them to evaluate the construction without collapsing into blanket skepticism or naïve trust.

misconceptions.title: Evaluation Means Grading the Author's Choices
misconceptions.body: Students write evaluations that read like a report card — "The author did a good job using vivid language." Real evaluation explains the consequence: what does the language make possible for a reader to think, feel, or believe that they couldn't without it? Teach them to measure effect, not effort. "Good job" tells us nothing; "this framing makes the reader assume X without being told X" tells us something.

misconceptions.title: Only Obvious Figurative Language Shapes Perception
misconceptions.body: Students go hunting for metaphors and similes and miss the real action: the passive constructions that hide agents, the nominalization that turns a process into a thing, the "neutral" vocabulary that smuggles assumptions. Watch for students who annotate every simile and leave the argumentative syntax unmarked. The most powerful perception-shaping is often the most grammatically unremarkable.

misconceptions.title: Critiquing Craft Requires Disagreeing with the Author
misconceptions.body: Students assume critique means finding fault, so when they admire a text, they write praise instead of analysis. Teach them that critique is a sustained examination of HOW the language works — admiration and critique aren't opposites. The sharpest critique of a text you love is harder and more valuable than a dismissal of one you hate.

misconceptions.title: The Author's Intention Is What Matters Most
misconceptions.body: Students anchor their evaluations to assumed authorial intent — "the author wanted the reader to feel X" — and stop there. Redirect them: the job is to evaluate the EFFECT of the language on actual readers, including effects the author may not have intended. An author can intend empathy and produce condescension; the language is the evidence, not the biography.

assignments.name: Perception Audit
assignments.body: Students select two sources covering the same event — one editorial, one news report, one literary nonfiction piece, any combination. They write a comparative critique identifying how each author's language constructs a different version of the same reality, with textual evidence from both. Length: 2–3 pages.

assignments.name: Rewrite to Reshape
assignments.body: Students take a passage of 150–200 words and rewrite it to produce the opposite perception of its subject without changing the core facts. Then they write a paragraph explaining exactly which language choices did the work and why. This forces precision about the relationship between word choice and reader experience.

assignments.name: Single-Text Deep Dive
assignments.body: Students choose a challenging nonfiction text (long-form journalism, a Supreme Court opinion excerpt, a canonical essay). They annotate every language choice that shapes perception, classify the technique, and write a critique evaluating the overall perceptual frame the language builds. Class discussion follows on where readers agree and diverge.

assignments.name: The Invisible Frame
assignments.body: Students find a text that presents itself as neutral or objective (encyclopedia entry, textbook passage, news wire story). Their task is to expose the perceptual frame the "neutral" language still constructs — whose assumptions are baked in, whose reality is centered, and what a reader must already believe for the framing to feel invisible.

mastery: Identifies specific language choices — diction, syntax, figurative language, framing devices — and traces their effect on reader perception with textual evidence, not generalization.
Evaluates whether the author's language achieves its apparent purpose, with reasoning grounded in effect rather than assumed intent.
Sustains critique without collapsing into either dismissal or uncritical praise — names both what the language accomplishes and where it strains or misleads.
Recognizes perception-shaping in "neutral" or unmarked language, not only in obviously rhetorical passages.
Produces written critique that is structured, evidence-specific, and accountable to a reader who might disagree.
notes: Vertical ramp: English III 8(D) asks students to "evaluate how the author's use of language informs and shapes perception" — English IV adds *critique* as a distinct cognitive operation layered on top of evaluation. The ramp is from measuring effect to rendering a defensible judgment about that effect, including its ethical and rhetorical implications.

DOK 4 justification: students must apply analytical criteria across an extended text, construct a reasoned judgment, and defend it against alternative readings — all of which require extended reasoning rather than retrieval or application of a single procedure.

This TEK sits at the intersection of Author's Purpose and Craft and Response Skills; strong English IV instruction treats 8(D) and 5(H) as companion standards — evaluating language use is incomplete without the synthesis move.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Evaluate the Use of Literary Devices to Achieve Specific Purposes
expectation: Evaluate the use of literary devices such as paradox, satire, and allegory to achieve specific purposes.
overview: This is the capstone literary-craft TEK — not identification, not explanation, but evaluation. Students must judge whether paradox, satire, or allegory actually does what the author needed it to do, and make an argument for that judgment. The work lives at the intersection of close reading and rhetorical thinking.
studentFriendly: You can read a piece of literature and judge whether the author's choice of paradox, satire, or allegory was the RIGHT tool for what they were trying to accomplish — and you can explain why, with evidence.
questionStems: The author's use of allegory throughout the selection MOST likely serves to —
Read paragraph X. The paradox in this passage contributes to the author's purpose by —
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates the author's use of satire to critique —
How does the allegorical structure of the selection develop the author's central argument?
The author's use of paradox in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Based on the selection, which statement BEST evaluates the effectiveness of the satire in achieving the author's purpose?
The literary device used in paragraph X MOST nearly functions as —
Which detail from the selection BEST supports the conclusion that the allegory is meant to represent —
How does the paradox introduced in paragraph X affect the development of the author's theme?
The satire in paragraphs X–Y is MOST effective in conveying —

misconceptions.title: Identifying the Device IS the Analysis
misconceptions.body: Students write "This is an example of satire" and stop — as if naming the device is the same as evaluating it. Teach them that identification is the first five seconds of the work. The real question is always: does this device accomplish what the author needed? Push them to argue effectiveness, not just presence.

misconceptions.title: Satire Means "Funny"
misconceptions.body: Students conflate satire with comedy and assume any satirical text must be humorous. Watch for students who call dark or biting satire "not really satire" because it doesn't make them laugh. Reframe: satire is a weapon aimed at a target — it can be vicious, despairing, or deadpan. The humor, when present, is the delivery mechanism, not the definition.

misconceptions.title: Allegory Is a One-to-One Decoder Ring
misconceptions.body: Students treat allegory like a substitution cipher — this character equals that historical figure, this event equals that political moment, done. Push them to ask what the allegorical frame allows the author to do that direct statement couldn't. The value of allegory is in the distance it creates, not the correspondence it establishes.

misconceptions.title: A Paradox Is Just a Contradiction
misconceptions.body: Students read paradox as logical error — the author said two things that can't both be true, so something is wrong. Teach them that paradox is a deliberate collision designed to produce a third idea that neither statement alone generates. The apparent contradiction IS the point; dismissing it as confusion means missing the argument entirely.

misconceptions.title: Evaluation Means Deciding Whether You Liked It
misconceptions.body: Students collapse "evaluate the literary device" into "say whether the book was good." They'll write "I thought the allegory was effective because I enjoyed reading it." Redirect them hard: evaluation requires a standard — what was the author trying to do, does the device accomplish that, what's the evidence? Personal enjoyment is not a criterion; purposeful execution is.

assignments.name: Effectiveness Verdict
assignments.body: Students select one literary device from an assigned text (paradox, satire, or allegory) and write a 1-page evaluative argument: what purpose does it serve, and does it succeed? They must cite at least three specific moments from the text and apply a clear standard of effectiveness — not personal preference.

assignments.name: Device Swap
assignments.body: Students take a passage that uses allegory and rewrite it as direct argument — then explain what is gained or lost. Or take a satirical passage and flatten it into earnest prose. The comparison surfaces what the original device was actually doing that literal language could not.

assignments.name: Satire Autopsy
assignments.body: Students read two satirical texts on the same target (e.g., two pieces criticizing the same institution or era) and evaluate which satire is more effective and why. They must compare craft choices — not just which argument they agree with.

assignments.name: Paradox Gallery
assignments.body: Students collect five paradoxes from across the semester's reading and rank them from least to most effective, with written justification for each ranking. Discussion follows: where did rankings diverge, and what standards were students applying?

mastery: Distinguishes between identifying a literary device and evaluating its effectiveness — consistently produces the second move, not just the first.
Applies an external standard to evaluation: identifies the author's purpose first, then judges whether the device serves it, with evidence.
Explains what paradox, satire, or allegory makes possible that direct statement could not — articulates the value of the form.
Engages with texts whose satirical or allegorical targets they find uncomfortable without letting political agreement drive their craft evaluation.
Produces written evaluations with specific textual evidence, a clear criterion for effectiveness, and a defensible judgment.
notes: Vertical alignment: English III's version of this TEK targets recognition and explanation of literary devices. English IV adds the evaluative layer — students must now judge whether the device achieves its purpose, not simply name or describe it. This is the final destination in a multi-year progression from identification to analysis to evaluation.

DOK 3 justification: evaluation against an authorial purpose requires strategic thinking and extended reasoning — students must construct a standard, apply it, and defend a judgment with evidence. Borders DOK 4 when students produce original evaluative arguments in extended writing.

STAAR note: while paradox, satire, and allegory are tested through prose analysis on STAAR, the evaluation frame is more commonly assessed in written responses and open-ended items than in standalone multiple-choice stems. The stems above mirror how STAAR frames craft questions in the reading comprehension sections.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Evaluate How Diction and Syntax Contribute to a Text's Effectiveness
expectation: Evaluate how the author's diction and syntax contribute to the effectiveness of a text.
overview: This is the capstone craft expectation for English IV — students don't just identify or explain word choice and sentence structure, they render a judgment about whether those choices actually work. The standard demands both precision (what exactly did the author do?) and evaluation (did it pay off?).
studentFriendly: You can read any text — a novel, an essay, a speech — and make a defensible argument about whether the author's specific word choices and sentence structures made the writing stronger or weaker, with evidence to back you up.
questionStems: The author's use of the word "[word]" in paragraph X MOST likely contributes to the effectiveness of the text by —
How does the syntax in paragraphs X–Y BEST develop the author's central argument?
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates how the author's diction shapes the reader's perception of [subject]?
The author's repeated use of [word/phrase] in paragraphs X and Y is MOST likely intended to —
Based on paragraph X, what is the MOST likely effect of the author's sentence structure on the reader?
Which statement BEST evaluates how the author's diction in paragraph X contributes to the overall effectiveness of the text?
The shift in the author's syntax between paragraphs X and Y MOST nearly suggests —
The author's use of [short/fragmented/parallel] sentence structure in paragraph X is MOST effective in conveying —
Which of the following BEST explains how the author's word choice in paragraph X supports the text's purpose?
Based on the selection as a whole, the author's diction and syntax work together MOST effectively to —

misconceptions.title: Evaluation Means Praise
misconceptions.body: Students read "evaluate" as "say nice things about." They write that the author's diction is "vivid" and "powerful" and call it done. Push them harder: evaluation requires a claim with a because — the diction is effective (or not) because it does X, and here is the textual evidence. If a student can't articulate what specifically would have been lost with different word choices, they haven't evaluated; they've complimented.

misconceptions.title: Diction and Syntax Are Two Separate Checkboxes
misconceptions.body: Students treat diction and syntax as two parallel items on a rubric — one paragraph each, nothing to do with each other. Teach them to look at the intersection: how does a string of short, punchy sentences amplify the weight of a single latinate word dropped at the end? Diction and syntax create meaning together; the richest analysis is always at the seam.

misconceptions.title: Longer Sentences Signal Sophistication; Shorter Sentences Signal Weakness
misconceptions.body: Students assign default value — complex syntax is "better writing." Watch for students who praise every long, winding sentence and dismiss fragments as mistakes. Teach them that effectiveness is always relative to purpose: a single-word sentence after three compound-complex ones can land harder than anything in the paragraph. Ask: did this choice serve the text's goal, whatever that goal is?

misconceptions.title: Loaded Words Are Easy to Spot
misconceptions.body: Students look for the obvious culprits — slurs, superlatives, extreme adjectives — and miss the quiet machinery of diction: words like "merely," "still," "simply," "naturally," "even." These low-profile words do enormous rhetorical work precisely because they slip under the radar. Teach students to slow down for the words that seem neutral; those are often the most deliberate choices in the text.

misconceptions.title: A "Good" Evaluation Ends with Agreeing the Text Worked
misconceptions.body: Students default to concluding that yes, the text is effective, because the author published it and published things must be good. The harder — and more intellectually honest — move is to find the seam where a diction or syntax choice misfires: where the author overreached, where the register shifted awkwardly, where a construction muddied instead of clarified. English IV students should be writing evaluations that include at least one dissent.

assignments.name: Effectiveness Verdict
assignments.body: Students read two passages from the same author — one from early in their career, one late. They write a one-page evaluation arguing which passage's diction and syntax are more effective and why, using specific textual evidence from both.

assignments.name: The Rewrite Test
assignments.body: Students take one paragraph from a published text and rewrite it with deliberately altered diction and syntax (simpler, more abstract, passive constructions instead of active, etc.). They then write a short analysis explaining what was lost — and what, if anything, was gained.

assignments.name: Annotation + Argument
assignments.body: Students annotate a full-page passage marking every syntactic and lexical choice doing identifiable work, then write a thesis-driven paragraph evaluating the text's overall effectiveness based on those annotations. Graded for specificity and the quality of the evaluative claim, not just identification.

assignments.name: The Dissent Paper
assignments.body: Students identify one well-regarded text and argue that a specific diction or syntax pattern undermines its effectiveness. They must acknowledge the counterargument (why readers typically see it as working) before dismantling it. Length: 1–2 pages.

mastery: Names specific diction and syntax choices and connects each to a demonstrable effect on the reader, not just a feature of the text.
Produces a defensible evaluative claim — effective or not, and why — rather than summarizing or describing what the author did.
Examines the interaction between diction and syntax rather than treating them as independent checklists.
Identifies moments where a craft choice misfires or underperforms, not only moments where it succeeds.
Grounds every evaluative judgment in textual evidence specific enough that a different passage could not have supplied it.
notes: Vertical ramp from English III: 110.38(8)(F) asks students to analyze how diction and syntax contribute to a text's effectiveness; English IV lifts the verb to evaluate — students must now render and defend a judgment, not merely describe the contribution. The craft knowledge is the same; the cognitive demand shifts from analysis to evaluation with argumentation.
DOK 4 justification: extended reasoning is required to defend an evaluative claim about craft across an entire text, weighing multiple choices against a self-constructed standard of effectiveness.

For STAAR alignment: this TEK maps directly to the Author's Purpose and Craft strand on STAAR EOC assessments; stems testing "most contributes to," "most likely intended to," and "best evaluates" phrasing are the dominant surface forms.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Analyze the Effects of Rhetorical Devices and Logical Fallacies
expectation: Analyze the effects of rhetorical devices and logical fallacies on the way the text is read and understood.
overview: English IV is the terminal course — this is the last time a teacher gets to ask students to account for HOW a text moves them before they walk into college, careers, and a world that will try to move them constantly. The skill here is effect-focused: not just naming the device, but tracing the mechanism by which it works or breaks down.
studentFriendly: You can read a speech, an essay, or an argument and explain not just what rhetorical moves the writer is making, but exactly how those moves — or their logical failures — shape the way a reader receives and trusts the message.
questionStems: The author's use of anaphora in paragraphs X–Y MOST likely affects the reader by —
Which sentence from the selection contains a logical fallacy that MOST undermines the author's argument?
The rhetorical question in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Read this passage from paragraph X. The author's appeal to authority in this passage MOST likely causes the reader to —
Which of the following BEST describes the effect of the either-or fallacy in paragraph X on the author's overall argument?
The author uses a series of rhetorical devices in paragraphs X–Y. Which device MOST contributes to the author's persuasive purpose?
Based on paragraph X, which statement BEST describes how the slippery slope argument affects the reader's understanding of the author's claim?
The author's use of loaded language in paragraph X MOST nearly causes the reader to —
Which sentence from the selection BEST illustrates the author using an ad hominem attack to redirect attention from a weakness in the argument?
Read paragraphs X and Y. How does the straw man fallacy in paragraph X affect the way the reader understands the counterargument presented in paragraph Y?

misconceptions.title: Naming the Device Is the Whole Job
misconceptions.body: Students list rhetorical devices like a scavenger hunt — "there's an anaphora, there's a metaphor" — and stop. The TEK is asking for effects: what does the device DO to the reader's experience, their trust, their emotional state, their sense of the argument's validity? Teach them to always follow the device name with a "so that" clause. If they can't complete it, they haven't analyzed anything.

misconceptions.title: Logical Fallacies Always Destroy an Argument
misconceptions.body: Students assume that finding a fallacy means the entire argument collapses. Push them deeper: fallacies often work — they persuade real audiences, sometimes enormously well. The analytical move is to explain HOW the fallacy shapes the reader's perception, not just declare it invalid. A slippery slope that frightens people into agreement is doing something worth studying, not just something worth rejecting.

misconceptions.title: Rhetorical Devices and Logical Fallacies Are Separate Categories
misconceptions.body: Students treat rhetorical devices as the "craft" list and fallacies as the "logic" list and never connect them. The richer reading notices that some devices function as fallacies in context — loaded language, appeals to false authority, emotionally manipulative analogies. Watch for students who treat them as two separate checklists rather than a single question: what is this text doing to the reader's reasoning?

misconceptions.title: "Effect" Means Emotional Effect Only
misconceptions.body: Students conflate rhetorical effect with emotional impact. They'll write "this makes the reader feel angry" and stop. The full picture includes cognitive effects — how the device shapes what the reader assumes, concludes, or overlooks. Teach them to ask: what does this make the reader SKIP? What does it prevent them from noticing? That's where the real analysis lives.

misconceptions.title: Fallacy Detection Equals Critical Thinking
misconceptions.body: Students think spotting a fallacy makes them a critical thinker. What's harder is explaining why the fallacy is still persuasive to the intended audience and what assumptions that audience must be holding for the fallacy to land. In English IV, the meta-move is understanding not just the text's logic but the reader the text is written for.

assignments.name: Effect Chain Analysis
assignments.body: Students select one rhetorical device and one logical fallacy from a mentor text. For each, they write a paragraph tracing the full effect chain: what the device does on the surface, what it triggers in the reader's reasoning or emotion, and how it either strengthens or undermines the author's credibility.

assignments.name: The Persuasive Autopsy
assignments.body: Students take a publicly circulated argument (op-ed, political advertisement, viral essay) and produce a close reading that identifies three rhetorical devices and two logical fallacies, explaining the mechanism of each. The final paragraph must address: given all these devices and fallacies, is the argument persuasive? To whom? Why?

assignments.name: Counter-Annotation
assignments.body: Students annotate a paired set of texts arguing opposite sides of the same issue. For each text, they mark rhetorical devices in one color and logical fallacies in another. Discussion: which text relies more heavily on fallacies? Does that make it less effective with its intended audience?

assignments.name: Build and Break
assignments.body: Students write a 300-word persuasive paragraph deliberately using two rhetorical devices and one logical fallacy. They then write a 200-word analysis of their own paragraph, explaining the intended effect of each move and acknowledging where the fallacy risks losing a critical reader.

mastery: Identifies specific rhetorical devices and logical fallacies by name and locates them in the text with precision.
Explains the effect of each device or fallacy on the reader's reasoning, trust, or emotional state — not just its presence or label.
Distinguishes between a fallacy that weakens an argument logically and one that nonetheless succeeds rhetorically with its target audience.
Connects rhetorical craft and logical structure in a unified reading, rather than treating devices and fallacies as separate inventories.
Produces written analysis that is claim-driven, evidence-anchored, and effect-focused — not a list of definitions applied to a text.
notes: Vertical ramp from English III: 110.38(8)(G) asks students to "analyze rhetorical devices and logical fallacies" — the same catalog but without the explicit emphasis on reading effects. English IV adds the phrase "on the way the text is read and understood," which shifts the standard from identification toward reception analysis. The student isn't just reading the text; they're reading the reader the text constructs.

DOK 4 justification: the effects-focused framing requires extended reasoning across multiple criteria — students must connect textual evidence to cognitive and emotional effects, evaluate whether fallacies succeed or fail with target audiences, and synthesize craft analysis into a unified claim. That's sustained, multi-directional thinking, not retrieval or skill application.

STAAR alignment: 8G is directly tested in the Author's Purpose and Craft reporting category. Released items frequently pair a rhetorical question or anaphora with an effect-focused distractor set, or embed a fallacy in an argumentative passage and ask how it affects the reader's understanding of the claim. The stems above model these patterns closely.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Plan a Piece of Writing for Various Purposes and Audiences
expectation: Plan a piece of writing appropriate for various purposes and audiences by generating ideas through a range of strategies such as brainstorming, journaling, reading, or discussing.
overview: This TEK is about what happens before the draft — the deliberate, strategic work of matching a writing purpose to an audience and using real generative strategies to get ideas worth developing. By English IV, planning should not be a worksheet students fill out to satisfy the teacher; it should be the actual thinking that determines whether the writing works.
studentFriendly: You can choose and use a planning strategy — brainstorming, journaling, reading around a topic, talking it through — to generate ideas that actually fit your purpose and the reader you're writing for.
questionStems: Based on the student's planning notes in paragraph X, which audience would BEST match the writer's stated purpose?
Which revision to the writer's plan in paragraph X would MOST likely strengthen the connection between purpose and audience?
The writer's use of [strategy] during the planning stage MOST likely helps the writer to —
Which sentence from the writer's journal entry BEST indicates the central idea the writer is developing?
Which planning strategy would MOST likely help a writer who is generating ideas for a persuasive piece aimed at a skeptical audience?
The writer's brainstorming notes in paragraph X suggest that the MOST likely intended audience for the draft is —
Based on the planning excerpt, which of the following claims is BEST supported by the writer's generated ideas?
Which revision to the planning notes in paragraph X would MOST likely result in a more focused draft?
The writer includes [detail/idea] in the planning stage in order to —
Which sentence from the planning document MOST clearly reflects the writer's awareness of audience expectations?

misconceptions.title: Planning Is Something You Do Once, at the Beginning
misconceptions.body: Students treat planning as a box to check before writing starts, then abandon it the moment they open a draft. Teach them that planning is recursive — a writer returns to it when a draft stalls, when the audience shifts, or when the purpose clarifies mid-draft. Watch for students who submit planning documents that bear zero resemblance to their final piece; that's not evidence that planning failed, it's evidence that planning was never actually used.

misconceptions.title: Brainstorming Means Writing Down Everything Indiscriminately
misconceptions.body: Students interpret brainstorming as a permission slip to be unfocused. They produce lists of loosely connected fragments and call it done. Push them to understand that brainstorming has a phase two: evaluation. You generate freely, then you interrogate what you've got — which ideas serve the purpose? which ones assume the wrong audience? Quantity without selection isn't planning; it's procrastination that looks like work.

misconceptions.title: "Purpose" Means the Assignment Prompt
misconceptions.body: By English IV, students still tend to define purpose as "what the teacher asked for." That's not purpose — that's a task. Purpose is the writer's own answer to: what do I want this piece to do to a reader? Push them deeper: to move, to persuade, to expose, to complicate. A student who can only answer "to complete the assignment" hasn't yet taken ownership of the writing.

misconceptions.title: Audience Is Whoever Reads It
misconceptions.body: Students name "a general reader" or "my teacher" as their audience without interrogating what that means for every decision they're about to make. Reframe audience as a set of assumptions: what does this person already know? what do they want from this kind of text? what will make them stop reading? A vague audience produces vague writing. Make them name a real person or a specific reader type and write the draft AT them.

misconceptions.title: Discussing Counts as Talking, Not Planning
misconceptions.body: When students are asked to discuss ideas as a generative strategy, they have conversations and then sit down to write as if the discussion never happened. They don't capture, they don't synthesize, they don't use the friction of another person's pushback. Teach them to treat discussion as a drafting tool: take notes in real time, mark the moments someone challenged their idea, and build the places of resistance into the plan. Talk that leaves no trace isn't a planning strategy.

assignments.name: Audience Portrait
assignments.body: Before drafting, students write a half-page description of their intended reader — not a demographic sketch, but a portrait: what this reader believes, what they distrust, what they need to hear in order to take the piece seriously. Students attach this portrait to every draft they submit so the teacher can assess whether the draft actually reflects the portrait.

assignments.name: Planning Strategy Comparison
assignments.body: Students plan the same piece twice using two different strategies (e.g., brainstorming and journaling). They write one paragraph comparing what each strategy surfaced and explaining which generated more usable material — and why.

assignments.name: Purpose Statement Before Every Draft
assignments.body: Students must submit a one-sentence purpose statement in the form "I want this piece to make my reader [verb] [what]" before any draft is accepted. The statement cannot be "to inform" or "to persuade" alone — it must name the specific effect. Teachers return purpose statements that are too vague before the draft is read.

assignments.name: Discussion-to-Draft Bridge
assignments.body: Students discuss a writing prompt in pairs for ten minutes while one partner takes notes. The note-taker then writes a planning outline using the discussion, including at least two moments where the other person pushed back. The outline must show how those moments of resistance shaped the plan.

assignments.name: Planning Audit
assignments.body: After a draft is returned, students compare their original planning document side by side with the draft and annotate where the plan held, where it broke down, and what caused the drift. This makes planning recursive rather than disposable.

mastery: Selects a planning strategy appropriate to the purpose and audience — not just the strategy they prefer or the one the teacher modeled most recently.
Produces planning materials that show genuine idea generation AND evaluation — not just a list, but evidence that ideas were tested against purpose and audience.
Writes a specific, defensible purpose statement that names what the piece should do to the reader, not just what topic it covers.
Identifies a specific audience and makes planning decisions — what to include, what to assume, what to explain — that reflect that audience's actual expectations and needs.
Returns to the plan during drafting when the piece stalls, rather than abandoning both the plan and the process.
notes: Vertical alignment: English III 9A carries the same planning expectation. The English IV ramp is contextual, not mechanical — students are applying planning strategies to more ambitious, self-directed writing tasks (research, extended argument, literary analysis) where the stakes for poor planning are higher and the recovery cost is steeper. The scaffold is gone; the expectation is internalized process.

DOK 2 justification: the core skill is applying a strategy to a purposeful task. The cognitive demand is real but sits at application — selecting a strategy, generating ideas, fitting them to purpose and audience — rather than extended analysis or evaluation of the plan's effectiveness (which would push to DOK 3).

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog in isolation — STAAR tests writing planning implicitly through revision items and through the writer's planning notes when provided. Question Stems above are modeled on STAAR's revision-task framing, where students evaluate planning documents, journal excerpts, and brainstorming notes as if they were passages.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Develop Drafts Using Strategic Organizational Structures
expectation: Develop drafts into a focused, structured, and coherent piece of writing in timed and open-ended situations by using strategic organizational structures appropriate to purpose, audience, topic, and context.
overview: This is the capstone drafting expectation — English IV students aren't just organizing paragraphs, they're choosing architecture. The "strategic" in the standard is the whole game: can a student look at a prompt, read the rhetorical situation, and select a structure that actually serves the purpose rather than defaulting to five-paragraph habit?
studentFriendly: You can read a writing prompt, identify what the audience needs and what the purpose requires, and choose an organizational structure that makes your argument or story land — not just the structure you always use.
questionStems: Which organizational structure BEST supports the writer's purpose in this draft?
The writer MOST likely chose to introduce the counterargument in paragraph three in order to —
Based on paragraphs two and four, the writer's organizational strategy is MOST nearly —
Which revision to the structure of paragraph five would MOST improve the coherence of the draft?
The writer's decision to open with a narrative anecdote before stating the central claim MOST likely serves to —
Which sentence from the draft BEST signals a shift in the writer's organizational pattern?
How does the structure of paragraphs three through five contribute to the development of the writer's argument?
The writer's arrangement of evidence in paragraph four suggests that the intended audience is MOST likely —
Which of the following organizational changes would MOST strengthen the draft's focus?
The writer MOST likely delayed the thesis until paragraph two in order to —

misconceptions.title: "Strategic" Means "Complicated"
misconceptions.body: Students hear "strategic organizational structures" and assume they need to do something elaborate — nested arguments, alternating perspectives, unconventional openings. Teach them that strategic means appropriate, not impressive. A clear chronological structure IS strategic when the purpose is narrative. A simple problem-solution structure IS strategic when the audience is unfamiliar with the issue. Watch for drafts that sacrifice coherence for perceived sophistication.

misconceptions.title: The Five-Paragraph Essay Is Always Safe
misconceptions.body: English IV students often treat the five-paragraph template as the universal default, especially under timed conditions. They produce technically competent structures that don't serve the actual prompt. Push them to notice when the five-paragraph shape actively works against the writing — when it forces artificial splitting of connected ideas, or when it signals that the student didn't actually read the rhetorical situation. Safe and effective are not the same thing.

misconceptions.title: Organization Is Something You Do After the Draft
misconceptions.body: Students often treat structure as a revision move — write it out, then reorganize. Teach them that structural decisions made before or during the first draft produce better writing than structural decisions made to rescue a finished draft. The outlining, even a loose one, is where the "strategic" actually happens. A writer who plans structure is a different writer than one who discovers it at the end.

misconceptions.title: Audience Is Decoration, Not Architecture
misconceptions.body: Students acknowledge audience in their introductions ("As a concerned citizen, you may wonder...") and then forget it entirely. The structure of a piece should be shaped by what the audience already knows, what they resist, and what they need to encounter first. Teach them: if you're writing for a skeptical audience, you can't bury the concession. If you're writing for a general audience, you can't open with jargon. Audience isn't a sentence — it's a blueprint constraint.

misconceptions.title: Timed Writing Gets a Structure Pass
misconceptions.body: Students believe timed conditions excuse weak organization — they'll write linearly and call it "what I had time for." This is the wrong adaptation. Teach them that timed writing rewards pre-planned structures even more than open-ended drafting does, because there's no time to reorganize mid-draft. The two minutes spent deciding on structure before writing saves ten minutes of revision chaos. Strong timed writers are strong structural thinkers first.

assignments.name: Structure Autopsy
assignments.body: Students receive three short student drafts responding to the same prompt — each organized differently (chronological, problem-solution, compare-contrast). They identify the organizational pattern in each, evaluate how well it fits the purpose and audience, and write a one-paragraph justification for which structure is most effective and why. Not which draft is best written — which structure is most strategic.

assignments.name: Blueprint Before Draft
assignments.body: For a given timed-writing prompt, students spend the first five minutes producing only a structural blueprint: a labeled skeleton of what each paragraph will do, not say. No sentences until the blueprint is approved or self-evaluated. Then they draft. Afterward, they reflect in writing on whether the structure held, where it drifted, and why.

assignments.name: Audience Swap
assignments.body: Students write the same argument for two different audiences — say, a school board and a student editorial audience — and must use a different organizational structure for each. They submit both drafts and a brief note explaining what changed structurally and why audience drove the change.

assignments.name: Structural Revision Only
assignments.body: Students take a completed draft and revise ONLY the organization — no new content, no sentence-level editing. They reorder, split, merge, or relocate paragraphs and submit a before/after with a paragraph explaining each structural move and its rationale.

mastery: Selects an organizational structure before or during early drafting based on an explicit reading of the prompt's purpose, audience, and context — not habit.
Produces drafts in which the structure is visible and consistent: a reader can identify the organizational pattern without the student explaining it.
Adjusts organizational structure between timed and open-ended writing tasks — timed drafts show evidence of quick structural planning; open-ended drafts show evidence of structural revision.
Articulates why a specific structure serves a specific rhetorical situation, rather than describing what the structure is.
Maintains coherence across the full draft so that structural choices made in the opening are honored through the conclusion.
notes: Vertical ramp from English III: English III 9(B)(i) carries the same expectation language. English IV is distinguished by increased rhetorical sophistication of the writing contexts — senior-level prompts require more complex structural decisions (argument with nuanced counterargument, multi-source synthesis, extended literary analysis) and the timed-writing expectation ramps in stakes, especially given college-readiness alignment.

DOK 3 justification: students must make and defend strategic decisions across a whole-text structure, requiring planning and strategic thinking beyond recall or skill application alone; DOK 4 enters when rubric-based evaluation of structural effectiveness is added.

This sub-expectation (B)(i) isolates organizational structure; (B)(ii) addresses transitions and syntax for coherence — the two work together but should be taught as distinct moves before being combined.

STAAR analog: this TEK is assessed through the Expository and Argumentative STAAR writing tasks; organizational coherence is scored on the STAAR Writing rubric under Organization/Progression. Stems above mirror the analytical lens STAAR applies when asking students to revise a peer draft for structural effectiveness.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: English IV Drafts — Develop Engaging Ideas with Rhetorical Depth
expectation: Develop drafts into a focused, structured, and coherent piece of writing in timed and open-ended situations by developing an engaging idea reflecting depth of thought with effective use of rhetorical devices, details, examples, and commentary.
overview: This is the intellectual engine of the drafting standard — not mechanics, not structure, but whether the student actually has something worth saying and can deploy rhetorical craft to say it. At the English IV level, "developing an idea" means sustained depth: every claim earns commentary, every example is leveraged, and rhetorical devices do work beyond decoration.
studentFriendly: You can take a focused idea and develop it with real depth — using specific details, well-chosen examples, and commentary that explains why it all matters, along with rhetorical moves that give your writing genuine energy and persuasive force.
questionStems: Which sentence in paragraph X BEST develops the writer's central idea through specific commentary?
The detail in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
How does the rhetorical device in paragraph X contribute to the development of the writer's argument?
Which sentence from the draft BEST demonstrates depth of thought rather than surface-level observation?
The writer's use of [device/example] in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Based on paragraphs X and Y, which statement BEST describes how the writer develops the central idea?
Which revision would MOST strengthen the development of the idea in paragraph X?
The commentary following the example in paragraph X MOST nearly functions to —
Which sentence from the draft provides the MOST focused support for the writer's controlling idea?
The writer MOST likely includes the detail in lines X–Y in order to —

misconceptions.title: Examples Are the Argument
misconceptions.body: Students pile on examples as if accumulation equals development. Three examples with no commentary is not depth — it's a list. Teach them the ratio: for every example offered, the writer owes the reader at least as much commentary explaining what the example proves, why it matters, and what it adds to the overall claim. Watch for drafts that end paragraphs on the example itself rather than on the writer's thinking.

misconceptions.title: Rhetorical Devices Are Decorative
misconceptions.body: Students treat rhetorical devices as garnish — they add an anaphora or a rhetorical question at the beginning because it "sounds good" and move on. Push them to ask what the device is doing rhetorically: Is it building emphasis? Implicating the reader? Creating a contrast that sharpens the claim? If the device could be removed with no loss to the argument, it wasn't working.

misconceptions.title: Depth Means Long
misconceptions.body: Students equate word count with intellectual seriousness. A paragraph that restates a general claim in six different phrasings is not deep — it's padded. Teach them to measure depth by how far an idea has traveled from its first statement by the end of the paragraph: what does the reader now understand that they didn't understand before the paragraph opened?

misconceptions.title: "Engaging" Means Informal
misconceptions.body: When students hear that writing should be engaging, they think it means casual, funny, or conversational. Teach them that engagement is a function of specificity and surprise, not register. The most engaging sentence in a formal essay is often the most precise one — the concrete detail that lands differently than the reader expected.

misconceptions.title: Commentary Means Restating the Example
misconceptions.body: Students follow an example with a sentence that essentially repeats what the example already showed. "This quote proves that the character is loyal" after quoting an act of loyalty is not commentary — it's echo. Teach them that commentary must move the argument forward: What does this example reveal that isn't already visible in the text? What does it tell us about the larger claim that we couldn't see without this specific example?
