assignments.name: The Commentary Ratio Check
assignments.body: Students submit a body paragraph draft. They then color-code it: claim in one color, evidence/example in a second, and commentary in a third. If commentary is the shortest color, they revise until commentary equals or exceeds evidence. Discussion follows on what "commentary" is actually doing.

assignments.name: Device Audit
assignments.body: Students identify every rhetorical device in their current draft and write one sentence per device explaining exactly what rhetorical work it is doing — not what it is, but what it accomplishes. Devices that cannot be explained in that sentence get cut or revised.

assignments.name: Depth Progression Map
assignments.body: Students write a short analytical trace of one paragraph: sentence by sentence, they explain how the idea moved. Where did it stay in place? Where did it advance? They then revise the places where the idea stalled.

assignments.name: The "So What?" Conference
assignments.body: Pairs read each other's drafts and mark every moment where they want to ask "So what?" The writer must respond to each mark in writing before the next draft. Teaches students to hear the reader's demand for commentary in real time.

assignments.name: Timed Depth Practice
assignments.body: Students write a focused body paragraph in 15 minutes on a given claim, then again in 25 minutes on a harder claim with a required rhetorical device. Compare across the two: where did time pressure flatten the thinking, and where did constraint actually sharpen it?

mastery: Develops a controlling idea across a full draft that remains focused and deepens rather than merely repeating itself.
Follows every example or detail with commentary that explicitly connects the evidence to the larger argument.
Deploys at least one rhetorical device per major section and can explain its rhetorical function if asked.
Produces writing in timed conditions that still demonstrates specific detail and purposeful commentary, not just narrative summary.
Revises for depth by adding commentary and analytical moves, not by adding additional examples.
notes: Vertical ramp: English III 9(B)(ii) asks students to develop engaging ideas with rhetorical devices, details, examples, and commentary — functionally identical wording. The English IV expectation is the same standard at the terminal level, which means the bar is not the presence of commentary but its quality and consistency across a sustained, complex piece. Evaluators should hold English IV writers to a stricter standard of genuine intellectual movement within paragraphs.

DOK 3 justification: students must apply knowledge of rhetorical craft to construct original arguments, not merely identify devices in others' writing. The commentary requirement demands strategic reasoning about how evidence functions, not recall or reproduction.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is assessed through the STAAR Writing task (not a multiple-choice passage question), so the question stems above are written in the style STAAR would use if evaluating a student-produced text excerpt or revision task — the closest analogs are revising/editing items and short-answer prompts on the writing composition component.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Revise Drafts to Improve Clarity, Development, Organization, Style, Diction, and Sentence Fluency
expectation: Revise drafts to improve clarity, development, organization, style, diction, and sentence fluency, both within and between sentences.
overview: Revision in English IV is not cleanup — it is the primary act of a mature writer. At this level, students should be reworking drafts across all six dimensions simultaneously, understanding that a change in diction ripples into sentence fluency, that reorganizing a body paragraph changes what development even means. The teacher's job is to get them out of proofreading mode and into the harder, slower work of reconceiving.
studentFriendly: You can look at your own draft the way an editor would — seeing not just what it says but what it could say — and reshape it at every level, from individual word choice to the logic of how the whole piece is organized.
questionStems: Which revision of the underlined sentence in paragraph X BEST improves the clarity of the writer's central claim?
The writer wants to add a sentence to paragraph X to improve development. Which sentence BEST accomplishes this goal?
Read the following sentence from paragraph X. Which revision MOST effectively improves the sentence's fluency while preserving the writer's meaning?
The writer is considering removing the sentence in paragraph X that begins "[sentence]." Should the writer keep or remove this sentence, and why?
Which of the following revisions to the underlined phrase in paragraph X MOST effectively improves the writer's diction?
The writer wants to reorganize paragraphs X and Y. Which sequence BEST improves the logical development of the essay?
Read paragraphs X and Y. Which sentence, if added between them, MOST effectively improves the transition and organization of the essay?
Which revision of the underlined sentence BEST improves sentence fluency without changing the writer's intended meaning?
The writer's essay lacks development in paragraph X. Which addition BEST supports the argument made in that paragraph?
Which revision to the concluding paragraph MOST effectively improves both the style and the clarity of the essay's final point?

misconceptions.title: Revision Means Fixing Mistakes
misconceptions.body: Students treat revision as error correction — they scan for comma splices and misspelled words and call it done. Watch for drafts that are technically clean but structurally unchanged from the first draft. Teach them that revision means re-seeing: if the paper looks the same after a "revision," they edited, they didn't revise. Push them to show you a before-and-after where actual structural or conceptual changes occurred.

misconceptions.title: All Six Revision Targets Are Separate Checklists
misconceptions.body: Students work through clarity, development, organization, style, diction, and sentence fluency as six independent passes — checking boxes linearly. The reality is that these dimensions are entangled. Teach them: if you swap a vague noun for a precise one, you've just changed diction AND clarity AND possibly sentence fluency all at once. The checklist is a scaffold, not the finish line.

misconceptions.title: "Development" Means Adding More Words
misconceptions.body: When students see underdeveloped paragraphs, their instinct is to add sentences — any sentences — until the paragraph feels longer. Teach them that development means deepening the reasoning, not stretching it. Watch for students who add a second example that says the same thing as the first, or who pad with summary when the reader needed analysis. More is not more; more-specific is more.

misconceptions.title: Style Is a Bonus, Not a Responsibility
misconceptions.body: Students treat style as decoration — something to add after the argument is "done." At English IV level, style IS part of the argument. A passive construction doesn't just sound weak; it obscures agency in ways that can undermine the claim. A sentence that buries its main verb in a subordinate clause isn't stylistically awkward — it's rhetorically unclear. Teach them that style choices are always choices about meaning.

misconceptions.title: Strong Writing Doesn't Need Much Revision
misconceptions.body: Students who produced a polished first draft assume revision is for weaker writers. Reframe this completely: professional writers revise more, not less, precisely because they can see the gap between what the draft says and what the idea demands. The skill ceiling on revision is unlimited. Push advanced students harder — give them professional examples of early versus final drafts to show that revision is where the writing actually happens.

assignments.name: Tracked-Changes Revision
assignments.body: Students revise a previous draft of their own — not a new piece — with tracked changes enabled. They must make at least one revision targeting each of the six dimensions and write a one-paragraph annotation explaining their three most significant choices. Grade the annotation as heavily as the revised draft.

assignments.name: Before-and-After Gallery
assignments.body: Students each submit one paragraph before and after revision. The class reads the pairs anonymously and identifies which dimension each revision most improved and what trade-offs, if any, the writer made. Discussion focuses on the entanglement of dimensions.

assignments.name: Revision Triage
assignments.body: Students receive a short student-sample essay (not their own) that has distinct problems in development, organization, and diction. They must produce a written revision plan — not the revised essay — explaining what they would change, in what order, and why the order matters. This separates planning from execution and surfaces whether they understand the hierarchy of revision decisions.

assignments.name: Diction Autopsy
assignments.body: Students isolate every word in their draft's strongest paragraph that could be more precise. They replace each, then read the original and revised versions aloud. The audible difference in sentence fluency is the lesson.

assignments.name: Cold-Read Revision
assignments.body: Students set aside a draft for at least 48 hours, then revise without rereading their original notes or outline. The estrangement from the draft forces them to encounter the text as a reader rather than as its author — the prerequisite for real revision.

mastery: Produces a revised draft with substantive changes across at least three dimensions — not surface-level corrections only — and can name and justify each significant revision.
Identifies where underdevelopment is causing clarity problems and adds reasoning or evidence that deepens the argument rather than restating it.
Adjusts sentence structure and diction simultaneously when fluency and precision require it, without treating them as unrelated passes.
Recognizes when an organizational sequence is undermining development and restructures the draft at the paragraph level rather than only within sentences.
Articulates the trade-offs of a revision choice — what the new version gains and what, if anything, it costs — rather than assuming all changes are improvements.
notes: Vertical ramp from English III: the expectation is identically worded in 110.38(9)(C). What changes at English IV is the expected quality of the writing students are revising and the sophistication of the choices they are expected to defend. English IV students should be revising texts they intend to publish, submit, or use as writing samples — the stakes are real, not simulated.

DOK 3 justification: revision requires strategic thinking — applying criteria across multiple dimensions, making trade-off judgments, and reconceiving the draft as a whole. It moves well past recall or skill application into extended strategic reasoning.

STAAR analog note: this TEK surfaces primarily in STAAR Expository and Argumentative revision items — "the writer wants to improve paragraph X" frames. Question stems mirror that testing format directly.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Edit Drafts to Demonstrate a Command of Standard English Conventions
expectation: Edit drafts to demonstrate a command of standard English conventions using a style guide as appropriate.
overview: By English IV, editing is not error-hunting — it is the exercise of judgment about what standard English requires and when a style authority governs that judgment. Students are expected to know conventions cold AND to know which guide applies: MLA, APA, Chicago, or whatever the disciplinary context demands.
studentFriendly: You can move through a draft and correct it with confidence — fixing grammar, punctuation, and mechanics — and you know when to open a style guide to settle a real question rather than guess.
questionStems: Which revision of the underlined sentence in paragraph X BEST corrects the grammatical error while preserving the author's meaning?
The writer wants to add a sentence to paragraph X that uses correct parallel structure. Which option BEST accomplishes this?
Read paragraph X. Which change would MOST improve the conventions in this paragraph?
The underlined portion of sentence Y contains an error in punctuation. Which of the following is the BEST revision?
Which sentence from the draft contains a subject-verb agreement error?
The writer is deciding between two versions of a sentence. Which version BEST demonstrates a command of standard English conventions?
Read the following passage. Which edit would MOST effectively eliminate the ambiguous pronoun reference in paragraph X?
Which revision of the underlined clause in paragraph X BEST corrects the modifier placement error?
The writer wants to ensure the draft follows MLA style for in-text citations. Which version of sentence X is MOST consistent with that style guide?
Which of the following changes would MOST improve the clarity and correctness of the draft without altering the writer's intended meaning?

misconceptions.title: Editing Is the Last Step, So It Barely Counts
misconceptions.body: Students treat editing as a low-stakes formality — a quick skim before submission. Push back hard: in English IV, editing reveals whether a writer actually understands how the language works. Sloppy conventions undermine every strong argument and every well-chosen word that precedes them. Teach them that a reader who stumbles over a misplaced comma stops reading the argument and starts reading the writer.

misconceptions.title: "My Meaning Is Clear, So the Error Doesn't Matter"
misconceptions.body: Watch for students who defend convention errors by pointing to comprehension — "you know what I meant." Standard English conventions are not just about meaning; they signal that the writer is in control of the medium. In academic and professional contexts, an editor or professor who reads "it's" for "its" three times in one paper has already formed a judgment about the writer's care and competence.

misconceptions.title: Style Guides Are Optional Decoration
misconceptions.body: Students assume style guides are for bibliographies only and skip them everywhere else. Reframe this: a style guide is a disciplinary contract. MLA governs English class; APA governs psychology; Chicago governs history. Using one correctly signals that you understand what discipline you're operating in. Teach them to treat the guide as a professional tool, not a bureaucratic inconvenience.

misconceptions.title: Proofreading Once Is Enough
misconceptions.body: Students read a draft once for errors and call it done. Teach them that experienced editors read separately for different error types — one pass for sentence-level grammar, one for punctuation, one for citation format. A single sweep catches the most obvious errors and misses the systematic ones. The pattern errors (comma splices, pronoun-antecedent agreement) are invisible precisely because the writer's brain autocorrects them on reading.

misconceptions.title: Grammar Rules Are Absolute
misconceptions.body: Students who know one rule apply it everywhere without nuance. They hyphenate every compound, comma-after-every-introductory-phrase, and passive-voice-is-always-wrong. Teach them that conventions are context-dependent and that style guides exist precisely because reasonable people disagree — the guide resolves the dispute within a disciplinary community. The skill is knowing when to apply a rule and when to consult the authority.

assignments.name: Error Taxonomy Draft
assignments.body: Students receive a peer's draft (anonymized) with eight deliberate errors covering four convention categories. They identify, categorize, and correct each error in writing, citing the specific rule or style guide entry that governs the correction.

assignments.name: Style Guide Showdown
assignments.body: Students take a single paragraph with three citation-style questions (comma usage in a list, formatting a title, integrating a block quote) and solve each using MLA, APA, and Chicago — then write one paragraph explaining where the guides agree and where they diverge.

assignments.name: Self-Edit Protocol
assignments.body: Students develop a personal editing checklist based on their most frequent error patterns (drawn from teacher feedback across the year). They apply this checklist to their own major draft, annotating each correction with the rule they applied. The annotation is graded alongside the draft.

assignments.name: Before and After
assignments.body: Students submit a draft, receive it back with errors marked but NOT corrected, edit, and submit the revision. The grade covers both the quality of corrections and evidence that they consulted a style guide for at least three decisions.

mastery: Identifies and corrects convention errors — grammar, punctuation, mechanics, usage — without being prompted to look harder.
Applies style guide conventions (MLA, APA, or Chicago) accurately to citation format, title styling, and punctuation questions the guide adjudicates.
Distinguishes between convention errors that distort meaning and those that merely violate convention — and prioritizes accordingly.
Annotates editing decisions with the rule or guide entry that governs the choice, demonstrating that corrections are principled rather than intuitive.
Applies multiple editing passes across a draft rather than treating a single read as sufficient.
notes: Vertical ramp: English III 9D carries the same expectation — edit drafts using a style guide. English IV does not add a new verb; it raises the bar on command and independence. By English IV, students are expected to handle conventions without scaffolding and to select and apply the correct style guide for the disciplinary context, not just comply with one assigned by the teacher.
DOK 3 justification: editing requires applying convention knowledge to novel text — not recall alone, but strategic decision-making about which rule applies and when to defer to a style authority.

STAAR note: this TEK maps most directly to the revising/editing strand of STAAR Writing and to embedded grammar questions in STAAR Reading. Stems 1–8 above mirror the revision-task and error-identification format STAAR uses; stems 9–10 address style guide application, which STAAR does not test directly but which defines the upper edge of mastery at this grade level.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Publish Written Work for Appropriate Audiences
expectation: Publish written work for appropriate audiences.
overview: Publishing is where audience awareness stops being a planning note and becomes a real constraint. In English IV — the last English course most students will take before college or career — publishing means deciding who actually needs to read this, what form that reader expects, and whether the piece is ready to meet that standard. The TEK is deceptively short; the thinking it demands is substantial.
studentFriendly: You can take a finished piece of writing, identify the real audience for it, and get it into a form and a place where that audience can actually receive it.
questionStems: The student revised the conclusion of the essay before submitting it to the school newspaper. Which revision MOST likely made the piece more appropriate for that audience?
Read the draft in the box. Which change would BEST prepare this piece for publication in a professional journal rather than a class blog?
Based on the context of the selection, the author MOST likely chose to publish this piece in a community newsletter rather than an academic magazine in order to —
Which sentence from the draft BEST indicates that the writer has considered the expectations of the intended publication?
The writer wants to submit this essay to a literary magazine that publishes personal narratives. Which revision would MOST likely make the piece appropriate for that venue?
Read paragraphs 3 and 4. The writer's decision to use [word/phrase] suggests the intended audience is MOST likely —
Which change to the draft would MOST likely make the tone more appropriate for a general public audience?
The student is preparing this editorial for publication in a local newspaper. Which sentence should be REVISED to better match that audience's expectations?
Based on the draft, the writer would MOST likely need to make which revision before submitting to a formal academic publication?
Which organizational choice in the draft BEST demonstrates the writer's awareness of what the intended audience already knows?

misconceptions.title: "Published" Means Posted Somewhere
misconceptions.body: Students treat publishing as a technical act — upload it, turn it in, print it out, done. They conflate distribution with publication. Teach them that publishing is a rhetorical decision: the venue shapes the expectations, and meeting those expectations is the writer's job. A piece "published" on a Google Doc that nobody reads in the form nobody expects is a missed exercise.

misconceptions.title: The Audience Is the Teacher
misconceptions.body: Four years of turning in work to teachers has calcified one audience in students' minds. Watch for students who write "appropriately" for an imagined editor but whose actual choices — register, assumed background knowledge, vocabulary, length — still signal "grading rubric, not reader." The fix is real venues with real expectations: actual publications, actual submission guidelines, actual formats students did not invent.

misconceptions.title: Editing for Publication Means Cleaning Up Grammar
misconceptions.body: Students reduce the publication-readiness checklist to surface correctness. Grammar is the floor, not the ceiling. Push them to ask harder questions: Is the opening right for this venue? Is the length appropriate? Does this assume too much or too little from this reader? Would this publication actually run this piece? Real editing for publication requires rethinking, not just proofreading.

misconceptions.title: Appropriate Audience Is Whoever Agrees with You
misconceptions.body: Students gravitate toward audiences who already share their position, and they call this "knowing your audience." It isn't. Appropriate audience means the audience for whom this form, topic, and argument is genuinely useful — which sometimes includes people who will push back. Watch for pieces that perform audience awareness without actually making the hard adjustments that a skeptical or unfamiliar reader would require.

misconceptions.title: Once It's Published, the Writer's Job Is Done
misconceptions.body: Students treat submission as the finish line. They do not read their own published work as a reader would, and they do not use the experience of publication (what landed, what didn't, what the editor changed) to revise their understanding of craft. In English IV, the metacognitive loop — what did publishing this teach me about writing? — is part of the expectation, not an extension activity.

assignments.name: Venue Match
assignments.body: Students identify three real external venues (a literary magazine, a local newspaper op-ed section, a school or community website) and write one piece formatted and revised specifically for one of them. They submit a one-paragraph rationale explaining why this venue, and what they changed from an earlier draft to meet its expectations.

assignments.name: Editor's Letter
assignments.body: Before submitting a final piece, students write a brief cover letter to the publication's editor explaining what the piece argues, why it fits the publication, and what the writer did in revision to make it ready. Grade the letter alongside the piece — if a student can't write the letter, the piece isn't ready.

assignments.name: Publication Autopsy
assignments.body: Students bring in two published pieces from the same genre but different venues (e.g., a personal essay in a general magazine vs. one in a literary journal). They annotate the differences in tone, length, assumed knowledge, and format, then apply the same analysis to their own draft before publishing it.

assignments.name: Class Publication
assignments.body: The class curates and publishes a collection (print or digital) of polished senior writing — with student editors who must write rejection or revision notes and a student editor-in-chief who writes an introduction. Every step is real: submission, editorial decision, revision, final publication.

assignments.name: Post-Publication Reflection
assignments.body: After a piece is published or submitted, students write a one-page reflection: What did the publication process reveal about the piece? What would they revise if they could? What did publishing it teach them about audience that revising alone couldn't?

mastery: Selects a publication venue whose audience, format, and expectations match the piece — and can articulate why.
Revises a draft specifically to meet the conventions and expectations of a named external audience, not a generic classroom audience.
Adjusts register, assumed knowledge, length, and format based on real venue requirements rather than personal preference.
Produces a piece that is substantively publication-ready — not merely grammatically correct, but rhetorically appropriate for the intended context.
Reflects on what the publication process revealed about audience and craft, identifying specific adjustments made and their rationale.
notes: Vertical ramp: English III's 9(E) carries the same expectation language, but in English IV the stakes are higher — this is the last formal writing course for most students, and the expectation is that publishing is not a classroom simulation but a real act with a real audience. Teachers should push beyond classroom "publication" to genuine external venues where possible.

DOK 3 justification: publishing requires applying understanding of audience, genre, and convention to make strategic revision decisions; DOK 4 emerges when students evaluate the effectiveness of their publication choices through reflection and comparison across venues.

STAAR does not test this TEK directly in a performance task context, but audience-appropriateness questions appear throughout the writing and revision strands — stems above mirror how STAAR frames revision-for-audience across genre. Note this in instruction.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Compose Literary Texts Using Genre Characteristics and Craft
expectation: Compose literary texts such as fiction and poetry using genre characteristics and craft.
overview: This is the capstone creative writing expectation — English IV students aren't experimenting with genre conventions anymore, they're deploying them with intention. The standard asks for craft, which means technical choices (point of view, line break, syntax, image) made in service of meaning, not decoration.
studentFriendly: You can write fiction or poetry that doesn't just follow the rules of the genre but uses those rules as tools — making deliberate choices about structure, voice, and language that shape what a reader experiences.
questionStems: The author's use of [point of view / narrative distance] in paragraphs X–Y MOST likely serves to —
Which line from the poem BEST demonstrates the speaker's use of a genre convention to develop theme?
Read this passage from the story. The author's choice to shift the setting at this point in the narrative MOST likely —
Which sentence from the excerpt BEST illustrates how the author uses pacing to build tension?
The figurative language in stanza X MOST nearly suggests that —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the author's use of [imagery / diction / syntax] contributes to the story's mood by —
The structural choice the author makes in lines X–Y of the poem MOST likely emphasizes —
Which detail from the passage BEST supports the inference that the narrator is an unreliable observer?
The author's decision to end the story with [specific detail / image] MOST likely suggests —
How does the author's use of [repetition / parallel structure / fragmentation] in stanza X affect the poem's meaning?

misconceptions.title: "Literary" Means Emotional, Not Technical
misconceptions.body: Students write from feeling and assume the feeling is enough — raw sincerity substitutes for craft. Teach them that emotion in literary writing is an effect produced by technical choices, not a substitute for them. A poem that moves a reader moves them because of line breaks, compression, and image, not because the writer felt something hard. If a student can't point to the technical moves in their own draft, they haven't made any.

misconceptions.title: Genre Rules Are Constraints to Escape
misconceptions.body: Students in English IV often arrive with a "break all the rules" orientation toward creative writing — they think sophistication means ignoring convention. Reframe this: you can only break a convention meaningfully if you know what it does. Unconventional structure that doesn't earn its strangeness is just confusion. Push them to identify which conventions they're departing from and why the departure creates meaning.

misconceptions.title: More Detail Always Makes Better Fiction
misconceptions.body: Students pile on sensory description thinking density equals quality. Watch for drafts where every noun has two adjectives and no scene actually advances. The real skill is selection — what a writer LEAVES OUT shapes a reader's experience as much as what's included. Show them how a single well-chosen detail can do more work than a paragraph of explanation.

misconceptions.title: Poetry Doesn't Have to Make Sense
misconceptions.body: When students write poetry, they sometimes confuse obscurity with depth, producing lines that gesture at profundity without landing anywhere. A poem can be strange, indirect, even deliberately ambiguous — but those moves should be purposeful. Teach them to ask: does this difficulty create productive tension for the reader, or am I just hiding? The best test is whether THEY can explain what the line is doing, even if that explanation never appears on the page.

misconceptions.title: Revision Is Fixing Mistakes
misconceptions.body: Students finish a draft and think revision means correcting grammar. In literary writing, revision is where the craft actually happens — where a writer decides the story needs to start three scenes later, or the poem's first stanza is actually its ending. Teach them that cutting a paragraph they love because it slows the piece down is not failure; it's craft. Grade revision documents separately so the process is visible and assessable.

assignments.name: Dual-Genre Experiment
assignments.body: Students write the same narrative moment twice — once as a prose fiction excerpt, once as a poem. After both drafts, they write a one-page reflection: what could fiction do that poetry couldn't, and vice versa? What did each genre force them to give up?

assignments.name: Craft Annotation
assignments.body: Students submit a final literary piece alongside an annotated draft in which every significant technical choice is labeled and briefly justified (point of view, line break, image, structural decision). The annotation is graded separately from the piece — it proves the choices were made, not found.

assignments.name: Imitation with Departure
assignments.body: Students select a published short story or poem they admire, identify three specific craft moves, imitate those moves in an original piece, then deliberately depart from one of them and explain why the departure serves their piece better than faithfulness would have.

assignments.name: Revision Portfolio
assignments.body: Students produce three full drafts of a single literary piece across the unit. Final grade weighs the revision arc — what changed, what was cut, what was discovered — as much as the final product.

assignments.name: Workshop with Craft Language
assignments.body: Peer workshop sessions run on one rule: all feedback must name a specific craft element (pacing, image, syntax, structure, point of view) and explain the effect it creates or fails to create on the reader. "I liked it" and "it was confusing" are not allowed without a craft word attached.

mastery: Produces a literary piece that demonstrates conscious deployment of genre conventions — choices are visible in the text and defensible in the writer's own words.
Makes structural and technical decisions (point of view, line break, image selection, pacing) that serve the piece's meaning rather than defaulting to the most familiar option.
Revises across multiple drafts in ways that reflect deepening craft awareness — cutting, restructuring, or reframing rather than only correcting surface errors.
Distinguishes between what the genre allows and what the piece requires, and navigates that tension with intention.
Gives and receives workshop feedback using specific craft vocabulary, demonstrating that they can read literary texts the same way they write them.
notes: Vertical alignment: English III 10(A) asks students to compose literary texts using genre characteristics and craft — the expectation is identical in language. The English IV ramp is implicit: seniors bring more reading experience, more exposure to complex models, and more time in workshop. The craft bar should be perceptibly higher. Teachers should resist accepting the same quality of work a junior produced; if a senior's poem reads like a competent English III poem, something is missing.

DOK 4 justification: Extended creative composition requiring synthesis of genre knowledge, technical decision-making, and recursive revision. No algorithm produces the product; sustained judgment is required throughout.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog — English IV end-of-course assessment does not include a creative writing composition task. Question stems above are written to mirror how STAAR tests literary craft in the reading strand (author's purpose, structural choices, figurative language) because those are the same analytical moves applied from the reader's side. Note this in classroom framing so students understand the connection between how they read and how they write.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Compose Informational Texts Using Genre Characteristics and Craft
expectation: Compose informational texts such as explanatory essays, reports, resumes, and personal essays using genre characteristics and craft.
overview: English IV is the last classroom any of these students will sit in before they write for the world — cover letters, college applications, workplace reports, personal statements. This TEK isn't genre tourism; it's the capstone ask: can a student move across informational forms, matching craft choices to what each genre actually demands?
studentFriendly: You can produce real informational writing — an explanatory essay, a polished resume, a personal essay — and make the choices inside each piece that fit what that genre is actually supposed to do.
questionStems: Which organizational structure BEST supports the purpose of an explanatory essay written to —
The writer includes the detail in paragraph X MOST likely in order to —
Which sentence from the report BEST establishes the writer's credibility on the topic?
Read the following resume bullet. Which revision MOST effectively communicates the applicant's contribution?
Which transition in paragraph X MOST clearly signals the relationship between the two ideas the writer is developing?
The personal essay's opening paragraph MOST likely serves to —
Which sentence from the explanatory essay BEST supports the writer's central claim?
A student wants to add a sentence to paragraph X to strengthen the report's explanation of [concept]. Which sentence BEST accomplishes this goal?
The writer's decision to use [genre feature] in this piece contributes MOST to the essay's —
Based on the context of the personal essay, the phrase in paragraph X MOST nearly means —

misconceptions.title: Informational Means Objective, So Voice Disappears
misconceptions.body: Students drain every drop of personality out of informational writing because they think "factual" equals "voiceless." Watch for passive-heavy, hedge-word-stuffed prose that reads like a Wikipedia stub. Teach them that craft still operates in informational writing — precise word choice, strategic sentence rhythm, deliberate structure — and that the personal essay genre flat-out demands a distinct voice. Voicelessness is not neutrality; it's just bad writing.

misconceptions.title: A Resume Is Just a List, Not Writing
misconceptions.body: Students treat resume composition as a formatting exercise — bullets in, done. Push them to see that every bullet is a persuasive miniature: verb choice, specificity, and quantification all carry rhetorical weight. "Helped with events" and "coordinated three community fundraisers serving 200+ participants" are not the same statement. The genre has conventions, but craft still determines whether the document works.

misconceptions.title: The Personal Essay Can Say Anything Because It's Personal
misconceptions.body: Students conflate "personal" with "unstructured" — they write diary entries and call them essays. A personal essay has a controlling idea, a deliberate arc, and craft choices that serve a reader who didn't live the experience. Reframe this: the word "personal" names the source material, not the absence of craft. Their experience is the raw material; the essay is what they build from it.

misconceptions.title: Explanatory Writing Doesn't Need Evidence
misconceptions.body: Students think explanation is self-sufficient — just state the idea clearly and move on. They skip the examples, data, and sourced details that actually do the explanatory work. Teach them that explanation without evidence is assertion, and assertion doesn't explain anything. Every claim in an explanatory essay should be tested against the question: "How does a reader who doesn't already believe this get there?"

misconceptions.title: Genre Features Are Decorative, Not Functional
misconceptions.body: Students learn genre features as a checklist — thesis, topic sentences, conclusion — and apply them mechanically without understanding what each one does. Watch for introductions that don't actually orient the reader, conclusions that only repeat the intro, and topic sentences that don't control their paragraphs. Teach them to ask of every genre feature: what problem does this solve for a reader who needs to follow my thinking?

assignments.name: Genre Flex Portfolio
assignments.body: Students produce three pieces across the unit: an explanatory essay on a topic in their field of interest, a professional resume targeting a realistic job or internship, and a personal essay on a moment of genuine consequence. Each piece is accompanied by a one-paragraph craft note naming two deliberate decisions the writer made for that specific genre. Grade the craft note alongside the piece.

assignments.name: Resume Surgery
assignments.body: Students receive a deliberately weak resume (fabricated, no real person) with vague bullets, passive constructions, and missing specificity. They revise it fully, then write a half-page memo to the fictional applicant explaining every change and why it matters. Teaches revision reasoning, not just revision instinct.

assignments.name: Same Event, Three Genres
assignments.body: Students pick one concrete experience or piece of information. They write about it three ways: as a section of a report (objective, structured), as a personal essay (narrative, reflective), and as a resume bullet (compressed, achievement-framed). Debrief focuses on what each genre reveals, hides, and demands.

assignments.name: Explanatory Essay Peer Audit
assignments.body: Partners swap explanatory essays and mark every claim that lacks supporting evidence. Writers revise based on the audit. The goal is concrete: no unsupported claim survives the final draft.

mastery: Produces informational writing in multiple genres — explanatory essay, report, resume, personal essay — with each piece reflecting the actual demands of that genre rather than a generic template.
Makes deliberate craft choices (structure, transitions, diction, evidence use) and can articulate why a choice serves the specific genre's purpose.
Writes resume content that is specific, verb-driven, and achievement-framed — not a list of duties.
Produces a personal essay with a controlling idea, a deliberate arc, and craft choices that serve a reader, not just the writer.
Identifies and corrects genre violations in their own drafts — moments where the wrong conventions or wrong voice leaked in from a different form.
notes: Vertical ramp from English III: 110.38(10)(B) carries the same informational genres expectation. English IV raises the bar at the craft level — students are expected to produce college- and career-ready pieces, not school-generic essays. The resume and personal essay expectations gain urgency at this course level because students are actively using them for applications.

The genre list ("explanatory essays, reports, resumes, and personal essays") is illustrative, not exhaustive — "such as" signals these are examples of the informational category. Instruction should cover the full range of informational forms students will encounter post-graduation.

DOK 3 justification: composing complete genre-specific pieces requires students to apply conventions, make strategic craft decisions, and self-monitor across a full writing task — not just recall features or execute a single procedure.

STAAR does not directly prompt students to produce a resume or personal essay on the assessment. Question stems above mirror how STAAR tests informational writing craft through revision and editing items — the closest analog for this TEK's testing footprint.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Compose Argumentative Texts Using Genre Characteristics and Craft
expectation: Compose argumentative texts using genre characteristics and craft.
overview: This is the capstone argumentative writing standard for English IV — students are not just making claims and citing evidence, they are controlling the full rhetorical machinery of argument: genre conventions, structural choices, tone, syntax, and evidence deployment as craft decisions. The standard demands that students write like writers, not like students filling in a template.
studentFriendly: You can write an argument — an editorial, an op-ed, a researched essay, a letter to a decision-maker — that actually sounds like someone worth reading, because you're making deliberate choices about genre, structure, voice, and evidence instead of following a formula.
questionStems: The writer's use of [rhetorical strategy] in paragraph X MOST likely serves to —
Which sentence from the essay BEST establishes the writer's central claim?
How does the organizational structure of paragraphs X–Y contribute to the persuasiveness of the argument?
The writer's choice to [address counterargument / use anecdote / open with a question] in paragraph X is MOST likely intended to —
Which piece of evidence from the essay BEST supports the claim made in paragraph X?
The tone of paragraph X is MOST nearly —
Which revision to paragraph X would MOST strengthen the writer's argument?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the writer's primary purpose is MOST likely to —
The writer's use of [specific syntactic choice — parallel structure, short declarative sentences, etc.] in paragraph X MOST likely emphasizes —
Which sentence from the essay BEST illustrates the writer's use of [concession / refutation / appeal to credibility]?

misconceptions.title: A Thesis Is the Whole Job
misconceptions.body: Students believe that placing a clear claim in paragraph one is what makes writing argumentative. Watch for essays with strong thesis sentences buried inside five paragraphs of summary — the claim was stated, never developed. Teach them that argumentative craft is what happens after the claim: how evidence is introduced, how counterarguments are named and turned, how the ending earns the position rather than just restating it.

misconceptions.title: "Genre Characteristics" Means Five-Paragraph Structure
misconceptions.body: Students treat genre as a container — intro, three body paragraphs, conclusion — and mistake that container for the genre itself. The genre is the op-ed, the policy memo, the open letter, the researched argument; each carries different conventions around audience address, structural moves, and evidence expectations. Teach them that a five-paragraph essay is a school genre, not a real-world one, and that English IV means learning to write in forms that exist outside the classroom.

misconceptions.title: More Evidence Means a Stronger Argument
misconceptions.body: Students pile quotations and statistics as if argument is a weight contest. The result is a string of sources with thin interpretation between them. Push them deeper: the strength of an argument is in what the writer does with evidence — how it's contextualized, what claim it's actually doing work for, and why it's more compelling than the evidence on the other side. One piece of evidence handled with precision beats five pieces dropped and sprinted past.

misconceptions.title: Counterargument Is a Formality to Get Through
misconceptions.body: Students write concession paragraphs to satisfy rubric requirements, then dismiss the opposing view with a single sentence ("However, this is wrong because..."). The real move is steelmanning: take the best version of the opposing argument, give it full credit where it's due, then explain precisely where it falls short or what it misses. Teachers should reject any counterargument section that could be cut without weakening the essay.

misconceptions.title: Craft Belongs in Fiction, Not Argument
misconceptions.body: Students compartmentalize: literary devices are for poems and stories, argumentation is for facts and logic. They write flat, functionless sentences in argumentative essays because they've been taught to sound "objective." Teach them that every syntactic choice — the short sentence after a long one, the rhetorical question that opens a section, the first-person pivot — is a craft decision that either earns or loses the reader. The best arguments are also the best writing.

assignments.name: Real-World Genre Study
assignments.body: Students select an argument genre — editorial, op-ed, open letter, policy brief — and analyze three published examples for genre conventions: structure, evidence types, audience address, tone. They then produce an original piece in that genre on a topic from their independent research unit. The genre analysis becomes their writer's note.

assignments.name: The Steelman Draft
assignments.body: Before writing their own argument, students write the strongest possible version of the opposing side — a full-length argument they genuinely disagree with. They then draft their own argument against the steelman version. Grade both pieces. Students who can't write the opposing side compellingly aren't ready to argue against it.

assignments.name: Evidence Audit
assignments.body: Midway through a draft, students color-code every sentence: claim, evidence, interpretation, or transition. Any draft with no color-coded interpretation is returned ungraded. The ratio of interpretation to evidence must be at least 1:1. Discussion follows: what does it mean when interpretation is thinner than evidence?

assignments.name: Argument Revision Workshop
assignments.body: Students exchange first drafts and write a one-page reader report — not line edits, but a structural critique: Where is the claim? Where does the argument earn ground, and where does it assert without earning? What's the strongest counterargument the writer hasn't handled? Authors revise in response to the reader report; both documents are submitted together.

assignments.name: Publication-Pitch Final
assignments.body: Students write an argumentative piece with a named, real-world publication venue in mind — a school newspaper, a local paper's letters column, a named magazine or online outlet. Final drafts include a cover letter explaining why the argument fits that venue's audience and conventions. The venue constraint forces genuine genre awareness.

mastery: Produces an argument in a recognizable real-world genre (op-ed, editorial, policy argument, open letter) that reflects that genre's conventions in structure, evidence, and audience address — not a five-paragraph template with a header swapped in.

Develops a central claim through deliberate structural choices: position, evidence, interpretation, counterargument handled with specificity, and a conclusion that extends the argument rather than restates it.
Makes visible craft decisions — in syntax, tone, concession, and transition — and can explain the rhetorical effect of those decisions when asked.
Handles counterargument substantively: names the strongest opposing position, grants what it gets right, and explains precisely where it is insufficient or incomplete.
Uses evidence interpretively — introduces, contextualizes, and responds to sources rather than dropping them as proof; the ratio of interpretation to quotation is at least equal.
notes: Vertical ramp from English III: English III 10C asks students to compose argumentative texts using genre characteristics and craft — same language, but English IV is the terminal course. The ramp is in the expectation of independence and sophistication: fewer scaffolded structures, more genuine genre awareness, and arguments complex enough to require real counterargument handling. By English IV, students should not need an argument template.

DOK 4 justification: students must make and defend composing decisions across a sustained piece, applying genre awareness, craft judgment, and argumentative reasoning simultaneously — no single correct product, requires extended reasoning and original synthesis.

STAAR analog: STAAR Expository and argumentative writing tasks directly assess this standard; question stems above mirror the analytical reading prompts students use when evaluating published arguments, which is the reading-side analog of this composing standard.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Compose Correspondence in a Professional or Friendly Structure
expectation: Compose correspondence in a professional or friendly structure.
overview: This TEK is deceptively high-stakes: English IV is the last classroom practice students get before the professional world demands it. The distinction between professional and friendly structure isn't just formatting — it's audience awareness, purpose control, and tonal discipline applied to real-world writing situations.
studentFriendly: You can write a letter, email, or formal message that sounds exactly right for the situation — whether you're writing to a college admissions office, a potential employer, or a friend — and a reader can immediately tell you know what you're doing.
questionStems: Read the letter in the box. Which sentence from the letter BEST establishes the writer's professional tone?
The writer's choice to open paragraph 1 with [phrase] is MOST likely intended to —
Which revision to the closing of the letter would MOST effectively maintain the professional structure?
Based on the letter, the writer's intended audience is MOST likely —
Which sentence from the correspondence BEST demonstrates the writer's awareness of purpose and audience?
The writer uses the phrase [phrase] in paragraph 2. This choice MOST nearly suggests that the writer —
A student wants to revise paragraph 3 to better match the formal register of the rest of the letter. Which revision BEST accomplishes this?
Which of the following MOST effectively describes how the structure of the letter contributes to the writer's credibility?
The transition between paragraph 1 and paragraph 2 is MOST likely intended to —
Which sentence, if added to the conclusion of the letter, would BEST maintain the established tone and structure?

misconceptions.title: "Professional" Means Stiff and Distant
misconceptions.body: Students conflate formal register with robotic impersonality. They drain all personality from professional correspondence and produce letters that read like legal boilerplate. Teach them that professional structure controls *form* — salutation, body organization, closing — while voice can still be clear, warm, and human. Show them well-crafted cover letters that are genuinely compelling without slipping into casual diction.

misconceptions.title: Friendly Letters Don't Need Structure
misconceptions.body: Students treat "friendly" as a license to ramble. They'll write informal correspondence with no clear purpose, no coherent progression, and no real closing — just conversational drift. Reframe this: friendly structure still has a beginning that orients the reader, a middle that develops a point or update, and a close that lands with intention. The structure is less rigid, not absent.

misconceptions.title: Format Is the Whole Assignment
misconceptions.body: Students learn the heading-salutation-body-closing template and stop there, assuming the format *is* the letter. Watch for technically formatted correspondence that says nothing — a block letter with no real content, no specific detail, no voice. Push them: the format is the skeleton; the writing is the body. A correctly formatted letter that fails to communicate is still a failure.

misconceptions.title: Email and Letter Are Interchangeable
misconceptions.body: Students increasingly assume that since email has replaced most formal correspondence, professional email has no special conventions. They'll open a professional email with "Hey" or close with nothing. Teach them that professional email inherits the expectations of formal letters — clear subject line, appropriate salutation, purposeful body paragraphs, courteous close — even if the medium is digital. The medium shifted; the stakes didn't.

misconceptions.title: Tone Shift Is Only a Vocabulary Problem
misconceptions.body: When students are told their tone is off, they swap a few words and call it fixed. They don't see that tone is structural — how long the sentences run, how much hedging appears, whether they lead with their purpose or bury it. Teach them to look at *when* and *how* they make requests, not just *which words* they use. A passive, buried request reads as unprofessional even when every word is appropriately formal.

assignments.name: Parallel Correspondence
assignments.body: Students write two letters on the same topic — one professional, one friendly — to two different recipients (e.g., requesting an internship extension to a supervisor, and updating a friend about the same delay). Compare them side by side: what changed structurally, tonally, lexically? Grade on deliberate control of both registers.

assignments.name: The Real Letter
assignments.body: Students draft an actual professional letter they need — college inquiry, scholarship follow-up, internship application, letter of appeal. Teacher reviews it as a real document, not just an assignment. Students send it after revision. The authentic audience raises the stakes.

assignments.name: Broken Letter Repair
assignments.body: Provide a student with a poorly formatted, tonally confused professional letter (fabricated for the class). Students identify every structural and tonal problem, label each one, then rewrite the letter correctly. This surfaces the conventions students can't yet name.

assignments.name: Email Audit
assignments.body: Students pull a professional email they have actually sent or received (or examine a provided sample set). They annotate it for structure, register, and effectiveness — then revise it to strengthen at least three specific elements.

assignments.name: Speed Genre Switch
assignments.body: Give students a piece of friendly correspondence and a time limit to convert it to professional correspondence for a new audience and purpose. Discuss which changes were automatic and which required real decisions — that discussion surfaces what students actually understand about register.

mastery: Selects the appropriate structural format (professional or friendly) based on audience, purpose, and context — and can articulate why.
Controls register throughout the correspondence without slipping between formal and casual diction within a single piece.
Uses the expected conventions of each structure (salutation, body organization, closing) without letting format substitute for content.
Produces correspondence with a clear purpose that is established early and developed through organized body paragraphs.
Revises correspondence based on audience feedback or self-evaluation, naming specific structural and tonal decisions.
notes: Vertical ramp: English III's 10D established the same expectation. English IV is the terminal course — the expectation here is full command of both structures for authentic, post-secondary-ready purposes. Teachers should treat this as capstone correspondence writing, not introduction.

DOK 3 justification: students must select and apply appropriate structural conventions strategically, making audience- and purpose-driven decisions that require reasoning beyond recall or skill rehearsal.

STAAR analog note: correspondence-as-genre is more likely to appear on STAAR as a revision/editing stimulus (a letter with embedded errors in structure, tone, or formatting) than as a standalone prompt. Question stems above model that testing framing.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Compose Literary Analysis Using Genre Characteristics and Craft
expectation: Compose literary analysis using genre characteristics and craft.
overview: This is the capstone writing TEK for English IV: students don't just interpret literature, they write about it with the rigor and form that literary analysis demands. The skill requires simultaneous command of what a text is doing AND how to build an argument about it — genre awareness, analytical claim, and writerly control at once.
studentFriendly: You can read a poem, story, or drama, identify what makes it tick as a piece of literature, and write a focused, well-crafted analysis that argues something real about how and why it works.
questionStems: Which sentence from the student's essay BEST states a controlling claim about the author's use of craft?
The student uses the evidence in paragraph 3 MOST likely to —
Which revision to the underlined sentence would MOST clearly connect the textual evidence to the student's claim?
The student's analysis in paragraph 2 would be MOST strengthened by —
Based on the student's essay, which statement BEST describes how the author's use of [genre characteristic] develops the central theme?
Which sentence from the essay MOST effectively distinguishes the student's interpretation from a plot summary?
The student's word choice in paragraph 4 suggests that the author's use of [craft element] is MOST likely intended to —
Which of the following transitions would MOST logically connect paragraph 3 to paragraph 4 in the student's analysis?
The student includes the quotation in paragraph 2 MOST likely to support the inference that —
Which concluding sentence would MOST effectively synthesize the student's argument about the author's craft choices?

misconceptions.title: Literary Analysis Is Just a Fancy Book Report
misconceptions.body: Students treat analysis as summary with a thesis stapled on top. They retell the plot, insert a few quotes, and call it done. Teach them that every sentence in a literary analysis should be doing one of three jobs: stating a claim, presenting evidence, or explaining how the evidence proves the claim. If a sentence is just telling what happened, it isn't analysis — it's dead weight.

misconceptions.title: The Thesis Is the Only Place for the Argument
misconceptions.body: Students front-load their entire claim in the thesis and then coast through body paragraphs, forgetting to argue. They assume showing up with good quotes is enough. Push them to understand that the argument is rebuilt sentence by sentence across the essay — the thesis announces, but the body paragraphs actually prove. Watch for essays that get progressively more descriptive the further from the thesis you read.

misconceptions.title: Quoting More Means Arguing Better
misconceptions.body: Students pile in block quotes and long excerpts, mistaking volume of evidence for quality of analysis. The ratio problem runs the other way: the more they quote, the less they're actually writing. Teach them that a short, precise quote followed by three sentences of unpacking is worth ten times more than a long quote followed by "this shows that." The quote is the door; analysis is what's inside.

misconceptions.title: Genre Characteristics Are Background Knowledge, Not Analytical Tools
misconceptions.body: Students know that a sonnet has 14 lines or that a short story has rising action, but they never use that knowledge to build an argument. They treat genre awareness as trivia to drop in the introduction. Reframe this: genre expectations are an argument waiting to happen. When a writer bends a convention, that's a claim. When the volta hits in line 10 instead of 9, that deviation means something — and the student's job is to say what.

misconceptions.title: "Craft" Means Only Figurative Language
misconceptions.body: Students default to metaphor, simile, and personification as the full inventory of craft. They ignore syntax, structure, point of view, pacing, white space, diction register, irony, and form. Broaden their toolkit explicitly: at English IV, identifying a simile without discussing what structural or tonal work it does in context is insufficient. The craft analysis should explain the decision, not just name the device.

assignments.name: Claim-First Draft
assignments.body: Students write the thesis and all topic sentences of a literary analysis before writing the body paragraphs. They share only the skeleton with a partner, who must be able to predict the argument without reading the evidence. Forces claim clarity before the quotes take over.

assignments.name: Genre Deviation Analysis
assignments.body: Students select a text that deliberately subverts a genre convention (a sonnet that breaks form, a story that withholds resolution, a play with no stage directions). They write a focused two-page analysis arguing what the deviation accomplishes rhetorically or thematically.

assignments.name: Evidence-to-Analysis Ratio Workshop
assignments.body: Students highlight all quoted or paraphrased evidence in one color and all original analytical sentences in another. If the evidence color wins, the draft gets returned for expansion before a grade is assigned. Students internalize the ratio viscerally.

assignments.name: Comparative Genre Analysis
assignments.body: Students analyze two texts in different genres that share a theme (a poem and a short story, a drama and an essay). The task: argue how genre itself shapes what can be said about the theme. Comparative framing forces genre characteristics to become analytical categories, not just labels.

assignments.name: Revision as Argument Audit
assignments.body: Students exchange drafts and annotate every sentence with one of three labels — Claim, Evidence, or Explanation. Sentences that don't fit any label are circled as candidates for cutting. Writers use the audit to revise before final submission.

mastery: Writes a thesis that makes an arguable, specific claim about how the author's genre choices or craft decisions produce a particular effect or meaning.
Selects and integrates textual evidence precisely — quotes are short, embedded, and followed by analysis that explains their function rather than their content.
Uses genre characteristics (form, convention, structure) as analytical categories, not background decoration.
Maintains analytical momentum across the entire essay, not just in the introduction and conclusion.
Demonstrates awareness of their own writerly choices — sentence structure, transitions, diction — as craft decisions that serve the argument.
notes: Vertical ramp from English III: Std 10(E) in English III requires composing literary analysis using genre characteristics and craft — the expectation language is nearly identical. The English IV distinction is the complexity and range of texts expected: senior-level courses should push students toward texts with genuine formal ambiguity (experimental fiction, hybrid genres, postmodern structure) rather than well-charted canonical works that follow conventions cleanly.

DOK 4 justification: students must generate an original, defensible claim about a text, select and evaluate evidence, and sustain an extended argument — requiring integration of knowledge across reading, genre study, and the writing process simultaneously.

STAAR analog note: STAAR tests literary analysis indirectly through revision tasks (Composition strand) and author's craft questions (Reading strand). The stems above mirror both modes — revision-task framing for the writing process questions, and author's craft framing for the analytical application. When used in test prep, pair with a released literary prose passage from STAAR English II or English I for the richest analog.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Compose a Rhetorical Analysis Using Genre Characteristics and Craft
expectation: Compose rhetorical analysis using genre characteristics and craft.
overview: This is the capstone composition TEK for English IV — students don't just identify rhetorical moves; they write about them with precision and genre fluency. The task demands that students hold two levels simultaneously: what the original author is doing AND what the student-writer is doing as they construct the analysis itself.
studentFriendly: You can write a formal rhetorical analysis that explains HOW a text works — its genre conventions, its craft choices, its persuasive strategies — with the same control and precision a real critic brings to serious writing.
questionStems: Which sentence from the student's rhetorical analysis BEST supports the claim that the author uses contrast to develop the central argument?
The student writer's use of the phrase "strategic ambiguity" in paragraph 3 MOST likely serves to —
Based on paragraphs 2 and 4, which of the following BEST describes how the student organizes the analysis?
Which evidence from the original text would MOST effectively support the student's claim in paragraph 5?
The student's analysis in paragraph 3 would MOST benefit from —
Which revision to the underlined sentence in paragraph 2 BEST clarifies the student's claim about the author's use of syntax?
How does the student's use of direct quotation in paragraph 4 contribute to the effectiveness of the analysis?
The student concludes the rhetorical analysis by returning to the opening image from the source text. This choice MOST likely serves to —
Based on the analysis as a whole, which statement BEST describes the student's central claim about the author's rhetorical strategy?
Which sentence from the analysis MOST clearly demonstrates the student's understanding of how genre conventions shape the author's message?

misconceptions.title: Rhetorical Analysis Is Just a Summary With Opinions
misconceptions.body: Students describe what the author says — plot summary with a few adjectives attached — and call it analysis. The genre requires a different move entirely: not what the text says but how it achieves its effect, and why those choices matter. Teach them the one-sentence test: if their claim would still be true if you swapped in a different essay on the same topic, it isn't a rhetorical claim — it's a content summary.

misconceptions.title: "Genre Characteristics" Means Label the Devices
misconceptions.body: Students inventory rhetorical devices — "the author uses anaphora in paragraph 2" — without connecting the device to genre expectations or argumentative function. Push them deeper: what does anaphora DO in an op-ed versus a eulogy versus a legal brief? The genre shapes what a device means. The analysis must explain the fit between the move and the form, not just name the move.

misconceptions.title: The Analysis Doesn't Have to Be Well-Written
misconceptions.body: Students pour energy into dissecting the source text and produce sloppy, unrevised prose in the analysis itself. The irony is visible and damaging: you cannot write a credible analysis of an author's craft in a sentence with three comma splices. Hold them to the standard that the analysis must model the control it describes — if they're praising the author's clarity, their own syntax should not be muddy.

misconceptions.title: Craft Critique Requires a Verdict on Whether the Author Succeeded
misconceptions.body: Students feel obligated to conclude that the author's rhetorical choices either "worked" or "didn't work." That binary collapses the task. Real rhetorical analysis explains the function of choices within the text's context — a choice that seems manipulative can be functionally effective; a choice that seems elegant can fail its audience. Teach them to evaluate function and effect separately, without flattening to thumbs-up/thumbs-down.

misconceptions.title: Any Source Text Works the Same Way
misconceptions.body: Students treat the genre of the source text as irrelevant — they analyze a political speech, a lyric essay, and a scientific op-ed using identical frameworks. Genre conventions determine what counts as a craft choice in the first place. Watch for analyses that would work on any text, in any genre, by any author — that's a sign the student hasn't engaged with genre at all. The framework must bend to fit the form.

assignments.name: Parallel Texts, Parallel Claims
assignments.body: Students receive two texts making similar arguments in different genres (e.g., an op-ed and a letter to the editor). They write a rhetorical analysis of one, then annotate how their framework would need to change to analyze the other. Forces genuine genre awareness.

assignments.name: Annotated Draft Exchange
assignments.body: Students exchange full rhetorical analysis drafts with a partner. The partner identifies every claim that is actually a content summary (not an analytical claim) and every analytical claim that lacks textual evidence. Writers revise based on the coded feedback. Run two rounds.

assignments.name: Genre Autopsy
assignments.body: Before writing, students map the source text's genre conventions — what the form requires, what it permits, what it forbids. The genre map becomes an appendix to the finished analysis. Grading weights the map equally with the essay to reward the thinking, not just the product.

assignments.name: Live Revision Workshop
assignments.body: Pull a student's weak paragraph (anonymized, with permission) onto the projector. Whole class rewrites it together in real time — turning summary sentences into analytical claims, connecting device to genre function, tightening syntax. Students revise their own parallel paragraph immediately after.

assignments.name: Published Rhetorical Analysis as Mentor Text
assignments.body: Students read one published piece of rhetorical criticism (a review essay, a literary critical piece, a rhetorical theory excerpt adapted for high school) and annotate how the critic handles genre, evidence, and claim structure. Then they write their own analysis using the mentor text's moves as a loose scaffold.

mastery: Produces an analytical claim that specifies a rhetorical strategy, connects it to genre conventions, and identifies its effect on audience — not a summary claim, not a verdict on the author's success.
Integrates textual evidence at the level of specific craft choices (word choice, syntax, structure, tone) rather than paraphrasing content.
Sustains genre awareness throughout — explains how the form's conventions shape what the author's choices mean and what they can accomplish.
Produces prose that models the control it describes: organized, precise, syntactically clean.
Evaluates rhetorical function and audience effect without collapsing to a binary judgment of whether the text "worked."
notes: Vertical jump from English III: English III 10(F) requires composing a rhetorical analysis; English IV holds the same expectation but as the terminal course — the standard of craft control, genre fluency, and analytical precision is implicitly higher, and source texts should be proportionally complex (longer, multi-modal, genre-hybrid, or historically distanced).

DOK 4 justification: Students must evaluate the rhetorical choices of an external author, construct an extended analytical argument, and sustain genre-appropriate craft in their own writing — simultaneous authorial and analytical control across an extended task.

No direct STAAR analog for student-composed rhetorical analysis in the assessment window; question stems above are written to mirror how STAAR tests the reading side of analysis (author's craft, text structure, rhetorical effect) applied to student-produced analytical writing as a revision/editing task — the most common assessment proxy.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Develop Questions for Formal and Informal Inquiry
expectation: Develop questions for formal and informal inquiry.
overview: The question is the research. Students who arrive at this TEK in English IV should be leaving with the ability to distinguish between a question worth a semester-long formal investigation and one worth a focused afternoon of reading — and to draft both with precision. The skill is not "think of something to wonder about" but "calibrate the scope and form of a question to the kind of inquiry it demands."
studentFriendly: You can look at a topic, decide whether it calls for deep formal research or a quick focused inquiry, and write a question that is sharp enough to actually guide your investigation without being so narrow it kills it.
questionStems: Which question would BEST guide a formal research project about the topic in this passage?
Based on the information in paragraphs X–Y, which question is MOST likely to require further formal inquiry?
The author's discussion in paragraph X MOST likely raises which research question?
Which question could BEST be answered through informal inquiry rather than formal research?
A student reading the passage wants to develop a formal research question. Which of the following questions is MOST appropriately scoped for that purpose?
Based on the central idea developed across paragraphs X–Z, which inquiry question MOST directly addresses a gap in the author's argument?
Which revision of the following question would MOST likely make it suitable for formal academic inquiry?
The evidence presented in paragraph X BEST supports which research question as a starting point for further investigation?
Which question is MOST likely to produce a focused, answerable thesis when researched formally?
Based on the passage, which question represents informal inquiry rather than a researchable claim requiring systematic investigation?

misconceptions.title: Broader Is Better
misconceptions.body: Students equate scope with ambition and write questions so wide they collapse under their own weight — "Why is society the way it is?" or "What causes all wars?" Watch for questions that could anchor a doctoral dissertation or fill an encyclopedia. Teach them that a researchable question has a horizon: it can actually be answered with the sources available in the time allowed. Scope is a craft decision, not a reflection of how much students care.

misconceptions.title: Formal Inquiry Just Means More Sources
misconceptions.body: Students think the only difference between formal and informal inquiry is the number of sources cited. Push them deeper: formal inquiry has a structure — a driving question, a methodology, a sustained argument. Informal inquiry is exploratory, iterative, often undirected. A student Googling a question out of curiosity is doing informal inquiry. A student constructing a literature review is doing formal inquiry. The distinction lives in the architecture, not the bibliography length.

misconceptions.title: The Question Is a Throwaway Step Before the Real Work
misconceptions.body: Students treat question development as a box to check before they get to "actual research." They write a placeholder question, find sources, then quietly abandon the question when the evidence goes a different direction — without revising the question to match. Teach them that revising a research question in light of early evidence is not a failure; it is the skill. The question and the research are in ongoing conversation.

misconceptions.title: A Good Question Already Has an Answer
misconceptions.body: Students default to questions they already know how to answer or that confirm what they already believe — not because they are lazy but because genuine uncertainty is uncomfortable. These questions read as informational rather than investigative: "What is climate change?" instead of "Why have certain coastal communities resisted climate adaptation policy despite documented risk?" Watch for questions that would be answered with a definition or a summary. Teach them that a research question lives in territory where the answer is actually unknown to them.

misconceptions.title: Informal Inquiry Doesn't Count as Real Inquiry
misconceptions.body: Students (and sometimes teachers) dismiss informal inquiry as pre-research — a warm-up before the serious work begins. But informal inquiry has its own discipline: following a thread of curiosity, knowing when to stop, synthesizing quickly without over-citing. In English IV, where students are preparing for college and career research cultures, both modes matter. Teach them that a professional who cannot conduct efficient informal inquiry — skimming, surfing, pattern-matching — is as underprepared as one who cannot sustain a formal project.

assignments.name: Question Taxonomy Sort
assignments.body: Give students a list of 15–20 sample research questions. They sort them into three categories: too broad, too narrow, appropriately scoped — then revise one from each "too" category. Discussion focuses on what changed in the revision and why.

assignments.name: Formal vs. Informal Triage
assignments.body: Present students with five real-world scenarios (a journalist on deadline, a scientist writing a grant, a student writing a synthesis essay, a hiring manager vetting a candidate, a consumer choosing a product). Students decide which scenario calls for formal inquiry, which for informal, and draft a question appropriate to each context. They then write a paragraph defending one of their choices.

assignments.name: Question Genealogy
assignments.body: Students pick a broad topic and generate a "question tree" — one broad question at the root, three mid-scope questions branching from it, and one precisely scoped formal research question at the tip. They annotate each branch with what kind of inquiry it calls for and what sources would answer it.

assignments.name: Question Revision Workshop
assignments.body: Students bring draft research questions from a current or recent project. Pairs exchange and diagnose: Is this formal or informal? Is it answerable? Is the scope right?
