Each student writes a revised question and a one-paragraph explanation of what changed and why. Grade the explanation as heavily as the revision.

mastery: Produces a formal research question that is scoped to a semester-length investigation, is genuinely uncertain (not pre-answered), and points toward a specific methodology or source type.
Produces an informal inquiry question that is focused, time-bounded, and appropriate to exploratory rather than systematic investigation.
Distinguishes between the two modes of inquiry in writing, explaining why a given question belongs to one category and not the other.
Revises an initial question in response to early research without abandoning the inquiry — adjusting scope, angle, or focus rather than starting over.
Explains the relationship between the quality of a research question and the quality of the research that follows from it.
notes: Vertical jump from English III: English III 11A asks students to develop questions for inquiry; English IV holds the same expectation but students are positioned at the end of a four-year arc — the tacit expectation is that question development is now self-directed and metacognitive, not scaffolded. Teachers should resist re-teaching the concept from scratch and instead push for precision and independence.
DOK 3 justification: developing a well-scoped research question requires strategic thinking — students must evaluate scope, mode, and answerability simultaneously, not retrieve a formula.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog; question stems above mirror how STAAR tests inquiry-adjacent skills through passage-based prompts about an author's use of evidence and gaps in argument. See QUESTION STEMS DOCTRINE note in HANDOFF.md.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Critique the Research Process at Each Step
expectation: Critique the research process at each step to implement changes as needs occur and are identified.
overview: This is metacognitive research — students don't just do the research, they audit it while it's happening. The skill is catching problems in real time: a narrowing question, a source ecosystem that's too thin, an argument that's drifted from the evidence. English IV is the capstone; students leave here ready to manage a research project without a teacher flagging every wrong turn.
studentFriendly: You can stop at any point in a research project and ask yourself whether what you're doing is actually working — and then change course before you've built too much on a shaky foundation.
questionStems: Read paragraph X of the student's research journal. Which change would MOST likely improve the validity of the research process described?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the researcher's decision to narrow the inquiry question was MOST likely motivated by —
Which revision to the research plan in paragraph X would BEST address the gap the student identified in the source evidence?
The researcher's note in paragraph X suggests that the next step in the process should MOST likely —
Which sentence from the research log BEST supports the conclusion that the researcher recognized a flaw in the methodology?
Based on the research plan in paragraph X, what problem with the process would the researcher MOST likely need to address before drawing a conclusion?
The researcher's decision described in paragraph X is BEST supported by which of the following principles of sound inquiry?
Which statement BEST describes how the researcher in paragraph X responds when a source fails to support the original thesis?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, which step in the research process was MOST compromised by the researcher's approach?
The researcher's self-evaluation in paragraph X reveals that the MOST significant weakness in the process so far is —

misconceptions.title: Critique Happens at the End, Not Throughout
misconceptions.body: Students treat research as a linear march: gather, draft, cite, done. Critique gets mentally filed under "revision," which they understand to mean "after you write." Teach them that a research audit at step three is worth ten hours of backfilling at the end. Build explicit checkpoints into the project timeline — not as busy work but as genuine decision gates where students must either proceed or pivot.

misconceptions.title: Changing Direction Means Failure
misconceptions.body: When a student's initial question leads nowhere productive, they often push through rather than adjust — treating the original plan as a contract they can't break. Watch for students who assemble a bibliography full of marginally relevant sources rather than admitting the question needs to change. The skill here is exactly the opposite: recognizing when the evidence is telling you something about the question, and having the intellectual flexibility to listen.

misconceptions.title: "Critiquing the Process" Means Blaming the Sources
misconceptions.body: Students conflate critiquing the research process with critiquing individual sources. They'll flag a weak article and think they've done metacognition. Push them deeper: was the search strategy sound? Was the inquiry question too broad to yield usable results? Were they searching in the right databases? Source critique is downstream — process critique asks whether the system that found the sources was well designed.

misconceptions.title: Any Change Mid-Project Is Scope Creep
misconceptions.body: Some students — especially those with strong organizational instincts — resist mid-process changes because they conflate flexibility with disorganization. Reframe this: a research plan is a hypothesis, and like any hypothesis, it should be responsive to what the evidence actually shows. Teach them to document changes and their rationale, so pivoting becomes part of the project's intellectual record rather than a sign that something went wrong.

misconceptions.title: Self-Evaluation Only Counts If It's Negative
misconceptions.body: Students think critiquing their process means finding things wrong with it. They'll produce a self-evaluation that's either defensive ("everything's fine") or overcorrecting ("everything's broken"). The real move is calibrated — naming what's working so they can double down, naming what isn't so they can fix it, and distinguishing between a genuine problem and a temporary roughness that the next step will resolve.

assignments.name: Research Checkpoint Memos
assignments.body: At three predetermined points in a multi-week research project, students write a one-page memo addressed to themselves: What decision did I just make? Why? What am I uncertain about? What might I need to change? Memos are collected and graded as part of the final project.

assignments.name: Process Autopsy
assignments.body: After submitting a completed research paper, students write a retrospective analysis of their own process — identifying two decisions that strengthened the work, two that weakened it, and one moment where they should have changed course but didn't. This is graded on honesty and specificity, not on whether the paper itself was strong.

assignments.name: Research Plan Stress Test
assignments.body: Students submit a research plan (question, source strategy, working thesis) before beginning. Pairs exchange plans and interrogate each other's with a structured set of questions: What breaks this? Where is the evidence likely to be thin? What happens if the thesis doesn't hold? Students revise their plans before beginning.

assignments.name: Live Research Audit
assignments.body: Mid-project, students bring everything they have so far — notes, sources, working thesis, search history — to a fifteen-minute conference. The teacher asks one diagnostic question: "Where are you most uncertain right now?" Students must answer with specificity and name one concrete change they'll make before the next session.

mastery: Identifies specific problems at specific steps in the research process — not just "I need better sources" but "my search terms are too narrow to surface the counterargument I need."
Implements changes mid-process without abandoning the overall inquiry — adjusts scope, question, or strategy while maintaining coherence.
Documents the rationale for changes so the research log reflects an evolving intellectual process, not a cleaned-up final version.
Distinguishes between a process flaw (bad search strategy) and a source flaw (weak article) and addresses each at the correct level.
Produces a final research product whose quality is traceable to the decisions documented in the process — the critique shaped the outcome.
notes: Vertical ramp: English III's 11(B) asks students to "follow the research plan" and make adjustments — the emphasis is on executing the plan responsively. English IV raises the bar to *critique* the process at each step, meaning the student must evaluate the process itself, not just respond to obstacles. The move is from adaptive execution to systemic self-audit.

DOK 4 justification: the expectation requires extended metacognitive reasoning — students must apply evaluative criteria to their own evolving process, justify changes, and synthesize what the evidence is telling them about the inquiry design. No single correct answer; judgment is the product.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is not directly assessed on STAAR as a standalone item. Question stems are written to mirror how STAAR tests research process and metacognitive decision-making through research log or journal excerpts — the most likely vehicle if assessed. Note this limitation when using stems for test prep.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Develop and Revise a Research Plan
expectation: Develop and revise a plan.
overview: A research plan isn't a to-do list — it's a living argument about how to get from a question to an answer. English IV students need to treat planning as recursive: what they find in early sources reshapes what they're looking for, which reshapes the plan itself. The revision half is the harder half, and most students skip it entirely.
studentFriendly: You can build a real research plan — a working map of your inquiry question, your source strategy, and your timeline — and then actually revise that plan when what you're finding changes what you need to know.
questionStems: Which revision to the student's research plan MOST effectively narrows the focus of the inquiry?
Based on the information in the passage, which source would BEST support the research question in paragraph 1?
The researcher revises the plan in paragraph 3 primarily to —
Which sentence from the research log BEST explains why the original plan needed revision?
A student conducting research on [topic] would MOST likely need to revise the plan after discovering —
Which addition to the research plan in paragraph 2 would MOST effectively address a gap in the inquiry?
The student's revised research question in paragraph 4 is MOST likely intended to —
Based on paragraphs 2 and 3, what does the researcher conclude about the original scope of the plan?
Which statement BEST describes the relationship between the initial plan and the revised plan in the passage?
The researcher's decision to eliminate [source type] from the plan in paragraph 5 MOST likely suggests that —

misconceptions.title: The Plan Is Something You Make Once and Follow
misconceptions.body: Students treat the research plan as a contract — they write it on day one and feel obligated to execute it exactly, even when early findings show the question is wrong or the scope is impossible. Teach them that revision is not failure; it's the sign of a researcher who is actually paying attention. Build in a required revision checkpoint with documentation of what changed and why.

misconceptions.title: "Revising the Plan" Means Expanding the Topic
misconceptions.body: When asked to revise, students almost universally add more — more subtopics, more sources, more questions. They almost never narrow. Push them toward the harder move: cutting what's not answerable, tightening what remains, and focusing the inquiry where the evidence actually lives. A sharper question is almost always a better question.

misconceptions.title: A Good Plan Means Having All the Sources First
misconceptions.body: Students want to find their sources before they write the plan, then reverse-engineer the question to fit what they already found. That's bibliography-building, not inquiry. Teach them that the plan guides the search — you decide what KIND of evidence you need before you go looking for it, and you revise the plan when the search reveals gaps you didn't anticipate.

misconceptions.title: The Research Question and the Plan Are the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students conflate the inquiry question with the plan. The question is one line; the plan is the architecture that answers it — scope, source types, search strategy, timeline, and checkpoints. Watch for students who submit a question and a list of websites and call it a plan. A plan without structure for HOW you'll answer the question is just a guess.

misconceptions.title: Revision Means Fixing Grammar in Your Notes
misconceptions.body: When students hear "revise your plan," they think proofreading — tidying up wording, correcting dates, cleaning formatting. Real revision means reconsidering the inquiry itself: Is the question still answerable? Are these the right source types? Is the scope realistic? Explicitly model what intellectual revision looks like, as distinct from surface-level editing.

assignments.name: Plan, Search, Revise, Document
assignments.body: Students draft an initial research plan for a self-selected inquiry question, conduct one round of preliminary searching, then produce a written revision documenting specifically what changed — scope, source types, question wording, or timeline — and why. The revision memo is graded separately from the plan itself.

assignments.name: Autopsy a Bad Plan
assignments.body: Provide students with a pre-written research plan that has four or five structural problems (vague question, wrong source types for the claim, no timeline, scope too broad). Students identify each flaw, explain why it's a problem, and produce a revised version. Debrief as a class before students touch their own plans.

assignments.name: Mid-Research Check-In Conference
assignments.body: Midway through a research unit, each student brings their original plan and a written revision to a 5-minute conference. The student explains what changed and what prompted the change. Teacher notes whether the revision reflects genuine responsiveness to new information or surface-level adjustment. Grade for intellectual honesty.

mastery: Produces a research plan with a focused, answerable inquiry question, a source-type rationale, and a realistic timeline — not just a topic and a list of links.
Documents plan revisions explicitly, naming what changed and why, rather than quietly rewriting the original.
Narrows scope in revision as often as they expand it, demonstrating responsiveness to what the evidence actually supports.
Distinguishes between the inquiry question and the research architecture that answers it.
Treats planning and revising as continuous throughout the research process, not as separate phases that each happen once.
notes: Vertical jump from English III: English III 11C is the same expectation at the surface — develop and revise a plan — but English IV requires the skill to operate at greater scope and sophistication, typically in the context of a sustained independent research project rather than a teacher-scaffolded unit. The revision cycle in English IV is expected to be student-initiated, not teacher-prompted.

DOK 3 justification: developing a plan requires strategic thinking about inquiry design; revising a plan requires evaluating findings against original goals and making reasoned adjustments — both exceed recall and skill application.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is not directly tested as a standalone item. Question stems are written to mirror how STAAR tests research process skills — typically embedded in paired-passage or research-scenario sets where students evaluate planning decisions, source selection, and revision choices as presented in a student's research log or annotated source list.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Modify the Major Research Question to Refocus the Research Plan
expectation: Modify the major research question as necessary to refocus the research plan.
overview: This TEK is about the moment researchers realize their original question doesn't fit the evidence — and instead of forcing the evidence to answer the wrong question, they adjust the question. That's not failure; that's research. English IV students need to internalize that inquiry is iterative, not linear.
studentFriendly: You can recognize when your research question isn't working anymore and change it based on what the evidence is actually telling you — without starting over from scratch.
questionStems: A student researcher discovers that most sources address a narrower aspect of the original question. Which revision to the research question BEST reflects the available evidence?
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the researcher MOST likely modified the original question in order to —
Which revised research question MOST effectively refocuses the inquiry based on the limitations identified in paragraph X?
A researcher's initial question is too broad to address in a single project. Which revision BEST narrows the question while preserving the original purpose?
The researcher in paragraph X concludes that the original question was unanswerable with available sources. Which action MOST likely represents the next step?
Which statement BEST describes the relationship between a revised research question and the sources already gathered?
Based on the research log excerpt in paragraphs X–Y, the researcher's decision to modify the question was MOST likely prompted by —
Which revision to the research question would MOST likely require the researcher to seek entirely new sources?
The researcher in paragraph X identifies a gap between the original question and the evidence collected. Which conclusion MOST likely follows from this observation?
Which of the following BEST explains why revising a research question mid-process can strengthen rather than weaken a research plan?

misconceptions.title: Changing the Question Means the Research Failed
misconceptions.body: Students treat a modified research question as evidence they did something wrong at the start. They resist revision even when the evidence clearly demands it. Teach them that professional researchers revise their questions constantly — the original question is a hypothesis about what's worth asking, not a contract. Frame question modification as a sign of engagement with the evidence, not retreat from it.

misconceptions.title: The New Question Just Has to Sound Different
misconceptions.body: When students do revise, they make cosmetic changes — swap a synonym, reorder the clause — without actually refocusing the inquiry. Watch for questions that changed in wording but not in scope or angle. Push them to articulate what the old question was missing and whether the new question genuinely closes that gap.

misconceptions.title: You Only Modify the Question Once
misconceptions.body: Students assume revision is a single event: the original question, then the fixed question, done. In practice, the research question may shift two or three times as evidence accumulates. Teach them to treat question revision as an ongoing diagnostic — every significant source is a chance to ask "is this still the right question?"

misconceptions.title: Modifying the Question Means Starting the Research Over
misconceptions.body: Students believe that if the question changes, everything gathered so far is wasted. This stalls them. Help them see that most of the existing sources remain relevant even to a refocused question — what changes is how they're used and what gaps still need filling. A modified question reorganizes the evidence; it rarely discards it.

misconceptions.title: A Narrower Question Is Always Better
misconceptions.body: Students learn early that research questions should be "focused," and they overcorrect — narrowing their question past the point of significance until it answers itself in a paragraph. Teach them that the goal is a question that is specific enough to be answerable AND broad enough to matter. Narrowing is a tool, not the destination.

assignments.name: Question Audit
assignments.body: At the midpoint of a research project, students submit a written audit: their original question, three pieces of evidence that created tension with it, and their revised question with a one-paragraph rationale. Graded on the quality of the rationale, not whether the question changed dramatically.

assignments.name: Research Log Protocol
assignments.body: Students keep a running research log with dated entries. Each entry answers three prompts: What did I find? How does it fit (or not fit) my current question? Does my question need to shift? Final product includes the log alongside the completed research paper.

assignments.name: Question Archaeology
assignments.body: Students exchange research questions and sources with a partner. Each partner evaluates whether the question accurately represents what the sources can actually answer, then proposes one modification. Writer responds in writing: accept, reject with explanation, or counter-propose.

mastery: Identifies specific moments in the research process when gathered evidence reveals a mismatch with the original question.
Articulates a clear rationale for why the question is being modified — names what changed in the evidence base, not just what changed in the wording.
Produces a revised question that is genuinely more answerable given available sources, without losing the inquiry's core purpose.
Evaluates whether existing sources remain usable after a question revision and identifies what new sources, if any, the new question requires.
Treats question revision as a recurring metacognitive check rather than a one-time fix.
notes: Vertical ramp from English III: English III Std 11(D) also involves modifying the research question; English IV holds students to the same skill but within the context of a more complex, independently managed research plan — the expectation is greater metacognitive ownership and less scaffolding to prompt the revision.

DOK 3 justification: students must evaluate the relationship between evolving evidence and an existing question, then make a strategic decision about revision — this requires reasoning across multiple inputs rather than recall or single-step application.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is most likely assessed through a research scenario passage in which a student researcher encounters a problem with their question — stems will test whether students can identify the prompt for revision and evaluate the quality of a proposed revised question. Stems above follow this scenario-based format.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Locate Relevant Sources
expectation: Locate relevant sources.
overview: Deceptively short, this standard carries serious weight at the English IV level: senior students should be doing real research, not Google-and-grab. The move is not finding sources — it is finding the RIGHT sources for the specific inquiry at hand, which demands knowing where to look, how to search, and how to tell relevant from adjacent.
studentFriendly: You can identify and locate sources that actually speak to your specific research question — not just sources that mention the topic, but ones whose evidence and perspective genuinely advance your inquiry.
questionStems: The student researching [topic] would MOST likely find relevant information in which of the following sources?
Which source would BEST support a research paper arguing that —
Based on the information in paragraph X, which type of source would MOST likely provide additional evidence for the student's claim?
A student writing a report about [topic] locates four sources. Which source is MOST relevant to the student's specific research question?
The student wants to find a primary source about [event/topic]. Which of the following would BEST serve that purpose?
Which search strategy would MOST likely help a student locate peer-reviewed sources on [topic]?
A student is researching [topic] and finds the following sources. Which source is LEAST relevant to the research question stated in paragraph X?
Based on the student's research question in paragraph X, which database or resource would MOST likely yield the most relevant results?
The student needs a source that provides [specific type of information]. Which of the following would BEST meet that need?
Which of the following revisions to the student's search terms would MOST likely produce more relevant results for the research question in paragraph X?

misconceptions.title: "Relevant" Means "About the Same Topic"
misconceptions.body: Students treat relevance as topical proximity — if the source mentions their subject, it counts. Push them to distinguish between sources that address the topic and sources that address the specific claim or question they are pursuing. A source about climate change is not automatically relevant to a paper about climate policy in Gulf Coast fishing communities. Teach them to test every source against their research question, not their research keyword.

misconceptions.title: The First Page of Results Is the Research
misconceptions.body: Students conflate locating sources with opening a browser and accepting whatever appears on the first results page. Watch for students who treat search-engine ranking as a proxy for relevance or credibility. English IV is the moment to require database navigation — EBSCO, JSTOR, ProQuest, government repositories — and to make unfiltered Google searches insufficient by design.

misconceptions.title: More Sources Equals Better Research
misconceptions.body: Students pile up sources to hit a minimum count, treating quantity as evidence of effort. The real skill is selecting fewer, better sources — ones that are specific enough to actually do work in the paper. Teach them that a senior research project with six precise, relevant sources is stronger than one padded to fifteen with tangential ones. Relevance is a filter, not a floor.

misconceptions.title: Primary and Secondary Sources Are Interchangeable
misconceptions.body: Students know the terms but treat them as stylistic choices rather than strategic ones. Watch for students who substitute a secondary account for a primary document when the assignment requires evidence from the source itself — or who locate a primary source they cannot interpret without the secondary context they skipped. Teach them that the type of source matters as much as its content, and that relevance includes matching source type to the role it needs to play in the argument.

misconceptions.title: Locating a Source Means the Research Is Done
misconceptions.body: Students mark a source as "found" and move on without verifying that it actually contains what they need. They'll cite a database record they never fully read or a PDF they skimmed for the abstract. Teach them that locating is step one, not the finish line — the source must be read, evaluated, and confirmed relevant before it earns a spot in the project. Build in a brief annotation requirement so students have to prove the source delivers.

assignments.name: Source Relevance Audit
assignments.body: Students bring five sources they have located for a current research project. In pairs, they evaluate each source against the specific research question — not the topic — and argue whether each source is relevant, adjacent, or off-target. Each student writes a one-paragraph reflection on which source they initially overvalued and why.

assignments.name: Database vs. Open Web Comparison
assignments.body: Students run the same search on an academic database and on a general search engine and document what each returns in the first ten results. They annotate three results from each, rating relevance to a shared class research question. Class discussion: what accounts for the difference, and what does that mean for where research should start?

assignments.name: Precision Search Workshop
assignments.body: Given a broad research question, students develop three increasingly specific search strings, run each, and compare the quality of results. They document which terms and Boolean operators most improved relevance and present their search logic in a one-page process reflection.

assignments.name: Source Type Mapping
assignments.body: Students identify what type of source — primary, secondary, peer-reviewed article, government data, news report — would best serve each section of an argumentative paper (background, claim, counterargument, data). They then locate one source per category and justify each choice in a brief annotation.

mastery: Searches academic databases and domain-specific repositories, not only general search engines, when locating sources.
Tests each located source against the specific research question rather than the general topic, and discards sources that are adjacent but not genuinely relevant.
Distinguishes between primary and secondary sources and selects the appropriate type for the role the source needs to play in the argument.
Refines search terms iteratively — adjusting keywords, using Boolean operators, applying date or publication-type filters — when initial results are not sufficiently relevant.
Produces brief annotations that confirm a source's relevance before incorporating it, demonstrating that locating and vetting are treated as separate, sequential steps.
notes: Vertical alignment: English III 11E carries the same expectation language. The ramp at English IV is in the sophistication of the inquiry context — senior capstone and college-application research demands sources at a greater level of specificity, disciplinary depth, and precision than the research tasks typical in English III. Teachers should engineer tasks that cannot be completed with surface-level source location.

DOK 2 justification: the skill is skill-level application — using a procedure (searching, filtering, evaluating for relevance) to accomplish a defined task. The cognitive demand increases when paired with 11A (question formulation) and 11D (distinguishing relevant from irrelevant), which push toward DOK 3.

This TEK has no direct STAAR analog as a standalone tested skill; STAAR embeds source relevance within multi-source synthesis passages. Question stems above are written to mirror how STAAR frames source-selection decisions within paired or multi-source reading tasks.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Synthesize Information from a Variety of Sources
expectation: Synthesize information from a variety of sources.
overview: Synthesis is the capstone research move — not summarizing each source in turn, but fusing them into a coherent argument where the student's own thinking holds the structure together. In English IV, students should no longer be reporting what sources say; they should be using sources as raw material for claims they own.
studentFriendly: You can pull together information from multiple sources — articles, data sets, interviews, primary documents — and build something new out of them: an argument, a position, or an explanation that goes beyond what any single source says.
questionStems: Which sentence from the selection BEST synthesizes the information presented in paragraphs X and Y?
Based on the information in both sources, which conclusion is MOST strongly supported?
The student's claim in paragraph X is BEST supported by which evidence from Sources 1 and 2 combined?
Which statement MOST accurately represents what both authors would agree on, based on the selections?
How does the information in Source 2 develop or complicate the argument introduced in Source 1?
Which sentence from the student's research paper BEST demonstrates synthesis rather than summary?
Based on all three sources, which inference about [topic] is MOST likely supported?
The writer uses Source 3 in paragraph X MOST likely to —
Which revision BEST integrates evidence from multiple sources to support the central claim?
The student's conclusion in paragraph X draws on Sources 1, 2, and 3 to suggest that —

misconceptions.title: Listing Sources in Order Is the Same as Synthesizing Them
misconceptions.body: Students write a source-by-source parade: "Source 1 says X. Source 2 says Y. Source 3 says Z." That's a tour, not a synthesis. Teach them that synthesis requires a claim of their own to organize the sources around — the sources don't drive the paragraph, the argument does. If you can remove the student's sentences and the sources still make sense standing alone, synthesis hasn't happened.

misconceptions.title: Agreement Across Sources Equals Synthesis
misconceptions.body: Students find two sources that say similar things and call it done — "Both sources agree that X, therefore X." Push them deeper: synthesis names what the agreement means, what the agreement leaves unresolved, or what a third source complicates. Consensus is a data point, not a conclusion.

misconceptions.title: The Most Recent Source Wins
misconceptions.body: When sources conflict, students default to the newest one or the most official-sounding one without reasoning through the tension. Watch for phrases like "but the latest study says" used as a trump card. Teach them that resolving source conflict requires explaining WHY one source is more applicable to their specific question — not just when it was published.

misconceptions.title: Synthesis Means Blending Every Source Into Every Paragraph
misconceptions.body: Students sometimes try to cite four sources in a single sentence to demonstrate they've "used" everything. The result is unreadable and actually obscures their thinking. Teach them that synthesis is structural — it happens across a piece, not within a sentence. One paragraph might build primarily on two sources; another might pivot on a third. The architecture is the synthesis, not the citation density.

misconceptions.title: Personal Opinion Has No Place in Synthesized Research
misconceptions.body: Students have absorbed the message that research writing is "objective" and strip out their own reasoning entirely, leaving a patchwork of quotations with no connective tissue. In English IV, the student's intellectual judgment — which sources to trust, how to weight conflicting evidence, what the data actually implies — IS the synthesis. Teach them that their thinking is the glue, not a violation of academic integrity.

assignments.name: Source Convergence Map
assignments.body: Students receive four sources on a shared topic and map them visually before writing: areas of agreement, disagreement, and gaps. They then draft a thesis that emerges from the map, not from any single source.

assignments.name: Synthesis vs. Summary Revision
assignments.body: Students receive a sample paragraph that summarizes three sources sequentially. They revise it into a genuine synthesis paragraph built around a single argumentative claim. Pairs compare revisions and defend their structural choices.

assignments.name: The Conflict Paragraph
assignments.body: Students identify a genuine contradiction between two sources in their research and write a single paragraph that holds the tension productively — neither dismissing one source nor pretending the contradiction doesn't exist. Graded on how honestly and usefully the conflict is handled.

assignments.name: Annotated Research Draft
assignments.body: Students submit a draft research essay with marginal annotations explaining, for each paragraph, which sources are in play and what their own reasoning contributes beyond what the sources say. This makes invisible thinking visible and gradeable.

mastery: Organizes multi-source evidence around the student's own claim rather than around individual sources.
Names and reasons through contradictions between sources rather than ignoring them or defaulting to one without explanation.
Distinguishes what sources say from what the sources, taken together, imply — and articulates that inference explicitly.
Integrates evidence from disparate source types (primary documents, data, secondary analysis) without flattening their differences.
Produces a final research product in which the student's intellectual presence — their reasoning and judgment — is audible throughout.
notes: Vertical ramp from English III: English III Std 11(F) carries the same expectation language, but English IV is the terminal course — synthesis here must produce publishable-quality argumentation, not a demonstration that the student has handled multiple sources. The bar shifts from "did they use sources together?" to "does their thinking organize the sources, or do the sources organize their thinking?"

DOK 4 justification: synthesis across a variety of sources with the student's own reasoning as the organizing structure requires extended thinking, judgment about source weight and credibility, and original argumentation — all DOK 4 territory.

STAAR note: STAAR tests synthesis through multi-source expository and argumentative writing prompts; the question stems above mirror the paired-passage and multi-source reading comprehension tasks students will encounter on-test. Stems 1–10 also reflect the "research simulation" format used in STAAR's Writing and English III/IV assessments.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Examine Sources for Credibility, Bias, and Accuracy
expectation: Examine sources for credibility, bias, and accuracy.
overview: This is the research skill that separates competent hunters from naive collectors. Students aren't just finding sources — they're interrogating them, treating every text as a witness that could be mistaken, compromised, or outright dishonest. By English IV, the expectation is that this interrogation is automatic and rigorous, not a checklist performed for a grade.
studentFriendly: You can pick up any source — article, website, study, editorial — and determine whether it's trustworthy, what angle it's pushing, and whether its facts hold up under scrutiny.
questionStems: Which detail from the source MOST likely indicates a bias in the author's perspective?
Based on the information in paragraphs X–Y, the reader can MOST reasonably conclude that the source's credibility is —
The author's use of [phrase] in paragraph X BEST suggests that the source is —
Which piece of evidence from the passage BEST supports the inference that the author has a conflict of interest?
A student researching [topic] would MOST likely consider Source A more credible than Source B because —
Which detail from paragraph X raises the MOST significant concern about the accuracy of the source's claims?
The author's failure to acknowledge [counterevidence/limitation] in paragraph X MOST likely indicates —
Based on both sources, which statement BEST describes the difference in each author's perspective on [topic]?
Which question would MOST help a reader evaluate the credibility of the source presented in paragraphs X–Y?
The information in paragraph X conflicts with the information in paragraph Y. This conflict MOST likely suggests —

misconceptions.title: Published Means Credible
misconceptions.body: Students treat publication — in print, on a recognized platform, or with a byline — as a credibility guarantee. Teach them that publication is a floor, not a ceiling: it means someone let it through, not that someone verified it. Push them to ask WHO published it, for WHAT audience, under WHAT editorial standards, with WHAT financial stake. A peer-reviewed journal article and a sponsored blog post can share the same physical shelf.

misconceptions.title: Bias Disqualifies a Source
misconceptions.body: When students spot bias, they throw the source out. The better move is to use it with eyes open — a biased source can still supply facts, quotes, or perspectives that matter, as long as the bias is named and accounted for. Teach them: every source has a vantage point; the question is whether that vantage point has been disclosed or disguised.

misconceptions.title: Accuracy Is Binary
misconceptions.body: Students think a source is either accurate or wrong, and one factual error ruins the whole thing. Real source work is messier — a source can be accurate on facts and misleading in framing, accurate in its primary claims and sloppy in its statistics, accurate in 2019 and outdated in 2026. Teach them to locate the specific claim in question and check THAT, not write off the whole document for one imprecision.

misconceptions.title: The Most Recent Source Wins
misconceptions.body: Students default to recency as the credibility tiebreaker: newer is better. Watch for this especially in scientific and historical research. A 2023 source that misreads a primary document is less accurate than a 1987 scholarly edition that got it right. Recency matters in fast-moving fields; it's irrelevant — or even misleading — when older sources have had time to be tested and refined.

misconceptions.title: Credibility Evaluation Is a One-Time Step
misconceptions.body: Students check a source once at the beginning of a project, clear it, and never revisit. But credibility is relational and contextual — a source that's credible for one claim may not be credible for another claim made two paragraphs later. Teach them that source interrogation is continuous: every major claim pulled from a source is a new opportunity to ask whether this particular source is qualified to make this particular assertion.

assignments.name: Source Tribunal
assignments.body: Students receive three sources on the same topic — one credible and balanced, one credible but biased, one inaccurate. Without labeling which is which, assign students to rank them by credibility and write a one-paragraph justification per source naming specific textual evidence. Compare rankings as a class.

assignments.name: Bias Autopsy
assignments.body: Students select a source they planned to use for a research project and write a structured analysis: What is the author's stated purpose? What perspective is centered? What evidence or counterevidence is absent? What language signals a slant? Length: one page, evidence-based.

assignments.name: Cross-Check Protocol
assignments.body: Students take three factual claims from one source and verify each against two independent sources. They report what matched, what conflicted, and what could not be verified — and explain what those findings mean for how they'll use the original source.

assignments.name: Living Annotation
assignments.body: As students work through a research unit, they annotate each source with a running credibility note — one sentence per source per session updating their assessment as they learn more. At the end of the unit, they write a reflective paragraph on how their evaluation of one source changed over time.

mastery: Identifies specific textual evidence — word choice, source of funding, omitted counterevidence, publication context — when making claims about credibility or bias.
Distinguishes between a source's bias and its accuracy, using both dimensions independently to evaluate usefulness.
Applies credibility evaluation at the claim level, not just the source level — questions whether a given source is qualified to make a specific assertion.
Revises source assessments when new information from other sources conflicts with or complicates earlier judgments.
Uses biased or limited sources purposefully, with explicit acknowledgment of their limitations in written work.
notes: Vertical capstone: English III 11G.i establishes the skill; English IV is the terminal application. The ramp is from performing the evaluation (III) to performing it automatically, at the claim level, inside longer research projects (IV). The expectation is that credibility interrogation no longer requires prompting.

DOK 3 justification: students must apply evaluative criteria flexibly across varied source types and contexts, make inferences about author motive and reliability from textual evidence, and synthesize findings across sources — strategic thinking, not recall.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is most directly tested through paired-passage research simulations and multi-source synthesis tasks on STAAR. Stems mirror the "evaluate the sources" question type common to STAAR's Expository and Argumentative writing prompts and the research reading passages.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Examine Sources for Faulty Reasoning
expectation: Examine sources for faulty reasoning such as straw man, false dilemma, faulty analogies, and non-sequitur.
overview: This is the capstone source-evaluation skill: students are not just checking whether a source is credible, they are reading the argument's internal logic and catching the places where the reasoning breaks down. By English IV, students need to name specific fallacies — not just sense that something feels off.
studentFriendly: You can read a source's argument and identify exactly where the reasoning fails — whether it's a straw man misrepresenting the other side, a false dilemma forcing a fake either/or, a faulty analogy comparing things that don't actually compare, or a non-sequitur where the conclusion just doesn't follow.
questionStems: The author's argument in paragraph X is MOST likely an example of a straw man because —
Which statement from the selection BEST illustrates a false dilemma?
The analogy the author uses in paragraph X is flawed MOST likely because —
Based on paragraphs X–Y, the author's conclusion does NOT logically follow from the evidence because —
Which sentence from the selection BEST identifies a non-sequitur in the author's argument?
The author's reasoning in paragraph X is MOST likely faulty because the argument —
Which of the following BEST describes the logical error in the author's claim that [claim]?
The author's use of the analogy in paragraph X MOST nearly suggests that the author assumes —
Based on the selection, which statement BEST identifies a weakness in the author's line of reasoning?
The author's argument in paragraphs X–Y would be MOST weakened by which of the following?

misconceptions.title: Fallacy Detection Is Just Opinion
misconceptions.body: Students think labeling something a fallacy is a matter of personal disagreement — "I just don't buy it." Teach them that fallacy identification is structural: a straw man misrepresents an opposing position regardless of whether you agree with the author's conclusion. The test is the logic, not the politics. Push them to name the move precisely before they call it wrong.

misconceptions.title: Straw Man and False Dilemma Are Basically the Same Thing
misconceptions.body: Students lump these together because both distort an argument. The distinction matters: a straw man misrepresents the opponent's actual position; a false dilemma artificially narrows the options available. Watch for students who write "the author set up a straw man" when the text is actually presenting two choices as exhaustive — that's a false dilemma. The fix is to walk through each fallacy with a clean example first, isolated from a longer argument.

misconceptions.title: A Faulty Analogy Is Any Analogy They Find Imperfect
misconceptions.body: Students who've learned to distrust figurative language start flagging every analogy as faulty. Teach them the standard: an analogy fails when the comparison breaks down on a dimension that is relevant to the argument — not just because the two things are different. Apples and oranges are different, but comparing their nutritional density is still valid. The question is always whether the similarity that matters to the claim actually holds.

misconceptions.title: Non-Sequitur Means "Random" or "Off-Topic"
misconceptions.body: Students use non-sequitur loosely to mean "weird" or "beside the point." In logical argument, a non-sequitur is specific: the conclusion does not follow from the premises, even when each premise might be true on its own. Redirect them: can they reconstruct the premises? Does the stated conclusion actually follow? If yes, it's not a non-sequitur — something else is going wrong.

misconceptions.title: Identifying the Fallacy Is the Whole Job
misconceptions.body: Students stop after naming the fallacy — "this is a false dilemma" — and think they're done. The stronger move is explaining what the fallacy does to the source's credibility and usefulness for research purposes. Teach them: a source with a non-sequitur in its core claim can't support the conclusion it's advertising. Fallacy identification only pays off when it connects to source evaluation — what can this source still be used for, and what can it not support?

assignments.name: Fallacy Taxonomy Card
assignments.body: Students create a reference card with a precise definition, structural diagram, and real-world example for each of the four named fallacies. The examples must come from actual sources — news editorials, op-eds, political speeches — not invented scenarios. Card is used throughout the research unit.

assignments.name: Source Dissection
assignments.body: Students choose a persuasive source from their current research topic and annotate it for faulty reasoning. For each fallacy found, they must name it, quote the passage, explain the structural failure, and state whether the source can still be used for any research claim — and if so, which one.

assignments.name: Rewrite the Fallacy
assignments.body: Students take a passage containing one of the four fallacies and rewrite it so the reasoning is logically sound. They then write a one-paragraph explanation of what they changed and why the original failed. This forces them to understand the fallacy from the inside rather than just spotting it from the outside.

assignments.name: Two Sources, One Claim
assignments.body: Give students two sources that reach the same conclusion through different arguments. Students identify any faulty reasoning in each and evaluate which source would be more defensible to cite — and why.

mastery: Names a specific fallacy (straw man, false dilemma, faulty analogy, non-sequitur) and connects the label to a precise textual passage rather than a general impression.
Explains the structural failure — not just "this is wrong" but why the reasoning does not hold on its own terms.
Distinguishes between similar fallacies (e.g., straw man vs. false dilemma) without conflating them.
Applies fallacy identification to source evaluation — states what the source can and cannot credibly support as a result of the logical error found.
Reads argumentation charitably first — considers whether the reasoning could be reconstructed before reaching for a fallacy label.
notes: Vertical alignment: English III introduces source examination for bias and logical fallacies broadly; English IV sharpens that to named, specific fallacy types — straw man, false dilemma, faulty analogy, non-sequitur. The ramp is from general skepticism to structural precision.

This sub-expectation (ii) sits inside 11(G) "examine sources for:"; the full standard is about research source evaluation, so fallacy detection is always in service of the question "can I use this source, and for what?"
DOK 3 justification: students must use conceptual knowledge (fallacy definitions) to analyze and evaluate the reasoning structure of authentic, complex sources — not recall definitions in isolation.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is not directly tested as a standalone STAAR item at the English IV level, but it maps closely to author's craft and argument analysis items that ask students to identify weaknesses in an argument or evaluate the validity of reasoning across texts. The question stems above mirror that testing context.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Display Academic Citations and Use Source Materials Ethically
expectation: Display academic citations, including for paraphrased and quoted text, and use source materials ethically to avoid plagiarism.
overview: This is the capstone of research ethics for English IV — students have been building toward independent, college-ready research, and citation is where that independence gets tested. The skill isn't mechanical formatting; it's understanding WHY attribution exists and what it costs when it fails.
studentFriendly: You can correctly cite any source you use — whether you quoted it directly or put it in your own words — and you understand why doing so matters beyond just avoiding a zero.
questionStems: Read the following passage from a student research paper. Which citation BEST reflects the information included in the passage?
A student paraphrased the following source material but did not include a citation. Which of the following MOST likely explains why the omission constitutes plagiarism?
Which of the following in-text citations MOST accurately corresponds to the works-cited entry provided?
Based on the passage, the student's use of the source is MOST nearly an example of —
A student included the following quotation without attribution. Which revision BEST corrects the ethical problem in the student's paper?
Which source citation in the research paper MOST likely indicates that the student used the source ethically?
Read the student's paraphrase in paragraph 4. The student MOST likely needs to revise this passage because —
Which sentence from the research paper BEST demonstrates appropriate integration of a paraphrased source?
Based on the works-cited entry in paragraph 6, what can the reader MOST likely conclude about how the student used the source?
Which revision to the student's in-text citation in paragraph 3 MOST accurately reflects the source information provided?

misconceptions.title: Paraphrasing Means You Don't Have to Cite
misconceptions.body: Students believe that if they put something "in their own words," the idea becomes theirs and citation is optional. Teach them the distinction between form and origin: paraphrasing changes the phrasing, not the intellectual source. Watch for the student who swaps three words from a sentence and considers the debt paid — that's still a citation required.

misconceptions.title: Citation Is a Formatting Exercise
misconceptions.body: Students treat citation style (MLA, APA, Chicago) as an arbitrary formatting ritual with no deeper logic. Reframe it: every element of a citation exists so a reader can locate the exact source. When a student omits a page number or publisher, ask them — could your reader find this? That's not a style question; it's a function question.

misconceptions.title: Patchwriting Is an Acceptable Middle Ground
misconceptions.body: Many students operate in a gray zone — they don't copy verbatim, but they don't truly paraphrase either. They swap synonyms, shuffle clauses, and call it their own. This is patchwriting, and it's plagiarism with extra steps. Teach them to close the source, wait, write from memory what they understood, and then verify. The idea should pass through the student's thinking before it reaches the page.

misconceptions.title: Only Cutting and Pasting Is Plagiarism
misconceptions.body: Students often have a narrow, theft-focused conception of plagiarism: they think it's only plagiarism if you literally copied a passage. They don't recognize self-plagiarism, the misrepresentation of a secondary source as primary, or failing to cite a summary as problems. Expand their definition: plagiarism is any misrepresentation of where an idea came from — including from their own prior work submitted elsewhere.

misconceptions.title: Getting Caught Is the Real Problem
misconceptions.body: Some students privately believe the ethical framing is performative — the real issue is detection, not integrity. This is worth confronting directly. Ask them to articulate what is actually harmed when a researcher misattributes an idea. The conversation should land on trust: academic fields run on the assumption that sources are real and attribution is honest. When it isn't, the whole enterprise of shared knowledge starts to collapse.

assignments.name: Citation Audit
assignments.body: Students exchange research drafts and highlight every claim, fact, and borrowed idea in a partner's paper. For each highlight, they determine whether the citation is present, correct, and sufficient. Findings are returned as a written annotation, not a grade — the writer revises before submission.

assignments.name: Patchwriting Clinic
assignments.body: Provide students with three examples of patchwriting alongside the original source. Students must (a) identify the patchwriting, (b) explain why it is still plagiarism, and (c) produce a genuine paraphrase with a correct citation.

assignments.name: Works-Cited Reconstruction
assignments.body: Give students a set of in-text citations from a model research paper and a stack of raw source information (URLs, author names, publication dates). Students build the works-cited page from scratch, then compare against a key. Errors are categorized: missing field, wrong format, wrong order.

assignments.name: Ethical Edge Cases
assignments.body: Present four borderline scenarios (AI-generated summary, a classmate's unpublished notes, a translated passage, a secondary citation). Students write a paragraph for each: does this require citation? Why or why not? What's the ethical obligation?

assignments.name: The Stakes Conversation
assignments.body: Students research a real-world case of academic or journalistic plagiarism (e.g., a retracted study, a fired journalist). They present a 2-minute summary: what happened, what was cited incorrectly or not at all, and what the professional consequence was.

mastery: Consistently includes in-text citations for both quoted and paraphrased material with no prompting.
Produces works-cited or reference entries with all required fields in the correct format for the assigned citation style.
Can explain, in their own words, the functional purpose of each element in a citation — not just the rule.
Identifies patchwriting in sample passages and distinguishes it from genuine paraphrase.
Applies ethical reasoning to novel citation scenarios (AI-generated text, secondary sources, self-referencing) without a checklist.
notes: Vertical alignment: English III's 11(H) requires the same citation and plagiarism avoidance standard. English IV is the terminal course — students are expected to apply this independently, across multiple citation styles, and to novel source types (databases, multimedia, AI-generated content). The skill no longer needs scaffolding; it needs internalization.

DOK 3 justification: correct citation requires strategic thinking — students must evaluate which elements apply, how a particular source type maps to a citation template, and whether their use of a source is ethical under multiple criteria.

STAAR analog note: 11(H) is not directly tested as a discrete skill on STAAR; it appears embedded in composition scoring and research task prompts. Question stems above mirror how STAAR and college-placement assessments evaluate citation reasoning through annotated student samples and revision tasks.
Voice locked per HANDOFF.md.

title: Use an Appropriate Mode of Delivery to Present Research Results
expectation: Use an appropriate mode of delivery, whether written, oral, or multimodal, to present results.
overview: This is the capstone move of the research strand — students don't just finish an inquiry, they make a deliberate rhetorical decision about HOW to deliver it. The word "appropriate" is doing all the work: the mode must fit the purpose, audience, and findings, not just default to whatever the student is most comfortable with.
studentFriendly: You choose how to deliver your research — written report, oral presentation, or something multimodal — and that choice itself has to be justified by who you're talking to and what your findings actually need.
questionStems: Based on the student's research findings in paragraph X, which mode of delivery would MOST likely be appropriate for a general public audience?
The student chose to present findings using a multimodal format. Which evidence from the report BEST supports the effectiveness of this choice?
Which statement BEST explains why the author selected an oral presentation rather than a written report to deliver these results?
Read paragraph X. The researcher's decision to include [visual/audio/text element] MOST likely serves to —
Which sentence from the presentation BEST demonstrates that the mode of delivery fits the intended audience?
The student MOST likely chose a written report over an oral presentation because —
Based on the information in paragraphs X–Y, what is the MOST likely effect of the multimodal elements on the audience's understanding of the research?
Which organizational choice BEST supports the clarity of the researcher's oral delivery in this passage?
The researcher includes [chart/image/transcript] alongside the written findings. This decision MOST nearly serves to —
Which statement would BEST support the conclusion that the student selected an appropriate mode of delivery for this research?

misconceptions.title: "Multimodal" Means Adding Slides to a Speech
misconceptions.body: Students conflate multimodal delivery with "a PowerPoint presentation." Teach them that multimodal means the modes are doing distinct, non-redundant work — the visual carries information the words cannot, or the audio creates an experience the text alone cannot. A slideshow where every bullet just echoes what the speaker said is not multimodal, it's illustrated oral delivery. Push them to ask: what is each mode contributing that the others can't?

misconceptions.title: The Written Report Is the Default, Everything Else Is Extra
misconceptions.body: When students have a choice, they reach for a written report because it feels like what school has always asked for. Watch for research that is clearly suited to oral or multimodal delivery — interviews, community narratives, performance art analyses — being flattened into five-paragraph prose. The assignment should push students to justify their mode choice in writing before they begin, not discover it at the end.

misconceptions.title: Appropriate Mode = Personal Preference
misconceptions.body: Students interpret "appropriate" as permission to do whatever they're most comfortable with. Reframe this: appropriate means the mode that best serves the audience and the findings. A student who is a confident public speaker still needs to ask whether their findings belong in spoken form — and a student who hates presenting still needs to ask whether their research demands a live audience. Comfort is not a rhetorical criterion.

misconceptions.title: Oral Presentation Means Reading a Script Aloud
misconceptions.body: When students choose oral delivery, they often produce a written report and read it word for word. Teach them that oral and written modes have different conventions for a reason — spoken argument uses repetition, forecasting, and pacing that written argument does not. A script read aloud is not an oral presentation; it's a written report performed badly. Hold them to the conventions of the mode they chose.

misconceptions.title: The Research Is Done When the Findings Are Found
misconceptions.body: Students treat delivery as the last step before they're done — something to get through. The delivery IS the inquiry's endpoint; it's the moment where research becomes communication, and communication requires an audience, a purpose, and a deliberate choice. Teach them that a poorly delivered strong finding is a failed research project, because the finding never reached anyone who could use it.

assignments.name: Mode Justification Memo
assignments.body: Before beginning any research presentation, students submit a one-page memo arguing for their chosen mode of delivery. They must name their audience, explain why the mode fits the findings, and identify one mode they rejected and why. Graded on reasoning, not on the "right" answer.

assignments.name: Cross-Mode Translation
assignments.body: Students take a completed research finding and deliver it three ways: a 300-word written abstract, a 3-minute oral summary, and a one-page multimodal brief. After all three, they write a paragraph explaining which mode did the best job and why — and which mode cost them something.

assignments.name: Audience-Specific Delivery
assignments.body: Students present the same research to two different audiences (e.g., a community stakeholder panel and a peer group) using different modes. Debrief focuses on how the mode shifted and what had to change to maintain appropriateness across contexts.

assignments.name: Peer Mode Critique
assignments.body: After presentations, students write a one-paragraph critique of a peer's mode choice — was it appropriate? What did it gain? What did it sacrifice? Graded for specificity and evidence from the actual presentation, not personal taste.

mastery: Selects a mode of delivery — written, oral, or multimodal — and articulates a clear rationale tied to audience, purpose, and the nature of the findings.

Executes the chosen mode with the conventions appropriate to it: oral delivery uses spoken argument conventions, written delivery uses prose conventions, multimodal delivery uses modes non-redundantly.
Demonstrates awareness that the choice of mode shapes what the audience receives — not just how, but what.
Adjusts elements of delivery (organization, pacing, visual design, diction) to serve the mode rather than defaulting to a one-size-fits-all template.
Reflects on the effectiveness of their mode choice after delivery, identifying what the mode enabled and what it constrained.
notes: Vertical alignment: English III's 11(I) carries the same expectation verbatim. The English IV context distinguishes itself through the complexity of the research and the expectation of full rhetorical intentionality — seniors are expected to select AND justify their mode, not just execute it. The move from III to IV is from competent delivery to deliberate communication.

DOK 3 / Apply / Evaluate justification: the task requires applying knowledge of rhetorical modes to a specific research context (DOK 3) and evaluating the fit between findings and form (Evaluate). The justification memo assignment pushes this toward DOK 4 when students defend their choices in extended reasoning.

STAAR analog note: this TEK is not directly assessed on STAAR as a discrete item; stems above model how STAAR would test the analog through reading a research presentation transcript and evaluating delivery choices as author's craft decisions.
